Don't write $\sum P_i = P$ when $P = 0$. > 5. **Forgetting to verify:** Always plug back and check that forces balance or elongations match. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]** **Q.B3.(a)** A **three-material composite bar** is built up from: - **Aluminium:** $A_1 = 400 \text{ mm}^2$, $E_1 = 70 \text{ GPa}$ - **Brass:** $A_2 = 300 \text{ mm}^2$, $E_2 = 100 \text{ GPa}$ - **Steel:** $A_3 = 250 \text{ mm}^2$, $E_3 = 200 \text{ GPa}$ The bar has a length of **800 mm** and is subjected to an axial load of **60 kN**. Determine: 1. The stress in each material. 2. The total elongation of the bar. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Convert moduli to N/mm²:** $$E_1 = 70 \times 10^3 \text{ N/mm}^2$$ $$E_2 = 100 \times 10^3 \text{ N/mm}^2$$ $$E_3 = 200 \times 10^3 \text{ N/mm}^2$$ **Step 2 — Compute axial rigidities:** $$E_1 A_1 = (70 \times 10^3)(400) = 28.0 \times 10^6 \text{ N}$$ $$E_2 A_2 = (100 \times 10^3)(300) = 30.0 \times 10^6 \text{ N}$$ $$E_3 A_3 = (200 \times 10^3)(250) = 50.0 \times 10^6 \text{ N}$$ **Step 3 — Sum of rigidities:** $$\sum E_i A_i = (28.0 + 30.0 + 50.0) \times 10^6 = 108.0 \times 10^6 \text{ N}$$ **Step 4 — Compute stresses:** $$\sigma_1 = \dfrac{P \, E_1}{\sum E_i A_i} = \dfrac{(60 \times 10^3)(70 \times 10^3)}{108.0 \times 10^6} = \mathbf{38.89 \text{ N/mm}^2}$$ $$\sigma_2 = \dfrac{P \, E_2}{\sum E_i A_i} = \dfrac{(60 \times 10^3)(100 \times 10^3)}{108.0 \times 10^6} = \mathbf{55.56 \text{ N/mm}^2}$$ $$\sigma_3 = \dfrac{P \, E_3}{\sum E_i A_i} = \dfrac{(60 \times 10^3)(200 \times 10^3)}{108.0 \times 10^6} = \mathbf{111.11 \text{ N/mm}^2}$$ **Step 5 — Verify stress ratio = modulus ratio:** $$\sigma_1 : \sigma_2 : \sigma_3 = 38.89 : 55.56 : 111.11 = 1 : 1.43 : 2.86 \approx 70 : 100 : 200 = E_1 : E_2 : E_3 \quad \checkmark$$ **Step 6 — Compute total elongation:** $$\delta = \dfrac{PL}{\sum E_i A_i} = \dfrac{(60 \times 10^3)(800)}{108.0 \times 10^6} = \mathbf{0.4444 \text{ mm}}$$ **Verification using individual elongations (must all be equal):** - Al: $\delta_1 = \sigma_1 L / E_1 = (38.89)(800)/(70 \times 10^3) = 0.4444$ mm ✓ - Brass: $\delta_2 = \sigma_2 L / E_2 = (55.56)(800)/(100 \times 10^3) = 0.4444$ mm ✓ - Steel: $\delta_3 = \sigma_3 L / E_3 = (111.11)(800)/(200 \times 10^3) = 0.4444$ mm ✓ --- **Q.B3.(b)** A composite bar of length **1.2 m** is rigidly fixed at both ends. It is made of a **steel portion** (length 600 mm, area 500 mm²) and a **bronze portion** (length 600 mm, area 750 mm²), placed end-to-end in **series**. The temperature of the bar is raised by **80 °C**. Find the stresses developed in steel and bronze. Take: $E_s = 200 \text{ GPa}$, $E_b = 100 \text{ GPa}$, $\alpha_s = 12 \times 10^{-6} /°\text{C}$, $\alpha_b = 18 \times 10^{-6} /°\text{C}$. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Free thermal expansion of each section (if independent):** $$\delta_s = L_s \alpha_s \Delta T = (600)(12 \times 10^{-6})(80) = 0.576 \text{ mm}$$ $$\delta_b = L_b \alpha_b \Delta T = (600)(18 \times 10^{-6})(80) = 0.864 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 2 — Compatibility (both must elongate by same amount $\delta$ since rigid walls):** Since the materials are bonded **in series** (end-to-end), the **force is the same in both sections** (let's call it $P$, the compressive reaction from walls). Force in steel (compression) = Force in bronze (compression) = $P$ (Newton's third law). **Step 3 — Total elongation of composite bar:** $$\delta = \delta_s + \delta_b = L_s \alpha_s \Delta T - \dfrac{P L_s}{E_s A_s} + L_b \alpha_b \Delta T - \dfrac{P L_b}{E_b A_b}$$ **Step 4 — Since both ends are rigid, total elongation $\delta = 0$:** $$L_s \alpha_s \Delta T + L_b \alpha_b \Delta T = P \left( \dfrac{L_s}{E_s A_s} + \dfrac{L_b}{E_b A_b} \right)$$ **Step 5 — Substitute values:** - LHS = $0.576 + 0.864 = 1.440 \text{ mm}$ - $\dfrac{L_s}{E_s A_s} = \dfrac{600}{(200 \times 10^3)(500)} = \dfrac{600}{100 \times 10^6} = 6.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mm/N}$ - $\dfrac{L_b}{E_b A_b} = \dfrac{600}{(100 \times 10^3)(750)} = \dfrac{600}{75 \times 10^6} = 8.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mm/N}$ **Step 6 — Solve for $P$:** $$1.440 = P \times (6.0 + 8.0) \times 10^{-6} = P \times 14.0 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$P = \dfrac{1.440}{14.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 102.857 \text{ kN} = 102.857 \times 10^3 \text{ N}$$ **Step 7 — Compute stresses:** $$\sigma_s = \dfrac{P}{A_s} = \dfrac{102.857 \times 10^3}{500} = 205.71 \text{ N/mm}^2 \quad \text{(compressive)}$$ $$\sigma_b = \dfrac{P}{A_b} = \dfrac{102.857 \times 10^3}{750} = 137.14 \text{ N/mm}^2 \quad \text{(compressive)}$$ **Step 8 — Verify elongations cancel:** Elongation of steel section: $0.576 - (102.857 \times 10^3)(6.0 \times 10^{-6}) = 0.576 - 0.617 = -0.041$ mm Elongation of bronze section: $0.864 - (102.857 \times 10^3)(8.0 \times 10^{-6}) = 0.864 - 0.823 = 0.041$ mm Sum = $-0.041 + 0.041 = 0$ ✓ (Confirms total elongation is zero, as required by rigid walls.) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout for Question B:** > 1. **Confusing parallel vs. series arrangement:** When materials are **side-by-side (concentric/parallel)**, force divides but strain is the same. When **end-to-end (series)**, force is the same but each section has its own strain. **Read the problem carefully.** > 2. **Sign convention errors:** Thermal expansion always tries to **elongate**; wall reaction is **compressive**. Mark compressive stress as **negative** if using $\pm$ sign convention. > 3. **Series vs. parallel detection test:** If all materials share the **same length** → parallel arrangement. If materials have **different lengths** joined end-to-end → series arrangement. > 4. **Always verify $\sum \delta_i = 0$** in series thermal problems as a check. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **🚀 RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Bars of Composite Sections** #### Core Principles (Memorize First) - ✅ **Strain compatibility:** $\varepsilon_1 = \varepsilon_2 = \ldots = \varepsilon$ (all bonded materials stretch together) - ✅ **Force equilibrium:** $P_{\text{ext}} = \sum \sigma_i A_i$ (total load is shared) - ✅ **Hooke's Law per material:** $\sigma_i = E_i \varepsilon_i$ - ✅ **Master formula:** $\varepsilon = P / \sum E_i A_i$ — *the single most important equation* #### Direction-of-Thinking (DOT) Heuristics - ✅ **Stiffer material carries more load** → higher stress (proportional to $E$). - ✅ **Stress ratio** $\sigma_1 : \sigma_2 = E_1 : E_2$ — quick check. - ✅ **Larger area carries more load** at constant stress, but here stress also varies, so load shares as $E_i A_i$. - ✅ **Modular ratio method** is faster for parallel arrangements (use steel as reference). #### Thermal Effects (High-Yield Exam Topic) - ✅ Material with **higher $\alpha$** goes into **compression** (restrained from expanding). - ✅ Material with **lower $\alpha$** goes into **tension** (pulled by the other). - ✅ In series: $\delta_{\text{total}} = 0$ (fixed ends) gives equilibrium equation. - ✅ In parallel: same force equation, but with common elongation. #### Common Numerical Pitfalls (Avoid These!) - ⚠️ Convert $E$ from **GPa to N/mm²** by multiplying by $10^3$. - ⚠️ Areas in **mm²**, lengths in **mm**, forces in **N** → stresses in **N/mm²** (MPa). - ⚠️ Check if **gap exists** before assuming full restraint in thermal problems. - ⚠️ Always **verify** your answer by checking $\sum P_i = P$ (force balance) or $\delta_1 = \delta_2$ (compatibility). #### Naval Architecture Applications to Remember - 🚢 Propeller shafts (steel + bronze sleeve) - 🚢 Masts (steel + aluminium sections) - 🚢 Rudder stocks (steel + stainless cladding) - 🚢 Stern frames and rudder horns - 🚢 Bimetallic thermal switches in engine rooms #### Quick Decision Tree for Problem Solving ``` Is the bar fully bonded & loaded axially? ├─ YES → Use P / Σ(EiAi) for strain │ → σi = Ei × ε │ → δ = PL / Σ(EiAi) │ └─ Thermal loading too? ├─ Fixed ends → δ = 0 constraint, find σi └─ Gap present → check gap vs free expansion ```

topics.title: Temperature Stresses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST303 - SOLID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Temperature Stresses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Solid Mechanics for Naval Architects**, **Temperature (Thermal) Stresses** are the internal stresses induced in a body, or in a structural member, when a change in temperature ($\Delta T$) is accompanied by a **geometric constraint** that prevents free thermal expansion or contraction.

A body that is *free* to expand or contract undergoes only **thermal strain** (no stress). A body that is *fully restrained* undergoes only **mechanical strain** equal and opposite to the free thermal strain, producing pure **thermal stress**. Real marine and ship structures almost always fall between these two extremes.

$$ \sigma_T = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T \quad \text{(for a fully restrained homogeneous bar)} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module 1**
> The treatment of thermal stresses is one of the **high-yield topics** in Module 1. Every semester, KTU's End Semester Evaluation (ESE) includes at least one direct numerical (Part A, 3 marks) or full derivation question (Part B, 14 marks) on temperature stresses in single or composite bars.

## 1.2 Intuition: The "Jammed Railing" Analogy

Imagine two children of different heights walking side-by-side along a corridor. The corridor widens in summer (thermal expansion) and narrows in winter (thermal contraction). If nothing constrains them, both children simply walk further apart or closer — *no pushing occurs*.

Now imagine they are tied together with a rigid belt (the **constraint**). When the corridor widens, the shorter child is *pulled forward* and feels tension; when it narrows, the shorter child is *pushed back* and feels compression. The **belt** carries the internal stress because it tries to keep them at the same spacing.

Substitute:
- *Children* → two bars of different materials
- *Corridor widening* → temperature rise ($\Delta T > 0$)
- *Belt* → the mechanical restraint (rigid wall, weld, riveted joint, shaft coupling)

This is exactly why riveted hull plates, propeller shafts, and bearing housings in ships experience **thermal stresses** even when no external mechanical load is applied.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Units (Naval Architecture Convention)

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Value (Mild Steel) | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Young's Modulus | $E$ | $\mathbf{2.0 \times 10^5}$ | $\text{N/mm}^2$ or $\text{MPa}$ |
| Coefficient of Linear Thermal Expansion | $\alpha$ | $\mathbf{12 \times 10^{-6}}$ | per $^\circ\text{C}$ (or /K) |
| Temperature Change | $\Delta T$ | problem-specific | $^\circ\text{C}$ or K |
| Length of member | $L$ | problem-specific | mm or m |
| Cross-sectional Area | $A$ | problem-specific | $\text{mm}^2$ or $\text{m}^2$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Naval Architects**: In shipbuilding, $\alpha$ for **steel ≈ 12 × 10⁻⁶ /°C**, for **aluminum alloys ≈ 23 × 10⁻⁶ /°C**, and for **bronze (propeller) ≈ 18–19 × 10⁻⁶ /°C**. These differences are why **composite propeller-shaft assemblies** and **bi-metallic cooling pipes** are classic KTU problem statements.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Three Restraint Regimes

A bar of length $L$, cross-section $A$, Young's modulus $E$, and coefficient of thermal expansion $\alpha$ is subjected to a uniform temperature change $\Delta T = T_{\text{final}} - T_{\text{initial}}$. Its **total axial strain** $\varepsilon_{\text{total}}$ can always be written as:

$$ \varepsilon_{\text{total}} = \varepsilon_{\text{mechanical}} + \varepsilon_{\text{thermal}} = \frac{\sigma}{E} + \alpha \cdot \Delta T $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This single equation is the **master equation** for all thermal stress problems at the KTU B.Tech level. Every derivation and numerical below is built from it.

Based on the boundary condition imposed on $\varepsilon_{\text{total}}$, three cases arise:

### Case A — Completely Free Bar (No Restraint)
- $\varepsilon_{\text{mechanical}} = 0$, so $\sigma = 0$
- The bar freely expands by $\delta_T = L \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$
- **Stress = 0**, but **deformation ≠ 0**

### Case B — Fully Restrained Bar (Rigid Walls)
- $\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = 0$
- The wall supplies a mechanical strain exactly equal to $-\alpha \cdot \Delta T$
- Resulting stress: $\sigma_T = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$
- Sign convention: **compressive** if $\Delta T > 0$ (heated bar pushed back), **tensile** if $\Delta T < 0$ (cooled bar held back)

### Case C — Partially Restrained Bar (Spring / Elastic Support)
- The bar expands by $\delta$ which is less than the free thermal expansion
- The mechanical strain is $\varepsilon_{\text{mech}} = \varepsilon_{\text{total}} - \alpha \cdot \Delta T = \delta/L - \alpha \cdot \Delta T$
- The stress is then $\sigma = E \cdot (\delta/L - \alpha \cdot \Delta T)$
- Determined by **equilibrium + compatibility** with the support stiffness

## 2.2 Composite Bars — The Real Naval Architecture Problem

A composite system comprises **two (or more) materials** rigidly connected so that they must elongate by the same amount. Let the subscripts 1 and 2 denote the two materials.

### Equilibrium
Since both bars share the load with rigid end-blocks, the **axial force in bar 1 must equal the axial force in bar 2**:

$$ \sigma_1 \cdot A_1 = \sigma_2 \cdot A_2 $$

### Compatibility
The total elongations of both bars are equal (they are mechanically linked):

$$ \delta_1 = \delta_2 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \frac{\sigma_1}{E_1} + \alpha_1 \Delta T = \frac{\sigma_2}{E_2} + \alpha_2 \Delta T $$

### The Two Master Results

Solving equilibrium and compatibility simultaneously yields:

$$ \sigma_1 = \frac{E_1 \, E_2 \, A_2 \, (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \, \Delta T}{E_1 A_1 + E_2 A_2} $$

$$ \sigma_2 = \frac{E_1 \, E_2 \, A_1 \, (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \, \Delta T}{E_1 A_1 + E_2 A_2} $$

The material with the **higher** $\alpha$ ends up in **compression** (its free expansion was suppressed), and the material with the **lower** $\alpha$ ends up in **tension** (it was stretched to keep up).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity Check Rule** (used by KTU examiners): $\sigma_1 \cdot A_1$ must equal $\sigma_2 \cdot A_2$ numerically. Always verify this — it is the #1 valuation trick used by KTU to award partial marks when the final answer has an arithmetic slip.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Situation | Governing Formula | Units (SI) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Free thermal expansion | $\delta_T = L \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | m |
| 2 | Master strain equation | $\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = \sigma / E + \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | Fully restrained single bar | $\sigma_T = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | Pa or N/mm² |
| 4 | Force in fully restrained bar | $P_T = E \cdot A \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | N |
| 5 | Composite bar stress (material 1) | $\sigma_1 = \dfrac{E_1 E_2 A_2 (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \Delta T}{E_1 A_1 + E_2 A_2}$ | Pa |
| 6 | Composite bar stress (material 2) | $\sigma_2 = \dfrac{E_1 E_2 A_1 (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \Delta T}{E_1 A_1 + E_2 A_2}$ | Pa |
| 7 | Common expansion of composite | $\delta = L \cdot \dfrac{E_1 A_1 \alpha_1 + E_2 A_2 \alpha_2}{E_1 A_1 + E_2 A_2} \cdot \Delta T$ | m |
| 8 | Bar with intermediate support of stiffness $k$ | $\sigma = \dfrac{E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T}{1 + EA/(kL)}$ | Pa |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineer's Reality Check**: For a steel bar of length $1\,\text{m}$ heated by $50\,^\circ\text{C}$, $\sigma_T = 2 \times 10^5 \times 12 \times 10^{-6} \times 50 = 120\,\text{MPa}$. This is **near the yield stress of mild steel**. This is why **expansion joints, slotted bolt holes, and sliding bearings** are mandatory in long marine piping and hull stringers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Stress in a Fully Restrained Single Bar

**Statement:** A homogeneous prismatic bar of length $L$, area $A$, Young's modulus $E$, and thermal expansion coefficient $\alpha$ is rigidly held between two immovable walls. The temperature rises uniformly by $\Delta T$. Derive the induced thermal stress.

### Step 1 — State the Geometry and Constraint
The bar's two ends are clamped. The total elongation must be **zero**:

$$ \delta_{\text{total}} = 0 $$

### Step 2 — Decompose Total Elongation
Total elongation has two parts — **mechanical** (due to internal stress) and **thermal** (due to temperature):

$$ \delta_{\text{total}} = \delta_{\text{mech}} + \delta_{\text{thermal}} $$

### Step 3 — Express Each Part
The mechanical part uses Hooke's law for an axially loaded bar:

$$ \delta_{\text{mech}} = \frac{P \cdot L}{A \cdot E} = \frac{\sigma \cdot L}{E} $$

The thermal part is the free expansion that *would* have occurred:

$$ \delta_{\text{thermal}} = L \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T $$

### Step 4 — Apply the Constraint $\delta_{\text{total}} = 0$

$$ \frac{\sigma \cdot L}{E} + L \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T = 0 $$

### Step 5 — Solve for Stress
Divide both sides by $L$ and rearrange:

$$ \sigma = -E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T $$

The negative sign indicates the stress is **compressive** when $\Delta T > 0$ (heating). The magnitude is the well-known thermal stress formula:

$$ \boxed{\sigma_T = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T} $$

### Step 6 — Resulting Axial Force in the Bar
Multiplying by the area:

$$ P_T = \sigma_T \cdot A = E \cdot A \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T $$

The reaction force at each wall equals $P_T$ and acts **inward** on the wall.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Composite Bar Between Two Rigid Walls

**Statement:** Two bars — material 1 and material 2 — are connected in parallel between two rigid walls (a typical **bi-metallic strip** or **clad plate** configuration). Derive the stresses $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$ when the temperature rises by $\Delta T$.

### Step 1 — Free-Body Diagram of the Joint
Cut a section through both bars. The internal axial forces in bars 1 and 2 must satisfy **horizontal equilibrium**:

$$ P_1 + P_2 = R_{\text{wall}} $$

But because the wall is rigid, the net force transmitted to it must equal what the wall reacts with. By symmetry of the cut, the two bars together push on the wall, so each bar carries an axial force but the **forces are internal to the assembly**. The wall's reaction equals the net force the two bars together exert. For equal end-displacements, equilibrium is:

$$ \sigma_1 \cdot A_1 = \sigma_2 \cdot A_2 $$

This equality means the two forces are **equal in magnitude and opposite in sign** when traced through the assembly — bar 1 pushes one way, bar 2 pushes the other way. Magnitudes must match.

### Step 2 — Compatibility (Equal Elongation)
The end-block forces both bars to undergo the same elongation $\delta$:

$$ \delta_1 = \delta_2 = \delta $$

For bar 1, the total elongation is mechanical minus thermal (since it is being pushed back):

$$ \delta = L \cdot \alpha_1 \cdot \Delta T - \frac{\sigma_1 \cdot L}{E_1} $$

For bar 2, the total elongation is mechanical plus thermal (it is being stretched):

$$ \delta = \frac{\sigma_2 \cdot L}{E_2} + L \cdot \alpha_2 \cdot \Delta T $$

Wait — this assumes $\alpha_1 > \alpha_2$. If we don't know this in advance, we simply write the strain-based compatibility using the master equation.

### Step 3 — Write Master Strain Equation for Each Bar

$$ \varepsilon_{\text{total}} = \frac{\sigma_1}{E_1} + \alpha_1 \Delta T \quad \text{(bar 1)} $$

$$ \varepsilon_{\text{total}} = \frac{\sigma_2}{E_2} + \alpha_2 \Delta T \quad \text{(bar 2)} $$

Equating the two expressions for the common total strain:

$$ \frac{\sigma_1}{E_1} + \alpha_1 \Delta T = \frac{\sigma_2}{E_2} + \alpha_2 \Delta T $$

### Step 4 — Use Equilibrium to Eliminate One Unknown
From $\sigma_1 A_1 = \sigma_2 A_2$, we have $\sigma_2 = \sigma_1 A_1 / A_2$. Substituting:

$$ \frac{\sigma_1}{E_1} + \alpha_1 \Delta T = \frac{\sigma_1 A_1}{E_2 A_2} + \alpha_2 \Delta T $$

### Step 5 — Solve for $\sigma_1$
Collect the $\sigma_1$ terms on one side:

$$ \sigma_1 \left( \frac{1}{E_1} - \frac{A_1}{E_2 A_2} \right) = (\alpha_2 - \alpha_1) \Delta T $$

Common denominator on the left:

$$ \sigma_1 \cdot \frac{E_2 A_2 - E_1 A_1}{E_1 E_2 A_2} = (\alpha_2 - \alpha_1) \Delta T $$

Multiply both sides by $E_1 E_2 A_2$:

$$ \sigma_1 \cdot (E_2 A_2 - E_1 A_1) = E_1 E_2 A_2 (\alpha_2 - \alpha_1) \Delta T $$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $-1$ to obtain the standard form:

$$ \sigma_1 = \frac{E_1 E_2 A_2 (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \Delta T}{E_1 A_1 + E_2 A_2} $$

### Step 6 — Solve for $\sigma_2$
By symmetric reasoning (swap indices 1 and 2):

$$ \sigma_2 = \frac{E_1 E_2 A_1 (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \Delta T}{E_1 A_1 + E_2 A_2} $$

### Step 7 — Verify the Sign Convention
- If $\alpha_1 > \alpha_2$: $\sigma_1 > 0$ in our sign convention (compressive, since bar 1 is the one whose expansion was suppressed), and $\sigma_2 < 0$ (tensile, since bar 2 was stretched).
- If $\alpha_1 < \alpha_2$: roles reverse.
- Equilibrium check: $\sigma_1 A_1 = \sigma_2 A_2$ ✓.

### Step 8 — Common Elongation
Substituting back gives the actual deformation of the assembly:

$$ \delta = L \cdot \frac{E_1 A_1 \alpha_1 + E_2 A_2 \alpha_2}{E_1 A_1 + E_2 A_2} \cdot \Delta T $$

---

## 3.3 Solved Numerical — Worked Example (Full Model Solution)

> **Problem**: A steel rod ($E_s = 200\,\text{GPa}$, $\alpha_s = 12 \times 10^{-6}\,/^\circ\text{C}$, $A_s = 600\,\text{mm}^2$) and a brass rod ($E_b = 100\,\text{GPa}$, $\alpha_b = 19 \times 10^{-6}\,/^\circ\text{C}$, $A_b = 400\,\text{mm}^2$) are connected in series between two rigid walls, as shown in standard textbook diagrams. The temperature of the assembly is raised by $\Delta T = 60\,^\circ\text{C}$. Find the stresses in each rod and the reaction at the walls.

**Solution:**

### Step 1 — Identify the Configuration
The two rods are in **series** (end-to-end), so the same axial force passes through both. This is a different configuration from parallel — here the **force is common**, not the displacement.

Equilibrium:

$$ P_s = P_b = R $$

### Step 2 — Compatibility
Total expansion of the assembly equals zero (walls rigid):

$$ \delta_s + \delta_b = 0 $$

Each rod's total elongation is mechanical plus thermal:

$$ \left( \frac{P \cdot L}{A_s E_s} + L \alpha_s \Delta T \right) + \left( \frac{P \cdot L}{A_b E_b} + L \alpha_b \Delta T \right) = 0 $$

(Lengths $L$ cancel if both rods are the same length; for unequal lengths, keep them as $L_s$ and $L_b$. Assume $L_s = L_b = L$ for this problem.)

### Step 3 — Solve for Force $P$

$$ P \left( \frac{1}{A_s E_s} + \frac{1}{A_b E_b} \right) = -(\alpha_s + \alpha_b) \Delta T $$

Compute each term (using $E$ in $\text{N/mm}^2 = \text{MPa}$ and $A$ in $\text{mm}^2$):

$$ \frac{1}{A_s E_s} = \frac{1}{600 \times 200 \times 10^3} = \frac{1}{1.2 \times 10^8} = 8.333 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{/N} $$

$$ \frac{1}{A_b E_b} = \frac{1}{400 \times 100 \times 10^3} = \frac{1}{4 \times 10^7} = 2.5 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{/N} $$

$$ \text{Sum} = 3.333 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{/N} $$

Right-hand side:

$$ -(\alpha_s + \alpha_b) \Delta T = -(12 + 19) \times 10^{-6} \times 60 = -1.86 \times 10^{-3} $$

Therefore:

$$ P = \frac{-1.86 \times 10^{-3}}{3.333 \times 10^{-8}} = -5.58 \times 10^4\,\text{N} = -55.8\,\text{kN} $$

The negative sign indicates the internal force is **compressive** in both rods (consistent with $\Delta T > 0$ and the bars being held between rigid walls in series).

### Step 4 — Compute Stresses

$$ \sigma_s = \frac{P}{A_s} = \frac{-55.8 \times 10^3}{600} = -93\,\text{MPa (compressive)} $$

$$ \sigma_b = \frac{P}{A_b} = \frac{-55.8 \times 10^3}{400} = -139.5\,\text{MPa (compressive)} $$

### Step 5 — Verify
Free thermal expansion of steel rod alone: $L \alpha_s \Delta T = L \times 12 \times 10^{-6} \times 60 = 7.2 \times 10^{-4} L$ (tensile strain)
Mechanical strain in steel rod: $\sigma_s / E_s = -93 \times 10^3 / (200 \times 10^3) = -4.65 \times 10^{-4}$ (compressive)
Total strain in steel: $-4.65 \times 10^{-4} + 7.2 \times 10^{-4} = 2.55 \times 10^{-4}$

Free thermal expansion of brass rod alone: $L \times 19 \times 10^{-6} \times 60 = 1.14 \times 10^{-3} L$
Mechanical strain in brass: $-139.5 \times 10^3 / (100 \times 10^3) = -1.395 \times 10^{-3}$
Total strain in brass: $-1.395 \times 10^{-3} + 1.14 \times 10^{-3} = -2.55 \times 10^{-4}$

Sum of total strains = $2.55 \times 10^{-4} + (-2.55 \times 10^{-4}) = 0$ ✓ (constraint satisfied)

**Answer**: $\sigma_s = -93\,\text{MPa}$, $\sigma_b = -139.5\,\text{MPa}$, wall reaction $R = 55.8\,\text{kN}$ (inward on the walls).

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — How a Temperature-Stress Problem Is Solved

The figure below maps the **logical flow** that a KTU examiner expects a student to follow when solving any temperature-stress problem. It is intentionally drawn as a block-flow diagram rather than a physical drawing, because the underlying mathematical logic is what earns marks.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PROBLEM START Identify geometry and restraint] --> B[Is the bar single material or composite]
    B -->|Single bar| C[Apply boundary condition Total elongation equals zero if rigid]
    B -->|Composite bars| D[Identify configuration Parallel or Series]
    D -->|Parallel side by side| E[Equilibrium Force balance sigma1 A1 equals sigma2 A2]
    D -->|Series end to end| F[Equilibrium Common axial force P1 equals P2]
    C --> G[Use master strain equation sigma by E plus alpha delta T equals epsilon total]
    E --> H[Use compatibility Equal total elongations delta1 equals delta2]
    F --> H
    G --> I[Solve for sigma and delta T]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Apply sign convention Compressive if heated Tensile if cooled]
    J --> K[Compute forces Stresses and wall reactions]
    K --> L[Sanity check Force balance and zero total deformation]
    L --> M[PROBLEM END Write final answer with units]
```

## 4.2 Schematic — Composite Bar (Parallel) Between Rigid Walls

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    WL[RIGID WALL LEFT]:::wall -->|clamp| BLK1[END BLOCK]
    BLK1 -->|Bar 1 Material 1 Area A1| MID[MIDDLE BLOCK]
    MID -->|Bar 2 Material 2 Area A2| BLK2[END BLOCK]
    BLK2 -->|clamp| WR[RIGID WALL RIGHT]:::wall
    MID -.->|dT temperature rise| MID
    classDef wall fill:#cbd5e1,stroke:#1e293b,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Reading the diagram**: The two bars share the same length $L$ between the end-blocks. The middle block ensures the two bars experience **equal total elongation** (compatibility). The two walls together enforce **zero net expansion** of the assembly.

## 4.3 Schematic — Single Bar in Series Between Rigid Walls

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W1[RIGID WALL LEFT]:::wall -->|clamp| E1[END 1]
    E1 -->|Bar length L area A| E2[END 2]
    E2 -->|clamp| W2[RIGID WALL RIGHT]:::wall
    E1 -.->|delta T total equals zero| E2
    classDef wall fill:#cbd5e1,stroke:#1e293b,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.4 Real-World Mapping — Naval Architecture Contexts

| Naval Component | Equivalent Schematic | Typical $\Delta T$ | Why Constraint Matters |
|---|---|---|---|
| Propeller shaft in stern tube | Series bar between bearings | $30\text{–}80\,^\circ\text{C}$ (hot bearing) | Shaft must not seize; thermal growth accommodated by clearances |
| Hull-deck plating in tropical waters | Composite parallel plates | $20\text{–}40\,^\circ\text{C}$ | Welded joints carry thermal load if expansion is restrained |
| Bimetallic cooling coil (steel + copper) | Parallel composite | $50\text{–}90\,^\circ\text{C}$ | Differential expansion produces sustained stress — design for it |
| Riveted hull seam | Series elastic restraints | cyclic $-10$ to $+40\,^\circ\text{C}$ | Thermal fatigue — origin of classic rivet failures |

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define thermal stress. Under what condition is the thermal stress in a uniform bar equal to zero, even when the bar is subjected to a temperature change?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** Thermal stress is the internal stress developed in a body when a change in temperature is accompanied by a constraint that prevents free thermal expansion or contraction of the body. Mathematically, for a fully restrained homogeneous bar, $\sigma_T = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$.
>
> **Condition for zero thermal stress (1 mark):** When the bar is **completely free** to expand or contract, no restraint exists, and hence $\sigma_T = 0$. The bar simply undergoes a free thermal strain $\varepsilon_T = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ with no internal force.

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Write the expression for the thermal stress developed in a composite bar system consisting of two different materials rigidly connected in parallel and constrained between two rigid walls, when the temperature rises by $\Delta T$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For two materials (1 and 2) with areas $A_1, A_2$, Young's moduli $E_1, E_2$, and coefficients of thermal expansion $\alpha_1, \alpha_2$:

$$ \sigma_1 = \frac{E_1 \, E_2 \, A_2 \, (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \, \Delta T}{E_1 A_1 + E_2 A_2} \quad ; \quad \sigma_2 = \frac{E_1 \, E_2 \, A_1 \, (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \, \Delta T}{E_1 A_1 + E_2 A_2} $$

**Sign Convention (1 mark):** The material with the **higher** $\alpha$ is in **compression**; the material with the **lower** $\alpha$ is in **tension**. Equilibrium requires $\sigma_1 A_1 = \sigma_2 A_2$ in magnitude.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper]`

**(a)** A steel bar of length $L = 1.2\,\text{m}$, cross-sectional area $A = 900\,\text{mm}^2$, Young's modulus $E = 200\,\text{GPa}$, and coefficient of thermal expansion $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6}\,/^\circ\text{C}$ is rigidly fixed between two walls at $30\,^\circ\text{C}$. If the temperature is raised uniformly to $90\,^\circ\text{C}$, determine:
   (i) the thermal stress developed,
   (ii) the axial force exerted on each wall, and
   (iii) the free thermal expansion that would have occurred if the bar were unconstrained. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Two bars, one of steel ($E_s = 200\,\text{GPa}$, $\alpha_s = 12 \times 10^{-6}\,/^\circ\text{C}$, $A_s = 1200\,\text{mm}^2$) and one of brass ($E_b = 105\,\text{GPa}$, $\alpha_b = 19 \times 10^{-6}\,/^\circ\text{C}$, $A_b = 800\,\text{mm}^2$), are placed **in parallel** between two rigid walls at the same temperature. The temperature of the system is raised by $\Delta T = 80\,^\circ\text{C}$. Determine the stresses developed in each bar and the reaction force at each wall. **[7 marks]**

### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Single Restrained Steel Bar**

Given: $L = 1.2\,\text{m} = 1200\,\text{mm}$, $A = 900\,\text{mm}^2$, $E = 200 \times 10^3\,\text{N/mm}^2$, $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6}\,/^\circ\text{C}$, $\Delta T = 90 - 30 = 60\,^\circ\text{C}$.

**(i) Thermal Stress:**

$$ \sigma_T = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T = (200 \times 10^3) \times (12 \times 10^{-6}) \times 60 $$

$$ \sigma_T = 144\,\text{N/mm}^2 = 144\,\text{MPa (compressive)} \quad \textbf{[3 marks]} $$

**(ii) Axial Force on Wall:**

$$ P_T = \sigma_T \cdot A = 144 \times 900 = 129{,}600\,\text{N} = 129.6\,\text{kN} $$

The force is directed **inward** on each wall. $\quad \textbf{[2 marks]}$

**(iii) Free Thermal Expansion (if unrestrained):**

$$ \delta_T = L \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T = 1200 \times 12 \times 10^{-6} \times 60 = 0.864\,\text{mm} $$

$$ \boxed{\delta_T = 0.864\,\text{mm}} \quad \textbf{[2 marks]} $$

---

**Part (b) — Composite Bars in Parallel**

Given: $\alpha_s = 12 \times 10^{-6}$, $\alpha_b = 19 \times 10^{-6}$. Since $\alpha_b > \alpha_s$, **brass wants to expand more** and will be held back → brass is in **compression**; steel is in **tension**.

Use the master formula with material 1 = brass, material 2 = steel:

$$ \sigma_b = \frac{E_b E_s A_s (\alpha_b - \alpha_s) \Delta T}{E_b A_b + E_s A_s} $$

Compute the denominator (with $E$ in N/mm², $A$ in mm²):

$$ E_b A_b = 105 \times 10^3 \times 800 = 8.4 \times 10^7\,\text{N} $$

$$ E_s A_s = 200 \times 10^3 \times 1200 = 2.4 \times 10^8\,\text{N} $$

$$ \text{Sum} = 8.4 \times 10^7 + 2.4 \times 10^8 = 3.24 \times 10^8\,\text{N} $$

Compute the numerator:

$$ E_b E_s A_s (\alpha_b - \alpha_s) \Delta T = (105 \times 10^3)(200 \times 10^3)(1200)(7 \times 10^{-6})(80) $$

$$ = (2.1 \times 10^{10})(1200)(5.6 \times 10^{-4}) = (2.52 \times 10^{13})(5.6 \times 10^{-4}) $$

$$ = 1.4112 \times 10^{10}\,\text{N·mm/°C × °C = N·mm (with units absorbed)} $$

More carefully, in consistent units:

$$ \sigma_b = \frac{(105 \times 10^3)(200 \times 10^3)(1200)(7 \times 10^{-6})(80)}{(105 \times 10^3)(800) + (200 \times 10^3)(1200)} $$

$$ = \frac{(2.1 \times 10^{10})(1200)(5.6 \times 10^{-4})}{8.4 \times 10^7 + 2.4 \times 10^8} = \frac{1.4112 \times 10^{10}}{3.24 \times 10^8} $$

$$ \sigma_b \approx 43.55\,\text{MPa (compressive in brass)} \quad \textbf{[3 marks]} $$

$$ \sigma_s = \frac{E_b E_s A_b (\alpha_b - \alpha_s) \Delta T}{E_b A_b + E_s A_s} $$

Numerator (with $A_b$ instead of $A_s$):

$$ = \frac{(105 \times 10^3)(200 \times 10^3)(800)(5.6 \times 10^{-4})}{3.24 \times 10^8} $$

$$ = \frac{(2.1 \times 10^{10})(800)(5.6 \times 10^{-4})}{3.24 \times 10^8} = \frac{9.408 \times 10^9}{3.24 \times 10^8} $$

$$ \sigma_s \approx 29.04\,\text{MPa (tensile in steel)} \quad \textbf{[2 marks]} $$

**Verification — Equilibrium Check:**

$$ \sigma_b A_b = 43.55 \times 800 = 34{,}840\,\text{N} $$

$$ \sigma_s A_s = 29.04 \times 1200 = 34{,}848\,\text{N} $$

Equal (within rounding) ✓. Reaction at each wall = **34.84 kN**. $\quad \textbf{[2 marks]} $$

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper]`

**(a)** Derive, from first principles, the expression for the thermal stress developed in a homogeneous prismatic bar rigidly fixed between two walls when the temperature is raised by $\Delta T$. State clearly the master strain equation used. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A steel tube of outer diameter $60\,\text{mm}$ and inner diameter $40\,\text{mm}$ encases a solid brass rod of diameter $40\,\text{mm}$, and both are rigidly bonded at the ends. If the temperature of the composite assembly is raised by $\Delta T = 100\,^\circ\text{C}$, find the stresses developed in the steel tube and the brass core. Use $E_s = 200\,\text{GPa}$, $E_b = 100\,\text{GPa}$, $\alpha_s = 12 \times 10^{-6}\,/^\circ\text{C}$, $\alpha_b = 19 \times 10^{-6}\,/^\circ\text{C}$. **[7 marks]**

### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Derivation**

See **Derivation I in Section 3.1** above. The complete derivation there should be reproduced:

1. Write $\delta_{\text{total}} = \delta_{\text{mech}} + \delta_{\text{thermal}} = 0$.
2. Substitute $\delta_{\text{mech}} = PL / (AE) = \sigma L / E$ and $\delta_{\text{thermal}} = L \alpha \Delta T$.
3. Simplify to obtain $\sigma_T = -E \alpha \Delta T$ (compression for heating).
4. State the master strain equation: $\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = \sigma / E + \alpha \Delta T$.

$\quad \textbf{[7 marks]}$ (Full credit awarded for stating boundary condition, writing both parts, simplifying, and stating master equation.)

---

**Part (b) — Steel Tube Encasing Brass Rod**

**Step 1 — Compute Areas**

$$ A_b = \frac{\pi}{4}(40)^2 = 400\pi \approx 1256.64\,\text{mm}^2 $$

$$ A_s = \frac{\pi}{4}\left[(60)^2 - (40)^2\right] = \frac{\pi}{4}(3600 - 1600) = 500\pi \approx 1570.80\,\text{mm}^2 $$

**Step 2 — Identify the Configuration**
This is **parallel** (both materials share the same length $L$ and must elongate by the same amount). Brass has higher $\alpha$ → brass is in compression, steel is in tension.

**Step 3 — Apply Master Formulas** (Material 1 = brass, Material 2 = steel):

$$ \sigma_b = \frac{E_b E_s A_s (\alpha_b - \alpha_s) \Delta T}{E_b A_b + E_s A_s} $$

Compute denominator in N:

$$ E_b A_b = (100 \times 10^3)(400\pi) = 4\pi \times 10^7 \approx 1.2566 \times 10^8\,\text{N} $$

$$ E_s A_s = (200 \times 10^3)(500\pi) = 1\pi \times 10^8 \approx 3.1416 \times 10^8\,\text{N} $$

$$ \text{Sum} = 5\pi \times 10^7 \approx 4.3982 \times 10^8\,\text{N} $$

Compute numerator:

$$ E_b E_s A_s (\alpha_b - \alpha_s) \Delta T = (100 \times 10^3)(200 \times 10^3)(500\pi)(7 \times 10^{-6})(100) $$

$$ = (2 \times 10^{10})(500\pi)(7 \times 10^{-4}) = (2 \times 10^{10})(3.5 \times 10^{-1}\pi) = 7\pi \times 10^9\,\text{N} $$

$$ \sigma_b = \frac{7\pi \times 10^9}{5\pi \times 10^7} = \frac{7 \times 10^9}{5 \times 10^7} = 140\,\text{N/mm}^2 $$

$$ \boxed{\sigma_b = 140\,\text{MPa (compressive)}} \quad \textbf{[3 marks]} $$

**Step 4 — Stress in Steel Tube**

$$ \sigma_s = \frac{E_b E_s A_b (\alpha_b - \alpha_s) \Delta T}{E_b A_b + E_s A_s} $$

$$ = \frac{(100 \times 10^3)(200 \times 10^3)(400\pi)(7 \times 10^{-6})(100)}{5\pi \times 10^7} $$

$$ = \frac{(2 \times 10^{10})(400\pi)(7 \times 10^{-4})}{5\pi \times 10^7} = \frac{5.6\pi \times 10^9}{5\pi \times 10^7} $$

$$ = \frac{5.6 \times 10^9}{5 \times 10^7} = 112\,\text{N/mm}^2 $$

$$ \boxed{\sigma_s = 112\,\text{MPa (tensile)}} \quad \textbf{[3 marks]} $$

**Step 5 — Verification**

$$ \sigma_b A_b = 140 \times 400\pi = 56{,}000\pi\,\text{N} $$

$$ \sigma_s A_s = 112 \times 500\pi = 56{,}000\pi\,\text{N} $$

Equal ✓. $\quad \textbf{[1 mark]} $$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Temperature Stress Questions (Module 1)**
> 1. **Forgetting the sign convention** — many students compute only the *magnitude* of $\sigma_T$ without specifying whether the bar is in tension or compression. KTU examiners expect an explicit sign or a verbal statement. **(Loses 1 mark per sub-question)**
> 2. **Confusing parallel vs series** — when two bars are *side-by-side* between end blocks, they share **displacement** (parallel). When they are *end-to-end*, they share **force** (series). Mixing these up is the most common conceptual error. **(Loses 2–4 marks)**
> 3. **Inconsistent units** — $E$ in MPa vs N/mm² vs GPa. Always normalize to N/mm² (= MPa) before computation. **(Loses 1–2 marks)**
> 4. **Skipping the equilibrium verification** — KTU examiners actively look for a check like $\sigma_1 A_1 = \sigma_2 A_2$. Including it can recover 1 mark even if the final numerical answer has a small arithmetic slip. **(Saves up to 1 mark)**
> 5. **Not stating the master strain equation** — when asked to "derive," students jump straight to the formula. KTU's valuation key explicitly awards a mark for writing $\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = \sigma / E + \alpha \Delta T$ as the starting point. **(Loses 1 mark)**
> 6. **Missing "free vs restrained" distinction** — for the 3-mark question, examiners expect you to *state* the condition under which stress is zero (free bar). Vague answers lose marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Thermal strain** is $\varepsilon_T = \alpha \Delta T$; it occurs *whether or not* the bar is restrained.
- **Thermal stress** is *zero* when the bar is **free**, and is $\sigma_T = E \alpha \Delta T$ when **fully restrained**. Real structures lie in between.
- The **master equation** $\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = \sigma / E + \alpha \Delta T$ is the single most important relationship in this topic — every problem reduces to applying it under the appropriate boundary condition.
- For **composite bars in parallel**: use $\sigma_1 A_1 = \sigma_2 A_2$ (equilibrium) and **equal total elongations** (compatibility).
- For **composite bars in series**: the **same axial force** $P$ acts in both bars, and **total elongation of the assembly = 0** (rigid walls).
- The material with the **higher** $\alpha$ is always in **compression** when $\Delta T > 0$; the one with lower $\alpha$ is in **tension**.
- In **naval architecture**: propeller shafts (series restraint), hull-deck plating (parallel restraint), and bimetallic cooling coils (parallel composite) are the three most common real-world scenarios.
- **Always verify** $\sigma_1 A_1 = \sigma_2 A_2$ before writing your final answer — it is the KTU examiner's "free pass" check.
- **Temperature change** $\Delta T$ is in **°C or K** — both work because they share the same scale increment.
- Numerical values to memorize for typical exam questions: $E_{\text{steel}} = 200\,\text{GPa}$, $\alpha_{\text{steel}} = 12 \times 10^{-6}/°\text{C}$, $E_{\text{brass}} = 100\,\text{GPa}$, $\alpha_{\text{brass}} = 19 \times 10^{-6}/°\text{C}$, $E_{\text{copper}} = 110\,\text{GPa}$, $\alpha_{\text{copper}} = 17 \times 10^{-6}/°\text{C}$.
- A thermal stress of just $\sigma_T = 120\,\text{MPa}$ can be reached in a fully restrained mild-steel bar with $\Delta T \approx 50\,°\text{C}$ — this is **close to yield**, which is why expansion joints are non-negotiable in marine design.

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST303 - SOLID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Poisson's Ratio

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Poisson's Ratio (ν or μ)** is defined as the negative of the ratio of **transverse (lateral) strain** to the **axial (longitudinal) strain** in a material subjected to a uniaxial tensile or compressive load within the elastic limit.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{lateral}}{\varepsilon_{longitudinal}} = -\frac{\varepsilon_{transverse}}{\varepsilon_{axial}}$$

The **negative sign** is conventionally introduced to make Poisson's ratio a **positive quantity**, since most materials contract laterally when stretched axially (and vice versa). For a homogeneous, isotropic material, the theoretical bounds of Poisson's ratio are:

$$-1 \leq \nu \leq 0.5$$

> [!NOTE]
> In naval structural engineering, the range **0.25 ≤ ν ≤ 0.35** is most common for shipbuilding steels and marine aluminium alloys.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Real-World Intuition

Imagine stretching a **rubber band** between your two hands. As you pull it apart (axial tension), you will observe that the rubber band becomes **noticeably thinner** in the middle. This "necking down" effect is what Poisson's ratio quantifies — it describes how a material "gives in" sideways when pulled along its length.

**Marine Engineering Analogy:**
Consider a **mooring rope** under tension on a ship. When the ship's anchor chain is pulled taut by wave action, the rope not only elongates but its diameter decreases. The ratio of this **diameter reduction** to the **length increase** is governed by the rope material's Poisson's ratio. For **synthetic fibre ropes** (like polyester or nylon used in modern mooring systems), this value is much higher than for steel wire ropes.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Metrics

- **Symbol:** ν (Greek letter "nu") or μ
- **Dimensional Nature:** **Dimensionless** (a pure ratio)
- **Units:** **None** (unitless scalar quantity)
- **Typical Range for Engineering Materials:** **0.0 to 0.5**

> [!TIP]
> Remember the gold standard values: **Steel ≈ 0.30**, **Aluminium ≈ 0.33**, **Concrete ≈ 0.20**, **Rubber ≈ 0.49–0.50**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical & Mathematical Foundations

### 2.1.1 The Sign Convention Logic

Let us unpack the underlying physics step by step:

1. **Step 1 — Apply uniaxial tensile load σ along the X-axis.**
   The axial strain along X is positive: εₓ > 0 (elongation).

2. **Step 2 — Observe the transverse response.**
   Without any applied load in Y or Z directions, the material contracts sideways: εᵧ < 0, ε_z < 0.

3. **Step 3 — Compute the ratio.**
   Without the negative sign: ν = εᵧ/εₓ = (negative)/(positive) = negative. But experiments universally report a **positive** value. Hence, the negative sign is introduced by convention:

   $$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_y}{\varepsilon_x}$$

### 2.1.2 Three-Dimensional Generalisation

For an isotropic, homogeneous material loaded uniaxially along X, the three normal strains are:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{\sigma_x}{E}, \quad \varepsilon_y = \varepsilon_z = -\nu \cdot \frac{\sigma_x}{E}$$

This shows that **two equal transverse strains** appear when loading is purely uniaxial (due to isotropy).

### 2.1.3 Volumetric Strain (Dilatation)

When a material is under triaxial stress, the volumetric strain θ is:

$$\theta = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z = \frac{1-2\nu}{E}(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When **ν = 0.5**, the material is **incompressible** (θ = 0). This is the theoretical maximum, approached by rubber and ideal fluids.

## 2.2 Relationships Between Elastic Constants

Poisson's ratio is the **bridge** connecting the four fundamental elastic constants:
- **E** — Young's Modulus (Modulus of Elasticity)
- **G** — Shear Modulus (Modulus of Rigidity)
- **K** — Bulk Modulus
- **ν** — Poisson's Ratio

Any two of these constants can determine the other two for an isotropic material.

## 2.3 ⚡ KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | $\nu = -\dfrac{\varepsilon_{lateral}}{\varepsilon_{longitudinal}}$ | Core definition of Poisson's ratio |
| 2 | $\varepsilon_{lateral} = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon_{axial}$ | Compute transverse strain from axial strain |
| 3 | $E = 2G(1+\nu)$ | Young's Modulus in terms of G and ν |
| 4 | $E = 3K(1-2\nu)$ | Young's Modulus in terms of K and ν |
| 5 | $G = \dfrac{3K(1-2\nu)}{2(1+\nu)}$ | Shear Modulus in terms of K and ν |
| 6 | $\nu = \dfrac{E}{2G} - 1$ | Poisson's ratio in terms of E and G |
| 7 | $\nu = \dfrac{3K - E}{6K}$ | Poisson's ratio in terms of E and K |
| 8 | $\theta = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$ | Volumetric strain definition |
| 9 | $\theta = \dfrac{1-2\nu}{E}(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z)$ | Volumetric strain under triaxial stress |
| 10 | $\dfrac{\sigma_x}{E} - \dfrac{\nu \sigma_y}{E} - \dfrac{\nu \sigma_z}{E} = \varepsilon_x$ | Generalised Hooke's Law (X-direction) |
| 11 | $\mu = \dfrac{1}{\nu}$ (NOT standard) | Recip relationship reminder (commonly confused) |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU board exams, formulas **(3), (4), (6), and (7)** are the **most frequently asked** derivation-based questions.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility in Naval Architecture

- **Hull Plate Selection:** Naval architects use Poisson's ratio to predict **transverse contraction** in plate panels subjected to bending moments from wave loads.
- **Composite Sandwich Panels:** Modern ship superstructures use composite cores; mismatched Poisson's ratios between core and skin cause **interfacial shear stresses** — critical in fire-resistant marine composites.
- **Pressure Hulls (Submarines):** For deep-diving submersibles, Poisson's ratio governs **radial expansion** under hydrostatic pressure, affecting sealing design.
- **Mooring & Towing Cables:** The lateral contraction of synthetic ropes reduces cross-sectional area, affecting **tensile capacity** under dynamic loading.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Relationship Between E, G, and ν

### Given:
A cylindrical bar of diameter *d* and length *L* subjected to an axial tensile force *P*.

### Step 1 — Axial Strain

Under axial load *P*, the axial stress is:

$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A}$$

where $A = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}$. The corresponding axial strain is:

$$\varepsilon_{axial} = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{P}{AE}$$

### Step 2 — Lateral Strain

By definition of Poisson's ratio:

$$\varepsilon_{lateral} = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon_{axial} = -\nu \cdot \frac{P}{AE}$$

### Step 3 — Change in Diameter

The change in diameter is:

$$\delta d = \varepsilon_{lateral} \cdot d = -\nu \cdot \frac{P d}{AE}$$

### Step 4 — Shear Stress Consideration

Now, consider a **45° inclined plane** within the same bar (a classic Mohr's circle argument). On this plane, the shear stress is:

$$\tau = \frac{\sigma}{2} = \frac{P}{2A}$$

### Step 5 — Shear Strain by Geometry

The shear strain γ on this plane is the **engineering shear strain**, which by simple geometry of the 45° element equals:

$$\gamma = \varepsilon_{axial} - \varepsilon_{lateral}$$

Substituting the values from Steps 1 and 2:

$$\gamma = \frac{P}{AE} - \left(-\nu \cdot \frac{P}{AE}\right) = \frac{P}{AE}(1 + \nu)$$

### Step 6 — Apply Shear Modulus Definition

By definition: $\tau = G \cdot \gamma$. Substituting:

$$\frac{P}{2A} = G \cdot \frac{P}{AE}(1 + \nu)$$

### Step 7 — Simplify

Cancel P and A from both sides:

$$\frac{1}{2} = \frac{G}{E}(1 + \nu)$$

Rearranging for E:

$$\boxed{E = 2G(1 + \nu)}$$

> [!TIP]
> This derivation is a **KTU favourite**. In the 2022 and 2024 KTU exams, similar 14-mark derivations were asked from this exact relationship.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Relationship Between E, K, and ν

### Step 1 — Hydrostatic Stress

Consider the same element subjected to equal triaxial compressive stress σ on all faces (hydrostatic compression). The hydrostatic pressure is:

$$p = -\sigma$$

### Step 2 — Volumetric Strain (Using Formula 9)

Since $\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z = 3\sigma$:

$$\theta = \frac{1-2\nu}{E}(3\sigma) = \frac{3(1-2\nu)\sigma}{E}$$

### Step 3 — Definition of Bulk Modulus

By definition:

$$K = -\frac{p}{\theta} = -\frac{\sigma}{\theta}$$

(Note: p = -σ for hydrostatic compression.)

### Step 4 — Substitute

$$K = \frac{\sigma}{\frac{3(1-2\nu)\sigma}{E}} = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Volumetric Strain of a Stretched Bar

### Step 1 — Setup

A bar of original volume V = L × A is stretched axially. New dimensions:
- Length: $L' = L(1 + \varepsilon_x)$
- Area: $A' = A(1 + \varepsilon_y)(1 + \varepsilon_z)$

### Step 2 — Final Volume

$$V' = L' A' = LA(1 + \varepsilon_x)(1 + \varepsilon_y)(1 + \varepsilon_z)$$

### Step 3 — Expansion (Ignoring Higher-Order Infinitesimals)

$$V' \approx V[1 + \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z]$$

### Step 4 — Volumetric Strain Definition

$$\theta = \frac{V' - V}{V} = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$$

For uniaxial loading: $\varepsilon_y = \varepsilon_z = -\nu \varepsilon_x$:

$$\theta = \varepsilon_x - 2\nu \varepsilon_x = \varepsilon_x(1 - 2\nu)$$

### Step 5 — Substitute Hooke's Law

$$\boxed{\theta = \frac{\sigma_x}{E}(1 - 2\nu)}$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A steel rod of 20 mm diameter and 200 mm length is subjected to an axial tensile load of 50 kN. Given E = 200 GPa and ν = 0.3, find:
(a) The change in length
(b) The change in diameter
(c) The change in volume

### Solution:

**Step (a):** Axial stress:
$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{50 \times 10^3}{\frac{\pi}{4}(0.020)^2} = \frac{50000}{3.1416 \times 10^{-4}} = 159.15 \text{ MPa}$$

Axial strain:
$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{159.15 \times 10^6}{200 \times 10^9} = 7.957 \times 10^{-4}$$

Change in length:
$$\delta L = \varepsilon_x \cdot L = 7.957 \times 10^{-4} \times 200 = 0.1591 \text{ mm}$$

**Step (b):** Lateral strain:
$$\varepsilon_y = -\nu \varepsilon_x = -0.3 \times 7.957 \times 10^{-4} = -2.387 \times 10^{-4}$$

Change in diameter:
$$\delta d = \varepsilon_y \cdot d = -2.387 \times 10^{-4} \times 20 = -0.00477 \text{ mm (decrease)}$$

**Step (c):** Volumetric strain:
$$\theta = \varepsilon_x(1 - 2\nu) = 7.957 \times 10^{-4} \times (1 - 0.6) = 7.957 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.4 = 3.183 \times 10^{-4}$$

Change in volume:
$$\delta V = \theta \cdot V = 3.183 \times 10^{-4} \times \left(\frac{\pi}{4}(20)^2 \times 200\right) = 3.183 \times 10^{-4} \times 62831.85 \approx 20.0 \text{ mm}^3$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Poisson Effect Stress Flow

The diagram below maps the **causal pathway** of the Poisson effect — from external load to lateral deformation — through a sequence of mechanical state transitions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["APPLIED LOAD: Axial Tension P"] --> B["AXIAL STRESS sigma_x = P over A"]
    B --> C["AXIAL STRAIN epsilon_x = sigma_x over E"]
    C --> D["LATERAL RESPONSE: Material contracts sideways"]
    D --> E["LATERAL STRAIN epsilon_y = epsilon_z = negative nu times epsilon_x"]
    E --> F["GEOMETRIC CHANGE: Diameter delta d = epsilon_y times d"]
    F --> G["VOLUMETRIC RESPONSE: Volume changes by theta times V"]
    G --> H{"MATERIAL CLASS CHECK"}
    H -->|"Steel nu=0.30"| I["MODERATE LATERAL CONTRACTION"]
    H -->|"Aluminium nu=0.33"| J["SLIGHTLY HIGHER CONTRACTION"]
    H -->|"Rubber nu=0.49"| K["NEAR INCOMPRESSIBLE VOLUME"]
    H -->|"Cork nu approx 0"| L["NEAR ZERO LATERAL STRAIN"]
    I --> M["OUTPUT: Combined Strain State"]
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N["USED IN NAVAL: Hull plate stress, mooring rope design"]
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow: Elastic Constant Inter-Relationships

This flowchart shows how Poisson's ratio acts as the **linking node** between E, G, and K — a common KTU derivation path.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["YOUNGS MODULUS E"] -->|"Equation 3: E = 2G(1+nu)"| B["SHEAR MODULUS G"]
    A -->|"Equation 4: E = 3K(1-2nu)"| C["BULK MODULUS K"]
    B -->|"Equation 6: nu = E over 2G minus 1"| D["POISSONS RATIO nu"]
    C -->|"Equation 7: nu = 3K-E over 6K"| D
    D -->|"Bridge Constant"| A
```

## 4.3 Schematic: 3D Stress Element Under Uniaxial Load

The following block diagram represents a **3D cubic element** subjected to uniaxial tension, illustrating the strain directions:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ELEMENT["3D CUBIC ELEMENT UNDER TENSION"]
        direction TB
        X["X-AXIS: AXIAL DIRECTION - epsilon_x POSITIVE - ELONGATION"]
        Y["Y-AXIS: LATERAL DIRECTION - epsilon_y NEGATIVE - CONTRACTION"]
        Z["Z-AXIS: LATERAL DIRECTION - epsilon_z NEGATIVE - CONTRACTION"]
    end
    X -->|"Coupled via nu"| Y
    X -->|"Coupled via nu"| Z
    Y -->|"Isotropy: equal strain"| Z
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Poisson's ratio. State its limits. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
Poisson's ratio (ν) is defined as the negative ratio of lateral strain to longitudinal strain in a material under uniaxial loading:

$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{lateral}}{\varepsilon_{longitudinal}}$$

It is a **dimensionless** quantity. For stable, isotropic, linear elastic materials, the theoretical limits are:

$$-1 \leq \nu \leq 0.5$$

In practice, most engineering materials (metals) fall in the range **0.25 ≤ ν ≤ 0.35**.

---

### Question 2: A material has Young's modulus 210 GPa and modulus of rigidity 84 GPa. Find its Poisson's ratio. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Using formula (6):

$$\nu = \frac{E}{2G} - 1 = \frac{210}{2 \times 84} - 1 = \frac{210}{168} - 1 = 1.25 - 1 = 0.25$$

**Poisson's ratio = 0.25**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### 🅰️ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Derive the relationship E = 2G(1 + ν) between Young's modulus, modulus of rigidity, and Poisson's ratio. (7 Marks)**

**Solution:**

[Follow the complete derivation from Section 3.1, Steps 1–7 above. Briefly outline:
1. Apply axial tension on a cylindrical bar — derive σ = P/A.
2. Axial strain: ε_axial = σ/E = P/(AE).
3. Lateral strain by Poisson's ratio: ε_lateral = -νP/(AE).
4. The change in diameter: δd = -νPd/(AE).
5. Consider a 45° inclined plane — shear stress τ = σ/2 = P/(2A).
6. Shear strain γ = ε_axial − ε_lateral = (P/AE)(1+ν).
7. Apply τ = Gγ → P/(2A) = G·(P/AE)(1+ν).
8. Simplify → E = 2G(1+ν).]

**(b) A metallic bar of 25 mm diameter and 300 mm length is subjected to an axial pull of 80 kN. If E = 200 GPa and ν = 0.3, determine the (i) change in length, (ii) change in diameter, and (iii) change in volume. (7 Marks)**

**Solution:**

**Given:** d = 25 mm, L = 300 mm, P = 80 kN = 80 × 10³ N, E = 200 × 10³ N/mm², ν = 0.3

**Step 1:** Cross-sectional area:

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(25)^2 = 490.87 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2:** Axial stress:

$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{80 \times 10^3}{490.87} = 162.97 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 3 (i):** Axial strain and change in length:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{162.97}{200 \times 10^3} = 8.149 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\delta L = \varepsilon_x \times L = 8.149 \times 10^{-4} \times 300 = 0.2445 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 (ii):** Lateral strain and change in diameter:

$$\varepsilon_y = -\nu \varepsilon_x = -0.3 \times 8.149 \times 10^{-4} = -2.445 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\delta d = \varepsilon_y \times d = -2.445 \times 10^{-4} \times 25 = -0.00611 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 (iii):** Original volume:

$$V = \frac{\pi}{4}(25)^2 \times 300 = 147262.15 \text{ mm}^3$$

Volumetric strain:

$$\theta = \varepsilon_x(1 - 2\nu) = 8.149 \times 10^{-4} \times (1 - 0.6) = 3.259 \times 10^{-4}$$

Change in volume:

$$\delta V = \theta \times V = 3.259 \times 10^{-4} \times 147262.15 = 48.0 \text{ mm}^3$$

---

### 🅱️ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Define the four elastic constants E, G, K, and ν. Derive the relationship E = 3K(1 − 2ν). (7 Marks)**

**Solution:**

**Definitions (2 marks):**
- **E** (Young's Modulus): Ratio of longitudinal stress to longitudinal strain.
- **G** (Shear Modulus): Ratio of shear stress to shear strain.
- **K** (Bulk Modulus): Ratio of direct stress to volumetric strain under hydrostatic pressure.
- **ν** (Poisson's Ratio): Negative ratio of lateral strain to longitudinal strain.

**Derivation (5 marks):** [Follow Section 3.2 derivation steps:
1. Apply hydrostatic stress σ on all faces.
2. Volumetric strain using formula: θ = 3(1−2ν)σ/E.
3. K = -p/θ = σ/θ (since p = -σ).
4. Substitute and simplify: K = E / [3(1−2ν)].
5. Rearrange: E = 3K(1−2ν).]

**(b) For a given material, E = 210 GPa and ν = 0.28. Determine G and K. Also find the volumetric strain when the material is subjected to a hydrostatic pressure of 150 MPa. (7 Marks)**

**Solution:**

**Given:** E = 210 × 10³ N/mm², ν = 0.28, p = 150 N/mm²

**Step 1:** Shear modulus:

$$G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)} = \frac{210 \times 10^3}{2(1+0.28)} = \frac{210 \times 10^3}{2.56} = 82.03 \times 10^3 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 82.03 \text{ GPa}$$

**Step 2:** Bulk modulus:

$$K = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)} = \frac{210 \times 10^3}{3(1-0.56)} = \frac{210 \times 10^3}{3 \times 0.44} = \frac{210 \times 10^3}{1.32} = 159.09 \times 10^3 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 159.09 \text{ GPa}$$

**Step 3:** Volumetric strain:

$$\theta = \frac{-p}{K} = \frac{-150}{159.09 \times 10^3} = -9.43 \times 10^{-4}$$

The negative sign indicates volume reduction (compression).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Trap:** Students often forget the negative sign in Poisson's ratio definition and write ν = ε_lateral/ε_axial, leading to a negative value. **Always** carry the negative sign explicitly.
> 2. **Formula Confusion:** Mixing up E = 2G(1+ν) with E = 3K(1−2ν) is a **common error**. Remember: G is associated with shape distortion (1+ν), K with volume change (1−2ν).
> 3. **Units:** E, G, K must be in **consistent units** (either all in GPa or all in N/mm²). Mixing them up causes numerical errors.
> 4. **Volumetric Strain Sign:** Under hydrostatic **compression**, θ is **negative** (volume decreases). Under hydrostatic **tension**, θ is **positive**. Students often drop the sign.
> 5. **In Part B derivations, do not skip the geometric reasoning** behind γ = ε_axial − ε_lateral. The 45° Mohr's circle argument is a frequently evaluated step in KTU valuation.

---

## 5.3 📝 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ Poisson's ratio (ν) is a **dimensionless** material property defined as **−ε_lateral/ε_axial**.
- ✅ It is **always positive** for stable, isotropic engineering materials.
- ✅ **Theoretical limits:** −1 ≤ ν ≤ 0.5 (practical range: 0 to 0.5).
- ✅ **ν = 0.5** → Incompressible material (rubber-like, volume preserved).
- ✅ **ν = 0** → Cork-like material (no lateral strain when axially loaded).
- ✅ **Relationships to memorize:**
  - E = 2G(1+ν) — couples E and G
  - E = 3K(1−2ν) — couples E and K
  - G = 3K(1−2ν)/[2(1+ν)] — couples G and K
- ✅ **Volumetric strain** under uniaxial stress: θ = (σ/E)(1−2ν).
- ✅ **Standard material values:** Steel (0.30), Aluminium (0.33), Concrete (0.20), Rubber (0.49), Glass (0.25).
- ✅ **Naval application:** Predicts lateral contraction in hull plates, pressure hulls, and mooring cables.
- ✅ In triaxial stress states, the **generalised Hooke's law** uses ν to couple strains in orthogonal directions.
- ✅ For 3D isotropic elasticity, **only TWO elastic constants** are independent; the others are derivable via ν.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Working Stress
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST303 - SOLID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Working Stress

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Working Stress** is the maximum permissible stress that a structural member is allowed to carry under service (working) conditions. It is obtained by dividing the ultimate stress (or yield stress, whichever is lower and more critical) of the material by a numerical quantity known as the **Factor of Safety (FoS)**. The entire design philosophy based on this principle is termed the **Working Stress Method (WSM)** of design.

Mathematically expressed as:

$$\sigma_{working} = \frac{\sigma_{ultimate}}{\text{Factor of Safety}}$$

Or, when the elastic limit governs:

$$\sigma_{working} = \frac{\sigma_{yield}}{\text{Factor of Safety}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition to Memorize:**
> *"Working stress is the stress to which a material is subjected in actual service, limited to a safe value below the elastic limit, and used as the basis for the design of structural members."*

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

Imagine you are towing a friend's small boat with a thin nylon rope. The rope has a breaking strength of, say, **100 kg**. If you apply the full 100 kg, the rope snaps instantly. So what do you do? You decide: *"I will only ever pull with a maximum of 20 kg."* That **20 kg** is your **working load**, and the ratio **100/20 = 5** is your **factor of safety**.

Now extend this to a real ship:
- A ship's bottom plate is made of mild steel with an ultimate tensile strength of about **400 MPa**.
- The classification society (e.g., IRS, Lloyd's Register) does **not** let the designer stress it up to 400 MPa — that would be near rupture.
- Instead, it permits a **working stress of around 160 MPa** (with FoS = 2.5 for steel).
- The structural design is then carried out so that under the worst expected still-water and wave loads, the calculated stress never exceeds **160 MPa**.

That 160 MPa is the **working stress** — the practical ceiling below which the structure operates safely, every day, for decades.

### Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Naval Architecture Context)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard FoS values used in ship structural design:**
> - **Mild Steel plates & stiffeners:** FoS = 2.5 (based on yield stress) → σ_working ≈ 0.6 σ_y
> - **High-tensile steel (HTS):** FoS = 2.0 → σ_working ≈ 0.5 σ_y
> - **Aluminium alloys (ship superstructures):** FoS = 2.5 to 3.0
> - **Wood (small boats, masts):** FoS = 4 to 6
> - **Bronze propellers:** FoS = 5 to 6 (based on ultimate stress)

### Three Pillars of Working Stress Design

| Concept | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Ultimate Stress (σ_u)** | Maximum stress material can withstand before fracture |
| **Yield Stress (σ_y)** | Stress at which material begins to deform permanently |
| **Working Stress (σ_w)** | Permissible stress under service load = σ_y / FoS |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Working Stress Method assumes that the material behaves elastically** throughout its service life. It does **not** account for plastic reserve strength — a limitation addressed later by the Limit State Method.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Breakdown of the Working Stress Method (WSM)

The WSM is built on five sequential logical steps. Each is essential to the KTU board's valuation:

- **Step 1 — Identify the loads.**
  Compute the *worst expected service loads* on the member. For a ship, this includes:
  - Still-water bending moment (SWBM)
  - Wave bending moment (WBM)
  - Dynamic wave loads (slamming, green water)
  - Cargo and ballast loads
  - Local loads (deck fittings, machinery)

- **Step 2 — Compute the resulting internal stresses.**
  Using the standard strength-of-materials relations:
  - Axial: σ = P/A
  - Bending: σ = M·y/I = M/Z
  - Shear: τ = V·Q/(I·b)
  - Torsion: τ = T·r/J
  - Combined: σ_resultant = σ_axial ± σ_bending (superposition)

- **Step 3 — Determine the material's limiting strength.**
  Pick the *governing* value: usually σ_y (yield) for ductile steel, σ_u (ultimate) for brittle materials like cast iron.

- **Step 4 — Apply the Factor of Safety (FoS).**
  Choose a FoS based on:
  - Material reliability
  - Type of loading (static, dynamic, fatigue)
  - Consequences of failure
  - Code requirements (IRS, DNV, ABS, LR)

- **Step 5 — Compare and size the member.**
  The member is sized so that **σ_calculated ≤ σ_working**.
  This is called the **strength check** or **section modulus check** in ship design.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Factor of Safety

The Factor of Safety is **not arbitrary**. It compensates for uncertainties in:

- Variability of material properties (σ_y can vary ±10% in a batch of steel)
- Inaccuracies in assumed loads (sea states are statistical, not deterministic)
- Construction imperfections (welding defects, initial distortion)
- Corrosion and wastage over the ship's life (typically 25 years)
- Stress concentrations at cut-outs, brackets, and connection points
- Dynamic amplification effects (slamming loads can be 2–3× static)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** In naval architecture, the working stress is the foundation of all classification society scantling rules. When you design a bulkhead, a bilge keel bracket, or a propeller blade, you start with σ_working and back-calculate the required thickness or section modulus.

### 2.3 Comparison: WSM vs Other Design Philosophies

| Design Method | Basis | FoS Range | Accounts for Plasticity? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Working Stress Method (WSM)** | Elastic stress ≤ σ_y/FoS | 1.5 to 4.0 | No |
| **Ultimate Stress Method (USM)** | Ultimate load / Working load ≥ FoS | 1.5 to 3.0 | Yes |
| **Limit State Method (LSM)** | Probabilistic, partial safety factors | Variable | Yes |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas in this table are KTU 2024 Board-Exam Gold.** Memorize units alongside the equations.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | σ_w = σ_y / n | Working stress from yield | MPa |
| 2 | σ_w = σ_u / n | Working stress from ultimate | MPa |
| 3 | n = σ_y / σ_w | Factor of Safety (yield-based) | Dimensionless |
| 4 | σ = P / A | Direct axial stress | N/mm² |
| 5 | σ = M / Z | Bending stress (Z = section modulus) | N/mm² |
| 6 | τ = V·Q / (I·b) | Transverse shear stress | N/mm² |
| 7 | τ = T·r / J | Torsional shear stress | N/mm² |
| 8 | σ_principal = (σ_x + σ_y)/2 ± √[((σ_x-σ_y)/2)² + τ²] | Principal stresses (max/min) | N/mm² |
| 9 | τ_max = √[((σ_x-σ_y)/2)² + τ²] | Maximum shear stress | N/mm² |
| 10 | Z_req = M / σ_w | Required section modulus | mm³ |
| 11 | σ_allowable = (1/FoS)·σ_ult | Generic allowable stress | MPa |
| 12 | E = σ / ε | Young's modulus (elastic constant) | MPa |

**Notations used:**
- σ_w = working stress, σ_y = yield stress, σ_u = ultimate stress
- n = Factor of Safety, P = axial load, A = cross-sectional area
- M = bending moment, Z = section modulus, V = shear force
- Q = first moment of area, I = second moment of area, b = width
- T = torque, r = radial distance, J = polar moment of inertia
- E = Young's modulus, ε = strain

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the **shipbuilding industry**, the working stress concept governs the scantling calculations done by ship designers. The Indian Register of Shipping (IRS), Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS, and Bureau Veritas all prescribe minimum scantlings (plate thicknesses, section moduli of stiffeners) so that the stresses from realistic loads do not exceed σ_w. Failure to comply means the ship is not class-worthy and cannot be insured or operated commercially.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick rule-of-thumb used in shipyards:** For ordinary mild steel hull plating, the working stress in bending is approximately **160 MPa** (≈ 0.6 × 250 MPa yield with FoS ≈ 1.6 on yield, or about 0.4 × 400 MPa ultimate with FoS ≈ 2.5). This is the value used in everyday hull-girder and plate-bending checks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Working Stress from Yield Criterion

**Given:** A ductile material with yield stress σ_y. The material must remain in the elastic region with a safety margin n.

**To Derive:** Expression for σ_working.

We start with the **definition of Factor of Safety** based on yield stress. By definition, the FoS is the ratio of the maximum permissible stress (yield, the limit of elastic behaviour) to the actual stress the material is allowed to carry in service:

$$n = \frac{\sigma_{yield}}{\sigma_{working}}$$

The reason we use σ_yield and not σ_ultimate in ductile materials is that the yielding point marks the **boundary of elastic behaviour**. Once a ductile material yields, it deforms plastically — meaning the ship plate, for example, would develop a permanent "set" that distorts the hull line. This is unacceptable in service.

Rearranging the definition to solve for σ_working, we apply the standard algebraic inversion:

$$\sigma_{working} = \frac{\sigma_{yield}}{n}$$

This is the **fundamental working-stress equation** for ductile materials (steels, aluminium alloys). For brittle materials (cast iron, concrete), σ_ultimate replaces σ_yield.

### 3.2 Derivation: Required Section Modulus for a Ship Stiffener

**Scenario:** A transverse deck beam in a frigate must support a bending moment M = 450 kN·m under combined sea and cargo loads. The beam is fabricated from mild steel with σ_yield = 250 MPa. The classification society requires FoS = 2.5 based on yield. Find the required section modulus Z.

**Step 1 — Compute the working stress.**

$$\sigma_{working} = \frac{\sigma_{yield}}{n} = \frac{250 \text{ MPa}}{2.5} = 100 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the bending stress equation.**

From the basic bending formula, the maximum bending stress in a beam is:

$$\sigma = \frac{M}{Z}$$

**Step 3 — Enforce the strength condition.**

For safe design, the calculated stress must not exceed the working stress:

$$\sigma \leq \sigma_{working}$$

$$\frac{M}{Z} \leq \sigma_{working}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the required section modulus.**

Rearranging the inequality, we obtain the **design equation**:

$$Z_{required} = \frac{M}{\sigma_{working}}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute numerical values (consistent SI units).**

Convert the bending moment to N·mm:
$$M = 450 \text{ kN·m} = 450 \times 10^3 \text{ N·m} = 450 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

Convert working stress to N/mm²:
$$\sigma_{working} = 100 \text{ MPa} = 100 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

Substitute:

$$Z_{required} = \frac{450 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}}{100 \text{ N/mm}^2}$$

Perform the numerical evaluation:

$$Z_{required} = 4.5 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3 = 4500 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Interpretation:** The ship designer must select a rolled section (e.g., a bulb-flat, angle, or T-bar) whose section modulus about the relevant axis is at least **4500 cm³**. Standard shipbuilding sections (like a 200×10 bulb-flat) are then chosen from steel-tables. ☐

### 3.3 Derivation: Relationship Between Working Stress and Strain (Hooke's Law Linkage)

For elastic design, the working strain is directly related to the working stress via Hooke's Law. This is a critical relationship because ship deflection criteria are often written in terms of allowable strain (typically 0.001 to 0.002 for ship hulls).

Starting from Hooke's Law:

$$\sigma_{working} = E \cdot \varepsilon_{working}$$

Solving for the working strain:

$$\varepsilon_{working} = \frac{\sigma_{working}}{E} = \frac{\sigma_{yield}}{n \cdot E}$$

For mild steel with E = 2 × 10⁵ MPa and σ_y = 250 MPa, with n = 2.5:

$$\varepsilon_{working} = \frac{250}{2.5 \times 2 \times 10^5} = \frac{250}{5 \times 10^5}$$

$$\varepsilon_{working} = 5 \times 10^{-4} = 0.0005 \text{ (dimensionless)}$$

This is the typical elastic strain limit used in ship hull deflection checks.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem — Combined Stress on a Ship's Rudder Stock

A ship's rudder stock is subjected simultaneously to:
- An axial tensile force P = 200 kN
- A bending moment M = 80 kN·m
- A torque T = 40 kN·m

The stock has a solid circular cross-section of diameter d = 150 mm and is made of a steel with σ_y = 260 MPa, σ_u = 460 MPa. Adopt a Factor of Safety n = 4.0 on ultimate stress. Determine whether the stock is safe under WSM.

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional properties of a solid circular section of diameter 150 mm:**

$$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (150)^2}{4} = 17671.46 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$Z = \frac{\pi d^3}{32} = \frac{\pi (150)^3}{32} = 331,340.7 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$J = \frac{\pi d^4}{16} = \frac{\pi (150)^4}{16} = 24,860,540 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 2 — Compute the working stress:**

$$\sigma_{working} = \frac{\sigma_{u}}{n} = \frac{460}{4.0} = 115 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the direct (axial) stress:**

$$\sigma_{axial} = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{200 \times 10^3}{17671.46} = 11.317 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the bending stress at the extreme fibre:**

$$\sigma_{bending} = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{80 \times 10^6}{331340.7} = 241.43 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the maximum shear stress due to torsion (at the surface, r = d/2 = 75 mm):**

$$\tau_{torsion} = \frac{T \cdot r}{J} = \frac{40 \times 10^6 \times 75}{24860540} = 120.65 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 6 — Identify the principal stresses.**

At the critical point (extreme fibre where σ_bending is maximum), the stress state is:

$$\sigma_x = \sigma_{axial} + \sigma_{bending} = 11.317 + 241.43 = 252.75 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_y = 0$$

$$\tau_{xy} = \tau_{torsion} = 120.65 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 7 — Apply the principal stress formula:**

$$\sigma_1 = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

$$\sigma_1 = \frac{252.75}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{252.75}{2}\right)^2 + (120.65)^2}$$

$$\sigma_1 = 126.375 + \sqrt{(126.375)^2 + (120.65)^2}$$

$$\sigma_1 = 126.375 + \sqrt{15970.64 + 14556.42}$$

$$\sigma_1 = 126.375 + \sqrt{30527.06}$$

$$\sigma_1 = 126.375 + 174.72 = 301.10 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_2 = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} - \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

$$\sigma_2 = 126.375 - 174.72 = -48.34 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 8 — Apply the strength check under WSM:**

The maximum principal stress must not exceed the working stress:

$$\sigma_1 = 301.10 \text{ MPa} > \sigma_{working} = 115 \text{ MPa}$$

**Conclusion:** The rudder stock is **NOT SAFE** in its present dimensions. The designer must either increase the diameter d or change the material. The required enlargement can be found by iteration or direct proportion since σ_bending ∝ 1/d³.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: The Working Stress Method (WSM) Design Loop

The following Mermaid diagram captures the iterative logical flow of designing a structural member using the Working Stress Method. This is the kind of "process flow" question KTU examiners love to ask (often for 3–5 marks).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Service Loads P, M, V, T] --> B[Compute Internal Stresses sigma_calc, tau_calc]
    B --> C{Select Governing Material Strength}
    C -->|Ductile Material| D[Use sigma_yield]
    C -->|Brittle Material| E[Use sigma_ultimate]
    D --> F[Choose Factor of Safety n from Code]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Compute Working Stress sigma_w = sigma_gov / n]
    G --> H{Strength Check}
    H -->|sigma_calc leq sigma_w| I[Design SAFE: Finalize Section]
    H -->|sigma_calc greater sigma_w| J[Revise Section: increase t, d, or Z]
    J --> B
    I --> K[Document Scantlings for Classification Approval]
    K --> L[End]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Stress Hierarchy on the Stress-Strain Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Zero Stress - Unloaded State] --> B[Proportional Limit: sigma proportional]
    B --> C[Elastic Limit: sigma elastic]
    C --> D[Yield Point: sigma_yield]
    D --> E[Ultimate Stress: sigma_ultimate]
    E --> F[Fracture Point: sigma_rupture]
    G[Working Stress sigma_w] -->|lies well below| C
    G -->|FoS leq 1 is unsafe| D
    H[Factor of Safety n] -->|n = sigma_y / sigma_w| G
```

**How to read this diagram:**
- The stress-strain curve is divided into regions: elastic, plastic, and failure.
- **Working stress** must always lie *below* the elastic limit — well before yielding — for the WSM to be valid.
- The **Factor of Safety** is the *distance* (in stress units) between the working stress point and the yield point on the vertical axis.

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Application of Working Stress in Ship Structural Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ship Structural Component] --> B{Component Type}
    B -->|Hull Plate| C[Check Plate Bending: sigma = 0.75 times k times p times l^2 / t^2]
    B -->|Stiffener| D[Check Section Modulus: Z_req = M / sigma_w]
    B -->|Rudder Stock| E[Check Combined Stress: Principal Stress Criterion]
    B -->|Shaft| F[Check Torsion + Bending: Equivalent Stress]
    C --> G[Verify sigma_calc leq sigma_w]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G -->|Pass| H[Component Approved by Classification Society]
    G -->|Fail| I[Resize: Increase t, d, or Z]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The same WSM concept (σ_calc ≤ σ_w) is applied repeatedly across different ship components, but the *formula* used to compute σ_calc changes with the type of loading. The working stress σ_w itself remains the property of the material + code (≈ 160 MPa for mild steel).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
