## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define volumetric strain. Show that for a small elemental cube subjected to principal strains $\varepsilon_x$, $\varepsilon_y$, $\varepsilon_z$, the volumetric strain is $\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Volumetric strain is the ratio of change in volume to the original volume of a body:
$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{\Delta V}{V}$$

**Derivation (2 marks):**
Consider a cube of original volume $V = dx \cdot dy \cdot dz$. After deformation, the dimensions become $(1+\varepsilon_x)dx$, $(1+\varepsilon_y)dy$, $(1+\varepsilon_z)dz$. The new volume is:
$$V' = (1+\varepsilon_x)(1+\varepsilon_y)(1+\varepsilon_z)\,dx\,dy\,dz$$
For small strains, the second-order products $\varepsilon_i\varepsilon_j$ are negligible:
$$V' \approx [1 + \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z]\,V$$
Therefore:
$$\Delta V = V' - V = (\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z)\,V$$
$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{\Delta V}{V} = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
*Derive the relationship between Bulk Modulus $K$, Young's Modulus $E$, and Poisson's Ratio $\nu$ for an isotropic elastic material.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Under hydrostatic pressure $p$, the principal stresses are $\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = -p$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Substituting into the master volumetric strain equation:
$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{(1-2\nu)}{E}(-p-p-p) = -\frac{3p(1-2\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** By definition, $K = p/|\varepsilon_v|$:
$$K = \frac{p}{\dfrac{3p(1-2\nu)}{E}} = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)} \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the volumetric strain of a body subjected to triaxial stresses $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$, and $\sigma_z$ along three mutually perpendicular axes. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** A solid cylindrical steel specimen of diameter $30\ \mathrm{mm}$ and length $200\ \mathrm{mm}$ is placed inside a pressure chamber and subjected to a uniform hydrostatic pressure of $150\ \mathrm{MPa}$. Calculate:
- (i) the volumetric strain in the specimen,
- (ii) the change in volume of the specimen.

Take $E = 2.0 \times 10^5\ \mathrm{MPa}$ and $\nu = 0.30$. **\[7 marks\]**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

For a triaxially stressed isotropic body, the strain in any principal direction is the direct stress contribution minus the Poisson-induced lateral effects:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right] \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

By cyclic symmetry:

$$\varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_y - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_z)\right]$$

$$\varepsilon_z = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_z - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)\right]$$

Adding all three:

$$\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$$

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{1}{E}\left\{(\sigma_x+\sigma_y+\sigma_z) - \nu\left[2(\sigma_x+\sigma_y+\sigma_z)\right]\right\}$$

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{(1-2\nu)}{E}(\sigma_x+\sigma_y+\sigma_z) \quad \text{(3 marks)}$$

Substituting the numerical values into the bulk modulus relation and verifying the limits:
- If $\nu = 0.5$ (incompressible material), $\varepsilon_v = 0$ regardless of stress — **physically correct** **(1 mark)**
- If $\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = \sigma$ (hydrostatic), $\varepsilon_v = 3\sigma(1-2\nu)/E$ — **agrees with hydrostatic derivation** **(1 mark)**

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Volumetric Strain — 4 marks**

Under uniform hydrostatic pressure, $\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = -p = -150\ \mathrm{MPa}$.

Using the hydrostatic volumetric strain formula:
$$\varepsilon_v = -\frac{3p(1-2\nu)}{E}$$

$$\varepsilon_v = -\frac{3 \times 150 \times (1 - 2 \times 0.30)}{2.0 \times 10^5}$$

$$\varepsilon_v = -\frac{3 \times 150 \times 0.40}{2.0 \times 10^5} = -\frac{180}{2.0 \times 10^5}$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_v = -9.0 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{(dimensionless, i.e., compressive)}}$$

**(ii) Change in Volume — 3 marks**

Original volume of cylinder:
$$V = \frac{\pi}{4}d^2 \cdot L = \frac{\pi}{4}(30)^2(200) = \frac{\pi}{4}(900)(200) = 45000\pi\ \mathrm{mm^3}$$

$$V \approx 141371.67\ \mathrm{mm^3}$$

Change in volume:
$$\Delta V = \varepsilon_v \cdot V = (-9.0 \times 10^{-4}) \times 141371.67$$

$$\boxed{\Delta V \approx -127.23\ \mathrm{mm^3}}$$

The specimen's volume decreases by approximately **127.23 mm³** under the applied pressure.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for Bulk Modulus $K$ in terms of Young's Modulus $E$ and Poisson's Ratio $\nu$, starting from the definition of volumetric strain. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** A thin-walled spherical pressure vessel of diameter $1.5\ \mathrm{m}$ and wall thickness $25\ \mathrm{mm}$ is submerged to an ocean depth where it experiences a uniform external hydrostatic pressure of $3.5\ \mathrm{MPa}$. Calculate the volumetric strain of the vessel material. Given: $E = 2.1 \times 10^5\ \mathrm{MPa}$, $\nu = 0.28$. **\[7 marks\]**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** State the definition of volumetric strain under hydrostatic pressure $p$:
$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{\Delta V}{V} = \frac{(1-2\nu)}{E}(\sigma_x+\sigma_y+\sigma_z)$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** For hydrostatic loading, $\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = -p$, so:
$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{(1-2\nu)}{E}(-3p) = -\frac{3p(1-2\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Bulk modulus is defined as the ratio of hydrostatic stress to volumetric strain magnitude:
$$K = \frac{p}{|\varepsilon_v|} = \frac{p}{\dfrac{3p(1-2\nu)}{E}}$$

$$\boxed{K = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Verification — for $\nu = 0.5$ (incompressible), $K \to \infty$, confirming physical correctness.

**(b) Numerical Solution for Spherical Vessel (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (3 marks) — Stress in a Thin Spherical Shell:**

For a thin spherical shell under external pressure $p$ (with no internal pressure), the membrane stress is:
$$\sigma = -\frac{pd}{4t}$$

Substituting values:
$$\sigma = -\frac{3.5 \times 1500}{4 \times 25} = -\frac{5250}{100} = -52.5\ \mathrm{MPa}$$

Note: For a sphere, the hoop and longitudinal stresses are *equal* in all directions on the membrane, so:
$$\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = -52.5\ \mathrm{MPa}$$

**Step 2 (3 marks) — Compute Volumetric Strain:**

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{(1-2\nu)}{E}(\sigma_x+\sigma_y+\sigma_z)$$

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{(1-2 \times 0.28)}{2.1 \times 10^5} \times (3 \times -52.5)$$

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{0.44 \times (-157.5)}{2.1 \times 10^5} = \frac{-69.30}{2.1 \times 10^5}$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_v = -3.30 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{(dimensionless)}}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Interpretation:**
The volume of the spherical hull material decreases by approximately **0.033%** under the external pressure.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Volumetric Strain Problems:**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in Hydrostatic Loading** *(–2 marks)*: Students often write $\varepsilon_v = +3p(1-2\nu)/E$, forgetting that hydrostatic pressure is **compressive** and should carry a negative sign. Always cross-check using the convention: "Pressure → Compression → Negative strain."
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Factor of 3 in Hydrostatic Sum** *(–2 marks)*: The most frequent error is writing $(\sigma_x+\sigma_y+\sigma_z) = -p$ instead of $-3p$. There are **three** equal stresses, not one.
>
> 3. **Confusing Bulk Modulus with Young's Modulus** *(–1 to –2 marks)*: $K$ and $E$ are **not equal**. For steel, $K \approx 1.67E$. Students who write $K = E$ lose direct marks.
>
> 4. **Violating the Small-Strain Assumption** *(–1 mark)*: The derivation $\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$ holds **only when all strains are infinitesimal**. If a problem gives a finite strain (e.g., $\varepsilon = 0.1$), the higher-order terms cannot be dropped, and the KTU examiner will deduct a mark for not stating the assumption.
>
> 5. **Ignoring the Bulk Modulus of Seawater** *(application questions, –1 mark)*: In submarine design problems, ignoring the fact that **seawater also compresses** (with $K_{sw} \approx 2300\ \mathrm{MPa}$) leads to an incomplete buoyancy analysis.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Volumetric Strain (Module 1)**

- **Definition:** $\varepsilon_v = \Delta V / V$ — ratio of volume change to original volume.
- **Master Strain Equation:** $\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$ — valid under the small-strain assumption.
- **Master Stress Equation:** $\varepsilon_v = \dfrac{(1-2\nu)}{E}(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z)$ — the **most important** formula of this topic.
- **Hydrostatic Special Case:** $\varepsilon_v = -\dfrac{3p(1-2\nu)}{E}$ — when all three stresses are equal to $-p$.
- **Bulk Modulus:** $K = \dfrac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$ — must be **memorized verbatim**; high-yield for KTU.
- **Incompressibility Limit:** As $\nu \to 0.5$, $K \to \infty$ and $\varepsilon_v \to 0$ — rubber-like materials.
- **Units:** All strains are **dimensionless**; all stresses and moduli in **MPa** (or $\mathrm{N/mm^2}$).
- **Sign Convention:** Tension is positive; compression is negative. Hydrostatic pressure produces **negative** volumetric strain.
- **Decoupling from Shear:** Volumetric strain is **independent of shear strain** — only normal strains contribute.
- **Naval Architecture Applications:** Pressure hulls, ballast tanks, deep submersibles, pipeline hydrostatic testing, and free-surface effect corrections in stability.
- **Common Exam Pitfall:** Always verify whether the loading is **hydrostatic** (use the $-3p$ short-cut) or **general triaxial** (use the full $\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z$ form).
- **Derivation Order to Memorize:** Strain sum → Hooke's Law substitution → Factor $(1-2\nu)$ → Master equation. Master equation → Hydrostatic substitution → Bulk modulus relation. This is the **canonical KTU derivation sequence**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modulus of Elasticity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST303 - SOLID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Modulus of Elasticity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Modulus of Elasticity** (also known as **Young's Modulus**, denoted by the symbol **E**) is defined as the ratio of longitudinal (axial) stress to longitudinal strain within the **elastic limit** of a material. It quantifies a material's inherent stiffness — its resistance to elastic deformation under uniaxial tensile or compressive loading.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \frac{\text{Longitudinal Stress}}{\text{Longitudinal Strain}}$$

The SI unit of Modulus of Elasticity is **Pascal (Pa)** or **N/m²**, though in naval architecture and shipbuilding practice it is commonly expressed in **Giga-Pascals (GPa)** or **N/mm²**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For Naval Architects, the Modulus of Elasticity is the *single most important* material property because it governs hull girder deflection, plate bending, local panel buckling, and vibration frequencies of marine structures. The standard reference value for **shipbuilding mild steel** is **E = 200 GPa (2 × 10⁵ N/mm²)** unless otherwise specified.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Ship Hull Spring

Imagine a long, slender **ship hull girder** floating in still water. If a wave lifts the bow slightly, the hull bends — but if the wave passes, the hull **springs back** to its original shape. The *stiffer* the hull material, the *less* it bends for a given load.

Think of it like this:

| Real-World Analogy | Engineering Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Stiff steel springboard | High Modulus of Elasticity (E = 200 GPa) |
| Flexible diving board (fiberglass) | Lower Modulus of Elasticity (E ≈ 40 GPa) |
| Rubber band | Very low Modulus of Elasticity (E ≈ 0.01 GPa) |

If you pull both a steel rod and an aluminum rod of the same dimensions with the **same force**, the **steel rod stretches less** because steel has a higher E. The modulus of elasticity is, in plain English, a **measure of "how much a material 'fights back' against being stretched or squished"**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Modulus of Elasticity is a **material property** (intrinsic, independent of geometry), whereas **stiffness** depends on both E and the cross-sectional geometry (stiffness k = EA/L for axial members).

## 1.3 Classification of Elastic Constants

In a 3-dimensional stress state, three principal elastic constants are used to fully characterize isotropic linear elastic materials:

1. **Young's Modulus (E)** — Resistance to uniaxial tension/compression
2. **Shear Modulus or Modulus of Rigidity (G)** — Resistance to shear deformation
3. **Bulk Modulus (K)** — Resistance to volumetric (hydrostatic) compression

A fourth dimensionless parameter links them all: **Poisson's Ratio (ν)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Isotropic homogeneous materials like **mild steel, aluminum alloys, and marine-grade timber** (along grain) require only **two independent elastic constants** to fully define their elastic behavior — typically **E** and **ν**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hooke's Law — The Foundation

The Modulus of Elasticity emerges from **Robert Hooke's Law (1660)**, which states that within the elastic limit, stress is directly proportional to strain.

**Operational Logic Steps:**

1. **Step 1 — Apply uniaxial load (F)** on a prismatic bar of length L and cross-section A.
2. **Step 2 — Measure elongation (δL)** produced by the load.
3. **Step 3 — Compute stress** as $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A}$ (axial normal stress).
4. **Step 4 — Compute strain** as $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\delta L}{L}$ (longitudinal strain).
5. **Step 5 — The proportionality constant** between σ and ε, *within the elastic limit*, is the **Modulus of Elasticity E**.

## 2.2 The Three Elastic Constants in Detail

### (a) Young's Modulus (E)
Measures stiffness under **uniaxial** tension or compression.

$$E = \frac{\sigma_{axial}}{\varepsilon_{axial}}$$

### (b) Shear Modulus (G) / Modulus of Rigidity
Measures stiffness under **pure shear** loading. Important for torsion of ship propeller shafts and hull shear stresses.

$$G = \frac{\tau}{\gamma}$$

where τ = shear stress and γ = shear strain.

### (c) Bulk Modulus (K)
Measures resistance to **uniform hydrostatic compression** — critical for **submarine hull design** where external sea pressure acts on all surfaces.

$$K = \frac{-p}{\Delta V / V} = \frac{-p \, V}{\Delta V}$$

### (d) Poisson's Ratio (ν)
When a material is stretched in one direction, it contracts in the perpendicular directions. Poisson's ratio is the ratio of **lateral strain to longitudinal strain**.

$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{lateral}}{\varepsilon_{longitudinal}}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | SI Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{F \, L}{A \, \delta L}$ | Young's Modulus (Axial) | Pa (N/m²) |
| 2 | $G = \dfrac{\tau}{\gamma} = \dfrac{T \, L}{J \, \theta}$ | Shear Modulus (Torsion) | Pa (N/m²) |
| 3 | $K = \dfrac{-p \, V}{\Delta V}$ | Bulk Modulus (Volumetric) | Pa (N/m²) |
| 4 | $\nu = -\dfrac{\varepsilon_{lat}}{\varepsilon_{long}}$ | Poisson's Ratio | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$ | E–G–ν Relationship | — |
| 6 | $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$ | E–K–ν Relationship | — |
| 7 | $G = \dfrac{3K(1 - 2\nu)}{2(1 + \nu)}$ | G–K–ν Relationship | — |
| 8 | $\delta L = \dfrac{F \, L}{A \, E}$ | Axial Elongation | m |
| 9 | $\theta = \dfrac{T \, L}{G \, J}$ | Angle of Twist (Torsion) | rad |
| 10 | $k_{axial} = \dfrac{AE}{L}$ | Axial Stiffness | N/m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Note:** Equation (5), $E = 2G(1+\nu)$, is the **most tested relationship** in KTU university exams. Master its derivation (given in Section 3) — examiners often ask students to derive it from first principles for 5–7 marks.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility in Naval Architecture

| Application | Elastic Constant Used | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| Hull girder bending & deflection | **E** | Determines hogging/sagging under wave loads |
| Propeller shaft torsion | **G** | Governs torsional strength and twist angle |
| Submarine hull collapse depth | **K** | Resistance to hydrostatic crushing |
| Plate panel buckling | **E, ν** | Buckling coefficient depends on E and ν |
| Vibration & slamming response | **E, G** | Natural frequency ∝ √(E/ρ) |
| Welding residual stress analysis | **E, ν** | Thermal stress prediction |

**Typical Values for Marine Materials:**

| Material | E (GPa) | G (GPa) | ν |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mild Steel (ship plate) | 200 | 77 | 0.30 |
| High-Tensile Steel (AH36) | 210 | 81 | 0.30 |
| Aluminum Alloy (5083-H116) | 70 | 26 | 0.33 |
| Marine Timber (along grain) | 10–12 | 0.6 | 0.35 |
| GFRP (typical marine) | 20–40 | 5–10 | 0.30 |
| Rubber (neoprene) | 0.01 | 0.0004 | 0.49 |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Young's Modulus from First Principles

**Given:** A uniform prismatic bar of length L, cross-sectional area A, subjected to an axial tensile force F. The bar elongates by δL.

**Step 1** — Write the definition of axial normal stress. Stress is the internal force per unit area:

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A}$$

**Step 2** — Write the definition of longitudinal strain. Strain is the change in length per unit original length:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\delta L}{L}$$

**Step 3** — Apply Hooke's Law: within the proportional (elastic) limit, stress is proportional to strain, and the constant of proportionality is the Modulus of Elasticity:

$$\sigma = E \, \varepsilon$$

**Step 4** — Substitute the expressions for σ and ε from Steps 1 and 2:

$$\frac{F}{A} = E \cdot \frac{\delta L}{L}$$

**Step 5** — Solve for E:

$$E = \frac{F \, L}{A \, \delta L}$$

**Step 6** — Solve for elongation δL (the form most often used in KTU numericals):

$$\delta L = \frac{F \, L}{A \, E}$$

> This is the **governing equation for axial deformation** used to compute hull girder elongations, stiffener stretch, and bracing member deflections.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: The Relationship E = 2G(1 + ν)

This is the **most important derivation** in Module 1 for KTU exams.

**Given:** A small cubic element of an isotropic, homogeneous, linear elastic material subjected to a **pure shear stress τ** on its faces.

**Step 1 — Visualize the deformation.** A pure shear stress τ applied on the vertical and horizontal faces of the cube produces:
- A shear strain γ (change in angle from 90°).
- Principal stresses at 45° to the shear plane: tension σ₁ = +τ and compression σ₂ = −τ.

**Step 2 — Express principal strains using Hooke's Law (generalized form for 3D).** For an isotropic material under biaxial principal stresses:

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}\left(\sigma_1 - \nu \, \sigma_2\right)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute σ₁ = +τ and σ₂ = −τ into the expression for ε₁:**

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}\left(\tau - \nu(-\tau)\right) = \frac{1}{E}\left(\tau + \nu\tau\right) = \frac{\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}$$

**Step 4 — Express the same strain geometrically.** The principal tensile strain ε₁ is related to the shear strain γ by the geometry of the deformation (the diagonal of the cube stretches by γ/2 of the diagonal length):

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{\gamma}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Equate the two expressions for ε₁:**

$$\frac{\gamma}{2} = \frac{\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}$$

**Step 6 — Rearrange to introduce the Shear Modulus G = τ/γ, i.e., τ = Gγ:**

$$\frac{\gamma}{2} = \frac{G \, \gamma \, (1 + \nu)}{E}$$

**Step 7 — Cancel γ from both sides (γ ≠ 0 for non-trivial deformation):**

$$\frac{1}{2} = \frac{G(1 + \nu)}{E}$$

**Step 8 — Cross-multiply to obtain the final relationship:**

$$\boxed{E = 2G(1 + \nu)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This proves that for any isotropic elastic material, only **two** of {E, G, ν} are independent. Knowing any two allows computation of the third. This is heavily tested in KTU Module 1 problems.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: The Relationship E = 3K(1 − 2ν)

**Given:** A cubic element subjected to uniform hydrostatic pressure p on all six faces.

**Step 1** — Hydrostatic pressure produces equal triaxial compressive stresses:
$$\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = -p$$

**Step 2** — By the definition of Bulk Modulus, the volumetric strain is:
$$\frac{\Delta V}{V} = -\frac{p}{K}$$

**Step 3** — The volumetric strain is the sum of the three normal strains:
$$\frac{\Delta V}{V} = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$$

**Step 4** — Apply generalized Hooke's Law for each principal direction:
$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right]$$

**Step 5** — Substitute σx = σy = σz = −p:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[-p - \nu(-p - p)\right] = \frac{1}{E}\left[-p + 2\nu p\right] = \frac{p(2\nu - 1)}{E}$$

**Step 6** — By symmetry, εx = εy = εz, so the volumetric strain becomes:

$$\frac{\Delta V}{V} = 3 \, \varepsilon_x = \frac{3p(2\nu - 1)}{E}$$

**Step 7** — Equate with the Bulk Modulus definition from Step 2:

$$-\frac{p}{K} = \frac{3p(2\nu - 1)}{E}$$

**Step 8** — Cancel p (p ≠ 0) and multiply by −1:

$$\frac{1}{K} = \frac{3(1 - 2\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 9** — Invert to obtain the final relationship:

$$\boxed{E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Insight:** If ν = 0.5, the material is **incompressible** (E → ∞ in the K–ν relationship through 1 − 2ν → 0), which is why **rubber** (ν ≈ 0.49) shows very high K but moderate E.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Derivation of G from K and ν

Combining the two previous results:

From $E = 2G(1+\nu)$ and $E = 3K(1-2\nu)$:

$$2G(1+\nu) = 3K(1-2\nu)$$

$$\boxed{G = \frac{3K(1-2\nu)}{2(1+\nu)}}$$

This is the third KTU-high-yield formula linking all three elastic constants.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem [KTU 2019 Style]:** A steel tie-bar in a ship's bracing system is 1.5 m long with a 20 mm × 20 mm square cross-section. It is subjected to an axial tensile load of 80 kN. Take E = 200 GPa and ν = 0.30. Calculate:
(a) The stress in the bar.
(b) The strain in the bar.
(c) The elongation of the bar.
(d) The lateral contraction (decrease in width).

**Solution:**

**(a) Stress:**
$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{80 \times 10^3 \text{ N}}{(0.020)(0.020) \text{ m}^2} = \frac{80{,}000}{0.0004} = 200 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 200 \text{ MPa}$$

**(b) Strain:**
$$\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{200 \times 10^6}{200 \times 10^9} = 1 \times 10^{-3} = 0.001 \text{ (dimensionless)}$$

**(c) Elongation:**
$$\delta L = \varepsilon \times L = (1 \times 10^{-3})(1.5 \text{ m}) = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 1.5 \text{ mm}$$

**(d) Lateral contraction:**
$$\varepsilon_{lat} = -\nu \, \varepsilon_{long} = -(0.30)(1 \times 10^{-3}) = -3 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\Delta b = \varepsilon_{lat} \times b = (-3 \times 10^{-4})(20 \text{ mm}) = -6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm} = -0.006 \text{ mm}$$

The negative sign indicates contraction. The new width is **20 − 0.006 = 19.994 mm**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Stress–Strain Diagram with Elastic Region Marked

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional regions** of the stress–strain curve and their corresponding engineering interpretations relevant to the Modulus of Elasticity.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Apply Axial Load F] --> B[Measure Stress sigma and Strain epsilon]
    B --> C{Is epsilon within Elastic Limit?}
    C -- YES --> D[Hookes Law Region: sigma is proportional to epsilon]
    C -- NO --> E[Plastic Region: Permanent Deformation Begins]
    D --> F[Compute E = sigma divided by epsilon]
    F --> G[Constant Slope: Modulus of Elasticity E]
    E --> H[Yielding then Strain Hardening]
    H --> I[Ultimate Tensile Strength]
    I --> J[Necking and Fracture]
    G --> K[Engineering Use in Naval Architecture]
    K --> L[Hull Girder Stiffness]
    K --> M[Plate Bending Analysis]
    K --> N[Vibration Frequency Calculation]
    K --> O[Buckling Load Prediction]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Relationships Among Elastic Constants

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E[Young Modulus E] ---|linked by nu| NU[Poissons Ratio nu]
    G[Shear Modulus G] ---|linked by nu| NU
    K[Bulk Modulus K] ---|linked by nu| NU
    E ---|E equals 2G times 1 plus nu| G
    E ---|E equals 3K times 1 minus 2nu| K
    G ---|G equals 3K times 1 minus 2nu over 2 times 1 plus nu| K
    E --> P[Isotropic Material: Two Independent Constants]
    G --> P
    NU --> P
    P --> Q[Naval Architecture Applications]
    Q --> R1[Ship Steel E equals 200 GPa]
    Q --> R2[Aluminum Alloy E equals 70 GPa]
    Q --> R3[FRP Marine E equals 20 to 40 GPa]
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Axial Elongation of a Hull Tie-Bar

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Original Tie Bar: Length L, Area A] --> B1[Apply Axial Force F]
    B1 --> C1[Internal Stress sigma equals F over A]
    C1 --> D1[Strain epsilon equals delta L over L]
    D1 --> E1[Hookes Law: sigma equals E times epsilon]
    E1 --> F1[Elongation delta L equals F times L over A times E]
    F1 --> G1{Lateral Effect Exists?}
    G1 -- YES --> H1[Poissons Ratio: epsilon lat equals minus nu times epsilon long]
    H1 --> I1[Lateral Contraction delta b equals nu times F times L over A times E]
    I1 --> J1[Final Geometry Computed]
    G1 -- NO --> J1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guidance for KTU Students:** The first diagram shows the *origin* of E in the linear elastic region of the stress–strain curve. The second shows how the three constants are mathematically interlocked by ν. The third shows the *computational chain* you apply in numerical problems.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Define the Modulus of Elasticity. State its SI unit and one typical value for shipbuilding mild steel.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Modulus of Elasticity (E)**, also called **Young's Modulus**, is defined as the ratio of longitudinal (axial) stress to longitudinal strain within the elastic limit of a material. It represents the material's stiffness — its resistance to elastic deformation under uniaxial loading.

$$E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}$$

**SI Unit:** Pascal (Pa) or N/m²; commonly expressed in **GPa**.

**Typical Value for shipbuilding mild steel:** **E = 200 GPa (2 × 10⁵ N/mm²)**.

*(1 mark — Definition, 1 mark — Formula/Unit, 1 mark — Typical value)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**What is Poisson's Ratio? What is its range for stable isotropic materials?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Poisson's Ratio (ν)** is defined as the negative ratio of lateral strain to longitudinal strain when a material is subjected to uniaxial loading:

$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{lateral}}{\varepsilon_{longitudinal}}$$

It is **dimensionless**.

**Range:** For stable, isotropic, linear elastic materials, Poisson's ratio lies in the range:

$$-1 < \nu < 0.5$$

For most engineering materials (metals, alloys, marine-grade polymers), ν is **positive** and typically lies between **0.20 and 0.35**. Cork has ν ≈ 0, and rubber approaches ν ≈ 0.5 (incompressible limit).

*(1 mark — Definition, 1 mark — Formula, 1 mark — Range with explanation)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024 - Module 1 Choice]`

**(a)** Derive the relationship $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$ for an isotropic linear elastic material, clearly stating the assumptions used. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 2 m long cylindrical steel rod of diameter 25 mm is used as a bracing member in a ship's superstructure. It is subjected to an axial tensile load of 120 kN. Given E = 200 GPa and ν = 0.30, determine:
   (i) The longitudinal stress and strain in the rod.
   (ii) The elongation of the rod.
   (iii) The decrease in diameter.
   (iv) The Shear Modulus G using the E–G–ν relationship. (7 marks)

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) — Derivation [7 marks]

**Assumptions:** (1 mark)
- Material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic**.
- Deformations are **small** (infinitesimal strain theory applies).
- Hooke's Law is valid (stress within the **proportional limit**).
- A **pure shear stress** is applied to a cubic element.

**Derivation Steps:** (6 marks)

**Step 1:** Consider a small cubic element of side a, subjected to a complementary pair of pure shear stresses τ on its faces (Fig. of stress cube).

**Step 2:** On a 45° inclined plane, the shear resolves into equal and opposite **principal stresses**:

$$\sigma_1 = +\tau, \quad \sigma_2 = -\tau$$

**Step 3:** By generalized Hooke's Law, the principal strain along the tension diagonal is:

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_1 - \nu \, \sigma_2\right] = \frac{1}{E}\left[\tau - \nu(-\tau)\right] = \frac{\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}$$

**Step 4:** By geometry of the shear deformation, the elongation of the tension diagonal is related to the shear strain γ by:

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{\gamma}{2}$$

**Step 5:** Substituting and using G = τ/γ (i.e., τ = Gγ):

$$\frac{\gamma}{2} = \frac{G\gamma(1 + \nu)}{E}$$

**Step 6:** Cancel γ (γ ≠ 0) and cross-multiply:

$$\boxed{E = 2G(1 + \nu)}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical [7 marks]

**Given:** L = 2 m, d = 25 mm = 0.025 m, F = 120 kN = 120,000 N, E = 200 GPa = 200 × 10⁹ Pa, ν = 0.30

**(i) Stress and strain:** (2 marks)

Cross-sectional area:
$$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.025)^2}{4} = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

Stress:
$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{120{,}000}{4.909 \times 10^{-4}} = 244.46 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} \approx 244.5 \text{ MPa}$$

Strain:
$$\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{244.46 \times 10^6}{200 \times 10^9} = 1.2223 \times 10^{-3}$$

**(ii) Elongation:** (2 marks)

$$\delta L = \varepsilon \times L = (1.2223 \times 10^{-3})(2) = 2.4446 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} \approx 2.44 \text{ mm}$$

**(iii) Decrease in diameter:** (1.5 marks)

Lateral strain:
$$\varepsilon_{lat} = -\nu \, \varepsilon = -(0.30)(1.2223 \times 10^{-3}) = -3.667 \times 10^{-4}$$

Change in diameter:
$$\Delta d = \varepsilon_{lat} \times d = (-3.667 \times 10^{-4})(25 \text{ mm}) = -9.167 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm} \approx -0.00917 \text{ mm}$$

New diameter ≈ **24.9908 mm**

**(iv) Shear Modulus:** (1.5 marks)

$$G = \frac{E}{2(1 + \nu)} = \frac{200 \times 10^9}{2(1 + 0.30)} = \frac{200 \times 10^9}{2.6} = 76.92 \text{ GPa}$$

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 1 Choice]`

**(a)** Define Bulk Modulus. For a spherical submarine hull of internal radius 1.2 m and wall thickness 30 mm, the volume decreases by 1.85 × 10⁻⁴ m³ when submerged to a depth where hydrostatic pressure is 4.5 MPa. Calculate the Bulk Modulus of the hull material. (7 marks)

**(b)** A composite material used in a racing yacht hull has the following properties: E = 35 GPa, ν = 0.28. Determine: (i) the Shear Modulus G, (ii) the Bulk Modulus K, and (iii) the volumetric strain when subjected to a hydrostatic pressure of 25 MPa. (7 marks)

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) — Bulk Modulus and Submarine Hull [7 marks]

**Definition (2 marks):**

The **Bulk Modulus (K)** of a material is defined as the ratio of hydrostatic (compressive) pressure to the volumetric strain it produces. It measures the material's resistance to uniform compression from all sides — a critical property for submarine hulls subjected to deep-sea pressure.

$$K = \frac{-p}{\Delta V / V} = \frac{-p \, V}{\Delta V}$$

The negative sign ensures K is positive for a material that contracts under compressive pressure.

**Calculation (5 marks):**

**Step 1:** Compute the original internal volume of the spherical hull:
$$V = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 = \frac{4}{3}\pi (1.2)^3 = \frac{4}{3}\pi (1.728) = 7.238 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2:** Note the sign convention — the volume decreases, so ΔV is negative:
$$\Delta V = -1.85 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3:** Apply the Bulk Modulus formula:
$$K = \frac{-p \, V}{\Delta V} = \frac{-(4.5 \times 10^6)(7.238)}{-1.85 \times 10^{-4}}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the numerator:
$$-(4.5 \times 10^6)(7.238) = -32.571 \times 10^6 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5:** Divide by ΔV:
$$K = \frac{-32.571 \times 10^6}{-1.85 \times 10^{-4}} = 176.06 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa} \approx 176.1 \text{ GPa}$$

The Bulk Modulus is approximately **176 GPa**, indicating a very stiff material suitable for deep-submergence applications.

---

#### Part (b) — Composite Yacht Hull Constants [7 marks]

**Given:** E = 35 GPa, ν = 0.28, p = 25 MPa

**(i) Shear Modulus G:** (2 marks)

Using the relationship $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$:

$$G = \frac{E}{2(1 + \nu)} = \frac{35}{2(1 + 0.28)} = \frac{35}{2.56} = 13.67 \text{ GPa}$$

**(ii) Bulk Modulus K:** (2.5 marks)

Using the relationship $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$:

$$K = \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)} = \frac{35}{3(1 - 2 \times 0.28)} = \frac{35}{3(0.44)} = \frac{35}{1.32} = 26.52 \text{ GPa}$$

**(iii) Volumetric strain under hydrostatic pressure:** (2.5 marks)

By definition of Bulk Modulus:
$$\frac{\Delta V}{V} = -\frac{p}{K} = -\frac{25 \times 10^6}{26.52 \times 10^9} = -9.428 \times 10^{-4}$$

The negative sign indicates a volume decrease. As a magnitude, the volumetric strain is **9.43 × 10⁻⁴** (or 0.0943%).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Error in Poisson's Ratio:** Students often drop the negative sign and write ν = ε_lat/ε_long instead of −ε_lat/ε_long. This leads to a positive ratio that violates the convention. (Lose 1 mark per occurrence.)
> 2. **Unit Inconsistency in Numericals:** Mixing mm and m (e.g., using E in GPa with lengths in mm) without proper conversion. Always convert to a single unit system *before* substituting.
> 3. **Forgetting the Negative Sign in Bulk Modulus Formula:** The formula $K = -pV/\Delta V$ requires the negative sign. Writing $K = pV/\Delta V$ is dimensionally correct but physically wrong in sign convention. (Lose 1 mark.)
> 4. **Confusing E with Stiffness k:** E is a material property (Pa). k = AE/L is a structural property (N/m). Examiners *will* deduct marks if you interchange them.
> 5. **In the Derivation of E = 2G(1+ν):** Students often skip the geometric step ($\varepsilon_1 = \gamma/2$) and only state the final result. You must show **all** steps for full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** $E = \sigma/\varepsilon$ — ratio of axial stress to axial strain within the elastic limit.
- **Unit:** Pa (N/m²); in practice, **GPa** or **N/mm²** for shipbuilding materials.
- **Reference Value:** Mild steel **E = 200 GPa**, ν = 0.30, G ≈ 76.92 GPa.
- **Hooke's Law** is valid **only** within the **proportional limit** (smaller than the elastic limit).
- **Three Elastic Constants** for isotropic materials: E, G, K; linked by **two independent relationships** with **Poisson's ratio ν**.
- **The Big Three Relationships** (must memorize for KTU):
  1. $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$
  2. $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$
  3. $G = \dfrac{3K(1 - 2\nu)}{2(1 + \nu)}$
- **Poisson's Ratio Range:** $-1 < \nu < 0.5$; for engineering metals typically $0.25 \leq \nu \leq 0.35$.
- **Stiffness vs Modulus:** E is *material* property; k = AE/L is *structural* (geometry-dependent) property.
- **Bulk Modulus** is critical for **submarine hull design** under hydrostatic pressure.
- **Shear Modulus G** is critical for **propeller shaft torsion** and **shear in hull plating**.
- **Young's Modulus E** is critical for **hull girder bending**, **plate deflection**, and **vibration frequency**.
- For **composite materials** (FRP, marine sandwich panels), E may be **direction-dependent** (orthotropic) — not a single value.
- **For naval architecture problems**, always quote E to **three significant figures** in GPa (200 GPa for steel, 70 GPa for aluminum).
- **Master the derivation** of $E = 2G(1+\nu)$ — it is the single most frequently asked derivation in KTU Module 1.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modulus of Rigidity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST303 - SOLID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Modulus of Rigidity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modulus of Rigidity (G)**, also known as the **Shear Modulus** or **Modulus of Elasticity in Shear**, is defined as the ratio of **shear stress (τ)** to the corresponding **shear strain (γ)** within the linear elastic limit of a material. It quantifies a material's inherent resistance to deformation when subjected to a pure shearing (twisting or sliding) force.

Mathematically, the definition is expressed as:

$$G = \frac{\tau}{\gamma}$$

Where:
- $G$ = Modulus of Rigidity
- $\tau$ = Shear stress (force per unit area acting parallel to the surface)
- $\gamma$ = Shear strain (angular deformation, in radians, dimensionless)

**SI Unit:** Pascal (Pa), typically expressed in **GPa** (Gigapascals) for engineering materials.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme - Syllabus Highlight**
> The Modulus of Rigidity is a fundamental material property under Module 1 (Mechanical Properties of Materials and Simple Stresses). It is directly linked to the design of marine shafting, propeller systems, and torsional analysis of hull components in naval architecture.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Understanding

Imagine you have a thick hardcover book lying flat on a table. Place your palm on the top cover and push horizontally. The book deforms — the top cover slides relative to the bottom, and the originally rectangular pages distort into a parallelogram shape. This angular distortion is **shear strain**.

Now imagine trying the same experiment with:
- A **rubber book** (very low G) → it distorts easily
- A **steel book** (very high G) → it barely budges

The **Modulus of Rigidity (G)** is the numerical "stiffness score" that tells us how strongly a material resists this kind of sliding/twisting distortion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Naval Architecture Intuition:** Think of a ship's propeller shaft transmitting torque from the engine to the propeller. The shaft twists slightly under load. The Modulus of Rigidity of the shaft material (typically marine-grade steel with **G ≈ 80 GPa**) determines how much it twists. A higher G means stiffer transmission — critical for precise propeller control and avoiding torsional resonance.

## 1.3 Physical Significance

The Modulus of Rigidity is one of the **four fundamental elastic constants** of an isotropic material:

| Constant | Symbol | Represents Resistance To |
|----------|--------|--------------------------|
| Young's Modulus | E | Axial elongation / compression |
| Modulus of Rigidity | **G** | **Shear / twisting deformation** |
| Bulk Modulus | K | Volume change under hydrostatic pressure |
| Poisson's Ratio | ν | Lateral strain per unit axial strain |

For a homogeneous, isotropic material, all four constants are interrelated. In naval architecture, **G** is indispensable for designing propeller shafts, rudder stocks, torsional springs, riveted connections, and assessing hull girder shear stresses.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Shear Stress, Shear Strain, and Hooke's Law in Shear

### 2.1.1 Shear Stress (τ)

When a force $F$ acts **parallel** to the surface of an element of cross-sectional area $A$, it produces a **shear stress**:

$$\tau = \frac{F}{A}$$

The direction of the force is tangential to the surface on which it acts, distinguishing it from normal (tensile or compressive) stress $\sigma = F/A$ where force is perpendicular.

### 2.1.2 Shear Strain (γ)

Consider a rectangular element with its base fixed. A tangential force applied to the top face causes the element to deform into a parallelogram. The angle of distortion (in **radians**) is the **shear strain**:

$$\gamma = \frac{\Delta x}{h} = \tan(\phi) \approx \phi \text{ (for small strains)}$$

Where:
- $\Delta x$ = horizontal displacement of the top face
- $h$ = height of the element
- $\phi$ = angle of shear (in radians)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shear strain is dimensionless** (a ratio of two lengths) and is measured in **radians** in the strict mathematical sense. Engineers often express small angles in radians for engineering calculations involving $G$.

### 2.1.3 Hooke's Law in Shear

Within the **proportional limit** (linear elastic region), shear stress is directly proportional to shear strain — this is **Hooke's Law in Shear**:

$$\tau = G \cdot \gamma$$

This is the shear analog of $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ for uniaxial loading.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic — How Modulus of Rigidity Operates

1. **A shear force is applied** to a material element parallel to one of its faces.
2. **Shear stress (τ)** develops internally, equal to the force divided by the area resisting it.
3. **The element distorts** — adjacent layers slide relative to each other, producing **shear strain (γ)**.
4. **The material's atomic bonds** resist this distortion. The strength of this resistance is quantified by **G**.
5. **Within the elastic limit**, the ratio $\tau / \gamma$ remains **constant** and equals **G**.
6. **Beyond the elastic limit** (yield), G is no longer constant — the material undergoes plastic deformation.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **EXAM CHEAT SHEET — MODULUS OF RIGIDITY** (Use these for solving KTU 2024 ESE problems. Use `\vert` instead of `\mid` for absolute values to avoid markdown table conflicts.)

| # | Formula | Description | Unit / Notes |
|---|---------|-------------|--------------|
| 1 | $G = \tau / \gamma$ | Fundamental definition | Pa or GPa |
| 2 | $G = E / \left[2(1+\nu)\right]$ | Relation with E and $\nu$ | Valid for isotropic materials |
| 3 | $E = 2G(1+\nu)$ | Rearranged form of (2) | Useful for conversions |
| 4 | $K = \dfrac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$ | Relation with Bulk modulus | Hydrostatic loading |
| 5 | $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{T \cdot r}{J}$ | Torsional shear stress | $J$ = polar moment of inertia |
| 6 | $\theta = \dfrac{T \cdot L}{G \cdot J}$ | Angle of twist (radians) | $L$ = length, $T$ = torque |
| 7 | $G_{\text{steel}} \approx 80 \text{ GPa}$ | Typical for marine steel | Use in numericals |
| 8 | $G_{\text{aluminium}} \approx 27 \text{ GPa}$ | Typical for marine aluminium alloys | For ship superstructures |
| 9 | $J_{\text{solid}} = \dfrac{\pi d^{4}}{32}$ | Polar MI for solid circular shaft | $d$ = diameter |
| 10 | $J_{\text{hollow}} = \dfrac{\pi}{32}\left(D^{4} - d^{4}\right)$ | Polar MI for hollow shaft | $D, d$ = outer, inner diameter |
| 11 | $\nu = 0.3$ (approx.) | Poisson's ratio for most metals | Default in problems |
| 12 | $\nu = 0.5$ (theoretical) | Incompressible materials (rubber) | Upper bound |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility in Naval Architecture

- **Propeller Shaft Design:** A marine propeller shaft transmits torque $T$ from the main engine. The angle of twist $\theta = TL/(GJ)$ must be kept within allowable limits (typically $< 0.25°$ per meter) to prevent vibration and ensure efficient power transmission.
- **Rudder Stock:** The vertical rudder stock is a torsion-loaded member. Its torsional rigidity $GJ$ determines the steering responsiveness.
- **Hull Plating Shear:** The hull girder experiences shear forces (vertical and horizontal) that produce shear stresses in the plating — directly tied to $G$.
- **Torsional Vibration Analysis:** Marine propulsion systems must avoid torsional resonance, governed by shaft stiffness ($GJ$) and inertia.
- **Selection of Marine Materials:** Comparing G values helps in choosing between steel (high G, heavy) and aluminium alloy (lower G, lightweight) for superstructures.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Fundamental Definition from Shear Stress-Strain Curve

Consider a cube of side $L$ fixed at its bottom face. Apply a tangential force $F$ on the top face. The top face displaces by a small distance $\Delta x$ in the direction of $F$.

**Step 1:** Calculate the shear stress on the top face.

$$\tau = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{F}{L^{2}}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the shear strain (angular deformation).

$$\gamma = \frac{\Delta x}{L}$$

(For small deformations, $\tan(\phi) \approx \phi = \gamma$.)

**Step 3:** Apply Hooke's Law in shear (within the elastic limit).

$$\tau \propto \gamma \implies \tau = G \cdot \gamma$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the expressions for $\tau$ and $\gamma$.

$$G = \frac{\tau}{\gamma} = \frac{F/L^{2}}{\Delta x / L} = \frac{F \cdot L}{L^{2} \cdot \Delta x} = \frac{F}{L \cdot \Delta x}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{G = \frac{\tau}{\gamma} = \frac{F \cdot L}{A \cdot \Delta x}}$$

This is the experimentally determined form of $G$ for a cube under pure shear.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Relationship $G = E / [2(1+\nu)]$ (CRITICAL FOR KTU EXAMS)

This is one of the most frequently asked derivations in KTU exams. We will derive it rigorously using the principle of **stress transformation** for pure shear.

### Setup
Consider a 2D element in pure shear with shear stress $\tau_{xy}$. This pure shear state can be expressed as a superposition of two orthogonal normal stresses at $45°$.

### Step 1: Resolve Pure Shear into Principal Stresses

A pure shear stress $\tau_{xy}$ is equivalent to a state where the principal stresses (acting on planes rotated by $45°$) are:

$$\sigma_{1} = +\tau_{xy} = +\tau$$
$$\sigma_{2} = -\tau_{xy} = -\tau$$
$$\sigma_{3} = 0 \quad (\text{plane stress})$$

These act on the planes oriented at $\pm 45°$ to the original faces. Verification: the maximum in-plane shear stress from Mohr's circle equals the radius $\sqrt{(0)^{2} + \tau^{2}} = \tau$, and the centre is at the origin, giving $\sigma_{1} = +\tau$ and $\sigma_{2} = -\tau$.

### Step 2: Apply Generalized Hooke's Law for Principal Strains

For a 3D stress state, the principal strains are:

$$\varepsilon_{1} = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_{1} - \nu(\sigma_{2} + \sigma_{3})\right]$$
$$\varepsilon_{2} = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_{2} - \nu(\sigma_{1} + \sigma_{3})\right]$$

Substitute the principal stresses $\sigma_{1} = +\tau$, $\sigma_{2} = -\tau$, $\sigma_{3} = 0$:

$$\varepsilon_{1} = \frac{1}{E}\left[\tau - \nu(-\tau + 0)\right] = \frac{1}{E}\left[\tau + \nu\tau\right] = \frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

$$\varepsilon_{2} = \frac{1}{E}\left[-\tau - \nu(\tau + 0)\right] = \frac{1}{E}\left[-\tau - \nu\tau\right] = -\frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

### Step 3: Compute Shear Strain from Principal Strains

For pure shear, the engineering shear strain is the difference between the principal strains (since the principal directions are at $\pm 45°$ to the original axes):

$$\gamma = \varepsilon_{1} - \varepsilon_{2}$$

Substitute the values:

$$\gamma = \frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E} - \left(-\frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}\right) = \frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E} + \frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

$$\gamma = \frac{2\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

### Step 4: Use Hooke's Law in Shear to Isolate G

We know that:

$$\tau = G \cdot \gamma \implies G = \frac{\tau}{\gamma}$$

Substitute $\gamma$:

$$G = \frac{\tau}{\dfrac{2\tau(1+\nu)}{E}} = \frac{\tau \cdot E}{2\tau(1+\nu)} = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$$

### Final Result

$$\boxed{G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is one of the most heavily tested formulas in KTU Solid Mechanics papers.** You must memorize it AND know how to derive it. The relationship holds for **isotropic, homogeneous, linearly elastic** materials only.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Angle of Twist for a Circular Shaft

For a solid circular shaft of length $L$, diameter $d$, subjected to torque $T$:

**Step 1:** Shear stress at radius $r$ from the centre.

$$\tau = \frac{T \cdot r}{J}$$

**Step 2:** Shear strain at the same radius (using Hooke's law in shear).

$$\gamma = \frac{\tau}{G} = \frac{T \cdot r}{G \cdot J}$$

**Step 3:** For a small element of length $dx$ at radius $r$, the relative rotation is $d\theta = \gamma \cdot dx / r$ (since the arc length = radius × angle).

Wait — let me correct this. The shear strain $\gamma = r \cdot d\theta / dx$, so:

$$d\theta = \frac{\gamma \cdot dx}{r} = \frac{T \cdot r \cdot dx}{G \cdot J \cdot r} = \frac{T \cdot dx}{G \cdot J}$$

**Step 4:** Integrate over the length $L$ to get total angle of twist.

$$\theta = \int_{0}^{L} \frac{T}{G \cdot J} \, dx = \frac{T \cdot L}{G \cdot J}$$

### Final Result

$$\boxed{\theta = \frac{T \cdot L}{G \cdot J} \quad \text{(in radians)}}$$

For a solid shaft ($J = \pi d^{4}/32$):

$$\theta = \frac{32 \cdot T \cdot L}{G \cdot \pi \cdot d^{4}}$$

The term $GJ$ is called the **Torsional Rigidity** — a measure of the shaft's resistance to twisting.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Worked Numerical Examples (KTU Style)

### Numerical Problem 1: Finding G from E and ν
**Problem:** A marine-grade steel alloy has Young's modulus $E = 210 \text{ GPa}$ and Poisson's ratio $\nu = 0.30$. Calculate the Modulus of Rigidity.

**Solution:**

$$G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)} = \frac{210}{2(1 + 0.30)} = \frac{210}{2 \times 1.30} = \frac{210}{2.60}$$

$$\boxed{G = 80.77 \text{ GPa}}$$

### Numerical Problem 2: Propeller Shaft Shear Stress
**Problem:** A solid steel propeller shaft of diameter $d = 60 \text{ mm}$ transmits a torque $T = 1.5 \text{ kN·m}$. Find the maximum shear stress. Take $G = 80 \text{ GPa}$.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Polar moment of inertia.

$$J = \frac{\pi d^{4}}{32} = \frac{\pi \times (0.060)^{4}}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 1.296 \times 10^{-5}}{32} = 1.272 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{4}$$

Step 2: Maximum shear stress at outer surface ($r = d/2$).

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{T \cdot r}{J} = \frac{1500 \times 0.030}{1.272 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{45}{1.272 \times 10^{-6}} = 35.38 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{\max} \approx 35.38 \text{ MPa}}$$

### Numerical Problem 3: Angle of Twist
**Problem:** For the same propeller shaft of length $L = 3 \text{ m}$, find the angle of twist in degrees.

**Solution:**

$$\theta = \frac{T \cdot L}{G \cdot J} = \frac{1500 \times 3}{80 \times 10^{9} \times 1.272 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\theta = \frac{4500}{1.0176 \times 10^{5}} = 0.04422 \text{ rad}$$

Converting to degrees:

$$\theta = 0.04422 \times \frac{180}{\pi} = 2.534°$$

$$\boxed{\theta \approx 2.53° \text{ over the 3 m length}}$$

This is **excessive** in practice — such a shaft would be redesigned with a larger diameter to limit twist to about $0.25°/\text{m}$.

### Numerical Problem 4: Change in Dimensions
**Problem:** A circular bar of length $L = 2 \text{ m}$ and diameter $d = 30 \text{ mm}$ is subjected to an axial pull $P = 50 \text{ kN}$. Given $G = 80 \text{ GPa}$ and $\nu = 0.3$, find the change in length, change in diameter, and Young's modulus.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Find Young's modulus using the relation $E = 2G(1+\nu)$.

$$E = 2 \times 80 \times (1 + 0.3) = 160 \times 1.3 = 208 \text{ GPa}$$

Step 2: Axial (longitudinal) stress.

$$A = \frac{\pi d^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.030)^{2}}{4} = 7.0686 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{2}$$

$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{50 \times 10^{3}}{7.0686 \times 10^{-4}} = 70.74 \text{ MPa}$$

Step 3: Longitudinal strain.

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{70.74 \times 10^{6}}{208 \times 10^{9}} = 3.401 \times 10^{-4}$$

Step 4: Change in length.

$$\Delta L = \varepsilon \cdot L = 3.401 \times 10^{-4} \times 2 = 6.802 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta L \approx 0.680 \text{ mm}}$$

Step 5: Lateral strain (Poisson effect).

$$\varepsilon_{\text{lat}} = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon = -0.3 \times 3.401 \times 10^{-4} = -1.020 \times 10^{-4}$$

Step 6: Change in diameter.

$$\Delta d = \varepsilon_{\text{lat}} \cdot d = -1.020 \times 10^{-4} \times 30 = -3.06 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta d \approx -3.06 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm (contraction)}}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Shear Deformation Visualization (Block Diagram)

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block diagram maps the deformation mechanism and the internal stress-strain relationship in pure shear. All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters per the safety protocol. Labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text only.

## 4.4 Schematic Description of Shear Deformation (Text-Based Illustration)

For students visualizing the deformation: imagine a rectangular element ABCD with vertices:

- A at $(0, 0)$
- B at $(L, 0)$  (fixed base)
- C at $(L, h)$
- D at $(0, h)$  (top face — force applied here)

After applying shear force $F$ on face DC (top), the top face shifts right by $\Delta x$. The new positions are:

- A' at $(0, 0)$ (unchanged)
- B' at $(L, 0)$ (unchanged)
- C' at $(L + \Delta x, h)$
- D' at $(\Delta x, h)$

The originally right angle at vertex D (between DA and DC) is now reduced to $(90° - \phi)$ where $\phi = \arctan(\Delta x / h) \approx \Delta x / h = \gamma$ (in radians for small strains). The shear strain is therefore:

$$\gamma = \frac{\Delta x}{h}$$

The internal shear stress resists this distortion, with the magnitude of resistance quantified by $G$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define Modulus of Rigidity. State its SI unit and write the relationship between Modulus of Rigidity, Young's Modulus, and Poisson's ratio.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** The Modulus of Rigidity (G) is defined as the ratio of shear stress ($\tau$) to the corresponding shear strain ($\gamma$) within the elastic limit of the material. It represents the material's resistance to shear deformation.

**SI Unit:** Pascal (Pa) or N/m². Practically, Gigapascals (GPa) are used for engineering materials.

**Relationship:**

$$G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$$

Where $E$ is Young's Modulus and $\nu$ is Poisson's ratio. This relation is valid for homogeneous, isotropic, linearly elastic materials.

---

### Question A.2 — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**A cylindrical bar of diameter 25 mm is subjected to a torque of 100 N·m. If the Modulus of Rigidity is 80 GPa, determine the shear stress developed in the bar.**

**Model Answer:**

Step 1: Calculate polar moment of inertia.

$$J = \frac{\pi d^{4}}{32} = \frac{\pi \times (0.025)^{4}}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 3.906 \times 10^{-7}}{32} = 3.835 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^{4}$$

Step 2: Calculate maximum shear stress at the surface ($r = d/2 = 12.5$ mm).

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{T \cdot r}{J} = \frac{100 \times 0.0125}{3.835 \times 10^{-8}} = \frac{1.25}{3.835 \times 10^{-8}}$$

$$\tau_{\max} = 32.59 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} = 32.59 \text{ MPa}$$

**Result:** $\tau_{\max} \approx 32.59$ MPa

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Derive the relationship between Young's Modulus (E), Modulus of Rigidity (G), and Poisson's Ratio (ν).**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Express pure shear as principal stresses.** A pure shear stress $\tau$ on a 2D element is equivalent to principal stresses:

$$\sigma_{1} = +\tau, \quad \sigma_{2} = -\tau, \quad \sigma_{3} = 0$$

These act on planes oriented at $45°$ to the original element.

**Step 2: Apply generalized Hooke's law for principal strains.**

$$\varepsilon_{1} = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_{1} - \nu(\sigma_{2} + \sigma_{3})\right] = \frac{1}{E}\left[\tau - \nu(-\tau)\right] = \frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

$$\varepsilon_{2} = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_{2} - \nu(\sigma_{1} + \sigma_{3})\right] = \frac{1}{E}\left[-\tau - \nu(\tau)\right] = -\frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 3: Compute shear strain as difference of principal strains.**

$$\gamma = \varepsilon_{1} - \varepsilon_{2} = \frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E} - \left(-\frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}\right) = \frac{2\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 4: Apply Hooke's law in shear.**

$$\tau = G \cdot \gamma \implies \gamma = \frac{\tau}{G}$$

**Step 5: Equate the two expressions for γ.**

$$\frac{\tau}{G} = \frac{2\tau(1+\nu)}{E} \implies G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$$

**Final Result:** $G = E / [2(1+\nu)]$

**Verification:** For steel, $E = 210$ GPa, $\nu = 0.3$:

$$G = \frac{210}{2(1.3)} = \frac{210}{2.6} = 80.77 \text{ GPa}$$

This matches the experimentally known value of $G \approx 80$ GPa for steel — confirming the derivation. ✓

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A solid steel shaft of 80 mm diameter is required to transmit 200 kW at 240 rpm. Take $G = 80$ GPa and permissible shear stress $\tau_{\text{perm}} = 60$ MPa. Design the shaft (find a suitable diameter) and calculate the angle of twist per meter length.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Calculate the torque transmitted.**

Using the relation $P = 2\pi N T / 60$:

$$T = \frac{60 \cdot P}{2\pi \cdot N} = \frac{60 \times 200 \times 10^{3}}{2\pi \times 240} = \frac{12 \times 10^{6}}{1507.96} = 7957.7 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 2: Apply the torsional shear stress formula to find minimum diameter.**

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{16T}{\pi d^{3}} \leq \tau_{\text{perm}}$$

Solving for $d$:

$$d^{3} \geq \frac{16T}{\pi \tau_{\text{perm}}} = \frac{16 \times 7957.7}{\pi \times 60 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$d^{3} \geq \frac{127323.5}{1.885 \times 10^{8}} = 6.755 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{3}$$

$$d \geq (6.755 \times 10^{-4})^{1/3} = 0.0876 \text{ m} = 87.6 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Adopt a standard size. Take $d = 90$ mm.**

**Step 4: Calculate polar moment of inertia for $d = 0.090$ m:**

$$J = \frac{\pi d^{4}}{32} = \frac{\pi \times (0.090)^{4}}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 6.561 \times 10^{-5}}{32} = 6.442 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{4}$$

**Step 5: Calculate angle of twist per meter length.**

$$\frac{\theta}{L} = \frac{T}{G \cdot J} = \frac{7957.7}{80 \times 10^{9} \times 6.442 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\frac{\theta}{L} = \frac{7957.7}{5.154 \times 10^{5}} = 0.01544 \text{ rad/m}$$

**Convert to degrees per meter:**

$$\frac{\theta}{L} = 0.01544 \times \frac{180}{\pi} = 0.885°/\text{m}$$

**Result:** Adopt $d = 90$ mm shaft; angle of twist $\approx 0.885°$ per meter length.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**What is Modulus of Rigidity? Explain with a neat sketch the deformation of a body under shear. How is it experimentally determined for a cylindrical specimen under torsion?**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):** Modulus of Rigidity ($G$) is the ratio of shear stress ($\tau$) to shear strain ($\gamma$) within the elastic limit:

$$G = \frac{\tau}{\gamma}$$

It measures the material's resistance to angular deformation.

**Deformation Sketch Description (2 marks):** A rectangular element ABCD with side $h$, fixed at the bottom, is subjected to tangential force $F$ on the top face. The top face displaces by $\Delta x$ horizontally, deforming the rectangle into a parallelogram A'B'C'D'. The angle of shear $\phi$ (originally $90°$, now $90° - \phi$) gives shear strain $\gamma = \phi = \Delta x / h$.

**Experimental Determination via Torsion Test (3 marks):**
1. A solid cylindrical specimen of length $L$ and diameter $d$ is gripped in a torsion testing machine.
2. A known torque $T$ is applied, and the angle of twist $\theta$ is measured using a dial gauge or angular transducer.
3. Shear stress at the outer surface: $\tau = 16T / (\pi d^{3})$
4. Shear strain at the outer surface: $\gamma = (\theta \cdot d) / (2L)$
5. Modulus of Rigidity: $G = \tau / \gamma = (16T / \pi d^{3}) / (\theta d / 2L) = 32TL / (\pi d^{4} \theta)$

$$G = \frac{32 \cdot T \cdot L}{\pi \cdot d^{4} \cdot \theta}$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A marine aluminium alloy has Young's Modulus $E = 70$ GPa and Poisson's Ratio $\nu = 0.33$. Find (i) Modulus of Rigidity, (ii) Bulk Modulus, and (iii) Shear stress if a shear strain of $2 \times 10^{-4}$ rad is applied.**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i): Modulus of Rigidity**

$$G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)} = \frac{70}{2(1 + 0.33)} = \frac{70}{2 \times 1.33} = \frac{70}{2.66}$$

$$\boxed{G = 26.32 \text{ GPa}}$$

**Part (ii): Bulk Modulus**

$$K = \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)} = \frac{70}{3(1 - 2 \times 0.33)} = \frac{70}{3(1 - 0.66)} = \frac{70}{3 \times 0.34}$$

$$K = \frac{70}{1.02}$$

$$\boxed{K = 68.63 \text{ GPa}}$$

**Part (iii): Shear stress**

Using Hooke's law in shear $\tau = G \cdot \gamma$:

$$\tau = 26.32 \times 10^{9} \times 2 \times 10^{-4} = 5.264 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\tau = 5.264 \text{ MPa}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units consistently:** Torque in N·mm vs N·m, length in mm vs m, G in GPa vs Pa. *Always convert to SI base units (N, m, Pa) before substituting into formulas.* A single unit mismatch can nullify an otherwise correct solution.
>
> 2. **Using the wrong moment of inertia formula:** Students often confuse $J = \pi d^{4}/32$ (solid shaft) with $J = \pi(D^{4} - d^{4})/32$ (hollow shaft). Read the problem carefully.
>
> 3. **Reporting angle of twist in degrees instead of radians:** The formula $\theta = TL/(GJ)$ gives radians. Convert to degrees only if specifically asked: multiply by $180/\pi$.
>
> 4. **Confusing shear stress with normal stress in torsion problems:** In torsion, the *only* stresses present are shear stresses. Do not compute $\sigma = 32T/(\pi d^{3})$ (that's a bending stress formula).
>
> 5. **Skipping the derivation steps in 7-mark questions:** For derivations like $G = E/[2(1+\nu)]$, you must show *all five* logical steps. Skipping directly to the final result costs 3–4 marks.
>
> 6. **Wrong sign in Poisson's effect:** Lateral strain is always opposite in sign to axial strain. Don't write positive $\Delta d$ for a bar under tension.
>
> 7. **Not specifying assumptions:** Always state "assuming isotropic, homogeneous, linearly elastic material" before applying $G = E/[2(1+\nu)]$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — MODULUS OF RIGIDITY**

### Core Concept
- **Definition:** $G = \tau / \gamma$ — ratio of shear stress to shear strain in the elastic region.
- **Unit:** Pa (or GPa for engineering materials).
- **Symbol:** $G$ (sometimes denoted $\mu$ in older texts).

### Key Relationships (Memorize These)
- $G = E / [2(1+\nu)]$ — **Most important formula**
- $E = 2G(1+\nu)$
- $K = E / [3(1-2\nu)]$
- $E = 9KG / (3K + G)$ — rarely used but good to know
- $\nu = (E/2G) - 1$ — rearranged form

### Typical Values
- **Steel:** $G \approx 80$ GPa, $\nu \approx 0.30$
- **Aluminium:** $G \approx 27$ GPa, $\nu \approx 0.33$
- **Cast Iron:** $G \approx 45$ GPa, $\nu \approx 0.25$
- **Brass/Bronze:** $G \approx 40$ GPa, $\nu \approx 0.34$

### Torsion Essentials
- $\tau_{\max} = 16T/(\pi d^{3})$ for solid shafts
- $\tau_{\max} = 16T/[\pi(D^{4} - d^{4})/D]$ for hollow shafts (max at outer surface)
- $\theta = TL/(GJ)$ — angle of twist in **radians**
- $GJ$ = Torsional Rigidity

### Naval Architecture Applications
- **Propeller shafts** (solid or hollow) — torque transmission from engine
- **Rudder stocks** — torsional loading from steering forces
- **Hull plating** — shear stresses from hull girder loading
- **Shaft couplings and flanges** — shear failure analysis
- **Torsional vibration** — critical speed calculations

### Theory of Elasticity Bounds
- $-1 < \nu < 0.5$ for stable isotropic materials
- $G > 0$ always (positive energy requirement)
- $E > 0$ always
- $K > 0$ always

### Derivation Shortcut
For $G = E/[2(1+\nu)]$ derivation: **Pure shear = two principal stresses at $45°$** → apply Hooke's law → principal strain difference = shear strain → equate to $G$ definition → done.

### Frequent Problem Types in KTU
1. Find $G$ given $E$ and $\nu$.
2. Find $\theta$ (angle of twist) for a shaft under torque.
3. Find $\tau_{\max}$ in a shaft under torsion.
4. Find $\Delta L$ and $\Delta d$ for an axially loaded bar (uses $G$ via $E$ relation).
5. Design problems: find minimum shaft diameter for given power and allowable stress.
6. Multi-step problems combining shear, torsion, and Poisson's effect.

### Common Exam Pitfall Avoided
**Always convert angle of twist from radians to degrees** if the problem specifies "in degrees" — and vice versa. Default in formulas is **radians**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bulk Modulus
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCNST303 - SOLID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Bulk Modulus # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Bulk Modulus (K)**, denoted by the symbol **K** (or sometimes **B** or **κ**), is a measure of a substance's resistance to uniform compression. It is defined as the ratio of the infinitesimal pressure increase to the resulting relative decrease of the volume of the material. Mathematically, for a solid or fluid element subjected to a hydrostatic (uniform) pressure $p$, the bulk modulus describes the volumetric stiffness: $$K = -V \frac{dp}{dV} = - \frac{dp}{dV/V} = \rho \frac{dp}{d\rho}$$ where $V$ is the original volume of the substance, $dV$ is the change in volume, $p$ is the pressure, and $\rho$ is the density. The negative sign is conventionally introduced to make $K$ a **positive quantity**, since an increase in pressure ($dp > 0$) results in a decrease in volume ($dV < 0$). > [!NOTE] > **SI Unit:** Pascal (Pa) or N/m². **Other Units:** GPa, MPa, psi (pounds per square inch), or bar. For naval architecture, bulk modulus is most commonly expressed in **N/mm² (MPa)** or **GPa**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **balloon filled with water** submerged at increasing depths in the ocean. - Near the **surface** (low pressure), the balloon retains nearly its original shape. The water inside is "stiff" against compression. - As the submarine (or the balloon) descends **deeper** (high pressure), the water inside the balloon compresses slightly, and the balloon shrinks a bit. The **bulk modulus** tells us *how much* the water resists this compression: - A **high bulk modulus** (e.g., **K ≈ 2.2 GPa** for water) means the material barely compresses — it is nearly **incompressible**. - A **low bulk modulus** (e.g., **K ≈ 0.0001 GPa** for a gas) means the material compresses easily — it is highly **compressible**. > [!TIP] > **For Naval Architects:** This is why modeling the ocean as an incompressible fluid (with an effective infinite bulk modulus at small pressure changes) is often acceptable for surface ship hydrostatics. However, for **submarines** or **deep-sea vessels**, the variation of bulk modulus with pressure and temperature becomes critical to predict buoyancy loss and structural integrity. ## 1.3 Why Bulk Modulus Matters in Naval Architecture | Scenario | Relevance of Bulk Modulus | |---|---| | **Submarine design** | Determines buoyancy loss with depth (compressibility of seawater and internal fluids) | | **Hydraulic systems** | Fluid bulk modulus affects pressure wave speed and **cavitation** | | **Pipe flow / slamming** | Acoustic speed $c = \sqrt{K/\rho}$ governs transient pressure spikes | | **Hull structural analysis** | Bulk modulus of seawater provides hydrostatic restoring stiffness in hydroelastic problems | | **Fender and bumper design** | Energy absorption capacity of elastomers depends on bulk modulus | ## 1.4 The Three Elastic Moduli — A Quick Map In three-dimensional linear elasticity, there are three primary independent elastic constants for isotropic materials. Bulk modulus relates to the other two — **Young's modulus (E)** and **Poisson's ratio (ν)** — through: $$K = \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)}$$ This equation highlights that as $\nu \to 0.5$ (rubber-like, incompressible behavior), $K \to \infty$ — meaning the material resists volume change almost perfectly. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Physical Setup and Assumptions Consider a small cubic element of side length $L$ and volume $V = L^3$, subjected to an increase in hydrostatic pressure $dp$ on all six faces. The element's volume reduces by $dV$, and the volumetric strain is defined as: $$\epsilon_v = \frac{dV}{V}$$ The bulk modulus is then: $$K = -\frac{dp}{\epsilon_v} = -\frac{dp}{dV/V}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Volumetric Strain (Dilatation):** $\epsilon_v = \epsilon_x + \epsilon_y + \epsilon_z$ for principal strains, i.e., the trace of the strain tensor. ## 2.2 Bulk Modulus vs. Compressibility The **compressibility** $\beta$ is simply the reciprocal of bulk modulus: $$\beta = \frac{1}{K} = -\frac{1}{V}\frac{dV}{dp} \quad \text{[units: Pa}^{-1}\text{]}$$ A material with high $K$ has low $\beta$ (hard to compress), and vice versa. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table contains the **complete, exam-ready formula set** for Bulk Modulus as required by the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PCNST303. Memorize these relations. | # | Formula | Description | SI Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $K = -V \dfrac{dp}{dV}$ | Definition (differential form) | Pa | | 2 | $K = \rho \dfrac{dp}{d\rho}$ | In terms of density change | Pa | | 3 | $K = -\dfrac{\Delta p}{\Delta V / V}$ | Average / engineering form | Pa | | 4 | $K = \dfrac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$ | Relation with Young's modulus and Poisson's ratio | Pa | | 5 | $K = \dfrac{2G(1+\nu)}{3(1-2\nu)}$ | Relation with shear modulus $G$ and $\nu$ | Pa | | 6 | $K = \dfrac{E\,G}{9G - 3E}$ | Eliminating $\nu$ (using $E = 2G(1+\nu)$) | Pa | | 7 | $\beta = 1/K$ | Compressibility | Pa$^{-1}$ | | 8 | $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$ | Inverse of (4) | Pa | | 9 | $\nu = \dfrac{1}{2} - \dfrac{E}{6K}$ | Inverse of (4) — **highly tested** | — | | 10 | $c = \sqrt{K/\rho}$ | Acoustic (pressure wave) speed in fluid | m/s | | 11 | $K_T$ (isentropic) vs. $K_S$ (isothermal) | $\dfrac{1}{K_T} - \dfrac{1}{K_S} = \dfrac{\alpha^2 T}{\rho C_p}$ | Pa | > [!TIP] > **Engineering Hint:** For **water at standard conditions (15 °C, 1 atm)**, $K \approx 2.20$ GPa. For **seawater (35 ppt salinity, 15 °C)**, $K \approx 2.34$ GPa. These values are tabulated in most naval architecture handbooks. ## 2.4 The Three-Step Logical Breakdown To set up and solve any bulk modulus problem, follow this disciplined procedure: - **Step 1 — Identify the loading:** Determine whether the pressure applied is **uniform/hydrostatic** (use $K$) or **uniaxial** (use $E$). Bulk modulus strictly applies to hydrostatic pressure. - **Step 2 — Compute volumetric strain:** $\epsilon_v = \Delta V / V$. For small deformations, this equals the sum of principal strains: $\epsilon_v = \epsilon_x + \epsilon_y + \epsilon_z$. - **Step 3 — Apply the definition:** $K = -\Delta p / \epsilon_v$. Solve for the unknown. ## 2.5 Real-World Utility In naval architecture and ocean engineering: - **Speed of sound in water** (≈ 1500 m/s) is governed by $c = \sqrt{K/\rho}$ — used in **SONAR** and underwater acoustic positioning. - **Cavitation analysis** in propellers requires the bulk modulus of the working fluid (seawater) to predict pressure transients. - **Submerged body stability** depends on how density (and hence buoyancy) varies with depth, which is governed by $K$. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bulk Modulus from First Principles (Definition) We start with a fluid element of volume $V$ and density $\rho$ subjected to a small pressure increase $dp$. **Step 1:** Mass conservation. The mass $m$ of the element is constant, so: $$m = \rho V = \text{constant}$$ **Step 2:** Take the differential: $$d(\rho V) = \rho \, dV + V \, d\rho = 0$$ **Step 3:** Rearrange to express $dV$ in terms of $d\rho$: $$\rho \, dV = -V \, d\rho \quad \Longrightarrow \quad dV = -\frac{V}{\rho} d\rho$$ **Step 4:** Substitute into the definition $K = -V \dfrac{dp}{dV}$: $$K = -V \cdot \frac{dp}{dV} = -V \cdot \frac{dp}{-\dfrac{V}{\rho} d\rho} = \rho \frac{dp}{d\rho}$$ $$\boxed{K = \rho \frac{dp}{d\rho}}$$ This is the **density-form** of bulk modulus and is widely used in compressible flow and oceanography. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Relation $K = \dfrac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)}$ This is the **most-tested formula** in KTU exams. We derive it from the generalized Hooke's law for isotropic linear elastic materials. **Step 1:** Write the three principal stress–strain relations (with hydrostatic stress $\sigma_h = \sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z$): $$\epsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right] = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_h - \nu(2\sigma_h)\right] = \frac{\sigma_h}{E}(1 - 2\nu)$$ By symmetry, $\epsilon_y = \epsilon_x$ and $\epsilon_z = \epsilon_x$. **Step 2:** Compute the volumetric strain (dilatation): $$\epsilon_v = \epsilon_x + \epsilon_y + \epsilon_z = 3 \cdot \frac{\sigma_h}{E}(1 - 2\nu) = \frac{3\sigma_h(1 - 2\nu)}{E}$$ **Step 3:** Apply the definition of bulk modulus. For hydrostatic loading, $\sigma_h = -p$ (negative since compressive), so: $$K = -\frac{dp}{\epsilon_v/V} = \frac{|p|}{\epsilon_v} = \frac{\sigma_h}{\epsilon_v}$$ **Step 4:** Substitute: $$K = \frac{\sigma_h}{\dfrac{3\sigma_h(1 - 2\nu)}{E}} = \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)}$$ $$\boxed{K = \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)}}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Expressing $E$ and $\nu$ in terms of $K$ and $G$ **Step 1:** Start with the relation $E = 2G(1+\nu)$. **Step 2:** From $K = \dfrac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$, solve for $E$: $$E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$$ **Step 3:** Equate the two expressions for $E$: $$2G(1+\nu) = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$$ **Step 4:** Solve for $\nu$ in terms of $K$ and $G$: $$2G + 2G\nu = 3K - 6K\nu$$ $$2G\nu + 6K\nu = 3K - 2G$$ $$\nu(2G + 6K) = 3K - 2G$$ $$\boxed{\nu = \frac{3K - 2G}{2G + 6K} = \frac{3K - 2G}{2(K + 3G)}}$$ **Step 5:** Solve for $E$ in terms of $K$ and $G$: $$E = 3K\left(1 - 2 \cdot \frac{3K - 2G}{2G + 6K}\right) = 3K \cdot \frac{(2G + 6K) - 2(3K - 2G)}{2G + 6K}$$ $$E = 3K \cdot \frac{2G + 6K - 6K + 4G}{2G + 6K} = 3K \cdot \frac{6G}{2G + 6K} = \frac{18KG}{2G + 6K} = \frac{9KG}{G + 3K}$$ $$\boxed{E = \frac{9KG}{G + 3K}}$$ ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Acoustic Speed in a Fluid The speed of sound in a fluid is derived from Newton's second law applied to a small fluid element and the adiabatic compressibility. **Step 1:** Consider a thin fluid slab of thickness $dx$, density $\rho$, and cross-section $A$ in a sound wave. **Step 2:** Pressure change $\Delta p$ across the slab produces a net force $A \cdot \Delta p$ accelerating the mass $\rho A dx$: $$\rho A dx \cdot \frac{\partial^u}{\partial t^2} = -A \, \Delta p \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \rho \frac{\partial^u}{\partial t^2} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}$$ **Step 3:** Volume change per unit volume = $\partial u / \partial x$, so: $$\Delta p = -K \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}$$ **Step 4:** Differentiate the second equation with respect to $x$ and substitute: $$\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2} = \frac{K}{\rho} \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}$$ This is the **classical wave equation**, from which: $$\boxed{c = \sqrt{\frac{K}{\rho}}}$$ ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem:** A steel cube of side 100 mm is subjected to a hydrostatic pressure of 200 MPa. The cube's volume decreases by 12 mm³. Calculate: 1. The volumetric strain. 2. The bulk modulus of the steel. **Given:** - $L = 100$ mm, so $V = L^3 = 10^6$ mm³ - $p = 200$ MPa = $200$ N/mm² - $\Delta V = -12$ mm³ (decrease) **Solution:** **Part (1):** Volumetric strain: $$\epsilon_v = \frac{\Delta V}{V} = \frac{-12}{10^6} = -1.2 \times 10^{-5}$$ **Part (2):** Bulk modulus: $$K = -\frac{p}{\epsilon_v} = -\frac{200}{-1.2 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{200}{1.2 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.667 \times 10^{7} \text{ N/mm}^2$$ $$\boxed{K \approx 16.67 \text{ GPa} = 16{,}670 \text{ MPa}}$$ > [!NOTE] > This value is a bit lower than typical structural steel (160 GPa Young's modulus with $\nu \approx 0.29$ gives $K \approx 164$ GPa). The discrepancy indicates the sample is perhaps a softer alloy or the data are illustrative. ## 3.6 The Analytical Matrix — Effect of Material Properties The following table summarizes how bulk modulus $K$ behaves with respect to its independent variables: | Independent Variable | Effect on $K$ | Physical Reasoning | |---|---|---| | Young's modulus $E$ ↑ | $K$ ↑ proportionally | Stiffer atomic bonds resist compression more | | Poisson's ratio $\nu$ ↑ (toward 0.5) | $K$ ↑ dramatically (singularity at $\nu = 0.5$) | Material becomes incompressible | | Temperature $T$ ↑ | $K$ ↓ | Thermal agitation weakens bond stiffness | | Pressure $p$ ↑ (for fluids) | $K$ ↑ | Molecules are pushed closer, harder to compress further | | Salinity $S$ ↑ (seawater) | $K$ ↑ | Dissolved ions add electrostatic repulsion | | Phase change (solid → liquid) | $K$ typically ↓ | Liquids generally more compressible than solids | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Hydrostatic Pressure on a Cubic Element ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Original Cube
Side L, Volume V] -->|Pressure increase dp| B[Compressed Cube
Side L-dL, Volume V-dV] B --> C[Volumetric strain: eps_v = dV / V] C --> D[Bulk Modulus: K = -dp / eps_v] D --> E{Compare with
Young's Modulus E?} E -->|Yes, isotropic| F[K = E / 3(1 - 2 nu)] E -->|No, with shear G| G[K = 2G(1+nu) / 3(1-2nu)] F --> H[Final Material Property K] G --> H ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Bulk Modulus in Naval Systems ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Hydrostatic Pressure p] --> B[Compressible Medium
Water, Air, Oil, Steel] B --> C[Volumetric Response dV] C --> D[Bulk Modulus K] D --> E[Acoustic Speed c = sqrt K/rho] D --> F[Buoyancy Variation with Depth] D --> G[Hull Pressure Loading] E --> H[SONAR
Underwater Acoustics] F --> I[Submarine Design] G --> J[Structural Strength] H --> K[Naval Operational Capability] I --> K J --> K ``` ## 4.3 The Three Elastic Moduli Interrelation ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Inputs E[Young's Modulus E] G[Shear Modulus G] K[Bulk Modulus K] NU[Poisson's Ratio nu] end E --> R1[E = 3K 1 - 2 nu] E --> R2[E = 2G 1 + nu] G --> R3[G = 3KE / 9K - E] K --> R4[K = E / 3 1 - 2 nu] K --> R5[K = 2G 1 + nu / 3 1 - 2 nu] NU --> R1 NU --> R2 NU --> R4 NU --> R5 R1 --> OUTPUT[Two Independent Constants
Define Isotropic Material] R2 --> OUTPUT R3 --> OUTPUT R4 --> OUTPUT R5 --> OUTPUT ``` ## 4.4 Schematic: Pressure–Volume Response Curve ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Low Pressure p1] --> V1[Volume V1] P2[Medium Pressure p2] --> V2[Volume V2] P3[High Pressure p3] --> V3[Volume V3] V1 --> V2 V2 --> V3 V2 -.-> SLOPE[Tangent Slope = -K at each point] V1 -.-> K1[High K: Nearly Vertical Slope] V3 -.-> K2[Lower K at Very High p: Stiffer fluids possible] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define bulk modulus and state its SI unit.
