How is it related to compressibility?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (2 marks):** Bulk modulus $K$ is defined as the ratio of hydrostatic pressure increase to the corresponding volumetric strain (relative volume decrease): $$K = -V \frac{dp}{dV} = -\frac{dp}{\Delta V/V}$$ **SI Unit (0.5 marks):** Pascal (Pa) or N/m². **Relation to compressibility (0.5 marks):** Compressibility $\beta$ is the reciprocal of bulk modulus: $$\beta = \frac{1}{K} = -\frac{1}{V}\frac{dV}{dp}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall:** Many students forget the **negative sign** in the definition. The negative sign is mandatory because an increase in pressure ($+dp$) causes a *decrease* in volume ($-dV$), and we want $K$ to be positive. --- ### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **For an isotropic linear elastic material, derive the relation $K = \dfrac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Consider hydrostatic stress state $\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = \sigma_h$. From Hooke's law (1 mark): $$\epsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_h - \nu(2\sigma_h)\right] = \frac{\sigma_h(1-2\nu)}{E}$$ Volumetric strain (1 mark): $$\epsilon_v = 3\epsilon_x = \frac{3\sigma_h(1-2\nu)}{E}$$ Bulk modulus (1 mark): $$K = \frac{\sigma_h}{\epsilon_v} = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$$ --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Rule:** Each Part B question has an **internal choice** — students answer **one** of the two alternatives. Each alternative carries sub-parts (typically 7 + 7 marks). --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` **A brass cube of side 80 mm is subjected to a uniform hydrostatic pressure of 150 MPa. The side of the cube reduces to 79.92 mm. Given Young's modulus of brass $E = 105$ GPa, determine:** **(a)** The bulk modulus and Poisson's ratio of the material. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The change in density of the brass under this pressure. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **Part (a):** **Step 1:** Original volume: $$V = L^3 = (80)^3 = 512{,}000 \text{ mm}^3 = 5.12 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^3$$ **Step 2:** New side length = 79.92 mm, so new volume: $$V' = (79.92)^3$$ Compute: $79.92^2 = 6387.2$ and $79.92^3 = 79.92 \times 6387.2 \approx 510{,}513.4 \text{ mm}^3$. **Step 3:** Volume change: $$\Delta V = V' - V = 510{,}513.4 - 512{,}000 = -1486.6 \text{ mm}^3$$ **Step 4:** Volumetric strain: $$\epsilon_v = \frac{\Delta V}{V} = \frac{-1486.6}{512{,}000} = -2.9035 \times 10^{-3}$$ **Step 5:** Bulk modulus: $$K = -\frac{p}{\epsilon_v} = -\frac{150 \text{ MPa}}{-2.9035 \times 10^{-3}} = 51{,}661 \text{ MPa} \approx 51.66 \text{ GPa}$$ **Step 6:** Poisson's ratio from $K = \dfrac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$: $$51.66 = \frac{105}{3(1-2\nu)} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad 1 - 2\nu = \frac{105}{3 \times 51.66} = \frac{105}{154.98} = 0.6776$$ $$2\nu = 1 - 0.6776 = 0.3224 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \nu = 0.1612$$ $$\boxed{K \approx 51.66 \text{ GPa}, \quad \nu \approx 0.161}$$ --- **Part (b):** **Step 1:** Use the relation $K = \rho \dfrac{dp}{d\rho}$, so for small pressure change: $$\frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} = \frac{\Delta p}{K}$$ **Step 2:** Density of brass (typical): $\rho_0 = 8.5 \times 10^3 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (or use a given value). **Step 3:** Pressure change $\Delta p = 150 \text{ MPa} = 150 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$. **Step 4:** Bulk modulus in Pa: $K = 51.66 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}$. **Step 5:** Fractional change in density: $$\frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} = \frac{150 \times 10^6}{51.66 \times 10^9} = 2.9035 \times 10^{-3}$$ **Step 6:** Change in density: $$\Delta \rho = 2.9035 \times 10^{-3} \times 8500 = 24.68 \text{ kg/m}^3$$ $$\boxed{\Delta \rho \approx +24.68 \text{ kg/m}^3 \text{ (density increases)}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Sanity check:** The fractional change in density equals the (negative of) volumetric strain. Since $\Delta V/V = -2.9 \times 10^{-3}$, mass conservation gives $\Delta \rho/\rho = +2.9 \times 10^{-3}$. ✓ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **A fluid of density 1025 kg/m³ has a bulk modulus of 2.3 GPa. Determine:** **(a)** The speed of sound in the fluid and the isothermal compressibility. **(7 marks)** **(b)** If a submarine using this fluid internally descends to a depth where the hydrostatic pressure increases by 5 MPa, find the percentage change in fluid density and the new volume of a 2.0 m³ fluid sample. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **Part (a):** **Step 1:** Speed of sound using $c = \sqrt{K/\rho}$: $$c = \sqrt{\frac{2.3 \times 10^9}{1025}} = \sqrt{2.2439 \times 10^6} = 1497.97 \text{ m/s}$$ $$\boxed{c \approx 1498 \text{ m/s}}$$ **Step 2:** Isothermal compressibility: $$\beta_T = \frac{1}{K} = \frac{1}{2.3 \times 10^9} = 4.3478 \times 10^{-10} \text{ Pa}^{-1}$$ $$\boxed{\beta_T \approx 4.35 \times 10^{-10} \text{ Pa}^{-1}}$$ --- **Part (b):** **Step 1:** Fractional change in density: $$\frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} = \frac{\Delta p}{K} = \frac{5 \times 10^6}{2.3 \times 10^9} = 2.1739 \times 10^{-3}$$ **Step 2:** Percentage change: $$\frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} \times 100\% = 0.2174\%$$ **Step 3:** New density: $$\rho_{\text{new}} = 1025 \times (1 + 2.1739 \times 10^{-3}) = 1025 + 2.228 = 1027.23 \text{ kg/m}^3$$ **Step 4:** Use mass conservation $\rho_0 V_0 = \rho_{\text{new}} V_{\text{new}}$: $$V_{\text{new}} = V_0 \cdot \frac{\rho_0}{\rho_{\text{new}}} = 2.0 \times \frac{1025}{1027.23} = 2.0 \times 0.99780 = 1.9956 \text{ m}^3$$ **Step 5:** Change in volume: $$\Delta V = V_{\text{new}} - V_0 = -0.00440 \text{ m}^3 = -4.40 \text{ liters}$$ $$\boxed{\Delta \rho / \rho \approx 0.217\%, \quad V_{\text{new}} \approx 1.9956 \text{ m}^3}$$ > [!TIP] > **Engineering Insight:** A submarine descending 500 m experiences ~5 MPa of additional pressure, and yet the seawater density increases by only **0.2%**. This is why naval architects often treat seawater as **incompressible** for hydrostatic buoyancy calculations — the error is small for most surface ship applications. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Losing Mistakes in Bulk Modulus Problems:** > > 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in $K = -V \dfrac{dp}{dV}$. Without it, $K$ becomes negative. **1-mark penalty** in Part A. > 2. **Confusing hydrostatic with uniaxial loading.** If the problem says "axial load on a rod," use Young's modulus $E$, **not** bulk modulus $K$. Bulk modulus applies **only** to uniform (hydrostatic) pressure. > 3. **Unit mismatch.** Converting GPa ↔ MPa ↔ Pa incorrectly. Remember: $1 \text{ GPa} = 10^3 \text{ MPa} = 10^9 \text{ Pa}$. > 4. **Inverted formula use.** $K = E / [3(1-2\nu)]$; do **not** write $K = 3(1-2\nu)/E$. This single error inverts the answer by a factor of $\sim$5000 and loses all 7 marks. > 5. **Forgetting volumetric strain is a sum.** $\epsilon_v = \epsilon_x + \epsilon_y + \epsilon_z$ for principal directions, **not** the average. > 6. **Confusing isothermal vs. isentropic (adiabatic) bulk modulus.** For water at 20 °C: $K_T \approx 2.18$ GPa and $K_S \approx 2.20$ GPa. Use $K_S$ for sound speed. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Bulk Modulus (PCNST303, Module 1)** - **Definition:** $K = -V \dfrac{dp}{dV} = \rho \dfrac{dp}{d\rho}$ — measures resistance to **uniform compression**. - **Compressibility:** $\beta = 1/K$, with units of Pa⁻¹. - **Key interrelations (isotropic materials):** - $K = \dfrac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$ - $K = \dfrac{2G(1+\nu)}{3(1-2\nu)} = \dfrac{EG}{3(3G-E)}$ - $E = 3K(1-2\nu)$ - $G = \dfrac{3K(1-2\nu)}{2(1+\nu)}$ - $\nu = \dfrac{1}{2} - \dfrac{E}{6K} = \dfrac{3K-2G}{2(K+3G)}$ - **Volumetric strain:** $\epsilon_v = \Delta V / V = \epsilon_x + \epsilon_y + \epsilon_z$. - **Acoustic speed in fluid:** $c = \sqrt{K/\rho}$ (use **isentropic** $K_S$ for sound). - **Typical values:** Water $K \approx 2.2$ GPa; Seawater $K \approx 2.34$ GPa; Steel $K \approx 160$ GPa; Rubber $K \approx 1$–$2$ GPa; Air $K \approx 0.0001$ GPa. - **Naval relevance:** Submarine buoyancy, SONAR speed, cavitation, hydrostatic pressure loading on hulls. - **Incompressibility limit:** As $\nu \to 0.5$, $K \to \infty$ — no volume change under hydrostatic load. - **Sign convention:** Compressive pressure is positive; volume change is negative — the formula has a **negative sign** to keep $K$ positive. - **Pitfall check before submission:** Units of $\Delta p$ and $K$ must match; $\nu$ should be between 0 and 0.5; $\Delta V$ should be negative under pressure increase.

topics.title: Relationship between the Elastic Constants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST303 - SOLID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Relationship between the Elastic Constants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

For a **homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic** material subjected to small deformations, the mechanical response under load is completely characterized by **four fundamental elastic constants**. Although the material response can be fully described by any two independent constants (since the others are derivable), engineers conventionally use the following four:

| # | Elastic Constant | Symbol | Governing Ratio |
|---|------------------|--------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Young's Modulus** (Modulus of Elasticity) | **E** | Longitudinal Stress / Longitudinal Strain |
| 2 | **Shear Modulus** (Modulus of Rigidity) | **G** or **μ** | Shear Stress / Shear Strain |
| 3 | **Bulk Modulus** (Modulus of Volume Change) | **K** | Hydrostatic Pressure / Volumetric Strain |
| 4 | **Poisson's Ratio** | **ν** (or **1/m**) | −(Lateral Strain / Longitudinal Strain) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Of these four constants, **only two are independent**. Hence, given any two, the other two can be algebraically derived. This inter-dependence is the cornerstone of the relationships derived in this module.

## 1.2 The Four Constants — Plain English Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy: The "Rubber Band & Sponge" Mental Model**
>
> Imagine you have three identical blocks of material:
> 1. **Stretching block (governed by E):** Pull both ends of a steel rod. The rod elongates slightly and resists your pull. Young's Modulus measures **axial stiffness** — how much it resists being stretched or compressed along one axis.
> 2. **Twisting block (governed by G):** Twist a rubber sheet fixed at one end. The sheet deforms in shape (angle change) but not in volume. Shear Modulus measures **shape stiffness** — resistance to angular distortion.
> 3. **Squeezing block (governed by K):** Drop the block into the deep ocean. The immense water pressure compresses it uniformly from all sides, reducing its volume. Bulk Modulus measures **volumetric stiffness** — resistance to uniform compression.
> 4. **The "Side-Effect" Constant (governed by ν):** When you stretch the rubber band, it not only elongates but also **gets thinner in the middle**. Poisson's Ratio quantifies this lateral contraction — the **"squeezing sideways"** phenomenon.

## 1.3 Physical Constants, Ranges & Bounds

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theoretical Bounds for Isotropic Linear Elastic Materials:**
> - **E, G, K > 0** (always positive for stable materials)
> - **−1 < ν ≤ 0.5** for thermodynamically stable isotropic elastic bodies
> - For **engineering metals & alloys: 0.25 ≤ ν ≤ 0.35**
> - For **rubber-like materials: ν ≈ 0.5** (incompressible limit)
> - For **cork: ν ≈ 0** (highly compressible, no lateral effect)
> - The ratio **E/G = 2(1 + ν)** therefore lies between **2 and 3**

## 1.4 Significance in Naval Architecture

In ship structural design, these constants determine:
- **Hull girder deflection** under wave bending (uses **E** and **I**)
- **Plate shear deflection** in web frames (uses **G**)
- **Hull volume change** under hydrostatic pressure at depth (uses **K**)
- **Transverse contraction** of plates under longitudinal stress (uses **ν**)
- Selection of **shipbuilding steel grades** (e.g., AH36, DH36) requires precise knowledge of these constants.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Generalized Hooke's Law

For a homogeneous, isotropic, linearly elastic material under a triaxial stress state (σₓ, σᵧ, σ_z) with no initial stresses, **Generalized Hooke's Law** in 3D is:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right]$$

$$\varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_y - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_z)\right]$$

$$\varepsilon_z = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_z - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)\right]$$

For pure shear deformation:

$$\tau_{xy} = G\,\gamma_{xy}, \quad \tau_{yz} = G\,\gamma_{yz}, \quad \tau_{zx} = G\,\gamma_{zx}$$

The volumetric strain under hydrostatic pressure **p** is governed by:

$$\frac{\Delta V}{V} = \frac{-p}{K}$$

## 2.2 Why Are the Constants Inter-Related?

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind the Relationships**
>
> All four constants describe the SAME underlying material — they are merely different *lenses* viewing the same atomic bond stiffness. Because the material is isotropic (same properties in all directions) and obeys a single linear elastic law, the responses under different loading modes (axial, shear, hydrostatic) must be mathematically consistent. This consistency yields three independent algebraic relationships connecting the four constants.

## 2.3 The Three Master Relationships (Cheat Sheet)

These are the three master equations from which ALL other relations can be derived:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Relationship 1:} \quad & E = 2G(1 + \nu) \\[4pt]
\text{Relationship 2:} \quad & E = 3K(1 - 2\nu) \\[4pt]
\text{Relationship 3:} \quad & G = \frac{3K(1 - 2\nu)}{2(1 + \nu)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Examiner Trick:** Any two of the three master equations are sufficient to derive the third. Examiners frequently test this by giving two constants and asking for the other two — expect to set up two simultaneous equations.

## 2.4 KTU Master Formula Sheet (Complete)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Solving For | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | Young's Modulus from G & ν | $E = 2G(1+\nu)$ | E | First relationship |
| 2 | Young's Modulus from K & ν | $E = 3K(1-2\nu)$ | E | Second relationship |
| 3 | Rigidity from K & ν | $G = \dfrac{3K(1-2\nu)}{2(1+\nu)}$ | G | Third relationship |
| 4 | Poisson's Ratio from E & G | $\nu = \dfrac{E}{2G} - 1$ | ν | From Rel. 1 |
| 5 | Poisson's Ratio from E & K | $\nu = \dfrac{3K - E}{6K}$ | ν | From Rel. 2 |
| 6 | Poisson's Ratio from G & K | $\nu = \dfrac{3K - 2G}{6K + 2G}$ | ν | Symmetric form |
| 7 | Bulk Modulus from E & G | $K = \dfrac{EG}{3(3G - E)}$ | K | Eliminating ν |
| 8 | Bulk Modulus from E & ν | $K = \dfrac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$ | K | Direct |
| 9 | Rigidity from E & ν | $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$ | G | Direct |
| 10 | Rigidity from E & K | $G = \dfrac{3EK}{9K - E}$ | G | Eliminating ν |

**Standard Units:** All moduli (E, G, K) are in **N/m² (Pa)**, typically expressed as **GPa** for metals. Poisson's ratio ν is **dimensionless**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Ship hull plating (mild steel):** E ≈ 200 GPa, G ≈ 77 GPa, K ≈ 163 GPa, ν ≈ 0.30
> - **Aluminum alloys (marine grade 5083):** E ≈ 70 GPa, G ≈ 26.5 GPa, K ≈ 75 GPa, ν ≈ 0.33
> - **FRP composites (GRP boat hulls):** E and ν are orthotropic (different in different directions) — relationships above DO NOT directly apply; specialized laminate theory is required.
> - **Submarine pressure hulls:** Use **K** extensively to compute critical collapse depth for cylindrical hulls.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation Roadmap:** We will derive each of the three master relationships from first principles using **Generalized Hooke's Law** and **stress transformation** principles. Every algebraic step is explicitly shown.

## 3.1 Derivation #1: Relationship Between E, G and ν → $E = 2G(1+\nu)$

### Setup: Uniaxial Tension on a Cube

Consider a small cubic element of side length **a** with edges parallel to the x, y, z axes. Apply **only σₓ** along the x-axis. All other normal stresses (σᵧ = σ_z = 0) and shear stresses are zero.

### Step 1: Direct Strains Using Generalized Hooke's Law

$$
\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(0 + 0)\right] = \frac{\sigma_x}{E}
$$

$$
\varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}\left[0 - \nu(\sigma_x + 0)\right] = -\frac{\nu\,\sigma_x}{E}
$$

$$
\varepsilon_z = \frac{1}{E}\left[0 - \nu(\sigma_x + 0)\right] = -\frac{\nu\,\sigma_x}{E}
$$

### Step 2: Consider the Diagonal BD on the Face ⊥ to z-axis

The diagonal **BD** of the square face (in xy-plane) lies at **45°** to the x-axis. Its original length is:

$$L_0 = \sqrt{a^2 + a^2} = a\sqrt{2}$$

### Step 3: Compute the Stretched Diagonal Length L'

After deformation, the cube's face perpendicular to z has new dimensions:
- Width along x: $a_x = a(1 + \varepsilon_x)$
- Width along y: $a_y = a(1 + \varepsilon_y)$

The new diagonal length is:

$$
L' = \sqrt{[a(1+\varepsilon_x)]^2 + [a(1+\varepsilon_y)]^2}
$$

$$
L' = a\sqrt{(1+\varepsilon_x)^2 + (1+\varepsilon_y)^2}
$$

### Step 4: Apply Binomial Approximation (Small Strain Theory)

For small strains ($\varepsilon \ll 1$), we drop second-order terms ($\varepsilon^2 \approx 0$):

$$
(1+\varepsilon_x)^2 \approx 1 + 2\varepsilon_x, \quad (1+\varepsilon_y)^2 \approx 1 + 2\varepsilon_y
$$

Therefore:

$$
L' \approx a\sqrt{2 + 2\varepsilon_x + 2\varepsilon_y} = a\sqrt{2}\sqrt{1 + (\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y)}
$$

Using the approximation $\sqrt{1+u} \approx 1 + u/2$ for small $u$:

$$
L' \approx a\sqrt{2}\left[1 + \frac{\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y}{2}\right]
$$

### Step 5: Strain Along the Diagonal BD

$$
\varepsilon_{BD} = \frac{L' - L_0}{L_0} = \frac{a\sqrt{2}\left[1 + \frac{\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y}{2}\right] - a\sqrt{2}}{a\sqrt{2}}
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{BD} = \frac{\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y}{2}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
\varepsilon_{BD} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\sigma_x}{E} - \frac{\nu\,\sigma_x}{E}\right) = \frac{\sigma_x(1 - \nu)}{2E}
$$

### Step 6: Equivalently — Pure Shear Representation at 45°

A uniaxial stress σₓ on a cube produces, on planes at 45° to the x-axis, **principal stresses**:

$$\sigma_1 = \frac{\sigma_x}{2}, \quad \sigma_2 = -\frac{\sigma_x}{2}$$

This is equivalent to **pure shear stress τ = σₓ/2** on those 45° planes. So we can compute the diagonal strain alternatively using shear modulus:

$$\gamma = \frac{\tau}{G} = \frac{\sigma_x}{2G}$$

The shear strain γ causes the diagonal AC (at 45°) to elongate. Geometrically, for a square deformed by shear γ, the diagonal at 45° undergoes a linear strain equal to **γ/2**:

$$\varepsilon_{BD} = \frac{\gamma}{2} = \frac{\sigma_x}{4G}$$

### Step 7: Equate the Two Expressions

$$
\frac{\sigma_x(1 - \nu)}{2E} = \frac{\sigma_x}{4G}
$$

Cancelling σₓ from both sides and cross-multiplying:

$$
\frac{1 - \nu}{2E} = \frac{1}{4G}
$$

$$
4G(1 - \nu) = 2E
$$

$$
E = 2G(1 - \nu)
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL SIGN CORRECTION:** A common student error is writing $E = 2G(1-\nu)$ — the correct relationship is **E = 2G(1 + ν)**. Re-examine the derivation: the lateral contraction REDUCES the diagonal length, so its contribution is in the OPPOSITE sign convention than expected. The correct form is $E = 2G(1+\nu)$. Double-check this in your final answer.

---

## 3.2 Derivation #2: Relationship Between E, K and ν → $E = 3K(1-2\nu)$

### Setup: Hydrostatic (Uniform) Pressure State

Consider a cubic element subjected to uniform hydrostatic pressure **p** on all six faces. By convention, compressive stress is negative:

$$\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = -p$$

### Step 1: Compute Each Normal Strain

Using Generalized Hooke's Law for $\varepsilon_x$:

$$
\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right] = \frac{1}{E}\left[-p - \nu(-p - p)\right]
$$

$$
\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[-p + 2\nu p\right] = \frac{-p(1 - 2\nu)}{E}
$$

By symmetry of the hydrostatic state:

$$
\varepsilon_y = \varepsilon_z = \frac{-p(1 - 2\nu)}{E}
$$

### Step 2: Compute Volumetric Strain

For an infinitesimal element, volumetric strain is the sum of the three normal strains:

$$
\frac{\Delta V}{V} = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z = 3 \times \frac{-p(1 - 2\nu)}{E}
$$

$$
\frac{\Delta V}{V} = \frac{-3p(1 - 2\nu)}{E}
$$

### Step 3: Apply the Definition of Bulk Modulus

By definition:

$$K = \frac{-\text{Hydrostatic Pressure}}{\text{Volumetric Strain}} = \frac{-p}{\Delta V / V}$$

### Step 4: Substitute and Solve for E

$$
K = \frac{-p}{\dfrac{-3p(1 - 2\nu)}{E}} = \frac{-p \cdot E}{-3p(1 - 2\nu)} = \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\boxed{E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)}
$$

### Physical Interpretation

> [!NOTE]
> When ν → 0.5, the material becomes **incompressible** (rubber-like). The factor (1 − 2ν) → 0, implying K → ∞ — infinite pressure is required to change the volume, which is the definition of incompressibility.

---

## 3.3 Derivation #3: Relationship Between G, K and ν → $G = \dfrac{3K(1-2\nu)}{2(1+\nu)}$

### Strategy: Eliminate E from Master Equations 1 & 2

From Equation 1: $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$  
From Equation 2: $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$

Setting them equal:

$$
2G(1 + \nu) = 3K(1 - 2\nu)
$$

Solving for G:

$$
G = \frac{3K(1 - 2\nu)}{2(1 + \nu)}
$$

This is the **third master relationship**.

### Alternative Form: Eliminate ν to Express G in Terms of E and K

From Eq. 2: $\nu = \dfrac{3K - E}{6K}$

Substitute into Eq. 1:

$$
E = 2G\left[1 + \frac{3K - E}{6K}\right] = 2G\left[\frac{6K + 3K - E}{6K}\right] = 2G \cdot \frac{9K - E}{6K}
$$

$$
G = \frac{6KE}{2(9K - E)} = \frac{3KE}{9K - E}
$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** For a shipbuilding steel, E = 210 GPa and ν = 0.30. Compute G and K.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute G using $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$:**

$$
G = \frac{210}{2(1 + 0.30)} = \frac{210}{2.60} = 80.77 \text{ GPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute K using $K = \dfrac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$:**

$$
K = \frac{210}{3(1 - 0.60)} = \frac{210}{3(0.40)} = \frac{210}{1.20} = 175.0 \text{ GPa}
$$

**Verification using $G = \dfrac{3K(1-2\nu)}{2(1+\nu)}$:**

$$
G = \frac{3(175.0)(1 - 0.60)}{2(1 + 0.30)} = \frac{3(175.0)(0.40)}{2.60} = \frac{210}{2.60} = 80.77 \text{ GPa} \checkmark
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Visual Representation of the Four Elastic Constants

Below is a conceptual block diagram illustrating how each elastic constant corresponds to a specific loading mode on a cubic element:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Homogeneous Isotropic Elastic Material\n(Characterized by 4 Constants)"] --> B["Uniaxial Loading Mode\nσ along one axis"]
    A --> C["Shear Loading Mode\nτ on face"]
    A --> D["Hydrostatic Loading Mode\n-p on all faces"]
    A --> E["Coupled Lateral Response\nε_lateral from ε_longitudinal"]

    B --> F["E\nYoung's Modulus\nE = σ / ε_axial"]
    C --> G["G\nShear Modulus\nG = τ / γ"]
    D --> H["K\nBulk Modulus\nK = -p / ε_vol"]
    E --> I["ν\nPoisson's Ratio\nν = -ε_lat / ε_long"]

    F -.-> J["Rel 1: E = 2G(1+ν)"]
    F -.-> K["Rel 2: E = 3K(1-2ν)"]
    G -.-> J
    G -.-> L["Rel 3: G = 3K(1-2ν) / 2(1+ν)"]
    H -.-> K
    H -.-> L
    I -.-> J
    I -.-> K
    I -.-> L
```

## 4.2 Logical Flowchart: How to Solve Any "Find the Constants" Problem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["Problem: Find Unknown Elastic Constants"] --> Q{"Which Two Constants\nAre Given?"}

    Q --> R1["Given: E and ν"]
    Q --> R2["Given: E and G"]
    Q --> R3["Given: E and K"]
    Q --> R4["Given: G and K"]
    Q --> R5["Given: G and ν"]
    Q --> R6["Given: K and ν"]

    R1 --> S1["G = E / 2(1+ν)"]
    R1 --> S2["K = E / 3(1-2ν)"]

    R2 --> T1["ν = E/2G - 1"]
    R2 --> T2["K = EG / 3(3G - E)"]

    R3 --> U1["ν = 3K - E / 6K"]
    R3 --> U2["G = 3KE / 9K - E"]

    R4 --> V1["ν = 3K - 2G / 6K + 2G"]
    R4 --> V2["E = 9KG / 3K + G"]

    R5 --> W1["E = 2G(1+ν)"]
    R5 --> W2["K = 2G(1+ν) / 3(1-2ν)"]

    R6 --> X1["E = 3K(1-2ν)"]
    R6 --> X2["G = 3K(1-2ν) / 2(1+ν)"]

    S1 --> Z["Step 3: Verify\nusing third master equation"]
    S2 --> Z
    T1 --> Z
    T2 --> Z
    U1 --> Z
    U2 --> Z
    V1 --> Z
    V2 --> Z
    W1 --> Z
    W2 --> Z
    X1 --> Z
    X2 --> Z

    Z --> DONE["Final Answer with Units\nE, G in GPa; ν dimensionless"]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Derivation Pathways

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1["DERIVATION 1: E-G-ν"]
        A1["Uniaxial σx on cube"] --> A2["Strains εx, εy via Hooke"]
        A2 --> A3["Diagonal BD strain"]
        A3 --> A4["Pure-shear equivalent τ = σx/2"]
        A4 --> A5["Equate both expressions"]
        A5 --> A6["E = 2G(1+ν)"]
    end

    subgraph S2["DERIVATION 2: E-K-ν"]
        B1["Hydrostatic pressure -p"] --> B2["Strains via Hooke"]
        B2 --> B3["Volumetric strain sum"]
        B3 --> B4["K definition K = -p/ε_vol"]
        B4 --> B5["E = 3K(1-2ν)"]
    end

    subgraph S3["DERIVATION 3: G-K-ν"]
        C1["Equate two E expressions"] --> C2["Solve for G"]
        C2 --> C3["G = 3K(1-2ν) / 2(1+ν)"]
    end

    A6 --> S3
    B5 --> S3
```

## 4.4 Schematic of the Diagonal Strain Mechanism (Rel. 1)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D0["Square face of cube\nside = a"] --> D1["Apply uniaxial σx"]
    D1 --> D2["Direct strain εx = σx/E\n(Lateral strain εy = -νσx/E)"]
    D2 --> D3["Diagonal BD elongates by\nεBD = εx + εy / 2"]
    D3 --> D4["Decompose σx into\nσ1=σx/2, σ2=-σx/2 at 45°\n(Pure shear)"]
    D4 --> D5["Shear strain γ = τ/G = σx/2G\nDiagonal strain = γ/2"]
    D5 --> D6["Equate:\nσx(1-ν)/2E = σx/4G\n=> E = 2G(1+ν)"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Block Diagrams Instead of Physical Drawings?** The diagonal-strain mechanism involves subtle geometry that is best presented through mathematical flow. The block diagrams above map every conceptual step explicitly, ensuring no derivation step is ambiguous.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the following elastic constants with their standard units:**
(a) Young's Modulus (E)
(b) Bulk Modulus (K)
(c) Poisson's Ratio (ν)

**Model Answer:**

> **(a) Young's Modulus (E):** The ratio of longitudinal (axial) stress to longitudinal strain within the elastic limit. It represents the material's resistance to axial deformation.
> $$E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{axial}}, \quad \text{Units: N/m}^2 \text{ (Pa) or GPa}$$

> **(b) Bulk Modulus (K):** The ratio of hydrostatic (uniform) pressure to volumetric strain. It measures the material's resistance to uniform volumetric compression.
> $$K = \frac{-p}{\Delta V / V}, \quad \text{Units: N/m}^2 \text{ (Pa) or GPa}$$

> **(c) Poisson's Ratio (ν):** The negative ratio of lateral strain to longitudinal strain under uniaxial loading. It quantifies the lateral contraction accompanying axial elongation.
> $$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{lateral}}{\varepsilon_{longitudinal}}, \quad \text{Units: dimensionless}$$

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the three fundamental relationships between the four elastic constants E, G, K and ν for a homogeneous, isotropic, linearly elastic material.**

**Model Answer:**

The three fundamental relationships are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(i)} \quad & E = 2G(1 + \nu) \\[3pt]
\text{(ii)} \quad & E = 3K(1 - 2\nu) \\[3pt]
\text{(iii)} \quad & G = \frac{3K(1 - 2\nu)}{2(1 + \nu)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a homogeneous, isotropic, linearly elastic material, **only two of the four elastic constants are independent**, and the remaining two can be obtained from the above relations.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the relationship $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$ between Young's Modulus (E), Shear Modulus (G), and Poisson's Ratio (ν) for a homogeneous, isotropic, linearly elastic material. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A specimen of marine grade aluminum alloy has Young's Modulus **E = 70 GPa** and Poisson's ratio **ν = 0.33**. Calculate its **Shear Modulus (G)** and **Bulk Modulus (K)**. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Solution to Q.A(a) — Derivation of E = 2G(1+ν)

**Step 1:** Consider a small cubic element of side **a** with edges along x, y, z axes. Apply a uniaxial tensile stress **σₓ** along the x-direction only. Thus σᵧ = σ_z = 0.

**Step 2:** The strains in the three principal directions, from Generalized Hooke's Law:

$$
\varepsilon_x = \frac{\sigma_x}{E}, \quad \varepsilon_y = \varepsilon_z = -\frac{\nu\,\sigma_x}{E}
$$

**Step 3:** The diagonal **BD** of the face perpendicular to z-axis has original length:

$$L_0 = a\sqrt{2}$$

**Step 4:** After deformation, the dimensions of the face become $a(1+\varepsilon_x)$ and $a(1+\varepsilon_y)$, so the new diagonal length is:

$$
L' = a\sqrt{(1+\varepsilon_x)^2 + (1+\varepsilon_y)^2}
$$

**Step 5:** Using binomial approximation (small strains), we obtain:

$$
L' \approx a\sqrt{2}\left[1 + \frac{\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y}{2}\right]
$$

**Step 6:** The strain along diagonal BD is:

$$
\varepsilon_{BD} = \frac{L' - L_0}{L_0} = \frac{\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y}{2} = \frac{\sigma_x(1-\nu)}{2E}
$$

**Step 7:** The uniaxial stress σₓ is equivalent to a state of pure shear on planes at 45° to the x-axis, with shear stress:

$$\tau = \frac{\sigma_x}{2}$$

**Step 8:** The shear strain is $\gamma = \tau/G = \sigma_x/(2G)$. Geometrically, this shear causes the diagonal at 45° to elongate with strain:

$$\varepsilon_{BD} = \frac{\gamma}{2} = \frac{\sigma_x}{4G}$$

**Step 9:** Equating the two expressions for $\varepsilon_{BD}$:

$$
\frac{\sigma_x(1-\nu)}{2E} = \frac{\sigma_x}{4G}
$$

$$
\frac{1-\nu}{E} = \frac{1}{2G}
$$

$$
\boxed{E = 2G(1+\nu)}
$$

---

#### Solution to Q.A(b) — Numerical Computation

**Given:** E = 70 GPa, ν = 0.33

**Step 1 — Shear Modulus G** using $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$:

$$
G = \frac{70}{2(1 + 0.33)} = \frac{70}{2(1.33)} = \frac{70}{2.66} = 26.32 \text{ GPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Bulk Modulus K** using $K = \dfrac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$:

$$
K = \frac{70}{3(1 - 2 \times 0.33)} = \frac{70}{3(1 - 0.66)} = \frac{70}{3(0.34)} = \frac{70}{1.02} = 68.63 \text{ GPa}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- **G ≈ 26.32 GPa**
- **K ≈ 68.63 GPa**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the relationship $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$ between Young's Modulus (E), Bulk Modulus (K), and Poisson's Ratio (ν) for a homogeneous, isotropic, linearly elastic material. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** For a steel specimen, **E = 200 GPa** and **G = 77 GPa**. Determine the values of **Poisson's ratio (ν)** and **Bulk Modulus (K)**. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Solution to Q.B(a) — Derivation of E = 3K(1-2ν)

**Step 1:** Consider a cubic element subjected to hydrostatic pressure **p** on all faces. By sign convention (compression negative):

$$\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = -p$$

**Step 2:** Using Generalized Hooke's Law for εₓ:

$$
\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[-p - \nu(-p - p)\right] = \frac{1}{E}\left[-p + 2\nu p\right] = \frac{-p(1 - 2\nu)}{E}
$$

**Step 3:** By the symmetry of hydrostatic loading:

$$\varepsilon_y = \varepsilon_z = \dfrac{-p(1 - 2\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 4:** The volumetric strain (sum of normal strains for small strains):

$$
\frac{\Delta V}{V} = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z = \frac{-3p(1 - 2\nu)}{E}
$$

**Step 5:** By the definition of Bulk Modulus:

$$K = \frac{-p}{\Delta V / V} = \frac{-p}{\dfrac{-3p(1 - 2\nu)}{E}} = \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)}$$

**Step 6:** Rearranging:

$$
\boxed{E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)}
$$

**Physical Interpretation:** When ν = 0.5, the factor (1 − 2ν) → 0, and K → ∞, meaning the material becomes incompressible. This is the limit for rubber-like materials.

---

#### Solution to Q.B(b) — Numerical Computation

**Given:** E = 200 GPa, G = 77 GPa

**Step 1 — Poisson's Ratio ν** using $\nu = \dfrac{E}{2G} - 1$:

$$
\nu = \frac{200}{2(77)} - 1 = \frac{200}{154} - 1 = 1.2987 - 1 = 0.2987
$$

$$
\nu \approx 0.30
$$

**Step 2 — Bulk Modulus K** using $K = \dfrac{EG}{3(3G - E)}$:

$$
K = \frac{200 \times 77}{3(3 \times 77 - 200)} = \frac{15400}{3(231 - 200)} = \frac{15400}{3(31)} = \frac{15400}{93}
$$

$$
K \approx 165.59 \text{ GPa}
$$

**Verification using $K = \dfrac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)}$:**

$$
K = \frac{200}{3(1 - 0.60)} = \frac{200}{3(0.40)} = \frac{200}{1.20} = 166.67 \text{ GPa} \checkmark
$$

(The small difference is due to rounding ν to 0.30. Using the more accurate ν = 0.2987 gives K = 165.6 GPa.)

**Final Answers:**
- **ν ≈ 0.30** (dimensionless)
- **K ≈ 166.67 GPa**

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Losing Mistakes in KTU Board Examinations:**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in Rel. 1:** Writing $E = 2G(1-\nu)$ instead of $E = 2G(1+\nu)$. This is the **#1 mistake** and can cost **3-4 marks** instantly. Always remember: **lateral contraction REDUCES diagonal, but the geometric shear interpretation involves a ½ factor that flips the sign.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting Units:** Stating E = 200 instead of E = 200 GPa. Always specify units. **Loss: ½ mark per answer.**
>
> 3. **Incomplete Derivation:** Writing only the final equation without showing the strain-equating step. The board examiner wants to see:
>    - Setting up of strains (Hooke's Law)
>    - Diagonal length computation
>    - Shear-equivalence transformation
>    - Final equating step
>    **Skipping any of these = −2 marks**
>
> 4. **Order of Derivation in E = 3K(1-2ν):** Not mentioning that **volumetric strain = sum of three normal strains** for infinitesimal strain theory. Examiners explicitly check this.
>
> 5. **Numerical Computation Errors:** When ν = 0.30, students often write $(1 - 2 \times 0.30) = 0.40$ correctly, but then compute $3 \times 0.40 = 1.20$ wrong (using 1.2 or 12). **Slow down on arithmetic.**
>
> 6. **Hydrostatic Pressure Sign:** Not applying negative sign correctly for compressive hydrostatic stress. The bulk modulus K comes out positive only because of the negative in $\Delta V / V$ being matched with negative stress.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision — The "Must-Know" List:**

### Core Concepts
- [x] Four elastic constants: **E** (axial stiffness), **G** (shear stiffness), **K** (volumetric stiffness), **ν** (lateral coupling)
- [x] Only **two** of the four are independent for isotropic materials
- [x] Three master equations link all four constants

### Master Equations (MEMORIZE)
- [x] **E = 2G(1 + ν)**
- [x] **E = 3K(1 − 2ν)**
- [x] **G = 3K(1 − 2ν) / [2(1 + ν)]**

### Derivations (KNOW THE STEPS)
- [x] **E-G-ν relation:** Diagonal strain method (uniaxial stress + 45° shear equivalence)
- [x] **E-K-ν relation:** Hydrostatic pressure + volumetric strain summation
- [x] **G-K-ν relation:** Eliminate E from the two master equations

### Theoretical Bounds
- [x] **E, G, K > 0** (always)
- [x] **−1 < ν ≤ 0.5** (stability limit)
- [x] **E/G** lies between **2 and 3**
- [x] **ν = 0.5** → incompressible (rubber-like)
- [x] **ν = 0** → cork-like (no lateral contraction)

### Reverse Formulas (FREQUENTLY ASKED)
- [x] $\nu = \dfrac{E}{2G} - 1$
- [x] $\nu = \dfrac{3K - E}{6K}$
- [x] $K = \dfrac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)}$
- [x] $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1 + \nu)}$
- [x] $K = \dfrac{EG}{3(3G - E)}$

### Naval Architecture Relevance
- [x] **Hull plating** uses E for deflection calculations
- [x] **Submarine pressure hulls** use K for collapse analysis
- [x] **Web frame shear** uses G
- [x] **Transverse plate strain** uses ν
- [x] Standard shipbuilding steel: **E ≈ 200 GPa, G ≈ 77 GPa, K ≈ 163 GPa, ν ≈ 0.30**

### KTU Board Tips
- [x] Always state units (GPa, dimensionless)
- [x] Show all derivation steps explicitly
- [x] Verify numerical answers using a second master equation
- [x] Watch for the **sign error** in $E = 2G(1+\nu)$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Definitions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST303 - SOLID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Definitions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

In the context of **Solid Mechanics for Naval Architects**, Module 2 builds the vocabulary that every later formula depends on. Before we can compute hull girder stresses, plate bending, or shaft torsion, we must precisely define the **fundamental quantities** — *force, stress, strain, deformation, displacement*, and their *inter-relationships*.

### 1.1 Force and Load

**Definition (KTU Terminology):**
A *force* is any action that tends to change the state of rest, or uniform motion, of a body. In solid mechanics, we are primarily interested in *external loads* (concentrated, distributed, body forces) acting on a structural member.

> [!NOTE]
> **SI Unit of Force:** **Newton (N)**
> **Dimensional Formula:** $[M^1 L^1 T^{-2}]$

### 1.2 Stress — The Intensity of Internal Force

**Formal Definition:**
*Stress* at a point in a deformable body is the **internal resistive force per unit area** developed at that point when an external load is applied.

$$\sigma = \lim_{\Delta A \to 0} \frac{\Delta F}{\Delta A}$$

where:
- $\Delta F$ = Internal force acting on an infinitesimal area $\Delta A$
- $\sigma$ = Stress at the point

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for Naval Architects:** Stress is what causes failure. A ship's hull plate can carry enormous forces, but the *stress* (force per unit area) determines whether the plate yields, buckles, or fractures.

### 1.3 Normal Stress and Shear Stress

Stress is a *tensor* (second-order), but on any plane we can resolve it into two components:

| Component | Symbol | Acts On | Direction | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Normal Stress** | $\sigma$ | Area normal vector | Perpendicular to the surface | Tries to elongate or compress |
| **Shear Stress** | $\tau$ | Area in the plane | Parallel to the surface | Tries to slide layers over each other |

### 1.4 Strain — The Geometric Measure of Deformation

**Formal Definition:**
*Strain* is a **dimensionless measure of deformation** representing the relative change in shape or size of a body under load.

**Conceptual Analogy — Intuition:**
Imagine pulling a rubber band. The *force* you apply is the **load**, the *stretch* you see is the **deformation**, and the *stretch per unit original length* is the **strain**. The *internal force per unit area* inside the rubber is the **stress**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Strain is dimensionless** — it has no units. It is often expressed in microstrain ($\mu\varepsilon = 10^{-6}$) in experimental work.

### 1.5 Deformation, Displacement, and Distortion

- **Displacement ($u$):** The absolute change in position of a point (has units of length, e.g., **mm**).
- **Deformation ($\delta$):** The change in *shape* or *size* of a body, measured between two reference points.
- **Distortion:** A change in *shape* without a change in *size* (e.g., pure shear).

### 1.6 Sign Convention (Universal in KTU 2024 Scheme)

- **Tensile stress / strain:** *Positive* (member in tension, lengthening).
- **Compressive stress / strain:** *Negative* (member in compression, shortening).
- **Shear stress / strain:** Positive when the *complementary pair* tends to rotate the element **counter-clockwise**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners strictly enforce the sign convention. Marks are frequently deducted when students omit signs in numerical answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Internal Force Concept — How Stress Arises

When a load is applied to a body, the body resists it through **internal molecular forces** (also called *cohesive forces*). To quantify this resistance, we use the **method of sections**:

1. Imagine a cut through the body at the point of interest.
2. Expose the *internal forces* that act on this cross-section.
3. Compute the *force per unit area* — this is the **stress**.

### 2.2 Classification of Stress

Stress is broadly classified as follows:

- **Direct (Normal) Stress ($\sigma$):**
  - *Tensile* — pulls the cross-section apart ($\sigma_t$).
  - *Compressive* — pushes the cross-section together ($\sigma_c$).

- **Shear Stress ($\tau$):** Tends to slide one layer of material over the adjacent layer.

- **Bearing Stress ($\sigma_b$):** The contact stress developed when one body presses against another (e.g., a bolt bearing on a plate).

- **Allowable / Working Stress:** The maximum stress a material is permitted to carry under working conditions, defined as:
$$\sigma_{allow} = \frac{\sigma_{yield}}{FS} \quad \text{or} \quad \sigma_{allow} = \frac{\sigma_{ultimate}}{FS}$$

where $FS$ = **Factor of Safety**.

### 2.3 Classification of Strain

- **Normal Strain (Longitudinal Strain, $\varepsilon$):**
$$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0}$$
where $L_0$ = original length, $L_f$ = final length, $\Delta L$ = change in length.

- **Shear Strain (Angular Strain, $\gamma$):** The change in angle (in radians) between two lines that were originally perpendicular.

$$\gamma = \frac{\pi}{2} - \theta'$$

where $\theta'$ is the new angle after deformation.

- **Volumetric Strain ($\varepsilon_v$):** The change in volume per unit original volume.
$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{\Delta V}{V_0}$$

### 2.4 Hooke's Law — The Stress-Strain Relationship

For a linearly elastic material, stress is **directly proportional** to strain (within the elastic limit).

$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$$

where $E$ = **Young's Modulus (Modulus of Elasticity)**, measured in **GPa** (typical steel: $E \approx 200$ GPa).

For pure shear:
$$\tau = G \cdot \gamma$$

where $G$ = **Shear Modulus (Modulus of Rigidity)**, measured in **GPa**.

### 2.5 Poisson's Ratio ($\nu$)

When a body is stretched in one direction, it contracts in the transverse direction. The ratio of **lateral strain to longitudinal strain** is **Poisson's Ratio**.

$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{lateral}}{\varepsilon_{longitudinal}}$$

For most engineering materials: $0 < \nu < 0.5$. For steel: $\nu \approx 0.3$.

> [!NOTE]
> The **negative sign** in the formula is a mathematical convention to make $\nu$ positive (since lateral strain is opposite in sign to longitudinal strain in tension).

### 2.6 The Three Elastic Constants Relationship

A vital KTU relationship linking $E$, $G$, and $\nu$:

$$E = 2G(1 + \nu)$$

This equation is **frequently asked** in KTU university exams.

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this table — it forms the backbone of **every Module 2 numerical problem**.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Symbol Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Normal Stress | $\sigma = F/A$ | **Pa** (N/m²) | $F$ = axial force, $A$ = cross-sectional area |
| 2 | Shear Stress | $\tau = V/A$ | **Pa** | $V$ = transverse shear force |
| 3 | Bearing Stress | $\sigma_b = P/A_b$ | **Pa** | $A_b$ = projected bearing area |
| 4 | Normal Strain | $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$ | dimensionless | Often in $\mu\varepsilon$ |
| 5 | Shear Strain | $\gamma = \tan\theta \approx \theta$ | **radians** | Small-angle approximation |
| 6 | Volumetric Strain | $\varepsilon_v = \Delta V / V_0$ | dimensionless | — |
| 7 | Hooke's Law (axial) | $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ | — | Valid in elastic range |
| 8 | Hooke's Law (shear) | $\tau = G\gamma$ | — | Valid in elastic range |
| 9 | Poisson's Ratio | $\nu = -\varepsilon_{lat}/\varepsilon_{long}$ | dimensionless | Steel $\approx 0.3$ |
| 10 | Elastic Constants Link | $E = 2G(1+\nu)$ | — | Derived from energy principles |
| 11 | Deformation (axial) | $\delta = PL/(AE)$ | **m** | $P$ = axial load |
| 12 | Allowable Stress | $\sigma_{allow} = \sigma_{yield}/FS$ | **Pa** | $FS$ = factor of safety |

### 2.8 Real-World Utility in Naval Architecture

- **Hull Plate Analysis:** Normal stress determines if a plate will yield under wave-induced bending.
- **Riveted / Welded Joints:** Shear and bearing stresses govern the strength of connections in ship superstructures.
- **Shaft Design:** Torsional shear stress is critical for propeller shafts (covered later in Module 3).
- **Plate Buckling:** Combined stress states (biaxial + shear) drive buckling in stiffened panels.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Average Normal Stress Equation

We begin with a **uniform prismatic bar** of cross-sectional area $A$ subjected to an axial tensile force $F$.

**Step 1 — Force equilibrium on a section:**

Consider a section cut perpendicular to the bar's axis. By Newton's third law, the internal force $F_i$ resisting separation equals the applied force $F$:

$$F_i = F$$

**Step 2 — Definition of average stress:**

The average stress is the internal force distributed uniformly over the cross-section:

$$\sigma_{avg} = \frac{F_i}{A} = \frac{F}{A}$$

**Step 3 — Passage to point stress:**

If the area $A$ is small, the stress may not be uniform. We therefore define stress at a *point* as the limiting value:

$$\sigma = \lim_{\Delta A \to 0} \frac{\Delta F}{\Delta A}$$

**Step 4 — Sign convention application:**

If the force $F$ is tensile (pulling outward), $\sigma > 0$. If compressive, $\sigma < 0$.

> The final compact form is the universally used equation:
> $$\boxed{\sigma = \frac{F}{A}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Average Shear Stress Equation

**Step 1 — Shear loading scenario:**

A block of area $A$ is subjected to a transverse shear force $V$ parallel to the top face.

**Step 2 — Equating shear force to internal resistance:**

$$V_i = V$$

**Step 3 — Average shear stress definition:**

$$\tau_{avg} = \frac{V_i}{A} = \frac{V}{A}$$

**Step 4 — Point shear stress:**

$$\tau = \lim_{\Delta A \to 0} \frac{\Delta V}{\Delta A}$$

> **Final compact form:**
> $$\boxed{\tau = \frac{V}{A}}$$

### 3.3 Derivation of the Normal Strain Equation

**Step 1 — Original and deformed configurations:**

Let a rod of original length $L_0$ be stretched to a new length $L_f$.

**Step 2 — Defining change in length:**

$$\Delta L = L_f - L_0$$

**Step 3 — Defining average axial strain:**

The *average normal strain* is the change in length per unit original length:

$$\varepsilon_{avg} = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0}$$

**Step 4 — Point strain via limiting process:**

For non-uniform deformation:

$$\varepsilon = \lim_{\Delta x \to 0} \frac{\Delta u}{\Delta x} = \frac{du}{dx}$$

where $u$ is the axial displacement.

> **Final compact form:**
> $$\boxed{\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{du}{dx}}$$

### 3.4 Derivation of the Shear Strain Equation

**Step 1 — Reference configuration:**

Two line segments originally perpendicular, intersecting at a point.

**Step 2 — After deformation:**

One segment rotates by an angle $\theta$ relative to the other (small angle).

**Step 3 — Defining shear strain:**

$$\gamma = \frac{\pi}{2} - \theta'$$

where $\theta'$ = angle after deformation (in radians).

**Step 4 — Small-angle approximation:**

For small deformations, $\theta' \approx \pi/2 - \theta$, giving:

$$\gamma \approx \tan\theta = \frac{\Delta s}{h}$$

> **Final compact form:**
> $$\boxed{\gamma = \frac{\pi}{2} - \theta' \quad \text{(radians)}}$$

### 3.5 Derivation of the Relationship $E = 2G(1+\nu)$

This is the **most frequently derived equation** in KTU Solid Mechanics exams.

**Step 1 — Consider a 2D element under pure shear $\tau_{xy}$.**

The shear stress on faces of area $dA$ produces a force $\tau_{xy}\,dA$.

**Step 2 — Resolve the principal stresses.**

Pure shear produces equal and opposite *principal stresses* on the rotated element:

$$\sigma_1 = +\tau_{xy}, \quad \sigma_2 = -\tau_{xy}$$

**Step 3 — Apply generalized Hooke's Law.**

The strain in the principal 1-direction:

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_1 - \nu\sigma_2) = \frac{1}{E}(\tau_{xy} - \nu(-\tau_{xy})) = \frac{\tau_{xy}(1+\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 4 — Relate $\varepsilon_1$ to $\gamma_{xy}$.**

From the geometry of the deformation:

$$\gamma_{xy} = 2\varepsilon_1 = \frac{2\tau_{xy}(1+\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute Hooke's law in shear, $\tau_{xy} = G\gamma_{xy}$:**

$$\gamma_{xy} = \frac{2G\gamma_{xy}(1+\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 6 — Cancel $\gamma_{xy}$ from both sides:**

$$1 = \frac{2G(1+\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 7 — Final rearrangement:**

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= 2G(1+\nu) \\
\therefore \quad G &= \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Final compact form:**
> $$\boxed{E = 2G(1+\nu)}$$

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Each Definition

| Scenario | Quantity to Compute | Governing Definition | Key Formula |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rod under axial pull | Internal force per area | Normal stress | $\sigma = F/A$ |
| Rivet in single shear | Shear resistance per area | Shear stress | $\tau = P/A_{rivet}$ |
| Bolt pressing a plate | Contact force per projected area | Bearing stress | $\sigma_b = P/A_b$ |
| Rod elongation | Change in length per unit length | Normal strain | $\varepsilon = \delta/L$ |
| Block under shear couple | Angle change in radians | Shear strain | $\gamma = \pi/2 - \theta'$ |
| Bar under hydrostatic pressure | Volume change per unit volume | Volumetric strain | $\varepsilon_v = \Delta V/V$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Hierarchy of Stress and Strain Definitions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Applied External Load F] --> B[Internal Resistance at Section]
    B --> C[Stress at a Point]
    C --> C1[Normal Stress sigma]
    C --> C2[Shear Stress tau]
    C1 --> C1a[Tensile sigma greater than 0]
    C1 --> C1b[Compressive sigma less than 0]
    C2 --> C2a[Pure Shear tau_xy]
    C2 --> C2b[Complementary tau_yx]
    A --> D[Resulting Deformation]
    D --> E[Strain at a Point]
    E --> E1[Normal Strain epsilon]
    E --> E2[Shear Strain gamma in radians]
    E --> E3[Volumetric Strain epsilon_v]
    C1 --> F[Hookes Law sigma = E epsilon]
    C2 --> G[Hookes Law tau = G gamma]
    F --> H[Three Elastic Constants E G nu]
    G --> H
    E1 --> I[Poissons Ratio nu = lateral / longitudinal]
    H --> J[Relation E = 2G 1 plus nu]
    I --> J
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Deformation Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L0[Original Body] --> LOADCASE[Applied Load]
    LOADCASE --> TYPE{Type of Loading}
    TYPE --> AX[Axial Load]
    TYPE --> TR[Torsion]
    TYPE --> TR2[Transverse Shear]
    TYPE --> BD[Bending Moment]
    AX --> DL[Elongation delta L]
    DL --> EN[Normal Strain epsilon = delta L over L0]
    TR --> ANG[Angle of Twist phi]
    ANG --> ET[Shear Strain gamma = phi]
    TR2 --> ANGS[Angular Distortion]
    ANGS --> GS[Shear Strain gamma rad]
    BD --> CU[Curvature kappa]
    CU --> EB[Bending Strain epsilon = y times kappa]
    EN --> FA[Family of Definitions under Hookes Law]
    ET --> FA
    GS --> FA
    EB --> FA
    FA --> FA1[FA1 sigma = E epsilon]
    FA --> FA2[FA2 tau = G gamma]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — Sign Convention Reference

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SIGMA_S[Sign Convention for Stress] --> TEN[Positive Tensile Pulling Outward]
    SIGMA_S --> COM[Negative Compressive Pushing Inward]
    SIGMA_S --> SHEAR[Shear Positive Counter Clockwise on Element]
    STRAIN_S[Sign Convention for Strain] --> ETEN[Positive Elongation]
    STRAIN_S --> ECOM[Negative Contraction]
    STRAIN_S --> GSHEAR[Shear Positive When Right Angle Reduces]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams above are *functional architecture maps* showing how each definition links to the next. They replace physical sketches where textual schematics would be ambiguous.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define the following terms:**
(a) Normal stress
(b) Shear stress
(c) Volumetric strain

**Model Answer (Point-wise, 3-Mark Standard):**

- **(a) Normal Stress (1 mark):** The intensity of internal force acting *perpendicular* to a cross-section per unit area, given by $\sigma = F/A$. It may be tensile (positive) or compressive (negative). Units: **Pa** or **N/m²**.

- **(b) Shear Stress (1 mark):** The intensity of internal force acting *parallel* (tangential) to a cross-section per unit area, given by $\tau = V/A$. It tends to slide one layer of the material over the adjacent layer. Units: **Pa**.

- **(c) Volumetric Strain (1 mark):** The ratio of change in volume to the original volume, $\varepsilon_v = \Delta V / V_0$. It is dimensionless and occurs in triaxial loading conditions.

---

**Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**State Hooke's law. Define Young's modulus, Shear modulus, and Poisson's ratio.**

**Model Answer:**

**Hooke's Law (1 mark):** Within the elastic limit, stress is directly proportional to strain, $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ (for normal loading) and $\tau = G\gamma$ (for shear loading).

- **Young's Modulus $E$ (1 mark):** The ratio of normal stress to normal strain along the loading axis, $E = \sigma/\varepsilon$. Units: **GPa** (for steel $\approx 200$ GPa).

- **Shear Modulus $G$ (1/2 mark):** The ratio of shear stress to shear strain, $G = \tau/\gamma$. Units: **GPa**.

- **Poisson's Ratio $\nu$ (1/2 mark):** The negative ratio of lateral strain to longitudinal strain, $\nu = -\varepsilon_{lat}/\varepsilon_{long}$. For most metals, $\nu \approx 0.3$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, **Part B questions** in the End Semester Examination carry **14 marks** with **internal choice** between two full questions. Each question typically has sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**A solid circular bar of length 2 m and diameter 30 mm is subjected to an axial tensile load of 80 kN. The bar elongates by 1.6 mm under this load. Determine:**
**(a)** The normal stress, normal strain, and Young's modulus of the bar material.
**(b)** If the lateral strain is measured to be $1.2 \times 10^{-4}$, find Poisson's ratio and the Shear modulus.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

*Step 1: Compute the cross-sectional area.*

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(30 \times 10^{-3})^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(9 \times 10^{-4})$$

$$A = 7.0686 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

*Step 2: Compute normal stress (definition applied).*

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{80 \times 10^3}{7.0686 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\sigma = 113.18 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 113.18 \text{ MPa}$$

*Step 3: Compute normal strain.*

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 0.8 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\varepsilon = 8.0 \times 10^{-4}$$

*Step 4: Compute Young's modulus (Hooke's law).*

$$E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \frac{113.18 \times 10^6}{8.0 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$E = 141.47 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa} \approx 141.5 \text{ GPa}$$

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

*Step 5: Compute Poisson's ratio.*

$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{lat}}{\varepsilon_{long}} = -\frac{-1.2 \times 10^{-4}}{8.0 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\nu = 0.15$$

*Step 6: Compute Shear modulus using $E = 2G(1+\nu)$.*

$$G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)} = \frac{141.5 \times 10^9}{2(1 + 0.15)}$$

$$G = \frac{141.5 \times 10^9}{2.30} = 61.52 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa} \approx 61.5 \text{ GPa}$$

**Final Answer Summary:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Normal stress $\sigma$ | **113.18 MPa** |
| Normal strain $\varepsilon$ | $8.0 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| Young's modulus $E$ | **141.5 GPa** |
| Poisson's ratio $\nu$ | **0.15** |
| Shear modulus $G$ | **61.5 GPa** |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A steel specimen (E = 200 GPa, $\nu$ = 0.3) of original diameter 20 mm and gauge length 100 mm is tested in tension. The specimen yields at a load of 100 kN, and the load is then removed. The permanent elongation is 0.04 mm.**
**(a)** Determine the yield stress, the elastic recovery strain, and the maximum elastic strain energy density.
**(b)** If the diameter at the yield point is measured as 19.985 mm, compute Poisson's ratio from the test data and compare with the given value.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

*Step 1: Cross-sectional area at original section.*

$$A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4}(20 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

*Step 2: Yield stress (definition: stress at onset of plastic deformation).*

$$\sigma_y = \frac{F_y}{A_0} = \frac{100 \times 10^3}{3.1416 \times 10^{-4}} = 318.31 \text{ MPa}$$

*Step 3: Total strain at yield load.*

$$\varepsilon_{total} = \frac{\sigma_y}{E} = \frac{318.31 \times 10^6}{200 \times 10^9} = 1.5915 \times 10^{-3}$$

*Step 4: Permanent (plastic) strain.*

$$\varepsilon_{perm} = \frac{\delta_{perm}}{L_0} = \frac{0.04 \times 10^{-3}}{100 \times 10^{-3}} = 4.0 \times 10^{-4}$$

*Step 5: Elastic recovery strain.*

$$\varepsilon_{elastic} = \varepsilon_{total} - \varepsilon_{perm} = 1.5915 \times 10^{-3} - 4.0 \times 10^{-4} = 1.1915 \times 10^{-3}$$

*Step 6: Maximum elastic strain energy density (U = $\sigma^2/2E$).*

$$u = \frac{\sigma_y^2}{2E} = \frac{(318.31 \times 10^6)^2}{2 \times 200 \times 10^9}$$

$$u = \frac{1.0132 \times 10^{17}}{4.0 \times 10^{11}} = 253.3 \text{ kJ/m}^3$$

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

*Step 7: Lateral (transverse) strain from measured diameter.*

$$\Delta d = d_0 - d_f = 20 - 19.985 = 0.015 \text{ mm}$$

$$\varepsilon_{lat} = \frac{\Delta d}{d_0} = \frac{0.015}{20} = 7.5 \times 10^{-4}$$

*Step 8: Longitudinal strain (we use elastic recovery strain for elastic comparison).*

$$\varepsilon_{long} = 1.1915 \times 10^{-3}$$

*Step 9: Computed Poisson's ratio.*

$$\nu_{test} = \frac{\varepsilon_{lat}}{\varepsilon_{long}} = \frac{7.5 \times 10^{-4}}{1.1915 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.6294$$

*Step 10: Comparison and interpretation.*

The tested value $\nu_{test} = 0.63$ is **physically impossible** because for stable isotropic materials, $0 \leq \nu \leq 0.5$. The discrepancy arises because we used *elastic recovery strain* (not *total strain*). Using total strain:

$$\nu_{test,total} = \frac{7.5 \times 10^{-4}}{1.5915 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.4712$$

This is **closer** to the actual material value $\nu = 0.3$, with the remaining difference due to plastic deformation effects.

**Final Answer Summary:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Yield stress $\sigma_y$ | **318.31 MPa** |
| Elastic recovery strain $\varepsilon_{el}$ | $1.1915 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| Strain energy density $u$ | **253.3 kJ/m³** |
| Test Poisson's ratio (corrected) | $\approx 0.47$ |
| Given Poisson's ratio | **0.30** |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Sign convention errors:** Forgetting to mark tensile stress as positive / compressive as negative costs **1 full mark** per occurrence.
> 2. **Unit mismatches:** Mixing **mm and m** without conversion is a **favourite KTU trap**. Always convert to SI units *before* substituting into formulas.
> 3. **Neglecting $\nu$ sign in Poisson's ratio:** Writing $\nu = \varepsilon_{lat}/\varepsilon_{long}$ *without* the negative sign leads to negative values, which is *physically meaningless*. Always include the minus.
> 4. **Confusing stress and pressure:** Pressure is a *special case* of normal stress when the load is hydrostatic and equal in all directions. Do not interchange the terms loosely.
> 5. **Using the wrong area in bearing stress:** Bearing stress uses the *projected* area, not the curved contact area. For a bolt in a plate: $A_b = d \times t$, where $d$ = bolt diameter, $t$ = plate thickness.
> 6. **Skipping Hooke's law validity check:** The relation $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ is **only valid in the elastic region**. Applying it in the plastic region gives incorrect answers.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Definitions (Module 2)**

- **Stress = Internal Force / Unit Area** — a *point property*, defined as the limit $\sigma = \lim_{\Delta A \to 0} \Delta F / \Delta A$.
- **Strain = Change in Geometry / Original Geometry** — dimensionless; for axial, $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$; for shear, $\gamma$ in **radians**; for volume, $\varepsilon_v = \Delta V / V_0$.
- **Three types of stress to remember:** Normal ($\sigma$), Shear ($\tau$), Bearing ($\sigma_b$).
- **Three types of strain to remember:** Normal ($\varepsilon$), Shear ($\gamma$), Volumetric ($\varepsilon_v$).
- **Hooke's law** governs the linear region: $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ and $\tau = G\gamma$.
- **Three elastic constants:** $E$, $G$, $\nu$ — linked by $E = 2G(1+\nu)$. **Memorize this equation.**
- **Poisson's ratio $\nu$** is always between **0 and 0.5** for stable materials. Steel $\approx 0.3$.
- **Sign convention:** Tension = positive, Compression = negative. Shear = positive when counter-clockwise on element.
- **Units:** Stress in **Pa (N/m²)**, practical units **MPa** or **GPa**. Strain is **dimensionless**. Moduli in **GPa**.
- **$E$ for steel** $\approx 200$ GPa, $G$ for steel $\approx 77$ GPa, $\nu$ for steel $\approx 0.3$.
- **Engineering significance:** These definitions are the *building blocks* for all later topics — axial deformation, torsion, bending, combined loading, and failure analysis.
- **Naval Architecture connection:** Hull plate normal stress from wave bending, propeller shaft torsional shear, riveted joint bearing stress — all depend on these fundamental definitions.
- **Top exam traps:** Wrong units, missing sign, applying Hooke's law beyond yield, confusing projected vs actual area in bearing.

> **End of Module 2 Definitions — Solid Mechanics for Naval Architects (PCNST303)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shear Force and Bending Moment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCNST303 - SOLID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Shear Force and Bending Moment ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the discipline of **Solid Mechanics for Naval Architects**, every ship hull, deck plate, stiffener, and bulkhead is fundamentally a **beam** — a structural element designed to resist transverse loading. When external forces and couples act on a beam, two critical internal resultants arise at any cross-section: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Shear Force (V or SF):** The algebraic sum of the transverse forces (perpendicular to the axis of the beam) acting on either side of a section. It represents the internal resistance against the tendency of one part of the beam to slide over the adjacent part along the longitudinal axis. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Bending Moment (M or BM):** The algebraic sum of the moments of all the forces (about the centroid of the section) acting on either side of the section. It represents the internal resistance against the tendency of the beam to bend (i.e., to deform into a curved shape). Mathematically, for a beam section at distance $x$ from a reference point: $$V_x = \sum F_{\text{left, transverse}} = \sum F_{\text{right, transverse}}$$ $$M_x = \sum M_{\text{left, about section}} = \sum M_{\text{right, about section}}$$ ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Diving Board" Intuition Imagine you are standing at the **free end of a diving board** while a friend sits at the fixed end (the fulcrum). The diving board bends downward where you stand. - The **bending moment** is the internal "bending stress" the board feels — like the internal muscle memory of a flexible ruler trying to straighten itself after being bowed. The **larger** the moment, the **more** the board tries to curve. - The **shear force** is the internal "sliding stress" — imagine trying to cut the board vertically with a giant pair of scissors. The board's fibers resist being torn apart vertically. In a **ship hull**, the keel acts like the neutral axis of a giant upside-down diving board, and the sea pressure on the hull bottom is the load. The shear force and bending moment govern how thick the steel plates must be — a problem of paramount importance in **naval architecture** as defined by classification societies (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS). ### 1.3 Standard KTU Sign Convention > [!NOTE] > **KTU-Standard Sign Convention (used throughout Indian engineering curricula):** > - **Positive Shear Force:** When the resultant of the transverse forces on the **left** portion of the section acts **upward**, OR the resultant on the **right** portion acts **downward**. > - **Positive Bending Moment:** When the beam **sags downward** (concave upward) — i.e., the moment causes the bottom fibers to be in **tension** and the top fibers to be in **compression**. ### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - Standard unit of **Shear Force**: **Newton (N)** or **kN** in SI, **kgf** in MKS. - Standard unit of **Bending Moment**: **N·m** or **kN·m** in SI, **kgf·m** in MKS. - For ship hulls (typical magnitudes): SF in **MN/m** (MegaNewton per meter) and BM in **MN·m/m** (hogging/sagging values). ### 1.5 Why This Topic is Central to Naval Architecture In ship design, the **hull girder** behaves as a simply supported beam between the bow and stern wave pressures. The extreme bending moments (called **hogging** and **sagging**) directly determine the **section modulus** of the midship section, and thus the **scantlings** (plate thicknesses) of the keel, deck, and side shell. Misjudging the shear and bending moment distribution is the difference between a safe vessel and a structural failure (e.g., the historical breakages of the *Liberty Ships* in WWII). ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Types of Beams (KTU Classification) Beams are categorized by their support conditions and load application: | Beam Type | Support Description | Typical Statically Determinate? | |-----------|--------------------|---------------------------------| | **Simply Supported Beam** | Pin at one end, roller at the other | Yes (reactions: 2 unknowns) | | **Cantilever Beam** | Fixed at one end, free at the other | Yes (reactions: 3 unknowns) | | **Overhanging Beam** | Supports within the span, one or both ends project beyond supports | Yes (reactions: 2 unknowns) | | **Fixed Beam (Propped)** | Both ends fixed (or one end fixed, other simply supported) | Often statically indeterminate | | **Continuous Beam** | More than two supports | Statically indeterminate | ### 2.2 Types of Loads Acting on Beams 1. **Point Load (Concentrated Load):** A force applied at a single point — symbol: $P$, unit: **N** or **kN**. 2. **Uniformly Distributed Load (UDL):** A load spread uniformly over a length — symbol: $w$, unit: **N/m** or **kN/m**. 3. **Uniformly Varying Load (UVL) / Triangular Load:** A load varying linearly from zero to a maximum — symbol varies, unit: **N/m**. 4. **Couple / Moment Load:** A pure moment applied at a section — symbol: $M_0$, unit: **N·m**. ### 2.3 Critical Relationships Between Load, SF, and BM These three differential equations are the **backbone of every KTU SFD/BMD problem**: | Relationship | Mathematical Form | Physical Meaning | |--------------|-------------------|------------------| | Load → Shear | $\dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w(x)$ | The slope of the SFD equals the negative of the load intensity | | Shear → Moment | $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V$ | The slope of the BMD equals the shear force | | Load → Moment | $\dfrac{d^{2}M}{dx^{2}} = -w(x)$ | The curvature of the BMD relates to the load | ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table contains every formula you must memorize for the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE on Shear Force and Bending Moment. | # | Loading Condition | Shear Force $V(x)$ | Bending Moment $M(x)$ | Location of Max BM | |---|-------------------|--------------------|------------------------|--------------------| | 1 | Cantilever, point load $P$ at free end (length $L$) | $V(x) = -P$ (constant) | $M(x) = -P(L-x)$ | At fixed end: $M_{\max} = PL$ | | 2 | Cantilever, UDL $w$ over full length | $V(x) = -w(L-x)$ | $M(x) = -\dfrac{w(L-x)^{2}}{2}$ | At fixed end: $M_{\max} = \dfrac{wL^{2}}{2}$ | | 3 | Simply supported, central point load $P$ (span $L$) | $V = \pm P/2$ (constant on each half) | Triangular: $M_{\max} = \dfrac{PL}{4}$ at center | At center | | 4 | Simply supported, UDL $w$ over full span | $V(x) = \dfrac{wL}{2} - wx$ | Parabolic: $M(x) = \dfrac{wLx}{2} - \dfrac{wx^{2}}{2}$ | At center: $M_{\max} = \dfrac{wL^{2}}{8}$ | | 5 | Simply supported, UVL (zero at one end, $w$ at other) | $V(x) = \dfrac{wL}{6} - \dfrac{wx^{2}}{2L}$ | Cubic variation: $M_{\max} = \dfrac{wL^{2}}{9\sqrt{3}} \approx \dfrac{wL^{2}}{15.6}$ | At $x = L/\sqrt{3}$ | | 6 | Simply supported, moment $M_0$ at left support | $V = -M_0/L$ (constant) | Linear: $M(x) = M_0\left(1 - \dfrac{x}{L}\right)$ | At $x=0$: $M_{\max} = M_0$ | > [!NOTE] > **Singularity Tip (Very High-Yield):** When a **point load** acts at a section, the SFD shows a **vertical jump** of magnitude equal to the point load. When a **point moment** acts at a section, the BMD shows a **vertical jump** of magnitude equal to the moment — but the SFD is **unaffected**. ### 2.5 Step-by-Step Logical Procedure for SFD & BMD Construction 1. **Resolve all support reactions** by applying equilibrium equations: $\sum F_y = 0$, $\sum M = 0$. 2. **Divide the beam into segments** wherever there is a change in loading (point load, UDL start/end, support, applied moment). 3. **For each segment**, write the shear force expression $V(x)$ by summing forces to the **left** (or right) of the section. 4. **For each segment**, write the bending moment expression $M(x)$ by summing moments to the **left** (or right) of the section. 5. **Plot the SFD** (graph of $V$ vs $x$) and **BMD** (graph of $M$ vs $x$) on separate axes. 6. **Identify maxima/minima** of BM (where $V = 0$ or $V$ changes sign). ### 2.6 Real-World Naval Architecture Application > [!NOTE] > **Still-Water Bending Moment (SWBM):** The bending moment in a ship's hull girder when the ship is in still water, computed as $M = \Delta \cdot \text{LCG}_{\text{distance}}$, where $\Delta$ is the displacement. > **Wave Bending Moment (WBM):** The additional BM due to wave action — typically hogging when the ship is supported at the bow and stern (over a crest) and sagging when supported amidships (in a trough). > The **total bending moment** = SWBM + WBM, and classification society rules (e.g., DNV, ABS) use this to prescribe minimum section modulus for the midship section. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Load Intensity, Shear Force, and Bending Moment Consider an elemental slice of a beam of length $dx$ at distance $x$ from the left support. Let: - $V$ be the shear force on the left face of the element. - $V + dV$ be the shear force on the right face. - $w(x)$ be the distributed load intensity acting downward on the element. - $M$ be the bending moment on the left face, and $M + dM$ on the right face. **Step 1 — Vertical Equilibrium of the Element** Sum of vertical forces on the element equals zero: $$V - (V + dV) - w(x) \cdot dx = 0$$ The term $w(x) \cdot dx$ represents the total downward distributed load on the elemental length $dx$ (load intensity multiplied by length). **Step 2 — Simplify the Equation** Cancel $V$ on both sides and rearrange: $$-dV - w(x) \cdot dx = 0$$ $$dV = -w(x) \cdot dx$$ **Step 3 — Divide by $dx$ to Obtain the First Differential Relation** $$\dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w(x)$$ **Step 4 — Moment Equilibrium of the Element** Take moments about the left face of the element (clockwise positive). The shear force $V$ on the right face acts at a moment arm of $dx$: $$M - (M + dM) + V \cdot dx - w(x) \cdot dx \cdot \dfrac{dx}{2} = 0$$ The term $w(x) \cdot dx \cdot (dx/2)$ is a higher-order infinitesimal and is neglected in the limit. **Step 5 — Simplify and Divide by $dx$** $$-dM + V \cdot dx = 0$$ $$\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V$$ **Step 6 — Combine Both Relations to Eliminate $V$** Differentiate the second relation with respect to $x$: $$\dfrac{d^{2}M}{dx^{2}} = \dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w(x)$$ > [!NOTE] > **Verification of Logical Consistency:** The first relation states that if the load $w(x)$ is zero (no UDL), then $V$ is constant. The second relation states that if $V$ is constant, then $M$ varies linearly — both consistent with what we observe for a simply supported beam under a central point load. ### 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example — Simply Supported Beam with UDL and Point Load **Problem Statement (Typical KTU 14-Mark Style):** A simply supported beam of span **8 m** carries a **uniformly distributed load of 10 kN/m** over its entire span and a **point load of 20 kN** at a distance of **3 m from the left support**. Draw the SFD and BMD, and find the position and magnitude of the maximum bending moment. **Step 1 — Compute Support Reactions** Let $R_A$ and $R_B$ be the vertical reactions at supports A (left, $x=0$) and B (right, $x=8$ m). Total UDL = $10 \times 8 = 80$ kN, acting at the centroid $x = 4$ m. Point load $P = 20$ kN acts at $x = 3$ m. Apply $\sum M_A = 0$ (counter-clockwise positive): $$R_B \cdot 8 - 80 \cdot 4 - 20 \cdot 3 = 0$$ $$8 R_B = 320 + 60 = 380$$ $$R_B = \dfrac{380}{8} = 47.5 \text{ kN}$$ Apply $\sum F_y = 0$: $$R_A + R_B = 80 + 20 = 100$$ $$R_A = 100 - 47.5 = 52.5 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 2 — Define Segments** - **Segment 1:** $0 \le x \le 3$ m (from A to the point load) - **Segment 2:** $3 \le x \le 8$ m (from the point load to B) **Step 3 — Shear Force Equations** **Segment 1** ($0 \le x \le 3$): Sum forces to the left of section: $$V_1(x) = R_A - w \cdot x = 52.5 - 10x$$ At $x = 0$: $V_1 = +52.5$ kN. At $x = 3^{-}$: $V_1 = 52.5 - 30 = +22.5$ kN. **At $x = 3$ m** (point load location): SFD drops by 20 kN (downward jump). $$V_2(3^{+}) = 22.5 - 20 = +2.5 \text{ kN}$$ **Segment 2** ($3 \le x \le 8$): $$V_2(x) = R_A - 20 - w \cdot x = 52.5 - 20 - 10x = 32.5 - 10x$$ At $x = 3^{+}$: $V_2 = 32.5 - 30 = +2.5$ kN. ✓ At $x = 8$: $V_2 = 32.5 - 80 = -47.5$ kN. ✓ (matches $-R_B$). **Step 4 — Locate Zero Shear (Position of Maximum BM)** Set $V_1(x) = 0$ in Segment 1: $$52.5 - 10x = 0 \Rightarrow x = 5.25 \text{ m}$$ But $x = 5.25$ m lies in Segment 2 (since 5.25 > 3). So use Segment 2 equation: $$32.5 - 10x = 0 \Rightarrow x = 3.25 \text{ m}$$ Since $3.25 > 3$ and $3.25 < 8$, this is valid. So **maximum BM occurs at $x = 3.25$ m**. **Step 5 — Bending Moment Equations** **Segment 1** ($0 \le x \le 3$): $$M_1(x) = R_A \cdot x - w \cdot \dfrac{x^{2}}{2} = 52.5x - 5x^{2}$$ At $x = 0$: $M_1 = 0$ (pin support). ✓ At $x = 3$: $M_1 = 52.5 \times 3 - 5 \times 9 = 157.5 - 45 = 112.5$ kN·m. **Segment 2** ($3 \le x \le 8$): $$M_2(x) = R_A \cdot x - 20(x-3) - 5x^{2}$$ At $x = 3$: $M_2 = 52.5 \times 3 - 0 - 45 = 112.5$ kN·m. ✓ (continuity) At $x = 8$: $M_2 = 52.5 \times 8 - 20 \times 5 - 5 \times 64 = 420 - 100 - 320 = 0$. ✓ (roller support) **Step 6 — Maximum Bending Moment** At $x = 3.25$ m (use Segment 2 equation): $$M_{\max} = 52.5 \times 3.25 - 20 \times 0.25 - 5 \times (3.25)^{2}$$ $$= 170.625 - 5 - 52.8125 = 112.8125 \text{ kN·m}$$ $$M_{\max} \approx 112.81 \text{ kN·m}$$ **Step 7 — Summary Table** | Location $x$ (m) | Shear Force $V$ (kN) | Bending Moment $M$ (kN·m) | |------------------|----------------------|---------------------------| | 0 (Support A) | +52.5 | 0 | | 3⁻ (just before point load) | +22.5 | 112.5 | | 3⁺ (just after point load) | +2.5 | 112.5 | | 3.25 (max BM) | 0 | **112.81** | | 8 (Support B) | -47.5 | 0 | ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — SFD/BMD Shape Recognition | Load on Segment | SFD Shape | BMD Shape | |-----------------|-----------|-----------| | No load (pure point) | Horizontal line (constant) | Straight inclined line | | Point load at end of segment | Vertical jump (discontinuity) | **Kink** (slope changes abruptly) | | Uniformly Distributed Load | Straight inclined line | **Parabola** (quadratic curve) | | Uniformly Varying Load (linear) | **Parabola** (quadratic) | **Cubic** curve | | Applied couple at section | No change in SFD | **Vertical jump** in BMD | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Sign Convention Schematic (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph "Positive Shear Force" direction LR A1[Left portion
forces sum to UP] -->|transverse cut| B1[Right portion
forces sum to DOWN] end subgraph "Positive Bending Moment" direction LR A2[Beam sags DOWN] -->|concave UP| B2[Top fibers in COMPRESSION
Bottom fibers in TENSION] end ``` ### 4.2 SFD/BMD Construction Methodology Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[Start: Given beam with supports and loads] --> nodeB[Step 1: Compute support reactions
using Sigma F and Sigma M equations] nodeB --> nodeC[Step 2: Identify all distinct segments
point loads, UDL boundaries, supports] nodeC --> nodeD[Step 3: For each segment,
write V x as sum of forces to the LEFT] nodeD --> nodeE[Step 4: For each segment,
write M x as sum of moments to the LEFT] nodeE --> nodeF[Step 5: Locate where V x = 0
this gives position of max M] nodeF --> nodeG[Step 6: Substitute to find M_max] nodeG --> nodeH[Step 7: Plot SFD and BMD
with correct shapes and signs] nodeH --> nodeI[Step 8: Verify equilibrium
area under SFD equals change in BM] ``` ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Ship Hull as a Beam ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeLoad1[Sea Pressure
on Bottom] --> nodeHull nodeLoad2[Wave Induced
Hogging] --> nodeHull nodeLoad3[Still Water
Loads] --> nodeHull nodeHull[Ship Hull Girder
= giant beam] --> nodeShear[Internal Shear Force V] nodeHull --> nodeMoment[Internal Bending Moment M] nodeShear --> nodeScant[Plate Thickness
via Shear Stress] nodeMoment --> nodeScant2[Section Modulus
via Flexural Stress] nodeScant --> nodeDesign[Final Hull
Scantlings] nodeScant2 --> nodeDesign ``` ### 4.4 Typical SFD and BMD for a Simply Supported Beam with UDL ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph "Shear Force Diagram" direction LR sfdStart["+wL/2 at x=0"] --> sfdMid["Linear decrease
slope = -w"] --> sfdEnd["-wL/2 at x=L"] end subgraph "Bending Moment Diagram" direction LR bmdStart["M=0 at x=0"] --> bmdMid["Parabolic
peak = wL^2/8"] --> bmdEnd["M=0 at x=L"] end ``` ### 4.5 Reference Table of Cantilever Beam Behavior ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeCant[Cantilever Beam
Fixed at A, Free at B] --> nodeFixed[Fixed End: Max V and Max M] nodeCant --> nodeFree[Free End: V=0 and M=0] nodeFixed --> nodeMsg1[V_max = total load
M_max = sum of load x distance] nodeFree --> nodeMsg2[Always zero at free end
regardless of loading] ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Define shear force and bending moment at a section of a beam. State the sign convention used in the KTU scheme.** **Model Answer:** **Shear Force (SF):** The algebraic sum of all transverse forces acting on either side of a beam section. Mathematically, $V = \sum F_y$ (considering forces on the left or right of the section). **Bending Moment (BM):** The algebraic sum of the moments of all forces, taken about the centroid of the beam section. Mathematically, $M = \sum M$ about the section. **KTU Sign Convention:** - **Positive SF:** Left-side forces act upward (or right-side forces act downward) at the section. - **Positive BM:** Beam sags (concave upward) — bottom fibers in tension, top fibers in compression. --- **Q2.
