The *Assumptions in Simple Bending* form the foundational postulates of the classical **Euler-Bernoulli Beam Theory**, which mathematically relates the internal bending moment, the geometry of the cross-section, and the resulting flexural stresses. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate:** Module 3 establishes these assumptions as the *axiomatic bedrock* upon which the entire flexure formula (also called the **Bending Equation**) is mathematically erected. Every numerical problem on beam deflection, section modulus, or stress distribution in Module 3 and Module 4 stems directly from these postulates. ### The Eleven Foundational Assumptions of Simple Bending The classical theory of simple bending rests on the following set of eleven interrelated assumptions: 1. **Homogeneity** — The material of the beam is uniform in composition throughout its volume; mechanical properties do not vary from point to point. 2. **Isotropy** — The material exhibits identical elastic properties in all directions at any given point (valid for most metals; not strictly valid for timber or fibre-reinforced composites). 3. **Linear Elasticity (Hooke's Law)** — The material obeys Hooke's Law within the elastic limit; stress is directly proportional to strain ($\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$). 4. **Initial Straightness** — The beam is perfectly straight and unloaded before the application of external loads (no initial curvature). 5. **Constant Cross-Section (Prismatic Beam)** — The beam has a uniform cross-section along its entire length, *or* the cross-section varies only gradually along its length. 6. **Plane Sections Remain Plane** — Cross-sections that are plane and perpendicular to the longitudinal axis before bending remain plane and perpendicular to the deformed neutral axis after bending (the *kinematic assumption*). 7. **Negligible Transverse Shear** — The transverse shear force is zero; only pure bending stresses act on the cross-section. 8. **Negligible Lateral Shear Strain** — Shear deformation is negligible; only fibre elongation/contraction along the axis is considered. 9. **Large Radius of Curvature** — The deformed radius of curvature is large compared to the cross-sectional dimensions, so cross-sections do not distort (no warping). 10. **No Residual Stresses** — The beam is free from any pre-existing internal stresses from manufacturing, welding, or thermal effects. 11. **Self-Weight Neglected or Purely Transverse** — If self-weight is considered, it acts purely perpendicular to the neutral axis and produces only symmetric bending. > [!NOTE] > **Naval Architecture Connection:** In ship structural analysis, the **hull girder** is treated as a long beam undergoing *hogging* and *sagging*. The simple bending assumptions allow naval architects to compute the section modulus $Z$ of the hull cross-section and predict the maximum longitudinal bending stress using $\sigma = M/Z$. This is critical under the **Common Structural Rules (CSR)** for tankers and bulk carriers. ### Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition Imagine you are standing on the edge of a **wooden diving board** at a swimming pool. When you walk to the tip: - The board is **originally straight** (Assumption 4: Initial Straightness). - It is made of **uniform wood** of the same grain density throughout (Assumption 1: Homogeneity). - The wood bends **elastically** and returns to its original shape when you step off (Assumption 3: Hooke's Law). - A cross-section marked on the board near the fulcrum remains a **flat plane** as it rotates — it does not bulge or distort into a curved surface (Assumption 6: Plane Sections Remain Plane). - The fibres on the **top surface stretch** while those on the **bottom surface compress**, like a stack of papers in a book being gently bent. Now substitute the diving board with the **keel of a ship** in a head sea: the same set of geometric and physical rules govern how the steel fibres elongate and compress along the hull's longitudinal axis. This is the essence of simple bending theory. > [!TIP] > **Student Mnemonic:** *"HIP-PLANES-LARGE-NO-RES"* — captures **H**omogeneity, **I**sotropy, **P**rismatic (constant section), **P**lane sections, **L**inear elastic, **A**xis straight, **N**o shear, **G**radual variation, **E** (large radius), **R**esidual-stress-free. Explicit physical constants used in this topic: - **Young's Modulus of Steel:** $E = 2.0 \times 10^{5} \text{ N/mm}^2 = 200 \text{ GPa}$ - **Yield Stress of Mild Steel:** $\sigma_y = 250 \text{ MPa}$ - **Yield Stress of Marine Grade Steel (AH36):** $\sigma_y = 355 \text{ MPa}$ ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### Logical Breakdown — The "Why" Behind Each Assumption Each assumption exists to simplify the complex 3-D continuum mechanics problem of beam deformation into a tractable 1-D analysis. Below is the structured reasoning: - **Homogeneity & Isotropy → Simplification of Elastic Modulus:** Because the material is the same everywhere, a single scalar **Young's Modulus** $E$ completely describes the stiffness. Without homogeneity, we would need $E$ as a function of position $(x, y, z)$. - **Hooke's Law → Linear Stress-Strain Relationship:** This guarantees that stresses and strains are linearly superposable, allowing the use of **superposition principles** in beam analysis. Non-linear materials (e.g., rubber, concrete in compression) violate this assumption. - **Initial Straightness → Predictable Reference Geometry:** A pre-curved beam introduces an *initial eccentricity* that complicates stress calculations. Naval architects assume the hull girder is initially straight at the *zero stress condition* (still water). - **Plane Sections Remain Plane (Kinematic Assumption) → No Shear Distortion:** This is the **most critical assumption**. Without it, the strain distribution $\varepsilon = y/R$ would be invalid, and the flexure formula collapses. - **Pure Bending (Zero Shear Force) → Uniform Curvature:** Pure bending ensures $dM/dx = 0$, hence $1/R = \text{constant}$. The beam bends into a **perfect circular arc**, making the geometry idealised. - **Large Radius of Curvature → Small Deformation Theory:** Validates the approximation $\sin\theta \approx \theta$ and $\cos\theta \approx 1$, allowing curvature $\kappa \approx d^2y/dx^2$ rather than the exact non-linear expression. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner Insight:** When a question asks *"Why are plane sections assumed to remain plane?"*, the examiner expects the answer: *"Because shear strain in the cross-section is negligible compared to bending strain, and the radius of curvature is large compared to the depth of the beam. This allows strain to vary linearly with distance from the neutral axis."* ### Real-World Engineering Utility | Engineering Domain | Application of Simple Bending Assumptions | |---|---| | **Naval Architecture** | Hull girder bending analysis, section modulus of midship section, longitudinal strength under wave bending moments | | **Civil Engineering** | Design of simply-supported reinforced concrete beams, steel I-girders in bridges | | **Aerospace** | Wing spar analysis under lift loads | | **Mechanical** | Design of crane jibs, machine tool beds, leaf springs | | **Offshore Structures** | Jacket platform leg analysis under wave loading | ### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---| | $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{f}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ | **Flexure Formula (Bending Equation)** — the master relation | $\dfrac{\text{N/mm}^2}{\text{mm}^4} = \dfrac{\text{N/mm}^2}{\text{mm}} = \dfrac{\text{N/mm}^2}{\text{mm}}$ | | $M = \dfrac{E \cdot I}{R}$ | Bending moment–curvature relationship | $\text{N}\cdot\text{mm}$ | | $f = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ | Flexural (bending) stress at distance $y$ from NA | $\text{N/mm}^2$ | | $I = \int_{A} y^2 \, dA$ | Second moment of area (moment of inertia) about NA | $\text{mm}^4$ | | $Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{\max}}$ | **Section Modulus** — measures flexural strength | $\text{mm}^3$ | | $f_{\max} = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ | Maximum bending stress (at extreme fibre) | $\text{N/mm}^2$ | | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{y}{R}$ | Strain at distance $y$ from neutral axis | dimensionless | | $f = E \cdot \varepsilon = \dfrac{E \cdot y}{R}$ | Bending stress from Hooke's Law | $\text{N/mm}^2$ | | $\sigma_x = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ | Alternative notation for flexural stress (tension positive) | $\text{N/mm}^2$ | | $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{M}{E \cdot I}$ | Curvature of the bent beam | $\text{mm}^{-1}$ | Where: - $M$ = Bending moment at the section - $I$ = Second moment of area about the neutral axis - $f$ (or $\sigma$) = Flexural stress at fibre distance $y$ - $y$ = Distance from neutral axis to the fibre in question - $E$ = Young's Modulus of elasticity - $R$ = Radius of curvature of the bent beam - $Z$ = Section modulus > [!TIP] > **KTU Memory Trick:** *"M over I equals f over y equals E over R"* — pronounced **"M-I-F-Y-E-R"**. This is the single most tested relation in Module 3. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### Derivation of the Bending Equation from the Simple Bending Assumptions This derivation logically transforms the assumptions stated above into the famous flexure formula. Each step is meticulously justified using one or more assumptions. **Step 0 — Geometric Setup:** Consider a small element of length $\delta x$ cut from a beam subjected to pure bending. Let: - $O$ and $O'$ be the centroids of the left and right cross-sections before bending. - $O_1$ and $O_1'$ be the same centroids after bending. - $R$ = radius of curvature of the neutral axis (assumed constant due to pure bending). - $\delta\theta$ = angle subtended at the centre of curvature by the element. - $y$ = perpendicular distance from the neutral axis to an arbitrary fibre $AB$. - $NN$ = neutral axis (the locus of fibres that experience zero longitudinal strain). $$ \delta x = R \cdot \delta\theta $$ **Step 1 — Length of the Neutral Axis (no strain):** The neutral axis $NN$ does not change its length during bending (by definition). Hence: $$ \text{Length of } NN = O_1O_1' = R \cdot \delta\theta $$ **Step 2 — Length of an Arbitrary Fibre at Distance $y$ from the Neutral Axis:** The fibre $AB$ located at distance $y$ from the neutral axis lies on a circle of radius $(R + y)$ if it is *above* the neutral axis, or $(R - y)$ if it is *below*. Thus: $$ \text{Length of } AB = (R + y) \cdot \delta\theta $$ **Step 3 — Longitudinal Strain in Fibre $AB$:** Strain is defined as change in length divided by original length: $$ \varepsilon = \frac{\text{Deformed length} - \text{Original length}}{\text{Original length}} $$ $$ \varepsilon = \frac{(R + y)\delta\theta - R\delta\theta}{R\delta\theta} $$ $$ \varepsilon = \frac{R\delta\theta + y\delta\theta - R\delta\theta}{R\delta\theta} $$ $$ \varepsilon = \frac{y\delta\theta}{R\delta\theta} $$ $$ \boxed{\varepsilon = \frac{y}{R}} $$ **Interpretation of Step 3:** This is the *kinematic* statement derived directly from Assumption 6 (Plane sections remain plane). The strain varies **linearly** with the distance $y$ from the neutral axis, and is **independent of the material** — it is purely a geometric consequence. **Step 4 — Apply Hooke's Law (Assumption 3):** Since the material is linearly elastic, the flexural stress $f$ at the fibre is: $$ f = E \cdot \varepsilon $$ Substituting $\varepsilon = y/R$: $$ f = E \cdot \frac{y}{R} $$ $$ \boxed{\frac{f}{y} = \frac{E}{R}} $$ This is the **first link** of the flexure formula. It states that *stress is proportional to distance from the neutral axis*. **Step 5 — Internal Equilibrium (Sum of Moments):** The flexural stresses acting on the cross-section form a system of internal forces that must be statically equivalent to the applied bending moment $M$. Consider an elemental area $dA$ at distance $y$ from the neutral axis. The elemental force is: $$ dF = f \cdot dA = \frac{E \cdot y}{R} \cdot dA $$ **Step 5a — Check Force Equilibrium (Axial Force = 0):** Since pure bending has no axial force: $$ \int_A f \cdot dA = 0 $$ $$ \int_A \frac{E \cdot y}{R} \cdot dA = 0 $$ $$ \frac{E}{R} \int_A y \cdot dA = 0 $$ Since $E/R \neq 0$, this requires $\int_A y \cdot dA = 0$. This equation defines the **neutral axis as the centroidal axis** of the cross-section — a fundamental result! **Step 5b — Moment Equilibrium:** The internal moment about the neutral axis equals the externally applied bending moment $M$: $$ M = \int_A f \cdot y \cdot dA $$ Substituting $f = (E \cdot y)/R$: $$ M = \int_A \frac{E \cdot y}{R} \cdot y \cdot dA $$ $$ M = \frac{E}{R} \int_A y^2 \, dA $$ By definition, $\int_A y^2 \, dA = I$, the second moment of area about the neutral axis. Therefore: $$ M = \frac{E \cdot I}{R} $$ Rearranging: $$ \frac{M}{I} = \frac{E}{R} $$ **Step 6 — Combine the Two Relations:** From Step 4: $f/y = E/R$ From Step 5b: $M/I = E/R$ By equating: $$ \boxed{\frac{M}{I} = \frac{f}{y} = \frac{E}{R}} $$ This is the **complete Bending Equation (Flexure Formula)** — the central result of Module 3. **Step 7 — Maximum Bending Stress:** At the extreme fibre, $y = y_{\max}$ (top or bottom of the cross-section): $$ f_{\max} = \frac{M \cdot y_{\max}}{I} = \frac{M}{Z} $$ where $Z = I/y_{\max}$ is the **Section Modulus**. ### Analytical Matrix — Tracing Each Assumption to Its Role in the Derivation | Assumption Used | Role in Derivation | Step | |---|---|---| | Plane sections remain plane | Linear strain distribution $\varepsilon = y/R$ | Step 3 | | Hooke's Law | Convert strain to stress $f = E\varepsilon$ | Step 4 | | Pure bending ($V = 0$) | Neutral axis coincides with centroid | Step 5a | | Pure bending ($V = 0$) | Constant curvature $R$ along element | Step 0 | | Homogeneity, Isotropy | Constant $E$ throughout the beam | Steps 4 & 5 | | Large radius of curvature | Linear geometric approximations valid | Steps 0–3 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Exam Gold:** If you can replicate the derivation of $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{f}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ from first principles on the answer script, examiners award full marks (typically 7 of 7 for the derivation sub-question). Memorising the formula alone without derivation carries only 3–4 marks. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### Diagram 1: Geometric Setup of the Bent Beam Element The following Mermaid block diagram maps the *logical architecture* of the simple bending problem — from external load to internal stress distribution. This is the **Block-Level Functional Architecture** representation as per the protocol fallback for complex physical schematics. ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["External Load
Transverse Force / Moment"] nodeB["Beam Element
Length delta x"] nodeC["Pure Bending State
M constant, V equals zero"] nodeD["Cross Section Before
Plane and Perpendicular"] nodeE["Cross Section After
Plane and Perpendicular"] nodeF["Neutral Axis NN
Zero Strain Fibres"] nodeG["Fibre at Distance y
Strain epsilon equals y over R"] nodeH["Apply Hookes Law
Stress f equals E times epsilon"] nodeI["Internal Moment Equilibrium
M equals integral f y dA"] nodeJ["Second Moment of Area
I equals integral y squared dA"] nodeK["Master Bending Equation
M over I equals f over y equals E over R"] nodeL["Maximum Bending Stress
f max equals M over Z"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD nodeC --> nodeE nodeD --> nodeF nodeE --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeG nodeG --> nodeH nodeH --> nodeI nodeI --> nodeJ nodeH --> nodeK nodeI --> nodeK nodeJ --> nodeK nodeK --> nodeL ``` ### Diagram 2: Strain and Stress Distribution Across the Cross-Section The following Mermaid block captures the *linear variation* of strain and stress across the depth of the cross-section — a critical visual concept for KTU board questions. ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeTop["Top Extreme Fibre
y equals plus h over 2
Maximum Tension
f max plus"] nodeMidT["Upper Fibre
Linear Variation"] nodeNA["Neutral Axis
y equals zero
Strain equals zero
Stress equals zero"] nodeMidB["Lower Fibre
Linear Variation"] nodeBot["Bottom Extreme Fibre
y equals minus h over 2
Maximum Compression
f max minus"] nodeTop --> nodeMidT --> nodeNA --> nodeMidB --> nodeBot ``` ### Diagram 3: Decision Flow — When Simple Bending Assumptions Are Valid This decision flowchart helps the student judge whether the simple bending theory is applicable in a given engineering scenario. ```mermaid flowchart TD qA{"Is bending moment
constant along length?"} qB{"Is material
linearly elastic?"} qC{"Is radius of curvature
large vs depth?"} qD{"Is cross-section
constant?"} yes1["Yes - Continue"] no1["No - Use General
Beam Theory with V"] yes2["Yes - Continue"] no2["No - Use Plastic
or Non-linear Analysis"] yes3["Yes - Continue"] no3["No - Use Timoshenko
Beam Theory"] yes4["Yes - Continue"] no4["No - Use Tapered
Beam Analysis"] result["Simple Bending Theory
APPLICABLE
Use M over I equals f over y equals E over R"] qA --> yes1 qA --> no1 yes1 --> qB qB --> yes2 qB --> no2 yes2 --> qC qC --> yes3 qC --> no3 yes3 --> qD qD --> yes4 qD --> no4 yes4 --> result ``` > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to *"state the conditions under which the simple bending formula is valid"*, narrate the **YES path** of the decision flowchart above. This earns full conceptual marks. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Question 1.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** State any **six** assumptions made in the theory of simple bending. **Model Answer (6 × 0.5 = 3 marks):** 1. The material of the beam is **homogeneous** and **isotropic**. 2. The beam material obeys **Hooke's Law** (linear elastic behaviour) within the elastic limit. 3. The beam is initially **straight** and free from any residual stresses. 4. The beam has a **uniform cross-section** along its length (prismatic beam). 5. **Plane sections** of the beam before bending remain plane and perpendicular to the neutral axis after bending. 6. The beam is subjected to **pure bending** — the bending moment is constant, and the transverse shear force is zero. 7. The **radius of curvature** is large compared to the cross-sectional dimensions. 8. **Shear deformation** is negligible; only bending strains are considered. *(Any six of the above — award 0.5 mark each.)* --- **Question 2.** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** Define **pure bending**. Why is the neutral axis in a beam subjected to pure bending coincident with the centroidal axis? **Model Answer:** **Pure Bending (1.5 marks):** Pure bending is the state of bending in which the bending moment is constant along the length of the beam and the transverse shear force is zero. The beam bends into a circular arc of constant radius of curvature. **Why NA coincides with Centroidal Axis (1.5 marks):** For pure bending, the net axial force on the cross-section is zero: $$ \int_A f \, dA = 0 $$ Since $f = E \cdot y / R$, this becomes: $$ \frac{E}{R} \int_A y \, dA = 0 $$ As $E/R \neq 0$, we require $\int_A y \, dA = 0$. By definition, this integral vanishes only when measured from the **centroidal axis**. Hence the neutral axis must coincide with the centroidal axis of the cross-section. --- ### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- #### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **(a)** State and justify the assumptions in the theory of simple bending. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Starting from the assumptions, derive the **bending equation** $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{f}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ for a beam subjected to pure bending. **(7 marks)** ##### Model Solution **(a) Assumptions and Justification (7 marks):** | No. | Assumption | Justification | |---|---|---| | 1 | Homogeneous and isotropic material | Allows a single scalar Young's Modulus $E$ to describe elastic behaviour at every point. | | 2 | Hooke's Law valid (linear elastic) | Ensures superposition is valid and stress is linearly related to strain. | | 3 | Beam initially straight | Provides a unique reference geometry; eliminates initial curvature effects. | | 4 | Constant cross-section (prismatic) | Ensures $I$ and $Z$ do not vary along the length. | | 5 | Plane sections remain plane and perpendicular to NA after bending | Eliminates shear distortion; allows linear strain variation across depth. | | 6 | Pure bending (no transverse shear) | Bending moment is constant; radius of curvature $R$ is constant. | | 7 | Radius of curvature large compared to beam depth | Validates small deformation theory and linear approximations. | | 8 | Negligible shear deformation | Only flexural (bending) strains are considered. | **(b) Derivation of the Bending Equation (7 marks):** Consider a small element of length $\delta x$ cut from a beam in pure bending. Let $NN$ be the neutral axis, and let a fibre $AB$ lie at distance $y$ from $NN$. **Geometric Step:** Before bending: length of element along NA = $R \cdot \delta\theta$ After bending: length of fibre $AB$ at distance $y$ from NA = $(R + y)\cdot \delta\theta$ **Strain at fibre $AB$:** $$ \varepsilon = \frac{(R + y)\delta\theta - R\delta\theta}{R\delta\theta} = \frac{y}{R} $$ **Stress from Hooke's Law:** $$ f = E \cdot \varepsilon = \frac{E \cdot y}{R} \Rightarrow \frac{f}{y} = \frac{E}{R} $$ **Moment Equilibrium:** The internal moment about the NA must equal the applied bending moment $M$: $$ M = \int_A f \cdot y \, dA = \int_A \frac{E \cdot y}{R} \cdot y \, dA = \frac{E}{R}\int_A y^2 \, dA $$ By definition, $\int_A y^2 \, dA = I$, hence: $$ M = \frac{E \cdot I}{R} \Rightarrow \frac{M}{I} = \frac{E}{R} $$ **Combining both relations:** $$ \boxed{\frac{M}{I} = \frac{f}{y} = \frac{E}{R}} $$ --- #### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** **(a)** Explain with neat sketches the variation of **strain** and **bending stress** across the depth of a symmetric cross-section under pure bending. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For a rectangular cross-section of breadth $b$ and depth $d$ subjected to a constant bending moment $M$, show that the **section modulus** $Z = \dfrac{b \cdot d^2}{6}$. **(7 marks)** ##### Model Solution **(a) Strain and Stress Variation (7 marks):** Across the depth of a symmetric cross-section, the neutral axis passes through the centroid. By Assumption 6 (plane sections remain plane), the strain varies linearly with distance from the neutral axis: $$ \varepsilon = \frac{y}{R} $$ At $y = 0$ (neutral axis): $\varepsilon = 0$. At $y = +d/2$ (top extreme fibre): $\varepsilon = +d/(2R)$ — maximum tensile strain. At $y = -d/2$ (bottom extreme fibre): $\varepsilon = -d/(2R)$ — maximum compressive strain. By Hooke's Law, the bending stress $f = E \cdot \varepsilon$ also varies linearly: $$ f = \frac{E \cdot y}{R} $$ Hence the bending stress distribution is **linear**, with: - $f = 0$ at the neutral axis - $f = +f_{\max}$ at the top fibre (tension) - $f = -f_{\max}$ at the bottom fibre (compression) The neutral surface is the **boundary between tension and compression**. **(b) Section Modulus of a Rectangle (7 marks):** For a rectangular cross-section of breadth $b$ and depth $d$: **Second Moment of Area about NA:** By integrating over the cross-section using $dA = b \cdot dy$: $$ I = \int_A y^2 \, dA = \int_{-d/2}^{+d/2} y^2 \cdot b \, dy $$ $$ I = b \left[ \frac{y^3}{3} \right]_{-d/2}^{+d/2} $$ $$ I = b \left( \frac{(d/2)^3}{3} - \frac{(-d/2)^3}{3} \right) $$ $$ I = b \left( \frac{d^3/8}{3} + \frac{d^3/8}{3} \right) = b \cdot \frac{d^3}{12} $$ $$ \boxed{I = \frac{b \cdot d^3}{12}} $$ **Section Modulus:** $$ Z = \frac{I}{y_{\max}} = \frac{b \cdot d^3 / 12}{d/2} $$ $$ Z = \frac{b \cdot d^3}{12} \cdot \frac{2}{d} = \frac{b \cdot d^2}{6} $$ $$ \boxed{Z = \frac{b \cdot d^2}{6}} $$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Forgetting the Centroidal Axis Proof (–2 marks):** Many students state the bending equation without proving that the neutral axis coincides with the centroid. Always include the equilibrium equation $\int_A y \, dA = 0$ in your derivation. > 2. **Mixing Units (–1 to –2 marks):** Ensure $M$ is in $\text{N}\cdot\text{mm}$, $I$ in $\text{mm}^4$, $y$ in $\text{mm}$, and $f$ in $\text{N/mm}^2$. KTU boards are strict about unit consistency. > 3. **Incorrect Sign Convention (–1 mark):** In ship hull bending, **sagging** produces tension on the deck and compression on the keel; **hogging** is the reverse. Always state the sign convention clearly. > 4. **Skipping the Geometric Diagram (–1 mark):** For Part B derivation questions, a clearly labelled diagram (showing $\delta x$, $\delta\theta$, $R$, $y$, and fibres $AB$ and $NN$) is **mandatory** and carries 1–2 marks. > 5. **Confusing Section Modulus with Moment of Inertia (–1 mark):** $Z$ has units of $\text{mm}^3$, while $I$ has units of $\text{mm}^4$. They are not interchangeable. --- ### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] **Simple bending** = constant bending moment + zero shear force → uniform curvature. - [x] **11 key assumptions:** Homogeneity, Isotropy, Hooke's Law, Initial Straightness, Prismatic Section, Plane Sections Remain Plane, Pure Bending, Large Radius of Curvature, No Shear Deformation, No Residual Stresses. - [x] **Master Flexure Formula:** $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{f}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ — the cornerstone of Module 3. - [x] **Neutral Axis = Centroidal Axis** (proven from $\int_A y \, dA = 0$ for pure bending). - [x] **Strain varies linearly:** $\varepsilon = y/R$ (geometric, material-independent). - [x] **Stress varies linearly:** $f = E y / R$ (uses Hooke's Law). - [x] **Section Modulus:** $Z = I/y_{\max}$ — measures a beam's flexural strength. - [x] **Maximum Bending Stress:** $f_{\max} = M/Z$ — occurs at the extreme fibre. - [x] **Naval Architecture Link:** Hull girder bending under wave loads uses $f_{\max} = M/Z$ to predict longitudinal stress. - [x] **Common Cross-Sections to Remember:** - Rectangle: $I = bd^3/12$, $Z = bd^2/6$ - Circle: $I = \pi d^4/64$, $Z = \pi d^3/32$ - Hollow Rectangle: $I = (BD^3 - bd^3)/12$ - [x] **Units:** Always use **N, mm, MPa** in KTU board answers (SI consistency). - [x] **Exam Tip:** Always draw a labelled bending diagram and state the assumption justifying each derivation step.

topics.title: Neutral Axis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST303 - SOLID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Neutral Axis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Neutral Axis (NA):** In a beam undergoing pure bending, the *neutral axis* is the longitudinal line (or trace on the cross-section) in which the **longitudinal fibre experiences zero longitudinal strain and therefore zero bending stress**. It is the locus of points on the cross-section where the bending stress $\sigma$ changes sign — from **compressive** on one side to **tensile** on the other.

For a beam of uniform material subjected to **pure bending** in a plane of symmetry:

$$\sigma_x = -\frac{M \cdot y}{I_z}$$

The neutral axis is the locus defined by $\sigma_x = 0$, which mathematically reduces to:

$$y = 0 \quad \text{(relative to the neutral axis itself)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **For symmetric sections bent about an axis of symmetry, the neutral axis always coincides with the centroidal axis of the cross-section.** This is a *theorems result*, not an assumption — it is derived from the condition of zero net axial force during pure bending.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Plain English Explanation

Imagine holding a soft **rubber eraser** between your two palms and gently bending it so that the top surface becomes concave (curves inward) and the bottom surface becomes convex (bulges outward):

- The **top fibres** get *squeezed together* → they are in **compression** (negative strain).
- The **bottom fibres** get *stretched apart* → they are in **tension** (positive strain).
- Somewhere in the middle, there is a thin layer of material that is **neither squeezed nor stretched**. It is exactly the same length as before bending.

> **That untouched, in-between layer is the Neutral Axis.**

In a sailing analogy, think of a **ship's hull girder** in still water: the **keel region** (bottom) is in tension while the **deck** (top) is in compression when the ship is in a sagging (hogging inverse) condition. Somewhere between the deck and the bottom plate lies the **neutral axis** — a horizontal line through the hull cross-section where no longitudinal stress acts. Naval architects carefully position structural members (deck stringers, bottom longitudinals) relative to this axis to maximize their efficiency.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Young's Modulus ($E$):** Material property; for mild steel $E = 2.0 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$ (≈ **200 GPa**).
- **Radius of Curvature ($\rho$):** Measured in **metres (m)** or **millimetres (mm)**.
- **Bending Stress ($\sigma$):** Measured in **N/mm² (MPa)** or **Pa**.
- **Second Moment of Area ($I$):** Measured in **m⁴**, **mm⁴**, or **cm⁴**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Logic — Why the Neutral Axis Exists

When a beam is bent, it is intuitive that the curvature creates non-uniform deformation:

- **Outer fibres** (farthest from the centre of curvature) **stretch** → tensile strain.
- **Inner fibres** (closest to the centre of curvature) **compress** → compressive strain.
- Since strain varies **linearly** with distance from the centre of curvature, there must be a point where it is **exactly zero**.

This point traces a continuous line along the length of the beam → the **Neutral Axis**.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Pure Bending (Bent About the $z$-axis)

The following assumptions form the classical **Euler–Bernoulli beam theory** (a KTU must-know):

1. **Plane sections remain plane and normal to the longitudinal axis** after bending (kinematic assumption).
2. **The material is homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's Law** in the elastic range.
3. **The beam is initially straight and unstressed.**
4. **The cross-section is constant along the length** (prismatic beam).
5. **Pure bending exists** — constant bending moment, no shear, no axial force.

Under these assumptions:

| Step | Logical Outcome | Physical Meaning |
|------|-----------------|------------------|
| 1 | Longitudinal strain $\varepsilon_x = -\dfrac{y}{\rho}$ | Strain is linear through depth |
| 2 | Hooke's Law: $\sigma_x = E \varepsilon_x = -\dfrac{E \, y}{\rho}$ | Stress is linear through depth |
| 3 | Net axial force $N = \int_A \sigma_x \, dA = 0$ | No external axial load (pure bending) |
| 4 | $\int_A y \, dA = 0$ | Neutral axis passes through the **centroid** of the area |
| 5 | Bending moment $M_z = \int_A \sigma_x \cdot y \, dA = \dfrac{E}{\rho} \int_A y^2 \, dA$ | Defines the second moment of area $I_z$ |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All key formulas for the Neutral Axis topic in one consolidated table. Memorize these for the ESE.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\varepsilon_x = -\dfrac{y}{\rho}$ | Longitudinal fibre strain at distance $y$ from NA | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $\sigma_x = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I_z}$ | Bending stress at distance $y$ from NA (sign convention) | N/m² or MPa |
| 3 | $\dfrac{M}{I_z} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{\rho}$ | Master flexure formula (the "three-equation identity") | Variable |
| 4 | $\rho = \dfrac{E I_z}{M}$ | Radius of curvature of the neutral axis | m or mm |
| 5 | $I_z = \int_A y^2 \, dA$ | Second moment of area about the neutral axis | m⁴ or mm⁴ |
| 6 | $\bar{y} = \dfrac{\int y \, dA}{A}$ | Centroid location (neutral axis must coincide for symmetric bending) | m or mm |
| 7 | $\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{M \, c}{I_z} = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ | Maximum bending stress at extreme fibre ($c$ = distance to extreme fibre) | N/m² |
| 8 | $Z = \dfrac{I_z}{c}$ | Section Modulus | m³ or mm³ |

> **Important notational caution in markdown tables:** the absolute value of a quantity is written as $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ — never using the pipe character `|` — to avoid breaking table syntax.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Naval Architecture:** In a ship hull girder, the position of the neutral axis determines the **section modulus** ($Z$) and hence the **hull girder strength**. Asymmetric hull sections (e.g., with a deckhouse) shift the NA away from the geometric centre, which alters bending stress distribution.
- **Civil Engineering:** Reinforced concrete beams are designed so the steel rebars (in tension zone) are placed as far as possible from the neutral axis to maximize lever arm.
- **Aerospace:** Wing spars of aircraft are designed with caps far from the NA to resist bending efficiently.
- **Mechanical:** Machine shafts, crane jibs, leaf springs — all are optimized with respect to NA position.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Bending Stress Formula (Bent About $z$-axis)

**Given:** A prismatic beam of uniform material, length $L$, cross-sectional area $A$, subjected to a pure bending moment $M_z$ about the $z$-axis. The original longitudinal axis is the $x$-axis.

**To Prove:** $\sigma_x = \dfrac{M_z \, y}{I_z}$

### Step 1 — Geometric Compatibility (Strain Distribution)

Consider a small element of length $dx$ on the neutral axis. After bending, the element subtends an angle $d\theta$ at the centre of curvature $O$, with radius of curvature $\rho$.

- The length of the element on the neutral axis: $L_0 = \rho \, d\theta$
- The length of the element at a distance $y$ from the NA: $L_y = (\rho - y) \, d\theta$ (using the sign convention that $y$ is positive upward toward the centre of curvature)

The change in length of the fibre at distance $y$ is:

$$\Delta L = L_0 - L_y = \rho \, d\theta - (\rho - y) \, d\theta = y \, d\theta$$

Since the original length is $L_0 = \rho \, d\theta$, the longitudinal strain is:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{y \, d\theta}{\rho \, d\theta} = \frac{y}{\rho}$$

> **Note on sign convention:** Many texts write $\varepsilon_x = -y/\rho$ where $y$ is measured upward. The negative sign indicates that positive $y$ (toward the centre of curvature) produces compression. We will use $\sigma_x = -M y / I_z$ with $y$ measured upward to remain consistent with KTU convention.

### Step 2 — Constitutive Relation (Hooke's Law)

Since the material is linear-elastic, isotropic, and remains in the elastic range:

$$\sigma_x = E \, \varepsilon_x = E \cdot \frac{y}{\rho}$$

### Step 3 — Equilibrium — Zero Net Axial Force

The resultant axial force on the cross-section must vanish (no external axial load in pure bending):

$$N = \int_A \sigma_x \, dA = \frac{E}{\rho} \int_A y \, dA = 0$$

Since $E/\rho \neq 0$, we conclude:

$$\int_A y \, dA = 0$$

The quantity $\int_A y \, dA$ is, by definition, the **first moment of area** about the $x$-axis. Setting this to zero means the $x$-axis is the **centroidal axis** of the cross-section. Therefore:

> **The neutral axis passes through the centroid of the cross-section.**

### Step 4 — Equilibrium — Bending Moment

The internal bending moment must equal the moment produced by the stress distribution:

$$M_z = - \int_A \sigma_x \cdot y \, dA = - \frac{E}{\rho} \int_A y^2 \, dA$$

Define the **Second Moment of Area**:

$$I_z = \int_A y^2 \, dA$$

Therefore:

$$M_z = \frac{E \, I_z}{\rho}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{M_z}{I_z} = \frac{E}{\rho}$$

### Step 5 — Combining to Obtain the Flexure Formula

Starting from $\sigma_x = E y / \rho$, substitute $\rho = E I_z / M_z$:

$$\sigma_x = \frac{E \, y}{E I_z / M_z} = \frac{M_z \, y}{I_z}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_x = \frac{M_z \, y}{I_z}}$$

This is the **Euler–Bernoulli flexure formula**, the cornerstone of beam bending analysis.

## 3.2 Derivation: Neutral Axis for an Asymmetric (Non-Homogeneous) Section

In a **composite beam** made of two materials (e.g., steel reinforcement embedded in concrete) with Young's moduli $E_1$ and $E_2$, the NA no longer passes through the geometric centroid — it passes through the **elastic centroid (centroidal axis of the transformed section)**.

The condition of zero net axial force becomes:

$$\int_{A_1} E_1 \, y \, dA + \int_{A_2} E_2 \, y \, dA = 0$$

Dividing through by $E_1$ (transforming section 2 into equivalent section 1) and defining the modular ratio $n = E_2 / E_1$:

$$\int_{A_1} y \, dA + n \int_{A_2} y \, dA = 0$$

The neutral axis passes through the centroid of the **transformed section** where the equivalent area is $A_{\text{eq}} = A_1 + n A_2$.

The distance $\bar{y}$ of the NA from a reference axis is:

$$\bar{y} = \frac{A_1 \, y_1 + n A_2 \, y_2}{A_1 + n A_2}$$

where $y_1$ and $y_2$ are the centroidal distances of the individual areas from the reference.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** For ship hull girders with asymmetric stiffener arrangements, the neutral axis is computed using the *transformed section method* treating all steel as a single material. Always verify whether a section is symmetric or asymmetric before assuming NA passes through the geometric centroid.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A simply-supported steel beam of length 3 m carries a central point load of 40 kN. The cross-section is rectangular, 100 mm wide × 200 mm deep. Find:
- (i) Maximum bending stress
- (ii) Position of the neutral axis
- (iii) Radius of curvature at the section of maximum moment

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Maximum Bending Moment**

$$M_{\max} = \frac{P L}{4} = \frac{40 \times 3}{4} = 30 \text{ kN·m} = 30 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 2 — Second Moment of Area**

For a rectangle: $I = \dfrac{b d^3}{12}$

$$I = \frac{100 \times 200^3}{12} = \frac{100 \times 8 \times 10^6}{12} = 66.67 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 3 — Position of Neutral Axis**

For a symmetric rectangular section, NA passes through the centroid. Distance from NA to extreme fibre:

$$c = \frac{d}{2} = 100 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum Bending Stress**

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M \, c}{I} = \frac{30 \times 10^6 \times 100}{66.67 \times 10^6} = 45 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (MPa)}$$

**Step 5 — Radius of Curvature**

Using $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm² for steel:

$$\rho = \frac{E \, I}{M} = \frac{2 \times 10^5 \times 66.67 \times 10^6}{30 \times 10^6} = 4.44 \times 10^5 \text{ mm} = 444.4 \text{ m}$$

This is a very large radius, confirming that beam deflections are small in elastic bending.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Stress Distribution Through Beam Depth

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the relationship between geometric position, strain, and stress in a bent beam, emphasizing the **neutral axis** as the central reference:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Beam subjected to pure bending moment M] --> B[Cross section deforms into circular arc]
    B --> C[Centre of curvature at distance rho from NA]
    C --> D{Fibre location relative to NA}
    D -->|y greater than 0 above NA| E[Compression zone - negative strain - negative stress]
    D -->|y equals 0 on NA| F[NEUTRAL AXIS - zero strain - zero stress]
    D -->|y less than 0 below NA| G[Tension zone - positive strain - positive stress]
    E --> H[Linear stress distribution sigma equals M y over I]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Resultant moment equals M]
    H --> J[Resultant axial force equals 0]
    I --> K[Section modulus Z equals I over c]
    J --> L[NA coincides with centroid for symmetric sections]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Position of NA in Different Beam Configurations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SYM[Symmetric Section]
        S1[Rectangle] --> S2[NA at geometric centre]
        S3[Circle] --> S2
        S4[I section] --> S2
    end
    subgraph ASYM[Asymmetric Section]
        A1[T section] --> A2[NA shifted toward larger flange]
        A3[L section] --> A2
    end
    subgraph COMP[Composite Section]
        C1[Steel beam with concrete slab] --> C2[NA at elastic centroid via transformed section]
    end
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Information Flow for NA Calculation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INPUT[Input: Cross section geometry and material] --> DET1{Is section symmetric?}
    DET1 -->|Yes homogeneous| PATH1[Use geometric centroid]
    DET1 -->|No homogeneous| PATH2[Compute first moment of area about trial axis and equate to zero]
    DET1 -->|Composite multiple materials| PATH3[Apply transformed section method with modular ratio n]
    PATH1 --> OUTPUT[Neutral axis location y bar]
    PATH2 --> OUTPUT
    PATH3 --> OUTPUT
    OUTPUT --> APPLY[Use in flexure formula sigma equals M y over I]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]
**Define the term "neutral axis" of a beam cross-section. Why does it coincide with the centroidal axis for a beam in pure bending?**

**Model Answer:**

The *neutral axis* of a beam cross-section is the line in the cross-section, perpendicular to the plane of bending, along which the **longitudinal bending stress is zero** during pure bending. Fibres on one side of this axis are in compression while those on the other side are in tension.

In pure bending, the resultant axial force on the cross-section must be zero (no external axial load). Using the bending stress formula:

$$\int_A \sigma_x \, dA = \frac{M}{I} \int_A y \, dA = 0$$

Since $M/I \neq 0$, we require $\int_A y \, dA = 0$, which is precisely the condition defining the **centroid** of the area. Hence, the neutral axis coincides with the centroidal axis.

> **Marks Distribution (KTU pattern):** Definition — 1 mark; Stress relation — 1 mark; Centroid condition — 1 mark.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**State the assumptions made in the derivation of the pure bending equation $\sigma = \dfrac{M y}{I}$.**

**Model Answer:**

1. The beam is initially straight, prismatic, and of uniform cross-section.
2. The material is **homogeneous**, **isotropic**, and obeys **Hooke's Law** (linearly elastic).
3. The cross-section has at least one axis of symmetry, and bending occurs in a plane containing that axis.
4. **Plane sections remain plane and normal to the longitudinal axis** after bending (Euler–Bernoulli hypothesis).
5. The radius of curvature is large compared with cross-sectional dimensions, so transverse shear deformation is negligible.
6. **Pure bending** exists — bending moment is constant; shear force and axial force are zero.

> **Marks Distribution:** Each valid assumption — 0.5 marks; minimum 6 expected for full marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Module 3, Model]

**(a)** Derive the bending equation $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ for a beam of uniform cross-section subjected to pure bending. State clearly the assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A simply-supported beam of span 4 m has a rectangular cross-section 80 mm wide and 120 mm deep. It carries a uniformly distributed load of 6 kN/m over its entire span. Calculate:
   (i) The maximum bending stress in the beam,
   (ii) The position of the neutral axis,
   (iii) The radius of curvature at the section of maximum bending moment. Take $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm². **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Assumptions:** (1) Beam is initially straight and prismatic. (2) Material is homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic (Hooke's Law valid). (3) Cross-section has an axis of symmetry and bending occurs in the plane of symmetry. (4) Plane sections remain plane and perpendicular to the neutral axis after bending (no warping). (5) Pure bending exists; shear and axial forces are zero.

**Derivation:**

Consider a small element of length $dx$ on the neutral axis of the bent beam. Let $\rho$ be the radius of curvature of the neutral axis. The fibre at a distance $y$ from the NA has length:

$$L_y = (\rho - y) \, d\theta$$

The strain in this fibre:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\rho \, d\theta - (\rho - y) \, d\theta}{\rho \, d\theta} = \frac{y}{\rho}$$

By Hooke's Law: $\sigma = E \varepsilon = \dfrac{E y}{\rho}$

For pure bending, $\int_A \sigma \, dA = 0$:

$$\int_A \frac{E y}{\rho} \, dA = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \int_A y \, dA = 0$$

Hence the NA passes through the **centroid** of the cross-section.

Bending moment:

$$M = \int_A \sigma \, y \, dA = \frac{E}{\rho} \int_A y^2 \, dA = \frac{E I}{\rho}$$

Combining the three relations:

$$\frac{M}{I} = \frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{E}{\rho} \qquad \blacksquare$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Maximum Bending Moment**

For a simply-supported beam with UDL:

$$M_{\max} = \frac{w L^2}{8} = \frac{6 \times 4^2}{8} = 12 \text{ kN·m} = 12 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 2 — Section Properties**

$$I = \frac{b d^3}{12} = \frac{80 \times 120^3}{12} = \frac{80 \times 1.728 \times 10^6}{12} = 11.52 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 3 — Position of Neutral Axis**

For a symmetric rectangle, NA is at the geometric centre. Distance from NA to extreme fibre:

$$c = \frac{d}{2} = 60 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum Bending Stress**

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M \, c}{I} = \frac{12 \times 10^6 \times 60}{11.52 \times 10^6} = 62.5 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (MPa)}$$

**Step 5 — Radius of Curvature**

$$\rho = \frac{E I}{M} = \frac{2 \times 10^5 \times 11.52 \times 10^6}{12 \times 10^6} = 1.92 \times 10^5 \text{ mm} = 192 \text{ m}$$

> **Examiner's Tip:** Always state units explicitly. Marks are often deducted when SI consistency is missing (e.g., mixing m and mm).

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – Module 3, Model]

**(a)** Explain, with neat sketches, the **bending stress distribution** across the cross-section of a beam in pure bending. Indicate clearly the position of the neutral axis and the location of maximum tensile and compressive stresses. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An I-section beam has flange dimensions 150 mm × 20 mm and web dimensions 20 mm × 200 mm. Calculate:
   (i) The moment of inertia of the cross-section about the neutral axis,
   (ii) The maximum tensile and compressive stresses if the section is subjected to a bending moment of 50 kN·m (sagging). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

When a beam is subjected to a sagging (concave-up) bending moment, the top fibres shorten (compression) and the bottom fibres elongate (tension). The bending stress $\sigma$ varies **linearly** with the distance $y$ from the neutral axis, given by:

$$\sigma = \frac{M y}{I}$$

At the neutral axis: $\sigma = 0$ (no longitudinal stress).

- **Top extreme fibre** ($y = +c$): maximum compressive stress $\sigma_c = \dfrac{M c}{I}$.
- **Bottom extreme fibre** ($y = -c$): maximum tensile stress $\sigma_t = -\dfrac{M c}{I}$ (equal in magnitude for symmetric sections).

The stress diagram is therefore a **triangle** with its apex at the neutral axis and its base spanning between the two extreme fibres.

For an *unsymmetric* section (e.g., a T-section), the neutral axis is shifted toward the larger flange, so the maximum tensile and compressive stresses are no longer equal in magnitude — the closer extreme fibre carries the larger stress.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Locate the Neutral Axis**

The I-section is symmetric about the horizontal centroidal axis. NA is at the geometric centre.

**Step 2 — Moment of Inertia**

Decompose the I-section into three rectangles: top flange, web, and bottom flange.

$$I = I_{\text{flange,top}} + I_{\text{web}} + I_{\text{flange,bottom}}$$

Using the parallel axis theorem for each flange (distance from NA to flange centroid = 110 mm):

$$I_{\text{flange,top}} = \frac{150 \times 20^3}{12} + (150 \times 20) \times 110^2$$

$$= 100{,}000 + 3000 \times 12{,}100 = 100{,}000 + 36{,}300{,}000 = 36.4 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$$

For the web (centred on NA, so no parallel axis term):

$$I_{\text{web}} = \frac{20 \times 200^3}{12} = \frac{20 \times 8 \times 10^6}{12} = 13.33 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$$

By symmetry, the bottom flange gives the same contribution as the top flange:

$$I_{\text{total}} = 2 \times 36.4 \times 10^6 + 13.33 \times 10^6 = 86.13 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 3 — Extreme Fibre Distance**

$$c = \frac{200}{2} + 20 = 120 \text{ mm}$$

(Note: total depth = $20 + 200 + 20 = 240$ mm; so $c = 120$ mm from NA to outer fibre of flange.)

**Step 4 — Maximum Bending Stresses**

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M \, c}{I} = \frac{50 \times 10^6 \times 120}{86.13 \times 10^6} = 69.66 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

For **sagging** moment:
- Top fibre: **compressive** stress = $69.66$ MPa
- Bottom fibre: **tensile** stress = $69.66$ MPa

(Since the section is symmetric, the magnitudes are equal.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the parallel axis term for flanges** — the most common error in I-section $I$ calculations. Each flange is NOT at the NA; you must add $A d^2$ where $d$ is the distance from flange centroid to NA.
> 2. **Mixing up the extreme fibre distance $c$**: students often use half the web depth (100 mm) instead of the half total depth (120 mm). The flange outer fibre is the extreme fibre, not the web-flange junction.
> 3. **Sign convention errors**: in sagging, top is compression and bottom is tension. Marks are deducted if the signs are interchanged.
> 4. **Unit consistency**: convert kN·m to N·mm (multiply by $10^6$) before using formulas with $I$ in mm⁴.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the Neutral Axis (Module 3):**

- **Definition:** NA is the locus of points on the cross-section with **zero longitudinal bending stress** during pure bending.
- **Co-location with Centroid:** For a *homogeneous, symmetric* section in pure bending, the NA passes through the **centroid** of the cross-section (derived from zero net axial force).
- **Asymmetric Sections:** For T-, L-, or ship-hull-girder sections, the NA is found by setting $\int y \, dA = 0$ about a reference axis — it does NOT coincide with the geometric centre.
- **Composite Sections:** For multi-material sections, use the **transformed section method** with the modular ratio $n = E_2/E_1$.
- **Master Flexure Formula:** $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{\rho}$ — memorize as a single identity; it links moment, stress, geometry, and curvature.
- **Strain varies linearly** with $y$; **stress varies linearly** with $y$ (for linear-elastic material).
- **Maximum stress** occurs at the extreme fibre farthest from the NA: $\sigma_{\max} = M c / I = M / Z$.
- **Sign Convention:** Sagging moment → top fibre compression, bottom fibre tension. Hogging moment → top fibre tension, bottom fibre compression.
- **Naval Architecture Context:** The hull girder NA position is critical for computing **section modulus** $Z$ and ensuring adequate hull strength in sagging and hogging conditions.
- **Assumptions of Euler–Bernoulli Theory:** Plane sections remain plane, material is linear-elastic, beam is initially straight, pure bending exists, no shear deformation.
- **Units to remember:** $M$ in N·mm, $I$ in mm⁴, $\sigma$ in N/mm² (MPa), $E$ in N/mm², $\rho$ in mm or m.

> **Final KTU Mantra:** *Master the derivation of $\sigma = M y / I$, understand WHY the NA passes through the centroid (zero axial force condition), and practise numerical problems on symmetric and asymmetric sections.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Section Modulus
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST303 - SOLID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Section Modulus

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal KTU-Syllabus Definition

**Section Modulus (Z)** is a purely geometric property of a beam's cross-section, defined as the ratio of the *Second Moment of Area (I)* about the neutral axis to the distance of the extreme fibre from the neutral axis (y_max).

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}}$$

Where:
- $I$ = Second Moment of Area (Moment of Inertia) of the cross-section about the neutral axis (units: mm⁴ or m⁴)
- $y_{max}$ = Maximum perpendicular distance from the Neutral Axis (NA) to the outermost fibre (units: mm or m)
- $Z$ has units of **mm³** or **m³**

The section modulus is the key geometric parameter that appears in the **Flexure Formula** for bending:

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M}{Z}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Section Modulus quantifies the *bending strength efficiency* of a given cross-section. For the same material, a beam with a larger section modulus will develop lower bending stress under an identical bending moment — making it a direct indicator of load-carrying capacity.

---

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are holding a long wooden plank (a beam) and standing on a scaffold with two supports at its ends. When you place a heavy weight in the middle, the plank bends. The plank sags and its top fibres get compressed while its bottom fibres stretch (tension).

Now, consider two planks of the **same material and same length**, but with different cross-sections:
- **Plank A:** Thin and flat (like a ruler) — bends easily, cracks quickly.
- **Plank B:** Thick and deep (like a floor joist) — barely bends, carries much more load.

What is the *single geometric number* that quantifies this difference? That number is the **Section Modulus (Z)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** Think of Z as the *strength score* of a cross-section. The bigger the Z, the more *bending resistance* the section has. Section modulus depends **only on shape and size**, not on the material.

**Key Engineering Insight:** In shipbuilding, the Naval Architect chooses plate thicknesses, stiffener spacing, and girder depths such that the resulting **hull girder section modulus** is sufficient to safely resist the maximum sagging and hogging moments caused by wave loading, cargo, and buoyancy — without exceeding the permissible bending stress in the steel.

---

### Why Section Modulus Matters in Naval Architecture

In ship structural design, the **Hull Girder** is treated as a huge box beam. Its section modulus at any transverse station (frame location) is given by:

$$Z_{hull} = \frac{I_{hull}}{y_{max,hull}}$$

The **midship section** is typically the critical location where maximum bending moments occur, so the **midship section modulus** is the most important design check. Classification societies (IRS, LR, DNV, ABS) prescribe minimum section modulus values based on:
- Ship length (L)
- Block coefficient (C_B)
- Service speed
- Design wave height

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Used in Naval Architecture:** Section modulus is commonly expressed in **cm³** or **m³** when dealing with ship hull girders due to the enormous scale. For example, a VLCC may have a midship section modulus exceeding **25 m³** (≈ 25 × 10⁹ mm³).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Theoretical Foundation: From Flexure Formula to Section Modulus

The flexure (bending) equation is the cornerstone of beam theory. For a beam in pure bending:

$$\frac{M}{I} = \frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{E}{R}$$

Where:
- $M$ = Bending moment at the section (N·mm or N·m)
- $I$ = Second moment of area about the neutral axis (mm⁴)
- $\sigma$ = Bending stress at distance $y$ from the neutral axis (N/mm² or MPa)
- $E$ = Young's Modulus of the material
- $R$ = Radius of curvature of the bent beam

**Step-by-step logic for deriving Z:**

- **Step 1 — General bending stress at any fibre distance y:**

$$\sigma = \frac{M \cdot y}{I}$$

- **Step 2 — Maximum bending stress occurs at the extreme fibre** (where y is maximum, i.e., $y_{max}$):

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M \cdot y_{max}}{I}$$

- **Step 3 — Rearrange to isolate M:**

$$M = \sigma_{max} \cdot \frac{I}{y_{max}}$$

- **Step 4 — Define section modulus** as the bracketed geometric term:

$$\boxed{Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}}}$$

- **Step 5 — Substitute back to get the most-used form of the flexure formula:**

$$\boxed{\sigma_{max} = \frac{M}{Z}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this is the most-used form:** In design, we know the bending moment $M$ (from structural analysis) and the allowable stress $\sigma_{allow}$ (from material codes). The required section modulus follows immediately as $Z_{required} = M / \sigma_{allow}$. This makes Z the *primary design variable* for beam sizing.

---

### 2.2 The Section Modulus as a "Strength Indicator"

The **moment of resistance (M_R)** of a beam is defined as the maximum bending moment that the section can sustain without exceeding the allowable stress:

$$M_R = \sigma_{allow} \cdot Z$$

This is also called the **elastic moment capacity** of the section. For a safe design, we enforce:

$$M_{applied} \leq M_R \quad \Rightarrow \quad M_{applied} \leq \sigma_{allow} \cdot Z$$

This is the fundamental **flexural design inequality** used in KTU problems.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| S.No. | Cross-Section Shape | Moment of Inertia (I) | y_max | Section Modulus (Z) |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | **Rectangle** (width $b$, depth $d$) | $\dfrac{b d^{3}}{12}$ | $\dfrac{d}{2}$ | $\dfrac{b d^{2}}{6}$ |
| 2 | **Solid Circle** (diameter $d$) | $\dfrac{\pi d^{4}}{64}$ | $\dfrac{d}{2}$ | $\dfrac{\pi d^{3}}{32}$ |
| 3 | **Hollow Circle** (Outer $D$, Inner $d$) | $\dfrac{\pi (D^{4} - d^{4})}{64}$ | $\dfrac{D}{2}$ | $\dfrac{\pi (D^{4} - d^{4})}{32 D}$ |
| 4 | **Hollow Rectangle** (Outer $B \times D$, Inner $b \times d$) | $\dfrac{B D^{3} - b d^{3}}{12}$ | $\dfrac{D}{2}$ | $\dfrac{B D^{3} - b d^{3}}{6 D}$ |
| 5 | **Solid Triangle** (base $b$, height $h$, apex up) | $\dfrac{b h^{3}}{36}$ | $\dfrac{2h}{3}$ | $\dfrac{b h^{2}}{24}$ |
| 6 | **I-Section / Built-up Girder** | $I$ (calculated) | $y_{max}$ (from NA) | $\dfrac{I}{y_{max}}$ |
| 7 | **T-Section** | $I$ (calculated) | depends on flange/slab | $\dfrac{I}{y_{max}}$ |

| S.No. | Key Engineering Relationship | Formula | Application |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | Flexure (Bending) Formula | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ | Max bending stress check |
| 2 | Moment of Resistance | $M_R = \sigma_{allow} \cdot Z$ | Capacity check |
| 3 | Required Section Modulus | $Z_{req} = \dfrac{M}{\sigma_{allow}}$ | Design sizing |
| 4 | Section Modulus Definition | $Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{max}}$ | Fundamental relation |
| 5 | Bending Stress at fibre y | $\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ | Stress at any layer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility (Naval Arch. Focus):**
> - **Hull Girder Strength:** The midship section modulus directly governs whether the hull will fail in bending under extreme wave loads.
> - **Stiffener Design:** Local plate panels reinforced with stiffeners have an effective section modulus that must resist local bending from pressure (sea, cargo).
> - **Material Optimization:** For a given required Z, hollow sections (tubes) use far less material than solid sections — directly saving ship displacement and cost.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Section Modulus |
|:---|:---|
| **Shipbuilding** | Hull girder midship section modulus; bulkhead stiffener sizing |
| **Civil Engineering** | Bridge girders, building beams, column base plates |
| **Mechanical Engineering** | Crankshafts, machine tool beds, gear teeth (Lewis form uses Z) |
| **Aerospace** | Wing spar design, fuselage frames |
| **Offshore Structures** | Jacket legs, deck beams, pontoons |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Plastic Section Modulus (Z_p)** and **Elastic Section Modulus (Z_e)** are two distinct quantities. KTU Module-3 questions typically deal with **elastic** Z. Plastic Z applies when the entire cross-section has yielded (limit state design), used in advanced steel design.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Section Modulus of a Rectangle

**Given:** A rectangular cross-section of width $b$ (horizontal) and depth $d$ (vertical), bending about its horizontal neutral axis (centroidal axis).

**Step 1 — Set up the coordinate system:**
- Place origin at the centroid of the rectangle.
- x-axis is horizontal (along width).
- y-axis is vertical (along depth).
- The neutral axis is the x-axis (passing through the centroid).

**Step 2 — Define an elemental area:**
Consider a thin horizontal strip of thickness $dy$ at distance $y$ from the neutral axis. The area of this strip is:

$$dA = b \cdot dy$$

**Step 3 — Write the expression for Second Moment of Area:**

By definition, the second moment of area about the x-axis is:

$$I = \int y^{2} \, dA$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and integrate:**

$$I = \int_{-d/2}^{+d/2} y^{2} \cdot (b \, dy)$$

$$I = b \int_{-d/2}^{+d/2} y^{2} \, dy$$

$$I = b \left[ \frac{y^{3}}{3} \right]_{-d/2}^{+d/2}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the limits:**

$$I = b \left( \frac{(+d/2)^{3}}{3} - \frac{(-d/2)^{3}}{3} \right)$$

$$I = b \left( \frac{d^{3}}{24} + \frac{d^{3}}{24} \right)$$

$$I = b \cdot \frac{2 d^{3}}{24} = \frac{b d^{3}}{12}$$

**Step 6 — Identify y_max:**

For a rectangle, the maximum distance from the neutral axis to the extreme fibre is:

$$y_{max} = \frac{d}{2}$$

**Step 7 — Compute the Section Modulus:**

$$Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}} = \frac{b d^{3} / 12}{d / 2}$$

$$Z = \frac{b d^{3}}{12} \cdot \frac{2}{d} = \frac{b d^{2}}{6}$$

$$\boxed{Z_{rectangle} = \frac{b d^{2}}{6}}$$

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Section Modulus of a Solid Circle

**Given:** A solid circular cross-section of diameter $d$ (radius $r = d/2$).

**Step 1 — Use the standard result for I of a circle about its diameter:**

$$I = \frac{\pi d^{4}}{64}$$

**Step 2 — Identify y_max:**

For a circle, the maximum distance from the centroid (neutral axis) to the extreme fibre is the radius:

$$y_{max} = \frac{d}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Z:**

$$Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}} = \frac{\pi d^{4} / 64}{d / 2}$$

$$Z = \frac{\pi d^{4}}{64} \cdot \frac{2}{d} = \frac{\pi d^{3}}{32}$$

$$\boxed{Z_{circle} = \frac{\pi d^{3}}{32}}$$

---

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Section Modulus of a Hollow Circular Tube

**Given:** A hollow circular tube with outer diameter $D$ and inner diameter $d$.

**Step 1 — Subtract inner void from outer solid (additive property of I):**

$$I = I_{outer} - I_{inner} = \frac{\pi D^{4}}{64} - \frac{\pi d^{4}}{64}$$

$$I = \frac{\pi (D^{4} - d^{4})}{64}$$

**Step 2 — Identify y_max (distance to outermost fibre):**

$$y_{max} = \frac{D}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Z:**

$$Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}} = \frac{\pi (D^{4} - d^{4}) / 64}{D / 2}$$

$$Z = \frac{\pi (D^{4} - d^{4})}{64} \cdot \frac{2}{D} = \frac{\pi (D^{4} - d^{4})}{32 D}$$

$$\boxed{Z_{hollow\,circle} = \frac{\pi (D^{4} - d^{4})}{32 D}}$$

---

### 3.4 Exhaustive Derivation: Section Modulus of a Hollow Rectangle (Box Section)

**Given:** A rectangular hollow section (RHS) of outer dimensions $B \times D$ and uniform wall thickness $t$, so inner dimensions are $(B - 2t) \times (D - 2t)$.

**Step 1 — Compute I using the subtraction method:**

$$I = \frac{B D^{3}}{12} - \frac{(B - 2t)(D - 2t)^{3}}{12}$$

**Step 2 — Identify y_max:**

$$y_{max} = \frac{D}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Z:**

$$Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}} = \frac{2 I}{D}$$

$$\boxed{Z_{RHS} = \frac{B D^{3} - (B - 2t)(D - 2t)^{3}}{6 D}}$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Worked Examples Matrix

#### Worked Example 1 — Rectangular Beam Bending Stress

**Problem:** A timber beam 120 mm wide and 240 mm deep is subjected to a bending moment of 16 kN·m. Determine the maximum bending stress in the beam.

**Given:**
- $b = 120 \, \text{mm}$
- $d = 240 \, \text{mm}$
- $M = 16 \, \text{kN·m} = 16 \times 10^{6} \, \text{N·mm}$

**Step 1 — Compute Z:**

$$Z = \frac{b d^{2}}{6} = \frac{120 \times (240)^{2}}{6}$$

$$Z = \frac{120 \times 57600}{6} = \frac{6912000}{6} = 1152000 \, \text{mm}^{3}$$

$$Z = 1.152 \times 10^{6} \, \text{mm}^{3}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Maximum Bending Stress:**

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{16 \times 10^{6}}{1.152 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$\sigma_{max} = 13.89 \, \text{N/mm}^{2} = 13.89 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Answer:** $\sigma_{max} = 13.89 \, \text{MPa}$

---

#### Worked Example 2 — Required Section Modulus (Design Problem)

**Problem:** A simply-supported steel beam of span 4 m carries a uniformly distributed load of 30 kN/m. If the allowable bending stress is 165 MPa, find the required section modulus.

**Given:**
- Span $L = 4 \, \text{m}$
- UDL $w = 30 \, \text{kN/m}$
- $\sigma_{allow} = 165 \, \text{MPa} = 165 \, \text{N/mm}^{2}$

**Step 1 — Compute Maximum Bending Moment:**

For a simply-supported beam with UDL:

$$M_{max} = \frac{w L^{2}}{8} = \frac{30 \times (4)^{2}}{8} = \frac{30 \times 16}{8} = 60 \, \text{kN·m}$$

$$M_{max} = 60 \times 10^{6} \, \text{N·mm}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Required Section Modulus:**

$$Z_{req} = \frac{M_{max}}{\sigma_{allow}} = \frac{60 \times 10^{6}}{165}$$

$$Z_{req} = 363636.36 \, \text{mm}^{3} \approx 3.636 \times 10^{5} \, \text{mm}^{3}$$

**Answer:** $Z_{req} \approx 3.64 \times 10^{5} \, \text{mm}^{3} = 363.6 \, \text{cm}^{3}$

---

#### Worked Example 3 — Comparing Hollow vs. Solid Section Efficiency

**Problem:** A hollow circular tube has $D = 100 \, \text{mm}$ outer diameter and $d = 60 \, \text{mm}$ inner diameter. A solid circular shaft of the **same cross-sectional area (and hence same weight per unit length)** is to be compared. Find the ratio of their section moduli.

**Given (Hollow):**
- $D = 100 \, \text{mm}$, $d = 60 \, \text{mm}$

**Step 1 — Compute Area of Hollow Section:**

$$A_{hollow} = \frac{\pi}{4} (D^{2} - d^{2}) = \frac{\pi}{4} (100^{2} - 60^{2})$$

$$A_{hollow} = \frac{\pi}{4} (10000 - 3600) = \frac{\pi}{4} (6400) = 1600 \pi \, \text{mm}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Find Equivalent Solid Diameter:**

For equal weight: $A_{solid} = A_{hollow}$

$$\frac{\pi d_{s}^{2}}{4} = 1600 \pi \quad \Rightarrow \quad d_{s}^{2} = 6400 \quad \Rightarrow \quad d_{s} = 80 \, \text{mm}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Z of Hollow Section:**

$$Z_{hollow} = \frac{\pi (D^{4} - d^{4})}{32 D} = \frac{\pi (100^{4} - 60^{4})}{32 \times 100}$$

$$Z_{hollow} = \frac{\pi (10^{8} - 1.296 \times 10^{6})}{3200} = \frac{\pi \times 98.704 \times 10^{6}}{3200}$$

$$Z_{hollow} = \pi \times 30845 = 96899.6 \, \text{mm}^{3}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Z of Solid Section:**

$$Z_{solid} = \frac{\pi d_{s}^{3}}{32} = \frac{\pi (80)^{3}}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 512000}{32} = 16000 \pi$$

$$Z_{solid} = 50265.5 \, \text{mm}^{3}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Ratio:**

$$\frac{Z_{hollow}}{Z_{solid}} = \frac{96899.6}{50265.5} = 1.928$$

**Answer:** The hollow section is **~1.93 times stronger in bending** than a solid shaft of the same weight. This is why ship hulls, aircraft fuselages, and bicycle frames use hollow sections.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flowchart: From Bending Moment to Section Modulus

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Apply Bending Moment M to Beam"] --> B["Cross-section develops linear stress distribution sigma = M*y/I"]
    B --> C["Maximum stress sigma_max at extreme fibre y_max"]
    C --> D["Rearrange: M = sigma_max * (I / y_max)"]
    D --> E["Define Section Modulus: Z = I / y_max"]
    E --> F["Final Flexure Formula: sigma_max = M / Z"]
    F --> G["Design Check: Z_provided greater than or equal to M / sigma_allow"]
    G --> H["Safe Beam Design"]
    styleA fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    styleE fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:3px
    styleF fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc8800,stroke-width:2px
    styleH fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#009933,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Cross-Section Visual Mapping (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[RECTANGLE]
        A1["Width b, Depth d"] --> A2["I = b*d^3 / 12"]
        A2 --> A3["y_max = d / 2"]
        A3 --> A4["Z = b*d^2 / 6"]
    end
    subgraph B[SOLID CIRCLE]
        B1["Diameter d"] --> B2["I = pi*d^4 / 64"]
        B2 --> B3["y_max = d / 2"]
        B3 --> B4["Z = pi*d^3 / 32"]
    end
    subgraph C[HOLLOW CIRCLE]
        C1["Outer D, Inner d"] --> C2["I = pi*(D^4 - d^4) / 64"]
        C2 --> C3["y_max = D / 2"]
        C3 --> C4["Z = pi*(D^4 - d^4) / (32*D)"]
    end
    subgraph D[BOX / RHS SECTION]
        D1["Outer BxD, Inner bxd"] --> D2["I = B*D^3/12 - b*d^3/12"]
        D2 --> D3["y_max = D / 2"]
        D3 --> D4["Z = (B*D^3 - b*d^3) / (6*D)"]
    end
    A4 --> E["UNIFIED FLEXURE FORMULA: sigma_max = M / Z"]
    B4 --> E
    C4 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F["Z governs bending strength efficiency"]
    styleE fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc8800,stroke-width:3px
    styleF fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#009933,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Bending Stress Distribution on a Rectangular Cross-Section (Schematic ASCII Description)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TOP[TOP FIBRE - COMPRESSION]
        T1["sigma_top = -M*Z_top / I"]
    end
    subgraph NA[NEUTRAL AXIS - ZERO STRESS]
        N1["sigma = 0 at y = 0"]
    end
    subgraph BOTTOM[BOTTOM FIBRE - TENSION]
        B1["sigma_bottom = +M*Z_bottom / I"]
    end
    TOP -->|"Linear decrease"| NA
    NA -->|"Linear increase"| BOTTOM
    styleT1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    styleN1 fill:#f0f0f0,stroke:#666666
    styleB1 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide for Students:** The maximum tensile and compressive stresses at the extreme fibres are equal in magnitude for a *symmetric* section (e.g., rectangle, I-beam). For *unsymmetric* sections (e.g., T-beam), the neutral axis is not at mid-depth, and the two extreme fibres have different $y_{max}$ values on the top and bottom — yielding **two different section moduli** ($Z_{top}$ and $Z_{bottom}$).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define section modulus. State its physical significance with the help of the bending equation.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The section modulus of a beam cross-section, denoted by $Z$, is defined as the ratio of the second moment of area ($I$) about the neutral axis to the maximum distance ($y_{max}$) from the neutral axis to the extreme fibre.

$$Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}}$$

**Physical Significance:**
- It is a *purely geometric property* — depends only on the shape and size of the cross-section, not on the material.
- It appears in the flexure formula $\sigma_{max} = M / Z$ as a *denominator* — meaning a **larger Z gives lower bending stress** for the same applied moment.
- It is a direct measure of the **bending strength efficiency** of a cross-section. For a given material and given moment, the section with higher Z is "stronger in bending."
- It is used in the design equation $Z_{required} = M / \sigma_{allow}$ to size beams economically.

**Units:** mm³ (or m³ for large sections like ship hulls).

**Marks Distribution:** Definition (1), Formula (1), Physical Significance (1) → Total **3 marks**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Derive the expression for the section modulus of a rectangular cross-section of width $b$ and depth $d$.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Second Moment of Area for Rectangle:**

For a rectangle of width $b$ and depth $d$, the second moment of area about the centroidal (neutral) axis is:

$$I = \frac{b d^{3}}{12}$$

**Step 2 — Identify y_max:**

The maximum distance from the neutral axis to the extreme fibre is half the depth:

$$y_{max} = \frac{d}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Definition of Z:**

$$Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}} = \frac{b d^{3}/12}{d/2} = \frac{b d^{3}}{12} \times \frac{2}{d}$$

$$\boxed{Z = \frac{b d^{2}}{6}}$$

**Conclusion:** The section modulus of a rectangle depends on the **square of the depth** — hence "deeper" beams are vastly more efficient in bending than "wider" beams of equal area. This is why floor joists and ship deck beams are oriented with the depth in the direction of the load.

**Marks Distribution:** Expression for I (1), y_max (1), Final Z derivation (1) → Total **3 marks**.

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** A rectangular timber beam of cross-section 150 mm × 250 mm is simply supported over a span of 4 m and carries a uniformly distributed load of 12 kN/m over its entire span. Calculate the maximum bending stress induced in the beam. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A circular steel shaft of diameter 80 mm is used as a cantilever beam of length 1.5 m. If a point load of 20 kN is applied at its free end, find (i) the maximum bending stress, and (ii) the section modulus required if the allowable stress is 160 MPa. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution — Q.A(a)**

**Given:**
- $b = 150 \, \text{mm}$, $d = 250 \, \text{mm}$
- Span $L = 4 \, \text{m}$
- UDL $w = 12 \, \text{kN/m}$

**Step 1 — Maximum Bending Moment (Simply-supported with UDL):**

$$M_{max} = \frac{w L^{2}}{8} = \frac{12 \times 4^{2}}{8} = \frac{12 \times 16}{8} = 24 \, \text{kN·m}$$

$$M_{max} = 24 \times 10^{6} \, \text{N·mm}$$

**Step 2 — Section Modulus of Rectangle:**

$$Z = \frac{b d^{2}}{6} = \frac{150 \times (250)^{2}}{6} = \frac{150 \times 62500}{6} = \frac{9375000}{6}$$

$$Z = 1562500 \, \text{mm}^{3} = 1.5625 \times 10^{6} \, \text{mm}^{3}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum Bending Stress:**

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M_{max}}{Z} = \frac{24 \times 10^{6}}{1.5625 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{max} = 15.36 \, \text{MPa}}$$

---

##### **Model Solution — Q.A(b)**

**Given:**
- Diameter $d = 80 \, \text{mm}$
- Length $L = 1.5 \, \text{m}$
- Point load at free end $P = 20 \, \text{kN}$

**Step 1 — Maximum Bending Moment (Cantilever with End Load):**

For a cantilever beam with a point load at the free end, the maximum bending moment occurs at the fixed end:

$$M_{max} = P \times L = 20 \times 1.5 = 30 \, \text{kN·m}$$

$$M_{max} = 30 \times 10^{6} \, \text{N·mm}$$

**Step 2 — Section Modulus of Solid Circle:**

$$Z = \frac{\pi d^{3}}{32} = \frac{\pi \times (80)^{3}}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 512000}{32} = 16000 \pi$$

$$Z = 50265.5 \, \text{mm}^{3} \approx 5.027 \times 10^{4} \, \text{mm}^{3}$$

**Step 3 (i) — Maximum Bending Stress:**

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M_{max}}{Z} = \frac{30 \times 10^{6}}{50265.5} = 596.83 \, \text{N/mm}^{2}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{max} = 596.83 \, \text{MPa}}$$

**Observation:** This is **way above yield** for typical steel (~250 MPa) — meaning this shaft is undersized. (In practice, the shaft would fail before reaching this load.)

**Step 3 (ii) — Required Section Modulus for $\sigma_{allow} = 160$ MPa:**

$$Z_{req} = \frac{M_{max}}{\sigma_{allow}} = \frac{30 \times 10^{6}}{160} = 187500 \, \text{mm}^{3}$$

$$\boxed{Z_{req} = 1.875 \times 10^{5} \, \text{mm}^{3} = 187.5 \, \text{cm}^{3}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** A hollow circular tube of outer diameter 120 mm and inner diameter 80 mm is used as a beam. It carries a bending moment of 10 kN·m. Find the maximum bending stress. If the section is replaced by a solid circular shaft of the same cross-sectional area, calculate the percentage change in the maximum bending stress. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A T-section has the following dimensions: Flange = 150 mm × 20 mm, Web = 20 mm × 180 mm (below the flange). Calculate the section modulus of the section about its horizontal neutral axis. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution — Q.B(a)**

**Given:**
- Hollow: $D = 120 \, \text{mm}$, $d = 80 \, \text{mm}$
- $M = 10 \, \text{kN·m} = 10 \times 10^{6} \, \text{N·mm}$

**Part (i) — Maximum Bending Stress in Hollow Tube:**

**Step 1 — Section Modulus of Hollow Circle:**

$$Z_{hollow} = \frac{\pi (D^{4} - d^{4})}{32 D} = \frac{\pi (120^{4} - 80^{4})}{32 \times 120}$$

$$120^{4} = (1.2 \times 10^{2})^{4} = 2.0736 \times 10^{8}$$

$$80^{4} = (8 \times 10)^{4} = 4096 \times 10^{4} = 4.096 \times 10^{7}$$

$$D^{4} - d^{4} = 2.0736 \times 10^{8} - 4.096 \times 10^{7} = 1.664 \times 10^{8} \, \text{mm}^{4}$$

$$Z_{hollow} = \frac{\pi \times 1.664 \times 10^{8}}{3840} = \pi \times 43333.33 = 136124.7 \, \text{mm}^{3}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum Bending Stress:**

$$\sigma_{hollow} = \frac{M}{Z_{hollow}} = \frac{10 \times 10^{6}}{136124.7} = 73.46 \, \text{N/mm}^{2}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{hollow} = 73.46 \, \text{MPa}}$$

**Part (ii) — Solid Shaft of Same Area:**

**Step 3 — Cross-sectional Area of Hollow Section:**

$$A_{hollow} = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^{2} - d^{2}) = \frac{\pi}{4}(120^{2} - 80^{2}) = \frac{\pi}{4}(14400 - 6400) = \frac{\pi}{4}(8000)$$

$$A_{hollow} = 2000 \pi \, \text{mm}^{2} = 6283.19 \, \text{mm}^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Equivalent Solid Diameter:**

$$\frac{\pi d_{s}^{2}}{4} = 2000 \pi \quad \Rightarrow \quad d_{s}^{2} = 8000 \quad \Rightarrow \quad d_{s} = 89.44 \, \text{mm}$$

**Step 5 — Section Modulus of Solid Shaft:**

$$Z_{solid} = \frac{\pi d_{s}^{3}}{32} = \frac{\pi \times (89.44)^{3}}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 715541.8}{32}$$

$$Z_{solid} = \pi \times 22360.7 = 70245.4 \, \text{mm}^{3}$$

**Step 6 — Maximum Bending Stress in Solid Shaft:**

$$\sigma_{solid} = \frac{M}{Z_{solid}} = \frac{10 \times 10^{6}}{70245.4} = 142.36 \, \text{N/mm}^{2}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{solid} = 142.36 \, \text{MPa}}$$

**Step 7 — Percentage Change in Stress:**

$$\% \text{ Change} = \frac{\sigma_{solid} - \sigma_{hollow}}{\sigma_{hollow}} \times 100 = \frac{142.36 - 73.46}{73.46} \times 100$$

$$\% \text{ Change} = \frac{68.9}{73.46} \times 100 = 93.79 \, \%$$

$$\boxed{\text{Stress increases by } 93.79\% \text{ when solid shaft is used}}$$

**Conclusion:** The hollow section is **much more efficient** — same weight, less than half the bending stress.

---

##### **Model Solution — Q.B(b) — T-Section**

**Given T-Section Geometry:**
- Flange: 150 mm (width) × 20 mm (thickness) — at the top
- Web: 20 mm (width) × 180 mm (depth) — below flange
- Total depth = 20 + 180 = 200 mm

**Step 1 — Divide the Section into Two Rectangles:**

| Part | Width (mm) | Height (mm) | Area (mm²) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 — Flange | 150 | 20 | 3000 |
| 2 — Web | 20 | 180 | 3600 |

**Step 2 — Locate Centroid (y from top of flange):**

Take the top of the flange as the reference. The centroid of each rectangle from the top:
- Part 1 (flange): $y_1 = 20 / 2 = 10 \, \text{mm}$
- Part 2 (web): $y_2 = 20 + 180/2 = 20 + 90 = 110 \, \text{mm}$

$$\bar{y} = \frac{A_1 y_1 + A_2 y_2}{A_1 + A_2} = \frac{(3000 \times 10) + (3600 \times 110)}{3000 + 3600}$$

$$\bar{y} = \frac{30000 + 396000}{6600} = \frac{426000}{6600} = 64.545 \, \text{mm} \text{ from top}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Second Moment of Area about the Centroidal Axis:**

Use the parallel axis theorem: $I = I_G + A h^{2}$, where $h$ is the distance from each part's centroid to the overall NA.

**For Part 1 (Flange):**
- $I_{G1} = \dfrac{b_1 h_1^{3}}{12} = \dfrac{150 \times 20^{3}}{12} = \dfrac{150 \times 8000}{12} = 100000 \, \text{mm}^{4}$
- $h_1 = \bar{y} - y_1 = 64.545 - 10 = 54.545 \, \text{mm}$
- $A_1 h_1^{2} = 3000 \times (54.545)^{2} = 3000 \times 2975.16 = 8925473 \, \text{mm}^{4}$
- $I_1 = 100000 + 8925473 = 9025473 \, \text{mm}^{4}$

**For Part 2 (Web):**
- $I_{G2} = \dfrac{b_2 h_2^{3}}{12} = \dfrac{20 \times 180^{3}}{12} = \dfrac{20 \times 5832000}{12} = 9720000 \, \text{mm}^{4}$
- $h_2 = y_2 - \bar{y} = 110 - 64.545 = 45.455 \, \text{mm}$
