- $A_2 h_2^{2} = 3600 \times (45.455)^{2} = 3600 \times 2066.18 = 7438264 \, \text{mm}^{4}$
- $I_2 = 9720000 + 7438264 = 17158264 \, \text{mm}^{4}$

**Step 4 — Total I:**

$$I = I_1 + I_2 = 9025473 + 17158264 = 26183737 \, \text{mm}^{4}$$

$$I \approx 2.618 \times 10^{7} \, \text{mm}^{4}$$

**Step 5 — Identify y_max:**

- Distance from NA to top fibre: $y_{top} = 64.545 \, \text{mm}$
- Distance from NA to bottom fibre: $y_{bot} = 200 - 64.545 = 135.455 \, \text{mm}$
- $y_{max} = 135.455 \, \text{mm}$ (the bottom is farther from NA)

**Step 6 — Compute Section Modulus:**

$$Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}} = \frac{2.618 \times 10^{7}}{135.455} = 193285 \, \text{mm}^{3}$$

$$\boxed{Z \approx 1.933 \times 10^{5} \, \text{mm}^{3} = 193.3 \, \text{cm}^{3}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to find NA first** for unsymmetric sections (T, L, channel) — students directly use $y_{max} = d/2$ and lose 3–4 marks.
> 2. **Sign errors in parallel axis theorem** — $h$ is the distance from each part's centroid to the **global NA**, not the section's edge.
> 3. **Picking the wrong $y_{max}$** — for an unsymmetric section, the governing $y_{max}$ is the **larger** of the two extreme-fibre distances.
> 4. **Unit conversion errors** — bending moment in kN·m must be converted to N·mm before using $\sigma$ in MPa.
> 5. **Skipping the explicit $Z = I / y_{max}$ step** — examiners expect you to write the formula, substitute, and then show the numerical answer.
> 6. **Hollow vs. Solid confusion** — for hollow sections, $y_{max} = D/2$ (the **outer** radius), not the mean radius.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Section Modulus — Final Revision Bullet Points**

- **Definition:** $Z = I / y_{max}$ — purely geometric, units = mm³ or m³.
- **Flexure Formula:** $\sigma_{max} = M / Z$ — the single most important equation.
- **Moment of Resistance:** $M_R = \sigma_{allow} \cdot Z$ — capacity of a section.
- **Design Equation:** $Z_{req} = M_{max} / \sigma_{allow}$ — sizes the section.
- **Key Rectangular Z:** $Z = bd^2 / 6$ — depth is squared, so depth >> width for efficiency.
- **Key Circular Z:** $Z = \pi d^3 / 32$ — used for shafts and tubular members.
- **Key Hollow Circular Z:** $Z = \pi (D^4 - d^4) / (32D)$ — much higher Z per unit weight than solid.
- **Key Hollow Rectangular Z:** $Z = (BD^3 - b d^3) / (6D)$ — standard RHS / box section.
- **For Unsymmetric Sections (T, L, Channel):** Must compute NA first; Z is computed for the **larger** $y_{max}$ (most distant extreme fibre from NA).
- **Hollow > Solid (Same Weight):** Hollow sections give ~1.5× to 2× higher Z for the same material weight — fundamental reason for using hollow tubes in ship hulls, aircraft wings, and offshore structures.
- **Naval Arch. Application:** Hull girder midship section modulus is the most critical longitudinal strength parameter; minimum values are prescribed by classification societies.
- **Z has NO material dependence** — only depends on geometry. Material enters through $\sigma_{allow}$.
- **Always check units** — mm³ ↔ cm³ ↔ m³ conversions: 1 m³ = 10⁹ mm³ = 10⁶ cm³.
- **For ship hulls:** Z is typically expressed in **m³** (e.g., 5 m³, 25 m³) due to scale.
- **Moment conversion:** 1 kN·m = 10⁶ N·mm; 1 N·m = 10³ N·mm.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flexural Rigidity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST303 - SOLID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Flexural Rigidity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Flexural Rigidity** is a fundamental structural property of a beam, plate, or shell that quantifies its resistance to bending deformation under the action of transverse loads or bending moments. Mathematically, it is defined as the product of the material's **Young's Modulus (E)** and the cross-section's **Second Moment of Area (I)**, often referred to as the **Area Moment of Inertia**.

For a slender beam undergoing pure bending, flexural rigidity is expressed as:

$$EI \quad \text{(Units: N·m}^2 \text{ or N·mm}^2\text{)}$$

For thin plates (which is of paramount importance in naval architecture for hull plating, deck plating, and bulkheads), flexural rigidity is defined as:

$$D = \frac{Eh^3}{12(1-\nu^2)} \quad \text{(Units: N·m)}$$

where $h$ is the plate thickness and $\nu$ is the Poisson's ratio of the material.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In the context of naval architecture and ship structural analysis, flexural rigidity governs three critical phenomena: **(1)** Hull girder bending strength, **(2)** Local panel buckling of plate fields between stiffeners, and **(3)** Bending of stiffened panels and grillages. Students must distinguish between **beam flexural rigidity (EI)** and **plate flexural rigidity (D)**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Bending Resistance" Property

Imagine holding two rulers — one made of steel and one made of soft plastic. When you apply the same bending force to both, the **steel ruler resists bending far more strongly** than the plastic ruler. This inherent resistance to bending is what we call **flexural rigidity**.

A second helpful analogy from naval architecture: Consider a **diving board** versus a **ship's deck plate**. A thin plywood diving board (low $EI$) bends noticeably under a small load, while a steel deck plate reinforced with stiffeners (high $EI$) shows virtually no deflection. The product $EI$ captures this combined effect of **material stiffness** ($E$) and **geometric distribution of material** ($I$).

The deeper intuition:
- **Material stiffness ($E$)** answers: *"How inherently stiff is the material itself?"* Steel ($E \approx 200$ GPa) is far stiffer than aluminum ($E \approx 70$ GPa) or wood ($E \approx 10$–$15$ GPa).
- **Geometric distribution ($I$)** answers: *"How is the material arranged away from the neutral axis?"* A hollow tube has a much higher $I$ than a solid rod of the same mass, because material is placed far from the neutral axis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Doubling the modulus doubles flexural rigidity, but doubling the depth of a beam (which appears squared in $I = bh^3/12$) increases flexural rigidity by a factor of **eight**. This is why ship hulls use **deep girders and stiffeners** rather than thicker but shallower plates.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Property | Symbol | Standard Range (Shipbuilding Steel) |
|---|---|---|
| Young's Modulus | $E$ | **200 GPa** to **210 GPa** |
| Yield Stress | $\sigma_y$ | 235 MPa (mild) to 355 MPa (high-tensile) |
| Poisson's Ratio | $\nu$ | **0.30** |
| Density | $\rho$ | 7850 kg/m³ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU naval architecture problems, **$E = 200$ GPa** and **$\nu = 0.3$** are the **default values** for mild shipbuilding steel unless explicitly stated otherwise in the problem statement.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why Flexural Rigidity Exists

The concept of flexural rigidity arises directly from the **Euler-Bernoulli Beam Theory** (for slender beams) and the **Kirchhoff-Love Plate Theory** (for thin plates). Both theories share a common underlying principle: **bending deformation produces internal stresses linearly proportional to distance from the neutral surface, and the resulting curvature is inversely proportional to flexural rigidity**.

### Step-by-Step Logical Framework

**Step 1 — Strain Compatibility:**
When a beam bends into a curvature $\kappa = 1/\rho$ (where $\rho$ is the radius of curvature), a longitudinal fiber at distance $z$ from the neutral axis experiences a strain $\varepsilon_z = -z/\rho$.

**Step 2 — Hooke's Law Application:**
Assuming linear elastic behavior, the bending stress at that fiber is $\sigma_z = E\varepsilon_z = -Ez/\rho$.

**Step 3 — Moment Equilibrium:**
The internal bending moment must equal the integral of stress times moment arm over the cross-section:
$$M = -\int_A \sigma_z \cdot z \, dA = \int_A \frac{Ez^2}{\rho} \, dA = \frac{E}{\rho}\int_A z^2 \, dA$$

**Step 4 — Identification of Second Moment of Area:**
Since $\int_A z^2 \, dA \equiv I$ (the second moment of area about the neutral axis), the equation reduces to the fundamental **Moment-Curvature Equation**:

$$M = \frac{EI}{\rho} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{M}{EI}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**
The product $EI$ appears as the **proportionality constant** between applied moment and resulting curvature. Hence, flexural rigidity is literally the **bending stiffness per unit curvature**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind the Formula:** A larger $EI$ means a smaller curvature for the same moment — i.e., the beam bends less. This is why flexural rigidity is called a "rigidity" rather than a "compliance."

## 2.2 Extension to Plate Bending — The Plate Flexural Rigidity D

For a thin plate under transverse load, classical plate theory yields a **biharmonic governing equation**:

$$D\nabla^4 w = q(x,y)$$

where $w$ is the transverse deflection and $q$ is the distributed load. The flexural rigidity of the plate is:

$$D = \frac{Eh^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$$

The factor $(1-\nu^2)$ in the denominator arises because in **plate bending (2D)**, lateral contraction is restrained (plane strain condition), whereas in **beam bending (1D)**, lateral contraction is unrestrained (plane stress). This makes plates **stiffer in bending** than a comparable beam strip by a factor of $1/(1-\nu^2)$. For $\nu = 0.3$, this factor is approximately **1.099** (a 9.9% increase).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $EI$ | Beam flexural rigidity | N·m² |
| 2 | $D = \dfrac{Eh^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$ | Plate flexural rigidity | N·m |
| 3 | $\dfrac{1}{\rho} = \dfrac{M}{EI}$ | Moment-curvature relationship | m⁻¹ |
| 4 | $EI\dfrac{d^2w}{dx^2} = M(x)$ | Euler-Bernoulli beam equation | N·m |
| 5 | $EI\dfrac{d^4w}{dx^4} = q(x)$ | Beam deflection under distributed load | N/m |
| 6 | $D\nabla^4 w = q$ | Kirchhoff plate equation | N/m² |
| 7 | $I = \dfrac{bh^3}{12}$ | Rectangular cross-section | m⁴ |
| 8 | $I = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{64}$ | Solid circular cross-section | m⁴ |
| 9 | $I = \dfrac{\pi (D_o^4 - D_i^4)}{64}$ | Hollow circular cross-section | m⁴ |
| 10 | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{Mc}{I}$ | Maximum bending stress | Pa |
| 11 | $\delta_{max} = \dfrac{PL^3}{48EI}$ | Center deflection of simply supported beam (center load) | m |
| 12 | $\theta = \dfrac{PL^2}{16EI}$ | End slope of simply supported beam (center load) | rad |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Always check whether the problem refers to **beam bending (EI)** or **plate bending (D)**. Mixing them up is the single most common error in naval architecture solid mechanics questions.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Flexural rigidity is the cornerstone of:

- **Hull Girder Strength Analysis:** The entire ship hull acts as a hollow box girder, and its $EI$ determines the still-water and wave-induced bending stresses. Classification societies (IRS, Lloyd's, DNV) mandate minimum section moduli based on $EI$ requirements.
- **Local Panel Buckling:** Stiffened plate panels between transverse frames are checked against elastic buckling using $D$, with the critical buckling stress being $\sigma_{cr} = k \dfrac{\pi^2 D}{b^2 t}$.
- **Grillage Analysis:** Bottom plating supported by longitudinal and transverse stiffeners forms a "grillage" — analyzed as an orthotropic plate with directional flexural rigidities $D_x$ and $D_y$.
- **Stiffener Design:** The effective flexural rigidity of a stiffened plate combines the plate's $D$ with the stiffener's $EI_s$ acting about its own neutral axis.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From Basic Beam Mechanics to Flexural Rigidity

We derive the fundamental relationship $M = EI/\rho$ starting from first principles.

### Step A — Kinematics of Pure Bending

Consider a beam segment of length $dx$ that deforms into an arc of radius $\rho$ under the action of a pure bending moment $M$. A fiber at distance $z$ from the neutral axis (NA) has an original length $dx$ and deforms to $(R + z)\,d\theta$ on the tension side or $(R - z)\,d\theta$ on the compression side, where $R = \rho$ (radius to neutral surface).

The longitudinal strain at distance $z$ is:

$$\varepsilon_z = \frac{(\rho + z)\,d\theta - \rho\,d\theta}{\rho\,d\theta} = \frac{z}{\rho}$$

### Step B — Application of Hooke's Law

For a linearly elastic material in uniaxial stress state (valid for slender beams where lateral stresses are negligible):

$$\sigma_z = E\varepsilon_z = \frac{Ez}{\rho}$$

### Step C — Stress Distribution

The stress varies **linearly** across the depth, being zero at the neutral axis ($z = 0$) and maximum at the extreme fibers ($z = \pm c$). This linear distribution is the foundation of the flexure formula.

### Step D — Moment Equilibrium

The resultant internal bending moment equals the integral of bending stress times lever arm over the cross-sectional area:

$$M = \int_A \sigma_z \cdot z \, dA = \int_A \frac{Ez^2}{\rho} \, dA$$

Since $E$ and $\rho$ are constants over the cross-section, they factor out:

$$M = \frac{E}{\rho}\int_A z^2 \, dA$$

### Step E — Definition of Second Moment of Area

By definition, the second moment of area (area moment of inertia) about the neutral axis is:

$$I \equiv \int_A z^2 \, dA \quad [\text{units: m}^4]$$

Substituting:

$$\boxed{M = \frac{EI}{\rho}}$$

Equivalently:

$$\frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{M}{EI} \quad \text{or} \quad \kappa = \frac{M}{EI}$$

### Step F — Governing Differential Equation

Since curvature $\kappa \approx d^2w/dx^2$ for small deflections in the Euler-Bernoulli framework:

$$EI\frac{d^2w}{dx^2} = M(x)$$

Differentiating twice with respect to $x$ and using $dM/dx = V$ (shear) and $dV/dx = -q$ (distributed load):

$$EI\frac{d^4w}{dx^4} = -q(x)$$

or equivalently:

$$EI\frac{d^4w}{dx^4} = q(x)$$

depending on sign convention. This is the **fundamental governing equation of beam bending**, with $EI$ appearing as the bending stiffness.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Plate Flexural Rigidity $D$

For a rectangular plate element of dimensions $dx \times dy \times h$ under transverse load, the plate theory assumes (Kirchhoff-Love hypotheses):

1. Straight normals remain straight and normal to the mid-surface after deformation.
2. Normal stress $\sigma_z \approx 0$ (thin plate assumption).
3. In-plane displacements $u$ and $v$ are linear through thickness.

The strain-displacement relations for the mid-surface are:

$$\varepsilon_x = -z\frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2}, \quad \varepsilon_y = -z\frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial y^2}, \quad \gamma_{xy} = -2z\frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x \partial y}$$

The stress-strain relations under **plane strain** (because lateral contraction is restrained in plate bending) are:

$$\sigma_x = \frac{E}{1-\nu^2}\left(\varepsilon_x + \nu\varepsilon_y\right), \quad \sigma_y = \frac{E}{1-\nu^2}\left(\varepsilon_y + \nu\varepsilon_x\right), \quad \tau_{xy} = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}\gamma_{xy}$$

Substituting the strain expressions and integrating through thickness to obtain moment resultants $M_x = \int_{-h/2}^{h/2}\sigma_x z\,dz$, etc.:

$$M_x = -D\left(\frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2} + \nu\frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial y^2}\right)$$

$$M_y = -D\left(\frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial y^2} + \nu\frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2}\right)$$

$$M_{xy} = -D(1-\nu)\frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x \partial y}$$

where the plate flexural rigidity emerges as:

$$\boxed{D = \frac{Eh^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}}$$

The factor $(1-\nu^2)$ distinguishes plate rigidity from a naive "beam strip" rigidity $Eh^3/12$ because the **in-plane restraint** of the surrounding plate (plane strain) makes it stiffer.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Beam vs. Plate

| Property | Beam (Euler-Bernoulli) | Plate (Kirchhoff-Love) |
|---|---|---|
| Geometry | 1D (length $L$) | 2D (length $a$, breadth $b$) |
| Rigidity term | $EI$ | $D = Eh^3/[12(1-\nu^2)]$ |
| Rigidity units | N·m² | N·m |
| Governing equation | $EI\,d^4w/dx^4 = q$ | $D\nabla^4 w = q$ |
| Order of ODE/PDE | 4th-order ODE | 4th-order PDE (biharmonic) |
| Stress state assumption | Plane stress (uniaxial) | Plane strain (biaxial restrained) |
| Lateral contraction factor | None (free) | $1/(1-\nu^2)$ |
| Typical naval arch. application | Stiffener, girder | Hull plate, deck plate |

## 3.4 Numerical Illustration — Computing Deflection Using Flexural Rigidity

**Problem Setup:** A simply supported steel beam of span $L = 4$ m, breadth $b = 100$ mm, depth $h = 200$ mm carries a central point load $P = 20$ kN. Compute the maximum deflection.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Material Properties:**
$E = 200 \times 10^9$ Pa, $\nu = 0.3$ (not needed for beam).

**Step 2 — Second Moment of Area:**
$I = \dfrac{bh^3}{12} = \dfrac{(0.100)(0.200)^3}{12} = \dfrac{(0.100)(0.008)}{12} = \dfrac{8 \times 10^{-4}}{12} = 6.667 \times 10^{-5}$ m⁴.

**Step 3 — Flexural Rigidity:**
$EI = (200 \times 10^9)(6.667 \times 10^{-5}) = 1.333 \times 10^{7}$ N·m².

**Step 4 — Maximum Deflection Formula (Simply Supported, Central Load):**
$\delta_{max} = \dfrac{PL^3}{48EI}$.

**Step 5 — Substituting Numerical Values:**
$\delta_{max} = \dfrac{(20 \times 10^3)(4)^3}{48 \times 1.333 \times 10^7} = \dfrac{(20 \times 10^3)(64)}{6.4 \times 10^8} = \dfrac{1.28 \times 10^6}{6.4 \times 10^8} = 2.0 \times 10^{-3}$ m = **2.0 mm**.

**Step 6 — Interpretation:**
A deflection of 2 mm over a 4 m span is well within the serviceability limit of $L/250 = 16$ mm, confirming the beam's adequacy.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: Define flexural rigidity of a beam. How is it different from plate flexural rigidity?
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

Flexural rigidity of a beam is the product of Young's Modulus ($E$) and the second moment of area ($I$) of the cross-section about the neutral axis. It represents the resistance of the beam to bending deformation and has units of **N·m²**. Mathematically, it is expressed as $EI$, where $E$ has units of Pa and $I$ has units of m⁴.

Plate flexural rigidity, denoted by $D$, applies to two-dimensional thin plate elements and is defined as:

$$D = \frac{Eh^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$$

The key differences are: (i) plate rigidity $D$ has units of N·m (not N·m²), (ii) it includes Poisson's ratio $\nu$ in the denominator due to plane strain conditions in 2D bending, and (iii) it depends on the cube of plate thickness $h^3$, whereas beam rigidity depends on the second moment of area which varies with cross-sectional geometry.

---

### Question A2: State the moment-curvature relationship and explain the physical significance of flexural rigidity.
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The moment-curvature relationship for a beam in pure bending is:

$$\frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{M}{EI}$$

where $1/\rho$ is the curvature, $M$ is the internal bending moment, and $EI$ is the flexural rigidity.

**Physical Significance:** The flexural rigidity $EI$ acts as the **proportionality constant** between the applied bending moment and the resulting curvature. A higher value of $EI$ implies that the beam bends less (smaller curvature) for the same applied moment, indicating greater resistance to bending. Physically, $E$ represents the material's inherent elastic stiffness, while $I$ represents how efficiently the cross-sectional geometry distributes material away from the neutral axis. This is why I-beams and hollow tubes are structurally efficient — they maximize $I$ for a given weight.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Poisson factor:** When computing plate rigidity $D$, students often write $D = Eh^3/12$, missing the crucial $(1-\nu^2)$ term. This costs **2 marks** in a 14-mark question.
> 2. **Unit confusion:** Mixing N·m² (beam) with N·m (plate) is a common error — examiners deduct **1 mark** for unit inconsistencies.
> 3. **Sign convention errors:** In the Euler-Bernoulli equation $EI\,d^2w/dx^2 = M(x)$, students frequently drop the sign or confuse sagging/hogging conventions.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert mm to m:** All dimensions must be in SI (meters) before computing $I$. A 100 mm dimension used as 100 instead of 0.1 m gives a wildly incorrect answer.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): Hull Plate Flexural Rigidity and Buckling Analysis
`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** A rectangular plate panel of a ship's side shell has dimensions $a = 800$ mm (length between stiffeners) and $b = 600$ mm (breadth between stiffeners), with plate thickness $h = 12$ mm. The material is mild steel with $E = 200$ GPa and $\nu = 0.3$. Calculate the **plate flexural rigidity** $D$ and the **second moment of area per unit width** of the plate.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** A simply supported steel beam of length $L = 6$ m, rectangular cross-section $b = 150$ mm wide and $d = 300$ mm deep, is subjected to a uniformly distributed load $w = 10$ kN/m over its entire span. Calculate the **maximum bending stress** and **maximum deflection** using flexural rigidity concepts.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A — Part (a):

**Given:**
$a = 0.800$ m, $b = 0.600$ m, $h = 0.012$ m, $E = 200 \times 10^9$ Pa, $\nu = 0.3$.

**Step 1 — Plate Flexural Rigidity:**

$$D = \frac{Eh^3}{12(1-\nu^2)} = \frac{(200 \times 10^9)(0.012)^3}{12(1 - 0.3^2)}$$

**Step 2 — Numerator Evaluation:**
$(0.012)^3 = 1.728 \times 10^{-6}$ m³
$(200 \times 10^9)(1.728 \times 10^{-6}) = 3.456 \times 10^5$ N·m.

**Step 3 — Denominator Evaluation:**
$12(1 - 0.09) = 12 \times 0.91 = 10.92$.

**Step 4 — Final Value:**
$$D = \frac{3.456 \times 10^5}{10.92} = 3.165 \times 10^4 \text{ N·m}$$

**Answer:** $\boxed{D \approx 3.165 \times 10^4 \text{ N·m}}$

**Step 5 — Second Moment of Area per Unit Width (for plate strip):**
$I_{\text{strip}} = \dfrac{h^3}{12} = \dfrac{(0.012)^3}{12} = \dfrac{1.728 \times 10^{-6}}{12} = 1.44 \times 10^{-7}$ m⁴/m.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A — Part (b):

**Given:**
$L = 6$ m, $b = 0.150$ m, $d = 0.300$ m, $w = 10 \times 10^3$ N/m, $E = 200 \times 10^9$ Pa.

**Step 1 — Second Moment of Area:**
$I = \dfrac{bd^3}{12} = \dfrac{(0.150)(0.300)^3}{12} = \dfrac{(0.150)(0.027)}{12} = \dfrac{4.05 \times 10^{-3}}{12} = 3.375 \times 10^{-4}$ m⁴.

**Step 2 — Flexural Rigidity:**
$EI = (200 \times 10^9)(3.375 \times 10^{-4}) = 6.75 \times 10^7$ N·m².

**Step 3 — Maximum Bending Moment (simply supported, UDL):**
$M_{max} = \dfrac{wL^2}{8} = \dfrac{(10 \times 10^3)(6)^2}{8} = \dfrac{360 \times 10^3}{8} = 45 \times 10^3$ N·m.

**Step 4 — Maximum Bending Stress:**
$\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M_{max} \cdot c}{I} = \dfrac{(45 \times 10^3)(0.150)}{3.375 \times 10^{-4}}$ where $c = d/2 = 0.150$ m.

$\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{6750}{3.375 \times 10^{-4}} = 2.0 \times 10^7$ Pa = **20 MPa**.

**Step 5 — Maximum Deflection (simply supported, UDL):**
$\delta_{max} = \dfrac{5wL^4}{384EI} = \dfrac{5(10 \times 10^3)(6)^4}{384 \times 6.75 \times 10^7}$.

Numerator: $5 \times 10^4 \times 1296 = 6.48 \times 10^7$.

Denominator: $384 \times 6.75 \times 10^7 = 2.592 \times 10^{10}$.

$\delta_{max} = \dfrac{6.48 \times 10^7}{2.592 \times 10^{10}} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m = **2.5 mm**.

**Step 6 — Serviceability Check:**
Allowable deflection = $L/250 = 6000/250 = 24$ mm. Since $2.5$ mm $< 24$ mm, the beam is **adequate in deflection**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Comprehensive Beam Analysis
`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Derive the moment-curvature relationship $M = EI/\rho$ from first principles, clearly stating all assumptions of Euler-Bernoulli beam theory.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** A ship deck plate is supported by longitudinal stiffeners (T-sections) spaced at $s = 600$ mm apart. The plate has thickness $t = 10$ mm, Young's modulus $E = 210$ GPa, and Poisson's ratio $\nu = 0.3$. Between stiffeners, the plate acts as a one-way slab spanning 600 mm under a uniform pressure $q = 50$ kPa. Treating the plate strip as a beam of width $s = 600$ mm, compute: (i) the effective flexural rigidity $EI$ of the plate strip per unit width, and (ii) the maximum bending moment per unit width and compare it with the simply supported strip moment.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B — Part (a):

**Assumptions of Euler-Bernoulli Beam Theory:**

1. The beam material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic** (Hooke's law applies).
2. **Plane sections remain plane and normal to the neutral axis** after bending (no shear deformation).
3. **Deflections are small** compared to the span, so that $\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta \approx \theta$ and curvature $\kappa \approx d^2w/dx^2$.
4. **Shear deformation is neglected** (beam is slender, $L/h > 20$).
5. The beam is initially **straight and unloaded**.

**Derivation (Recapitulated with All Steps):**

A beam segment of original length $dx$ bends to subtend angle $d\theta$ at the center of curvature $\rho$. A fiber at distance $z$ from the neutral axis has its length changed from $dx$ to $(\rho - z)\,d\theta$.

**Strain:**
$$\varepsilon = \frac{(\rho - z)d\theta - \rho\,d\theta}{\rho\,d\theta} = -\frac{z}{\rho}$$

**Stress (Hooke's Law):**
$$\sigma = E\varepsilon = -\frac{Ez}{\rho}$$

**Moment about neutral axis:**
$$M = -\int_A \sigma \cdot z\,dA = \int_A \frac{Ez^2}{\rho}\,dA = \frac{E}{\rho}\int_A z^2\,dA$$

**Since $I = \int_A z^2\,dA$:**
$$\boxed{M = \frac{EI}{\rho}} \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{M}{EI}$$

This is the fundamental moment-curvature equation. Q.E.D.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B — Part (b):

**Given:**
$s = 0.600$ m (plate strip width = stiffener spacing), $t = 0.010$ m, $E = 210 \times 10^9$ Pa, $\nu = 0.3$, $q = 50 \times 10^3$ Pa, span $L = s = 0.600$ m.

**Step 1 — Plate Strip as a Beam:**
Per unit width (b = 1 m), the strip has:
$I_{\text{strip}} = \dfrac{(1.0)(t)^3}{12} = \dfrac{(1.0)(0.010)^3}{12} = 8.333 \times 10^{-8}$ m⁴/m.

**Step 2 — Beam Flexural Rigidity of Strip:**
$EI_{\text{strip}} = (210 \times 10^9)(8.333 \times 10^{-8}) = 1.75 \times 10^4$ N·m²/m.

**Step 3 — Plate Flexural Rigidity (for comparison):**
$D = \dfrac{E t^3}{12(1-\nu^2)} = \dfrac{(210 \times 10^9)(0.010)^3}{12(1-0.09)} = \dfrac{(210 \times 10^9)(10^{-6})}{10.92} = \dfrac{2.1 \times 10^5}{10.92} = 1.923 \times 10^4$ N·m/m.

Note: $D$ per unit width is slightly higher than the beam strip rigidity $EI_{\text{strip}}/b$ due to the $1/(1-\nu^2)$ factor.

**Step 4 — Distributed Load per Unit Width:**
$q^* = q \times b = (50 \times 10^3)(1.0) = 50 \times 10^3$ N/m per meter width.

**Step 5 — Maximum Bending Moment per Unit Width (Simply Supported Strip):**
$M_{max}^* = \dfrac{q^* L^2}{8} = \dfrac{(50 \times 10^3)(0.600)^2}{8} = \dfrac{(50 \times 10^3)(0.36)}{8} = \dfrac{18{,}000}{8} = 2250$ N·m/m.

**Step 6 — Comparison and Interpretation:**
The bending moment of **2250 N·m/m** acts on the plate strip spanning between two stiffeners. The effective flexural rigidity used depends on whether we treat it as a 1D beam (use $EI_{\text{strip}}$) or as part of a 2D plate field (use $D$). For the 1D analysis above, $EI_{\text{strip}} = 1.75 \times 10^4$ N·m²/m, which would give a maximum deflection:

$$\delta_{max} = \frac{5 q^* L^4}{384 EI_{\text{strip}}} = \frac{5(50 \times 10^3)(0.600)^4}{384 \times 1.75 \times 10^4} = \frac{5 \times 50{,}000 \times 0.1296}{6.72 \times 10^6} = \frac{32{,}400}{6.72 \times 10^6} = 4.82 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} \approx 4.82 \text{ mm}$$

This is a preliminary estimate; the actual 2D plate analysis would use $D$ and would yield a different (typically smaller) deflection due to two-way load distribution.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Points for KTU 2024 ESE:**

- ✅ **Beam flexural rigidity = $EI$** with units **N·m²**. Compute $I$ about the **neutral axis** of bending.
- ✅ **Plate flexural rigidity = $D = Eh^3/[12(1-\nu^2)]$** with units **N·m**. The Poisson factor distinguishes 2D plate behavior from 1D beam behavior.
- ✅ **Moment-curvature equation:** $1/\rho = M/EI$ is the master relationship — every beam bending formula traces back to this.
- ✅ **Governing ODE for beams:** $EI\,d^4w/dx^4 = q(x)$. Integrating four times with appropriate boundary conditions yields deflections and slopes.
- ✅ **Governing PDE for plates:** $D\nabla^4 w = q(x,y)$. Biharmonic equation requires two boundary conditions on each edge (deflection and slope).
- ✅ **Depth cubed dominance:** In rectangular beams, $I = bh^3/12$ — increasing depth $h$ is far more efficient than increasing breadth $b$ for boosting flexural rigidity.
- ✅ **Plate thickness cubed dominance:** Plate rigidity scales with $h^3$, so doubling plate thickness increases $D$ by a factor of **8**.
- ✅ **Poisson ratio for shipbuilding steel:** Always use $\nu = 0.30$ unless specified otherwise.
- ✅ **Young's modulus for shipbuilding steel:** Use $E = 200$ GPa (mild) or $E = 210$ GPa (higher-grade) as per problem statement.
- ✅ **Naval architecture applications to remember:**
  1. Hull girder bending stress = $M_{\text{hull}} \cdot y/I_{\text{hull}}$
  2. Local panel buckling = depends on $D$ and boundary conditions
  3. Grillage analysis = orthotropic plate with directional $D_x, D_y$
  4. Stiffener deflection check = $\delta \leq L/250$ or as per class rules
- ✅ **Unit conversions are critical:** Convert all dimensions to **meters** before computing $I$ or $D$; convert loads to **N**; stresses will emerge in **Pa**.
- ✅ **Sign convention for Euler-Bernoulli:** Sagging moment produces positive curvature ($d^2w/dx^2 > 0$ for downward $w$); verify with problem-specific free-body diagrams.
- ✅ **Failure to account for Poisson's ratio in plate problems costs 2 marks** — a recurring KTU examiner pitfall.
- ✅ **Recall the difference:** $EI$ governs **beam stiffness**; $D$ governs **plate stiffness**; both are forms of flexural rigidity but with different physical meanings and units.

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Flexural Rigidity** is a fundamental structural property of a beam, plate, or shell that quantifies its resistance to bending deformation under the action of transverse loads or bending moments. Mathematically, it is defined as the product of the material's **Young's Modulus (E)** and the cross-section's **Second Moment of Area (I)**, often referred to as the **Area Moment of Inertia**.

For a slender beam undergoing pure bending, flexural rigidity is expressed as:

$$EI \quad \text{(Units: N·m}^2 \text{ or N·mm}^2\text{)}$$

For thin plates (which is of paramount importance in naval architecture for hull plating, deck plating, and bulkheads), flexural rigidity is defined as:

$$D = \frac{Eh^3}{12(1-\nu^2)} \quad \text{(Units: N·m)}$$

where $h$ is the plate thickness and $\nu$ is the Poisson's ratio of the material.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In the context of naval architecture and ship structural analysis, flexural rigidity governs three critical phenomena: **(1)** Hull girder bending strength, **(2)** Local panel buckling of plate fields between stiffeners, and **(3)** Bending of stiffened panels and grillages. Students must distinguish between **beam flexural rigidity (EI)** and **plate flexural rigidity (D)**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Bending Resistance" Property

Imagine holding two rulers — one made of steel and one made of soft plastic. When you apply the same bending force to both, the **steel ruler resists bending far more strongly** than the plastic ruler. This inherent resistance to bending is what we call **flexural rigidity**.

A second helpful analogy from naval architecture: Consider a **diving board** versus a **ship's deck plate**. A thin plywood diving board (low $EI$) bends noticeably under a small load, while a steel deck plate reinforced with stiffeners (high $EI$) shows virtually no deflection. The product $EI$ captures this combined effect of **material stiffness** ($E$) and **geometric distribution of material** ($I$).

The deeper intuition:
- **Material stiffness ($E$)** answers: *"How inherently stiff is the material itself?"* Steel ($E \approx 200$ GPa) is far stiffer than aluminum ($E \approx 70$ GPa) or wood ($E \approx 10$–$15$ GPa).
- **Geometric distribution ($I$)** answers: *"How is the material arranged away from the neutral axis?"* A hollow tube has a much higher $I$ than a solid rod of the same mass, because material is placed far from the neutral axis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Doubling the modulus doubles flexural rigidity, but doubling the depth of a beam (which appears squared in $I = bh^3/12$) increases flexural rigidity by a factor of **eight**. This is why ship hulls use **deep girders and stiffeners** rather than thicker but shallower plates.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Property | Symbol | Standard Range (Shipbuilding Steel) |
|---|---|---|
| Young's Modulus | $E$ | **200 GPa** to **210 GPa** |
| Yield Stress | $\sigma_y$ | 235 MPa (mild) to 355 MPa (high-tensile) |
| Poisson's Ratio | $\nu$ | **0.30** |
| Density | $\rho$ | 7850 kg/m³ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU naval architecture problems, **$E = 200$ GPa** and **$\nu = 0.3$** are the **default values** for mild shipbuilding steel unless explicitly stated otherwise in the problem statement.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why Flexural Rigidity Exists

The concept of flexural rigidity arises directly from the **Euler-Bernoulli Beam Theory** (for slender beams) and the **Kirchhoff-Love Plate Theory** (for thin plates). Both theories share a common underlying principle: **bending deformation produces internal stresses linearly proportional to distance from the neutral surface, and the resulting curvature is inversely proportional to flexural rigidity**.

### Step-by-Step Logical Framework

**Step 1 — Strain Compatibility:**
When a beam bends into a curvature $\kappa = 1/\rho$ (where $\rho$ is the radius of curvature), a longitudinal fiber at distance $z$ from the neutral axis experiences a strain $\varepsilon_z = -z/\rho$.

**Step 2 — Hooke's Law Application:**
Assuming linear elastic behavior, the bending stress at that fiber is $\sigma_z = E\varepsilon_z = -Ez/\rho$.

**Step 3 — Moment Equilibrium:**
The internal bending moment must equal the integral of stress times moment arm over the cross-section:
$$M = -\int_A \sigma_z \cdot z \, dA = \int_A \frac{Ez^2}{\rho} \, dA = \frac{E}{\rho}\int_A z^2 \, dA$$

**Step 4 — Identification of Second Moment of Area:**
Since $\int_A z^2 \, dA \equiv I$ (the second moment of area about the neutral axis), the equation reduces to the fundamental **Moment-Curvature Equation**:

$$M = \frac{EI}{\rho} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{M}{EI}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**
The product $EI$ appears as the **proportionality constant** between applied moment and resulting curvature. Hence, flexural rigidity is literally the **bending stiffness per unit curvature**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind the Formula:** A larger $EI$ means a smaller curvature for the same moment — i.e., the beam bends less. This is why flexural rigidity is called a "rigidity" rather than a "compliance."

## 2.2 Extension to Plate Bending — The Plate Flexural Rigidity D

For a thin plate under transverse load, classical plate theory yields a **biharmonic governing equation**:

$$D\nabla^4 w = q(x,y)$$

where $w$ is the transverse deflection and $q$ is the distributed load. The flexural rigidity of the plate is:

$$D = \frac{Eh^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$$

The factor $(1-\nu^2)$ in the denominator arises because in **plate bending (2D)**, lateral contraction is restrained (plane strain condition), whereas in **beam bending (1D)**, lateral contraction is unrestrained (plane stress). This makes plates **stiffer in bending** than a comparable beam strip by a factor of $1/(1-\nu^2)$. For $\nu = 0.3$, this factor is approximately **1.099** (a 9.9% increase).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $EI$ | Beam flexural rigidity | N·m² |
| 2 | $D = \dfrac{Eh^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$ | Plate flexural rigidity | N·m |
| 3 | $\dfrac{1}{\rho} = \dfrac{M}{EI}$ | Moment-curvature relationship | m⁻¹ |
| 4 | $EI\dfrac{d^2w}{dx^2} = M(x)$ | Euler-Bernoulli beam equation | N·m |
| 5 | $EI\dfrac{d^4w}{dx^4} = q(x)$ | Beam deflection under distributed load | N/m |
| 6 | $D\nabla^4 w = q$ | Kirchhoff plate equation | N/m² |
| 7 | $I = \dfrac{bh^3}{12}$ | Rectangular cross-section | m⁴ |
| 8 | $I = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{64}$ | Solid circular cross-section | m⁴ |
| 9 | $I = \dfrac{\pi (D_o^4 - D_i^4)}{64}$ | Hollow circular cross-section | m⁴ |
| 10 | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{Mc}{I}$ | Maximum bending stress | Pa |
| 11 | $\delta_{max} = \dfrac{PL^3}{48EI}$ | Center deflection of simply supported beam (center load) | m |
| 12 | $\theta = \dfrac{PL^2}{16EI}$ | End slope of simply supported beam (center load) | rad |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Always check whether the problem refers to **beam bending (EI)** or **plate bending (D)**. Mixing them up is the single most common error in naval architecture solid mechanics questions.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Flexural rigidity is the cornerstone of:

- **Hull Girder Strength Analysis:** The entire ship hull acts as a hollow box girder, and its $EI$ determines the still-water and wave-induced bending stresses. Classification societies (IRS, Lloyd's, DNV) mandate minimum section moduli based on $EI$ requirements.
- **Local Panel Buckling:** Stiffened plate panels between transverse frames are checked against elastic buckling using $D$, with the critical buckling stress being $\sigma_{cr} = k \dfrac{\pi^2 D}{b^2 t}$.
- **Grillage Analysis:** Bottom plating supported by longitudinal and transverse stiffeners forms a "grillage" — analyzed as an orthotropic plate with directional flexural rigidities $D_x$ and $D_y$.
- **Stiffener Design:** The effective flexural rigidity of a stiffened plate combines the plate's $D$ with the stiffener's $EI_s$ acting about its own neutral axis.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From Basic Beam Mechanics to Flexural Rigidity

We derive the fundamental relationship $M = EI/\rho$ starting from first principles.

### Step A — Kinematics of Pure Bending

Consider a beam segment of length $dx$ that deforms into an arc of radius $\rho$ under the action of a pure bending moment $M$. A fiber at distance $z$ from the neutral axis (NA) has an original length $dx$ and deforms to $(R + z)\,d\theta$ on the tension side or $(R - z)\,d\theta$ on the compression side, where $R = \rho$ (radius to neutral surface).

The longitudinal strain at distance $z$ is:

$$\varepsilon_z = \frac{(\rho + z)\,d\theta - \rho\,d\theta}{\rho\,d\theta} = \frac{z}{\rho}$$

### Step B — Application of Hooke's Law

For a linearly elastic material in uniaxial stress state (valid for slender beams where lateral stresses are negligible):

$$\sigma_z = E\varepsilon_z = \frac{Ez}{\rho}$$

### Step C — Stress Distribution

The stress varies **linearly** across the depth, being zero at the neutral axis ($z = 0$) and maximum at the extreme fibers ($z = \pm c$). This linear distribution is the foundation of the flexure formula.

### Step D — Moment Equilibrium

The resultant internal bending moment equals the integral of bending stress times lever arm over the cross-sectional area:

$$M = \int_A \sigma_z \cdot z \, dA = \int_A \frac{Ez^2}{\rho} \, dA$$

Since $E$ and $\rho$ are constants over the cross-section, they factor out:

$$M = \frac{E}{\rho}\int_A z^2 \, dA$$

### Step E — Definition of Second Moment of Area

By definition, the second moment of area (area moment of inertia) about the neutral axis is:

$$I \equiv \int_A z^2 \, dA \quad [\text{units: m}^4]$$

Substituting:

$$\boxed{M = \frac{EI}{\rho}}$$

Equivalently:

$$\frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{M}{EI} \quad \text{or} \quad \kappa = \frac{M}{EI}$$

### Step F — Governing Differential Equation

Since curvature $\kappa \approx d^2w/dx^2$ for small deflections in the Euler-Bernoulli framework:

$$EI\frac{d^2w}{dx^2} = M(x)$$

Differentiating twice with respect to $x$ and using $dM/dx = V$ (shear) and $dV/dx = -q$ (distributed load):

$$EI\frac{d^4w}{dx^4} = -q(x)$$

or equivalently:

$$EI\frac{d^4w}{dx^4} = q(x)$$

depending on sign convention. This is the **fundamental governing equation of beam bending**, with $EI$ appearing as the bending stiffness.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Plate Flexural Rigidity $D$

For a rectangular plate element of dimensions $dx \times dy \times h$ under transverse load, the plate theory assumes (Kirchhoff-Love hypotheses):

1. Straight normals remain straight and normal to the mid-surface after deformation.
2. Normal stress $\sigma_z \approx 0$ (thin plate assumption).
3. In-plane displacements $u$ and $v$ are linear through thickness.

The strain-displacement relations for the mid-surface are:

$$\varepsilon_x = -z\frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2}, \quad \varepsilon_y = -z\frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial y^2}, \quad \gamma_{xy} = -2z\frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x \partial y}$$

The stress-strain relations under **plane strain** (because lateral contraction is restrained in plate bending) are:

$$\sigma_x = \frac{E}{1-\nu^2}\left(\varepsilon_x + \nu\varepsilon_y\right), \quad \sigma_y = \frac{E}{1-\nu^2}\left(\varepsilon_y + \nu\varepsilon_x\right), \quad \tau_{xy} = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}\gamma_{xy}$$

Substituting the strain expressions and integrating through thickness to obtain moment resultants $M_x = \int_{-h/2}^{h/2}\sigma_x z\,dz$, etc.:

$$M_x = -D\left(\frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2} + \nu\frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial y^2}\right)$$

$$M_y = -D\left(\frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial y^2} + \nu\frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2}\right)$$

$$M_{xy} = -D(1-\nu)\frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x \partial y}$$

where the plate flexural rigidity emerges as:

$$\boxed{D = \frac{Eh^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}}$$

The factor $(1-\nu^2)$ distinguishes plate rigidity from a naive "beam strip" rigidity $Eh^3/12$ because the **in-plane restraint** of the surrounding plate (plane strain) makes it stiffer.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Beam vs. Plate

| Property | Beam (Euler-Bernoulli) | Plate (Kirchhoff-Love) |
|---|---|---|
| Geometry | 1D (length $L$) | 2D (length $a$, breadth $b$) |
| Rigidity term | $EI$ | $D = Eh^3/[12(1-\nu^2)]$ |
| Rigidity units | N·m² | N·m |
| Governing equation | $EI\,d^4w/dx^4 = q$ | $D\nabla^4 w = q$ |
| Order of ODE/PDE | 4th-order ODE | 4th-order PDE (biharmonic) |
| Stress state assumption | Plane stress (uniaxial) | Plane strain (biaxial restrained) |
| Lateral contraction factor | None (free) | $1/(1-\nu^2)$ |
| Typical naval arch. application | Stiffener, girder | Hull plate, deck plate |

## 3.4 Numerical Illustration — Computing Deflection Using Flexural Rigidity

**Problem Setup:** A simply supported steel beam of span $L = 4$ m, breadth $b = 100$ mm, depth $h = 200$ mm carries a central point load $P = 20$ kN. Compute the maximum deflection.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Material Properties:**
$E = 200 \times 10^9$ Pa, $\nu = 0.3$ (not needed for beam).

**Step 2 — Second Moment of Area:**
$I = \dfrac{bh^3}{12} = \dfrac{(0.100)(0.200)^3}{12} = \dfrac{(0.100)(0.008)}{12} = \dfrac{8 \times 10^{-4}}{12} = 6.667 \times 10^{-5}$ m⁴.

**Step 3 — Flexural Rigidity:**
$EI = (200 \times 10^9)(6.667 \times 10^{-5}) = 1.333 \times 10^7$ N·m².

**Step 4 — Maximum Deflection Formula (Simply Supported, Central Load):**
$\delta_{max} = \dfrac{PL^3}{48EI}$.

**Step 5 — Substituting Numerical Values:**
$\delta_{max} = \dfrac{(20 \times 10^3)(4)^3}{48 \times 1.333 \times 10^7} = \dfrac{(20 \times 10^3)(64)}{6.4 \times 10^8} = \dfrac{1.28 \times 10^6}{6.4 \times 10^8} = 2.0 \times 10^{-3}$ m = **2.0 mm**.

**Step 6 — Interpretation:**
A deflection of 2 mm over a 4 m span is well within the serviceability limit of $L/250 = 16$ mm, confirming the beam's adequacy.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Flow Diagram — Flexural Rigidity in Naval Architecture Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Input Data E nu Geometry] --> B1[Compute Second Moment of Area I]
    A2[Plate Thickness h] --> B2[Compute Plate Rigidity D]
    A1 --> B2
    B1 --> C1[Flexural Rigidity EI]
    B2 --> C2[Flexural Rigidity D]
    C1 --> D1[Hull Girder Bending Stress]
    C1 --> D2[Stiffener Deflection Check]
    C2 --> D3[Local Panel Buckling Check]
    C2 --> D4[Grillage Plate Analysis]
    C2 --> D5[Plating Stress Check]
    D1 --> E1[Class Approval Check]
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E1
    D4 --> E1
    D5 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[Structural Adequacy Verified]
    E1 --> F2[Redesign Required]
```

**Figure 4.1:** Block-level architecture showing how flexural rigidity ($EI$ for beams, $D$ for plates) feeds into the four primary structural analyses required for a ship hull: hull girder bending, stiffener deflection, local panel buckling, and grillage/plating checks. Each branch terminates in a class society approval verification.

## 4.2 Conceptual Relationship Diagram — Bending Moment to Curvature to Flexural Rigidity

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Applied Load P or q x] --> L2[Bending Moment M x]
    L2 --> L3[Curvature 1 over rho]
    L3 --> L4[Deflection w x]
    L1 --> L5[Shear Force V x]
    L5 --> L2
    L4 --> L6[Slope theta x]
    L6 --> L3
    L3 --> L7{1 over rho equals M over EI}
    L7 --> L8[Flexural Rigidity EI is the Stiffness Coefficient]
    L8 --> L9[High EI means Small Curvature Stiff Structure]
    L8 --> L10[Low EI means Large Curvature Compliant Structure]
```

**Figure 4.2:** Conceptual map showing how applied loads generate bending moments, which in turn produce curvature and deflection through the inverse relationship $1/\rho = M/EI$. The product $EI$ serves as the **stiffness coefficient** that links cause (moment) to effect (curvature).

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Plate vs. Beam Rigidity in Ship Structures

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Ship Hull Structural Element] --> P2{Is the Element a 1D Member or 2D Surface}
    P2 --> P3[Stiffener or Girder]
    P2 --> P4[Plate Panel or Shell]
    P3 --> Q1[Use Beam Theory EI]
    P4 --> Q2[Use Plate Theory D]
    Q1 --> R1[I equals integral z squared dA]
    Q2 --> R2[D equals E h cubed over 12 times 1 minus nu squared]
    R1 --> S1[Compute Deflection Stress]
    R2 --> S1
    S1 --> T1[Apply Classification Rules]
    T1 --> U1[Final Structural Verification]
```

**Figure 4.3:** Decision tree guiding naval architects in selecting the appropriate flexural rigidity formulation based on whether the structural element behaves as a 1D beam (use $EI$) or a 2D plate (use $D$).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: Define flexural rigidity of a beam. How is it different from plate flexural rigidity?
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

Flexural rigidity of a beam is the product of Young's Modulus ($E$) and the second moment of area ($I$) of the cross-section about the neutral axis. It represents the resistance of the beam to bending deformation and has units of **N·m²**. Mathematically, it is expressed as $EI$, where $E$ has units of Pa and $I$ has units of m⁴.

Plate flexural rigidity, denoted by $D$, applies to two-dimensional thin plate elements and is defined as:

$$D = \frac{Eh^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$$

The key differences are: (i) plate rigidity $D$ has units of N·m (not N·m²), (ii) it includes Poisson's ratio $\nu$ in the denominator due to plane strain conditions in 2D bending, and (iii) it depends on the cube of plate thickness $h^3$, whereas beam rigidity depends on the second moment of area which varies with cross-sectional geometry.

---

### Question A2: State the moment-curvature relationship and explain the physical significance of flexural rigidity.
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The moment-curvature relationship for a beam in pure bending is:

$$\frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{M}{EI}$$

where $1/\rho$ is the curvature, $M$ is the internal bending moment, and $EI$ is the flexural rigidity.

**Physical Significance:** The flexural rigidity $EI$ acts as the **proportionality constant** between the applied bending moment and the resulting curvature. A higher value of $EI$ implies that the beam bends less (smaller curvature) for the same applied moment, indicating greater resistance to bending. Physically, $E$ represents the material's inherent elastic stiffness, while $I$ represents how efficiently the cross-sectional geometry distributes material away from the neutral axis. This is why I-beams and hollow tubes are structurally efficient — they maximize $I$ for a given weight.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Poisson factor:** When computing plate rigidity $D$, students often write $D = Eh^3/12$, missing the crucial $(1-\nu^2)$ term. This costs **2 marks** in a 14-mark question.
> 2. **Unit confusion:** Mixing N·m² (beam) with N·m (plate) is a common error — examiners deduct **1 mark** for unit inconsistencies.
> 3. **Sign convention errors:** In the Euler-Bernoulli equation $EI\,d^2w/dx^2 = M(x)$, students frequently drop the sign or confuse sagging/hogging conventions.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert mm to m:** All dimensions must be in SI (meters) before computing $I$. A 100 mm dimension used as 100 instead of 0.1 m gives a wildly incorrect answer.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): Hull Plate Flexural Rigidity and Buckling Analysis
`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** A rectangular plate panel of a ship's side shell has dimensions $a = 800$ mm (length between stiffeners) and $b = 600$ mm (breadth between stiffeners), with plate thickness $h = 12$ mm. The material is mild steel with $E = 200$ GPa and $\nu = 0.3$. Calculate the **plate flexural rigidity** $D$ and the **second moment of area per unit width** of the plate.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** A simply supported steel beam of length $L = 6$ m, rectangular cross-section $b = 150$ mm wide and $d = 300$ mm deep, is subjected to a uniformly distributed load $w = 10$ kN/m over its entire span. Calculate the **maximum bending stress** and **maximum deflection** using flexural rigidity concepts.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A — Part (a):

**Given:**
$a = 0.800$ m, $b = 0.600$ m, $h = 0.012$ m, $E = 200 \times 10^9$ Pa, $\nu = 0.3$.

**Step 1 — Plate Flexural Rigidity:**

$$D = \frac{Eh^3}{12(1-\nu^2)} = \frac{(200 \times 10^9)(0.012)^3}{12(1 - 0.3^2)}$$

**Step 2 — Numerator Evaluation:**
$(0.012)^3 = 1.728 \times 10^{-6}$ m³
$(200 \times 10^9)(1.728 \times 10^{-6}) = 3.456 \times 10^5$ N·m.

**Step 3 — Denominator Evaluation:**
$12(1 - 0.09) = 12 \times 0.91 = 10.92$.

**Step 4 — Final Value:**
$$D = \frac{3.456 \times 10^5}{10.92} = 3.165 \times 10^4 \text{ N·m}$$

**Answer:** $\boxed{D \approx 3.165 \times 10^4 \text{ N·m}}$

**Step 5 — Second Moment of Area per Unit Width (for plate strip):**
$I_{\text{strip}} = \dfrac{h^3}{12} = \dfrac{(0.012)^3}{12} = \dfrac{1.728 \times 10^{-6}}{12} = 1.44 \times 10^{-7}$ m⁴/m.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A — Part (b):

**Given:**
$L = 6$ m, $b = 0.150$ m, $d = 0.300$ m, $w = 10 \times 10^3$ N/m, $E = 200 \times 10^9$ Pa.

**Step 1 — Second Moment of Area:**
$I = \dfrac{bd^3}{12} = \dfrac{(0.150)(0.300)^3}{12} = \dfrac{(0.150)(0.027)}{12} = \dfrac{4.05 \times 10^{-3}}{12} = 3.375 \times 10^{-4}$ m⁴.

**Step 2 — Flexural Rigidity:**
$EI = (200 \times 10^9)(3.375 \times 10^{-4}) = 6.75 \times 10^7$ N·m².

**Step 3 — Maximum Bending Moment (simply supported, UDL):**
$M_{max} = \dfrac{wL^2}{8} = \dfrac{(10 \times 10^3)(6)^2}{8} = \dfrac{360 \times 10^3}{8} = 45 \times 10^3$ N·m.

**Step 4 — Maximum Bending Stress:**
$\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M_{max} \cdot c}{I} = \dfrac{(45 \times 10^3)(0.150)}{3.375 \times 10^{-4}}$ where $c = d/2 = 0.150$ m.

$\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{6750}{3.375 \times 10^{-4}} = 2.0 \times 10^7$ Pa = **20 MPa**.

**Step 5 — Maximum Deflection (simply supported, UDL):**
$\delta_{max} = \dfrac{5wL^4}{384EI} = \dfrac{5(10 \times 10^3)(6)^4}{384 \times 6.75 \times 10^7}$.

Numerator: $5 \times 10^4 \times 1296 = 6.48 \times 10^7$.

Denominator: $384 \times 6.75 \times 10^7 = 2.592 \times 10^{10}$.

$\delta_{max} = \dfrac{6.48 \times 10^7}{2.592 \times 10^{10}} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m = **2.5 mm**.

**Step 6 — Serviceability Check:**
Allowable deflection = $L/250 = 6000/250 = 24$ mm. Since $2.5$ mm $< 24$ mm, the beam is **adequate in deflection**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Comprehensive Beam Analysis
`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Derive the moment-curvature relationship $M = EI/\rho$ from first principles, clearly stating all assumptions of Euler-Bernoulli beam theory.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** A ship deck plate is supported by longitudinal stiffeners (T-sections) spaced at $s = 600$ mm apart. The plate has thickness $t = 10$ mm, Young's modulus $E = 210$ GPa, and Poisson's ratio $\nu = 0.3$. Between stiffeners, the plate acts as a one-way slab spanning 600 mm under a uniform pressure $q = 50$ kPa. Treating the plate strip as a beam of width $s = 600$ mm, compute: (i) the effective flexural rigidity $EI$ of the plate strip per unit width, and (ii) the maximum bending moment per unit width and compare it with the simply supported strip moment.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B — Part (a):

**Assumptions of Euler-Bernoulli Beam Theory:**

1. The beam material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic** (Hooke's law applies).
2. **Plane sections remain plane and normal to the neutral axis** after bending (no shear deformation).
3. **Deflections are small** compared to the span, so that $\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta \approx \theta$ and curvature $\kappa \approx d^2w/dx^2$.
4. **Shear deformation is neglected** (beam is slender, $L/h > 20$).
5. The beam is initially **straight and unloaded**.

**Derivation (Recapitulated with All Steps):**

A beam segment of original length $dx$ bends to subtend angle $d\theta$ at the center of curvature $\rho$. A fiber at distance $z$ from the neutral axis has its length changed from $dx$ to $(\rho - z)\,d\theta$.

**Strain:**
$$\varepsilon = \frac{(\rho - z)d\theta - \rho\,d\theta}{\rho\,d\theta} = -\frac{z}{\rho}$$

**Stress (Hooke's Law):**
$$\sigma = E\varepsilon = -\frac{Ez}{\rho}$$

**Moment about neutral axis:**
$$M = -\int_A \sigma \cdot z\,dA = \int_A \frac{Ez^2}{\rho}\,dA = \frac{E}{\rho}\int_A z^2\,dA$$

**Since $I = \int_A z^2\,dA$:**
$$\boxed{M = \frac{EI}{\rho}} \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{M}{EI}$$

This is the fundamental moment-curvature equation. Q.E.D.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B — Part (b):

**Given:**
$s = 0.600$ m (plate strip width = stiffener spacing), $t = 0.010$ m, $E = 210 \times 10^9$ Pa, $\nu = 0.3$, $q = 50 \times 10^3$ Pa, span $L = s = 0.600$ m.

**Step 1 — Plate Strip as a Beam:**
Per unit width (b = 1 m), the strip has:
$I_{\text{strip}} = \dfrac{(1.0)(t)^3}{12} = \dfrac{(1.0)(0.010)^3}{12} = 8.333 \times 10^{-8}$ m⁴/m.

**Step 2 — Beam Flexural Rigidity of Strip:**
$EI_{\text{strip}} = (210 \times 10^9)(8.333 \times 10^{-8}) = 1.75 \times 10^4$ N·m²/m.

**Step 3 — Plate Flexural Rigidity (for comparison):**
$D = \dfrac{E t^3}{12(1-\nu^2)} = \dfrac{(210 \times 10^9)(0.010)^3}{12(1-0.09)} = \dfrac{(210 \times 10^9)(10^{-6})}{10.92} = \dfrac{2.1 \times 10^5}{10.92} = 1.923 \times 10^4$ N·m/m.

Note: $D$ per unit width is slightly higher than the beam strip rigidity $EI_{\text{strip}}/b$ due to the $1/(1-\nu^2)$ factor.

**Step 4 — Distributed Load per Unit Width:**
$q^* = q \times b = (50 \times 10^3)(1.0) = 50 \times 10^3$ N/m per meter width.

**Step 5 — Maximum Bending Moment per Unit Width (Simply Supported Strip):**
$M_{max}^* = \dfrac{q^* L^2}{8} = \dfrac{(50 \times 10^3)(0.600)^2}{8} = \dfrac{(50 \times 10^3)(0.36)}{8} = \dfrac{18{,}000}{8} = 2250$ N·m/m.

**Step 6 — Comparison and Interpretation:**
The bending moment of **2250 N·m/m** acts on the plate strip spanning between two stiffeners. The effective flexural rigidity used depends on whether we treat it as a 1D beam (use $EI_{\text{strip}}$) or as part of a 2D plate field (use $D$). For the 1D analysis above, $EI_{\text{strip}} = 1.75 \times 10^4$ N·m²/m, which would give a maximum deflection:

$$\delta_{max} = \frac{5 q^* L^4}{384 EI_{\text{strip}}} = \frac{5(50 \times 10^3)(0.600)^4}{384 \times 1.75 \times 10^4} = \frac{5 \times 50{,}000 \times 0.1296}{6.72 \times 10^6} = \frac{32{,}400}{6.72 \times 10^6} = 4.82 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} \approx 4.82 \text{ mm}$$

This is a preliminary estimate; the actual 2D plate analysis would use $D$ and would yield a different (typically smaller) deflection due to two-way load distribution.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Points for KTU 2024 ESE:**

- ✅ **Beam flexural rigidity = $EI$** with units **N·m²**. Compute $I$ about the **neutral axis** of bending.
- ✅ **Plate flexural rigidity = $D = Eh^3/[12(1-\nu^2)]$** with units **N·m**. The Poisson factor distinguishes 2D plate behavior from 1D beam behavior.
- ✅ **Moment-curvature equation:** $1/\rho = M/EI$ is the master relationship — every beam bending formula traces back to this.
- ✅ **Governing ODE for beams:** $EI\,d^4w/dx^4 = q(x)$. Integrating four times with appropriate boundary conditions yields deflections and slopes.
- ✅ **Governing PDE for plates:** $D\nabla^4 w = q(x,y)$. Biharmonic equation requires two boundary conditions on each edge (deflection and slope).
- ✅ **Depth cubed dominance:** In rectangular beams, $I = bh^3/12$ — increasing depth $h$ is far more efficient than increasing breadth $b$ for boosting flexural rigidity.
- ✅ **Plate thickness cubed dominance:** Plate rigidity scales with $h^3$, so doubling plate thickness increases $D$ by a factor of **8**.
- ✅ **Poisson ratio for shipbuilding steel:** Always use $\nu = 0.30$ unless specified otherwise.
- ✅ **Young's modulus for shipbuilding steel:** Use $E = 200$ GPa (mild) or $E = 210$ GPa (higher-grade) as per problem statement.
- ✅ **Naval architecture applications to remember:**
  1. Hull girder bending stress = $M_{\text{hull}} \cdot y/I_{\text{hull}}$
  2. Local panel buckling = depends on $D$ and boundary conditions
  3. Grillage analysis = orthotropic plate with directional $D_x, D_y$
  4. Stiffener deflection check = $\delta \leq L/250$ or as per class rules
- ✅ **Unit conversions are critical:** Convert all dimensions to **meters** before computing $I$ or $D$; convert loads to **N**; stresses will emerge in **Pa**.
- ✅ **Sign convention for Euler-Bernoulli:** Sagging moment produces positive curvature ($d^2w/dx^2 > 0$ for downward $w$); verify with problem-specific free-body diagrams.
- ✅ **Failure to account for Poisson's ratio in plate problems costs 2 marks** — a recurring KTU examiner pitfall.
- ✅ **Recall the difference:** $EI$ governs **beam stiffness**; $D$ governs **plate stiffness**; both are forms of flexural rigidity but with different physical meanings and units.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Stresses in Symmetrical Sections

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Solid Mechanics for Naval Architects**, the term **Stresses in Symmetrical Sections** refers to the analysis and evaluation of the internal stress distribution produced when a transverse bending moment acts upon a structural cross-section that possesses **one or more axes of symmetry**. A *symmetrical section* is one in which the geometry is mirrored about a principal centroidal axis (commonly the horizontal neutral axis or the vertical axis of symmetry, or both). The bending stress at any fiber located at a perpendicular distance **y** from the neutral axis is given by the **Euler–Bernoulli Flexure Formula**:

$$\sigma = \frac{M \cdot y}{I}$$

where **M** is the bending moment, **y** is the distance from the neutral axis (NA), and **I** is the **Second Moment of Area** (also called the Area Moment of Inertia) about that axis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For naval architectural structures (particularly the hull girder), a cross-section is considered *symmetrical* when it is symmetric about the **horizontal centerline (the neutral axis under hogging/sagging)**. The hull girder, although longitudinally stiffened, is treated as a beam whose *effective* midship section is symmetrical about the horizontal axis passing through its centroid.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are holding a long, flat wooden ruler horizontally and pushing down on its center with both hands. The ruler bends into a shallow "U" shape (this is called **sagging**). Now look at the cross-section of the ruler:

- The **top fibers** are squeezed together → they experience **compressive stress**.
- The **bottom fibers** are stretched apart → they experience **tensile stress**.
- Somewhere in the middle, there is a layer that does **not** experience any stress at all → this is the **Neutral Axis (NA)**.

For a *symmetrical* section (like a rectangle, I-beam, or a ship's hull section), this neutral axis passes exactly through the **centroid** of the cross-section because the material above and below the axis is balanced geometrically.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:** A symmetrical section behaves predictably under bending — stress increases *linearly* as you move away from the centerline, and it is symmetric (same magnitude above and below the NA at equal distances). This predictability is exactly why ship designers prefer symmetric midship sections.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following **standard metric** values are essential for KTU 2024 numerical problems:

- **Young's Modulus for mild steel (shipbuilding steel):** $E = 2.0 \times 10^{5} \text{ N/mm}^2 = \textbf{200 GPa}$
- **Young's Modulus for marine aluminium alloys:** $E \approx 70 \text{ GPa}$
- **Yield stress for typical shipbuilding steel (Grade A):** $\sigma_y = \textbf{235 MPa}$
- **Section Modulus unit:** $\text{mm}^3$ (SI), $\text{in}^3$ (Imperial)
- **Bending stress unit:** $\text{N/mm}^2 = \text{MPa}$ (SI)

> [!TIP]
> Always carry forward units in your KTU board-exam solutions — examiners specifically award **partial marks** for correctly written units even when numerical errors occur.

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Assumptions of Pure Bending Theory

The flexure formula $\sigma = \frac{My}{I}$ is valid **only** under the following conditions, which form the backbone of KTU's Module 3 testing:

1. The beam material is **homogeneous** and **isotropic** (uniform properties in all directions).
2. The beam is **initially straight** and has a **constant cross-section** along its length (prismatic).
3. The beam is subjected to **pure bending** (constant bending moment, no shear force).
4. **Plane sections remain plane and normal to the deformed neutral axis** after bending (Euler–Bernoulli hypothesis).
5. The material obeys **Hooke's Law** within the elastic limit: $\sigma = E \varepsilon$.
6. The section possesses at least **one axis of symmetry**, and loading occurs in the plane of symmetry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Violation Alert:** If the section is *unsymmetrical* (e.g., a channel section loaded through its centroid but not through its shear center), or if the load does **not** lie in a plane of symmetry, **unsymmetrical bending** occurs. The neutral axis no longer coincides with the centroidal axis, and the analysis becomes more complex (uses product of inertia). KTU 2024 Module 3 specifically restricts the scope to **symmetrical sections**, simplifying this analysis.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of the Flexure Formula

**Step 1 — Strain Distribution (Geometry of Deformation):**
When a beam bends into a circular arc of radius **R**, the longitudinal fiber at distance **y** from the neutral axis undergoes a strain:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{y}{R}$$

**Step 2 — Stress Distribution (Hooke's Law):**
Since the material is linearly elastic, the bending stress at distance **y** is:

$$\sigma = E \varepsilon = \frac{E \cdot y}{R}$$

This shows the stress varies **linearly** with **y** — zero at the NA, maximum at the extreme fiber.

**Step 3 — Moment Equilibrium (Statics):**
The internal moment **M** about the NA must equal the integral of $\sigma \cdot y$ over the entire cross-section:

$$M = \int_A \sigma \cdot y \, dA = \int_A \frac{E \cdot y^2}{R} \, dA = \frac{E}{R} \int_A y^2 \, dA$$

**Step 4 — Definition of Second Moment of Area:**

$$I = \int_A y^2 \, dA \quad \text{(units: mm}^4\text{ or m}^4\text{)}$$

**Step 5 — Combine to Get Flexure Formula:**

$$M = \frac{E I}{R} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{1}{R} = \frac{M}{EI}$$

Substituting back:

$$\sigma = \frac{E \cdot y}{R} = \frac{E \cdot y \cdot M}{E I} = \frac{M \cdot y}{I}$$

**Step 6 — Maximum Bending Stress:**
At the extreme fiber, $y = y_{\max}$, so:

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M \cdot y_{\max}}{I} = \frac{M}{Z}$$

where **Z** is the **Section Modulus**, defined as $Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{\max}}$.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

The table below is the **master reference** you should memorize for KTU 2024 ESE Module 3 problems. **All units are in SI (mm / N / MPa) unless stated.**

| # | Quantity / Section Type | Formula for $I$ (about NA) | Formula for $Z = I/y_{\max}$ | $y_{\max}$ (Extreme Fiber Distance) |
|---|--------------------------|------------------------------|-------------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| 1 | Solid Rectangle (b × h, h = depth in bending plane) | $I_{NA} = \dfrac{b h^{3}}{12}$ | $Z = \dfrac{b h^{2}}{6}$ | $h/2$ |
| 2 | Hollow Rectangle (outer B × H, inner b × h) | $I_{NA} = \dfrac{B H^{3} - b h^{3}}{12}$ | $Z = \dfrac{B H^{3} - b h^{3}}{6 H}$ | $H/2$ |
| 3 | Solid Circle (diameter d) | $I_{NA} = \dfrac{\pi d^{4}}{64}$ | $Z = \dfrac{\pi d^{3}}{32}$ | $d/2$ |
| 4 | Hollow Circle (outer D, inner d) | $I_{NA} = \dfrac{\pi (D^{4} - d^{4})}{64}$ | $Z = \dfrac{\pi (D^{4} - d^{4})}{32 D}$ | $D/2$ |
| 5 | I-Section (flanges B×t_f, web h_w×t_w) | $I_{NA} = \dfrac{B H^{3} - (B - t_w) h_{w}^{3}}{12}$ | $Z = \dfrac{I_{NA}}{H/2}$ | $H/2$ |
| 6 | T-Section (flange B×t_f, stem h_s×t_s) | $I_{NA} = \dfrac{B t_{f}^{3}}{12} + B t_{f}\left(\bar{y} - \dfrac{t_{f}}{2}\right)^{2} + \dfrac{t_{s} h_{s}^{3}}{12} + t_{s} h_{s}\left(\bar{y}^{\prime}\right)^{2}$ | $Z = \dfrac{I_{NA}}{\max(y_{\text{top}}, y_{\text{bot}})}$ | $\bar{y}$ from bottom or $H - \bar{y}$ |
| 7 | Triangle (base b, height h, vertex up) | $I_{NA} = \dfrac{b h^{3}}{36}$ | $Z = \dfrac{b h^{2}}{24}$ | $h/3$ (from base) or $2h/3$ (from vertex) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Watch:** The most common mistake is confusing the **depth in the bending plane** with the **width**. For a rectangle bending about its horizontal NA, $h$ must be the vertical dimension (depth), **not** the horizontal dimension (breadth $b$). Swapping these gives an answer off by orders of magnitude.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

For a **Naval Architect**, the section modulus **Z** of the ship's midship section is *the single most important hull-girder strength parameter**:

- **Rule-based design** (e.g., **DNV**, **ABS**, **Lloyd's Register**) specifies a *minimum* section modulus based on the vessel's length, breadth, block coefficient, and design wave bending moment.
- The required **Z** is then translated into a *scantling arrangement*: plate thicknesses, longitudinal stiffener sizes, and web frame spacing.
- For a typical **Panamax bulk carrier**, the required midship section modulus is on the order of **$Z \approx 20$ to $30 \text{ m}^3$**, achieved by a combination of deck plating, bottom plating, and side shell longitudinal stiffeners.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Insight:** Doubling the section modulus Z of a hull cross-section (for the same material and weight) can roughly halve the maximum bending stress. This is why **doubly-symmetric** hull sections (e.g., box girders in warships) are favored for vessels subjected to high bending moments.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Flexure Formula from First Principles

The following derivation is laid out step-by-step for KTU full-mark scoring. Each step is shown with its underlying principle.

**Starting Premises:**
- Pure bending of a prismatic beam.
- Symmetrical cross-section.
- Material obeys Hooke's Law within elastic limit.

---

**Step 1 — Geometry of Deformation (Pure Bending Kinematics)**

Consider a small element of length $dx$ of the beam, originally bounded by two transverse planes $a\,a$ and $b\,b$ which are perpendicular to the neutral axis. After bending, these planes rotate by an angle $d\theta$ and remain plane but are no longer parallel.

The fiber located at a distance **y** from the NA changes its original length $dx$ to a new length $(R + y)\,d\theta$, where **R** is the radius of curvature of the neutral axis. Therefore, the change in length of this fiber is:

$$\Delta L = (R + y)\,d\theta - dx$$

Since $dx = R \cdot d\theta$, we can substitute:

$$\Delta L = (R + y)\,d\theta - R\,d\theta = y\,d\theta$$

The **longitudinal strain** $\varepsilon$ at distance $y$ is:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{dx} = \frac{y\,d\theta}{R\,d\theta} = \frac{y}{R}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Anchor:** The ratio $d\theta/dx = 1/R$ is the curvature $\kappa$ of the beam. Hence strain is directly proportional to distance from the NA.

---

**Step 2 — Apply Hooke's Law to Convert Strain to Stress**

For a linearly elastic material:

$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon = E \cdot \frac{y}{R} = \frac{E \cdot y}{R}$$

This is the fundamental stress distribution — **linear in y**.

---

**Step 3 — Resultant Force on the Cross-Section**

The internal axial force on any cross-section is the integral of $\sigma$ over the area:

$$F = \int_A \sigma \, dA = \int_A \frac{E \cdot y}{R} \, dA = \frac{E}{R} \int_A y \, dA$$

For a beam in pure bending, there is **no net axial force** (only a moment):

$$F = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \int_A y \, dA = 0$$

This is precisely the **definition of the centroid** — therefore, the neutral axis must pass through the centroid. For a symmetrical section, this is automatic.

---

**Step 4 — Internal Bending Moment from Stress Distribution**

Taking moments about the neutral axis:

$$M = \int_A \sigma \cdot y \, dA = \int_A \frac{E \cdot y}{R} \cdot y \, dA = \frac{E}{R} \int_A y^{2} \, dA$$

By definition of the second moment of area:

$$I_{NA} = \int_A y^{2} \, dA$$

Therefore:

$$M = \frac{E \cdot I_{NA}}{R} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{1}{R} = \frac{M}{E \cdot I_{NA}}$$

---

**Step 5 — Substitute Curvature Back into Stress Expression**

$$\sigma = \frac{E \cdot y}{R} = E \cdot y \cdot \frac{M}{E \cdot I_{NA}} = \frac{M \cdot y}{I_{NA}}$$

This is the **Euler–Bernoulli Flexure Formula**, valid for any beam in pure bending.

---

**Step 6 — Maximum Stress at the Extreme Fiber**

The maximum bending stress occurs at the farthest fiber from the NA, $y = y_{\max}$:

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M \cdot y_{\max}}{I_{NA}} = \frac{M}{Z}$$

where $Z = \dfrac{I_{NA}}{y_{\max}}$ is the **section modulus**.

**Q.E.D. (Quod Erat Demonstrandum)**

---

### 3.2 Derivative Proof: Why the Neutral Axis Passes Through the Centroid for Symmetrical Sections

Given: A symmetrical cross-section about the horizontal axis.

Consider the first moment of area above the NA:

$$Q_{NA} = \int_{A_{\text{above}}} y \, dA$$

For a section symmetric about the horizontal centroidal axis, the area distribution above this axis is a **mirror image** of the area below. Since the integrand $y \, dA$ has opposite signs above and below the NA, the contributions cancel out exactly:

$$Q_{NA} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \bar{y} = \frac{Q_{NA}}{A} = 0$$

Hence the NA is at $\bar{y} = 0$ (i.e., passes through the centroid).

### 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (Numerical Derivation)

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):** A simply supported steel beam of span **L = 4 m** carries a uniformly distributed load (UDL) of **w = 20 kN/m** over its entire span. The cross-section is a **symmetric I-section** with overall depth $H = 250$ mm, flange width $B = 150$ mm, flange thickness $t_f = 15$ mm, web thickness $t_w = 10$ mm, and web height $h_w = H - 2 t_f = 220$ mm. Calculate the maximum bending stress. Take $E = 2 \times 10^5$ MPa.

**Solution — Step by Step:**

**Step 1: Maximum Bending Moment for Simply Supported Beam with UDL:**

$$M_{\max} = \frac{w L^{2}}{8} = \frac{20 \times 4^{2}}{8} = \frac{320}{8} = 40 \text{ kN·m} = 40 \times 10^{6} \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Second Moment of Area:**

For the I-section (treating it as a full rectangle minus two side cut-outs):

$$I = \frac{B H^{3}}{12} - \frac{(B - t_w) h_{w}^{3}}{12}$$

Plugging in numerical values:

$$I = \frac{150 \times (250)^{3}}{12} - \frac{(150 - 10) \times (220)^{3}}{12}$$

Computing each term separately:

$$\frac{150 \times (250)^{3}}{12} = \frac{150 \times 15{,}625{,}000}{12} = \frac{2{,}343{,}750{,}000}{12} = 195{,}312{,}500 \text{ mm}^{4}$$

$$\frac{(150 - 10) \times (220)^{3}}{12} = \frac{140 \times 10{,}648{,}000}{12} = \frac{1{,}490{,}720{,}000}{12} = 124{,}226{,}667 \text{ mm}^{4}$$

Therefore:

$$I = 195{,}312{,}500 - 124{,}226{,}667 = 71{,}085{,}833 \text{ mm}^{4}$$

Converting to cm⁴ for cleaner reporting:

$$I \approx 71.09 \times 10^{6} \text{ mm}^{4} = 7109 \text{ cm}^{4}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Section Modulus:**

$$Z = \frac{I}{y_{\max}} = \frac{71.09 \times 10^{6}}{125} = 568{,}720 \text{ mm}^{3} \approx 568.72 \text{ cm}^{3}$$

**Step 4: Calculate Maximum Bending Stress:**

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M_{\max}}{Z} = \frac{40 \times 10^{6}}{568{,}720} = 70.33 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5: Verify Elastic Limit Compliance:**

$$\sigma_{\max} = 70.33 \text{ MPa} < \sigma_{y} = 235 \text{ MPa} \quad \checkmark \text{ (Safe)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Answer-Writing Tip:** Always conclude numerical problems with a **safe/unsafe** verdict based on the comparison with the yield stress. This single concluding sentence often fetches the **last 1 mark** in KTU board valuations.

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Stress Analysis Workflow for Naval Architects

The flow below represents the **operational workflow** that a Naval Architect follows when checking the bending strength of a ship's hull midship section against classification society rules. Note the input is the effective midship section geometry (treated as a *symmetrical section* under vertical hull-girder bending).

```mermaid

```
