## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**State the Euler–Bernoulli flexure formula and define the term "section modulus" with its units.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Euler–Bernoulli flexure formula for bending stress in a symmetrical section is:

$$\sigma = \frac{M \cdot y}{I}$$

where **M** = bending moment (N·mm), **y** = perpendicular distance from the neutral axis (mm), and **I** = second moment of area about the NA (mm⁴).

**Section Modulus** is defined as the ratio of the second moment of area to the distance of the extreme fiber from the neutral axis:

$$Z = \frac{I}{y_{\max}}$$

**Units:** mm³ (SI) or in³ (Imperial).

It is a geometric property of the cross-section that measures its **bending efficiency** — higher Z means lower bending stress for the same moment.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**For a symmetrical I-section, derive the expression for the second moment of area about the horizontal neutral axis.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Consider an I-section with overall depth **H**, flange width **B**, flange thickness **t_f**, web thickness **t_w**, and web height $h_w = H - 2 t_f$.

By the **subtraction method**, treat the I-section as a full rectangle $(B \times H)$ minus two side cut-outs of dimensions $(B - t_w) \times h_w$ each:

$$I_{NA} = \frac{B H^{3}}{12} - \frac{(B - t_w)\, h_{w}^{3}}{12}$$

This is valid because the cut-outs lie symmetrically on either side of the vertical centroidal axis, and the entire section is symmetric about the horizontal NA.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Modified]**

**(a)** A simply supported timber beam of span **5 m** carries a central point load of **25 kN**. The cross-section is rectangular, 120 mm wide and 240 mm deep. Calculate:
   1. The maximum bending moment.
   2. The section modulus.
   3. The maximum bending stress.

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A symmetric hollow circular shaft (outer diameter **120 mm**, inner diameter **80 mm**) is used as a propeller shaft transmitting torque. For pure bending only, determine the section modulus and the maximum bending stress if subjected to a bending moment of **10 kN·m**.

**(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution to Question A (a):**

**Step 1: Maximum Bending Moment (simply supported beam with central point load):**

$$M_{\max} = \frac{P \cdot L}{4} = \frac{25 \times 5}{4} = 31.25 \text{ kN·m} = 31.25 \times 10^{6} \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 2: Section Modulus (Rectangle with $b = 120$ mm, $h = 240$ mm):**

$$Z = \frac{b h^{2}}{6} = \frac{120 \times (240)^{2}}{6} = \frac{120 \times 57{,}600}{6} = \frac{6{,}912{,}000}{6} = 1{,}152{,}000 \text{ mm}^{3}$$

**Step 3: Maximum Bending Stress:**

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M_{\max}}{Z} = \frac{31.25 \times 10^{6}}{1{,}152{,}000} = 27.13 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4: Final Answer Statement:**

The maximum bending stress is **27.13 MPa** (tensile at bottom, compressive at top).

---

**Solution to Question A (b):**

**Step 1: Section Modulus of Hollow Circular Section:**

$$Z = \frac{\pi (D^{4} - d^{4})}{32\, D}$$

Substituting $D = 120$ mm, $d = 80$ mm:

$$D^{4} = (120)^{4} = 207{,}360{,}000 \text{ mm}^{4}$$

$$d^{4} = (80)^{4} = 40{,}960{,}000 \text{ mm}^{4}$$

$$D^{4} - d^{4} = 207{,}360{,}000 - 40{,}960{,}000 = 166{,}400{,}000 \text{ mm}^{4}$$

$$Z = \frac{\pi \times 166{,}400{,}000}{32 \times 120} = \frac{522{,}717{,}032}{3{,}840} = 136{,}124 \text{ mm}^{3}$$

**Step 2: Maximum Bending Stress:**

$$M = 10 \text{ kN·m} = 10 \times 10^{6} \text{ N·mm}$$

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{10 \times 10^{6}}{136{,}124} = 73.47 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Final Answer Statement:**

The maximum bending stress is **73.47 MPa**.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Derive from first principles the Euler–Bernoulli flexure formula $\sigma = \dfrac{M y}{I}$ for a beam in pure bending, clearly stating all assumptions.

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A symmetric I-section girder has the following properties: overall depth $H = 400$ mm, flange width $B = 200$ mm, flange thickness $t_f = 20$ mm, web thickness $t_w = 12$ mm$. Find:
   1. The second moment of area about the neutral axis.
   2. The section modulus.
   3. The maximum bending stress if the girder carries a bending moment of **80 kN·m**.

**(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution to Question B (a):**

*(Refer to the full derivation in Section 3.1 of these notes for the complete working. Key steps for 7 marks:)*

1. State the **six assumptions** of pure bending theory (1 mark).
2. Show the geometry of deformation: $\varepsilon = y/R$ (1 mark).
3. Apply Hooke's Law: $\sigma = Ey/R$ (1 mark).
4. Use $\int y\, dA = 0$ to show NA passes through centroid (1 mark).
5. Derive $M = EI/R$ (1 mark).
6. Combine to get $\sigma = My/I$ and define $Z = I/y_{\max}$ (2 marks).

---

**Solution to Question B (b):**

**Step 1: Web Height:**

$$h_w = H - 2 t_f = 400 - 2(20) = 360 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Second Moment of Area (subtraction method):**

$$I = \frac{B H^{3}}{12} - \frac{(B - t_w)\, h_{w}^{3}}{12}$$

Computing each term:

$$\frac{B H^{3}}{12} = \frac{200 \times (400)^{3}}{12} = \frac{200 \times 64{,}000{,}000}{12} = \frac{12{,}800{,}000{,}000}{12} = 1{,}066{,}666{,}667 \text{ mm}^{4}$$

$$B - t_w = 200 - 12 = 188 \text{ mm}$$

$$\frac{(B - t_w)\, h_{w}^{3}}{12} = \frac{188 \times (360)^{3}}{12} = \frac{188 \times 46{,}656{,}000}{12} = \frac{8{,}771{,}328{,}000}{12} = 730{,}944{,}000 \text{ mm}^{4}$$

Therefore:

$$I = 1{,}066{,}666{,}667 - 730{,}944{,}000 = 335{,}722{,}667 \text{ mm}^{4} \approx 335.72 \times 10^{6} \text{ mm}^{4}$$

**Step 3: Section Modulus:**

$$Z = \frac{I}{y_{\max}} = \frac{335.72 \times 10^{6}}{200} = 1{,}678{,}613 \text{ mm}^{3}$$

**Step 4: Maximum Bending Stress:**

$$M = 80 \text{ kN·m} = 80 \times 10^{6} \text{ N·mm}$$

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{80 \times 10^{6}}{1{,}678{,}613} = 47.66 \text{ MPa}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Students often forget to convert kN·m to N·mm before dividing by Z (which is in mm³). Always multiply by $10^6$ for the moment and divide correctly.
> 2. **Confusing $h$ with $b$:** In the rectangle formula $Z = bh^2/6$, $h$ is the dimension *parallel to the load* (depth in bending plane). Swapping $b$ and $h$ produces errors of order $(h/b)$.
> 3. **Forgetting the subtraction in hollow sections:** For hollow rectangular or circular sections, students sometimes use only the outer dimensions, ignoring the inner cut-out. This produces a *grossly underestimated* Z and *overestimated* stress.
> 4. **Not identifying the correct neutral axis for unsymmetric sections:** This topic assumes *symmetry*, so NA passes through the centroid automatically. If the examiner gives an unsymmetric section by mistake, locate the centroid first using $\bar{y} = \Sigma A_i y_i / \Sigma A_i$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Stresses in Symmetrical Sections**

- **Flexure Formula:** $\sigma = My/I = M/Z$ — memorize in **both** forms.
- **Maximum stress location:** Always at the *extreme fiber* (farthest from NA).
- **For symmetrical sections:** NA passes through the **centroid** automatically (no need to locate it separately).
- **Section Modulus Z** depends only on geometry — not on material or load.
- **Doubling Z** (for same M) **halves** $\sigma_{\max}$.
- **Second Moment of Area** is always a **fourth-power** quantity (mm⁴) — depth direction dominates.
- **Standard formulas to memorize:**
   - Rectangle: $I = bh^3/12$, $Z = bh^2/6$
   - Hollow Rectangle: $I = (BH^3 - bh^3)/12$
   - Circle: $I = \pi d^4/64$, $Z = \pi d^3/32$
   - Hollow Circle: $I = \pi(D^4 - d^4)/64$
   - I-Section: $I = [BH^3 - (B-t_w) h_w^3]/12$
- **Units consistency:** $M$ in N·mm, $I$ in mm⁴, $Z$ in mm³, $\sigma$ in MPa (N/mm²).
- **Always compare** $\sigma_{\max}$ against $\sigma_y$ (yield stress) to conclude whether the section is **safe or unsafe**.
- **Naval Architecture context:** Hull girder section modulus $Z$ is the *primary* strength parameter checked against classification society rules (DNV, ABS, LR).
- **Stress sign convention:** Positive bending moment (sagging) → top fiber in **compression**, bottom fiber in **tension**. Reverse for hogging.
- **Failure mode under excessive bending:** Yielding at the extreme fiber first, then progressive plastification (for ductile steel).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bending Stress Distribution, Shear Stress and Shear Stress Distribution in Beams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST303 - SOLID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Bending Stress Distribution, Shear Stress and Shear Stress Distribution in Beams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Bending Stress — Formal Definition

**Bending stress** (also called **flexural stress** or **normal stress due to bending**) is the normal stress induced in a beam when it is subjected to a transverse loading that produces bending moment. According to the **Euler–Bernoulli beam theory** and the **flexure formula**, the longitudinal (axial) stress at any point in the cross-section varies **linearly** with the distance from the **Neutral Axis (NA)**.

The governing equation is universally written as:

$$\sigma_b = \frac{M \cdot y}{I_{NA}}$$

where **M** is the bending moment at the section, **y** is the perpendicular distance from the Neutral Axis, and **I_{NA}** is the **second moment of area** about the Neutral Axis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCNST303):** "Bending stress distribution refers to the linear variation of longitudinal normal stress across a beam cross-section, reaching maximum tensile stress at the extreme fibre on one side and maximum compressive stress at the extreme fibre on the opposite side, both being equal in magnitude, with **zero stress at the neutral axis**."

## 1.2 Shear Stress — Formal Definition

**Shear stress** in beams arises due to the **transverse shear force (V)** acting at a cross-section. Unlike bending stress which acts normal to the cross-section, shear stress acts **parallel** (tangential) to the cross-section. It is **not uniform** across the cross-section; it follows a **parabolic distribution** for common shapes. The governing expression is the **Zhuravskii ( Jourawski ) shear formula**:

$$\tau = \frac{V \cdot Q}{I \cdot b}$$

where **V** is the transverse shear force, **Q** is the **first moment of area** of the portion of the cross-section lying beyond the point of interest about the Neutral Axis, **I** is the second moment of area of the entire cross-section about the NA, and **b** is the width of the section at the level where shear stress is being computed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** In Naval Architecture, shear stress distribution is critical for designing **ship hull girders**, **deck plating**, **bulkhead stiffeners**, and **transverse frames**, where shear flow governs plate-panel buckling and connection design.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

### Bending Stress — The "Bending Book" Analogy
Imagine holding a thick paperback book horizontally with both hands and pressing your palms inward from the ends (simulating a downward load in the middle). The **top pages** are compressed (pushed together) — they experience **compressive stress**. The **bottom pages** are stretched apart — they experience **tensile stress**. The pages in the **middle** neither stretch nor compress — this is the **Neutral Axis**, where bending stress is **zero**. The stress increases **linearly** as we move away from this middle layer. This is precisely how bending stress is distributed in a beam.

### Shear Stress — The "Bread Slice" Analogy
Consider a loaf of bread. When you cut a slice with a serrated knife, the knife applies a horizontal (transverse) force. This force is resisted by the bread's internal shear — molecules sliding past each other. Similarly, when a beam carries a transverse load, the upper fibres try to slide relative to the lower fibres. The resistance to this sliding is the **shear stress**. It is **maximum at the neutral axis** (where the sliding tendency is greatest between the top half pulling one way and bottom half pulling the other) and **zero at the top and bottom surfaces** (where there are no fibres above to slide relative to).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU:** Bending stress (σ) acts **perpendicular** to the cross-section and varies **linearly** with y. Shear stress (τ) acts **parallel** to the cross-section and varies **parabolically** (typically) with y.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following are the standard units and reference values used throughout KTU board problems:

| Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit | Typical Naval Arch. Range |
|----------|--------|---------|---------------------------|
| Bending Moment | M | **N·m** or **kN·m** | 10³ – 10⁷ N·m (ship hull girder) |
| Section Modulus | Z | **m³** or **mm³** | 10⁻³ – 10⁰ m³ |
| Bending Stress | σ_b | **Pa** = **N/m²** or **MPa** | 50 – 250 MPa (mild steel hull) |
| Shear Force | V | **N** or **kN** | 10³ – 10⁶ N |
| Shear Stress | τ | **Pa** or **MPa** | 20 – 100 MPa |
| First Moment of Area | Q | **m³** or **mm³** | — |
| Breadth of Section | b | **m** or **mm** | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Yield stress for typical shipbuilding steel (ASTM A36):** σ_y ≈ **250 MPa**. **Shear yield:** τ_y ≈ **0.577 × σ_y ≈ 145 MPa**. KTU questions frequently use σ_allow = **165 MPa** and τ_allow = **100 MPa** as design limits.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Assumptions of Simple Bending Theory (KTU Foundation)

The flexure formula $\sigma_b = \frac{My}{I}$ is derived under the following **classical assumptions** — KTU examiners frequently test these:

1. **The beam material is homogeneous and isotropic** (same properties in all directions).
2. **The beam has a plane of symmetry** and loading is applied in this plane.
3. **Plane sections before bending remain plane and normal to the longitudinal axis after bending** (Bernoulli's hypothesis). This means **shear deformation is neglected**.
4. **The beam material obeys Hooke's Law** within the elastic limit — stress is proportional to strain.
5. **The beam is initially straight** and has a uniform cross-section.
6. **Self-weight of the beam is neglected** unless specifically included.
7. **Pure bending** is assumed (constant bending moment, zero shear) for the elementary derivation.
8. **The modulus of elasticity (E) is the same in tension and compression**.
9. **The radius of curvature is large** compared with cross-sectional dimensions (so that plane sections remain essentially plane).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these matter for Naval Architects:** Ship hulls are **NOT** initially straight — they are curved (hogging/sagging). However, local stiffener analysis still uses simple bending theory because the **local radius of curvature is much larger** than the stiffener cross-section dimensions.

## 2.2 Neutral Axis: Location and Significance

The **Neutral Axis (NA)** is the line in the cross-section where bending stress is **zero**. For a symmetrical section, it passes through the **centroid**. For an unsymmetrical section, it is determined by:

$$\bar{y} = \frac{\sum A_i \cdot y_i}{\sum A_i}$$

The distance from the NA to the extreme fibre is **y_max**. The ratio:

$$Z = \frac{I_{NA}}{y_{max}}$$

is called the **Section Modulus** — a measure of the beam's resistance to bending.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Shortcut:** For a symmetric section, NA passes through the centroid, so $Z = I / c$, where **c** = distance from NA to the outermost fibre.

## 2.3 Moment of Resistance (M.R.)

The **Moment of Resistance** is the internal resisting moment developed by the bending stresses. It equals the external applied bending moment at equilibrium. Using the flexure formula:

$$M_R = \frac{\sigma_b \cdot I}{y} = \sigma_b \cdot Z$$

Equilibrium condition: $M_R = M_{external}$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates ALL formulas a KTU student must memorize for Module 3 problems. **Notice:** Pipe symbols are avoided using `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve table syntax.

| # | Formula | Description / Notation | Governing Equation |
|---|---------|------------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Flexure Formula | Normal bending stress | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I_{NA}}$ |
| 2 | Section Modulus | Resistance to bending | $Z = \dfrac{I_{NA}}{y_{max}}$ |
| 3 | Max Bending Stress | At extreme fibre | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ |
| 4 | Moment of Resistance | Internal moment | $M_R = \sigma_{max} \cdot Z$ |
| 5 | Curvature Relation | From beam geometry | $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{M}{EI}$ |
| 6 | Shear Stress Formula | Zhuravskii / Jourawski | $\tau = \dfrac{V \cdot Q}{I \cdot b}$ |
| 7 | First Moment of Area | For area above point | $Q = A' \cdot \bar{y}'$ |
| 8 | Average Shear Stress | Rectangular approximation | $\tau_{avg} = \dfrac{V}{A}$ |
| 9 | Max Shear — Rect. Section | At NA | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{3V}{2A} = 1.5\,\tau_{avg}$ |
| 10 | Max Shear — Circular Section | At NA | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{4V}{3A} \approx 1.333\,\tau_{avg}$ |
| 11 | Max Shear — I-Section | In web at NA | $\tau_{max,web} = \dfrac{V}{t_w \cdot d}$ (approx.) |
| 12 | Shear Flow | Force per unit length | $q = \dfrac{V \cdot Q}{I}$ |
| 13 | Modulus of Elasticity | From Hooke's law | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\epsilon}$ |
| 14 | Bending Equation | Combined form | $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ |
| 15 | Section Modulus — Rectangle | Width b, depth d | $Z = \dfrac{b \cdot d^{2}}{6}$ |
| 16 | Section Modulus — Circle | Diameter D | $Z = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D^{3}}{32}$ |
| 17 | Moment of Inertia — Rectangle | About NA | $I = \dfrac{b \cdot d^{3}}{12}$ |
| 18 | Moment of Inertia — Circle | About centroid | $I = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D^{4}}{64}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Trap:** In handwritten KTU exams, students often write the absolute value as a single pipe, which can confuse the examiner with the "or" symbol. **Always write $\vert y \vert$ or $\mid y \mid$ explicitly** in clean notation.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

### Naval Architecture Applications
- **Hull Girder Bending Stress:** The entire ship hull acts as a **box girder**. Bending stress distribution is calculated for **sagging** (in still water) and **hogging** (in a wave) conditions per **IACS Common Structural Rules (CSR)**. The maximum bending stress at deck or bottom must not exceed **0.7 × σ_y** in extreme conditions.
- **Shear Stress in Web Frames:** In **bulk carriers** and **tankers**, the side shell and transverse web frames experience shear flow. The shear stress distribution helps determine the **scantling (thickness)** of plate panels.
- **Stiffener Design:** T-bar, L-bar, and flat-bar stiffeners are sized using section modulus **Z** to ensure σ_max ≤ σ_allow.
- **Longitudinal Strength:** The ship's longitudinal section modulus $Z_{long} = I_{NA} / y_{max}$ is critical for **Classification Society approval** (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, BV).

### Civil/Mechanical Applications
- Building beams, machine shafts, crane girders, bridges, railway sleepers, aircraft wing spars — all use the same flexure formula.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 DERIVATION 1: Flexure Formula $\sigma_b = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$

### Starting Geometry

Consider a beam segment of length **dx** subjected to a **pure bending moment M**. Originally straight, it deforms into an arc of radius **R**.

**Step 1: Strain in a longitudinal fibre at distance y from NA**

A fibre at the NA has length **dx** (no change). A fibre at distance **y** from NA lies on an arc of radius $(R + y)$, so its length becomes $(R + y) \cdot d\theta$, where $d\theta = dx / R$.

$$\text{Original fibre length} = dx$$
$$\text{Deformed fibre length} = (R + y) \cdot d\theta = (R + y) \cdot \dfrac{dx}{R}$$

**Step 2: Compute strain (change in length ÷ original length)**

$$\epsilon = \dfrac{(R + y) \dfrac{dx}{R} - dx}{dx}$$

$$\epsilon = \dfrac{(R + y) - R}{R} = \dfrac{y}{R}$$

**Step 3: Apply Hooke's Law (σ = E·ε)**

$$\sigma_b = E \cdot \epsilon = \dfrac{E \cdot y}{R}$$

**Step 4: Internal Moment Equilibrium — Sum moments of stresses about NA**

The force on an elemental area $dA$ at distance $y$ is $\sigma_b \cdot dA$. The moment of this force about the NA is $\sigma_b \cdot y \cdot dA$. Integrating across the entire cross-section:

$$M = \int_A \sigma_b \cdot y \cdot dA = \int_A \dfrac{E \cdot y}{R} \cdot y \cdot dA = \dfrac{E}{R} \int_A y^{2} \, dA$$

**Step 5: Recognize $\int_A y^{2}\, dA = I_{NA}$ (Second Moment of Area)**

$$M = \dfrac{E \cdot I}{R} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{M}{E \cdot I}$$

**Step 6: Eliminate E/R between Step 3 and Step 5**

From Step 3: $\sigma_b / y = E/R$. From Step 5: $E/R = M/I$.

Equating:

$$\dfrac{\sigma_b}{y} = \dfrac{M}{I}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_b = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the famous "Three-equal" relationship** that KTU examiners love to test:
> $$\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$$

### Key Deductions from the Derivation

1. **Bending stress varies linearly with y** (since M and I are constants for a given section at a given location).
2. **σ_b = 0 at y = 0** (Neutral Axis).
3. **σ_b is maximum at the extreme fibre** ($y = y_{max}$).
4. **Above NA: σ is compressive (negative convention) or tensile (positive)** depending on the sign of M and y.

---

## 3.2 DERIVATION 2: Shear Stress Formula $\tau = \dfrac{V \cdot Q}{I \cdot b}$

This derivation requires **equilibrium of an elemental beam segment** of length **dx**.

**Step 1: Consider a beam element of length dx**

The cross-section has shear force **V** and bending moment **M** on the left face, and **V + dV** and **M + dM** on the right face (since internal forces change along the beam).

**Step 2: Bending stress on left face at distance y from NA**

$$\sigma_1 = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$$

**Step 3: Bending stress on right face at distance y from NA**

$$\sigma_2 = \dfrac{(M + dM) \cdot y}{I}$$

**Step 4: Consider an isolated sub-element above the level y**

Cut the cross-section horizontally at distance **y** from the NA. Take the **upper portion** as a free body. The horizontal forces on this upper portion are:
- $\sigma_1 \cdot dA$ on the left vertical face (over area above y)
- $\sigma_2 \cdot dA$ on the right vertical face (over area above y)
- $\tau \cdot b \cdot dx$ on the horizontal cut face (acting tangentially, balancing the imbalance)

**Step 5: Apply horizontal equilibrium** (Σ F_x = 0)

$$\int_{y}^{y_{max}} \sigma_2 \, dA - \int_{y}^{y_{max}} \sigma_1 \, dA - \tau \cdot b \cdot dx = 0$$

Substituting $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$:

$$\int_{y}^{y_{max}} \dfrac{(M + dM) \cdot y'}{I} \, dA - \int_{y}^{y_{max}} \dfrac{M \cdot y'}{I} \, dA = \tau \cdot b \cdot dx$$

**Step 6: Factor out constants**

$$\dfrac{dM}{I} \int_{y}^{y_{max}} y' \, dA = \tau \cdot b \cdot dx$$

The integral $\int_{y}^{y_{max}} y' \, dA$ is the **first moment of area of the upper portion** about the NA. This is denoted **Q**:

$$Q = \int_{y}^{y_{max}} y' \, dA = A' \cdot \bar{y}'$$

where $A'$ is the area of the upper portion and $\bar{y}'$ is the distance from NA to the centroid of that upper portion.

**Step 7: Substitute dM = V·dx**

$$\dfrac{V \cdot dx}{I} \cdot Q = \tau \cdot b \cdot dx$$

**Step 8: Cancel dx and solve for τ**

$$\boxed{\tau = \dfrac{V \cdot Q}{I \cdot b}}$$

---

## 3.3 DERIVATION 3: Shear Stress Distribution in a Rectangular Section

For a rectangular cross-section of **width b** and **depth d**, compute $\tau$ at a distance **y** from the NA.

**Step 1: Geometry**

Total area: $A = b \cdot d$. Second moment of area: $I = \dfrac{b \cdot d^{3}}{12}$.

**Step 2: Compute Q at distance y from NA**

The area above level y consists of a rectangle of width **b** and height $(d/2 - y)$. Its centroid is at distance:

$$\bar{y}' = y + \dfrac{(d/2 - y)}{2} = \dfrac{y + d/2}{2} = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{d}{2} + y\right)$$

Area: $A' = b \cdot (d/2 - y)$

Therefore:

$$Q = A' \cdot \bar{y}' = b \cdot \left(\dfrac{d}{2} - y\right) \cdot \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{d}{2} + y\right)$$

$$Q = \dfrac{b}{2}\left[\left(\dfrac{d}{2}\right)^{2} - y^{2}\right] = \dfrac{b}{2}\left(\dfrac{d^{2}}{4} - y^{2}\right)$$

**Step 3: Apply the shear formula**

$$\tau = \dfrac{V \cdot Q}{I \cdot b} = \dfrac{V}{I \cdot b} \cdot \dfrac{b}{2}\left(\dfrac{d^{2}}{4} - y^{2}\right)$$

$$\tau = \dfrac{V}{2I}\left(\dfrac{d^{2}}{4} - y^{2}\right)$$

**Step 4: Substitute $I = b d^{3}/12$**

$$\tau = \dfrac{V}{2 \cdot (b d^{3}/12)}\left(\dfrac{d^{2}}{4} - y^{2}\right) = \dfrac{6 V}{b d^{3}}\left(\dfrac{d^{2}}{4} - y^{2}\right)$$

**Step 5: Find τ_max at y = 0 (Neutral Axis)**

$$\tau_{max} = \dfrac{6V}{b d^{3}} \cdot \dfrac{d^{2}}{4} = \dfrac{6V}{4 b d} = \dfrac{3V}{2bd}$$

Since $A = bd$:

$$\boxed{\tau_{max,rect} = \dfrac{3V}{2A} = 1.5 \cdot \tau_{avg}}$$

**Step 6: Verify τ = 0 at y = d/2 (top fibre)**

$$\tau = \dfrac{6V}{b d^{3}}\left(\dfrac{d^{2}}{4} - \dfrac{d^{2}}{4}\right) = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Parabolic distribution:** The shear stress distribution in a rectangular beam is a **parabola** — zero at top and bottom, maximum at NA, with average value $\tau_{avg} = V/A = V/(bd)$.

---

## 3.4 DERIVATION 4: Shear Stress Distribution in a Circular Section

For a solid circular cross-section of diameter **D** (radius **R_c**):

**Step 1: Geometry**

$I = \dfrac{\pi D^{4}}{64}$

**Step 2: Compute Q at distance y from centre**

A chord cuts the circle at height y. Above this chord is a circular segment. Using the standard formula for first moment of area of a circular segment:

$$Q = \dfrac{2}{3}(R_{c}^{2} - y^{2})^{3/2}$$

where $R_c = D/2$.

**Step 3: Apply shear formula**

At the point on the chord, the width is $b = 2\sqrt{R_{c}^{2} - y^{2}}$.

$$\tau = \dfrac{V \cdot Q}{I \cdot b} = \dfrac{V \cdot \dfrac{2}{3}(R_{c}^{2} - y^{2})^{3/2}}{\dfrac{\pi D^{4}}{64} \cdot 2\sqrt{R_{c}^{2} - y^{2}}}$$

**Step 4: Simplify**

$$\tau = \dfrac{V \cdot \dfrac{2}{3}(R_{c}^{2} - y^{2})}{2 \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^{4}}{64}} = \dfrac{64 V (R_{c}^{2} - y^{2})}{3\pi D^{4}}$$

Since $D = 2R_c$, $D^{4} = 16 R_c^{4}$:

$$\tau = \dfrac{64 V (R_{c}^{2} - y^{2})}{3\pi \cdot 16 R_c^{4}} = \dfrac{4V(R_{c}^{2} - y^{2})}{3\pi R_c^{4}}$$

**Step 5: Maximum at y = 0**

$$\boxed{\tau_{max,circ} = \dfrac{4V}{3\pi R_c^{2}} = \dfrac{4V}{3A}}$$

since $A = \pi R_c^{2}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparison ratios (frequently tested in KTU):**
> - Rectangular section: $\tau_{max}/\tau_{avg} = 3/2 = 1.5$
> - Circular section: $\tau_{max}/\tau_{avg} = 4/3 \approx 1.333$
> - Thin-walled tube: $\tau_{max}/\tau_{avg} = 2.0$
> - I-section (thin web): $\tau_{max}/\tau_{avg}$ can be **>> 2** depending on web thickness

---

## 3.5 DERIVATION 5: Shear Stress Distribution in an I-Section

This is the most important derivation for **Naval Architecture** since ship hull girders are essentially I-sections with plate panels.

Consider an **I-section** with:
- Flange width: **B**, flange thickness: **t_f**
- Web height (between flanges): **h**, web thickness: **t_w**
- Total depth: **D = h + 2t_f**

**Step 1: Compute I_NA about the centroidal axis**

$$I = \dfrac{B \cdot D^{3}}{12} - \dfrac{(B - t_{w}) \cdot h^{3}}{12}$$

**Step 2: Region 1 — Within the Flange (above NA)**

Consider a point at distance y from NA, inside the top flange ($0 \le y \le t_f$ from the top, or equivalently $h/2 \le y \le D/2$ from NA). The area above this point is a rectangle of width **B** and height $(D/2 - y)$. Its centroid is at distance $(y + (D/2 - y)/2)/1 = (y + D/2)/2$ — wait, let me redo this carefully.

For a point at distance **y** above NA in the top flange:
- Area above = $B \times (D/2 - y)$
- Centroid of this area from NA = $y + (D/2 - y)/2 = (D/2 + y)/2$

$$Q_{flange} = B \cdot \left(\dfrac{D}{2} - y\right) \cdot \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{D}{2} + y\right) = \dfrac{B}{2}\left[\left(\dfrac{D}{2}\right)^{2} - y^{2}\right]$$

Width at this point: $b = B$ (within flange)

$$\tau_{flange} = \dfrac{V \cdot Q_{flange}}{I \cdot B} = \dfrac{V}{2I}\left[\left(\dfrac{D}{2}\right)^{2} - y^{2}\right]$$

**Step 3: Region 2 — Within the Web**

For a point at distance **y** above NA inside the web ($y \le h/2$):

The area above this point consists of the **entire top flange** plus the portion of the web from $y$ to $h/2$.

$$Q_{web} = Q_{flange\,total} + Q_{web\,partial}$$

$Q_{flange\,total}$ (entire top flange): Area = $B \cdot t_f$, centroid from NA = $h/2 + t_f/2$.

$$Q_{flange\,total} = B \cdot t_f \cdot \left(\dfrac{h}{2} + \dfrac{t_f}{2}\right)$$

$Q_{web\,partial}$: Area = $t_w \cdot (h/2 - y)$, centroid from NA = $(y + h/2)/2$.

$$Q_{web\,partial} = t_w \cdot \left(\dfrac{h}{2} - y\right) \cdot \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{h}{2} + y\right) = \dfrac{t_w}{2}\left[\left(\dfrac{h}{2}\right)^{2} - y^{2}\right]$$

Total: $Q_{web} = B \cdot t_f \cdot \left(\dfrac{h + t_f}{2}\right) + \dfrac{t_w}{2}\left[\left(\dfrac{h}{2}\right)^{2} - y^{2}\right]$

Width at this point: $b = t_w$

$$\tau_{web} = \dfrac{V \cdot Q_{web}}{I \cdot t_w}$$

**Step 4: Maximum Shear Stress — At NA (y = 0) within the web**

At NA, the area above is the entire top half:
- Top flange: area $B \cdot t_f$, centroid at $(h + t_f)/2$ above NA
- Top half of web: area $t_w \cdot h/2$, centroid at $h/4$ above NA

$$Q_{max} = B \cdot t_f \cdot \dfrac{(h + t_f)}{2} + t_w \cdot \dfrac{h}{2} \cdot \dfrac{h}{4} = \dfrac{B \cdot t_f (h + t_f)}{2} + \dfrac{t_w \cdot h^{2}}{8}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{max,I-section} = \dfrac{V \cdot Q_{max}}{I \cdot t_w}}$$

**Step 5: Shear Stress Jump at Flange-Web Junction**

There is a **discontinuity** in shear stress at the junction (y = h/2) because the width changes abruptly from B to t_w. Specifically:

- Just above the junction (in flange): $\tau = \dfrac{V \cdot Q_{flange,total}}{I \cdot B}$
- Just below the junction (in web): $\tau = \dfrac{V \cdot Q_{flange,total}}{I \cdot t_w}$

Ratio of jump: $\dfrac{\tau_{web}}{\tau_{flange}} = \dfrac{B}{t_w}$ — typically very large (e.g., 10–20×)!

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Observation:** For standard I-beams where $B/t_w \approx 20$, the shear stress in the flange is only ~5% of the web stress. Designers often **neglect shear stress in flanges** and assume **100% of V is carried by the web**.

---

## 3.6 DERIVATION 6: Shear Stress Distribution in a T-Section (Asymmetric)

T-sections are common in **ship stiffeners** (e.g., T-bulb sections, angle bars).

For a T-section with flange at top, dimensions: flange $B \times t_f$, web $t_w \times h$ (below flange):

**Step 1: Locate NA**

By symmetry of T-section, NA is horizontal but offset vertically from the geometric centre. Let $\bar{y}$ = distance from top of flange to NA.

$$\bar{y} = \dfrac{B \cdot t_f \cdot (t_f/2) + t_w \cdot h \cdot (t_f + h/2)}{B \cdot t_f + t_w \cdot h}$$

**Step 2: Compute I_NA**

$$I_{NA} = \left[\dfrac{B t_f^{3}}{12} + B t_f (\bar{y} - t_f/2)^{2}\right] + \left[\dfrac{t_w h^{3}}{12} + t_w h (t_f + h/2 - \bar{y})^{2}\right]$$

**Step 3: Compute Q at NA**

Area above NA = top flange = $B \cdot t_f$. Centroid at $\bar{y} - t_f/2$ above NA.

$$Q_{NA} = B \cdot t_f \cdot (\bar{y} - t_f/2)$$

$$\tau_{max} = \dfrac{V \cdot B \cdot t_f \cdot (\bar{y} - t_f/2)}{I_{NA} \cdot t_w}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Bending Stress in T-section:** Maximum tensile stress occurs at the bottom of the web (farthest from NA), while maximum compressive stress occurs at the top of the flange. These are NOT equal in magnitude! This asymmetry is critical for **welded T-stiffeners** where fatigue cracks initiate at the heel.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: Bending Stress Distribution Across a Rectangular Beam

This Mermaid block represents a **block-level functional architecture flow** of how bending stress is generated and distributed through the cross-section. (Adapting physical rendering to Mermaid's safe syntax.)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Load P applied to beam] --> B[Internal Bending Moment M develops at section]
    B --> C[Fibres above NA experience Compression]
    B --> D[Fibres below NA experience Tension]
    B --> E[Fibres at NA experience Zero Stress]
    C --> F[Stress magnitude increases linearly with distance y from NA]
    D --> F
    E --> G[Neutral Axis located at centroid for symmetric section]
    F --> H[sigma equals M times y over I flexure formula]
    G --> I[Section modulus Z equals I over y max]
    H --> J[Maximum stress at extreme fibre sigma max equals M over Z]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Check against allowable stress sigma allow]
    K --> L{Design Safe?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Section Acceptable]
    L -- No --> N[Increase Section Modulus Z]
```

**Interpretation:**
- The flowchart shows the logical progression from external load → bending moment → stress distribution → design check.
- The **linear** stress variation is captured in node F.
- The **centroid-NA coincidence** is captured in node G.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Shear Stress Distribution Comparison Across Sections

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Beam Cross Section] --> B[Rectangular Section]
    A --> C[Circular Section]
    A --> D[I Section]
    A --> E[T Section]
    B --> F[Parabolic distribution tau equals 1.5 V over A at NA]
    C --> G[Parabolic distribution tau equals 1.333 V over A at NA]
    D --> H[Small in flanges JUMP at flange web junction]
    D --> I[Large in web tau max at NA]
    E --> J[Asymmetric distribution due to offset NA]
    F --> K[Common ratio for quick design]
    G --> K
    H --> L[Critical for ship hull girders]
    I --> L
    J --> M[Common for welded stiffeners]
```

**Interpretation:**
- Shows that different cross-sections produce different shear stress magnitudes and shapes.
- Highlights **Naval Architecture relevance** (nodes L and M).

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Bending + Shear Combined State at a Beam Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Beam Element of length dx] --> B[Left Face M and V]
    A --> C[Right Face M plus dM and V plus dV]
    B --> D[Bending stress sigma one at distance y]
    C --> E[Bending stress sigma two at distance y]
    D --> F[Net imbalance of horizontal forces]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Equilibrium requires shear stress tau on horizontal plane]
    G --> H[tau equals V times Q over I times b]
    H --> I[Q equals first moment of area beyond point y]
    I --> J[Distribution parabolic for most shapes]
    J --> K[Zero at top and bottom fibres]
    J --> L[Maximum at Neutral Axis]
```

**Interpretation:**
- Illustrates the **derivation logic** of the Zhuravskii formula.
- Connects the differential bending stresses (left vs right) to the necessity of horizontal shear stress.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4: I-Section Shear Stress Profile (Side-by-Side View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[I Section Vertical Cross Section] --> B[Top Flange of width B and thickness t_f]
    A --> C[Web of height h and thickness t_w]
    A --> D[Bottom Flange identical to Top Flange]
    B --> E[Shear stress in flange SMALL tau approx V Q over I B]
    C --> F[Shear stress in web LARGE tau equals V Q over I t_w]
    D --> G[Shear stress in bottom flange SMALL by symmetry]
    E --> H[tau peaks near flange web junction slightly less than NA value]
    F --> I[tau peaks at NA equals V Q max over I t_w]
    G --> J[Mirror image of top flange]
    H --> K[JUMP DISCONTINUITY at junction by factor B over t_w]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Treat web as primary shear carrier in design]
```

**Interpretation:**
- The **discontinuity (jump)** at the flange-web junction is a critical concept.
- KTU examiners frequently ask why $\tau$ jumps: it's because the **width b** in the formula changes abruptly.

---

## 4.5 Diagram 5: T-Section Stress Asymmetry in Naval Stiffener

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[T Stiffener welded to ship deck plate] --> B[NA located offset from geometric centre]
    B --> C[Top flange in compression region]
    B --> D[Web stem extends below NA]
    C --> E[Smaller distance y top to NA]
    D --> F[Larger distance y bottom to NA]
    E --> G[Smaller compressive stress at top of flange]
    F --> H[Larger tensile stress at bottom of web]
    G --> I[Asymmetric stress distribution]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Fatigue critical at weld toe due to stress concentration]
```

**Interpretation:**
- Explains the **fatigue concern** in welded T-stiffeners used in ship hulls.
- Shows why $\sigma_{max,tensile} \neq \sigma_{max,compressive}$ for asymmetric sections.

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**State the assumptions made in the derivation of the simple bending theory (flexure formula).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The flexure formula $\sigma_b = My/I$ is derived under the following assumptions:

1. **The beam material is homogeneous and isotropic**, meaning it has uniform and identical mechanical properties in all directions at every point.

2. **The beam has a cross-section that is uniform** along its length and possesses at least one plane of symmetry, with all loads applied in this plane.

3. **Plane sections before bending remain plane and perpendicular to the longitudinal axis** after bending (Euler-Bernoulli hypothesis). This neglects shear deformation.

4. **The material obeys Hooke's Law** within the elastic limit, so that stress is directly proportional to strain.

5. **The beam is initially straight** and **self-weight is neglected** unless explicitly included.

6. **The radius of curvature is large** compared to the cross-sectional dimensions.

7. **Pure bending** is assumed for the elementary derivation (constant M, V = 0).

*(Allocation: 1 mark for stating any 3 assumptions; 1 mark for stating 3 more; 1 mark for clearly mentioning Euler-Bernoulli plane-sections-remain-plane hypothesis.)*

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define "Section Modulus" and write its formula. Why is it important in beam design?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition:** The Section Modulus (Z) is the ratio of the second moment of area (I) of a cross-section about its neutral axis to the distance (y_max) from the neutral axis to the extreme fibre.

$$Z = \dfrac{I_{NA}}{y_{max}}$$

**Units:** mm³ (in SI) or m³ for large sections.

**Importance in beam design:**

1. The maximum bending stress in a beam under moment M is directly given by $\sigma_{max} = M/Z$. A larger Z means lower stress.

2. For quick **section selection** during preliminary design, designers compare the required Z (= M_allowable / σ_allowable) with the Z of standard rolled sections from a steel table.

3. It combines geometric properties (I and y_max) into a single parameter that characterizes the beam's bending resistance.

4. It is essential in **Naval Architecture** for computing the hull girder section modulus, a key approval parameter for **Classification Societies (DNV, LR, ABS)**.

*(Allocation: 1 mark for definition + formula; 1 mark for formula explanation; 1 mark for design importance.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — 14 Marks

**A simply supported steel beam of span 4 m carries a uniformly distributed load (UDL) of 20 kN/m over its entire length. The beam cross-section is rectangular with width b = 120 mm and depth d = 240 mm. Determine:**

**(a)** [7 marks] The maximum bending stress and the bending stress distribution across the depth at the section of maximum bending moment.

**(b)** [7 marks] The maximum shear stress and draw the shear stress distribution diagram across the depth. Take E = 200 GPa.

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — Maximum Bending Stress and Distribution

**Step 1: Compute maximum bending moment**

For a simply supported beam with UDL:

$$M_{max} = \dfrac{wL^{2}}{8} = \dfrac{20 \times 4^{2}}{8} = \dfrac{20 \times 16}{8} = 40 \text{ kN·m} = 40 \times 10^{6} \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 2: Compute second moment of area**

$$I = \dfrac{b \cdot d^{3}}{12} = \dfrac{120 \times 240^{3}}{12} = \dfrac{120 \times 1.3824 \times 10^{7}}{12}$$

$$I = 120 \times 1.3824 \times 10^{7} / 12 = 1.3824 \times 10^{8} \text{ mm}^{4}$$

**Step 3: Compute section modulus**

$$Z = \dfrac{I}{d/2} = \dfrac{1.3824 \times 10^{8}}{120} = 1.152 \times 10^{6} \text{ mm}^{3}$$

**Step 4: Maximum bending stress**

$$\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M_{max}}{Z} = \dfrac{40 \times 10^{6}}{1.152 \times 10^{6}} = 34.72 \text{ N/mm}^{2} = 34.72 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5: Stress distribution (linear)**

At a distance y from NA:

$$\sigma(y) = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I} = \dfrac{40 \times 10^{6} \cdot y}{1.3824 \times 10^{8}} = 0.2893 \cdot y \text{ MPa}$$

At y = +120 mm (top): $\sigma_{top} = 34.72$ MPa (compressive, if sagging)
At y = −120 mm (bottom): $\sigma_{bottom} = -34.72$ MPa (tensile)
At y = 0 (NA): $\sigma_{NA} = 0$ MPa

**Bending stress distribution table:**

| y (mm) | σ (MPa) |
|--------|---------|
| +120 | +34.72 (compression) |
| +60 | +17.36 |
| 0 | 0 (Neutral Axis) |
| −60 | −17.36 (tension) |
| −120 | −34.72 |

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — Maximum Shear Stress and Distribution

**Step 1: Compute maximum shear force**

$$V_{max} = \dfrac{wL}{2} = \dfrac{20 \times 4}{2} = 40 \text{ kN} = 40{,}000 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2: Maximum shear stress for rectangular section**

$$\tau_{max} = \dfrac{3V}{2A} = \dfrac{3 \times 40{,}000}{2 \times (120 \times 240)} = \dfrac{120{,}000}{57{,}600}$$

$$\tau_{max} = 2.083 \text{ N/mm}^{2} = 2.083 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: General shear stress at distance y from NA**

$$\tau(y) = \dfrac{V}{2I}\left[\left(\dfrac{d}{2}\right)^{2} - y^{2}\right] = \dfrac{6V}{bd^{3}}\left[\left(\dfrac{d}{2}\right)^{2} - y^{2}\right]$$

$$\tau(y) = \dfrac{6 \times 40{,}000}{120 \times 240^{3}}\left[120^{2} - y^{2}\right]$$

$$\tau(y) = \dfrac{240{,}000}{1.658 \times 10^{9}}\left[14{,}400 - y^{2}\right]$$

$$\tau(y) = 1.448 \times 10^{-4}\left[14{,}400 - y^{2}\right] \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4: Shear stress distribution values**

| y (mm) | τ (MPa) |
|--------|---------|
| ±120 (top/bottom) | 0 |
| ±90 | 1.13 |
| ±60 | 1.82 |
| ±30 | 2.04 |
| 0 (NA) | 2.083 (max) |

**Distribution Shape:** Parabola opening downwards, peaking at NA.

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam - May 2024] — 14 Marks

**A cast iron beam of circular cross-section (diameter D = 150 mm) is simply supported over a span of 3 m and carries a concentrated load of 30 kN at the mid-span. Calculate:**

**(a)** [7 marks] The maximum tensile and compressive bending stresses in the beam, and verify if the beam is safe. Take allowable tensile stress = 30 MPa and allowable compressive stress = 60 MPa.

**(b)** [7 marks] The maximum shear stress in the beam and compare it with the average shear stress. Also, compute the shear stress at a depth of 30 mm from the neutral axis.

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — Maximum Tensile and Compressive Bending Stresses

**Step 1: Reactions and maximum bending moment**

For a simply supported beam with central point load:

$$R_A = R_B = \dfrac{P}{2} = \dfrac{30}{2} = 15 \text{ kN}$$

Maximum moment at mid-span:

$$M_{max} = \dfrac{P \cdot L}{4} = \dfrac{30 \times 3}{4} = 22.5 \text{ kN·m} = 22.5 \times 10^{6} \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 2: Section properties of circular section**

$$I = \dfrac{\pi D^{4}}{64} = \dfrac{\pi \times 150^{4}}{64} = \dfrac{\pi \times 5.0625 \times 10^{8}}{64}$$

$$I = 2.485 \times 10^{7} \text{ mm}^{4}$$

$$Z = \dfrac{I}{D/2} = \dfrac{2.485 \times 10^{7}}{75} = 3.313 \times 10^{5} \text{ mm}^{3}$$

**Step 3: Maximum bending stress (same magnitude for circular section)**

$$\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M_{max}}{Z} = \dfrac{22.5 \times 10^{6}}{3.313 \times 10^{5}} = 67.92 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4: Identify tension and compression zones**

For a simply supported beam with downward load (sagging):
- **Top fibres:** Compression → $\sigma_c = 67.92$ MPa (compressive)
- **Bottom fibres:** Tension → $\sigma_t = 67.92$ MPa (tensile)

**Step 5: Safety check**

- Tensile check: $\sigma_t = 67.92$ MPa vs. $\sigma_{allow,t} = 30$ MPa → **67.92 > 30** ❌ **NOT SAFE in tension**
- Compressive check: $\sigma_c = 67.92$ MPa vs. $\sigma_{allow,c} = 60$ MPa → **67.92 > 60** ❌ **NOT SAFE in compression**

**Conclusion:** The beam is **unsafe**. To make it safe, increase diameter D or use a different material.

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — Maximum Shear Stress and Comparison

**Step 1: Maximum shear force**

For a simply supported beam with central point load:

$$V_{max} = \dfrac{P}{2} = \dfrac{30}{2} = 15 \text{ kN} = 15{,}000 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2: Area of circular section**

$$A = \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times 150^{2}}{4} = 17{,}671.5 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

**Step 3: Average shear stress**

$$\tau_{avg} = \dfrac{V}{A} = \dfrac{15{,}000}{17{,}671.5} = 0.849 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4: Maximum shear stress at NA (using formula for circular section)**

$$\tau_{max} = \dfrac{4V}{3A} = \dfrac{4 \times 15{,}000}{3 \times 17{,}671.5} = \dfrac{60{,}000}{53{,}014.5}$$

$$\tau_{max} = 1.132 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5: Comparison ratio**

$$\dfrac{\tau_{max}}{\tau_{avg}} = \dfrac{4/3}{1} = 1.333$$

This matches the theoretical factor of **4/3** for circular sections.

**Step 6: Shear stress at y = 30 mm from NA**

For circular section:

$$\tau(y) = \dfrac{4V(R^{2} - y^{2})}{3\pi R^{4}}$$

where $R = D/2 = 75$ mm.

$$\tau(30) = \dfrac{4 \times 15{,}000 \times (75^{2} - 30^{2})}{3\pi \times 75^{4}}$$

$$= \dfrac{60{,}000 \times (5625 - 900)}{3\pi \times 3.164 \times 10^{7}}$$

$$= \dfrac{60{,}000 \times 4725}{2.982 \times 10^{8}}$$

$$= \dfrac{2.835 \times 10^{8}}{2.982 \times 10^{8}} = 0.951 \text{ MPa}$$

**Verification:** Since 30 mm < 75 mm, $\tau(30) < \tau_{max}(1.132)$ ✓ and $\tau(30) > \tau_{avg}(0.849)$ ✓

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Loss Traps for Module 3 (Bending & Shear Stress):**
>
> 1. **Unit Mismatch Trap (1-2 marks lost):** Bending moment must be in **N·mm** when using mm-based cross-sections. Students forget to convert kN·m → N·mm (multiply by 10⁶). Result: stress off by factor of 10⁶.
>
> 2. **Confusing I (Second Moment) with Z (Section Modulus):** $\sigma = M \cdot y / I$ is the *general* formula. $\sigma_{max} = M / Z$ is the *shortcut* that only works when **y = y_max**. Using $M/Z$ with non-extreme-fibre points is **WRONG** and costs full marks.
>
> 3. **Shear Stress at Wrong Location:** $\tau_{max}$ for rectangular is at **NA, not at the top/bottom**. For I-section, the formula $\tau = VQ/(Ib)$ uses **b = width at the point of interest**. Using $b = B$ (flange width) at the web level gives an incorrect (low) value.
>
> 4. **Sign Convention Errors:** Bending stress changes sign across NA. Top fibre (in sagging) is **compressive (−)**, bottom is **tensile (+)**. KTU examiners expect students to clearly indicate tension/compression in diagrams.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the 3V/2A Factor for Rectangular Beams:** A very common error is to write $\tau_{max} = V/A$ for a rectangular beam. This gives only the **average** stress, which is **50% lower** than the actual maximum. **Always use $\tau_{max} = 3V/(2A)$ for rectangular sections.**
>
> 6. **First Moment of Area (Q) Calculation Mistake:** Students confuse Q with I. Q involves the **area above (or below) the point of interest** times the distance from its centroid to the **NA**, NOT the second moment. For a point at the top of the section, Q = 0 → τ = 0 ✓.
>
> 7. **I-Section Asymmetric Web/Flange:** When solving for I-section shear stress, students often forget to compute **Q_max** as the first moment of the **entire top half** (flange + half-web), not just the flange.
>
> 8. **Allowable Stress Confusion:** Cast iron and concrete have **different allowable stresses in tension vs. compression**. Forgetting to check both separately loses 1-2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — MODULE 3**

### Bending Stress Essentials
- **Flexure formula:** $\sigma_b = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I_{NA}}$
- **Maximum bending stress** occurs at the **extreme fibre**: $\sigma_{max} = M / Z$
- **Zero stress** at the **Neutral Axis**
- **Linear variation** with y (NOT parabolic)
- For symmetric section, NA = centroid
- Section Modulus **Z = I / y_max** is the design parameter
- Combined relation: $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$
- Curvature: $1/R = M/(EI)$

### Shear Stress Essentials
- **Zhuravskii formula:** $\tau = \dfrac{V \cdot Q}{I \cdot b}$
- **Q** = first moment of area of the part beyond the point of interest
- **Zero** at top and bottom surfaces
- **Maximum at Neutral Axis** for standard sections
- **Parabolic** distribution for rectangular, circular, I-flange
- **Shape factors** (τ_max/τ_avg):
  - Rectangular: **3/2 = 1.5**
  - Circular: **4/3 ≈ 1.333**
  - Thin tube: **2.0**
  - I-section (web only): highly variable

### Section Properties Quick Reference
- Rectangle: $I = bd^{3}/12$, $Z = bd^{2}/6$
- Circle: $I = \pi D^{4}/64$, $Z = \pi D^{3}/32$
- I-Section: Use parallel-axis theorem for flange + web
- T-Section: NA offset from geometric centre

### Naval Architecture Highlights
- Hull girder bending: **sagging** (still water) and **hogging** (wave) conditions
- IACS Common Structural Rules (CSR) govern ship hull design
- Shear flow critical for **plate panel** design in stiffened panels
- T-bulb and angle-bar stiffeners use asymmetric stress analysis

### Key Sign Conventions
- Sagging moment M (+): **Top compression, Bottom tension**
- Hogging moment M (−): **Top tension, Bottom compression**
- Downward shear V (+): induces **clockwise** element rotation

### Master Formula List (Must Memorize)
1. $\sigma_b = My/I$
2. $\sigma_{max} = M/Z$
3. $\tau = VQ/(Ib)$
4. $\tau_{max,rect} = 3V/(2A)$
5. $\tau_{max,circ} = 4V/(3A)$
6. $M/EI = 1/R$

### Common Pitfalls to Avoid (Re-emphasis)
- ❌ Using $V/A$ instead of $3V/(2A)$ for rectangular beams
- ❌ Confusing I and Z
- ❌ Forgetting unit conversion (kN·m to N·mm)
- ❌ Mixing tension/compression zones
- ❌ Using $b = $ full width for I-section web calculations
- ❌ Ignoring shear stress contribution in flanges of I-section

---

> **END OF MODULE 3 NOTES — PCNST303 SOLID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS**

topics.title: Assumptions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST303 - SOLID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Assumptions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Solid Mechanics for Naval Architects**, the term **"Assumptions"** refers to the set of idealized, axiomatic statements that form the foundational basis upon which the classical theory of **bending of beams** (covered in Module 3) is mathematically derived and validated. These assumptions simplify the complex physical behavior of real engineering materials and geometries into a tractable analytical framework that satisfies the **Euler-Bernoulli Beam Theory** and the principles of **strength of materials**.

The principal assumptions are collectively referred to in KTU syllabus terminology as the **"Fundamental Assumptions of the Theory of Bending."** Together, they allow us to write the governing bending equation, calculate **Bending Stresses (Flexural Stresses)**, and apply the results to ship structural components such as **hull girders, deck plating, stiffeners, and transverse frames**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):**
> Module 3 of PCNST303 specifically requires students to "state and apply the fundamental assumptions used in the derivation of bending stresses in beams." A direct 2-mark or 3-mark question is frequently asked, and these assumptions must be memorized verbatim for both Part A and Part B examinations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **thin wooden ruler (or a steel scale)** held firmly between your two hands and gently bent into a shallow arc. What do you physically observe?

- The **top surface** of the ruler gets **shorter** (compressed).
- The **bottom surface** of the ruler gets **longer** (stretched).
- Somewhere in between, there is a layer that **does not change length at all** — this is the **Neutral Axis**.
- The ruler, which was originally straight, now assumes a smooth, continuous curve.
- A line drawn vertically on the side of the ruler before bending still remains **straight and perpendicular** to the curved axis after bending.

Now, in the actual mathematics of solid mechanics, we *assume* that all of these observable behaviors occur in a **perfectly predictable, ideal manner** for *every* material and *every* geometry we analyze. We assume the material has no hidden flaws, no internal voids, behaves identically in every direction, and stretches/compresses proportionally to the applied force.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy for Naval Architects:**
> Think of a **ship's hull girder** floating in still water. The hull bends (sags) due to its own weight and buoyancy distribution. The top deck goes into **compression** and the bottom hull (keel area) goes into **tension**. All the design rules in shipbuilding (Bureau of Indian Standards, Lloyd's Register, DNV) ultimately rely on the basic beam-bending assumptions you are about to study. Without these assumptions, calculating the **section modulus** of a ship's midship section would be mathematically impossible.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

The following standard values are universally accepted in the bending theory of ship structures and are highlighted as per KTU 2024 Scheme requirements:

- **Young's Modulus of Structural Steel (Shipbuilding Grade):** **E = 200 GPa (2 × 10⁵ N/mm²)**
- **Poisson's Ratio for Steel:** **ν = 0.30**
- **Yield Stress of Mild Steel (Grade A):** **σ_y = 235 MPa**
- **Density of Seawater:** **ρ_sw = 1025 kg/m³**
- **Acceleration due to Gravity:** **g = 9.81 m/s²**

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Enumerated List of Fundamental Assumptions

Below is the **complete, KTU Board-approved enumeration** of the ten fundamental assumptions used in the derivation of pure bending theory. Each assumption is essential and dropping any one of them fundamentally alters the resulting equations.

### Assumption 1: Homogeneity
The material of the beam is **homogeneous**, meaning it has **uniform composition and identical mechanical properties at every point** within its volume. There are no localized variations in stiffness, density, or strength.

- **Why it matters:** It allows us to treat material properties (E, ρ) as **constants** rather than as spatial functions.
- **Naval relevance:** Ship hull steel plates are assumed homogeneous for global strength calculations, although in reality, weld zones and corroded areas are not.

### Assumption 2: Isotropy
The material is **isotropic**, meaning its elastic properties are **identical in all directions** at any given point.

- **Why it matters:** This allows the use of a **single Young's Modulus (E)** regardless of the orientation of the fiber.
- **Naval relevance:** Mild steel is approximately isotropic; however, **fiber-reinforced composites** used in modern naval architecture are anisotropic and require **orthotropic plate theory** instead.

### Assumption 3: Continuity
The material is **continuous** — it has no voids, cracks, or internal discontinuities. The molecular structure is treated as a perfectly uninterrupted continuum.

- **Why it matters:** It allows the use of **infinitesimal calculus** (differentiation, integration) over the cross-section.
- **Naval relevance:** Fatigue cracks and corrosion pits violate this assumption locally but are ignored in first-principle strength calculations.

### Assumption 4: Initial Straightness
The beam is **initially perfectly straight** (unstressed geometry) and possesses a **uniform cross-section** along its entire length.

- **Why it matters:** All bending strains are measured relative to a known reference (straight) configuration.
- **Naval relevance:** A ship's hull girder has a slight curvature (hog or sag) in the as-built condition, but this is treated separately as initial deformation, not as part of pure bending stress.

### Assumption 5: Perfect Elasticity (Hooke's Law)
The material is **perfectly elastic** and **obeys Hooke's Law** within the working stress range. This means stress is **directly proportional to strain** ($\sigma = E\varepsilon$), and the material returns to its original shape upon unloading.

- **Why it matters:** Establishes the **linear stress-strain relationship** required for the flexure formula.
- **Naval relevance:** Ship structures are designed to remain well within the elastic limit under normal seagoing loads.

### Assumption 6: Equal Modulus in Tension and Compression
The value of **Young's Modulus (E) is the same in tension and in compression**. The stress-strain curve is symmetric about the origin.

- **Why it matters:** This makes the **neutral axis coincide with the centroidal axis**, which is critical to the classical formula $\sigma = \dfrac{My}{I}$.
- **Naval relevance:** True for steel, but **cast iron** has different E values in tension and compression — that is why cast iron beams have a *shifted* neutral axis.

### Assumption 7: Plane Sections Remain Plane and Perpendicular (Euler-Bernoulli Hypothesis)
This is the **most important assumption** of all. A cross-section that is plane and perpendicular to the axis of the beam **before bending remains plane and perpendicular to the deformed axis after bending**. In other words, cross-sections do not warp or distort.

- **Why it matters:** This implies that **longitudinal fibers experience only axial strain** (no shear strain), and the strain varies **linearly** with distance from the neutral axis.
- **Naval relevance:** For slender hull girders with **L/d > 10**, this assumption is highly accurate. For **deep beams** or near supports, **Timoshenko Beam Theory** must be used.

### Assumption 8: Negligible Self-Weight
The **self-weight of the beam is neglected** in pure bending analysis. Only the externally applied bending moment is considered.

- **Why it matters:** Isolates pure bending from combined loading (bending + axial + shear).
- **Naval relevance:** The lightship weight of a vessel acts as a distributed load causing bending; this is added as an external load, not as a self-weight effect on the beam element.

### Assumption 9: Small Deformations
The **deflection of the beam is very small** compared to its cross-sectional dimensions. Rotations of cross-sections are infinitesimal.

- **Why it matters:** Allows the use of the approximate curvature formula $\dfrac{1}{R} \approx \dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2}$ instead of the exact expression $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{y''}{(1+y'^2)^{3/2}}$.
- **Naval relevance:** A ship's hull typically deflects only a few centimeters over a 100-meter length, so this is well satisfied.

### Assumption 10: Pure Bending Condition
The beam is subjected to **pure bending only**, meaning the **shear force is zero** and the **bending moment is constant** along the length being analyzed. Transverse shear deformation is neglected.

- **Why it matters:** Under pure bending, the cross-section is subjected to **normal stress only** (no shear stress), simplifying the 2D stress state to a 1D problem.
- **Naval relevance:** At the **midship section** of a ship (in still water bending), the shear force is approximately zero, making it the critical section for pure bending.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:**
> The Euler-Bernoulli assumption (Assumption 7) and the small deformation assumption (Assumption 9) together allow us to conclude that **plane sections rotate about the neutral axis** as rigid bodies — this is the geometric heart of bending theory.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table contains the **complete high-yield formula set** derived from the above assumptions. Memorize these for the KTU 2024 ESE.

| **Formula** | **Name / Meaning** | **Variables** | **Units (SI)** | **Validity Condition** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ | Hooke's Law | $\sigma$ = stress, $E$ = Young's Modulus, $\varepsilon$ = strain | N/m², Pa | Within elastic limit |
| $\varepsilon = \dfrac{y}{R}$ | Strain at distance y | $y$ = distance from N.A., $R$ = radius of curvature | m | Pure bending |
| $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{M}{EI}$ | Moment-Curvature Relation | $M$ = Bending moment, $I$ = Second moment of area | m⁻¹, N·m, m⁴ | Small deflections |
| $\dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ | Master Bending Equation | $\sigma$ = Bending stress | Pa, N/m² | Pure bending, elastic |
| $\sigma = \dfrac{My}{I}$ | Flexure Formula (Bending Stress) | $y$ = distance from N.A. | N/m² | All beams |
| $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y}$ | Alternative form | — | — | KTU 2024 preferred form |
| $I = \int_A y^2 dA$ | Second Moment of Area (MOI) | $A$ = cross-sectional area | m⁴ | Defined for any shape |
| $Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{max}}$ | Section Modulus | $y_{max}$ = max distance to extreme fiber | m³ | Symmetric & unsymmetric bending |
| $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ | Maximum Bending Stress | — | N/m² = Pa | Design formula |
| $R = \dfrac{EI}{M}$ | Radius of Curvature | — | m | Pure bending |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Do not confuse $\vert M \vert$ with vertical bar table separators.** In LaTeX, use $\vert M \vert$ or $\mid M \vert$ for the absolute value of $M$ — never the bare pipe character `|` inside a markdown table row.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These assumptions underpin the **design of every marine and civil engineering structure** in existence:

1. **Ship hull girder bending analysis** — the bending moment, shear force, and section modulus of the midship section.
2. **Bridge girder design** — both steel and reinforced concrete.
3. **Aircraft wing spar analysis** — when the wing is treated as a cantilever beam.
4. **Crane jib and offshore platform leg analysis** — pure bending components.
5. **Pressure vessel head design** — where the head bends away from the cylindrical shell.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Master Bending Equation from the Assumptions

This derivation is the **core KTU expected answer** for any 7 or 14 mark question. Each step is numbered to match the assumption that justifies it.

**Step 1 — Geometry of Deformed Beam (using Assumption 7 & 9)**

Consider a small segment of length $dx$ of a beam, bent to a radius of curvature $R$ under a constant bending moment $M$. A longitudinal fiber at a distance $y$ from the neutral axis has an original length $dx$ and a deformed length $(R - y)d\theta$ (for fibers above the N.A., $y$ is taken as positive upward; for compression, $y$ is negative).

The change in length of the fiber is:

$$
\Delta L = (R - y)d\theta - dx
$$

Since $dx = R\,d\theta$, we can substitute:

$$
\Delta L = (R - y)d\theta - R\,d\theta = -y\,d\theta
$$

**Step 2 — Linear Strain Distribution (justified by Assumption 7)**

The longitudinal strain at distance $y$ is:

$$
\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L} = \dfrac{-y\,d\theta}{R\,d\theta} = -\dfrac{y}{R}
$$

The negative sign indicates that fibers above the neutral axis ($y > 0$) are in compression (negative strain), while fibers below ($y < 0$) are in tension. For simplicity, we often write:

$$
\varepsilon = \dfrac{y}{R}
$$

This proves that **strain varies linearly with distance y from the neutral axis** — a direct consequence of the "plane sections remain plane" assumption.

**Step 3 — Apply Hooke's Law (using Assumption 5)**

Since the material is elastic:

$$
\sigma = E\,\varepsilon
$$

Substituting the strain:

$$
\sigma = E \cdot \dfrac{y}{R} = \dfrac{E\,y}{R}
$$

This shows that **bending stress is also linear in y** (a direct consequence of Assumptions 5 and 7).

**Step 4 — Equilibrium of Internal Forces (using Assumption 6)**

For the beam to be in equilibrium, the net axial force on any cross-section must be zero:

$$
F_x = \int_A \sigma\, dA = 0
$$

Substituting the stress expression:

$$
\int_A \dfrac{E\,y}{R}\,dA = \dfrac{E}{R}\int_A y\,dA = 0
$$

Since $E$ and $R$ are constants, we require:

$$
\int_A y\,dA = 0
$$

This integral is the **first moment of area** about the neutral axis, and it equals zero **if and only if the neutral axis passes through the centroid** of the cross-section. This proves that **the neutral axis coincides with the centroidal axis** (a result of Assumptions 5, 6, and 7).

**Step 5 — Moment Equilibrium (using Assumption 10)**

The internal resisting moment must equal the externally applied bending moment:

$$
M = \int_A \sigma \cdot y\,dA
$$

Substituting $\sigma = \dfrac{E\,y}{R}$:

$$
M = \int_A \dfrac{E\,y}{R} \cdot y\,dA = \dfrac{E}{R}\int_A y^2\,dA
$$

The integral $\int_A y^2\,dA$ is defined as the **Second Moment of Area (MOI)**, denoted $I$:

$$
I = \int_A y^2\,dA
$$

Therefore:

$$
M = \dfrac{EI}{R}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{M}{EI}
$$

**Step 6 — The Master Bending Equation**

Combining the stress expression from Step 3 with the moment-curvature relation from Step 5:

$$
\sigma = \dfrac{E\,y}{R}
$$

and

$$
\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{M}{EI}
$$

Eliminating $R$ between these two equations:

$$
\sigma = E\,y \cdot \dfrac{M}{EI} = \dfrac{M\,y}{I}
$$

The final, board-exam-ready **Master Bending Equation** is:

$$
\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — When Assumptions Are Valid vs. Invalid

| **Assumption** | **Valid When** | **Invalid When (Use Modified Theory)** | **Naval Architecture Example** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Homogeneity & Isotropy | Steel hull, aluminum superstructure | FRP composite panels, plywood | Use **Classical Lamination Theory** |
| Plane sections remain plane | Slender beam (L/h > 10) | Deep beam, near support | Use **Timoshenko Beam Theory** (shear deformation) |
| Small deformations | Hull sag < L/250 | Highly flexible offshore hose | Use **Large deflection theory** |
| Pure bending | Midship section in still water | Sections near bow/stern | Add **shear stress** ($\tau = VQ/Ib$) |
| E_tension = E_compression | Steel, aluminum | Cast iron, concrete | **Neutral axis shifts** from centroid |
| Linear elasticity | Working stress < 0.5 σ_y | Plastic collapse scenario | Use **Plastic Bending Theory** |
| Self-weight neglected | Local stiffener analysis | Long vertical members (masts) | Add **distributed self-weight load** |
| Initial straightness | New hull design | Vessel with significant hull deformation | Apply **initial deflection** correction |
| Material continuity | Sound hull plating | Fatigued/cracked plate | Use **Fracture Mechanics** |
| Small deflections | All normal ship conditions | Crash scenarios, grounding | Use **Non-linear dynamics** |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — The Architecture of Bending Assumptions

The following diagram illustrates how each fundamental assumption logically flows into the next, ultimately producing the master bending equation. This block-level functional architecture is the **conceptual map** KTU examiners expect students to internalize.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Assumption 1: Homogeneity] --> B1[Uniform material property E]
    A2[Assumption 2: Isotropy] --> B1
    A3[Assumption 3: Continuity] --> B1
    A1 --> C1[Linear strain field: epsilon equals y by R]
    A2 --> C1
    A5[Assumption 5: Hookes Law] --> C1
    A7[Assumption 7: Plane sections remain plane] --> C1
    C1 --> D1[Bending stress linear in y: sigma equals E y by R]
    A5 --> D1
    A6[Assumption 6: Equal E in tension and compression] --> E1[Neutral axis through centroid]
    D1 --> E1
    A10[Assumption 10: Pure bending] --> F1[Resisting moment equals integral of sigma y dA]
    A9[Assumption 9: Small deformations] --> F1
    F1 --> G1[Master Bending Equation: M by I equals sigma by y equals E by R]
    B1 --> G1
    E1 --> G1
    A4[Assumption 4: Initial straightness] --> G1
    A8[Assumption 8: Self weight neglected] --> G1
    G1 --> H1[FLEXURE FORMULA: sigma equals M y by I]
    H1 --> I1[Section modulus: Z equals I by y max]
    I1 --> J1[Max bending stress: sigma max equals M by Z]
    J1 --> K1[Ship hull girder design]
    J1 --> K2[Stiffener and frame design]
    J1 --> K3[Deck plating analysis]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Deformation Geometry of a Bent Beam

This second diagram represents the geometric basis (Assumption 7) showing how a plane cross-section rotates and translates during pure bending. A physical sketch of "before and after bending" of a beam segment is conceptually translated into a clean functional flow.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Original beam segment of length dx] --> P1[Apply pure bending moment M]
    P1 --> P2[Cross sections rotate through small angle d theta]
    P2 --> P3[Top fiber shortens by amount y d theta compression]
    P2 --> P4[Bottom fiber lengthens by amount y d theta tension]
    P2 --> P5[Neutral layer unchanged zero strain]
    P3 --> P6[Strain varies linearly with y]
    P4 --> P6
    P5 --> P6
    P6 --> P7[Apply Hookes Law: sigma equals E epsilon]
    P7 --> P8[Bending stress linear with distance y from N.A.]
    P8 --> P9[Calculate I equals integral of y squared dA]
    P9 --> P10[Final formula: sigma equals M y by I]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note for Students:**
> In the above flow, the block "P2" (cross-sections rotate) is the geometric manifestation of **Assumption 7** (Euler-Bernoulli). The flow P6 → P7 is **Assumption 5** (Hooke's Law). The flow P9 → P10 invokes the **definition of Second Moment of Area**. The whole derivation can be read off the diagram in a 2-minute revision session.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**State the fundamental assumptions made in the theory of pure bending.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The following assumptions are made in the derivation of pure bending theory:

1. The beam material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and continuous** throughout its volume.
2. The beam is **initially straight** and of **uniform cross-section**.
3. The material is **perfectly elastic** and obeys **Hooke's Law** ($\sigma = E\varepsilon$) within the elastic limit.
4. **Young's Modulus is the same in tension and compression**, so the neutral axis coincides with the centroidal axis.
5. **Plane sections remain plane and perpendicular to the neutral axis** after bending (Euler-Bernoulli hypothesis).
6. The **self-weight of the beam is neglected** and only the applied bending moment is considered.
7. **Deflections are small** compared to the cross-sectional dimensions.
8. The beam is subjected to **pure bending** — shear force is zero and bending moment is constant.

> [!TIP]
> **Allocation Tip:** Each assumption is worth approximately 0.375 marks. In a 3-mark question, listing 6–8 assumptions concisely (in bullet form) with one line of explanation for the most critical one (Euler-Bernoulli) earns full marks.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Explain the Euler-Bernoulli hypothesis of beam bending. Why is it important?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Euler-Bernoulli hypothesis** states that a cross-section of a beam, which is **plane and perpendicular to the longitudinal axis before bending, remains plane and perpendicular to the deformed neutral axis after bending**. In other words, cross-sections do not warp or distort; they only translate and rotate as rigid bodies.

This assumption is important because:

1. It implies that **shear deformation is zero**, so all fibers experience only **axial (longitudinal) strain**.
2. It leads to a **linear strain distribution** across the cross-section: $\varepsilon = y/R$.
3. Combined with Hooke's Law, it produces a **linear bending stress distribution**: $\sigma = My/I$.
4. It is the **foundation of the classical flexure formula** and is highly accurate for slender beams with span-to-depth ratio greater than 10, which covers most ship structural members.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write "plane sections remain plane" but forget to add **"and perpendicular to the neutral axis."** The perpendicularity is what eliminates shear strain — omitting it loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** State and explain the fundamental assumptions used in the derivation of the bending equation $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ for a beam under pure bending.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A simply supported steel beam of span **4 m** carries a uniformly distributed load of **30 kN/m** over its entire length. The beam has a rectangular cross-section of breadth **100 mm** and depth **200 mm**. Calculate the **maximum bending stress** in the beam. Take $E = 200$ GPa.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

The fundamental assumptions are:

1. **Material Homogeneity, Isotropy, and Continuity** (1 mark):
The material is uniform in composition, has identical elastic properties in all directions, and has no internal voids. This allows the use of a single elastic constant $E$ throughout the volume.

2. **Initial Straightness and Uniform Cross-Section** (0.5 marks):
The beam is perfectly straight in its unloaded state and has a constant cross-section along its length, providing a known reference configuration.

3. **Perfect Elasticity and Hooke's Law** (1 mark):
The material is linearly elastic: $\sigma = E\varepsilon$. This is essential for the linear stress-strain relationship.

4. **Equal Modulus in Tension and Compression** (0.5 marks):
$E_{tension} = E_{compression}$, ensuring the neutral axis passes through the centroid.

5. **Euler-Bernoulli Hypothesis** (1.5 marks):
Plane cross-sections remain plane and perpendicular to the neutral axis after bending. This implies that:
- Longitudinal strain varies linearly: $\varepsilon = y/R$
- Shear deformation is neglected
- Strain is purely axial (no warping)

6. **Pure Bending Condition** (1 mark):
The bending moment is constant and the shear force is zero. Hence, only normal (bending) stresses exist, and shear stresses are absent.

7. **Small Deformations** (0.5 marks):
Deflections are small compared to the cross-sectional dimensions, so the curvature can be approximated as $1/R \approx d^2y/dx^2$.

8. **Self-weight Neglected** (0.5 marks):
The beam's own weight is not considered; only external loads cause bending.

> The combined effect of these assumptions is the master equation $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- Span: $L = 4$ m
- UDL: $w = 30$ kN/m
- Breadth: $b = 100$ mm = $0.1$ m
- Depth: $d = 200$ mm = $0.2$ m
- $E = 200$ GPa

**Step 1 — Maximum Bending Moment**

For a simply supported beam with UDL, maximum bending moment occurs at mid-span:

$$
M_{max} = \dfrac{wL^2}{8}
$$

$$
M_{max} = \dfrac{30 \times 4^2}{8} = \dfrac{30 \times 16}{8} = 60 \text{ kN·m}
$$

**Step 2 — Second Moment of Area for Rectangular Section**

$$
I = \dfrac{bd^3}{12}
$$

$$
I = \dfrac{0.1 \times (0.2)^3}{12} = \dfrac{0.1 \times 0.008}{12} = \dfrac{0.0008}{12}
$$

$$
I = 6.667 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^4
$$

**Step 3 — Section Modulus**

$$
Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{max}} = \dfrac{I}{d/2}
$$

$$
Z = \dfrac{6.667 \times 10^{-5}}{0.1} = 6.667 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 4 — Maximum Bending Stress**

$$
\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M_{max}}{Z} = \dfrac{60 \times 10^3}{6.667 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
\sigma_{max} = 90 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 90 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Final Answer:** The maximum bending stress in the beam is **90 MPa**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Unit consistency is critical.** Convert everything to SI (m, N, Pa) before substitution. Mixing mm with kN·m is the #1 reason students lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Always compute $M_{max}$ first** — students who jump straight to stress often use the wrong moment value (e.g., $wL^2/2$ instead of $wL^2/8$).
> 3. **Identify the maximum moment location** — for a SSB with UDL, it is at the center; for a cantilever, it is at the fixed end.
> 4. **For rectangular sections, use the shortcut** $Z = bd^2/6$ to save 30 seconds and reduce arithmetic errors.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Starting from the assumptions of pure bending, derive the bending equation $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$. Clearly state which assumption is invoked at each step.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An I-section steel beam has the following dimensions: **flange width = 150 mm, flange thickness = 15 mm, web thickness = 10 mm, overall depth = 250 mm**. If the maximum bending moment acting on the beam is **40 kN·m**, determine the **maximum bending stress** developed in the beam.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks (Concise Derivation)

**Step 1 — Geometry (Assumption 7 & 9):**
A beam segment $dx$ bends to radius $R$. A fiber at distance $y$ from the neutral axis has its length change by $\Delta L = -y\,d\theta$. Therefore:

$$
\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L} = \dfrac{-y\,d\theta}{R\,d\theta} = -\dfrac{y}{R}
$$

(0.5 marks)

**Step 2 — Hooke's Law (Assumption 5):**

$$
\sigma = E\varepsilon = \dfrac{Ey}{R}
$$

This shows bending stress varies linearly with $y$. (1 mark)

**Step 3 — Axial Equilibrium (Assumptions 5, 6, 7):**

$$
\int_A \sigma\,dA = 0 \Rightarrow \dfrac{E}{R}\int_A y\,dA = 0 \Rightarrow \int_A y\,dA = 0
$$

Thus the **neutral axis passes through the centroid** of the cross-section. (1.5 marks)

**Step 4 — Moment Equilibrium (Assumption 10):**

$$
M = \int_A \sigma\,y\,dA = \dfrac{E}{R}\int_A y^2\,dA = \dfrac{EI}{R}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{M}{EI}
$$

(1.5 marks)

**Step 5 — Combining the Equations:**

From Step 2: $\sigma = Ey/R$
From Step 4: $1/R = M/EI$

Eliminating $R$:

$$
\sigma = Ey \cdot \dfrac{M}{EI} = \dfrac{My}{I}
$$

The final **Master Bending Equation** is:

$$
\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}
$$

(1.5 marks)

> Assumption invoked summary: **Step 1 → Assumptions 7, 9; Step 2 → Assumption 5; Step 3 → Assumptions 5, 6; Step 4 → Assumption 10; Step 5 → All assumptions combined.** (1 mark)

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given I-section:**
- Flange width: $b_f = 150$ mm
- Flange thickness: $t_f = 15$ mm
- Web thickness: $t_w = 10$ mm
- Overall depth: $D = 250$ mm
- Bending moment: $M = 40$ kN·m

**Step 1 — Web Dimensions**

Web height (between flanges):

$$
h_w = D - 2t_f = 250 - 2(15) = 220 \text{ mm}
$$

Web area: $b_w \times h_w = 10 \times 220 = 2200$ mm²
Flange area (each): $b_f \times t_f = 150 \times 15 = 2250$ mm²
Two flanges total area: $2 \times 2250 = 4500$ mm²

**Step 2 — Second Moment of Area (I)**

Method: Treat as a **rectangle ($b_f \times D$) minus two side rectangles (one on each side of the web).**

Outer rectangle: $b_f \times D = 150 \times 250$
