Inner cutouts: $(b_f - t_w)/2$ on each side of the web, height $h_w$

$$
I = \dfrac{b_f D^3}{12} - \dfrac{(b_f - t_w) h_w^3}{12}
$$

$$
I = \dfrac{150 \times 250^3}{12} - \dfrac{(150 - 10) \times 220^3}{12}
$$

$$
I = \dfrac{150 \times 15{,}625{,}000}{12} - \dfrac{140 \times 10{,}648{,}000}{12}
$$

$$
I = \dfrac{2{,}343{,}750{,}000}{12} - \dfrac{1{,}490{,}720{,}000}{12}
$$

$$
I = 195{,}312{,}500 - 124{,}226{,}667 = 71{,}085{,}833 \text{ mm}^4
$$

$$
I \approx 71.09 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4 = 7.109 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^4
$$

**Step 3 — Section Modulus**

$$
y_{max} = \dfrac{D}{2} = \dfrac{250}{2} = 125 \text{ mm} = 0.125 \text{ m}
$$

$$
Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{max}} = \dfrac{71.09 \times 10^6}{125} = 568{,}720 \text{ mm}^3
$$

$$
Z = 5.687 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 4 — Maximum Bending Stress**

$$
\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M}{Z} = \dfrac{40 \times 10^3}{5.687 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
\sigma_{max} = 70.34 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} \approx 70.3 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Final Answer:** The maximum bending stress in the I-section beam is **≈ 70.3 MPa**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning for I-section problems:**
> 1. **Most common error:** Students use the wrong formula for I, such as $bh^3/12$ for the whole I-beam. Always break the section into simpler shapes (rectangles) and use the **subtraction method** or **parallel axis theorem**.
> 2. **Sign of $y_{max}$:** For a symmetric I-section, $y_{max} = D/2$ (from neutral axis to extreme fiber). For unsymmetric sections, you must compute the centroid first.
> 3. **Unit trap:** When using mm throughout, remember $M$ must be in **N·mm** (not kN·m) to get stress in MPa directly.
> 4. **The value of $E$ is not needed** for stress calculation — $E$ cancels out. $E$ is only needed for **deflection** problems.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Assumptions" Topic, Module 3, PCNST303**

- **10 fundamental assumptions** of pure bending theory form the foundation of Module 3.
- The **Euler-Bernoulli hypothesis** (plane sections remain plane and perpendicular) is the **most critical** assumption and is worth 2–3 marks by itself.
- All assumptions together yield the **master equation**: $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$.
- **Neutral axis = Centroidal axis** is true **only** because $E_{tension} = E_{compression}$ (a critical consequence to remember).
- For a **rectangular section**: $I = bd^3/12$ and $Z = bd^2/6$.
- For an **I-section**: Use the **subtraction method** (outer rectangle minus inner cutouts) or the **parallel axis theorem** for the flanges.
- The **section modulus Z** is the most important design parameter — it directly gives $\sigma_{max} = M/Z$.
- **For naval architecture:** the assumption of "pure bending" is most accurate at the **midship section** of a ship.
- **Validity limits:** L/d > 10 for Euler-Bernoulli; small deflections for $1/R \approx d^2y/dx^2$.
- **Standard values to memorize:** $E_{steel} = 200$ GPa, $\nu = 0.30$, $\sigma_{y,steel} = 235$ MPa.
- **Examiner's favourite 3-mark question:** "State the assumptions of pure bending" — answer must be in **bullet/numbered list form**, not paragraph form.
- **Examiner's favourite 7-mark question:** "Derive the bending equation" — must show **each step with the corresponding assumption** clearly labeled.
- **Always convert units to SI** (N, m, Pa) before plugging into the flexure formula.
- **Maximum bending moment locations:** SSB with UDL → mid-span; Cantilever with UDL → fixed end; SSB with point load at center → under the load.

> **End of Module 3 — Topic: Assumptions | PCNST303 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shear Stress Distribution for Rectangular, Circular, Triangular, I and T Sections
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST303 - SOLID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Shear Stress Distribution for Rectangular, Circular, Triangular, I and T Sections

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Shear Stress Distribution** refers to the variation of transverse shear stress ($\tau$) across the cross-section of a beam, computed using the **flexure formula for shear** (also called the **Zhuravskii-Bredt formula**). For a beam subjected to a transverse shear force $V$ at a section, the shear stress at any fiber located at a distance $y$ from the **Neutral Axis (NA)** is given by:

$$\tau = \frac{V \cdot Q}{I \cdot b}$$

where:
- $V$ = transverse shear force at the section (in **N**)
- $Q$ = **First Moment of Area** of the portion of the cross-section beyond the fiber about the Neutral Axis (in **mm³** or **m³**)
- $I$ = **Second Moment of Area** of the entire cross-section about the Neutral Axis (in **mm⁴** or **m⁴**)
- $b$ = width of the section at the fiber where stress is computed (in **mm** or **m**)
- $\tau$ = shear stress at the fiber (in **N/mm² = MPa** or **N/m² = Pa**)

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Sliced Cake" Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a thick, layered cake being pushed sideways with a knife. The knife doesn't cut cleanly in one go — the **top and bottom layers** (extreme fibers) experience **zero** shear stress because they are free surfaces, while the **central jam layer** (neutral axis) experiences the **maximum** shear stress because the largest area of cake above AND below it is sliding past the cut. This is exactly how shear stress distributes in a beam — **zero at top/bottom, maximum at the neutral axis**, with a smooth variation in between.

## 1.3 Key Assumptions Underlying the Formula

- The beam material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic** (Hooke's law applies).
- The cross-section is **prismatic** (constant along the length).
- The shear stress is **uniformly distributed** across the width $b$ at a given fiber.
- Plane sections remain **plane and perpendicular** to the deformed neutral axis (Euler-Bernoulli beam theory).
- The beam is subjected to **pure bending combined with transverse shear**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Logic Behind $\tau = \frac{VQ}{Ib}$

The derivation is rooted in **static equilibrium** of an elemental beam segment of length $dx$:

1. **Cut a segment** of the beam between two cross-sections separated by $dx$.
2. The bending stresses on the two faces form a **couple**, producing a net **horizontal force** on any sub-area above the fiber.
3. To maintain **horizontal equilibrium** of the sub-area, an **equal and opposite horizontal shear force** must act on the bottom face of this sub-area.
4. This horizontal shear force, divided by the face area, gives the **transverse shear stress** (since complementary shear stresses are equal in magnitude).

The result: $\tau = \dfrac{VQ}{Ib}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Q"?** $Q = A\bar{y}$ where $A$ is the area of the portion of the cross-section **beyond** the fiber (farther from NA) and $\bar{y}$ is the distance from the **centroid of that area** to the Neutral Axis. Q represents how much "material mass" is offset from the NA on one side.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic for Any Cross-Section

To compute shear stress distribution for any section:

1. **Determine the Neutral Axis (NA):** Find the centroid of the cross-section. Measure all distances from the NA.
2. **Compute $I$:** Find the second moment of area of the **entire** cross-section about the NA.
3. **For a fiber at distance $y$ from NA:** Compute $Q(y) = A \cdot \bar{y}$, where $A$ is the area **above** (or below) the fiber and $\bar{y}$ is its centroidal distance from the NA.
4. **Measure $b$:** The width of the cross-section at the fiber.
5. **Apply** $\tau = \dfrac{VQ}{Ib}$ to get shear stress.
6. **Find $\tau_{max}$:** Occurs at the NA where $Q$ is maximum and $b$ is typically largest.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| **Cross-Section** | **Maximum Shear Stress $\tau_{max}$** | **Location of $\tau_{max}$** | **Distribution Shape** | **$\tau$ at Extreme Fiber** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Rectangular** ($b \times d$) | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{3V}{2A} = \dfrac{3V}{2bd}$ | Neutral Axis (center) | **Parabolic** | $0$ |
| **Circular** (radius $R$) | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{4V}{3A} = \dfrac{4V}{3\pi R^2}$ | Neutral Axis (center) | **Parabolic** | $0$ |
| **Solid Equilateral Triangle** (side $a$) | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{3V}{A} = \dfrac{4\sqrt{3}V}{a^2}$ | At 2/3 height from apex (NA) | **Curved (parabolic-like)** | $0$ |
| **I-Section** | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{V}{b_w \cdot D}$ (approx.) or full $VQ/Ib$ calc. | NA within the **web** | **Stepped (parabolic in web, low in flanges)** | $\approx 0$ |
| **T-Section** | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{VQ_{NA}}{I \cdot b_{min}}$ | At NA (width is minimum = stem width) | **Asymmetric parabolic** | $0$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Boundary Conditions for ALL sections:**
> 1. $\tau = 0$ at the **top and bottom extreme fibers** (free surfaces — no shear at boundaries).
> 2. $\tau = \tau_{max}$ at the **Neutral Axis** (where $Q$ is maximum).
> 3. The distribution is **symmetric about the NA** for sections with horizontal axis of symmetry.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **Naval Architecture**, shear stress distribution analysis is critical for:
- **Ship hull girder** design: The hull acts as a long beam; shear stress must be checked at deck, bottom, and side shells.
- **Bulkhead and stiffener** design in I/T profiles.
- **Shaft design**: Circular cross-sections of propeller shafts experience torsional and transverse shear.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 DERIVATION 1: Rectangular Section ($b \times d$)

**Given:** Width $= b$, Depth $= d$, Shear Force $= V$.

**Step 1:** Find Neutral Axis (NA). Due to symmetry, NA passes through the **centroid** at mid-depth, so NA is at $d/2$ from top and bottom.

**Step 2:** Second moment of area about NA:

$$I = \frac{bd^3}{12}$$

**Step 3:** Consider a fiber at distance $y$ from NA. The area **above** the fiber is a rectangle of width $b$ and height $(d/2 - y)$.

$$A = b \left(\frac{d}{2} - y\right)$$

**Step 4:** The centroid of this area is at distance $\bar{y}$ from NA:

$$\bar{y} = \frac{y + d/2}{2} = \frac{d/2 + y}{2}$$

Wait — the distance from the **NA to the centroid** of the area above is:

$$\bar{y} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{d}{2} + y\right) = \frac{d/2 + y}{2}$$

**Step 5:** Compute $Q$:

$$Q = A \cdot \bar{y} = b\left(\frac{d}{2} - y\right) \cdot \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{d}{2} + y\right) = \frac{b}{2}\left[\left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^2 - y^2\right]$$

**Step 6:** Apply the shear formula $\tau = \dfrac{VQ}{Ib}$:

$$\tau = \frac{V \cdot \frac{b}{2}\left[\frac{d^2}{4} - y^2\right]}{\frac{bd^3}{12} \cdot b} = \frac{6V}{bd^3}\left[\frac{d^2}{4} - y^2\right]$$

**Step 7:** Simplify — the classic parabolic form:

$$\boxed{\tau = \frac{3V}{2bd}\left[1 - \left(\frac{2y}{d}\right)^2\right]}$$

**Step 8:** Maximum shear stress occurs at $y = 0$ (NA):

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{3V}{2bd} = \frac{3V}{2A} = 1.5 \cdot \frac{V}{A}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Naval Architects:** The "**1.5 rule**" for rectangular sections is fundamental. A rectangular timber deck plate or flat plate in a ship's hull will carry a shear force $V$ with peak shear stress $50\%$ higher than the **average** shear ($V/A$).

---

## 3.2 DERIVATION 2: Solid Circular Section (Radius $R$)

**Given:** Radius $= R$, Shear Force $= V$.

**Step 1:** NA passes through the **center** (by symmetry).

**Step 2:** Second moment of area about diameter (NA):

$$I = \frac{\pi R^4}{4}$$

**Step 3:** Consider a fiber at distance $y$ from NA. The area above is a circular segment.

The area of a circular segment above the chord at distance $y$ from center:

$$A = R^2 \cos^{-1}\left(\frac{y}{R}\right) - y\sqrt{R^2 - y^2}$$

**Step 4:** The centroidal distance of this segment from the center:

$$\bar{y} = \frac{4R^2 \sqrt{R^2 - y^2}}{3 \cdot \left[2A\right]}$$

This simplifies (after careful integration) to a known form. Using the **standard result for circular sections**:

$$Q(y) = \frac{2}{3}\left(R^2 - y^2\right)^{3/2}$$

**Step 5:** Width at the fiber:

$$b = 2\sqrt{R^2 - y^2}$$

**Step 6:** Apply shear formula:

$$\tau = \frac{V \cdot \frac{2}{3}\left(R^2 - y^2\right)^{3/2}}{\frac{\pi R^4}{4} \cdot 2\sqrt{R^2 - y^2}} = \frac{4V}{3\pi R^4}\left(R^2 - y^2\right)$$

**Step 7:** Simplified form:

$$\boxed{\tau = \frac{4V}{3\pi R^4}\left(R^2 - y^2\right)}$$

**Step 8:** Maximum at center ($y = 0$):

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{4V}{3\pi R^2} = \frac{4V}{3A}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** For a circular cross-section (e.g., a propeller shaft, mast, or cylindrical hull stiffener), the average shear is $V/A$, but the **peak shear at NA is $\frac{4}{3}$ times the average** — about $33\%$ higher than the rectangular case.

---

## 3.3 DERIVATION 3: Solid Triangular Section

**Given:** Isosceles triangle with base $b$ and height $h$. Shear Force $= V$.

**Step 1:** Locate the Neutral Axis. For a triangle with base at the bottom and apex at top, the centroid is at $\frac{h}{3}$ from the base. So the NA is at $\frac{h}{3}$ from base and $\frac{2h}{3}$ from apex.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trick:** The NA of a triangle does NOT pass through the mid-height — it is at $\frac{h}{3}$ from the **base** (or $\frac{2h}{3}$ from the apex).

**Step 2:** Second moment of area about NA (centroidal axis parallel to base):

$$I = \frac{bh^3}{36}$$

**Step 3:** Consider a fiber at distance $y_1$ from the apex (measured downward). The width at this level (by similar triangles, where the triangle tapers linearly from base $b$ at the bottom to $0$ at the apex):

$$b_{y_1} = b \cdot \frac{y_1}{h}$$

**Step 4:** Area of triangle above the fiber (a smaller similar triangle of height $y_1$ and base $b \cdot y_1/h$):

$$A = \frac{1}{2} \cdot b \cdot \frac{y_1}{h} \cdot y_1 = \frac{b y_1^2}{2h}$$

**Step 5:** Centroid of this smaller triangle is at $\frac{y_1}{3}$ from the fiber (since centroid is at 1/3 of its own height from its base). Distance from NA:

Distance from apex to NA $= \frac{2h}{3}$. So distance from NA to centroid of upper portion:

$$\bar{y} = \frac{2h}{3} - \frac{y_1}{3} = \frac{2h - y_1}{3}$$

**Step 6:** Compute $Q$:

$$Q = A \cdot \bar{y} = \frac{b y_1^2}{2h} \cdot \frac{2h - y_1}{3} = \frac{b y_1^2 (2h - y_1)}{6h}$$

**Step 7:** Apply shear formula with $b = b y_1/h$:

$$\tau = \frac{V \cdot \frac{b y_1^2(2h - y_1)}{6h}}{\frac{bh^3}{36} \cdot \frac{b y_1}{h}} = \frac{6V \cdot y_1 (2h - y_1)}{b^2 h^2}$$

This is a complex expression. The maximum occurs at a specific $y_1$. Setting $\frac{d\tau}{dy_1} = 0$:

$$\frac{d}{dy_1}\left[y_1(2h - y_1)\right] = 2h - 2y_1 = 0 \implies y_1 = h$$

This gives zero, meaning the **maximum is at the NA**. To find the max, we evaluate at $y_1 = 2h/3$ (NA location):

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{6V \cdot (2h/3)(2h - 2h/3)}{b^2 h^2} = \frac{6V \cdot (2h/3)(4h/3)}{b^2 h^2} = \frac{16V}{3b^2 h}$$

For an **equilateral triangle** of side $a$: $h = \dfrac{a\sqrt{3}}{2}$, $A = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{4}a^2$.

$$\boxed{\tau_{max} = \frac{3V}{A} = \frac{4\sqrt{3} \cdot V}{a^2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Triangular sections give the highest peak shear stress ($3V/A$ at NA) — three times the average. Triangular gussets in ship structures (used in brackets, frames) need careful shear analysis at the NA, which is closer to the base than the mid-height.

---

## 3.4 DERIVATION 4: I-Section

**Given:** I-section with flange width $B$, flange thickness $t_f$, web height $h_w$, web thickness $t_w$. Total depth $D = h_w + 2t_f$.

**Step 1:** NA passes through the **center** (by symmetry). $I$ is calculated about the horizontal centroidal axis.

For standard I-section (or use parallel axis theorem):

$$I = \frac{BD^3 - (B - t_w)h_w^3}{12}$$

**Step 2:** Shear stress in the **flange** (at distance $y$ from NA, where $|y| > h_w/2$):

The area beyond the fiber is a small rectangle in the flange tip. Compute $Q$ and $b = B$.

**Step 3:** Shear stress in the **web** (at distance $y$ from NA, where $|y| \leq h_w/2$):

Area above the fiber in the web = $\left(\dfrac{h_w}{2} - y\right) \cdot t_w$, plus the entire flange area.

$$Q_{flange} = B \cdot t_f \cdot \left(\frac{h_w}{2} + \frac{t_f}{2}\right)$$

$$Q_{web} = t_w \cdot \left(\frac{h_w}{2} - y\right) \cdot \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{h_w}{2} + y\right) = \frac{t_w}{2}\left[\left(\frac{h_w}{2}\right)^2 - y^2\right]$$

Total $Q$ in web region:

$$Q(y) = Q_{flange} + Q_{web} = B \cdot t_f \cdot \left(\frac{h_w + t_f}{2}\right) + \frac{t_w}{2}\left[\frac{h_w^2}{4} - y^2\right]$$

**Step 4:** Width in web $= t_w$:

$$\tau_{web}(y) = \frac{V \cdot Q(y)}{I \cdot t_w}$$

**Step 5:** Maximum shear stress at NA ($y = 0$):

$$Q_{max} = B \cdot t_f \cdot \frac{h_w + t_f}{2} + \frac{t_w h_w^2}{8}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{max} = \frac{V \cdot Q_{max}}{I \cdot t_w}}$$

**Step 6:** Shear stress at the **flange-web junction** ($y = h_w/2$):

$$Q_{junction} = B \cdot t_f \cdot \left(\frac{h_w + t_f}{2}\right)$$

$$\tau_{junction} = \frac{V \cdot B \cdot t_f \cdot (h_w + t_f)/2}{I \cdot t_w}$$

This is typically **less** than $\tau_{max}$ because the width jumps from $t_w$ to $B$ at the junction, causing a **sharp drop** in $\tau$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distribution Shape:** In I-sections, the shear stress distribution looks like a **bow-tie/hourglass** — almost zero in the flanges (because $b = B$ is large), rising sharply at the flange-web junction, then peaking in the web, and symmetric about the NA.

---

## 3.5 DERIVATION 5: T-Section

**Given:** Flange width $B$, flange thickness $t_f$, stem (web) height $h_s$, stem width $b_s$. Total depth $D = t_f + h_s$.

**Step 1:** Locate the NA. Take reference from the **top of the flange** (measuring downward):

$$\bar{y}_{NA} = \frac{A_f \cdot \bar{y}_f + A_s \cdot \bar{y}_s}{A_f + A_s}$$

where:
- $A_f = B \cdot t_f$, $\bar{y}_f = t_f/2$
- $A_s = b_s \cdot h_s$, $\bar{y}_s = t_f + h_s/2$

**Step 2:** Compute $I$ about the NA (using parallel axis theorem):

$$I = I_f + A_f d_f^2 + I_s + A_s d_s^2$$

where $d_f$ and $d_s$ are distances from each part's centroid to the NA.

**Step 3:** Shear stress in the **flange** ($0 \leq y \leq t_f$ from top):

Consider a fiber at depth $y$ from top. Area above = $B \cdot y$. Centroid at $y/2$ from top, so its distance from NA:

$$\bar{y}_{flange} = \bar{y}_{NA} - \frac{y}{2}$$

Width $b = B$ (in the flange region).

$$\tau_{flange}(y) = \frac{V \cdot B \cdot y \cdot (\bar{y}_{NA} - y/2)}{I \cdot B} = \frac{V \cdot y \cdot (\bar{y}_{NA} - y/2)}{I}$$

**Step 4:** Shear stress in the **stem** ($t_f \leq y \leq D$, or equivalently $|y| \leq h_s/2$ from NA):

Area above the fiber = flange + part of stem.

$$Q(y) = A_f \cdot d_f + b_s \cdot (y_{NA} - y_{stem top}) \cdot \frac{(y_{NA} - y_{stem top})}{2}$$

Width $b = b_s$ (narrow).

$$\tau_{stem}(y) = \frac{V \cdot Q(y)}{I \cdot b_s}$$

**Step 5:** Maximum shear stress — at the NA where the width is smallest ($b = b_s$) and $Q$ is maximum:

$$Q_{max} = A_f \cdot d_f = B \cdot t_f \cdot \left(\bar{y}_{NA} - \frac{t_f}{2}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{max} = \frac{V \cdot B \cdot t_f \cdot \left(\bar{y}_{NA} - \frac{t_f}{2}\right)}{I \cdot b_s}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Asymmetry Alert:** Unlike the I-section, the T-section has an **asymmetric** shear stress distribution. The NA is closer to the flange (because it's the larger area), and the peak shear stress occurs at the NA, located in the **stem** (the narrower portion). This is critical for ship stiffeners, T-bars in hull frames, and bulb sections.

---

## 3.6 Summary Table: Key Results

| **Section** | **$\tau_{max}$** | **$\tau_{max}/\tau_{avg}$** |
|---|---|---|
| Rectangular | $1.5 \, V/A$ | $1.5$ |
| Circular | $1.333 \, V/A$ | $1.333$ |
| Triangular | $3.0 \, V/A$ | $3.0$ |
| I-Section (in web) | $> 1.5 \, V/A$ | Varies (depends on geometry) |
| T-Section (in stem) | Depends on geometry | Varies (can be very high) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["BEAM WITH TRANSVERSE SHEAR FORCE V"] --> B["Identify Cross Section Type"]
    B --> C1["Rectangular bxd"]
    B --> C2["Circular Radius R"]
    B --> C3["Triangular base b height h"]
    B --> C4["I-Section"]
    B --> C5["T-Section"]

    C1 --> D1["NA at mid depth"]
    C2 --> D2["NA at center"]
    C3 --> D3["NA at h/3 from base"]
    C4 --> D4["NA at mid depth symmetric"]
    C5 --> D5["NA closer to flange centroid"]

    D1 --> E1["I equals bd cubed by 12"]
    D2 --> E2["I equals pi R to the 4 by 4"]
    D3 --> E3["I equals bh cubed by 36"]
    D4 --> E4["I via parallel axis theorem"]
    D5 --> E5["I via parallel axis theorem"]

    E1 --> F1["Parabolic tau from 0 to 1.5 V over A"]
    E2 --> F2["Parabolic tau from 0 to 1.333 V over A"]
    E3 --> F3["Curved tau from 0 to 3.0 V over A at NA"]
    E4 --> F4["Stepped distribution peak in web"]
    E5 --> F5["Asymmetric peak at NA in stem"]

    F1 --> G["UNIVERSAL RULE tau equals VQ over Ib"]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    F5 --> G

    G --> H["Maximum at NA where Q is largest and b is minimum"]
    H --> I["Zero at top and bottom free surfaces"]
```

## 4.2 Comparative Distribution Shapes (Conceptual)

| **Section** | **Distribution Shape (NA horizontal, top/bottom vertical)** |
|---|---|
| **Rectangular** | Smooth parabola — $\tau = 0$ at top, rises to max at NA, falls to $0$ at bottom |
| **Circular** | Smooth parabola — $\tau = 0$ at top, max at center, $0$ at bottom |
| **Triangular** | Asymmetric — $\tau = 0$ at apex, rises, peaks at NA (closer to base), falls to $0$ at base |
| **I-Section** | "Bow-tie" — Very low in flanges (large $b$), **sharp jump up** at flange-web junction, parabolic in web peaking at NA |
| **T-Section** | Asymmetric — Low in flange, **sharp jump** at flange-stem junction, peaks at NA in stem |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visualization Tip for Students:** When you draw these distributions, always remember:
> - **Width $b$ is in the denominator** → where the section is WIDE, stress is LOW.
> - **Where the section is NARROW** (e.g., web of I, stem of T), stress is HIGH.
> - This is why **I-beams and T-beams** carry shear mainly through their webs/stems, and why flanges are designed for **bending (axial) stress**, not shear.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define the term "First Moment of Area" $Q$ and state its significance in shear stress calculation.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The **First Moment of Area** $Q$ (also called the **statical moment of area**) of a portion of a cross-section about the Neutral Axis is defined as the product of the area of that portion and the perpendicular distance from the Neutral Axis to the centroid of that area:

$$Q = A \cdot \bar{y}$$

where $A$ is the area of the cross-section beyond the fiber under consideration, and $\bar{y}$ is the distance from the Neutral Axis to the centroid of $A$.

**Significance:**
1. It quantifies how the cross-sectional area is distributed about the NA.
2. It is the foundation of the **shear stress formula** $\tau = \dfrac{VQ}{Ib}$.
3. $\tau_{max}$ occurs at the NA because $Q$ is maximum there (entire area is on one side).
4. $Q = 0$ at the extreme fibers, leading to $\tau = 0$ at top and bottom.

---

### **Question 2: For which cross-section is the maximum shear stress equal to $\frac{3V}{2A}$? Derive or justify.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The maximum shear stress equals $\dfrac{3V}{2A} = 1.5 \cdot \dfrac{V}{A}$ for a **rectangular cross-section** (width $b$, depth $d$).

**Justification:** For a rectangular section,
- $I = \dfrac{bd^3}{12}$
- $Q_{max}$ at NA $= \dfrac{bd^2}{8}$ (area above NA $\times$ its centroidal distance from NA $= b \cdot d/2 \cdot d/4 = bd^2/8$)
- $b_{NA} = b$

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{V \cdot Q_{max}}{I \cdot b} = \frac{V \cdot bd^2/8}{bd^3/12 \cdot b} = \frac{3V}{2bd} = \frac{3V}{2A}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks): Rectangular + I-Section Combined** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

A simply supported beam of span 4 m carries a uniformly distributed load of 30 kN/m over its entire span. The cross-section of the beam is:
- **(a)** A **rectangular section** of width 200 mm and depth 400 mm — Determine the **maximum shear stress** and sketch the **shear stress distribution** at a section of maximum shear force. **(7 marks)**
- **(b)** If the cross-section is changed to an **I-section** with flange width = 200 mm, flange thickness = 25 mm, web thickness = 25 mm, and total depth = 400 mm — Calculate the **maximum shear stress** in the web and the **shear stress at the flange-web junction**. **(7 marks)**

#### **Solution to Part (a):**

**Step 1:** Maximum shear force for a simply supported beam with UDL:

$$V_{max} = \frac{wL}{2} = \frac{30 \times 4}{2} = 60 \text{ kN} = 60{,}000 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2:** Area of cross-section:

$$A = b \times d = 200 \times 400 = 80{,}000 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 3:** Apply the rectangular section formula:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{3V}{2A} = \frac{3 \times 60{,}000}{2 \times 80{,}000} = 1.125 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 1.125 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4:** Distribution is **parabolic** with:
- $\tau = 0$ at top fiber ($y = 200$ mm)
- $\tau = 1.125$ MPa at NA ($y = 0$)
- $\tau = 0$ at bottom fiber ($y = -200$ mm)

**Distribution equation:** $\tau(y) = 1.125 \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{2y}{400}\right)^2\right]$ MPa

---

#### **Solution to Part (b):**

**Given:**
- Flange: $B = 200$ mm, $t_f = 25$ mm
- Web: $t_w = 25$ mm, $h_w = 400 - 2(25) = 350$ mm
- Total depth $D = 400$ mm

**Step 1:** Compute $I$ about NA (centroidal axis, by symmetry NA is at mid-depth):

$$I = \frac{BD^3 - (B - t_w)h_w^3}{12} = \frac{200 \times 400^3 - 175 \times 350^3}{12}$$

$$I = \frac{200 \times 64 \times 10^6 - 175 \times 42.875 \times 10^6}{12}$$

$$I = \frac{12.8 \times 10^9 - 7.503 \times 10^9}{12} = \frac{5.297 \times 10^9}{12} = 441.4 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 2:** Compute $Q_{max}$ at NA (entire area above the NA):

The area above NA consists of:
- Top flange: $A_f = 200 \times 25 = 5000$ mm², centroid at $175 + 12.5 = 187.5$ mm from NA
- Half web: $A_{hw} = 25 \times 175 = 4375$ mm², centroid at $87.5$ mm from NA

$$Q_{max} = (5000 \times 187.5) + (4375 \times 87.5) = 937{,}500 + 382{,}812.5 = 1{,}320{,}312.5 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 3:** Maximum shear stress (in web at NA):

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{V \cdot Q_{max}}{I \cdot t_w} = \frac{60{,}000 \times 1{,}320{,}312.5}{441.4 \times 10^6 \times 25} = \frac{7.922 \times 10^{10}}{1.1035 \times 10^{10}} = 7.18 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4:** Shear stress at flange-web junction ($y = h_w/2 = 175$ mm from NA):

Area above junction = entire top flange = $5000$ mm², centroid at $187.5$ mm from NA.

$$Q_{junction} = 5000 \times 187.5 = 937{,}500 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$\tau_{junction} = \frac{60{,}000 \times 937{,}500}{441.4 \times 10^6 \times 25} = \frac{5.625 \times 10^{10}}{1.1035 \times 10^{10}} = 5.10 \text{ MPa}$$

**Result:** $\tau_{max} = 7.18$ MPa in web at NA; $\tau_{junction} = 5.10$ MPa at flange-web junction.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes Students Make:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract** $(B - t_w)h_w^3$ when computing $I$ for I-sections. Use the "**subtraction method**": $I = I_{outer} - I_{cutout}$.
> 2. **Using wrong $b$**: At the flange-web junction, the width suddenly changes from $B$ to $t_w$. Some students mistakenly use $b = B$ in the web region, leading to **drastically wrong (too low) shear stress**.
> 3. **Mixing units**: $I$ in mm⁴, $Q$ in mm³, $V$ in N → $\tau$ in N/mm² = MPa. Mixing cm and mm is the #1 unit error.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Circular + T-Section Combined** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** A solid circular shaft of diameter 150 mm transmits a transverse shear force of 80 kN. Compute the **maximum shear stress** in the shaft and the **shear stress at a radius of 50 mm from the center**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A T-section has flange dimensions $150 \text{ mm} \times 20 \text{ mm}$ and stem dimensions $30 \text{ mm} \times 80 \text{ mm}$. If a shear force of 25 kN acts on it, determine the **location of the Neutral Axis**, the **moment of inertia** about the NA, and the **maximum shear stress**. **(7 marks)**

#### **Solution to Part (a):**

**Given:** Diameter $D = 150$ mm, so $R = 75$ mm. $V = 80$ kN $= 80{,}000$ N.

**Step 1:** Area:

$$A = \pi R^2 = \pi \times 75^2 = 17{,}671.5 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2:** Second moment of area:

$$I = \frac{\pi R^4}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 75^4}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 31{,}640{,}625}{4} = 24{,}850{,}000 \text{ mm}^4 = 2.485 \times 10^7 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 3:** Maximum shear stress at NA ($y = 0$):

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{4V}{3A} = \frac{4 \times 80{,}000}{3 \times 17{,}671.5} = 6.034 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4:** Shear stress at $y = 50$ mm from NA:

$$\tau = \frac{4V}{3\pi R^4}\left(R^2 - y^2\right) = \frac{4 \times 80{,}000}{3\pi \times 75^4}(75^2 - 50^2)$$

$$\tau = \frac{320{,}000}{3\pi \times 31{,}640{,}625}(5625 - 2500) = \frac{320{,}000 \times 3125}{2.982 \times 10^8} = 3.35 \text{ MPa}$$

**Result:** $\tau_{max} = 6.034$ MPa (at center); $\tau_{at\, y=50} = 3.35$ MPa.

---

#### **Solution to Part (b):**

**Given:**
- Flange: $B = 150$ mm, $t_f = 20$ mm
- Stem: $b_s = 30$ mm, $h_s = 80$ mm
- $V = 25$ kN $= 25{,}000$ N

**Step 1:** Locate NA (taking reference from **top of flange**):

- $A_f = 150 \times 20 = 3000$ mm², $\bar{y}_f = 10$ mm
- $A_s = 30 \times 80 = 2400$ mm², $\bar{y}_s = 20 + 40 = 60$ mm

$$\bar{y}_{NA} = \frac{3000 \times 10 + 2400 \times 60}{3000 + 2400} = \frac{30{,}000 + 144{,}000}{5400} = 32.22 \text{ mm from top}$$

**Step 2:** Compute $I$ about NA:

- $I_f$ (own axis) $= \dfrac{150 \times 20^3}{12} = 100{,}000$ mm⁴
- $d_f = 32.22 - 10 = 22.22$ mm
- $A_f d_f^2 = 3000 \times 22.22^2 = 1{,}481{,}482$ mm⁴
- $I_s$ (own axis) $= \dfrac{30 \times 80^3}{12} = 1{,}280{,}000$ mm⁴
- $d_s = 60 - 32.22 = 27.78$ mm
- $A_s d_s^2 = 2400 \times 27.78^2 = 1{,}851{,}826$ mm⁴

$$I = 100{,}000 + 1{,}481{,}482 + 1{,}280{,}000 + 1{,}851{,}826 = 4{,}713{,}308 \text{ mm}^4 \approx 4.713 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 3:** Maximum shear stress at NA (in stem, where $b = b_s = 30$ mm):

$Q_{max}$ at NA = area of flange $\times$ its centroidal distance from NA:

$$Q_{max} = 3000 \times 22.22 = 66{,}667 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{V \cdot Q_{max}}{I \cdot b_s} = \frac{25{,}000 \times 66{,}667}{4.713 \times 10^6 \times 30} = \frac{1.667 \times 10^9}{1.414 \times 10^8} = 11.79 \text{ MPa}$$

**Result:** NA is at **32.22 mm from top**; $I = 4.713 \times 10^6$ mm⁴; $\tau_{max} = 11.79$ MPa in the stem.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — T-Section Errors:**
> 1. **Forgetting parallel axis theorem** — students compute $I$ using only the "own axis" formula and lose 3-4 marks.
> 2. **Using $b = B$ (flange width) instead of $b = b_s$ (stem width)** at the NA. Since NA lies in the stem, you must use the **stem width** for $b$.
> 3. **Forgetting to measure from NA** — distances must be from the Neutral Axis, not from the top of the section.
> 4. **Symmetry assumption**: T-section is **NOT** symmetric about horizontal axis — never assume NA is at mid-height!

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 KTU Rapid-Fire Revision — Shear Stress Distribution**

- ✅ **Master Formula (Universal):** $\tau = \dfrac{VQ}{Ib}$ — applicable to **all** cross-sections.
- ✅ **Units:** $V$ in N, $Q$ in mm³, $I$ in mm⁴, $b$ in mm → $\tau$ in N/mm² = **MPa**.
- ✅ **Rectangular:** $\tau_{max} = 1.5 \, V/A$ at NA; **parabolic** distribution.
- ✅ **Circular:** $\tau_{max} = 1.333 \, V/A$ at center; **parabolic** distribution.
- ✅ **Triangular:** $\tau_{max} = 3 \, V/A$ at NA (which is at $h/3$ from base, NOT mid-height).
- ✅ **I-Section:** $\tau_{max}$ in **web** at NA; very low in **flanges** (large $b$). The distribution has a "**stepped**" appearance.
- ✅ **T-Section:** $\tau_{max}$ in **stem** at NA; NA is **closer to the flange** (the larger area dominates centroid location).
- ✅ **Universal Boundary Conditions:** $\tau = 0$ at top and bottom extreme fibers; $\tau = \tau_{max}$ at NA.
- ✅ **Width Effect:** Stress is **inversely proportional to width $b$** — narrow regions carry **more** shear.
- ✅ **Quick Numerical Check:** $\tau_{max}/\tau_{avg}$ is always $\geq 1$. Rectangular: 1.5; Circular: 1.333; Triangular: 3.0.
- ✅ **Naval Arch. Application:** Ship hull girder shear, bulkhead stiffeners, shaft design.
- ✅ **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Never assume NA is at mid-height for **asymmetric** sections (T, L, Z) — **always compute centroid first**!
- ✅ **Drawing Tip:** Always show (1) the cross-section, (2) the NA location, (3) the parabolic/curved distribution, (4) the maximum value labeled clearly.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principal Stresses and Strains: Definition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST303 - SOLID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Principal Stresses and Strains: Definition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Principal Stresses** are defined as the **maximum and minimum normal stresses** that occur at a point in a stressed material, acting on planes where the **shear stress is identically zero**. These planes are mutually perpendicular and are called the **Principal Planes**.

At any general point in a 2D or 3D stressed body, the stress state is described by a stress tensor. However, this tensor is **coordinate-dependent** — its components change as we rotate our reference axes. The principal stresses are the **eigenvalues** of the stress tensor, representing the **invariant** (rotation-independent) normal stress magnitudes at that point.

Formally, for a 2D state of plane stress at a point with normal stresses $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$ and shear stress $\tau_{xy}$:

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

where:
- $\sigma_1$ = **Maximum Principal Stress** (algebraically largest)
- $\sigma_2$ = **Minimum Principal Stress** (algebraically smallest)

> [!NOTE]
> The convention used in KTU 2024 follows the **standard ranking convention**:
> $$\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \sigma_3$$
> (algebraic ordering, where compressive stresses are negative).

### 1.2 Principal Strains — The Companion Definition

By direct analogy with stress, **Principal Strains** are the **normal strains** acting on planes (or along directions) where the **shear strain is zero**. They are the **eigenvalues of the strain tensor**.

For a 2D strain state with normal strains $\epsilon_x$, $\epsilon_y$ and shear strain $\gamma_{xy}$:

$$\epsilon_{1,2} = \frac{\epsilon_x + \epsilon_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\epsilon_x - \epsilon_y}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\gamma_{xy}}{2}\right)^2}$$

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy: The Spinning Coin on a Tilted Table**
> 
> Imagine placing a small round coin on a table that has a complex, tilted load distribution underneath. If you look at the coin from one angle, the surface force per unit area (stress) appears to be a confusing mix of pulling and sliding forces (normal + shear).
> 
> However, if you **rotate your viewpoint** to a specific angle, suddenly the coin "sees" only a pure push or pure pull — no sliding at all! That pure push or pull **magnitude** is the **principal stress**, and the orientation at which it occurs is the **principal plane direction**.
> 
> In naval architecture, this is exactly why we care: a ship's hull plating at a given point may experience a complex biaxial stress (from hull girder bending + hydrostatic pressure + local panel bending). To check if the steel will yield, we MUST find the **maximum principal stress** — because yielding depends on the **worst normal stress**, not on any arbitrary direction.

### 1.4 Physical Significance for Naval Architects

In a **ship's hull girder**, the midship section is subject to:
- **Hogging/Sagging** bending → longitudinal normal stress $\sigma_x$
- **Hydrostatic pressure** → transverse normal stress $\sigma_y$
- **Shear forces** → shear stress $\tau_{xy}$ from wave loading and racking

Classification societies (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS) require checking **equivalent von Mises** or **maximum principal stress** against permissible limits. Hence, the ability to extract principal stresses from any given stress state is a **foundational design skill**.

### 1.5 Why "Principal"?

The word *principal* comes from the Latin *princeps* meaning "first" or "chief". These are the **chief (most important) stresses** at a point because:

| Reason | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Magnitude Extremity** | They are the maximum and minimum possible normal stresses obtainable by rotating the axes. |
| **Zero Shear** | On principal planes, the material is being pulled or pushed purely — no tendency to slide. |
| **Material Failure Criterion** | Yielding, cracking, and brittle failure all depend on principal stresses, not arbitrary directional stresses. |
| **Invariance** | Their values do not depend on the orientation of the reference axes chosen. |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Theoretical Foundation — Why Principal Stresses Exist

The Cauchy stress tensor at a point is a **symmetric second-order tensor** in 3D:

$$\boldsymbol{\sigma} = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_x & \tau_{xy} & \tau_{xz} \\ \tau_{xy} & \sigma_y & \tau_{yz} \\ \tau_{xz} & \tau_{yz} & \sigma_z \end{bmatrix}$$

By the **Spectral Theorem** of linear algebra (which applies because the tensor is symmetric and real), there exists an **orthogonal transformation** (rotation of axes) that **diagonalizes** this matrix. The diagonal entries of the diagonalized matrix are the **eigenvalues**, which are precisely the **principal stresses** $\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \sigma_3$.

The corresponding orthogonal directions (eigenvectors) are the **principal directions**.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Principal Stress Derivation (Conceptual)

1. **Start with an arbitrary element** in a 2D stress state: $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$, $\tau_{xy}$.
2. **Rotate the element by an angle $\theta$** to a new orientation.
3. **Compute the transformed normal and shear stresses** on the rotated faces using **stress transformation equations**.
4. **Set the transformed shear stress equal to zero** — this gives the **principal angle** $\theta_p$.
5. **Substitute $\theta_p$ back** into the normal stress transformation to obtain $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$.

### 2.3 The Principal Angle Equation

The orientation of the principal plane is found by setting the rotated shear stress to zero:

$$\tan(2\theta_p) = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$$

This gives **two values of $\theta_p$** separated by **$90°$** — corresponding to the two principal planes (one for $\sigma_1$, one for $\sigma_2$).

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-ready cheat sheet** for Principal Stresses and Strains — Definition level. Memorize these before solving KTU university exam questions.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Units (SI) | Condition / Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Principal Stress (2D) | $\sigma_{1,2} = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | $\text{Pa}$ or $\text{N/mm}^2$ | Always algebraically: $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2$ |
| 2 | Maximum Shear Stress (2D) | $\tau_{\max} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | $\text{Pa}$ or $\text{N/mm}^2$ | $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2}$ |
| 3 | Principal Angle | $\tan(2\theta_p) = \dfrac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$ | radians or degrees | Two values, $90°$ apart |
| 4 | Principal Strain (2D) | $\epsilon_{1,2} = \dfrac{\epsilon_x + \epsilon_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\epsilon_x - \epsilon_y}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{\gamma_{xy}}{2}\right)^2}$ | dimensionless | Same structure as stress formula |
| 5 | Stress Invariant $I_1$ | $I_1 = \sigma_x + \sigma_y = \sigma_1 + \sigma_2$ | $\text{Pa}$ | **Independent of rotation** |
| 6 | Stress Invariant $I_2$ | $I_2 = \sigma_x \sigma_y - \tau_{xy}^2 = \sigma_1 \sigma_2$ | $\text{Pa}^2$ | **Independent of rotation** |
| 7 | Principal Stress Invariant 3D | $I_1 = \sigma_1 + \sigma_2 + \sigma_3$ | $\text{Pa}$ | Trace of stress tensor |
| 8 | Max Shear in 3D | $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}$ | $\text{Pa}$ | Occurs on $45°$ plane between $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_3$ |
| 9 | Average Normal Stress | $\sigma_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}$ | $\text{Pa}$ | Center of Mohr's Circle |
| 10 | Mohr's Circle Radius | $R = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | $\text{Pa}$ | Equals $\tau_{\max}$ in 2D |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Principal Stresses |
|---|---|
| **Naval Architecture** | Hull plating yield assessment, brittle fracture check, fatigue life estimation. |
| **Pressure Vessel Design** | Finding the orientation of maximum stress in cylindrical shells under internal pressure. |
| **Mechanical Design** | Shafts under combined bending and torsion → principal stress check before shaft yield. |
| **Civil Engineering** | Dams, retaining walls, and bridges with complex biaxial loading states. |
| **Aerospace** | Composite laminate stress analysis, where principal direction governs ply failure. |
| **Geotechnical** | Soil element principal stress for Mohr-Coulomb failure criterion. |

### 2.6 The Invariance Principle (Why It Matters)

> [!NOTE]
> The quantities $I_1$ and $I_2$ are called **Stress Invariants** because they remain **unchanged** under any rotation of the reference axes. Since $\sigma_1 + \sigma_2$ and $\sigma_1 \sigma_2$ can be computed from *any* orientation of the stress element, the principal stresses are **intrinsic properties of the point**, not artifacts of how we choose to look at it.

This invariance is what makes principal stresses the **physically meaningful** descriptors of stress state — independent of the engineer's choice of coordinate system.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Principal Stresses from Stress Transformation

We begin with a 2D stress element subjected to $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$, and $\tau_{xy}$ on faces perpendicular to the x and y axes. We wish to find the orientation $\theta_p$ at which the shear stress on the rotated face vanishes.

**Step 1: Consider the equilibrium of a rotated wedge element**

Take a small triangular wedge cut from the original element, with one face inclined at angle $\theta$ to the x-axis. Let the inclined face have area $dA$, the vertical face have area $dA \cos\theta$, and the horizontal face have area $dA \sin\theta$. The stresses acting on the inclined face are the unknown normal stress $\sigma_\theta$ (outward) and shear stress $\tau_\theta$ (acting along the face).

**Step 2: Resolve forces perpendicular to the inclined face (gives $\sigma_\theta$)**

Summing forces in the direction normal to the inclined face:

$$\sigma_\theta \cdot dA = \sigma_x \cdot dA\cos\theta \cdot \cos\theta + \sigma_y \cdot dA\sin\theta \cdot \sin\theta + 2\tau_{xy} \cdot dA\cos\theta \cdot \sin\theta$$

Dividing throughout by $dA$:

$$\sigma_\theta = \sigma_x \cos^2\theta + \sigma_y \sin^2\theta + 2\tau_{xy} \sin\theta \cos\theta$$

**Step 3: Apply the double-angle trigonometric identities**

Recall the identities:
- $\cos^2\theta = \dfrac{1 + \cos(2\theta)}{2}$
- $\sin^2\theta = \dfrac{1 - \cos(2\theta)}{2}$
- $2\sin\theta\cos\theta = \sin(2\theta)$

Substituting these into the $\sigma_\theta$ expression:

$$\sigma_\theta = \sigma_x \cdot \frac{1 + \cos(2\theta)}{2} + \sigma_y \cdot \frac{1 - \cos(2\theta)}{2} + \tau_{xy} \sin(2\theta)$$

Grouping the constant and $\cos(2\theta)$ terms:

$$\sigma_\theta = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\sin(2\theta)$$

**Step 4: Resolve forces parallel to the inclined face (gives $\tau_\theta$)**

A similar equilibrium analysis along the inclined face gives:

$$\tau_\theta = -(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)\sin\theta\cos\theta + \tau_{xy}(\cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta)$$

Applying the same double-angle identities:

$$\tau_\theta = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta)$$

**Step 5: Apply the condition for principal stress — shear stress must vanish**

For a principal plane, $\tau_\theta = 0$. Therefore:

$$0 = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta_p) + \tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta_p)$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta_p) = \tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta_p)$$

Dividing both sides by $\cos(2\theta_p) \cdot \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}$ (assuming $\sigma_x \neq \sigma_y$):

$$\tan(2\theta_p) = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$$

This is the **principal angle equation**.

**Step 6: Find the principal stress values by substituting $\theta_p$**

We need to substitute the value of $\theta_p$ back into the $\sigma_\theta$ expression. To do this efficiently, we use the identity:

$$\cos(2\theta_p) = \pm \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{\sqrt{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + (2\tau_{xy})^2}}$$

$$\sin(2\theta_p) = \pm \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sqrt{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + (2\tau_{xy})^2}}$$

Substituting these into the $\sigma_\theta$ expression:

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + (2\tau_{xy})^2}$$

Simplifying the factor of $\frac{1}{2}$ inside the square root:

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

**This is the principal stress formula.**

**Step 7: Verification of maximum shear stress**

The maximum shear stress in 2D occurs at the angle $45°$ away from the principal plane. Setting $\dfrac{d\tau_\theta}{d\theta} = 0$:

$$\frac{d\tau_\theta}{d\theta} = -(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)\cos(2\theta) - 2\tau_{xy}\sin(2\theta) = 0$$

This gives $\tan(2\theta_s) = -\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2\tau_{xy}}$, which is the **negative reciprocal** of the principal angle equation — confirming that the maximum shear plane is at $45°$ to the principal plane. Substituting back:

$$\tau_{\max} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2}$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison Between Stress and Strain Formulations

| Feature | Principal Stress | Principal Strain |
|---|---|---|
| **Tensor Type** | Cauchy stress tensor $\sigma_{ij}$ | Infinitesimal strain tensor $\epsilon_{ij}$ |
| **Mathematical Nature** | Eigenvalues of stress tensor | Eigenvalues of strain tensor |
| **Symmetry** | $\sigma_{ij} = \sigma_{ji}$ (momentum balance) | $\epsilon_{ij} = \epsilon_{ji}$ (definition) |
| **Principal Equation (2D)** | $\sigma_{1,2} = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | $\epsilon_{1,2} = \dfrac{\epsilon_x + \epsilon_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\epsilon_x - \epsilon_y}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{\gamma_{xy}}{2}\right)^2}$ |
| **Principal Angle** | $\tan(2\theta_p) = \dfrac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$ | $\tan(2\theta_p) = \dfrac{\gamma_{xy}}{\epsilon_x - \epsilon_y}$ |
| **Zero Condition** | Shear stress $\tau_\theta = 0$ | Shear strain $\dfrac{\gamma_\theta}{2} = 0$ |
| **Max Shear** | $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2}$ | $\dfrac{\gamma_{\max}}{2} = \dfrac{\epsilon_1 - \epsilon_2}{2}$ |
| **Physical Meaning** | Direction of pure normal stress | Direction of pure normal strain |
| **Unit** | $\text{Pa} = \text{N/m}^2$ | dimensionless (m/m) |

### 3.3 Special Cases — Useful for KTU Problems

**Case 1: Uniaxial Stress ($\sigma_y = 0, \tau_{xy} = 0$)**
$$\sigma_1 = \sigma_x, \quad \sigma_2 = 0$$

**Case 2: Pure Shear ($\sigma_x = \sigma_y = 0, \tau_{xy} = \tau$)**
$$\sigma_1 = +\tau, \quad \sigma_2 = -\tau, \quad \theta_p = 45°$$

**Case 3: Hydrostatic Stress ($\sigma_x = \sigma_y = -p, \tau_{xy} = 0$)**
$$\sigma_1 = \sigma_2 = -p, \quad \theta_p \text{ is indeterminate (any direction)}$$

**Case 4: Equal Biaxial Tension ($\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma, \tau_{xy} = 0$)**
$$\sigma_1 = \sigma_2 = \sigma, \quad \theta_p \text{ is indeterminate}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip**: In Cases 3 and 4, the principal directions are **indeterminate** because the stress state is **isotropic** — every direction looks the same. The student should state this explicitly in the answer for full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram — The Principal Stress Concept

The following block diagram shows the logical flow of how we go from an arbitrary stress state to principal stresses, and the role of Mohr's circle in this transformation.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Arbitrary 2D Stress State at a Point] --> B[Stress Tensor sigma_ij in x-y frame]
    B --> C[Apply Stress Transformation Equations]
    C --> D[Rotate element by angle theta]
    D --> E{Set Shear Stress tau_theta = 0}
    E --> F[Solve for principal angle theta_p]
    F --> G[Substitute theta_p back into sigma_theta]
    G --> H[Principal Stress sigma_1 - Maximum]
    G --> I[Principal Stress sigma_2 - Minimum]
    H --> J[Principal Direction n1]
    I --> K[Principal Direction n2]
    J --> L[Final Output: Orthogonal Principal Basis]
    K --> L
    B --> M[Alternative Path: Mohrs Circle Construction]
    M --> N[Plot points X and Y on sigma-tau plane]
    N --> O[Draw circle with center at sigma_avg]
    O --> P[Read sigma_1 and sigma_2 as circle intercepts]
    P --> H
    P --> I
```

### 4.2 Mohr's Circle Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Point X: sigma_x, -tau_xy] --> C[Center of Circle at sigma_avg, 0]
    P2[Point Y: sigma_y, +tau_xy] --> C
    C --> R[Radius R = tau_max]
    R --> S1[Right Intercept: sigma_1 Maximum Principal]
    R --> S2[Left Intercept: sigma_2 Minimum Principal]
    R --> T1[Top of Circle: +tau_max]
    R --> T2[Bottom of Circle: -tau_max]
    S1 --> OUT[Output: Principal Stress Magnitudes]
    S2 --> OUT
```

### 4.3 3D Principal Stress State — Eigenvalue Visualization

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Principal Stress sigma_1 - Along n1] --> V1[Direction n1 - Eigenvector 1]
    S2[Principal Stress sigma_2 - Along n2] --> V2[Direction n2 - Eigenvector 2]
    S3[Principal Stress sigma_3 - Along n3] --> V3[Direction n3 - Eigenvector 3]
    V1 --> ORTH[Orthogonal Coordinate System]
    V2 --> ORTH
    V3 --> ORTH
    ORTH --> INV[Invariants I1, I2, I3 Independent of Frame]
    S1 --> INV
    S2 --> INV
    S3 --> INV
    INV --> APP[Engineering Application: Yield Check, Fatigue Analysis]
```

### 4.4 Hull Plating Stress State — Naval Architecture Application

```mermaid
graph TD
    HG[Hull Girder Bending] --> S1[Longitudinal Stress sigma_x]
    HP[Hydrostatic Pressure] --> S2[Transverse Stress sigma_y]
    WL[Wave Loading - Shear] --> S3[In-plane Shear tau_xy]
    S1 --> COMB[Combined Stress Element on Hull Plate]
    S2 --> COMB
    S3 --> COMB
    COMB --> PS[Apply Principal Stress Formula]
    PS --> COMP[Compare sigma_1 with Allowable per Classification Society]
    COMP --> DEC{sigma_1 less than allowable?}
    DEC -->|Yes| SAFE[Plate Design is SAFE]
    DEC -->|No| REDESIGN[REDESIGN - Increase Plate Thickness or Stiffener Spacing]
```

### 4.5 Comparison: Principal Stress vs Principal Strain — Block Mapping

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph STRESS_DOMAIN
        A1[Stress Tensor sigma_ij] --> A2[Eigenvalues sigma_1, sigma_2, sigma_3]
        A2 --> A3[Principal Planes with zero shear]
    end
    subgraph STRAIN_DOMAIN
        B1[Strain Tensor epsilon_ij] --> B2[Eigenvalues epsilon_1, epsilon_2, epsilon_3]
        B2 --> B3[Principal Directions with zero shear strain]
    end
    A3 -.Analogous.-> B3
    A1 -.Hookes Law Connection.-> B1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block Diagram Reading Note:** In the diagrams above, arrows represent the **logical flow of computation** or **cause-effect relationships**, not physical force vectors. The output of each block is the **input to the next**, allowing the reader to trace how a stress state is decomposed into its principal components.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Define the terms: (i) Principal stresses, (ii) Principal planes, and (iii) Principal directions. State the condition that identifies a principal plane.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **(i) Principal Stresses [1 mark]:** The maximum and minimum normal stresses occurring at a point in a stressed material, obtained on mutually perpendicular planes where the shear stress is zero. Denoted as $\sigma_1$ (maximum) and $\sigma_2$ (minimum) in 2D, and $\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \sigma_3$ in 3D.
>
> **(ii) Principal Planes [1 mark]:** The planes on which the principal stresses act. These planes are mutually perpendicular (orthogonal) at the point of consideration, and the shear stress on each of these planes is identically zero.
>
> **(iii) Principal Directions [0.5 mark]:** The directions normal to the principal planes. They represent the orientations along which only pure normal stress (or pure normal strain) exists, with no shear component.
>
> **Condition for Principal Plane [0.5 mark]:** The condition that identifies a principal plane is that the **shear stress on that plane must be zero** ($\tau_\theta = 0$). Mathematically, this is satisfied at the principal angle:
> $$\tan(2\theta_p) = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**What are principal strains? How are they related to principal stresses for a linearly elastic isotropic material?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition [1.5 marks]:** Principal strains are the normal strains at a point acting along three mutually perpendicular directions (principal directions) on which the **shear strain is zero**. They are the **eigenvalues of the strain tensor** $\epsilon_{ij}$ and represent the maximum and minimum normal strain magnitudes that can be obtained at a point by rotating the reference frame.
>
> For a 2D state:
> $$\epsilon_{1,2} = \frac{\epsilon_x + \epsilon_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\epsilon_x - \epsilon_y}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\gamma_{xy}}{2}\right)^2}$$
>
> **Relation to Principal Stresses for Linear Elastic Isotropic Material [1.5 marks]:** By **Generalized Hooke's Law**, for a homogeneous, isotropic, linearly elastic material with Young's modulus $E$ and Poisson's ratio $\nu$:
> $$\epsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3)\right]$$
> $$\epsilon_2 = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_2 - \nu(\sigma_1 + \sigma_3)\right]$$
> $$\epsilon_3 = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_3 - \nu(\sigma_1 + \sigma_2)\right]$$
>
> For a 2D plane stress case (where $\sigma_3 = 0$):
> $$\epsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_1 - \nu\sigma_2), \quad \epsilon_2 = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_2 - \nu\sigma_1)$$
>
> **Important Note:** The **principal directions of stress and strain coincide** for isotropic materials — this is a key property that simplifies combined stress analysis in naval structural design.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

**QUESTION A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the principal stresses in a 2D stress state. Clearly state the assumption used in the derivation and identify the principal angle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** At a point on the surface of a ship's hull plate, the stresses measured with respect to a set of rectangular axes are: $\sigma_x = 80 \text{ MPa}$ (tension), $\sigma_y = -40 \text{ MPa}$ (compression), and $\tau_{xy} = 30 \text{ MPa}$. Determine:
  - (i) The magnitudes and directions of the principal stresses.
  - (ii) The maximum shear stress and its orientation. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Derivation [7 Marks]:**

Refer to the complete derivation provided in **Section 3.1** of these notes (Steps 1 through 7). The key results are:

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define the inclined wedge with face area $dA$, and write force equilibrium normal to the inclined face.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Apply the double-angle identities to obtain:
$$\sigma_\theta = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\sin(2\theta)$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Similarly, derive the shear stress transformation:
$$\tau_\theta = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta)$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Set $\tau_\theta = 0$ for the principal plane condition. Solve to obtain:
$$\tan(2\theta_p) = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$$

**Step 5 (1.5 marks):** Substitute the principal angle back into $\sigma_\theta$ to obtain:
$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

**Assumption used:** Plane stress condition, equilibrium of a small wedge element, and static equilibrium under body-force-free conditions.

**Principal angle identification:** The angle $\theta_p$ from $\tan(2\theta_p) = \dfrac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$ gives the orientation of the principal plane. There are two values separated by $90°$, corresponding to $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 Marks]:**

**Given:**
- $\sigma_x = 80 \text{ MPa}$, $\sigma_y = -40 \text{ MPa}$, $\tau_{xy} = 30 \text{ MPa}$

**Part (i) — Principal Stresses and Directions [4 marks]:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute the average normal stress:
$$\sigma_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} = \frac{80 + (-40)}{2} = \frac{40}{2} = 20 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute the radius of Mohr's circle (which equals half the difference of principal stresses):
$$R = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{80 - (-40)}{2}\right)^2 + (30)^2}$$

$$R = \sqrt{(60)^2 + (30)^2} = \sqrt{3600 + 900} = \sqrt{4500}$$

$$R = \sqrt{4500} = \sqrt{900 \times 5} = 30\sqrt{5} \approx 67.08 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Compute principal stresses:
$$\sigma_1 = \sigma_{\text{avg}} + R = 20 + 30\sqrt{5} = 20 + 67.08 \approx 87.08 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_2 = \sigma_{\text{avg}} - R = 20 - 30\sqrt{5} = 20 - 67.08 \approx -47.08 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Compute the principal angle:
$$\tan(2\theta_p) = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y} = \frac{2 \times 30}{80 - (-40)} = \frac{60}{120} = 0.5$$

$$2\theta_p = \tan^{-1}(0.5) = 26.57°$$

$$\theta_p = 13.28° \text{ (counter-clockwise from x-axis to direction of } \sigma_1\text{)}$$

**Part (ii) — Maximum Shear Stress and Orientation [3 marks]:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Maximum shear stress:
$$\tau_{\max} = R = 30\sqrt{5} \approx 67.08 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Orientation of maximum shear stress (rotated $45°$ from principal direction):
$$\theta_s = \theta_p + 45° = 13.28° + 45° = 58.28°$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Normal stress on the plane of maximum shear:
$$\sigma_{\text{on max shear plane}} = \sigma_{\text{avg}} = 20 \text{ MPa}$$

**Final Answer Box for Q-A:**
- $\sigma_1 = 87.08 \text{ MPa (tension)}$ at $\theta_p = 13.28°$
- $\sigma_2 = -47.08 \text{ MPa (compression)}$ at $\theta_p + 90° = 103.28°$
- $\tau_{\max} = 67.08 \text{ MPa}$ at $\theta_s = 58.28°$

---

**QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**

**(a)** Define the term **stress invariant**. Show that the sum and product of principal stresses are invariants of the stress tensor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a stress element in a ship deck plating, the following values are measured: $\sigma_x = 120 \text{ MPa}$, $\sigma_y = 60 \text{ MPa}$, $\tau_{xy} = 25 \text{ MPa}$. Calculate:
  - (i) The principal stresses.
  - (ii) The principal angle.
  - (iii) Verify the invariance of $I_1$ and $I_2$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Definition and Proof of Stress Invariants [7 Marks]:**

**Definition [2 marks]:** A **stress invariant** is a quantity computed from the components of the stress tensor at a point whose value **does not change** when the coordinate axes are rotated. Invariants are intrinsic to the stress state at the point, independent of the observer's frame of reference.

**Proof that $I_1 = \sigma_x + \sigma_y = \sigma_1 + \sigma_2$ is invariant [2.5 marks]:**

From the principal stress formula:
$$\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 = \left[\frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + R\right] + \left[\frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} - R\right] = \sigma_x + \sigma_y$$

The terms involving $R$ (which contains $\theta_p$) **cancel out**, leaving only the original components. Therefore, $\sigma_1 + \sigma_2$ depends only on $\sigma_x + \sigma_y$, which is independent of orientation. Hence, $I_1$ is an invariant.

**Proof that $I_2 = \sigma_x\sigma_y - \tau_{xy}^2 = \sigma_1\sigma_2$ is invariant [2.5 marks]:**

Using the standard product expansion:
$$\sigma_1 \cdot \sigma_2 = \left(\frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 - \left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 - \tau_{xy}^2$$

Expanding the first two terms:
$$\sigma_1 \cdot \sigma_2 = \frac{(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)^2 - (\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2}{4} - \tau_{xy}^2$$

The numerator simplifies to:
$$(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)^2 - (\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 = [\sigma_x^2 + 2\sigma_x\sigma_y + \sigma_y^2] - [\sigma_x^2 - 2\sigma_x\sigma_y + \sigma_y^2] = 4\sigma_x\sigma_y$$

Therefore:
$$\sigma_1 \cdot \sigma_2 = \frac{4\sigma_x\sigma_y}{4} - \tau_{xy}^2 = \sigma_x\sigma_y - \tau_{xy}^2$$

This shows that $\sigma_1\sigma_2$ depends only on $\sigma_x\sigma_y$ and $\tau_{xy}^2$, which are frame-independent. Hence, $I_2$ is an invariant.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 Marks]:**

**Given:**
- $\sigma_x = 120 \text{ MPa}$, $\sigma_y = 60 \text{ MPa}$, $\tau_{xy} = 25 \text{ MPa}$

**Part (i) — Principal Stresses [3 marks]:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Average stress:
$$\sigma_{\text{avg}} = \frac{120 + 60}{2} = 90 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Radius:
$$R = \sqrt{\left(\frac{120 - 60}{2}\right)^2 + (25)^2} = \sqrt{(30)^2 + 625} = \sqrt{900 + 625} = \sqrt{1525}$$

$$R = \sqrt{1525} \approx 39.05 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Principal stresses:
$$\sigma_1 = 90 + 39.05 = 129.05 \text{ MPa (tension)}$$

$$\sigma_2 = 90 - 39.05 = 50.95 \text{ MPa (tension)}$$

**Part (ii) — Principal Angle [2 marks]:**

$$\tan(2\theta_p) = \frac{2 \times 25}{120 - 60} = \frac{50}{60} = 0.8333$$

$$2\theta_p = \tan^{-1}(0.8333) = 39.81°$$

$$\theta_p = 19.90° \approx 19.9° \text{ (CCW from x-axis to } \sigma_1 \text{ direction)}$$

**Part (iii) — Verification of Invariance [2 marks]:**

**Compute $I_1$ from original components:**
$$I_1 = \sigma_x + \sigma_y = 120 + 60 = 180 \text{ MPa}$$

**Compute $I_1$ from principal stresses:**
$$I_1 = \sigma_1 + \sigma_2 = 129.05 + 50.95 = 180.00 \text{ MPa} \checkmark$$

**Compute $I_2$ from original components:**
$$I_2 = \sigma_x\sigma_y - \tau_{xy}^2 = (120)(60) - (25)^2 = 7200 - 625 = 6575 \text{ MPa}^2$$

**Compute $I_2$ from principal stresses:**
$$I_2 = \sigma_1 \cdot \sigma_2 = (129.05)(50.95) = 6575.10 \text{ MPa}^2 \checkmark$$

(The small difference is due to rounding of $\sqrt{1525}$.)

**Final Answer Box for Q-B:**
- $\sigma_1 = 129.05 \text{ MPa}$, $\sigma_2 = 50.95 \text{ MPa}$
- $\theta_p = 19.9°$ (CCW from x-axis)
- $I_1 = 180 \text{ MPa}$ (verified), $I_2 = 6575 \text{ MPa}^2$ (verified)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Sign Convention Error [−1 mark typical]:** Students frequently forget that $\sigma_2$ is the *algebraically minimum* (not the minimum magnitude) principal stress. If $\sigma_y$ is given as compression (negative), it may still be the larger of the two in magnitude but is $\sigma_2$ algebraically.
> 
> 2. **Angle Range Mistake [−2 marks typical]:** When computing $\theta_p$ from $\tan(2\theta_p) = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$, the student must check whether $\theta_p$ or $\theta_p + 90°$ corresponds to $\sigma_1$. Use the sign of $\frac{d\sigma_\theta}{d\theta}$ at $\theta_p$ to determine the maximum.
> 
> 3. **Units Inconsistency [−1 mark typical]:** Always write units clearly. If $\sigma_x$ is in MPa, $\tau_{xy}$ must also be in MPa. The principal stress result is in MPa.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting the "Principal" Identification [−1 mark typical]:** State explicitly that the values obtained are called "principal stresses" and identify which is $\sigma_1$ and which is $\sigma_2$. Don't just leave numerical values.
> 
> 5. **Not Verifying Invariant [−1 mark typical]:** In problems where invariants are mentioned, the student should **verify** $I_1$ and $I_2$ by computing them from both the original and the principal components.
> 
> 6. **Confusion Between $\theta_p$ for $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$ [−1 mark typical]:** Both principal directions are perpendicular; if $\theta_{p1}$ corresponds to $\sigma_1$, then $\theta_{p2} = \theta_{p1} + 90°$ corresponds to $\sigma_2$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Principal Stresses and Strains: Definition**

#### Core Definitions
- **Principal Stresses** ($\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \sigma_3$): Normal stresses on planes where shear stress is zero.
- **Principal Planes**: Mutually perpendicular planes carrying only normal stress, no shear stress.
- **Principal Directions**: The unit vectors normal to the principal planes — these are the **eigenvectors** of the stress tensor.
- **Principal Strains** ($\epsilon_1, \epsilon_2, \epsilon_3$): Normal strains along directions where shear strain vanishes.

#### The Two Pillars — Must Memorize
- **Principal Stress Formula (2D):**
  $$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$
- **Principal Angle Formula:**
  $$\tan(2\theta_p) = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$$

#### Invariants (Rotation-Independent Quantities)
- $I_1 = \sigma_1 + \sigma_2 = \sigma_x + \sigma_y$ (sum invariant)
- $I_2 = \sigma_1 \sigma_2 = \sigma_x\sigma_y - \tau_{xy}^2$ (product invariant)
- For 3D: $I_1 = \sigma_1 + \sigma_2 + \sigma_3 = \sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z$

#### Maximum Shear Stress
- 2D: $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2}$
- 3D: $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}$ (absolute max)
- Occurs on planes oriented at **$45°$** to the principal planes.

#### Algebraic Ordering Convention (KTU Standard)
- $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \sigma_3$ (most positive to most negative)
- $\epsilon_1 \geq \epsilon_2 \geq \epsilon_3$

#### Special Cases — Recognize Quickly
- **Pure shear** ($\sigma_x = \sigma_y = 0$): $\sigma_1 = \tau$, $\sigma_2 = -\tau$, $\theta_p = 45°$.
- **Uniaxial stress** ($\sigma_y = \tau_{xy} = 0$): $\sigma_1 = \sigma_x$, $\sigma_2 = 0$.
- **Isotropic state** ($\sigma_x = \sigma_y$, $\tau_{xy} = 0$): Indeterminate principal direction.

#### Relationship Between Principal Stress and Principal Strain
- For **linear elastic isotropic** material, the **principal directions of stress and strain coincide**.
- Generalized Hooke's law: $\epsilon_1 = \dfrac{1}{E}[\sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3)]$

#### Mohr's Circle Memory Aid
- **Center** of circle = $\sigma_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}$
- **Radius** of circle = $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2}$
- **Right intercept** of circle (on $\sigma$-axis) = $\sigma_1$
- **Left intercept** of circle (on $\sigma$-axis) = $\sigma_2$

#### Physical Significance
- Principal stresses govern **material failure** (yield, fracture, fatigue).
- They are **intrinsic** to the point — not artifacts of coordinate choice.
- In **ship structural design**, $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$ are checked against classification society allowables.

#### Exam-Day Quick Tips
1. Always **check the sign convention** before plugging in.
2. Always **state which value is $\sigma_1$ and which is $\sigma_2$**.
3. For **angle determination**, verify by substituting $\theta_p$ back into $\sigma_\theta$ to confirm which is maximum.
4. If $\sigma_x = \sigma_y$ and $\tau_{xy} \neq 0$, then $\theta_p = 45°$ directly.
5. If $\sigma_x = \sigma_y$ and $\tau_{xy} = 0$, then **all directions are principal** — isotropic state.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mohr's Circle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST303 - SOLID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Mohr's Circle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Mohr's Circle** is a two-dimensional graphical-analytical representation of the **stress transformation equations** for a material point under plane stress. Proposed by the German civil engineer **Christian Otto Mohr (1882)**, it is a geometric locus of all possible normal stress ($\sigma_{n}$) and shear stress ($\tau$) components acting on every possible inclined plane passing through a given point in a stressed body.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> "Mohr's circle is a graphical method to determine the normal and shear stresses on an inclined plane, principal stresses, maximum shear stress, and the corresponding planes of orientation from the known stress state $(\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \tau_{xy})$ at a point."

In **Naval Architecture**, Mohr's Circle is indispensable for analyzing:
- **Hull plating** under combined longitudinal bending and hydrodynamic pressure.
- **Transverse frames and girders** subjected to combined shear and bending.
- **Biaxial stress states** in ship structural members (deck plates, side shells, bottom shells).
- **Plate and stiffener intersections** in a stressed hull girder.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Physical Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Spinning Pizza Dough"**
> Imagine a tiny square element (a "pizza slice") cut out of a ship's hull plate. This square is sitting at some angle. Now imagine you slowly rotate this square through every possible angle from $0°$ to $180°$ while the surrounding hull material pushes and pulls on its four faces. The stresses on its faces change continuously. If you plot the *normal stress* (pushing perpendicular to the face) on the horizontal axis and the *shear stress* (sliding along the face) on the vertical axis for every orientation, the tip of this vector traces a perfect **circle** — that circle is **Mohr's Circle**.

This powerful visualization instantly reveals the *extreme* values:
- The **rightmost point** of the circle = **Maximum Principal Stress** ($\sigma_1$).
- The **leftmost point** = **Minimum Principal Stress** ($\sigma_2$).
- The **topmost** and **bottommost** points = **Maximum Shear Stress** ($\tau_{max}$).

## 1.3 Physical Units and Sign Convention

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Unit (SI) | Shipbuilding Unit |
|----------|--------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Normal Stress | $\sigma$ | $\text{Pa} = \text{N/m}^2$ | $\text{MPa}$ (typical hull steel) |
| Shear Stress | $\tau$ | $\text{Pa} = \text{N/m}^2$ | $\text{MPa}$ |
| Angle | $\theta$ | radians (rad) | degrees (°) |
| Stress Radius | $R$ | $\text{MPa}$ | $\text{MPa}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Convention (must be strictly followed):**
> - **Tensile stresses** are **POSITIVE** ($\sigma > 0$).
> - **Compressive stresses** are **NEGATIVE** ($\sigma < 0$).
> - **Shear stress is POSITIVE** if it produces **counter-clockwise rotation** of the element.
> - Angles $\theta$ are measured **counter-clockwise** from the reference $x$-axis to the outward normal of the inclined plane.
> - On Mohr's Circle, angles are **DOUBLED** ($2\theta$) and **rotation direction is REVERSED** (clockwise on Mohr's circle = counter-clockwise in physical space). This reversal is the most error-prone point for students.

## 1.4 Why Mohr's Circle Matters for Naval Architects

A ship at sea experiences a **complex multi-axial stress state**:
- **Hogging/Sagging** of the hull girder produces longitudinal bending stresses ($\sigma_x$).
- **Hydrostatic pressure** produces transverse stresses ($\sigma_y$) on the hull plating.
- **Shear forces** at supports (e.g., at midship from wave loading) produce shear stresses ($\tau_{xy}$).

The designer must identify the **critical planes** (where failure is most likely) — these are precisely the **principal stress planes** (where shear is zero) and the **maximum shear planes** (inclined at 45° to principal directions). **Mohr's Circle provides a fast, visual, and computationally efficient path** to find these critical values, which is why it is a cornerstone of the **KR/ABS/LR/DNV Ship Structural Rules** design workflow.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Stress State (Plane Stress Assumption)

We consider a 2D differential element of hull plating in the $x$–$y$ plane, subjected to:
- Normal stress $\sigma_x$ on the $x$-face.
- Normal stress $\sigma_y$ on the $y$-face.
- Shear stress $\tau_{xy}$ on the $x$-face (with complementary $\tau_{yx} = \tau_{xy}$ on the $y$-face by moment equilibrium).

> [!NOTE]
> **Plane Stress Assumption:** This is valid for thin plating where the out-of-plane stress $\sigma_z \approx 0$. In a ship's hull plate (typical thickness $t = 10\text{–}30\,\text{mm}$), this holds to excellent engineering accuracy.

## 2.2 Stress Transformation Equations (The Foundation)

When the element is rotated by an angle $\theta$ (counter-clockwise) to a new orientation $x'$–$y'$, the stresses on the new faces are given by the **transformation equations**:

$$
\sigma_{x'} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\sin(2\theta)
$$

$$
\sigma_{y'} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} - \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos(2\theta) - \tau_{xy}\sin(2\theta)
$$

$$
\tau_{x'y'} = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key observation:** $\sigma_{x'} + \sigma_{y'} = \sigma_x + \sigma_y$ (always). This is the **Invariance of the First Stress Invariant** $I_1$. Physically, the sum of normal stresses on perpendicular faces is independent of orientation.

## 2.3 Construction Logic of Mohr's Circle — Step by Step

- **Step 1 — Establish the Stress Plane:** Plot $\sigma$ (normal stress) along the **horizontal axis** and $\tau$ (shear stress) along the **vertical axis**. **NOTE**: In KTU convention, $\tau$ is plotted **downward positive** for clockwise shear in physical space (or upward positive — always check the sign convention used in class).

- **Step 2 — Plot the X-Face Point ($X$):** Coordinates are $(\sigma_x, -\tau_{xy})$ in KTU convention (or $(\sigma_x, +\tau_{xy})$ in some books). This is the "pole" for rotation.

- **Step 3 — Plot the Y-Face Point ($Y$):** Coordinates are $(\sigma_y, +\tau_{xy})$ (opposite shear sign). Notice that $X$ and $Y$ are always **diametrically opposite** on the circle.

- **Step 4 — Locate the Center $C$:** Center lies on the $\sigma$-axis at:
$$C = \left(\frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2},\ 0\right)$$

- **Step 5 — Determine the Radius $R$:** Distance from $C$ to point $X$ (or $Y$):
$$R = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^{2} + \tau_{xy}^{2}}$$

- **Step 6 — Draw the Circle:** With center $C$ and radius $R$.

- **Step 7 — Identify Critical Points:**
  - **Rightmost point** of circle = $\sigma_1$ (major principal stress).
  - **Leftmost point** of circle = $\sigma_2$ (minor principal stress).
  - **Topmost/Bottommost points** = $\tau_{max}$ (maximum in-plane shear stress).

## 2.4 Principal Stresses and Their Orientations

**Principal Stresses** are the **extreme normal stresses** (where shear is zero). From the geometry of the circle:

$$
\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm R = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^{2} + \tau_{xy}^{2}}
$$

The angle to the **major principal plane** is found by the **principal angle** $\theta_p$:

$$
\tan(2\theta_p) = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}
$$

The angle to the **maximum shear plane** is always $\theta_s = \theta_p \pm 45°$.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This formula table is the **single most important reference** for KTU 2024 ESE and internal assessments. Memorize the structure; derive the details.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Stress Transformation (Normal) | $\sigma_{x'} = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\sin(2\theta)$ | $\text{MPa}$ | Valid for plane stress |
| 2 | Stress Transformation (Shear) | $\tau_{x'y'} = -\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta)$ | $\text{MPa}$ | Sign is KTU convention |
| 3 | First Stress Invariant | $I_1 = \sigma_x + \sigma_y = \sigma_{x'} + \sigma_{y'} = \sigma_1 + \sigma_2$ | $\text{MPa}$ | Orientation-independent |
| 4 | Mohr's Circle Center | $C = \left(\sigma_{avg},\ 0\right) = \left(\dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2},\ 0\right)$ | $(\text{MPa},\ \text{MPa})$ | Lies on $\sigma$-axis |
| 5 | Mohr's Circle Radius | $R = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^{2} + \tau_{xy}^{2}}$ | $\text{MPa}$ | Always positive |
| 6 | Principal Stresses | $\sigma_{1,2} = \sigma_{avg} \pm R$ | $\text{MPa}$ | $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2$ (algebraically) |
| 7 | Maximum In-Plane Shear | $\tau_{max} = R = \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2}$ | $\text{MPa}$ | Occurs at $\theta_s = \theta_p \pm 45°$ |
| 8 | Principal Angle | $\tan(2\theta_p) = \dfrac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$ | $\text{rad}$ or $°$ | $2\theta_p$ measured on Mohr's circle |
| 9 | Absolute Maximum Shear (3D) | $\tau_{abs,max} = \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}$ | $\text{MPa}$ | $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \sigma_3$ |
| 10 | Pole Relation | $\overline{XX'} = $ same vector direction (used for finding rotated plane stress) | — | The "Pole Method" |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Naval / Marine Application | Mohr's Circle Use |
|----------------------------|-------------------|
| **Hull girder bending** | Compute principal stresses at deck, bottom, and side shell from $\sigma_x$ (bending) + $\sigma_y$ (hydrostatic) + $\tau_{xy}$ (shear). |
| **Plate panel buckling** (DNV-RP-C201) | Find principal compressive stress to check against buckling allowables. |
| **Fillet weld design** (AWS D1.1 / ISO 12215) | Resolve multi-axial stresses into principal stresses to compute vector force on weld throat. |
| **Bracket and lug connections** | Identify principal planes for fatigue analysis (hot-spot stress at welded joints). |
| **Propeller blade root** | Combined bending + torsion → use Mohr's circle to get $\sigma_1$, $\sigma_2$, $\tau_{max}$. |
