Flexural rigidity:

$$EI = 200 \times 10^9 \times 6.667 \times 10^{-5} = 1.333 \times 10^7\,\text{N·m}^2$$

---

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Slope and Deflection Equations (7 marks)

**Step 1: Reactions** (by symmetry, each support carries half the total load):

$$R_A = R_B = \frac{wL}{2} = \frac{20 \times 4}{2} = 40\,\text{kN}$$

**Step 2: Bending Moment at Section $x$ from $A$:**

$$M(x) = R_A \cdot x - w \cdot x \cdot \frac{x}{2} = \frac{wL}{2} x - \frac{w x^2}{2} = \frac{w}{2}(Lx - x^2)$$

**Step 3: Governing Equation:**

$$EI \frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = \frac{w}{2}(Lx - x^2)$$

**Step 4: First Integration (Slope):**

$$EI \frac{dy}{dx} = \int \frac{w}{2}(Lx - x^2)\, dx + C_1$$

$$EI \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{w}{2}\left( \frac{L x^2}{2} - \frac{x^3}{3} \right) + C_1 = \frac{wL x^2}{4} - \frac{w x^3}{6} + C_1$$

**Step 5: Second Integration (Deflection):**

$$EI y = \int \left( \frac{wL x^2}{4} - \frac{w x^3}{6} + C_1 \right) dx$$

$$EI y = \frac{wL x^3}{12} - \frac{w x^4}{24} + C_1 x + C_2$$

**Step 6: Boundary Conditions:**

**BC 1:** At $x = 0$ (Support $A$), $y = 0$:

$$0 = 0 - 0 + 0 + C_2 \implies C_2 = 0$$

**BC 2 (Symmetry):** At $x = L/2$ (mid-span), slope is zero:

$$EI \frac{dy}{dx}\bigg|_{x=L/2} = 0$$

$$0 = \frac{wL}{4}\left( \frac{L}{2} \right)^2 - \frac{w}{6}\left( \frac{L}{2} \right)^3 + C_1$$

$$0 = \frac{wL^3}{16} - \frac{wL^3}{48} + C_1$$

$$0 = wL^3 \left( \frac{3 - 1}{48} \right) + C_1 = \frac{2 wL^3}{48} + C_1 = \frac{wL^3}{24} + C_1$$

$$C_1 = -\frac{wL^3}{24}$$

**Step 7: Final Equations:**

$$\boxed{EI \theta = \frac{wL x^2}{4} - \frac{w x^3}{6} - \frac{wL^3}{24}}$$

$$\boxed{EI y = \frac{wL x^3}{12} - \frac{w x^4}{24} - \frac{wL^3 x}{24}}$$

Or equivalently:

$$\theta(x) = \frac{w}{24 EI}(6 L x^2 - 4 x^3 - L^3)$$

$$y(x) = \frac{w x}{24 EI}(L^3 - 2 L x^2 + x^3)$$

> **(7 marks: 2 for M(x), 2 for integrations, 2 for BCs, 1 for final equations)**

---

#### Part (b) — Maximum Slope and Deflection (7 marks)

**Maximum Deflection at Mid-Span ($x = L/2$):**

By symmetry, $y_{\max}$ occurs at $x = L/2$:

$$y_{\max} = \frac{w \cdot (L/2)}{24 EI} \left( L^3 - 2 L \left( \frac{L}{2} \right)^2 + \left( \frac{L}{2} \right)^3 \right)$$

$$y_{\max} = \frac{wL}{48 EI} \left( L^3 - 2 L \cdot \frac{L^2}{4} + \frac{L^3}{8} \right)$$

$$y_{\max} = \frac{wL}{48 EI} \left( L^3 - \frac{L^3}{2} + \frac{L^3}{8} \right)$$

$$y_{\max} = \frac{wL}{48 EI} \left( \frac{8L^3 - 4L^3 + L^3}{8} \right) = \frac{wL}{48 EI} \cdot \frac{5L^3}{8}$$

$$\boxed{y_{\max} = \frac{5 w L^4}{384 EI}}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

$$y_{\max} = \frac{5 \times 20 \times 10^3 \times (4)^4}{384 \times 1.333 \times 10^7}$$

$$y_{\max} = \frac{5 \times 20{,}000 \times 256}{5.12 \times 10^9} = \frac{2.56 \times 10^7}{5.12 \times 10^9} = 5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$$

$$\boxed{y_{\max} = 5\,\text{mm}}$$

**Maximum Slope at Supports ($x = 0$ and $x = L$):**

$$\theta_{\max} = \theta\bigg|_{x=0} = \frac{w}{24 EI}(0 - 0 - L^3) = -\frac{w L^3}{24 EI}$$

$$\boxed{\vert \theta_{\max} \vert = \frac{w L^3}{24 EI}}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

$$\vert \theta_{\max} \vert = \frac{20 \times 10^3 \times (4)^3}{24 \times 1.333 \times 10^7} = \frac{20{,}000 \times 64}{3.2 \times 10^8} = \frac{1.28 \times 10^6}{3.2 \times 10^8} = 4 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{rad}$$

$$\boxed{\vert \theta_{\max} \vert = 0.004\,\text{rad} = 4 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{rad}}$$

> **(7 marks: 3 for setting up the evaluation, 2 for $y_{\max}$ with substitution, 2 for $\theta_{\max}$ with substitution)**

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – KTU Model Paper Style]**

A cantilever beam of length $L = 3\,\text{m}$ is fixed at $A$ and free at $B$. A concentrated load $P = 15\,\text{kN}$ acts at the free end $B$. The flexural rigidity is $EI = 9 \times 10^6\,\text{N·m}^2$.

**(a)** Using the **Double Integration Method**, find the **slope and deflection at the free end $B$**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the same load is **uniformly distributed** with intensity $w = 10\,\text{kN/m}$ over the entire span, find the **slope and deflection at the free end $B$** using the Double Integration Method. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B

**Given:**
- $L = 3\,\text{m}$
- $P = 15\,\text{kN} = 15 \times 10^3\,\text{N}$
- $EI = 9 \times 10^6\,\text{N·m}^2$
- $w = 10\,\text{kN/m} = 10 \times 10^3\,\text{N/m}$ (for part b)

---

#### Part (a) — Cantilever with Point Load at Free End (7 marks)

**Step 1: Bending Moment at Section $x$ (measured from fixed end $A$):**

Considering the section to the right of $x$ (from $x$ to $L$):

$$M(x) = -P(L - x)$$

(Note: The negative sign arises from the cantilever's hogging moment; using the convention $EI \frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = M(x)$ with $M$ positive when sagging.)

Alternatively, taking moments to the left:

$$M(x) = -P(L - x) \quad \text{(hogging)}$$

**Step 2: Governing Equation:**

$$EI \frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = -P(L - x) = -PL + P x$$

**Step 3: First Integration:**

$$EI \frac{dy}{dx} = \int (-PL + Px)\, dx + C_1 = -PL x + \frac{P x^2}{2} + C_1$$

**Step 4: Second Integration:**

$$EI y = -\frac{PL x^2}{2} + \frac{P x^3}{6} + C_1 x + C_2$$

**Step 5: Boundary Conditions at Fixed End $A$ ($x = 0$):**

**BC 1:** $y(0) = 0$:

$$0 = 0 + 0 + 0 + C_2 \implies C_2 = 0$$

**BC 2:** $\frac{dy}{dx}\bigg|_{x=0} = 0$ (no rotation at fixed end):

$$0 = 0 + 0 + C_1 \implies C_1 = 0$$

**Step 6: Final Equations:**

$$EI \theta = -PL x + \frac{P x^2}{2} = \frac{P}{2}(x^2 - 2 L x) = -\frac{P x}{2}(2L - x)$$

$$EI y = -\frac{PL x^2}{2} + \frac{P x^3}{6} = \frac{P x^2}{6}(x - 3L)$$

**Step 7: Slope and Deflection at Free End $B$ ($x = L$):**

$$EI \theta_B = -PL \cdot L + \frac{P L^2}{2} = -PL^2 + \frac{PL^2}{2} = -\frac{PL^2}{2}$$

$$\theta_B = -\frac{PL^2}{2 EI}$$

**Magnitude:**

$$\boxed{\vert \theta_B \vert = \frac{P L^2}{2 EI} = \frac{15 \times 10^3 \times 9}{2 \times 9 \times 10^6} = \frac{135{,}000}{18 \times 10^6} = 7.5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{rad}}$$

**Deflection at $B$:**

$$EI y_B = -\frac{PL \cdot L^2}{2} + \frac{P L^3}{6} = -\frac{PL^3}{2} + \frac{PL^3}{6} = PL^3 \left( -\frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{6} \right) = -\frac{PL^3}{3}$$

$$\boxed{\vert y_B \vert = \frac{P L^3}{3 EI} = \frac{15 \times 10^3 \times 27}{3 \times 9 \times 10^6} = \frac{405{,}000}{27 \times 10^6} = 0.015\,\text{m} = 15\,\text{mm}}$$

> **(7 marks: 2 for $M(x)$, 2 for integration, 1 for BCs, 2 for final numerical answer)**

---

#### Part (b) — Cantilever with UDL (7 marks)

**Step 1: Bending Moment at Section $x$ (from fixed end $A$):**

$$M(x) = -\frac{w(L - x)^2}{2}$$

**Step 2: Governing Equation:**

$$EI \frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = -\frac{w(L - x)^2}{2} = -\frac{w}{2}(L^2 - 2 L x + x^2)$$

**Step 3: First Integration:**

$$EI \frac{dy}{dx} = -\frac{w}{2}\left( L^2 x - L x^2 + \frac{x^3}{3} \right) + C_1$$

**Step 4: Second Integration:**

$$EI y = -\frac{w}{2}\left( \frac{L^2 x^2}{2} - \frac{L x^3}{3} + \frac{x^4}{12} \right) + C_1 x + C_2$$

**Step 5: Boundary Conditions at $x = 0$:**

Same as before: $C_1 = 0$ and $C_2 = 0$.

**Step 6: Final Equations at $x = L$:**

$$EI \theta_B = -\frac{w}{2}\left( L^3 - L^3 + \frac{L^3}{3} \right) = -\frac{w}{2} \cdot \frac{L^3}{3} = -\frac{w L^3}{6}$$

$$\boxed{\vert \theta_B \vert = \frac{w L^3}{6 EI}}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

$$\vert \theta_B \vert = \frac{10 \times 10^3 \times 27}{6 \times 9 \times 10^6} = \frac{270{,}000}{54 \times 10^6} = 5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{rad}$$

**Deflection at $B$:**

$$EI y_B = -\frac{w}{2}\left( \frac{L^4}{2} - \frac{L^4}{3} + \frac{L^4}{12} \right) = -\frac{w L^4}{2} \left( \frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{3} + \frac{1}{12} \right)$$

$$= -\frac{w L^4}{2} \left( \frac{6 - 4 + 1}{12} \right) = -\frac{w L^4}{2} \cdot \frac{3}{12} = -\frac{w L^4}{8}$$

$$\boxed{\vert y_B \vert = \frac{w L^4}{8 EI}}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

$$\vert y_B \vert = \frac{10 \times 10^3 \times 81}{8 \times 9 \times 10^6} = \frac{810{,}000}{72 \times 10^6} = 0.01125\,\text{m} = 11.25\,\text{mm}$$

> **(7 marks: 2 for $M(x)$, 2 for integration, 1 for BCs, 2 for final numerical answer)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Mix-Up:** Many students confuse the sign of $M(x)$ in cantilevers. The hogging moment is **negative** in the standard sagging-positive convention, which flips the signs in the final slope and deflection. Always state the convention you're using explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting the Symmetry BC:** For simply supported symmetric beams, students often write only two support BCs (both at $y = 0$) and fail to use the **mid-span slope = 0** condition, which is the easiest way to find $C_1$.
> 3. **Unit Inconsistency:** $E$ in GPa, $I$ in m⁴, $L$ in m — mixing these gives wrong answers by orders of magnitude. KTU deducts **2 marks** for unit errors in numerical problems.
> 4. **Integration Constant Algebra:** $C_1$ and $C_2$ must be **explicitly** written with their numerical (or symbolic) values in the final answer block. Skipping this step costs **1 mark**.
> 5. **Forgetting the $C_1 x$ term:** After the second integration, the slope integration constant $C_1$ becomes a multiplier of $x$ in the deflection equation. A very common error is to omit this term.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Revision Bullets — KTU Module 4: Double Integration Method**

- **Governing Equation:** $EI \dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2} = M(x)$ — derived from equating $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{M}{EI}$ and $\dfrac{1}{R} \approx \dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2}$ (small-slope assumption).
- **Algorithm:** Section the beam $\rightarrow$ Find $M(x)$ $\rightarrow$ Integrate twice $\rightarrow$ Apply BCs $\rightarrow$ Get $\theta(x)$ and $y(x)$ $\rightarrow$ Compute maxima.
- **Two Constants of Integration:** $C_1$ (from first integration, slope) and $C_2$ (from second integration, deflection) must be evaluated from **two independent boundary conditions**.
- **Boundary Conditions Cheat Sheet:**
  - Fixed end $\Rightarrow y = 0$ and $\frac{dy}{dx} = 0$.
  - Simple/roller support $\Rightarrow y = 0$ only.
  - Free end $\Rightarrow$ no prescribed BCs; values are computed.
  - Symmetric beam $\Rightarrow \frac{dy}{dx} = 0$ at the axis of symmetry.
- **Standard Results (Simply Supported Beam):**
  - Central point load: $y_{\max} = \dfrac{PL^3}{48EI}$, $\theta_{\max} = \dfrac{PL^2}{16EI}$.
  - Full UDL: $y_{\max} = \dfrac{5wL^4}{384EI}$, $\theta_{\max} = \dfrac{wL^3}{24EI}$.
- **Standard Results (Cantilever Beam):**
  - Point load at free end: $y_{\max} = \dfrac{PL^3}{3EI}$, $\theta_{\max} = \dfrac{PL^2}{2EI}$.
  - Full UDL: $y_{\max} = \dfrac{wL^4}{8EI}$, $\theta_{\max} = \dfrac{wL^3}{6EI}$.
- **Units Vigilance:** Convert all to SI: $E$ in Pa, $I$ in m⁴, $L$ in m, $w$ in N/m, $P$ in N. Final $y$ in m (or convert to mm).
- **Method Suitability:** Best for **prismatic beams** with **continuous, single-region loading**. For multiple concentrated loads, **Macaulay's Method** (Module 5) is more efficient.
- **Sign Convention:** Always declare your convention at the start of the solution — KTU evaluators expect clarity here.
- **Continuity Conditions:** For stepped or piecewise loads, both $y$ and $\frac{dy}{dx}$ must be continuous at the junctions between regions.
- **Final Formula to Memorize:** $y_{\max} = \dfrac{5wL^4}{384EI}$ (most-tested KTU result for SS beam under UDL).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Theory of Columns: Introduction to Columns
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST303 - SOLID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Theory of Columns: Introduction to Columns

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Column:** A structural member subjected primarily to **axial compressive load**, whose cross-sectional dimensions are small compared to its length, is called a **column** (or **strut** if the load capacity is small / secondary member).

A column is distinguished from a short compression block (which fails by **crushing** of the material) by the fact that long, slender columns fail by **lateral elastic instability**, a phenomenon called **buckling**. The member deflects laterally (bows sideways) long before the material reaches its yield stress.

> [!NOTE]
> In Naval Architecture, columns appear in the form of **ship masts, deck pillars, hatch corner posts, derrick posts, and rudder stocks**. Their buckling behaviour directly governs the safety and stiffness of the vessel's superstructure.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Picture)

Imagine trying to break a **thin plastic ruler** held vertically between your palms:
- If the ruler is **short and thick**, you crush it — the material itself fails.
- If the ruler is **long and thin**, it suddenly **kinks sideways** with a *snap* — the material did not break, but the straight shape became unstable.

This sideways *kinking* is **buckling**. The ruler did not need to be crushed; its geometry simply made the straight configuration *unstable*. This is exactly the failure mode a naval architect must predict for a **mast** in a ship — a tall, slender steel tube can collapse sideways under a much smaller load than its material strength would suggest.

## 1.3 Key Distinguishing Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Strut** | A compression member of secondary importance, e.g., a bracing rod |
| **Column** | A principal compression member, e.g., a ship mast or building pillar |
| **Buckling** | Sudden lateral deflection of a slender member under axial compression |
| **Crushing** | Direct compressive yielding/cracking of the material itself |
| **Slenderness Ratio ($\lambda$)** | Ratio of effective length to least radius of gyration |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric Used:** The **Slenderness Ratio** $\lambda = \dfrac{L_e}{k}$, where $L_e$ is effective length in **mm** and $k$ is the least radius of gyration in **mm**. It is a **dimensionless** number.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Columns

Columns are classified based on their **slenderness ratio** and the corresponding failure mode. The KTU syllabus emphasises this three-fold classification:

### 2.1.1 Short Columns
- **Slenderness ratio** $\lambda \le 30$ to $40$ (some codes use $\le 12$ to $30$).
- Failure is purely by **crushing** of material.
- Governed by **compressive strength** $\sigma_c$.
- Design stress = Yield stress / Crushing stress.
- Buckling is *not* the design driver.

### 2.1.2 Medium Columns
- **Slenderness ratio** $30 < \lambda \le \lambda_c$ (where $\lambda_c$ is the transition slenderness).
- Failure is by a **combination** of crushing and inelastic buckling.
- Governed by empirical **straight-line formulas** such as:
  - **Rankine–Gordon formula**
  - **Johnson's parabolic formula**
  - **IS 800 / AISC straight-line formula**
- This regime is critical for **ship deck pillars and hatch coamings**.

### 2.1.3 Long Columns
- **Slenderness ratio** $\lambda \ge \lambda_c$ (transition slenderness).
- Failure is purely by **elastic (Euler) buckling**.
- Material remains in the elastic range.
- Governed by **Euler's critical load** $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^2}$.

> [!NOTE]
> The **transition slenderness ratio** for mild steel is $\lambda_c = \pi\sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\sigma_y}}$. For $E = 200\,\text{GPa}$ and $\sigma_y = 250\,\text{MPa}$, $\lambda_c \approx 88.9$.

## 2.2 End Conditions of Columns

A column's buckling load depends critically on how the **ends are restrained**. The KTU 2024 syllabus lists four idealised end conditions:

| End Condition | Description | Effective Length Factor $n$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Both ends **hinged (pinned)** | Free to rotate, no translation | $1.0$ |
| One end **fixed**, other **free** | Cantilever type | $2.0$ |
| Both ends **fixed** | No rotation, no translation | $0.5$ |
| One end **fixed**, other **hinged** | Propped cantilever | $0.7$ (≈ $0.699$) |

The **effective length** is defined as $L_e = nL$, where $L$ is the actual length and $n$ is the factor from the table above.

## 2.3 Why Buckling Occurs (The "Why" Behind It)

- A perfectly straight column under axial load is in a state of **unstable equilibrium**.
- Any **infinitesimal lateral disturbance** (vibration, eccentricity of load, initial crookedness) causes a small lateral deflection.
- This deflection creates a **bending moment** $M = P \cdot y$ at the mid-section.
- The bending moment **increases** the deflection, which **increases** the moment, and so on.
- This **positive feedback loop** drives the column to fail suddenly when the applied load reaches $P_{cr}$.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** This is why **derrick posts and masts on ships** are designed with a *safety factor of 3 to 5* on Euler's load. The slightest accidental lateral load (e.g., a wave slam) can drastically reduce the safe axial capacity.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **high-yield formula set** for the Module-4 viva and ESE. Memorise these.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^2}$ | **Euler's critical buckling load** (long columns) | $\text{N}$ |
| 2 | $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E}{\lambda^2}$ | Euler's critical stress | $\text{Pa}$ or $\text{N/mm}^2$ |
| 3 | $\lambda = \dfrac{L_e}{k_{min}}$ | Slenderness ratio | dimensionless |
| 4 | $k_{min} = \sqrt{\dfrac{I_{min}}{A}}$ | Least radius of gyration | $\text{mm}$ |
| 5 | $L_e = n L$ | Effective length | $\text{mm}$ |
| 6 | $\lambda_c = \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\sigma_y}}$ | Transition slenderness ratio | dimensionless |
| 7 | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E A}{\lambda^2}$ | Euler load using slenderness ratio directly | $\text{N}$ |
| 8 | $M = -P \cdot y$ | Bending moment in buckled column (Euler) | $\text{N·mm}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **NOTE on notation used in KTU papers:** Often the formula is written as $P = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{l_e^{\,2}}$ where $l_e$ is effective length. Both forms are accepted.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Euler's Buckling Load (Pinned-Pinned Column)

This is the most heavily-weighted derivation in Module 4. It is essential for a 14-mark question.

### 3.1.1 Assumptions
1. Column is perfectly straight, homogeneous and prismatic.
2. The axial compressive load $P$ is applied through the centroid.
3. The column material obeys **Hooke's law** (elastic range).
4. Self-weight is neglected.
5. End conditions: both ends **hinged**.

### 3.1.2 Setup of the Differential Equation

Consider a column $AB$ of length $L$ hinged at both ends and subjected to an axial compressive load $P$ at the ends. Under a small lateral disturbance, the column deflects by $y$ at a section distance $x$ from end $A$.

The **bending moment** at section $x$ is given by:

$$
M = -P \cdot y
$$

(The negative sign is by convention — the moment produced by the axial load opposes the concavity of the deflection curve.)

The **Euler–Bernoulli** differential equation of the deflection curve is:

$$
E I \dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} = M
$$

Substituting the moment expression:

$$
E I \dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} = -P \cdot y
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} + \left( \dfrac{P}{E I} \right) y = 0
$$

### 3.1.3 Solution of the Differential Equation

Let $k^2 = \dfrac{P}{E I}$. The equation becomes:

$$
\dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} + k^{2} y = 0
$$

The general solution of this standard second-order linear homogeneous ODE is:

$$
y(x) = A \sin(kx) + B \cos(kx)
$$

### 3.1.4 Applying Boundary Conditions

**Boundary Condition 1:** At $x = 0$, $y = 0$ (hinged end $A$ has no deflection).

$$
0 = A \sin(0) + B \cos(0) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 0 = 0 + B \cdot 1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; B = 0
$$

**Boundary Condition 2:** At $x = L$, $y = 0$ (hinged end $B$ has no deflection).

$$
0 = A \sin(kL) + 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; A \sin(kL) = 0
$$

For a **non-trivial (buckled) solution**, $A \neq 0$. Therefore:

$$
\sin(kL) = 0
$$

This means $kL = n\pi$, where $n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$

The **smallest** value of $k$ is obtained for $n = 1$:

$$
kL = \pi \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; k = \dfrac{\pi}{L}
$$

### 3.1.5 Solving for the Critical Load

Recall $k^2 = \dfrac{P}{E I}$. Squaring the result:

$$
\left( \dfrac{\pi}{L} \right)^{2} = \dfrac{P_{cr}}{E I}
$$

Solving for $P_{cr}$:

$$
P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{L^{2}}
$$

This is **Euler's critical buckling load** for a column hinged at both ends. The complete derivation is summarised in a unified aligned block:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Given:}\quad & EI\,\dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} + P\,y = 0 \\
\text{Let:}\quad & k^{2} = \dfrac{P}{EI} \\
\text{General solution:}\quad & y(x) = A\sin(kx) + B\cos(kx) \\
\text{BC at } x=0:\quad & B = 0 \\
\text{BC at } x=L:\quad & A\sin(kL) = 0 \\
\text{For non-trivial solution:}\quad & kL = n\pi,\;\;n=1,2,\ldots \\
\text{Minimum (first mode):}\quad & kL = \pi \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; k = \dfrac{\pi}{L} \\
\text{Critical load:}\quad & P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2}\,E\,I}{L^{2}} \quad\blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Critical Stress and Slenderness Form

Starting from Euler's load and using $\sigma_{cr} = P_{cr}/A$:

$$
\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{P_{cr}}{A} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{A L^{2}}
$$

Now substitute $I = A k^{2}$, where $k$ is the **radius of gyration**:

$$
\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E \,(A k^{2})}{A L^{2}} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E k^{2}}{L^{2}}
$$

Recognising the slenderness ratio $\lambda = L/k$, we have $k/L = 1/\lambda$, so $k^2/L^2 = 1/\lambda^{2}$:

$$
\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E}{\lambda^{2}}
$$

This is **Euler's critical stress**, and the corresponding load becomes:

$$
P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E A}{\lambda^{2}}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Transition Slenderness Ratio $\lambda_c$

Euler's theory is valid only as long as $\sigma_{cr} \le \sigma_y$ (yield stress). At the limit:

$$
\sigma_{cr} = \sigma_y \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \dfrac{\pi^{2} E}{\lambda_{c}^{\,2}} = \sigma_y
$$

Solving for $\lambda_c$:

$$
\lambda_{c}^{\,2} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E}{\sigma_y} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \lambda_{c} = \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\sigma_y}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Example (Mild Steel):** For $E = 2 \times 10^{5}\,\text{N/mm}^{2}$ and $\sigma_y = 250\,\text{N/mm}^{2}$:

$$
\lambda_{c} = \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 10^{5}}{250}} = \pi \sqrt{800} \approx \pi \times 28.284 \approx 88.86
$$

> Any column with $\lambda > 88.86$ fails by **elastic buckling** (Euler regime).

## 3.4 Effective Length Concept (Quick Derivation)

For other end conditions, the effective length is the distance between consecutive **points of zero bending moment** (i.e., points of contraflexure). Equivalently, the column is treated as a hinged-hinged column of length $L_e = nL$, and Euler's formula is rewritten as:

$$
P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{L_{e}^{\,2}} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{n^{2} L^{2}}
$$

| End Condition | $n$ | $L_e$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Hinged–Hinged | $1.0$ | $L$ |
| Fixed–Fixed | $0.5$ | $L/2$ |
| Fixed–Free | $2.0$ | $2L$ |
| Fixed–Hinged | $\approx 0.7$ | $0.7 L$ |

> [!WARNING]
> A **common KTU mistake**: Students write $L_{e} = nL$ with $n$ defined the wrong way (e.g., $n=0.5$ but writing $L_{e} = L/0.5 = 2L$). Always confirm whether $n$ is a **multiplier** or a **fraction** before substituting.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Column Classification Flow

The following Mermaid flow-chart maps the **decision logic** a Naval Architect follows when designing a compression member (e.g., a derrick post on a tanker). It is the recommended *Functional Block Architecture* representation for Module-4 problems on KTU board exams.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Axial Compression Member] --> B[Compute Slenderness Ratio lambda = Le over k]
    B --> C{lambda less than or equal to 30}
    C -- Yes --> D[Short Column: Failure by Crushing]
    D --> D1[Use Compressive Strength sigma_c]
    D1 --> D2[Design Stress = Crushing Stress]
    C -- No --> E{lambda less than or equal to lambda_c}
    E -- Yes --> F[Medium Column: Inelastic Buckling]
    F --> F1[Use Rankine or Johnson Formula]
    F1 --> F2[Apply Empirical Constants]
    E -- No --> G[Long Column: Elastic Buckling]
    G --> G1[Use Euler Critical Load Pcr = pi squared EI over Le squared]
    G1 --> G2[Compute Critical Stress = pi squared E over lambda squared]
    G2 --> H[Apply Factor of Safety FOS 3 to 5]
    H --> I[Final Design Load = Pcr divided by FOS]
    I --> J[End: Safe Cross-Section Selected]
    D2 --> J
    F2 --> J
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: End-Conditions Effective Length Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    EC1[Hinged Hinged] -->|n = 1.0| EL1[Le = L]
    EC2[Fixed Fixed] -->|n = 0.5| EL2[Le = L over 2]
    EC3[Fixed Free] -->|n = 2.0| EL3[Le = 2L]
    EC4[Fixed Hinged] -->|n = 0.7| EL4[Le = 0.7L]
    EL1 --> OUT[Apply Euler Formula Pcr = pi squared EI over Le squared]
    EL2 --> OUT
    EL3 --> OUT
    EL4 --> OUT
    OUT --> RESULT[Critical Buckling Load Pcr]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Buckling Failure Path

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Apply Axial Load P] --> S2{Is column perfectly straight and concentric load?}
    S2 -- Yes ideal --> S3[Unstable Equilibrium in straight shape]
    S2 -- No real column --> S4[Initial Imperfection e]
    S4 --> S5[Lateral Deflection y]
    S3 --> S5
    S5 --> S6[Bending Moment M = P times y]
    S6 --> S7[Additional Curvature]
    S7 --> S8{Is P less than Pcr?}
    S8 -- Yes --> S9[Stable Deflection Equilibrium]
    S8 -- No --> S10[Runaway Deflection: Buckling Collapse]
    S9 --> S11[Column Returns to Stable State]
    S10 --> S12[Column Fails by Lateral Instability]
```

> [!NOTE]
> These block-level architectures are perfectly suited for KTU 14-mark answers. The examiners often allocate 2–3 marks for a "well-labelled sketch" — a clear functional flow-chart earns full credit without needing an artistic drawing.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q1, 3 Marks]**

**Q1.** Define a **column**. Distinguish clearly between a **short column** and a **long column** with reference to the **slenderness ratio**.

**Model Answer:**

A column is a structural member subjected primarily to axial compressive load, whose length is large compared to its lateral cross-sectional dimensions.

- A **short column** has a slenderness ratio $\lambda \le 30$ (varies by code). It fails by **direct crushing** of the material. The ultimate load depends on the **compressive strength** of the material.
- A **long column** has a slenderness ratio $\lambda \ge \lambda_c$ (the transition slenderness). It fails by **elastic buckling** at a load far below the crushing load. The critical load is given by **Euler's formula** $P_{cr} = \pi^{2}EI/L_{e}^{2}$.

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q2, 3 Marks]**

**Q2.** What is the **effective length** of a column? State the effective length factors for all four idealised end conditions.

**Model Answer:**

The **effective length** $L_e$ of a column is the length of an equivalent **hinged-hinged column** that would buckle at the same load as the actual column. It is given by $L_e = nL$, where $n$ is the effective-length factor.

| End Condition | $n$ |
| :--- | :--- |
| Hinged–Hinged | $1.0$ |
| Fixed–Fixed | $0.5$ |
| Fixed–Free | $2.0$ |
| Fixed–Hinged | $0.7$ |

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, Q-Option-A, 14 Marks]**

### **Question A**

**(a)** Derive the **Euler's critical load** for a long column with both ends **hinged**. State all assumptions clearly. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A hollow circular steel column has an **outer diameter 80 mm** and **inner diameter 60 mm**, length **3.5 m**, with both ends **fixed**. Using $E = 2 \times 10^{5}\,\text{N/mm}^{2}$, calculate the **Euler's critical load** and the corresponding **critical stress**. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks)**

> The full derivation as shown in **Section 3.1** above is the model answer. Key checkpoints for marks:
> 1. Statement of assumptions (1 mark)
> 2. Bending moment $M = -Py$ (1 mark)
> 3. Euler–Bernoulli equation $EI\,y'' = M$ (1 mark)
> 4. Differential equation and general solution (2 marks)
> 5. Boundary conditions and the final $P_{cr} = \pi^{2}EI/L^{2}$ (2 marks)

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Moment of Inertia of hollow circular section**

$$
I = \dfrac{\pi}{64} \left(D_{o}^{4} - D_{i}^{4}\right)
$$

$$
I = \dfrac{\pi}{64}\left(80^{4} - 60^{4}\right) = \dfrac{\pi}{64}\left(40\,960\,000 - 12\,960\,000\right)
$$

$$
I = \dfrac{\pi}{64} \times 28\,000\,000 = 1\,374\,447\,\text{mm}^{4}
$$

**Step 2: Effective length (Fixed–Fixed, $n = 0.5$)**

$$
L_e = 0.5 \times 3500 = 1750\,\text{mm}
$$

**Step 3: Euler's critical load**

$$
P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} \times 2 \times 10^{5} \times 1\,374\,447}{1750^{2}}
$$

$$
P_{cr} = \dfrac{9.8696 \times 2 \times 10^{5} \times 1\,374\,447}{3\,062\,500}
$$

$$
P_{cr} = \dfrac{2.7133 \times 10^{12}}{3.0625 \times 10^{6}} \approx 8.86 \times 10^{5}\,\text{N}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{cr} \approx 886\,\text{kN}}
$$

**Step 4: Cross-sectional area**

$$
A = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\left(D_{o}^{2} - D_{i}^{2}\right) = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\left(80^{2} - 60^{2}\right) = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(6400 - 3600) = 2199.1\,\text{mm}^{2}
$$

**Step 5: Critical stress**

$$
\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{P_{cr}}{A} = \dfrac{8.86 \times 10^{5}}{2199.1} \approx 402.9\,\text{N/mm}^{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_{cr} \approx 403\,\text{MPa}}
$$

> Since $\sigma_{cr} = 403\,\text{MPa} > \sigma_y = 250\,\text{MPa}$, the assumption of elastic buckling is **violated** — this is in fact a *medium column*. The proper design would require Johnson's formula. (Students are expected to note this in 14-mark answers for full credit.)

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4, Q-Option-B, 14 Marks]**

### **Question B**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **four idealised end conditions** of a column and their **effective length factors**. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A **mild-steel column** of **I-section** has flanges $120 \times 12\,\text{mm}$ and web $200 \times 10\,\text{mm}$ (overall depth 224 mm). It is $5\,\text{m}$ long and **fixed at one end and hinged at the other**. Determine the **Euler's critical load**. Take $E = 2 \times 10^{5}\,\text{N/mm}^{2}$. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) End Conditions (7 Marks)**

The four idealised end conditions and their effective length factors $n$ are:

1. **Hinged–Hinged:** $n = 1.0$, $L_e = L$. Both ends free to rotate but laterally restrained.
2. **Fixed–Fixed:** $n = 0.5$, $L_e = L/2$. Both ends fully restrained against rotation and translation.
3. **Fixed–Free:** $n = 2.0$, $L_e = 2L$. One end is a cantilever; least stiff configuration.
4. **Fixed–Hinged:** $n = 0.7$, $L_e = 0.7L$. One end restrained, other end pinned.

A labelled diagram showing each case is required (1.5 marks per case, totalling 6 marks; plus 1 mark for the summary table).

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Compute moments of inertia of the I-section about both centroidal axes.**

**About the strong axis (xx, bending in plane of web):**

Treating the section as a large rectangle $224 \times 10$ (web) plus two flange rectangles $120 \times 12$:

- For the web (rectangle $224 \times 10$):
$$
I_{xx,\,web} = \dfrac{10 \times 224^{3}}{12} = \dfrac{10 \times 11\,239\,424}{12} = 9\,366\,187\,\text{mm}^{4}
$$

- For one flange ($120 \times 12$), centroid at distance $d$ from neutral axis of section:
$$
d = \dfrac{224}{2} - \dfrac{12}{2} = 112 - 6 = 106\,\text{mm}
$$

$$
I_{xx,\,flange} = \dfrac{120 \times 12^{3}}{12} + (120 \times 12) \times 106^{2} = 17\,280 + 1520 \times 11\,236
$$

$$
I_{xx,\,flange} = 17\,280 + 17\,078\,720 = 17\,096\,000\,\text{mm}^{4}
$$

- Total (two flanges + web):
$$
I_{xx} = 9\,366\,187 + 2 \times 17\,096\,000 = 43\,558\,187\,\text{mm}^{4}
$$

**About the weak axis (yy, buckling plane of flanges):**

- For web ($224 \times 10$):
$$
I_{yy,\,web} = \dfrac{224 \times 10^{3}}{12} = 18\,667\,\text{mm}^{4}
$$

- For one flange ($120 \times 12$, with neutral axis through its own centre):
$$
I_{yy,\,flange} = \dfrac{12 \times 120^{3}}{12} = 1\,728\,000\,\text{mm}^{4}
$$

- Total:
$$
I_{yy} = 18\,667 + 2 \times 1\,728\,000 = 3\,474\,667\,\text{mm}^{4}
$$

**Step 2: Least moment of inertia (column buckles about weak axis)**

$$
I_{min} = I_{yy} = 3\,474\,667\,\text{mm}^{4}
$$

**Step 3: Effective length (Fixed–Hinged, $n = 0.7$)**

$$
L_e = 0.7 \times 5000 = 3500\,\text{mm}
$$

**Step 4: Euler's critical load**

$$
P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} \times 2 \times 10^{5} \times 3\,474\,667}{3500^{2}}
$$

$$
P_{cr} = \dfrac{9.8696 \times 2 \times 10^{5} \times 3\,474\,667}{12\,250\,000}
$$

$$
P_{cr} = \dfrac{6.8601 \times 10^{12}}{1.225 \times 10^{7}} \approx 5.60 \times 10^{5}\,\text{N}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{cr} \approx 560\,\text{kN}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** In I-section column problems, the most common mistake is using the **strong-axis** $I_{xx}$ (which is *larger*) instead of the **weak-axis** $I_{yy}$ (which is *smaller*). A column **always buckles about the axis with the least $I$**. Using $I_{xx}$ will inflate the critical load and cause the design to be **unsafe** — guaranteed loss of 2–3 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 4 — Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ A **column** is a *long* compression member that fails by **lateral buckling**, not by crushing.
- ✅ Buckling is **sudden**, **lateral**, and **catastrophic** — the material itself is usually intact after failure.
- ✅ **Euler's formula** $P_{cr} = \pi^{2}EI/L_{e}^{2}$ applies **only** when $\lambda \ge \lambda_c$ (i.e., elastic buckling).
- ✅ The **slenderness ratio** $\lambda = L_{e}/k_{min}$ is a *dimensionless* index that decides the failure mode:
  - $\lambda \le 30$: short column → crushing governs.
  - $30 < \lambda \le \lambda_c$: medium column → Johnson's / Rankine / straight-line formula.
  - $\lambda \ge \lambda_c$: long column → Euler governs.
- ✅ The **transition slenderness** for mild steel is $\lambda_c \approx 88.9$ (for $E=200\,\text{GPa}$, $\sigma_y=250\,\text{MPa}$).
- ✅ The **effective length factor** $n$: 1.0 / 0.5 / 0.7 / 2.0 for the four standard end conditions.
- ✅ Always use the **least** $I$ (and least radius of gyration) when applying Euler's formula — the column chooses the weakest plane.
- ✅ Euler's theory assumes **perfectly straight, concentrically loaded, elastic** columns. Real columns have imperfections — hence the FOS of 3 to 5.
- ✅ The **critical stress** $\sigma_{cr} = \pi^{2}E/\lambda^{2}$ — *not* the yield stress — is the design limit for long columns.
- ✅ **Naval architecture applications:** ship masts, deck pillars, derrick posts, rudder stocks, hatch corner posts, A-frames, king posts.
- ✅ Always **state the assumptions** before deriving — it is mandatory for 14-mark answers and earns at least 1 mark.
- ✅ **Euler is invalid if $\sigma_{cr} > \sigma_y$**. Always verify by computing $\sigma_{cr}$ and comparing with $\sigma_y$ at the end of a long-column problem.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Buckling Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST303 - SOLID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Buckling Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Buckling** is a *stability failure mode* characterized by a sudden, large lateral (out-of-plane) deflection of a structural member subjected to compressive in-plane loading, occurring at a load level that may be **significantly lower** than the load required to cause material yielding or compressive crushing.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme terminology for Naval Architecture, buckling theory addresses the loss of *geometric stability* of slender structural components used in ship hulls, superstructures, masts, deck pillars, bulkheads, and plate panels. The governing principle is that **equilibrium becomes unstable** when the applied compressive load reaches a critical value, beyond which any small perturbation grows uncontrollably.

> [!NOTE]
> **Strength vs. Stability Distinction (KTU High-Yield Concept)**
> - **Strength failure** → governed by material yielding ($\sigma = \sigma_y$); *stress-driven*.
> - **Stability failure (buckling)** → governed by geometry and stiffness ($P_{cr} = \pi^2 EI / L_e^2$); *stiffness-driven*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Thin Plastic Ruler**
> Hold a thin plastic ruler vertically on a table and press down on the top with your thumb. At low force, it simply compresses. Suddenly, at a *specific* compressive load, it snaps sideways with a visible "pop" — that is **buckling**. You did not crush the plastic; you merely exceeded the critical load at which its straight configuration could no longer remain stable. The same phenomenon governs the **deck plating of a ship under hull girder bending** and the **web of a stiffened panel under thrust from a heavy engine** at sea.

For Naval Architects, this is not merely an academic curiosity: a ship hull is essentially a long, hollow, thin-walled **box girder**, and its plating, stiffeners, and frames are all susceptible to buckling well before the steel reaches yield strength. Classification societies (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS) dedicate entire chapters of their rules to controlling **plate and column buckling** because a single buckled panel can trigger progressive structural collapse at sea.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Young's Modulus of structural steel:** $E = 210 \text{ GPa} = 2.1 \times 10^{11} \text{ N/m}^2$
- **Poisson's ratio of structural steel:** $\nu = 0.30$
- **Slenderness ratio:** $\lambda = L_e / r$ (dimensionless)
- **Effective length factor $K$**: depends on end conditions (range: $0.5 \le K \le 2.0$)
- **Plate aspect ratio:** $\alpha = a / b$ (where $a$ = length, $b$ = width of the loaded edge)
- **Standard yield strength for ship plate (mild steel):** $\sigma_y = 235 \text{ MPa}$

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Euler's Theory of Column Buckling (Long Columns)

Leonhard Euler (1757) established that a perfectly straight, prismatic, axially loaded column will buckle elastically when the compressive load reaches a critical value at which the **bending stiffness** can no longer resist the destabilizing moment from any infinitesimal lateral perturbation.

#### 2.1.1 Assumptions of Euler's Theory

- Column is initially perfectly straight and prismatic.
- Material is homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's law (linearly elastic).
- Self-weight of the column is neglected.
- Column fails only by **flexural buckling** (no torsion, no local buckling).
- End loads are applied **exactly along the centroidal axis** (no eccentricity).
- Cross-section is constant along the length.

#### 2.1.2 Why Euler's Theory? — The "How" Behind the Buckling Load

A slender column carries compressive load $P$ along its axis. Imagine the column is given an infinitesimally small lateral nudge (perturbation). The internal bending moment that develops at any section is $M(x) = P \cdot y(x)$, where $y(x)$ is the lateral deflection. The Euler-Bernoulli beam equation:

$$EI \frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = -M(x) = -P y(x)$$

has a *trivial solution* $y = 0$ for any $P$, but a *non-trivial lateral deflection* becomes possible only when:

$$P \ge P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 EI}{L_e^2}$$

This is the **bifurcation load** — the point at which the straight configuration loses stability.

### 2.2 Effective Length Concept

The actual column length $L$ is converted to an **effective length** $L_e$ that represents the equivalent length of a pin-ended column having the same buckling load:

$$L_e = K \cdot L$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Effective length factor $K$ — KTU Board Favourite**

| End Condition (Top–Bottom) | $K$ | $L_e$ | Physical Picture |
|----------------------------|-----|-------|------------------|
| Pinned – Pinned | 1.0 | $L$ | Both ends free to rotate |
| Fixed – Fixed | 0.5 | $L/2$ | Strongest case |
| Fixed – Free (cantilever) | 2.0 | $2L$ | Weakest case (e.g., mast column) |
| Fixed – Pinned | 0.7 | $0.7L$ | Common in real frames |
| Fixed – Guided (translation fixed, rotation free) | 1.0 | $L$ | Anti-symmetric mode |

### 2.3 Slenderness Ratio Categories — Column Behaviour Map

$$\lambda = \frac{L_e}{r}, \qquad r = \sqrt{\frac{I}{A}}$$

- **Long (slender) columns:** $\lambda \ge \lambda_0$ → Euler elastic buckling governs.
- **Short (stocky) columns:** $\lambda \le \lambda_c$ → Material crushing/yielding governs.
- **Intermediate columns:** $\lambda_c < \lambda < \lambda_0$ → Johnson's parabolic formula governs.

The **critical slenderness ratio** (transition Euler ↔ Johnson):

$$\lambda_0 = \sqrt{\frac{2\pi^2 E}{\sigma_y}}$$

For mild steel ($E = 210$ GPa, $\sigma_y = 235$ MPa): $\lambda_0 \approx 132.7$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Buckling Theory)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for KTU 2024 Scheme exam preparation on this topic. Memorize it.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Validity / Range |
|---|---------|----------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{L_e^2}$ | Euler critical load (N) | Elastic, long columns |
| 2 | $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E}{\lambda^2} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E}{(L_e/r)^2}$ | Euler critical stress (Pa) | $\sigma_{cr} \le \sigma_y$ |
| 3 | $L_e = K L$ | Effective length (m) | $K$ from end condition table |
| 4 | $r = \sqrt{I/A}$ | Radius of gyration (m) | Minimum $r$ governs buckling axis |
| 5 | $\sigma_{cr} = a - b\lambda^2$ | Johnson parabolic formula (Pa) | Intermediate columns |
| 6 | $a = \sigma_y$, $b = \dfrac{\sigma_y^2}{4\pi^2 E}$ | Johnson constants (Pa, Pa) | Mild steel, $a = b\lambda_c^2$ |
| 7 | $\lambda_0 = \sqrt{2\pi^2 E / \sigma_y}$ | Critical slenderness ratio | $\lambda_0 \approx 132.7$ (mild steel) |
| 8 | $\sigma_{cr}^{plate} = \dfrac{k \pi^2 E}{12(1-\nu^2)} \left(\dfrac{t}{b}\right)^2$ | Plate critical stress (Pa) | Ship hull plating |
| 9 | $k_{min} = 4.0$ | Bryan coefficient (long simply supported plate, uniaxial) | $\alpha = a/b \to \infty$ |
| 10 | $k_{min} = 6.97$ | Bryan coefficient (long clamped plate, uniaxial) | $\alpha \to \infty$ |
| 11 | $k = \left(\dfrac{m}{\alpha} + \dfrac{\alpha}{m}\right)^2$ | Buckling coefficient (general simply supported plate) | $m$ = number of half-waves |
| 12 | $\sigma_{cr}^{cyl} = \dfrac{E \cdot t / R}{\sqrt{3(1-\nu^2)}}$ | Cylindrical shell buckling (Pa) | External pressure, thin shells |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Naval Architecture

- **Hull plating** between stiffeners is analyzed as a *rectangular plate* (Eq. 8).
- **Stiffener webs** are analyzed as *columns* (Eq. 1) supported by the plate.
- **Ship masts, king posts, deck pillars** → Euler column buckling.
- **Hull girder shear web** → plate buckling under shear loading ($k$ depends on aspect ratio).
- **Submarine pressure hulls** → cylindrical shell buckling under hydrostatic pressure.
- **Classification Society Rules** (DNV, LR, ABS) are essentially **empirical buckling design codes** derived from these classical equations, with safety factors and imperfection allowances.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Euler's Critical Buckling Load (Pinned–Pinned Column)

We derive the buckling load for a prismatic column of length $L$, with both ends pin-supported, subjected to an axial compressive load $P$ applied through the centroidal axis.

**Step 1: State the governing differential equation.**

For a small lateral deflection $y(x)$ of the column, the bending moment at any section $x$ is due to the axial load times the lateral displacement:

$$M(x) = P \cdot y(x)$$

Applying the Euler-Bernoulli beam relation $EI \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = -M(x)$ (negative sign by sign convention of curvature vs. moment):

$$EI \frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} + P y(x) = 0$$

**Step 2: Introduce notation $k^2 = P/(EI)$.**

$$\frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} + k^2 y = 0$$

**Step 3: Write the general solution of the second-order linear ODE.**

$$y(x) = A \sin(kx) + B \cos(kx)$$

**Step 4: Apply the pinned–pinned boundary conditions.**

At $x = 0$: $y(0) = 0 \implies B = 0$.

At $x = L$: $y(L) = 0 \implies A \sin(kL) = 0$.

The trivial solution is $A = 0$ (no buckling). The **non-trivial** buckled shape exists when:

$$\sin(kL) = 0 \implies kL = n\pi, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

**Step 5: Identify the lowest buckling mode ($n=1$ yields minimum load).**

$$k_{min} = \frac{\pi}{L} \implies k_{min}^2 = \frac{\pi^2}{L^2} = \frac{P_{cr}}{EI}$$

**Step 6: Solve for the critical buckling load.**

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 EI}{L^2}$$

**Step 7: Generalize to other end conditions via effective length.**

Replacing $L$ with $L_e = KL$:

$$\boxed{P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 EI}{(KL)^2} = \frac{\pi^2 EI}{L_e^2}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Critical Buckling Stress and Slenderness Ratio

Starting from $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{L_e^2}$, divide both sides by the cross-sectional area $A$ to obtain the average axial stress at incipient buckling:

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{P_{cr}}{A} = \frac{\pi^2 EI}{A L_e^2}$$

Using the definition of the radius of gyration $r = \sqrt{I/A}$, hence $I = Ar^2$:

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E (Ar^2)}{A L_e^2} = \frac{\pi^2 E}{(L_e/r)^2} = \frac{\pi^2 E}{\lambda^2}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E}{\lambda^2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Validity check (this is what examiners love to test):** The Euler formula is valid **only if** $\sigma_{cr} \le \sigma_y$. If $\sigma_{cr}$ computed from this formula exceeds $\sigma_y$, the material yields before it can buckle, and the Euler formula is no longer valid.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Johnson's Parabolic Formula for Intermediate Columns

The Euler formula over-predicts the strength of intermediate columns because yielding initiates locally at the maximum stress fibre before elastic buckling can occur. Johnson proposed an empirical formula that **passes smoothly through $\sigma_y$ at $\lambda = \lambda_c$** and matches Euler at $\lambda = \lambda_0$.

**Step 1: Assume a parabolic form $\sigma_{cr} = a - b\lambda^2$.**

This is the simplest curve that:
- Equals $\sigma_y$ at $\lambda = \lambda_c$ (yielding onset).
- Equals Euler's $\sigma_{cr} = \pi^2 E/\lambda^2$ at $\lambda = \lambda_0$ (elastic buckling onset).

**Step 2: Set the constant $a = \sigma_y$.**

$$\sigma_{cr} = \sigma_y - b\lambda^2$$

**Step 3: Apply continuity at $\lambda = \lambda_0$** (Euler stress equals $\sigma_y$ at the critical slenderness ratio).

From Euler: $\sigma_y = \pi^2 E / \lambda_0^2 \implies \lambda_0^2 = \pi^2 E / \sigma_y$.

Also at $\lambda_0$: $\sigma_y - b\lambda_0^2 = \sigma_y \implies b\lambda_0^2 = 0$... 

> **Correction:** The classical Johnson derivation matches Euler's *tangent* at the transition point, ensuring smoothness. Equating derivatives: $-2b\lambda = -2\pi^2 E / \lambda^3$ at $\lambda = \lambda_0$:

$$b = \frac{\pi^2 E}{\lambda_0^4} \cdot \lambda_0^2 = \frac{\pi^2 E}{\lambda_0^2} \cdot \frac{1}{\lambda_0^2}$$

But the **standard Johnson form** used in textbooks is the simple parabola through the two points:

$$\sigma_{cr} = \sigma_y \left[1 - \frac{\sigma_y}{4\pi^2 E}\lambda^2\right]$$

Derivation by substituting the boundary conditions:
- At $\lambda = 0$: $\sigma_{cr} = \sigma_y$ ✓ (gives $a = \sigma_y$).
- Tangent match with Euler at $\lambda = \lambda_0$:

$$\left.\frac{d\sigma_{cr}}{d\lambda}\right|_{\lambda_0} = -2b\lambda_0 = -\frac{2\pi^2 E}{\lambda_0^3}$$

$$b = \frac{\pi^2 E}{\lambda_0^4} = \frac{\pi^2 E}{(\pi^2 E/\sigma_y)^2} = \frac{\sigma_y^2}{\pi^2 E}$$

Then matching $\sigma_y$ at $\lambda_0$ with the parabola: $\sigma_y = \sigma_y - b\lambda_0^2 \Rightarrow$ over-determined, so the **engineering Johnson form** is the simple parabola:

$$\boxed{\sigma_{cr} = \sigma_y - \frac{\sigma_y^2}{4\pi^2 E}\lambda^2 = \sigma_y\left[1 - \frac{\sigma_y}{4\pi^2 E}\lambda^2\right]}$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Plate Buckling (Bryan, 1891)

For a **rectangular plate** of length $a$, width $b$ (loaded direction), thickness $t$, simply supported on all four edges, under uniform in-plane compressive stress $\sigma_x$ along the $a$-direction:

**Step 1: Governing differential equation for plate bending (Kirchhoff plate theory).**

$$D\left(\frac{\partial^4 w}{\partial x^4} + 2\frac{\partial^4 w}{\partial x^2 \partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^4 w}{\partial y^4}\right) + N_x \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2} = 0$$

where $D = \dfrac{Et^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$ is the **flexural rigidity of the plate per unit width** and $N_x = \sigma_x \cdot t$ is the in-plane compressive force per unit length.

**Step 2: Assume a double trigonometric (Navier) solution satisfying simply supported boundaries.**

$$w(x,y) = \sum_{m=1}^{\infty}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} A_{mn}\sin\left(\frac{m\pi x}{a}\right)\sin\left(\frac{n\pi y}{b}\right)$$

**Step 3: Substitute and collect coefficients.**

The leading term (single $m$, $n=1$ — first mode in transverse direction) gives:

$$D\left(\frac{m\pi}{a}\right)^4 + 2D\left(\frac{m\pi}{a}\right)^2\left(\frac{\pi}{b}\right)^2 + D\left(\frac{\pi}{b}\right)^4 - N_x\left(\frac{m\pi}{a}\right)^2 = 0$$

**Step 4: Solve for $N_x$ (the critical buckling load per unit length).**

$$N_{x,cr} = D\left[\left(\frac{m\pi}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\pi}{b}\right)^2\right]^2 \cdot \frac{1}{(m\pi/a)^2}$$

**Step 5: Convert to critical stress $\sigma_{x,cr} = N_{x,cr}/t$.**

$$\sigma_{x,cr} = \frac{D}{t}\left[\left(\frac{m\pi}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\pi}{b}\right)^2\right]^2 \cdot \left(\frac{a}{m\pi}\right)^2$$

**Step 6: Express as the canonical Bryan formula.**

Substitute $D = Et^3/[12(1-\nu^2)]$ and define aspect ratio $\alpha = a/b$:

$$\boxed{\sigma_{cr}^{plate} = \frac{k \pi^2 E}{12(1-\nu^2)}\left(\frac{t}{b}\right)^2}$$

with the **buckling coefficient**:

$$k = \left(\frac{m}{\alpha} + \frac{\alpha}{m}\right)^2$$

**Step 7: For a long plate ($\alpha \to \infty$), the minimum $k$ occurs at $m=1$.**

$$k_{min} = \left(\frac{1}{\alpha} + \alpha\right)^2 \to 4.0 \text{ as } \alpha \to \infty$$

For a **clamped long plate**, $k_{min} = 6.97$.

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Exam-Standard)

> **Problem:** A structural steel column ($E = 210$ GPa, $\sigma_y = 235$ MPa) in a ship deck support has length $L = 4$ m, square hollow section $100 \times 100 \times 6$ mm, both ends fixed. Determine: (a) the slenderness ratio, (b) whether Euler or Johnson formula applies, and (c) the critical buckling load.

**Given data:**
- $E = 210 \times 10^9$ Pa, $\sigma_y = 235 \times 10^6$ Pa
- $L = 4$ m, fixed–fixed $\Rightarrow K = 0.5 \Rightarrow L_e = 2$ m
- SHS $100 \times 100 \times 6$: $A = 22.4 \times 10^{-4}$ m², $I = 341 \times 10^{-8}$ m⁴ (manufacturer data)

**Step (a): Slenderness ratio.**

$$r = \sqrt{\frac{I}{A}} = \sqrt{\frac{341 \times 10^{-8}}{22.4 \times 10^{-4}}} = \sqrt{1.522 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.01234 \text{ m} = 12.34 \text{ mm}$$

$$\lambda = \frac{L_e}{r} = \frac{2.0}{0.01234} = 162.1$$

**Step (b): Check critical slenderness ratio.**

$$\lambda_0 = \sqrt{\frac{2\pi^2 E}{\sigma_y}} = \sqrt{\frac{2\pi^2 \times 210 \times 10^9}{235 \times 10^6}} = \sqrt{17,635} = 132.8$$

Since $\lambda = 162.1 > \lambda_0 = 132.8$, the column is in the **slender / Euler regime**.

**Step (c): Apply Euler's formula.**

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 EI}{L_e^2} = \frac{\pi^2 \times 210 \times 10^9 \times 341 \times 10^{-8}}{(2.0)^2}$$

$$P_{cr} = \frac{9.8696 \times 210 \times 10^9 \times 341 \times 10^{-8}}{4} = \frac{9.8696 \times 210 \times 341}{4} \times 10$$

$$P_{cr} = \frac{9.8696 \times 71,610}{4} = \frac{706,752}{4} = 176,688 \text{ N} \approx 176.7 \text{ kN}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification via stress:** $\sigma_{cr} = P_{cr}/A = 176,688 / 22.4 \times 10^{-4} = 78.9$ MPa $< \sigma_y$ ✓ (Euler valid).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Column Buckling Mode Selection

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **decision flow** a Naval Architect follows when classifying a structural member into the correct buckling regime and applying the corresponding formula.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Column Geometry and Material] --> B[Compute L, K, A, I_min]
    B --> C[Compute L_e = K times L]
    C --> D[Compute r = sqrt I over A]
    D --> E[Compute slenderness lambda = L_e over r]
    E --> F{Is lambda less than lambda_c?}
    F -- Yes --> G[SHORT COLUMN: Yielding governs, sigma_cr = sigma_y]
    F -- No --> H{Is lambda less than lambda_0?}
    H -- No --> I[LONG COLUMN: Euler elastic buckling, P_cr = pi squared EI over L_e squared]
    H -- Yes --> J[INTERMEDIATE COLUMN: Johnson formula, sigma_cr = sigma_y minus b lambda squared]
    I --> K[Output: P_cr and design safety factor]
    G --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Compare with classification society rule P_cr_design]
    L --> M[Final Design Verdict: SAFE or UNSAFE]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Effective Length Factor K versus End Condition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Pinned-Pinned] -->|K = 1.0| A2[Le = L]
    B1[Fixed-Fixed] -->|K = 0.5| B2[Le = L over 2]
    C1[Fixed-Free] -->|K = 2.0| C2[Le = 2L]
    D1[Fixed-Pinned] -->|K = 0.7| D2[Le = 0.7L]
    E1[Fixed-Guided] -->|K = 1.0| E2[Le = L]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Plate Buckling Coefficient k versus Boundary Condition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Input: Plate Geometry, Aspect Ratio alpha, Edge Conditions, Loading Type] --> P1[Select Navier or Levy solution method]
    P1 --> P2{Edge Conditions?}
    P2 -- SSSS long plate alpha > 1 --> P3[k = 4.0 for uniaxial compression]
    P2 -- SCSC long plate alpha > 1 --> P4[k = 6.97 for uniaxial compression]
    P2 -- SSSS finite alpha --> P5[k = m over alpha plus alpha over m squared, m = 1, 2, 3 dots]
    P2 -- Shear loading --> P6[k depends on alpha, min near alpha = 1.0]
    P3 --> P7[Compute sigma_cr using Bryan formula]
    P4 --> P7
    P5 --> P7
    P6 --> P7
    P7 --> P8[Apply reduction factor for imperfections and residual stress]
    P8 --> P9[Output: Allowable working stress]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Buckling Modes in a Ship Hull Panel

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M0[Ship hull plate panel] --> M1{Type of instability}
    M1 --> M1A[Overall hull girder buckling]
    M1 --> M1B[Plate panel buckling between stiffeners]
    M1 --> M1C[Stiffener column buckling with attached plate]
    M1 --> M1D[Stiffener web local buckling]
    M1 --> M1E[Stiffener flange tripping]
    M1A --> M2[Use Euler column formula with full hull section properties]
    M1B --> M3[Use Bryan plate formula, k from boundary table]
    M1C --> M4[Use Euler formula with effective width of plate included as flange area]
    M1D --> M5[Web under shear and compression, k approx 5.0 to 6.0]
    M1E --> M6[Flange local buckling, use half wavelength of stiffener spacing]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visual interpretation of plate buckling:** For a long simply supported plate under uniaxial compression, the plate buckles into a single half-wave across the width, with multiple half-waves along the length. The number of half-waves along the length $m$ depends on the aspect ratio and the level of in-plane stress, and is determined by minimizing $k$ with respect to integer $m$.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
> **Q1.** Differentiate between **strength failure** and **stability failure** of a structural member. Give one example of each from a ship structure.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Strength Failure | Stability Failure (Buckling) |
|--------|------------------|------------------------------|
| **Cause** | Material reaching yield or ultimate stress | Loss of geometric equilibrium |
| **Governed by** | Stress ($\sigma$) and material property $\sigma_y$ | Stiffness ($EI$), geometry ($L$, $r$) |
| **Load level** | High (close to plastic capacity) | Often much lower than yield load |
| **Failure mode** | Plastic deformation / crushing | Sudden lateral deflection |
| **Ship example** | Yielding of a thick rudder stock | Euler buckling of a slender mast |
| **Design control** | $\sigma < \sigma_y / SF$ | $P < P_{cr} / SF$ |

(3 marks awarded for: correct distinction table or 3 valid contrasting points with ship examples)

---

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]**
> **Q2.** Define **slenderness ratio** and **critical slenderness ratio**. Explain their significance in column buckling.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **slenderness ratio** is defined as $\lambda = L_e / r$, where $L_e$ is the effective length of the column and $r$ is the minimum radius of gyration of its cross-section. It is a measure of how *slender* (i.e., susceptible to buckling) a column is. **(1 mark)**

The **critical slenderness ratio** $\lambda_0 = \sqrt{2\pi^2 E / \sigma_y}$ is the slenderness ratio at which Euler's elastic buckling stress equals the material yield stress. **(1 mark)**

**Significance:** If $\lambda > \lambda_0$, the column is *slender* and Euler's formula governs; if $\lambda < \lambda_0$, the column is *stocky/intermediate* and Johnson's parabolic formula or yielding governs. For mild steel, $\lambda_0 \approx 132.7$. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Question]**
> **Q3A (14 marks).** A mild steel column ($E = 200$ GPa, $\sigma_y = 250$ MPa) in the superstructure of a naval vessel has a hollow circular cross-section with outer diameter $D = 120$ mm and inner diameter $d = 100$ mm. The column length is $L = 3.5$ m, pin-ended at both ends. Determine:
>
> **(a)** The slenderness ratio and classify the column as long, intermediate, or short. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** The critical buckling load using the appropriate formula. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q3A:

**Part (a) — Slenderness ratio (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Cross-sectional area and moment of inertia for hollow circular section.

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.120^2 - 0.100^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.0144 - 0.0100) = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.0044)$$

$$A = 3.456 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$I = \frac{\pi}{64}(D^4 - d^4) = \frac{\pi}{64}(0.120^4 - 0.100^4) = \frac{\pi}{64}(2.0736 \times 10^{-4} - 1.0 \times 10^{-4})$$

$$I = \frac{\pi}{64}(1.0736 \times 10^{-4}) = 5.270 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^4$$

**Step 2:** Radius of gyration.

$$r = \sqrt{\frac{I}{A}} = \sqrt{\frac{5.270 \times 10^{-6}}{3.456 \times 10^{-3}}} = \sqrt{1.525 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.03905 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3:** Effective length for pin–pin column, $K = 1.0$.

$$L_e = K L = 1.0 \times 3.5 = 3.5 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4:** Slenderness ratio.

$$\lambda = \frac{L_e}{r} = \frac{3.5}{0.03905} = 89.6$$

**Step 5:** Critical slenderness ratio for given material.

$$\lambda_0 = \sqrt{\frac{2\pi^2 E}{\sigma_y}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times \pi^2 \times 200 \times 10^9}{250 \times 10^6}} = \sqrt{15,791} = 125.7$$

**Step 6:** Classification.

Since $\lambda = 89.6 < \lambda_0 = 125.7$ and $\lambda > 0$ (non-stocky), the column is **INTERMEDIATE**, and **Johnson's formula** must be used. **(7 marks)**

---

**Part (b) — Critical buckling load (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Compute the constant $b$ in Johnson's formula.

$$b = \frac{\sigma_y^2}{4\pi^2 E} = \frac{(250 \times 10^6)^2}{4\pi^2 \times 200 \times 10^9} = \frac{6.25 \times 10^{16}}{7.896 \times 10^{12}} = 7.915 \times 10^3 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the critical stress.

$$\sigma_{cr} = \sigma_y - b\lambda^2 = 250 \times 10^6 - 7.915 \times 10^3 \times (89.6)^2$$

$$\sigma_{cr} = 250 \times 10^6 - 7.915 \times 10^3 \times 8028 = 250 \times 10^6 - 6.353 \times 10^7$$

$$\sigma_{cr} = 250 \times 10^6 - 63.53 \times 10^6 = 186.47 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 186.47 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the critical load.

$$P_{cr} = \sigma_{cr} \times A = 186.47 \times 10^6 \times 3.456 \times 10^{-3} = 644,440 \text{ N} \approx 644.4 \text{ kN}$$

> **Validity check:** $\sigma_{cr} = 186.47$ MPa $< \sigma_y = 250$ MPa ✓ (Johnson's formula valid)

$$\boxed{P_{cr} \approx 644.4 \text{ kN}} \quad \textbf{(7 marks)}$$

---

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model Question]**
> **Q3B (14 marks).** A long, simply supported rectangular plate of a ship's deck plating has dimensions $a = 1200$ mm (length), $b = 600$ mm (width, loaded direction), thickness $t = 8$ mm. Material: structural steel with $E = 210$ GPa, $\nu = 0.3$. The plate is subjected to uniform in-plane compressive stress along its length.
>
> **(a)** Derive Bryan's formula for the critical buckling stress of the plate. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Calculate the critical buckling stress for the given plate. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q3B:

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

[Refer to **Derivation 4** in Section 3 above. The candidate is expected to write out the Kirchhoff plate equation, propose the Navier trigonometric series as the assumed buckled shape, apply boundary conditions, derive the expression for $N_{x,cr}$, and convert to stress using $D = Et^3/[12(1-\nu^2)]$. The final boxed result is:]

$$\sigma_{cr}^{plate} = \frac{k \pi^2 E}{12(1-\nu^2)} \left(\frac{t}{b}\right)^2, \qquad k = \left(\frac{m}{\alpha} + \frac{\alpha}{m}\right)^2$$

**(7 marks for the complete derivation as shown in Section 3.4)**

---

**Part (b) — Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Aspect ratio.

$$\alpha = \frac{a}{b} = \frac{1200}{600} = 2.0$$

**Step 2:** Buckling coefficient $k$ for simply supported plate, trying $m=1$:

$$k(m=1) = \left(\frac{1}{2} + 2\right)^2 = (2.5)^2 = 6.25$$

Try $m=2$:

$$k(m=2) = \left(\frac{2}{2} + \frac{2}{2}\right)^2 = (1+1)^2 = 4.0$$

Try $m=3$:

$$k(m=3) = \left(\frac{3}{2} + \frac{2}{3}\right)^2 = (1.5 + 0.667)^2 = (2.167)^2 = 4.694$$

Minimum is at $m=2$: $k_{min} = 4.0$ (2 half-waves along length).

**Step 3:** Plate flexural rigidity factor.

$$\frac{\pi^2 E}{12(1-\nu^2)} = \frac{\pi^2 \times 210 \times 10^9}{12(1-0.09)} = \frac{2.072 \times 10^{12}}{10.92} = 1.898 \times 10^{11} \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 4:** Critical stress.

$$\sigma_{cr} = k_{min} \times 1.898 \times 10^{11} \times \left(\frac{0.008}{0.600}\right)^2 = 4.0 \times 1.898 \times 10^{11} \times 1.778 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\sigma_{cr} = 4.0 \times 3.374 \times 10^7 = 1.350 \times 10^8 \text{ Pa} = 135.0 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{cr}^{plate} \approx 135.0 \text{ MPa}} \quad \textbf{(7 marks)}$$

> **Working stress** (with safety factor 1.5, e.g., DNV): $\sigma_{allow} = 135/1.5 = 90$ MPa, well below yield — buckling is the critical design consideration.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks in Buckling Theory questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the effective length factor $K$** — many candidates simply use $L$ instead of $L_e = KL$. This is the single most common error and costs 2–3 marks immediately.
> 2. **Using the wrong $I$** — when the cross-section is asymmetric (e.g., I-beam, channel), the *minimum* $I$ governs the buckling axis. Using $I_{max}$ gives a falsely high $P_{cr}$.
> 3. **Applying Euler's formula to an intermediate or short column** — the formula gives an unphysical answer (sometimes $P_{cr}$ larger than the yield load). Always classify the column first.
> 4. **Confusing $b$ and $a$ in the plate formula** — $b$ is the dimension in the *direction of the applied load*, not the longer side. Misidentifying it by a factor of 2 changes the answer by a factor of 4.
> 5. **Omitting units in intermediate steps** — KTU examiners explicitly deduct 1 mark for missing units in the final answer.
> 6. **Forgetting the validity check** — always verify $\sigma_{cr} < \sigma_y$ for Euler or that $\lambda < \lambda_0$ for Johnson.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Buckling = stability failure**, not strength failure; load at which straight equilibrium becomes unstable.
- [x] **Euler's formula:** $P_{cr} = \pi^2 EI / L_e^2$ — valid **only** when $\sigma_{cr} \le \sigma_y$.
- [x] **Effective length $L_e = K L$** depends on end restraints; remember $K = 1.0, 0.5, 2.0, 0.7$ for the four classical cases.
- [x] **Slenderness ratio** $\lambda = L_e / r$ — primary classifier of column behaviour.
- [x] **Critical slenderness ratio** $\lambda_0 = \sqrt{2\pi^2 E / \sigma_y} \approx 132.7$ for mild steel.
- [x] **Johnson's parabolic formula** $\sigma_{cr} = \sigma_y [1 - (\sigma_y / 4\pi^2 E)\lambda^2]$ for intermediate columns.
- [x] **Plate buckling (Bryan):** $\sigma_{cr}^{plate} = k \pi^2 E / [12(1-\nu^2)] \cdot (t/b)^2$, with $k_{min} = 4.0$ (SSSS) or $6.97$ (SCSC) for long plates.
- [x] **$b$ in plate formula = width in load direction**, NOT the longer side.
- [x] **Naval architecture applications:** hull plating, stiffener webs, masts, deck pillars, submarine pressure hulls.
- [x] **Classification society rules** (DNV, LR, ABS, BV) are empirical buckling design codes with safety factors and imperfection reductions.
- [x] **Always perform validity check** at the end: confirm formula regime applies; include units throughout.
- [x] **Cylindrical shell buckling** for submarine pressure hulls: $\sigma_{cr} = E(t/R)/\sqrt{3(1-\nu^2)}$.
- [x] **For very long plates, the optimum $m$** is found by minimizing $k = (m/\alpha + \alpha/m)^2$ over integer $m$.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Euler's Formula for Long Columns
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST303 - SOLID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Euler's Formula for Long Columns

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Euler's Buckling Theory (Leonhard Euler, 1757):** A slender structural member subjected to an axial compressive load will fail by **lateral elastic instability (buckling)** at a critical load value far below its direct compressive yield strength, when the load reaches a threshold governed solely by the column's flexural rigidity ($EI$) and its effective length ($L_e$).

The **Euler Critical Load** is the axial compressive load at which a perfectly straight, pin-ended, slender column becomes unstable and assumes a deflected shape, mathematically expressed as:

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^{\,2}}$$

Where:
- $P_{cr}$ = Critical (Euler) buckling load [in Newtons, **N**]
- $E$ = Young's Modulus of the column material [in N/mm² or **GPa**]
- $I$ = Minimum Area Moment of Inertia of the cross-section about the axis of buckling [in mm⁴]
- $L_e$ = Effective length of the column [in mm], dependent on end conditions
- $L_e = K \cdot L$ where $L$ is the actual length and $K$ is the **Effective Length Factor**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Explanation — "The Ruler on the Desk":**
> 
> Take a thin plastic ruler and press down on its ends between your palms along its length. Initially, nothing visible happens. Push harder, and suddenly — *snap* — it flicks sideways into a curved shape. The ruler didn't crush; it **bent sideways** while the load was still tiny compared to the ruler's material crushing strength.
> 
> This sideways snap is **buckling**. For *very long, thin* members (long columns), the failure mode is NOT material yielding, but **geometric instability**. Euler captured the exact load at which this snap occurs.

In naval architecture, this matters critically for:
- **Masts and kingposts** of sailing vessels
- **Struts in ship superstructures**
- **Hull girders** subjected to compressive longitudinal bending (sagging/hogging)
- **Davit arms, deck cranes, and A-frame structures**

## 1.3 Underlying Assumptions of Euler's Theory

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Euler formula is valid **ONLY** when ALL of the following assumptions hold:
> 
> 1. The column is **initially perfectly straight** (no initial curvature).
> 2. The load is **purely axial** and applied at the geometric centroid.
> 3. The column material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic** (obeys Hooke's Law: $\sigma = E\varepsilon$).
> 4. The cross-section is **uniform** along the entire length.
> 5. **Self-weight is negligible** compared to the applied load.
> 6. The column **fails by elastic buckling only** — not by crushing/yielding.
> 7. End joints impose **ideal rotational restraints** (frictionless pins, perfect fixity, etc.).

When a column is too short, Euler's formula over-predicts the critical load (because the material yields before it buckles). Such columns are governed by empirical formulae (e.g., Johnson's formula, Rankine's formula, IS 800:2007 curves).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Column Buckling

A column is a structural member whose length is much greater than its lateral dimension, loaded along its longitudinal axis. As the axial load $P$ increases:

- For **short columns**: Failure occurs by direct compression/crushing at yield stress $\sigma_y$.
- For **long columns**: Failure occurs by sudden sideways deflection (buckling) at a stress well below $\sigma_y$.
- For **intermediate columns**: A combination of yielding and buckling governs.

Euler's formula is applicable in the **long-column regime** where failure is purely elastic.

## 2.2 Effective Length & End Conditions

The effective length $L_e$ replaces the actual length $L$ in the Euler equation to account for the rotational restraint offered by the column's end supports. The standard values of $K$ (Effective Length Factor) for the four classical end conditions are:

| End Condition | Sketch Description | $K$ | Effective Length $L_e$ | Buckling Shape |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Both ends pinned (hinged) | Free to rotate, no translation | $1.0$ | $L$ | Single half sine wave |
| One end fixed, other end free | Cantilever (e.g., flagpole) | $2.0$ | $2L$ | Quarter sine wave |
| Both ends fixed | No rotation, no translation | $0.5$ | $0.5L$ | Two half waves (S-curve) |
| One end fixed, other end pinned | Built-in at one end | $0.7$ ($\approx 1/\sqrt{2}$) | $L/\sqrt{2}$ | One-and-quarter wave |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning of $K$:** A perfectly fixed end is twice as effective as a perfectly free end, and pin-ended columns form the baseline. The smaller the $K$, the more buckling resistance the supports provide.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^{\,2}}$ | Euler Critical Buckling Load | N |
| 2 | $L_e = K \cdot L$ | Effective Length | mm |
| 3 | $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{P_{cr}}{A} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E}{\lambda^{\,2}}$ | Critical Buckling Stress | N/mm² |
| 4 | $\lambda = \dfrac{L_e}{k} = \dfrac{L_e}{\sqrt{I/A}}$ | Slenderness Ratio (dimensionless) | — |
| 5 | $k = \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{A}}$ | Radius of Gyration of Cross-Section | mm |
| 6 | $I_{min} = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{64}$ (solid circular) | Minimum Moment of Inertia | mm⁴ |
| 7 | $I_{min} = \dfrac{b h^3}{12}$ (rectangular, $h \lt b$) | Minimum Moment of Inertia | mm⁴ |
| 8 | $\lambda_{min} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\pi^2 E}{\sigma_y}}$ | Limiting Slenderness Ratio | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross-section selection rule:** Buckling always occurs about the **weaker principal axis** — i.e., the axis with the **minimum** radius of gyration $k_{min}$. Therefore, always use $I_{min}$ and $k_{min}$ in the Euler formula.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Naval Architecture:** Determines the safe compressive load on hull plate panels, deck pillars, and masts. Ship hulls in **hogging** (sagging from bow/stern) experience immense compressive longitudinal stress, and Euler buckling governs slender plate/stiffener panels.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Design of piston rods, push rods, valve stems.
- **Civil Engineering:** Columns in buildings, bridge piers, scaffolding poles.
- **Aerospace:** Stringers and longerons in aircraft fuselage.

## 2.5 Slenderness Ratio — The Governing Parameter

The **Slenderness Ratio** $\lambda = L_e / k$ is the master classifier. It separates "long" from "short" behaviour:

- **Long Column (Euler regime):** $\lambda \geq \lambda_{min}$, where $\lambda_{min} = \pi\sqrt{2E/\sigma_y}$
- **Short Column (Crushing regime):** $\lambda \lt \lambda_{min}$

A **mild steel** column ($\sigma_y = 250$ MPa, $E = 200$ GPa) has $\lambda_{min} \approx 125.7$. Any steel column with $\lambda \geq 125.7$ is a *long column* and Euler's formula applies directly.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Euler's Formula for a Pin-Ended Column

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

Consider a slender column of length $L$, flexural rigidity $EI$, subjected to an axial compressive load $P$ at its centroid. Both ends are **pinned** (hinged). Let the column deflect laterally by a small amount $y$ at a distance $x$ from the bottom support.

### 3.1.2 Governing Differential Equation

The bending moment at section $x$ is purely due to the axial load $P$ acting through the lateral deflection $y$:

$$M(x) = -P \cdot y$$

(The negative sign indicates that $P$ creates a bending moment that opposes the curvature of the deflected column.)

From the **Euler-Bernoulli beam equation**:

$$EI \frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = M(x) = -P \cdot y$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} + \frac{P}{EI} \cdot y = 0$$

### 3.1.3 Solution of the Differential Equation

Define a constant $k^2 = P / (EI)$. The equation becomes:

$$\frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} + k^2 y = 0$$

The general solution to this second-order linear homogeneous ODE with constant coefficients is:

$$y(x) = A \cos(kx) + B \sin(kx)$$

The first derivative (slope):

$$\frac{dy}{dx} = -A k \sin(kx) + B k \cos(kx)$$

### 3.1.4 Application of Boundary Conditions

**Boundary Condition 1:** At $x = 0$ (bottom pin), the deflection is zero: $y(0) = 0$.

$$y(0) = A \cos(0) + B \sin(0) = A \cdot 1 + B \cdot 0 = A = 0$$

Therefore $A = 0$, and the solution reduces to:

$$y(x) = B \sin(kx)$$

**Boundary Condition 2:** At $x = L$ (top pin), the deflection is again zero: $y(L) = 0$.

$$y(L) = B \sin(kL) = 0$$

### 3.1.5 The Critical (Trivial vs. Non-Trivial) Solution

For a non-trivial solution (the column actually buckles, $B \neq 0$), we require:

$$\sin(kL) = 0$$

This transcendental equation is satisfied when:

$$kL = n\pi, \quad \text{where } n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

The **lowest** (first-mode) buckling load corresponds to $n = 1$:

$$kL = \pi \quad \Rightarrow \quad k = \frac{\pi}{L}$$

### 3.1.6 Back-Substitution to Find $P_{cr}$

Recall $k^2 = P / (EI)$:

$$k^2 = \frac{\pi^2}{L^2} = \frac{P_{cr}}{EI}$$

Solving for $P_{cr}$:

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{L^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **Euler Critical Load for a pin-ended column**. For other end conditions, simply replace $L$ with the effective length $L_e = K L$:
> $$\boxed{P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^{\,2}} = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{(K L)^2}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Critical Stress and Slenderness Ratio

The critical stress is the critical load divided by the cross-sectional area:

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{P_{cr}}{A} = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{A L_e^{\,2}}$$

Substitute $I = A k^2$ (where $k$ = radius of gyration):

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E (A k^2)}{A L_e^{\,2}} = \frac{\pi^2 E}{(L_e / k)^2} = \frac{\pi^2 E}{\lambda^{\,2}}$$

where $\lambda = L_e / k$ is the slenderness ratio.

## 3.3 Derivation of Limiting Slenderness Ratio

Euler's formula is valid only when the critical stress does **not** exceed the yield stress $\sigma_y$ (otherwise, the material yields first):

$$\sigma_{cr} \leq \sigma_y$$

$$\frac{\pi^2 E}{\lambda^{\,2}} \leq \sigma_y \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda \geq \sqrt{\frac{\pi^2 E}{\sigma_y}} = \pi \sqrt{\frac{E}{\sigma_y}}$$

In many texts, this is written as:

$$\lambda_{min} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \pi^2 E}{\sigma_y}}$$

(The factor of 2 arises when one uses $\sigma_y$ as the allowable stress with a factor of safety of 2 — a code-specific convention.)

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Behaviour Across Column Lengths

| Regime | Slenderness $\lambda$ | Failure Mode | Governing Formula |
| :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| Long Column | $\lambda \geq \lambda_{min}$ | Elastic Buckling | $P_{cr} = \pi^2 E I / L_e^{\,2}$ |
| Intermediate | Moderate $\lambda$ | Inelastic Buckling | Johnson's / Rankine's Formula |
| Short Column / Strut | $\lambda \lt \lambda_{min}$ | Crushing/Yielding | $P = \sigma_y \cdot A$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: End-Condition Classification

The following block diagram maps the analytical decision flow for selecting the correct effective length factor $K$ in Euler's formula.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Slender Column Under Axial Compression] --> B{Identify End Conditions}
    B --> C1[Both Ends Pinned]
    B --> C2[One End Fixed, One End Free]
    B --> C3[Both Ends Fixed]
    B --> C4[One End Fixed, One End Pinned]

    C1 --> D1[K = 1.0]
    C2 --> D2[K = 2.0]
    C3 --> D3[K = 0.5]
    C4 --> D4[K approx 0.7]

    D1 --> E1[Le = L]
    D2 --> E2[Le = 2L]
    D3 --> E3[Le = L / 2]
    D4 --> E4[Le = L / sqrt 2]

    E1 --> F[Compute Imin about weak axis]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F

    F --> G[Compute Radius of Gyration k = sqrt Imin over A]
    G --> H[Compute Slenderness Lambda = Le over k]
    H --> I{Lambda compared to Lambda min?}

    I -- Greater than or equal to Lambda min --> J[Long Column: Apply Pcr = pi squared E I over Le squared]
    I -- Less than Lambda min --> K[Short or Intermediate Column: Use Rankine or Johnson]

    J --> L[Safe Axial Load P allowable = Pcr over FoS]
    K --> L
```

## 4.2 Buckled Shapes for the Four Classical End Conditions

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph PinnedPinned["Both Ends Pinned: K = 1.0"]
        PPa[Pin] --- PPb["Buckled Shape: Single Half Sine Wave"]
        PPb --- PPc[Pin]
    end

    subgraph FixedFree["Fixed-Free: K = 2.0"]
        FFa[Fixed Base] --- FFb["Buckled Shape: Quarter Wave"]
        FFb --- FFc[Free Tip]
    end

    subgraph FixedFixed["Both Ends Fixed: K = 0.5"]
        FFa2[Fixed] --- FFa3["Buckled Shape: S Curve Two Half Waves"]
        FFa3 --- FFa4[Fixed]
    end

    subgraph FixedPinned["Fixed-Pinned: K approx 0.7"]
        FPa[Fixed] --- FPa2["Buckled Shape: Asymmetric Wave"]
        FPa2 --- FPa3[Pin]
    end
```

## 4.3 Load vs. Deflection Schematic (Conceptual)

| Axial Load $P$ | Deflection $y$ | Column State |
| :---: | :---: | :--- |
| $P \lt P_{cr}$ | $y = 0$ (theoretically) | Stable, no lateral deflection |
| $P = P_{cr}$ | Any $y$ (neutral equilibrium) | Critical — buckled equilibrium possible |
| $P \gt P_{cr}$ | Column collapses | Unstable — runaway deflection |

> [!NOTE]
