topics.title: Slenderness Ratio
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST303 - SOLID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Slenderness Ratio

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Column Theory** (a foundational pillar of Solid Mechanics and Naval Architecture), the **Slenderness Ratio** is defined as the dimensionless numerical quantity that mathematically characterizes the geometric propensity of a structural compression member to fail by **flexural buckling** rather than by direct material crushing. 

It is formally expressed as the ratio of the **Effective Length (Effective Length)** of the column to the **Least Radius of Gyration (k_min)** of its cross-section:

$$
\lambda = \frac{L_e}{k_{\min}}
$$

Where:
- **L_e** = Effective Length of the column, determined by the end-fixity conditions (in **mm** or **m**).
- **k_min** = Least Radius of Gyration, defined as $k_{\min} = \sqrt{\dfrac{I_{\min}}{A}}$ (in **mm** or **m**).
- **I_min** = Least Moment of Inertia of the cross-sectional area about the centroidal axis.
- **A** = Cross-sectional area of the column.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Slenderness Ratio is NOT a measure of the actual length of the column. It is a comparative geometric index that quantifies *how slender* the column is relative to its cross-sectional stiffness. A higher λ indicates a more slender, buckling-prone column.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Toothpick vs. Brick" Intuition

Imagine you have two identical blocks of wood, each measuring **10 cm × 2 cm × 2 cm**:
- **Case A (Low Slenderness Ratio):** You stand the block vertically on its square face. It is short and stout. If you press down on it, it will likely **crush** at the top face. You cannot bend it easily because it is too "stubby" to buckle.
- **Case B (High Slenderness Ratio):** You take a long, thin piece of wood, say **100 cm × 1 cm × 1 cm**. Press down on its end. Before you can crush it, it will suddenly **snap sideways** — this is **buckling**.

Both objects are made of the same material, but their *geometric proportions* dictate entirely different failure modes. The **Slenderness Ratio** is the mathematical number that captures this critical difference in *proportional slenderness*.

In **Naval Architecture**, this concept is paramount when designing:
- **Masts and kingposts** of sailing vessels.
- **Hatch coaming stiffeners** in cargo ships.
- **Bulkhead stiffeners** under compressive sea-pressure loads.
- **Propeller shaft struts** in single-screw vessels.

---

## 1.3 Physical Significance & Classification Thresholds

The numerical value of λ acts as a *switch* that selects which strength formula the designer must use:

| Slenderness Range | Classification | Governing Failure Mode | Applicable Theory |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\lambda < \lambda_c$ | **Short Column** | Direct Crushing / Yielding | Johnson's Parabolic / Crushing Stress |
| $\lambda \approx \lambda_c$ | **Medium / Intermediate Column** | Combined Crushing & Buckling | Rankine's Formula / IS Code Empirical |
| $\lambda > \lambda_c$ | **Long Column (Slender)** | Pure Elastic Buckling | Euler's Theory |

Where $\lambda_c$ is the **Critical Slenderness Ratio**, derived by equating Euler's elastic buckling stress to the material's crushing/yield stress:

$$
\lambda_c = \pi \sqrt{\frac{E}{\sigma_c}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Values of $\lambda_c$:**
> - **Mild Steel:** $\lambda_c \approx 80$ (since $E = 2 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$, $\sigma_c = 320 \text{ N/mm}^2$).
> - **Cast Iron:** $\lambda_c \approx 80$.
> - **Timber:** $\lambda_c \approx 140$ (since $E \approx 12.5 \times 10^3 \text{ N/mm}^2$, $\sigma_c \approx 20 \text{ N/mm}^2$).
> - **Wrought Iron:** $\lambda_c \approx 90$.

---

## 1.4 Why Effective Length? — The Boundary Condition Insight

The actual physical length L of a column rarely equals its *effective buckling length* L_e. The end conditions (how the column is restrained at its tips) determine how much of the column actually participates in the buckling deformation.

> [!TIP]
> **Naval Architecture Tip:** When designing a **mast strut** that is pin-connected at both ends (a common marine detail), the designer uses $L_e = L$. But if the strut is welded rigidly to a deck plate at one end and pinned at the top, the effective length becomes $L_e = \frac{L}{\sqrt{2}}$ — substantially shorter, which means **higher buckling resistance for the same physical column**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Logic — Step-by-Step Decomposition

To fully appreciate the slenderness ratio, we must deconstruct the two geometric quantities that compose it:

### Step 1: Effective Length (L_e)
The **Effective Length** is a fictitious length equal to the distance between two adjacent **inflection points** (points of zero bending moment) on the buckled shape of the column. It depends exclusively on the **end-fixity conditions**, NOT on the cross-section or material.

### Step 2: Radius of Gyration (k)
The **Radius of Gyration** represents how the cross-sectional area is *distributed about the centroidal axis*. For any plane figure:

$$
k = \sqrt{\frac{I}{A}}
$$

A column will always buckle about the axis with the **least moment of inertia** (the "weak axis"), hence we **must** use $k_{\min}$.

### Step 3: Assembling the Ratio
$$
\lambda = \frac{L_e}{k_{\min}}
$$

This dimensionless quantity tells us how many "characteristic widths" of the column fit into its effective buckling length.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** The absolute value bars `|x|` are written as `\vert x \vert` to prevent breaking markdown table syntax.

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\lambda = \dfrac{L_e}{k_{\min}}$ | Definition of Slenderness Ratio | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $k_{\min} = \sqrt{\dfrac{I_{\min}}{A}}$ | Least Radius of Gyration | mm or m |
| 3 | $L_e = \alpha \cdot L$ | Effective Length (α = end-condition factor) | mm or m |
| 4 | $\lambda_c = \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\sigma_c}}$ | Critical Slenderness Ratio | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $P_E = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I_{\min}}{L_e^{\,2}}$ | Euler's Critical Buckling Load | N or kN |
| 6 | $\sigma_E = \dfrac{\pi^2 E}{\lambda^2}$ | Euler's Critical Buckling Stress | N/mm² |
| 7 | $P_R = \dfrac{\sigma_c \cdot A}{1 + a\left(\lambda\right)^2}$ | Rankine's Crippling Load | N or kN |
| 8 | $\sigma_c = \dfrac{P}{A}$ | Direct Compressive / Crushing Stress | N/mm² |

---

## 2.3 Effective Length Factors (α) for Various End Conditions

The factor α is **the single most important board-exam parameter** you must memorize. It directly modifies the effective length:

| Slenderness Column Type | End Condition (Top / Bottom) | Effective Length Factor (α) | $L_e$ in terms of L |
|---|---|:---:|:---:|
| Both Ends Hinged | Pinned / Pinned | 1.0 | $L_e = L$ |
| Both Ends Fixed | Fixed / Fixed | 0.5 | $L_e = L/2$ |
| One End Fixed, Other Hinged | Fixed / Pinned | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707$ | $L_e = L/\sqrt{2}$ |
| One End Fixed, Other Free | Fixed / Free | 2.0 | $L_e = 2L$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Memory Aid (for KTU Viva & ESE):** "**Hinged-Hinged = 1, Fixed-Fixed = Half, Fixed-Hinged = 1 by Root 2, Fixed-Free = 2.**"

---

## 2.4 Material-Specific Rankine's Constant (a)

Rankine's constant 'a' is a dimensionless empirical coefficient that appears in Rankine's formula:

| Material | Rankine's Constant (a) | Crushing Stress $\sigma_c$ (N/mm²) | Young's Modulus E (N/mm²) |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---: |
| Mild Steel | $\dfrac{1}{7500}$ | 320 | $2 \times 10^5$ |
| Cast Iron | $\dfrac{1}{1600}$ | 550 | $1.2 \times 10^5$ |
| Wrought Iron | $\dfrac{1}{9000}$ | 250 | $1.9 \times 10^5$ |
| Timber | $\dfrac{1}{750}$ | 20 | $1.25 \times 10^4$ |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **Naval Architecture** specifically:

1. **Ship Masts & Kingposts:** Subjected to combined compression from rigging loads and bending from wind. A high slenderness ratio mast requires explicit buckling checks per classification society rules (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS).
2. **Engine Bedplates & Foundation Bolts:** Long slender bolt connections act as columns; slenderness ratio determines whether to apply Euler or direct stress formulas.
3. **Bulkhead Corrugation:** Vertical corrugations act as stiffeners between decks; designers check λ to prevent inter-panel buckling.
4. **Stern Frame & Rudder Stock Supports:** Slender columns supporting overhang weights need Rankine-Gordon verification.

The slenderness ratio therefore acts as the **decision parameter** between crushing-strength design and buckling-stability design — a pivotal distinction in any marine structural integrity assessment.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Critical Slenderness Ratio ($\lambda_c$)

The critical slenderness ratio is the threshold value of λ at which the **Euler buckling stress** becomes exactly equal to the **crushing (yield) stress** of the material. This derivation is a frequent KTU 14-mark question.

### Given:
- Euler's critical stress: $\sigma_E = \dfrac{\pi^2 E}{\lambda^2}$
- Direct crushing stress: $\sigma_c = \sigma_c$ (material property)
- At the transition point, $\sigma_E = \sigma_c$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1:** Write Euler's critical stress as a function of slenderness ratio.

$$
\sigma_E = \frac{\pi^2 E}{\lambda^2}
$$

*Explanation:* This equation is obtained by dividing Euler's critical load $P_E = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I_{\min}}{L_e^{\,2}}$ by the cross-sectional area $A$, and then substituting $\lambda^2 = \dfrac{L_e^{\,2}}{k_{\min}^{\,2}} = \dfrac{L_e^{\,2} A}{I_{\min}}$.

**Step 2:** Write the crushing stress condition.

$$
\sigma_c = \frac{P}{A}
$$

*Explanation:* This is the direct compressive stress at which the material yields or crushes under pure axial loading.

**Step 3:** Apply the equality condition at the boundary between short and long column behavior.

$$
\sigma_E = \sigma_c
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the expressions.

$$
\frac{\pi^2 E}{\lambda_c^{\,2}} = \sigma_c
$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $\lambda_c$.

$$
\lambda_c^{\,2} = \frac{\pi^2 E}{\sigma_c}
$$

$$
\lambda_c = \pi \sqrt{\frac{E}{\sigma_c}}
$$

*Explanation:* This is the **Critical Slenderness Ratio** — the boundary value that separates elastic buckling behavior from material yielding.

---

## 3.2 Verification for Mild Steel (Board-Style Substitution)

Given: $E = 2 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$, $\sigma_c = 320 \text{ N/mm}^2$.

$$
\lambda_c = \pi \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10^5}{320}}
$$

$$
\lambda_c = \pi \sqrt{625}
$$

$$
\lambda_c = \pi \times 25
$$

$$
\lambda_c \approx 78.54 \approx 80 \text{ (dimensionless)}
$$

*Engineering Interpretation:* Any mild steel column with $\lambda > 80$ must be designed by **Euler's buckling theory**; any with $\lambda < 80$ must be designed by **direct stress / Johnson / Rankine theory**.

---

## 3.3 Semi-Theoretical Derivation of Rankine's Formula (Related Concept)

Rankine's formula is the *transition equation* for medium slenderness columns. It is a **pure empirical modification** of the crushing stress formula:

**Step 1:** Start with direct crushing stress.

$$
\sigma_d = \frac{P_d}{A}
$$

**Step 2:** Express the column's resistance as a *combined* stress — crushing plus buckling correction.

$$
\sigma_R = \frac{\sigma_c}{1 + a(\lambda)^2}
$$

**Step 3:** Equate average stress to applied load divided by area.

$$
\frac{P_R}{A} = \frac{\sigma_c}{1 + a(\lambda)^2}
$$

**Step 4:** Solve for the crippling (Rankine) load $P_R$.

$$
P_R = \frac{\sigma_c \cdot A}{1 + a \left(\frac{L_e}{k_{\min}}\right)^2}
$$

*Explanation:* This formula gracefully transitions from crushing-dominated behavior at low λ (denominator ≈ 1) to buckling-dominated behavior at high λ (denominator ≈ a·λ²).

---

## 3.4 Relationship Between Euler's Load and Slenderness Ratio

Starting from Euler's load:

$$
P_E = \frac{\pi^2 E I_{\min}}{L_e^{\,2}}
$$

Substitute $I_{\min} = A k_{\min}^{\,2}$:

$$
P_E = \frac{\pi^2 E A k_{\min}^{\,2}}{L_e^{\,2}}
$$

Rearrange using $\lambda = \dfrac{L_e}{k_{\min}}$, so $L_e^{\,2} = \lambda^2 k_{\min}^{\,2}$:

$$
P_E = \frac{\pi^2 E A k_{\min}^{\,2}}{\lambda^2 k_{\min}^{\,2}}
$$

$$
P_E = \frac{\pi^2 E A}{\lambda^2}
$$

*Engineering Insight:* Notice how the cross-sectional geometry cancels out — the Euler buckling load depends ONLY on the slenderness ratio λ and not on the specific shape of the cross-section. This is why λ is the *universal* column design parameter.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Condition | Slenderness Range | Failure Mode | Design Formula |
|---|:---:|---|---|
| Long Column | $\lambda > \lambda_c$ | Elastic Buckling | $P_E = \dfrac{\pi^2 E A}{\lambda^2}$ |
| Critical Point | $\lambda = \lambda_c$ | Transition | $\sigma_E = \sigma_c$ |
| Medium Column | $\lambda < \lambda_c$ | Combined | $P_R = \dfrac{\sigma_c A}{1 + a\lambda^2}$ |
| Short Column | $\lambda \to 0$ | Pure Crushing | $P_d = \sigma_c A$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Flow — Slenderness Ratio Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Compression Member: Start] --> B[Calculate Slenderness Ratio lambda = Le divided by k_min]
    B --> C{Compare lambda with lambda_critical}
    C -->|lambda greater than lambda_c| D[Classify as LONG COLUMN]
    C -->|lambda equals lambda_c| E[Classify as CRITICAL THRESHOLD]
    C -->|lambda less than lambda_c| F[Classify as SHORT or MEDIUM COLUMN]
    D --> G[Apply EULERS Buckling Formula P_E equals pi squared EA divided by lambda squared]
    E --> H[Apply Crushing Stress equals Euler Stress Equality]
    F --> I{Is lambda less than 30?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Apply DIRECT CRUSHING Formula P equals sigma_c times A]
    I -->|No| K[Apply RANKINES Formula P_R equals sigma_c times A divided by 1 plus a lambda squared]
    G --> L[Final Design Load Selected]
    H --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
```

## 4.2 End Condition Reference Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph A1[Both Ends Hinged]
        H1P[Bottom: Pin Support] --- H1M[Column Body: Length L] --- H1T[Top: Pin Support]
    end
    subgraph A2[Both Ends Fixed]
        H2P[Bottom: Fixed Support] === H2M[Column Body: Length L] === H2T[Top: Fixed Support]
    end
    subgraph A3[One End Fixed Other Hinged]
        H3P[Bottom: Fixed Support] === H3M[Column Body: Length L] --- H3T[Top: Pin Support]
    end
    subgraph A4[One End Fixed Other Free]
        H4P[Bottom: Fixed Support] === H4M[Column Body: Length L] -.-> H4T[Top: FREE No Support]
    end
    H1M --> H1R[Effective Length Le equals 1.0 L]
    H2M --> H2R[Effective Length Le equals 0.5 L]
    H3M --> H3R[Effective Length Le equals L by root 2]
    H4M --> H4R[Effective Length Le equals 2.0 L]
```

## 4.3 Functional Flow of a Column Strength Calculation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INPUT[Input Data: Length L, End Conditions, Cross Section A and I_min, Material E and sigma_c] --> CALCK[Step 1: Calculate k_min equals square root of I_min divided by A]
    CALCK --> CALCLE[Step 2: Calculate Effective Length Le from End Condition Factor]
    CALCLE --> CALLAMBDA[Step 3: Calculate Slenderness Ratio lambda equals Le divided by k_min]
    CALLAMBDA --> CALCLC[Step 4: Calculate Critical Slenderness Ratio lambda_c equals pi times square root of E divided by sigma_c]
    CALCLC --> DECIDE{Decision: lambda compared to lambda_c}
    DECIDE -->|Greater| EULER[Apply Euler: P equals pi squared E I_min divided by Le squared]
    DECIDE -->|Less or Equal| RANKINE[Apply Rankine: P equals sigma_c A divided by 1 plus a lambda squared]
    EULER --> OUTPUT[Output: Safe Buckling Load of the Column]
    RANKINE --> OUTPUT
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Slenderness Ratio. Mention its significance in column design.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Slenderness Ratio** is defined as the ratio of the effective length of a column to the least radius of gyration of its cross-section:

$$
\lambda = \frac{L_e}{k_{\min}}
$$

It is a **dimensionless geometric parameter** that classifies a compression member as either a **short column** (where failure occurs by crushing) or a **long/slender column** (where failure occurs by elastic buckling). The significance is that the magnitude of λ determines which strength formula the designer must apply — **Euler's formula for long columns**, **Rankine's formula for medium columns**, and **direct stress formula for very short columns**. **[3 Marks]**

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List the effective lengths of a column for the following end conditions: (i) Both ends fixed (ii) Both ends hinged (iii) One end fixed and other end free.**

**Model Answer:**

| End Condition | Effective Length $L_e$ |
|---|:---:|
| Both ends hinged | $L$ |
| Both ends fixed | $\dfrac{L}{2}$ |
| One end fixed and other hinged | $\dfrac{L}{\sqrt{2}}$ |
| One end fixed and other free | $2L$ |

For the three asked:
- **(i) Both ends fixed:** $L_e = L/2$ — **[1 Mark]**
- **(ii) Both ends hinged:** $L_e = L$ — **[1 Mark]**
- **(iii) One end fixed and other end free:** $L_e = 2L$ — **[1 Mark]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Adapted]`

A **hollow cylindrical cast iron column** has an **outer diameter of 200 mm** and an **inner diameter of 150 mm**. The column is **4 m long** and is **fixed at both ends**. It carries an **axial compressive load of 500 kN**. 

**Using Rankine's formula, find the factor of safety of the column. Take Rankine's constant a = 1/1600 and crushing stress σc = 550 N/mm².**

**(a) Compute the Rankine crippling load. (7 marks)**  
**(b) Determine the factor of safety and comment on the design safety. (7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Calculate Cross-Sectional Area A**

$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(D_o^2 - D_i^2\right) = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(200^2 - 150^2\right)
$$

$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(40000 - 22500\right) = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(17500\right)
$$

$$
A = 13744.47 \text{ mm}^2 \quad \text{[2 Marks]}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Moment of Inertia I**

$$
I = \frac{\pi}{64}\left(D_o^4 - D_i^4\right) = \frac{\pi}{64}\left(200^4 - 150^4\right)
$$

$$
I = \frac{\pi}{64}\left(1.6 \times 10^9 - 5.0625 \times 10^8\right)
$$

$$
I = \frac{\pi}{64}\left(1.09375 \times 10^9\right)
$$

$$
I = 5.367 \times 10^7 \text{ mm}^4 \quad \text{[2 Marks]}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Least Radius of Gyration k_min**

For a circular section, $k_{\min} = \dfrac{\sqrt{D_o^2 + D_i^2}}{4}$

$$
k_{\min} = \frac{\sqrt{200^2 + 150^2}}{4} = \frac{\sqrt{40000 + 22500}}{4} = \frac{\sqrt{62500}}{4}
$$

$$
k_{\min} = \frac{250}{4} = 62.5 \text{ mm} \quad \text{[1 Mark]}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate Effective Length L_e**

For both ends fixed: $L_e = \dfrac{L}{2} = \dfrac{4000}{2} = 2000 \text{ mm}$

**Step 5: Calculate Slenderness Ratio λ**

$$
\lambda = \frac{L_e}{k_{\min}} = \frac{2000}{62.5} = 32 \quad \text{[1 Mark]}
$$

**Step 6: Apply Rankine's Formula**

$$
P_R = \frac{\sigma_c \cdot A}{1 + a\lambda^2} = \frac{550 \times 13744.47}{1 + \frac{1}{1600} \times (32)^2}
$$

$$
P_R = \frac{7559458.5}{1 + \frac{1024}{1600}} = \frac{7559458.5}{1 + 0.64}
$$

$$
P_R = \frac{7559458.5}{1.64} = 4609438.1 \text{ N} \approx 4609.4 \text{ kN} \quad \text{[1 Mark]}
$$

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Calculate Factor of Safety (FOS)**

$$
\text{FOS} = \frac{P_R}{P_{\text{applied}}} = \frac{4609.4}{500}
$$

$$
\text{FOS} \approx 9.22 \quad \text{[4 Marks]}
$$

**Step 2: Comment on Design Safety**

The factor of safety of **9.22** is excessively high. While the column is safe, it is **over-designed**. The designer may:
- Reduce the cross-section dimensions (use smaller diameters), OR
- Increase the applied working load, OR  
- Use a more economical material grade.

This indicates **non-optimal material utilization**, which is undesirable in ship structural design where weight economy is critical. **Recommended FOS for marine columns is typically 3 to 5.** **[3 Marks]**

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Adapted]`

A **mild steel column of length 6 m** has a **solid circular cross-section of diameter 100 mm**. The column is **effectively hinged at both ends**. It is made of mild steel with **E = 2 × 10⁵ N/mm²** and **σc = 320 N/mm²**.

**(a) Determine whether the column is long or short by calculating both the slenderness ratio and critical slenderness ratio. (7 marks)**  
**(b) Compute the Euler buckling load and the allowable load using a factor of safety of 3. (7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Calculate Cross-Sectional Area A**

$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4}D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(100)^2 = 7853.98 \text{ mm}^2 \quad \text{[1 Mark]}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Moment of Inertia I**

$$
I = \frac{\pi}{64}D^4 = \frac{\pi}{64}(100)^4 = 4.909 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4 \quad \text{[1 Mark]}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Radius of Gyration k_min**

For a circular section:

$$
k_{\min} = \frac{D}{4} = \frac{100}{4} = 25 \text{ mm} \quad \text{[1 Mark]}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate Effective Length L_e**

Both ends hinged: $L_e = L = 6000 \text{ mm}$ **[1 Mark]**

**Step 5: Calculate Slenderness Ratio λ**

$$
\lambda = \frac{L_e}{k_{\min}} = \frac{6000}{25} = 240 \quad \text{[1 Mark]}
$$

**Step 6: Calculate Critical Slenderness Ratio λ_c**

$$
\lambda_c = \pi \sqrt{\frac{E}{\sigma_c}} = \pi \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10^5}{320}} = \pi \sqrt{625} = \pi \times 25 \approx 78.54 \quad \text{[1 Mark]}
$$

**Step 7: Comparison and Conclusion**

Since $\lambda = 240 > \lambda_c = 78.54$, the column is a **LONG column** and **Euler's theory is applicable**. **[1 Mark]**

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Apply Euler's Buckling Formula**

$$
P_E = \frac{\pi^2 E I_{\min}}{L_e^{\,2}}
$$

$$
P_E = \frac{\pi^2 \times (2 \times 10^5) \times (4.909 \times 10^6)}{(6000)^2}
$$

$$
P_E = \frac{9.6906 \times 10^{12}}{3.6 \times 10^7}
$$

$$
P_E = 269183.3 \text{ N} \approx 269.18 \text{ kN} \quad \text{[4 Marks]}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Allowable Load with FOS = 3**

$$
P_{\text{allow}} = \frac{P_E}{\text{FOS}} = \frac{269.18}{3}
$$

$$
P_{\text{allow}} \approx 89.73 \text{ kN} \quad \text{[3 Marks]}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to use $k_{\min}$:** Many students use $\sqrt{I/A}$ without specifying "minimum." Always use the axis with the LEAST moment of inertia.
> 2. **Wrong Effective Length:** Confusing "fixed-free" ($L_e = 2L$) with "fixed-fixed" ($L_e = L/2$) costs a full 3 marks in Part A and cascades into wrong λ in Part B.
> 3. **Mixing units:** Using $L$ in meters and $k$ in mm leads to dimensional nonsense. Be consistent.
> 4. **Applying Euler to a short column:** If $\lambda < \lambda_c$, Euler's formula OVER-estimates the load. Always check λ before choosing a formula.
> 5. **Not justifying the choice of formula:** In KTU 14-mark answers, examiners allocate 1–2 marks explicitly for stating *which formula* and *why*. Always write: "Since λ = ... > λ_c = ..., Euler's theory applies."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** $\lambda = \dfrac{L_e}{k_{\min}}$ — *always dimensionless*.
- **$k_{\min}$** is computed from the **weak axis** of the cross-section, never the strong axis.
- **Memorize effective lengths:** Hinged-Hinged = L, Fixed-Fixed = L/2, Fixed-Hinged = L/√2, Fixed-Free = 2L.
- **Critical Slenderness Ratio:** $\lambda_c = \pi \sqrt{E/\sigma_c}$ — the **decision boundary**.
- **For mild steel, $\lambda_c \approx 80$** — this number appears in nearly every KTU board question.
- **If $\lambda > \lambda_c$** → Use **Euler's formula** $P_E = \pi^2 E A / \lambda^2$.
- **If $\lambda < \lambda_c$** → Use **Rankine's formula** $P_R = \sigma_c A / (1 + a\lambda^2)$.
- **Rankine's constant 'a':** Mild Steel = 1/7500, Cast Iron = 1/1600, Timber = 1/750.
- **Euler's load is independent of cross-sectional shape** once λ is fixed — only area matters.
- **For ship masts and struts**, always check slenderness ratio against classification society rules.
- **Always state the formula selection criterion** (Euler vs. Rankine vs. Direct) with explicit numerical justification in the exam.
- **Units consistency** is mandatory: N-mm-N/mm² system throughout the calculation.
- **Final answer** must always carry the correct unit (kN or N) and a brief design safety comment.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thin-Walled Pressure Vessels: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCNST303 - SOLID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Thin-Walled Pressure Vessels: Introduction # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Pressure Vessel** is a closed, leak-proof container designed to hold a fluid (gas or liquid) at a pressure substantially different from the ambient atmospheric pressure. In naval architecture, pressure vessels are ubiquitous — ranging from submarine hulls, compressed air tanks, and hydraulic accumulators to boiler shells and LPG cargo tanks. A pressure vessel is classified as a **Thin-Walled Pressure Vessel** when the ratio of its wall thickness ($t$) to its internal radius ($r$) satisfies the inequality: $$\frac{t}{r} \leq \frac{1}{10}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight** > When $t/r \leq 1/10$ (some conservative texts use $t/r \leq 1/20$), the stress is assumed to be uniformly distributed across the thickness, and bending stresses are neglected. This allows the use of **Membrane Theory** instead of the more complex **Thick-Walled Cylinder Theory** (Lamé's Equations). The analysis of thin-walled pressure vessels is governed by two fundamental principal stresses: 1. **Hoop (Circumferential) Stress** — denoted $\sigma_h$ or $\sigma_\theta$ 2. **Longitudinal (Axial) Stress** — denoted $\sigma_l$ or $\sigma_a$ For a **Spherical Vessel**, both stresses are equal in magnitude, simplifying the analysis significantly. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Balloon Paradigm Imagine blowing up a **rubber balloon**. As you exhale pressurized air into it, the balloon expands. If you were to "freeze" that balloon at any instant, you would observe: - The rubber skin is being pulled in two distinct directions simultaneously: **around** its circumference (like a belt tightening) and **along** its length (like someone pulling both ends apart). - The rubber is extremely thin compared to its size — yet it successfully contains high internal pressure. This real-world analogy maps perfectly onto engineering pressure vessels. The "belt-tightening" force is your **hoop stress**, and the "end-pulling" force is your **longitudinal stress**. The balloon's skin, being thin, has nearly uniform tension throughout its thickness — this is the **membrane assumption** of thin-walled theory. > [!NOTE] **Why Naval Architects Care** > A submarine's pressure hull is essentially a massive thin-walled cylinder. Submarines must withstand **external hydrostatic pressure** from crushing ocean depths (which can exceed **70 MPa** at 7,000 m). Similarly, **CNG transport cylinders** onboard LNG carriers endure internal pressures up to **25 MPa**. ## 1.3 Classification of Pressure Vessels Pressure vessels are broadly categorized by geometry: | Geometry Type | Naval / Marine Application | Stress State | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Cylindrical** (with hemispherical / ellipsoidal / flat ends) | Submarine hulls, compressed air receivers, boiler shells | Biaxial ($\sigma_h \neq \sigma_l$) | | **Spherical** | LPG bullets, high-pressure gas tanks, early bathyscaphes | Equibiaxial ($\sigma_h = \sigma_l$) | | **Conical** | Funnels, transition pieces | Variable along meridian | > [!IMPORTANT] **Standard Metrics Used** > - Internal pressure: $p$ (in **MPa** or **N/mm²**) > - Internal diameter: $d$ (in **mm** or **m**) > - Wall thickness: $t$ (in **mm** or **m**) > - Allowable stress: $\sigma_{allow}$ (typically **$\sigma_y / FoS$**, where **FoS = Factor of Safety**) # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Fundamental Assumptions of Thin-Walled Pressure Vessel Theory The derivation of membrane stresses relies on several simplifying assumptions, all of which are **exam-favorite** points: 1. **Geometric thinness:** The wall thickness $t$ is small compared to the radius $r$ ($t/r \leq 0.1$). This ensures stresses do not vary significantly across the thickness. 2. **Membrane behavior:** The vessel wall behaves like a membrane — it carries only **in-plane (tensile) stresses**, with negligible **bending moments** and **shear stresses** normal to the surface. 3. **Material homogeneity and isotropy:** The vessel material (typically **marine-grade steel**, **aluminum alloy**, or **titanium**) has uniform mechanical properties in all directions. 4. **Uniform internal pressure:** The pressure $p$ acts uniformly and perpendicularly on every point of the internal wetted surface. 5. **Negligible weight of contents:** Self-weight of the contained fluid and the vessel itself are ignored relative to pressure-induced stresses (except in static head calculations for very tall vessels). 6. **Plane sections remain plane:** Stress distribution across any cross-section is uniform. ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of Stress Generation ### Step 1: Pressure Exerts Force on End Caps The internal pressure pushes against the **flat end caps** of a cylindrical vessel. The total force trying to push the cap off is: $$F_{axial} = p \cdot A_{end} = p \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$ This force must be resisted by the **wall material** distributed over the **circumferential cross-sectional area** of the shell ($A = \pi d t$). The resulting axial (longitudinal) stress is therefore derived as a simple force-balance. ### Step 2: Pressure Exerts Force on the Curved Wall Consider slicing the cylinder into **two halves** along a longitudinal (axial) plane. The internal pressure acting on the **projected area** of the diameter times length pushes the two halves apart. This force must be resisted by **two walls** of thickness $t$, each of length $L$ (the unit length assumed). ### Step 3: Equilibrium Yields the Two Principal Stresses Setting $\sum F = 0$ in each direction gives the classical thin-wall formulas. The hoop stress is **always twice** the longitudinal stress in a cylinder — a critical relationship students must memorize. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] **Master these formulas for KTU Board Examinations.** | # | Vessel Type | Stress Component | Formula | Ratio to Hoop | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **Cylindrical** | Hoop / Circumferential | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{p d}{2 t}$ | **1.0** (Reference) | | 2 | **Cylindrical** | Longitudinal / Axial | $\sigma_l = \dfrac{p d}{4 t}$ | **0.5** | | 3 | **Spherical** | Membrane (any meridian) | $\sigma = \dfrac{p d}{4 t}$ | **0.5** | | 4 | **Cylindrical** | Maximum Shear (in-plane) | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{\sigma_h - \sigma_l}{2} = \dfrac{p d}{8 t}$ | — | | 5 | **Spherical** | Maximum Shear | $\tau_{max} = 0$ (Equibiaxial) | — | | 6 | **Cylindrical** | Principal Stress Sum | $\sigma_h + \sigma_l = \dfrac{3 p d}{4 t}$ | — | ### 2.4 Why the Hoop Stress is Always Twice the Longitudinal Stress This is the **single most important conceptual fact** in thin-walled pressure vessel theory. It arises from the following geometric reasoning: - The **end-cap area** being pushed by pressure is $\frac{\pi d^2}{4}$ (proportional to $d^2$). - The **longitudinal cross-sectional area** resisting this force is $\pi d t$ (proportional to $d \cdot t$). - For the **hoop direction**, the projected area being pushed is $d \cdot L$ (proportional to $d$). - The **two hoop walls** resisting the force have combined area $2 t L$ (proportional to $t$). When you compute $\dfrac{\sigma_h}{\sigma_l}$ algebraically, the **factor of 2** emerges naturally: $$\frac{\sigma_h}{\sigma_l} = \frac{p d / (2t)}{p d / (4t)} = 2$$ > [!NOTE] **Real-World Engineering Utility** > 1. **Submarine hull design:** Hoop stress governs the collapse depth calculation for a cylindrical pressure hull — this determines the **crush depth** of the vessel. > 2. **CNG / LNG tank certification:** Marine transport tanks are designed such that $\sigma_h < \sigma_{allow}$, since hoop stress is the dominant failure mode. > 3. **Boiler design:** Cylindrical boiler shells fail by **longitudinal cracking** (due to hoop stress) far more often than circumferential cracking — a 2:1 frequency ratio observed in failure statistics. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Longitudinal (Axial) Stress in a Cylindrical Vessel ### Setup Consider a **cylindrical pressure vessel** of internal diameter $d$, wall thickness $t$, and length $L$, subjected to internal gauge pressure $p$. We isolate the **end cap** of the vessel and apply equilibrium to the entire cylindrical shell. ### Force Balance (Axial Direction) The internal pressure exerts an **axial force** on the end cap trying to blow it off: $$F_{pressure} = p \times A_{end} = p \times \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$ This force is resisted by the **circumferential cross-section** of the cylindrical shell wall. The resisting cross-sectional area is the perimeter times the thickness: $$A_{wall} = (\pi d) \times t$$ ### Applying Equilibrium For static equilibrium of the shell (assuming the end cap is rigid and the vessel does not move): $$\sum F_{axial} = 0$$ $$p \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \sigma_l \cdot \pi d \cdot t$$ ### Solving for $\sigma_l$ We rearrange the equation to isolate $\sigma_l$: $$\sigma_l \cdot \pi d \cdot t = p \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$ Dividing both sides by $\pi d t$: $$\sigma_l = \frac{p \cdot d}{4 t}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] **Final Result (Memorize):** > $$\boxed{\sigma_l = \frac{p d}{4 t}}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hoop (Circumferential) Stress in a Cylindrical Vessel ### Setup Now we slice the cylinder with a **longitudinal (axial) plane**, separating it into two equal halves. We analyze one half. The internal pressure acts on the **projected area** (the rectangle of width = diameter $d$ and length $L$) pushing the two halves apart: $$A_{projected} = d \times L$$ The force tending to separate the halves: $$F_{separating} = p \times d \times L$$ ### Force Balance (Hoop Direction) This separating force is resisted by the **two longitudinal walls** of the cut section. The total resisting cross-sectional area is: $$A_{resisting} = 2 \times (t \times L) = 2 t L$$ ### Applying Equilibrium For the half-cylinder to remain in equilibrium: $$\sum F_{hoop} = 0$$ $$p \cdot d \cdot L = \sigma_h \cdot 2 t \cdot L$$ ### Solving for $\sigma_h$ Divide both sides by $2 t L$: $$\sigma_h = \frac{p \cdot d}{2 t}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] **Final Result (Memorize):** > $$\boxed{\sigma_h = \frac{p d}{2 t}}$$ ### Verification of the 2:1 Ratio $$\frac{\sigma_h}{\sigma_l} = \frac{p d / (2t)}{p d / (4t)} = \frac{4 t}{2 t} = 2 \quad \checkmark$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Membrane Stress in a Spherical Vessel ### Setup Consider a **complete spherical pressure vessel** of internal diameter $d$ and wall thickness $t$, subjected to internal pressure $p$. By symmetry, all meridional (longitudinal) and circumferential (hoop) stresses are **identical in magnitude** at any point on the surface. This is the **equibiaxial stress state**. We cut the sphere into **two equal hemispheres** along a great circle. ### Force Balance The internal pressure acts on the **projected circular area** pushing the hemispheres apart: $$F_{pressure} = p \times \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$ The resisting force comes from the **membrane stress** acting on the **circumferential cross-section** of the spherical shell — the entire great circle: $$A_{wall} = (\pi d) \times t$$ ### Applying Equilibrium $$p \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \sigma_{sphere} \cdot \pi d \cdot t$$ ### Solving for $\sigma_{sphere}$ Divide both sides by $\pi d t$: $$\sigma_{sphere} = \frac{p d}{4 t}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] **Final Result (Memorize):** > $$\boxed{\sigma_{sphere} = \frac{p d}{4 t}}$$ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix A consolidated comparison table for quick revision: | Parameter | Cylindrical ($\sigma_h$) | Cylindrical ($\sigma_l$) | Spherical ($\sigma$) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Magnitude** | $\dfrac{p d}{2 t}$ | $\dfrac{p d}{4 t}$ | $\dfrac{p d}{4 t}$ | | **Maximum value?** | **Yes — critical** | Half of hoop | Equal to axial | | **Causes failure mode** | Longitudinal crack | Circumferential crack | Equal in both | | **Efficiency for same material** | Lower | Higher | **Most efficient** | | **Material usage per unit volume** | Higher | Higher | **Lowest (33% less)** | > [!NOTE] **Engineering Insight** > For a given pressure, diameter, and allowable stress, a **sphere uses 33% less material** than a cylinder. This is why **submarine rescue chambers** and **deep-sea bathyscaphe spheres** are spherical — material efficiency translates directly to depth capability and payload. --- ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style) **Problem:** A cylindrical pressure vessel has an internal diameter of **600 mm** and a wall thickness of **10 mm**. It is subjected to an internal pressure of **3 MPa**. Calculate: 1. The hoop stress 2. The longitudinal stress 3. The maximum in-plane shear stress ### Given Data $$d = 600 \text{ mm}, \quad t = 10 \text{ mm}, \quad p = 3 \text{ MPa} = 3 \text{ N/mm}^2$$ ### Step 1: Verify Thin-Wall Assumption $$r = \frac{d}{2} = 300 \text{ mm}$$ $$\frac{t}{r} = \frac{10}{300} = 0.0333 \leq 0.1 \quad \checkmark$$ Thin-wall theory is valid. ### Step 2: Hoop Stress $$\sigma_h = \frac{p d}{2 t} = \frac{3 \times 600}{2 \times 10} = \frac{1800}{20} = 90 \text{ MPa}$$ ### Step 3: Longitudinal Stress $$\sigma_l = \frac{p d}{4 t} = \frac{3 \times 600}{4 \times 10} = \frac{1800}{40} = 45 \text{ MPa}$$ ### Step 4: Maximum In-Plane Shear Stress $$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_h - \sigma_l}{2} = \frac{90 - 45}{2} = 22.5 \text{ MPa}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] **Answer Summary:** > - $\sigma_h = 90$ MPa (Hoop) > - $\sigma_l = 45$ MPa (Longitudinal) > - $\tau_{max} = 22.5$ MPa # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Stress Generation Architecture in a Cylindrical Pressure Vessel The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the **causal architecture** of how internal pressure generates the two principal stresses, mapping the physical reasoning used in derivations: ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Internal Pressure p
Acts on Inner Wall"] --> B["Pressure Acts on End Cap
Projected Area pi d squared / 4"] A --> C["Pressure Acts on Curved Wall
Projected Area d times L"] B --> D["Axial Force F_axial
F = p pi d squared / 4"] C --> E["Hoop Force F_hoop
F = p d L"] D --> F["Resisted by Wall Area
A = pi d t"] E --> G["Resisted by Two Walls
A = 2 t L"] F --> H["Equilibrium Equation
p pi d squared / 4 = sigma_l pi d t"] G --> I["Equilibrium Equation
p d L = sigma_h 2 t L"] H --> J["Longitudinal Stress
sigma_l = p d / 4 t"] I --> K["Hoop Stress
sigma_h = p d / 2 t"] J --> L["Ratio
sigma_h / sigma_l = 2"] K --> L L --> M["Critical Design Stress
Hoop Stress Governs Failure"] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Pressure Vessel Classification Matrix ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph VESSELS["PRESSURE VESSELS"] A1["Thin-Walled
t over r less than or equal 0.1"] A2["Thick-Walled
t over r greater than 0.1"] end A1 --> B1["Cylindrical"] A1 --> B2["Spherical"] A1 --> B3["Conical"] B1 --> C1["Hoop Stress
sigma_h = p d / 2 t"] B1 --> C2["Longitudinal Stress
sigma_l = p d / 4 t"] B2 --> C3["Membrane Stress
sigma = p d / 4 t"] B3 --> C4["Variable along meridian
Lamé Type Analysis"] C1 --> D1["Dominant Failure Mode
Longitudinal Cracks"] C2 --> D2["Secondary Failure Mode
Circumferential Cracks"] C3 --> D3["Equibiaxial
No Preferred Crack Direction"] ``` ## 4.3 Free-Body Schematic Description (Cylindrical Vessel Hoop Analysis) For clarity, the geometry used in **Derivation 2** can be visualized as follows: > **Visual Interpretation of the Hoop Stress Free-Body:** > - Imagine a horizontal cylinder lying on its side. > - Make a vertical cut through the entire length of the cylinder, splitting it into a **top half** and a **bottom half**. > - The internal pressure pushes the top half **upward** over the projected rectangular area ($d \times L$). > - This upward force must be balanced by tensile stress in the **two cut edges** (left wall and right wall), each of cross-section $t \times L$. > - Equilibrium directly yields $\sigma_h = p d / (2 t)$. ## 4.4 Mermaid Stress Element Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph CYLELEM["Stress Element on Cylindrical Wall"] SE1["Normal Stress in Hoop Direction
sigma_h acting tangentially"] SE2["Normal Stress in Axial Direction
sigma_l acting longitudinally"] SE3["Shear Stress on Faces
tau approx 0 for membrane"] end SE1 --> OUT1["Principal Stress 1
sigma_1 = p d / 2 t"] SE2 --> OUT2["Principal Stress 2
sigma_2 = p d / 4 t"] SE3 --> OUT3["tau_xy approx 0"] subgraph SPHELEM["Stress Element on Spherical Wall"] SS1["Meridional Stress
sigma_m = p d / 4 t"] SS2["Hoop Stress
sigma_h = p d / 4 t"] end SS1 --> SOUT["Equibiaxial State
sigma_1 = sigma_2 = p d / 4 t"] SS2 --> SOUT ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define a thin-walled pressure vessel. State the condition for a vessel to be classified as thin-walled. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** A **thin-walled pressure vessel** is a closed container whose wall thickness is small compared to its internal radius, allowing the use of membrane theory for stress analysis. The condition is: $$\frac{t}{r} \leq \frac{1}{10}$$ (or equivalently $\dfrac{t}{d} \leq \dfrac{1}{20}$). Under this condition, stress is assumed **uniform across the wall thickness**, and bending stresses are negligible. *(3 marks: Definition + Condition + Justification)* --- ### Question 2: Why is the hoop stress in a cylindrical pressure vessel twice the longitudinal stress? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** The hoop stress $\sigma_h$ and longitudinal stress $\sigma_l$ in a cylindrical pressure vessel are: $$\sigma_h = \frac{p d}{2 t}, \qquad \sigma_l = \frac{p d}{4 t}$$ The ratio is: $$\frac{\sigma_h}{\sigma_l} = \frac{pd/(2t)}{pd/(4t)} = 2$$ This 2:1 ratio arises because the **end-cap area** ($\pi d^2/4$, proportional to $d^2$) that produces the longitudinal force is resisted by the **circumferential wall area** ($\pi d t$, proportional to $d$), whereas the **hoop force** acts over a projected area ($d L$, proportional to $d$) resisted by **two walls** ($2 t L$). Consequently, **hoop stress governs the design** of cylindrical pressure vessels. *(3 marks: Formula + Ratio derivation + Practical implication)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A: Comprehensive Cylindrical Vessel Analysis (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Adapted for 2024 Scheme]` > A cylindrical pressure vessel is constructed from a marine-grade steel with internal diameter **800 mm** and wall thickness **12 mm**. It operates at an internal gauge pressure of **4 MPa**. The yield strength of the material is **250 MPa**, and a factor of safety of **2.5** is prescribed. #### Part (a): Calculate the hoop and longitudinal stresses developed in the vessel wall. Determine whether the design is safe. (7 marks) **Solution:** **Step 1 — Verify Thin-Wall Assumption:** $$r = \frac{d}{2} = 400 \text{ mm}, \quad \frac{t}{r} = \frac{12}{400} = 0.03 \leq 0.1 \quad \checkmark$$ **Step 2 — Compute Hoop Stress:** $$\sigma_h = \frac{p d}{2 t} = \frac{4 \times 800}{2 \times 12} = \frac{3200}{24} = 133.33 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 3 — Compute Longitudinal Stress:** $$\sigma_l = \frac{p d}{4 t} = \frac{4 \times 800}{4 \times 12} = \frac{3200}{48} = 66.67 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 4 — Compute Allowable Stress:** $$\sigma_{allow} = \frac{\sigma_y}{FoS} = \frac{250}{2.5} = 100 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 5 — Safety Check:** Since the **hoop stress (133.33 MPa) > allowable stress (100 MPa)**, the design **is NOT safe**. The vessel must be redesigned. *(7 marks: 2 for thin-wall check, 2 for stresses, 2 for allowable, 1 for verdict)* --- #### Part (b): Find the minimum thickness required to make the vessel safe. Also calculate the maximum in-plane shear stress at this thickness. (7 marks) **Solution:** **Step 1 — Governing Stress (Hoop):** Since hoop stress is critical, set $\sigma_h = \sigma_{allow}$: $$\frac{p d}{2 t_{min}} = \sigma_{allow}$$ $$\frac{4 \times 800}{2 \times t_{min}} = 100$$ $$t_{min} = \frac{4 \times 800}{2 \times 100} = \frac{3200}{200} = 16 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 2 — Recompute Longitudinal Stress at $t_{min} = 16$ mm:** $$\sigma_l = \frac{p d}{4 t_{min}} = \frac{4 \times 800}{4 \times 16} = \frac{3200}{64} = 50 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 3 — Maximum In-Plane Shear Stress:** $$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_h - \sigma_l}{2} = \frac{100 - 50}{2} = 25 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 4 — Verification of Thin-Wall Assumption at New Thickness:** $$\frac{t_{min}}{r} = \frac{16}{400} = 0.04 \leq 0.1 \quad \checkmark$$ > [!IMPORTANT] **Final Answer for Part (b):** > - Minimum required thickness: $t_{min} = 16$ mm > - Maximum in-plane shear stress: $\tau_{max} = 25$ MPa *(7 marks: 2 for setup, 2 for thickness, 2 for shear, 1 for verification)* --- ### Question B (Alternative Choice): Spherical Vessel Design (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Adapted for 2024 Scheme]` > A spherical pressure vessel used as a **submarine buoyancy compensation tank** has an internal diameter of **2 m** and is constructed from **titanium alloy** with yield strength **900 MPa**. The vessel operates at an internal pressure of **5 MPa**. #### Part (a): Derive the formula for membrane stress in a spherical pressure vessel and calculate its magnitude. (7 marks) **Solution:** **Step 1 — Setup:** Cut the sphere into two equal hemispheres along a great circle. Let $d$ = internal diameter, $t$ = thickness, $p$ = internal pressure. **Step 2 — Force from Pressure on Projected Area:** $$F_{pressure} = p \times \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$ **Step 3 — Resisting Wall Cross-Section (the great circle):** $$A_{wall} = \pi d \cdot t$$ **Step 4 — Equilibrium Equation:** $$p \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \sigma \cdot \pi d \cdot t$$ **Step 5 — Solving:** $$\sigma = \frac{p d}{4 t}$$ **Step 6 — Numerical Calculation:** Given $d = 2000$ mm, $t = ?$ (assumed **20 mm**), $p = 5$ MPa: $$\sigma = \frac{5 \times 2000}{4 \times 20} = \frac{10000}{80} = 125 \text{ MPa}$$ *(7 marks: 4 for derivation steps, 3 for numerical value)* --- #### Part (b): Calculate the factor of safety against yielding and the strain energy density stored in the wall material. Assume $E = 110$ GPa and $\nu = 0.3$. (7 marks) **Solution:** **Step 1 — Factor of Safety:** $$FoS = \frac{\sigma_y}{\sigma} = \frac{900}{125} = 7.2$$ **Step 2 — Strain Energy Density Formula:** For equibiaxial stress with $\sigma_1 = \sigma_2 = \sigma$: $$U_0 = \frac{1}{2E} \left( \sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 - 2\nu \sigma_1 \sigma_2 \right)$$ **Step 3 — Substitution:** $$U_0 = \frac{1}{2 \times 110 \times 10^3} \left( 125^2 + 125^2 - 2 \times 0.3 \times 125 \times 125 \right) \text{ MPa}$$ $$U_0 = \frac{1}{220000} \left( 15625 + 15625 - 9375 \right) = \frac{21875}{220000}$$ $$U_0 = 0.0994 \text{ MPa} = 99.4 \text{ kPa} = 99.4 \text{ kJ/m}^3$$ > [!IMPORTANT] **Final Answer for Part (b):** > - Factor of Safety: $FoS = 7.2$ > - Strain Energy Density: $U_0 \approx 99.4 \text{ kJ/m}^3$ *(7 marks: 2 for FoS, 3 for strain energy formula, 2 for numerical answer)* --- > [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Forgetting to verify the thin-wall assumption** before applying the formulas — costs **1 mark** minimum. > 2. **Mixing up $d$ and $r$** — formulas use **internal diameter $d$**, not radius $r$, but some texts use $r$ directly. Always check the formula sheet carefully. > 3. **Unit inconsistency** — mixing mm and m, or MPa and Pa, is the **#1 silent killer** of correct answers. Convert all dimensions to a **consistent unit system** (preferably mm and MPa). > 4. **Choosing the wrong governing stress** for design thickness — **hoop stress** is critical in cylinders. Using longitudinal stress gives a non-conservative (unsafe) design. > 5. **Sign convention errors** — internal pressure produces **tensile** stresses in the wall. If asked for compressive stresses, they only arise under **external pressure** (e.g., submerged submarine hulls). > 6. **Forgetting to subtract 1 mark** per missing unit in the final numerical answer. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] **High-Density Revision Summary:** - **Thin-Walled Criterion:** $\dfrac{t}{r} \leq \dfrac{1}{10}$ (or $\dfrac{t}{d} \leq \dfrac{1}{20}$). **Always verify** before applying formulas. - **Hoop Stress Formula:** $\sigma_h = \dfrac{p d}{2 t}$ — **largest** stress in a cylinder. - **Longitudinal Stress Formula:** $\sigma_l = \dfrac{p d}{4 t}$ — exactly **half** of hoop. - **Spherical Stress Formula:** $\sigma_{sphere} = \dfrac{p d}{4 t}$ — **equibiaxial** state. - **Critical 2:1 Ratio:** Always remember $\sigma_h = 2 \sigma_l$ for cylindrical vessels. - **Maximum In-Plane Shear (Cylinder):** $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{\sigma_h - \sigma_l}{2} = \dfrac{p d}{8 t}$. - **Spherical vessels are most material-efficient** — use **33% less material** than cylinders for the same pressure, diameter, and allowable stress. - **Hoop stress governs design** of cylindrical pressure vessels — failures initiate as **longitudinal cracks** (perpendicular to hoop direction). - **Allowable Stress Rule:** $\sigma_{allow} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{FoS}$ — set $\sigma_h \leq \sigma_{allow}$ to find minimum thickness. - **Principal Stress State:** $\sigma_1 = \sigma_h$ and $\sigma_2 = \sigma_l$ (both tensile for internal pressure). $\sigma_3 \approx 0$ for thin walls (radial stress neglected). - **Sign Convention:** Tension is **positive**. Internal pressure produces **tensile** membrane stresses. - **Marine Relevance:** Submarine hulls (external crushing pressure), CNG cylinders, boiler shells, LNG cargo tanks — all designed using these formulas. - **Spherical stress equals longitudinal cylindrical stress** — useful trick for cross-comparison questions. - **Verify thin-wall assumption twice**: once in the problem's given state, and once again **after computing** the minimum thickness required for safety.

topics.title: Biaxial Tension and Compression in Thin-Walled Pressure Vessels (Cylindrical and Spherical)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST303 - SOLID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Biaxial Tension and Compression in Thin-Walled Pressure Vessels (Cylindrical and Spherical)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **thin-walled pressure vessel** is a closed hollow structure designed to hold a fluid (liquid or gas) under internal or external pressure, whose wall thickness **t** is very small compared to its mean radius **r** (or mean diameter **D**). The standard engineering threshold used by KTU and most naval architecture textbooks is:

$$\frac{t}{D} \leq \frac{1}{20} \quad \text{to} \quad \frac{1}{10}$$

When such a vessel is subjected to internal pressure **p**, the membrane (skin) of the wall carries a state of stress called **biaxial tension** because the principal stresses act along two mutually perpendicular directions in the plane of the wall surface, while the third principal stress (radial, through the wall thickness) is negligibly small and is normally neglected.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Membrane Theory Assumption):**
> For thin-walled vessels, stresses are assumed to be **uniformly distributed** across the wall thickness. The radial stress $\sigma_r \leq p/2$ is considered negligible compared to the hoop and longitudinal stresses. This simplification allows the use of **static equilibrium** on a free body rather than full elasticity theory.

### 1.2 The Two Families of Pressure Vessels Covered

| Vessel Type | Geometry | Naval Architecture Context |
|-------------|----------|----------------------------|
| **Cylindrical** | Cylinder with hemispherical or elliptical end caps | Submarine outer casings, marine boilers, cargo oil tanks, air flasks |
| **Spherical** | Hollow sphere | Submarine pressure hulls (deep submersibles), LPG cargo tanks, buoyance spheres |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine blowing up a **party balloon**. As you exhale into it, the rubber stretches in all directions. The rubber skin is under tension in every direction along its surface — this is essentially the state of stress in a pressure vessel.

- A **cylindrical balloon** (long and thin) will develop a *higher* tension in its circumferential (hoop) direction than in its axial direction. This is because the hoop "wraps" the pressurized fluid, while the longitudinal stress only needs to balance the pressure acting on the circular end cap.
- A **spherical balloon** has **equal tension in all directions** on its surface, because geometry is perfectly symmetric — there are no "weak" directions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marine Analogy:** A **SCUBA diving cylinder** (used by naval divers) is a thin-walled cylinder. If you wrapped the cylinder in chains, the chains would feel a tension — this tension is the *hoop stress*. The end caps of the cylinder are pulled outward by pressure; the cylinder wall must develop a *longitudinal stress* to hold them in place, exactly like the pulling force on a cork holding pressurized soda.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Young's Modulus** for marine-grade steel: **E = 200 GPa (2 × 10⁵ N/mm²)**
- **Poisson's ratio** for steel: **ν = 0.3** (dimensionless)
- **Design pressure** for typical naval pressure hulls: 0.5 – 30 MPa
- **Wall thickness to diameter ratio** (thin-wall assumption): **t/D ≤ 1/20** for KTU problems (some texts use 1/10)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Using t/r instead of t/D in stress formulas. For KTU numericals, always confirm whether the problem gives **diameter D** or **radius r**, and use the corresponding formula consistently.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Stress States in a Thin-Walled Cylindrical Vessel

When internal pressure **p** acts on a cylinder of diameter **D**, length **L**, and wall thickness **t**, two principal stresses develop in the wall:

**Step 1 — Identify the two orthogonal directions in the wall plane:**
- **Hoop (circumferential) direction** — tangent to the circular cross-section
- **Longitudinal (axial) direction** — parallel to the cylinder's axis

**Step 2 — Apply static equilibrium (Membrane Theory):**
- For the hoop direction: Cut the cylinder with a diametral plane. The internal pressure pushes the two halves apart, and the resulting hoop stress $\sigma_h$ resists this on the two wall cut-sections.
- For the longitudinal direction: Cut the cylinder with a transverse plane. The pressure acts on the circular end cap of area $\pi D^2/4$ and is resisted by the wall ring of area $\pi D t$.

**Step 3 — Resulting stress magnitudes (full derivation in Section 3):**

$$\sigma_h = \frac{pD}{2t} = \frac{pr}{t} \quad \text{(Hoop / Circumferential stress)}$$

$$\sigma_L = \frac{pD}{4t} = \frac{pr}{2t} \quad \text{(Longitudinal / Axial stress)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Ratio:** $\sigma_h = 2\sigma_L$. The hoop stress is **always twice** the longitudinal stress in a thin-walled cylinder. This is why pressure vessel failures almost always begin as a **longitudinal crack** (the wall splits along its length, perpendicular to the highest tension — the hoop direction).

### 2.2 Stress State in a Thin-Walled Spherical Vessel

By symmetry, a sphere has no preferred direction on its surface. Therefore the stress is the same in every direction tangent to the surface:

$$\sigma = \frac{pD}{4t} = \frac{pr}{2t}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Material Efficiency Insight:** For the same internal pressure, diameter, and thickness, a sphere experiences **half the maximum stress** of a cylinder. This is why modern submarine pressure hulls (deep-diving submersibles like the *Alvin* or *Trieste*) often use **spherical** shapes — it dramatically reduces the required plate thickness and weight, which is critical for buoyancy and dive depth.

### 2.3 Biaxial Strain Field (Using Generalized Hooke's Law)

For a state of plane stress (no radial stress assumed) in the cylinder wall:

$$\varepsilon_h = \frac{1}{E}\left(\sigma_h - \nu \sigma_L\right)$$

$$\varepsilon_L = \frac{1}{E}\left(\sigma_L - \nu \sigma_h\right)$$

For a sphere (with equal biaxial stress $\sigma$ in two perpendicular tangent directions):

$$\varepsilon = \frac{1}{E}\left(\sigma - \nu \sigma\right) = \frac{\sigma(1-\nu)}{E}$$

### 2.4 Volumetric Strain (Crucial for Naval Architecture — Tank Capacity Calculations)

The fractional change in volume is used to size fuel tanks, ballast tanks, and cargo holds.

**Cylindrical Vessel:**

$$\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_h + \varepsilon_L + \varepsilon_r = (1-2\nu)\frac{\sigma_h + \sigma_L}{E} = \frac{3pD(1-2\nu)}{4tE}$$

**Spherical Vessel:**

$$\varepsilon_v = 2\varepsilon + \varepsilon_r = 2(1-\nu)\frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{pD(1-\nu)}{2tE}$$

### 2.5 Changes in Dimensions

**Cylindrical Vessel — Diameter change:**

$$\Delta D = D \cdot \varepsilon_h = \frac{pD^2}{4tE}(2-\nu)$$

**Cylindrical Vessel — Length change:**

$$\Delta L = L \cdot \varepsilon_L = \frac{pDL}{4tE}(1-2\nu)$$

**Spherical Vessel — Diameter change:**

$$\Delta D = D \cdot \varepsilon = \frac{pD^2(1-\nu)}{4tE}$$

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Cylindrical Vessel | Spherical Vessel | Units (SI) |
|---|----------|--------------------|------------------|------------|
| 1 | Hoop stress $\sigma_h$ | $\dfrac{pD}{2t}$ | $\dfrac{pD}{4t}$ | N/m² (Pa) |
| 2 | Longitudinal stress $\sigma_L$ | $\dfrac{pD}{4t}$ | N/A (equals $\sigma_h$) | N/m² (Pa) |
| 3 | Radial stress $\sigma_r$ | Neglected ($<p$) | Neglected ($<p$) | N/m² (Pa) |
| 4 | Hoop strain $\varepsilon_h$ | $\dfrac{1}{E}(\sigma_h - \nu\sigma_L)$ | $\dfrac{\sigma(1-\nu)}{E}$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | Longitudinal strain $\varepsilon_L$ | $\dfrac{1}{E}(\sigma_L - \nu\sigma_h)$ | $\dfrac{\sigma(1-\nu)}{E}$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Volumetric strain $\varepsilon_v$ | $\dfrac{3pD(1-2\nu)}{4tE}$ | $\dfrac{pD(1-\nu)}{2tE}$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Change in diameter $\Delta D$ | $\dfrac{pD^2}{4tE}(2-\nu)$ | $\dfrac{pD^2(1-\nu)}{4tE}$ | m |
| 8 | Change in length $\Delta L$ | $\dfrac{pDL}{4tE}(1-2\nu)$ | N/A | m |
| 9 | Change in volume $\Delta V$ | $V \cdot \varepsilon_v$ | $V \cdot \varepsilon_v$ | m³ |
| 10 | Thin-wall validity | $t/D \leq 1/20$ | $t/D \leq 1/20$ | dimensionless |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility in Naval Architecture

1. **Submarine Pressure Hull Design** — Deep-diving submarines must withstand crushing external ocean pressure. Spherical or cylindrical-spherical hulls are analyzed using these formulas.
2. **Marine Boilers and Steam Drums** — Cylindrical pressure vessels form the heart of steam plants; hoop stress dictates plate thickness.
3. **LNG/LPG Cargo Tanks** — Membrane-type and Moss-type (spherical) LNG carriers rely on this biaxial analysis.
4. **Diving Cylinders and Air Flasks** — Compressed breathing gas storage for naval divers.
5. **Hydraulic Accumulators and Fire-Fighting Systems** — Shipboard safety systems.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When asked for "design thickness," never forget to add a **corrosion allowance** (typically 1–2 mm) and apply a **factor of safety** (typically 3–4 for marine pressure vessels as per classification societies like DNV, Lloyd's Register, or ABS).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Hoop Stress in a Thin-Walled Cylinder

**Setup:** Consider a cylindrical vessel of internal diameter **D**, length **L**, wall thickness **t**, containing fluid at gauge pressure **p**. Make a diametral cut, isolating half the cylinder.

**Step 1 — Free Body Analysis:**
The internal pressure acts on the projected (diametral) area of length **L**:

$$A_{projected} = D \times L$$

**Step 2 — Total Force Due to Pressure (pushing the two halves apart):**

$$F_{pressure} = p \times A_{projected} = pDL$$

**Step 3 — Resisting Force from Hoop Stress:**
The hoop stress $\sigma_h$ acts on the two cut wall sections. Each section has an area of $\text{height} \times \text{thickness} = L \times t$. There are two such sections:

$$F_{resisting} = 2 \times (\sigma_h \times L \times t) = 2\sigma_h L t$$

**Step 4 — Apply Static Equilibrium:** $F_{pressure} = F_{resisting}$

$$pDL = 2\sigma_h L t$$

**Step 5 — Cancel Length L and solve:**

$$\sigma_h = \frac{pD}{2t}$$

**Step 6 — Alternative form using radius** $r = D/2$:

$$\sigma_h = \frac{pD}{2t} = \frac{p(2r)}{2t} = \frac{pr}{t}$$

> **Logic Explanation:** The "2" in the denominator comes from the fact that the resisting cross-sectional area is split into **two** wall strips (one on each side of the diametral cut). The "D" comes from the projected area on which pressure acts.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Longitudinal Stress in a Thin-Walled Cylinder

**Setup:** Now make a transverse (cross-sectional) cut, separating one end cap from the rest of the cylinder.

**Step 1 — Free Body Analysis:**
The internal pressure acts on the full circular end cap area:

$$A_{cap} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

**Step 2 — Total Force Due to Pressure (pushing the cap off the cylinder):**

$$F_{pressure} = p \times A_{cap} = \frac{p\pi D^2}{4}$$

**Step 3 — Resisting Force from Longitudinal Stress:**
The longitudinal stress $\sigma_L$ acts uniformly on the **ring** of wall material at the cut. The cross-sectional area of this ring is:

$$A_{ring} = \pi D \times t$$

**Step 4 — Resisting Force:**

$$F_{resisting} = \sigma_L \times A_{ring} = \sigma_L \pi D t$$

**Step 5 — Apply Static Equilibrium:** $F_{pressure} = F_{resisting}$

$$\frac{p\pi D^2}{4} = \sigma_L \pi D t$$

**Step 6 — Cancel $\pi$ and one D, solve:**

$$\sigma_L = \frac{pD}{4t}$$

**Step 7 — Ratio of stresses:**

$$\frac{\sigma_h}{\sigma_L} = \frac{pD/2t}{pD/4t} = 2 \quad \therefore \sigma_h = 2\sigma_L$$

> **Logic Explanation:** The longitudinal stress is **half** the hoop stress because the projected end-cap area ($\pi D^2/4$) is half of what it would be if the pressure acted on a diametral section of the same length.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Stress in a Thin-Walled Spherical Vessel

**Setup:** Cut the sphere into two equal hemispheres with a diametral plane.

**Step 1 — Free Body Analysis:**
Pressure acts on the projected circular area of diameter **D**:

$$A_{projected} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

**Step 2 — Total Force Due to Pressure:**

$$F_{pressure} = \frac{p\pi D^2}{4}$$

**Step 3 — Resisting Force from Membrane Stress:**
The uniform stress $\sigma$ acts around the entire ring at the cut. The ring area is:

$$A_{ring} = \pi D \times t$$

**Step 4 — Resisting Force:**

$$F_{resisting} = \sigma \pi D t$$

**Step 5 — Equilibrium:**

$$\frac{p\pi D^2}{4} = \sigma \pi D t$$

**Step 6 — Solve:**

$$\sigma = \frac{pD}{4t}$$

> **Logic Explanation:** This result is **identical** to the longitudinal stress in a cylinder — but in a sphere, this stress acts in **every direction** on the surface, not just axially. This isotropy is the geometric reason for the sphere's material efficiency.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Volumetric Strain in a Cylindrical Vessel

**Step 1 — Initial Volume of Cylinder Wall Material (thin shell):**

$$V = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \times L$$

**Step 2 — Logarithmic Differential (for small strains, $\Delta V/V \approx dV/V$):**

$$\frac{dV}{V} = 2\frac{dD}{D} + \frac{dL}{L} = 2\varepsilon_h + \varepsilon_L$$

$$\varepsilon_v = 2\varepsilon_h + \varepsilon_L$$

> (The factor of 2 for diameter comes from $D^2$ — and radial strain is neglected for thin walls.)

**Step 3 — Substitute Hooke's Law values:**

$$\varepsilon_h = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_h - \nu\sigma_L), \quad \varepsilon_L = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_L - \nu\sigma_h)$$

**Step 4 — Combine:**

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{2}{E}(\sigma_h - \nu\sigma_L) + \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_L - \nu\sigma_h)$$

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{1}{E}\left[2\sigma_h - 2\nu\sigma_L + \sigma_L - \nu\sigma_h\right]$$

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_h(2 - \nu) + \sigma_L(1 - 2\nu)\right]$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $\sigma_h = pD/2t$ and $\sigma_L = pD/4t$:**

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{1}{E}\left[\frac{pD}{2t}(2 - \nu) + \frac{pD}{4t}(1 - 2\nu)\right]$$

**Step 6 — Common denominator 4t:**

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{pD}{4tE}\left[2(2 - \nu) + (1 - 2\nu)\right]$$

**Step 7 — Expand:**

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{pD}{4tE}\left[4 - 2\nu + 1 - 2\nu\right] = \frac{pD}{4tE}(5 - 4\nu)$$

> **KTU-Approved Compact Form:** $\varepsilon_v = \dfrac{3pD(1-2\nu)}{4tE}$ (this is the same as $(5-4\nu)/3$ only when $\nu = 0.5$; for general $\nu$, the **correct** form is below).

**Correcting Step 7 using the symmetric identity** $\varepsilon_v = (1-2\nu)(\sigma_h + \sigma_L)/E$:

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{(1-2\nu)}{E}\left(\frac{pD}{2t} + \frac{pD}{4t}\right) = \frac{(1-2\nu)}{E} \cdot \frac{3pD}{4t}$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_v^{cyl} = \frac{3pD(1-2\nu)}{4tE}}$$

**Step 8 — Final Volume Change:**

$$\Delta V = V \cdot \varepsilon_v = \frac{\pi D^2 L}{4} \cdot \frac{3pD(1-2\nu)}{4tE}$$

$$\Delta V = \frac{3\pi p D^3 L (1-2\nu)}{16tE}$$

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Volumetric Strain in a Spherical Vessel

**Step 1 — Initial Volume:**

$$V = \frac{\pi D^3}{6}$$

**Step 2 — Logarithmic Differential:**

$$\frac{dV}{V} = 3\frac{dD}{D} = 3\varepsilon$$

**Step 3 — Stress-strain relation (equal biaxial):**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma(1-\nu)}{E} = \frac{pD(1-\nu)}{4tE}$$

**Step 4 — Volumetric strain:**

$$\varepsilon_v = 3\varepsilon = \frac{3pD(1-\nu)}{4tE}$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_v^{sph} = \frac{3pD(1-\nu)}{4tE}}$$

**Step 5 — Volume change:**

$$\Delta V = V \cdot \varepsilon_v = \frac{\pi D^3}{6} \cdot \frac{3pD(1-\nu)}{4tE} = \frac{\pi p D^4 (1-\nu)}{8tE}$$

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Worked Numerical Demonstration

**Sample KTU-Style Problem:**
A thin-walled cylindrical pressure vessel has **D = 1.0 m**, **t = 10 mm**, and is subjected to internal pressure **p = 2 MPa**. Material: Steel with **E = 200 GPa**, **ν = 0.3**. Determine:
(i) Hoop and longitudinal stresses
(ii) Strains in hoop and longitudinal directions
(iii) Volumetric strain
(iv) Change in diameter and length per metre of vessel

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert all to consistent SI units:**
- D = 1.0 m, t = 0.010 m, p = 2 × 10⁶ Pa, E = 2 × 10¹¹ Pa

**Step 2 — Hoop stress:**

$$\sigma_h = \frac{pD}{2t} = \frac{2 \times 10^6 \times 1.0}{2 \times 0.010} = \frac{2 \times 10^6}{0.020} = 100 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Longitudinal stress:**

$$\sigma_L = \frac{pD}{4t} = \frac{2 \times 10^6 \times 1.0}{4 \times 0.010} = 50 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Hoop strain:**

$$\varepsilon_h = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_h - \nu\sigma_L) = \frac{1}{2 \times 10^{11}}(100 \times 10^6 - 0.3 \times 50 \times 10^6)$$

$$\varepsilon_h = \frac{1}{2 \times 10^{11}}(100 - 15) \times 10^6 = \frac{85 \times 10^6}{2 \times 10^{11}} = 4.25 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 5 — Longitudinal strain:**

$$\varepsilon_L = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_L - \nu\sigma_h) = \frac{1}{2 \times 10^{11}}(50 \times 10^6 - 0.3 \times 100 \times 10^6)$$

$$\varepsilon_L = \frac{1}{2 \times 10^{11}}(50 - 30) \times 10^6 = \frac{20 \times 10^6}{2 \times 10^{11}} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 6 — Volumetric strain:**

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{3pD(1-2\nu)}{4tE} = \frac{3 \times 2 \times 10^6 \times 1.0 \times (1 - 0.6)}{4 \times 0.010 \times 2 \times 10^{11}}$$

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{6 \times 10^6 \times 0.4}{8 \times 10^9} = \frac{2.4 \times 10^6}{8 \times 10^9} = 3.0 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 7 — Change in diameter (for 1 m vessel):**

$$\Delta D = D \cdot \varepsilon_h = 1.0 \times 4.25 \times 10^{-4} = 4.25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.425 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 8 — Change in length (for 1 m vessel):**

$$\Delta L = L \cdot \varepsilon_L = 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-4} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.1 \text{ mm}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity Check:** $\Delta D / D = 0.425/1000 = 4.25 \times 10^{-4}$ ✓ matches $\varepsilon_h$. This is a standard KTU cross-verification trick that earns full method marks even if a numerical step has a minor slip.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Thin-Walled Pressure Vessel Stress System

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional flow** of the analysis pipeline: from input parameters through stress generation, equilibrium, and finally to the design outputs used in naval architecture.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Parameters: p, D, t, L, E, nu] --> B{Vessel Geometry?}
    B -->|Cylindrical| C1[Compute sigma_h = pD/2t]
    B -->|Spherical| C2[Compute sigma_s = pD/4t]
    C1 --> D1[Compute sigma_L = pD/4t]
    D1 --> E1[Apply Biaxial Hooke's Law]
    C2 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[Compute epsilon_h and epsilon_L]
    F1 --> G1[Compute Volumetric Strain]
    G1 --> H1[Compute Delta D, Delta L, Delta V]
    H1 --> I1[Output: Design thickness and Deflection checks]
    I1 --> J1{Naval Architecture Check}
    J1 --> K1[Submarine hull]
    J1 --> K2[Marine boiler]
    J1 --> K3[LNG cargo tank]
    J1 --> K4[Diving cylinder]
```

### 4.2 Stress Direction Schematic — Cylindrical Pressure Vessel

This Mermaid block shows the directional flow of forces and stresses for the two principal biaxial directions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Internal Pressure p] --> F1[Force on End Cap: p times piD squared over 4]
    P --> F2[Force on Diametral Area: p times D times L]
    F1 --> S1[Longitudinal Stress sigma_L in Cylinder Wall]
    F2 --> S2[Hoop Stress sigma_h in Cylinder Wall]
    S1 --> R1[Wall resists with Area pi D t]
    S2 --> R2[Wall resists with Area 2 L t]
    R1 --> O1[Equilibrium: sigma_L = pD over 4t]
    R2 --> O2[Equilibrium: sigma_h = pD over 2t]
    O1 --> CR[Critical Direction: sigma_h = 2 sigma_L]
    O2 --> CR
    CR --> FR[Failure Mode: Longitudinal Crack]
```

### 4.3 Comparison Block — Cylinder vs Sphere Stress Distribution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph CYL[Cylindrical Vessel]
        C1A[Direction 1: Hoop]
        C1B[Direction 2: Longitudinal]
        C1C[Stress: sigma_h = pD/2t]
        C1D[Stress: sigma_L = pD/4t]
        C1E[State: ANISOTROPIC biaxial]
    end
    subgraph SPH[Spherical Vessel]
        S1A[Direction 1: Any tangent 1]
        S1B[Direction 2: Any tangent 2 perpendicular]
        S1C[Stress: sigma = pD/4t]
        S1D[Identical in all directions]
        S1E[State: ISOTROPIC biaxial]
    end
    C1A --> C1C
    C1B --> C1D
    C1C --> C1E
    C1D --> C1E
    S1A --> S1C
    S1B --> S1C
    S1C --> S1D
    S1D --> S1E
    C1E --> EFF[Spherical = Half the max stress of Cylinder for same p, D, t]
    S1E --> EFF
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block represents a logical processing step in the analysis. The arrows show the **direction of dependency** — i.e., what information must be available before the next step can be performed. This mirrors how a KTU examiner expects you to structure a numerical solution: known → formula → substitution → result.

### 4.4 Free Body Diagram — Hoop Stress Derivation (Text Schematic)

> **Cylinder of diameter D and length L, cut along a diametral plane:**
>
> ```
>         ↑ σ_h (left wall)            σ_h (right wall) ↑
>         ┃                              ┃
>     ════╪══════════════════════════════╪════
>         ┃←————————  pDL  ————————————→┃
>     ════╪══════════════════════════════╪════
>         ┃                              ┃
>         ↓                              ↓
> ```
>
> **Left side:** Internal pressure pushes outward with total force $pDL$. The hoop stress $\sigma_h$ acts on wall area $Lt$ on the left cut section.
>
> **Right side:** Mirror image — the right wall also resists with $\sigma_h$ on its area $Lt$.

> [!TIP]
> **Visualisation Tip for KTU:** Always draw FBDs **even if not explicitly asked**. They earn you 1–2 method marks during valuation, especially if your final numerical answer has a calculation slip.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Define a thin-walled pressure vessel. State the condition under which a pressure vessel can be classified as thin-walled and justify why the radial stress is neglected in such vessels.

**Model Answer:**

A thin-walled pressure vessel is a closed hollow structure whose wall thickness **t** is very small compared to its mean diameter **D**, used to contain fluids under pressure.

**Thin-wall condition:** $t/D \leq 1/20$ (or as per some codes, $t/D \leq 1/10$).

**Justification for neglecting radial stress:**
The radial stress varies from **p** at the inner surface to **0** at the outer surface, with an average value of $p/2$. This is at least one order of magnitude smaller than the hoop and longitudinal stresses (which are of order $pD/t$, very large for thin walls). Hence, $\sigma_r$ is neglected and the wall is treated as being in a state of plane stress.

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
For a thin-walled cylindrical pressure vessel subjected to internal pressure, derive the ratio of hoop stress to longitudinal stress. Explain why pressure vessels fail by longitudinal cracking rather than circumferential cracking.

**Model Answer:**

$$\frac{\sigma_h}{\sigma_L} = \frac{pD/2t}{pD/4t} = 2$$

Therefore, $\sigma_h = 2\sigma_L$.

**Failure explanation:** The hoop stress is **twice** the longitudinal stress and is therefore the maximum principal stress. Cracks propagate **perpendicular to the maximum tensile stress** (as per maximum normal stress theory). Hence, the failure crack runs **longitudinally** (parallel to the cylinder axis), splitting the vessel open along its length.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Cylindrical Pressure Vessel

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]**

A thin-walled cylindrical pressure vessel is **2.0 m long**, has an **internal diameter of 1.2 m**, and a **wall thickness of 15 mm**. The vessel is filled with compressed air at an internal gauge pressure of **1.5 MPa**. The material of the vessel is mild steel with Young's modulus **E = 200 GPa** and Poisson's ratio **ν = 0.3**.

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Determine the hoop stress, longitudinal stress, hoop strain, and longitudinal strain in the vessel wall.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Find the change in diameter, change in length, and change in volume of the vessel.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify given values:**
- D = 1.2 m, t = 0.015 m, L = 2.0 m, p = 1.5 × 10⁶ Pa
- E = 200 × 10⁹ Pa, ν = 0.3

**Step 2 — Hoop stress:**

$$\sigma_h = \frac{pD}{2t} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^6 \times 1.2}{2 \times 0.015} = \frac{1.8 \times 10^6}{0.030} = 60 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 60 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Longitudinal stress:**

$$\sigma_L = \frac{pD}{4t} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^6 \times 1.2}{4 \times 0.015} = \frac{1.8 \times 10^6}{0.060} = 30 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 30 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Hoop strain (using biaxial Hooke's Law):**

$$\varepsilon_h = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_h - \nu\sigma_L) = \frac{1}{200 \times 10^9}(60 \times 10^6 - 0.3 \times 30 \times 10^6)$$

$$\varepsilon_h = \frac{1}{200 \times 10^9}(60 - 9) \times 10^6 = \frac{51 \times 10^6}{200 \times 10^9} = 2.55 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 5 — Longitudinal strain:**

$$\varepsilon_L = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_L - \nu\sigma_h) = \frac{1}{200 \times 10^9}(30 \times 10^6 - 0.3 \times 60 \times 10^6)$$

$$\varepsilon_L = \frac{1}{200 \times 10^9}(30 - 18) \times 10^6 = \frac{12 \times 10^6}{200 \times 10^9} = 6.0 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Step 1 — Change in diameter:**

$$\Delta D = D \cdot \varepsilon_h = 1.2 \times 2.55 \times 10^{-4} = 3.06 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.306 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Change in length:**

$$\Delta L = L \cdot \varepsilon_L = 2.0 \times 6.0 \times 10^{-5} = 1.2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.12 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Volumetric strain of vessel (shell volume):**

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{3pD(1-2\nu)}{4tE} = \frac{3 \times 1.5 \times 10^6 \times 1.2 \times (1 - 0.6)}{4 \times 0.015 \times 200 \times 10^9}$$

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{3 \times 1.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.4 \times 10^6}{4 \times 0.015 \times 200 \times 10^9} = \frac{2.16 \times 10^6}{1.2 \times 10^{10}} = 1.8 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 4 — Initial volume of the shell material:**

$$V = \pi D L t = \pi \times 1.2 \times 2.0 \times 0.015 = 0.1131 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 5 — Change in volume:**

$$\Delta V = V \cdot \varepsilon_v = 0.1131 \times 1.8 \times 10^{-4} = 2.036 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3 \approx 20.36 \text{ cm}^3$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on volume interpretation:** This $\Delta V$ is the change in the volume of the **shell material itself** (the wall), not the internal fluid volume. The internal volume of the vessel *increases* by the same amount as the wall expands outward.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Spherical Pressure Vessel *(Module Internal Choice)*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]**

A thin-walled spherical pressure vessel used as a buoy in marine survey work has an **internal diameter of 800 mm** and **wall thickness of 8 mm**. The internal gauge pressure is **2.5 MPa**. The material has **E = 210 GPa** and **ν = 0.28**.

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Calculate the membrane stress, the strain in any tangent direction, and the change in diameter of the sphere.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Determine the volumetric strain and the change in volume of the shell material. Compare the stresses in this sphere with those that would arise in a cylinder of the same diameter, length, and thickness, and comment on the material efficiency of the spherical shape.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Step 1 — Given values:**
- D = 0.8 m, t = 0.008 m, p = 2.5 × 10⁶ Pa
- E = 210 × 10⁹ Pa, ν = 0.28

**Step 2 — Membrane stress in sphere:**

$$\sigma = \frac{pD}{4t} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^6 \times 0.8}{4 \times 0.008} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^6}{0.032} = 62.5 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 62.5 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Tangent strain (equal in all directions):**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma(1-\nu)}{E} = \frac{62.5 \times 10^6 \times (1 - 0.28)}{210 \times 10^9}$$

$$\varepsilon = \frac{62.5 \times 10^6 \times 0.72}{210 \times 10^9} = \frac{45 \times 10^6}{210 \times 10^9} = 2.143 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 4 — Change in diameter:**

$$\Delta D = D \cdot \varepsilon = 0.8 \times 2.143 \times 10^{-4} = 1.714 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.171 \text{ mm}$$

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Step 1 — Volumetric strain:**
