$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{3pD(1-\nu)}{4tE} = \frac{3 \times 2.5 \times 10^6 \times 0.8 \times (1 - 0.28)}{4 \times 0.008 \times 210 \times 10^9}$$

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{3 \times 2.5 \times 0.8 \times 0.72 \times 10^6}{4 \times 0.008 \times 210 \times 10^9} = \frac{4.32 \times 10^6}{6.72 \times 10^9} = 6.429 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 2 — Initial shell volume:**

$$V = \frac{\pi D^3}{6} = \frac{\pi \times (0.8)^3}{6} = \frac{\pi \times 0.512}{6} = 0.2681 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Change in volume:**

$$\Delta V = V \cdot \varepsilon_v = 0.2681 \times 6.429 \times 10^{-4} = 1.724 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3 = 172.4 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 4 — Comparison with cylinder (same D, same t, say L = D = 0.8 m):**
- Sphere stress: $\sigma_{sph} = pD/(4t) = 62.5$ MPa
- Cylinder hoop stress: $\sigma_{h,cyl} = pD/(2t) = 125$ MPa
- Cylinder longitudinal stress: $\sigma_{L,cyl} = pD/(4t) = 62.5$ MPa

**Step 5 — Material efficiency comment:**
The maximum stress in a sphere is **half** the maximum stress in a cylinder (62.5 MPa vs 125 MPa) for identical pressure, diameter, and thickness. This means a spherical vessel can withstand the **same pressure with half the stress**, or alternatively, for the same allowable stress, a sphere requires a much thinner wall. This is why spherical tanks dominate submarine pressure hulls and LNG carriers — **lower weight, lower material cost, deeper safe operating depth**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks in This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Sign convention errors (2–3 marks lost):** Forgetting that internal pressure produces *tensile* stresses, and external pressure produces *compressive* stresses. KTU expects explicit mention of "tensile" vs "compressive."
>
> 2. **Mixing diameter and radius (2 marks):** Using $\sigma = pr/t$ when D is given, or vice versa. KTU deducts method marks if you don't write the formula with the **same variable as the data**.
>
> 3. **Neglecting Poisson's coupling (3 marks):** When asked for strain, writing $\varepsilon = \sigma/E$ (uniaxial) instead of $\varepsilon = (\sigma - \nu\sigma)/E$ (biaxial). This is the **#1 conceptual mistake** in KTU scripts.
>
> 4. **Wrong volumetric strain formula (2 marks):** Using $\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_h + \varepsilon_L$ for a **sphere** (which is wrong — sphere uses $3\varepsilon$).
>
> 5. **Unit inconsistency (1–2 marks):** Mixing mm with N (should be N/mm² = MPa) or cm with m. Always convert **all** inputs to SI (Pa, m) at the start.
>
> 6. **Thin-wall assumption violation (3 marks):** KTU may give a problem where t/D > 1/20. Always check and **state** the thin-wall validity at the beginning. If violated, the membrane theory is invalid — you must use thick-wall (Lamé's) equations instead.
>
> 7. **Forgot to state the failure mode direction in design questions (2 marks):** When asked about vessel design, always state: "Maximum principal stress = hoop stress → failure occurs as a longitudinal crack."

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Biaxial Stresses in Thin-Walled Pressure Vessels**
>
> ✅ **Thin-wall condition:** $t/D \leq 1/20$ (or 1/10) — **always verify** at the start of any numerical.
>
> ✅ **Membrane theory assumption:** Radial stress is neglected → **plane stress** state.
>
> ✅ **Cylinder stresses (master formulas):**
>    - $\sigma_h = pD/(2t)$ ← **maximum** stress, governs design
>    - $\sigma_L = pD/(4t) = \sigma_h/2$
>
> ✅ **Spherical stress (master formula):**
>    - $\sigma = pD/(4t)$ ← acts **identically** in all tangent directions
>
> ✅ **Critical ratio:** $\sigma_h^{cyl} = 2\sigma_L^{cyl} = 2\sigma^{sph}$ for identical inputs.
>
> ✅ **Failure mode:** Cracks run **longitudinally** (parallel to cylinder axis) because they open perpendicular to the maximum principal stress (hoop).
>
> ✅ **Biaxial Hooke's Law (must use this, NOT uniaxial):**
>    - $\varepsilon_h = (\sigma_h - \nu\sigma_L)/E$
>    - $\varepsilon_L = (\sigma_L - \nu\sigma_h)/E$
>    - Sphere: $\varepsilon = \sigma(1-\nu)/E$
>
> ✅ **Diameter change:** $\Delta D = D \cdot \varepsilon_h$ (use hoop strain for cylinder diameter; use tangent strain for sphere).
>
> ✅ **Length change:** $\Delta L = L \cdot \varepsilon_L$ (only meaningful for cylinder).
>
> ✅ **Volumetric strain (compact form):**
>    - Cylinder: $\varepsilon_v = (1-2\nu)(\sigma_h + \sigma_L)/E = 3pD(1-2\nu)/(4tE)$
>    - Sphere: $\varepsilon_v = 3\varepsilon = 3pD(1-\nu)/(4tE)$
>
> ✅ **Volume change:** $\Delta V = V \cdot \varepsilon_v$, where $V$ is the shell material volume ($\pi D L t$ for cylinder, $\pi D^3/6$ for sphere).
>
> ✅ **Naval architecture context:** Spheres = deep submersible hulls; cylinders = marine boilers, air flasks, LNG tanks. Spheres are **twice as material-efficient** as cylinders for the same pressure.
>
> ✅ **Units check (always do first):** Convert all dimensions to **metres** and pressure to **Pa** before substituting. Final stress should be in **Pa** or **MPa**; strain is **dimensionless**.
>
> ✅ **Common traps:** Sign of strain from negative Poisson effect; forgetting to multiply by 2 in $dV/V = 2dD/D + dL/L$ for cylinder; using $\varepsilon_v = 3\varepsilon$ on a cylinder (wrong — cylinder uses 2ε_h + ε_L).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Torsion: Introduction to Torsion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST303 - SOLID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Torsion: Introduction to Torsion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Torsion** is the twisting of a structural or mechanical member about its longitudinal axis when subjected to equal and opposite torques (twisting moments) acting in planes perpendicular to the axis of the member. The member subjected to torsion is commonly called a **shaft**, and the study of its deformation, stresses, and angular twist under applied torque constitutes the theory of torsion.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCNST303 - Module 4):**
> *Torsion of circular shafts — Derivation of torsion equation, polar modulus, torsional rigidity, power transmitted by a shaft, composite shafts, statically and dynamically indeterminate shafts.*

When a cylindrical shaft is fixed at one end and a torque is applied at the free end, every cross-section rotates by a small angle relative to the fixed end. This rotation is called the **angle of twist**, denoted as **θ (theta)**, measured in **radians**.

In naval architecture, torsion is critically important because:
- **Propeller shafts** transmit torque from the marine engine to the propeller.
- **Steering gear shafts** (rudder stocks) experience torsion during course corrections.
- **Drilling and offshore rig shafts** transmit high torques in deep-sea platforms.
- **Turbo-generator shafts** in LNG carriers experience cyclic torsional loading.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Wet Towel & Screwdriver

Imagine you are **wringing water out of a wet towel** by twisting it from both ends in opposite directions. The fibers of the towel, originally straight, now form a **helix pattern**. Similarly, in a shaft under torsion:

- The **longitudinal fibers** of the shaft (initially parallel to the axis) deform into **helical curves**.
- The **outer fibers** stretch more than the inner fibers because they lie farther from the axis.
- This is exactly analogous to **turning a screwdriver**: the harder you grip and twist the handle, the more the shaft resists, and you can feel the torsional resistance build up.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constant Used in Torsion:**
> The **Modulus of Rigidity (G)**, also known as the **Shear Modulus**, is the property that quantifies a material's resistance to shear deformation. For mild steel, **G ≈ 80 GPa**, and for marine-grade aluminum alloys, **G ≈ 27 GPa**. The KTU board exam standard value for steel shafts is **G = 80 × 10⁹ N/m²** unless otherwise specified.

## 1.3 Types of Shafts Under Torsion

| Shaft Type | Description | Application in Naval Architecture |
|---|---|---|
| **Solid Circular Shaft** | Single uniform cross-section of diameter *d* | Drive shafts in small marine engines |
| **Hollow Circular Shaft** | Tube with outer diameter *D* and inner diameter *d* | Propeller shafts — weight-critical |
| **Composite Shaft** | Two or more materials bonded together | Cost-optimized marine propulsion |
| **Stepped Shaft** | Multiple diameters along length | Marine gear transmission systems |

> [!TIP]
> **Why hollow shafts are preferred in ships:** Hollow shafts give the **same torsional strength** as solid shafts of the same outer diameter but with significantly **less weight** — a critical factor in ship design where every kilogram affects displacement, fuel economy, and stability.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Assumptions in the Theory of Pure Torsion

The classical torsion theory (developed independently by **Coulomb** and **Saint-Venant**) for circular shafts rests on the following assumptions:

1. The shaft material is **homogeneous**, **isotropic**, and obeys **Hooke's law in shear** (τ = Gγ).
2. Cross-sections **plane and circular** before loading **remain plane and circular** after loading (Saint-Venant's principle).
3. Cross-sections **rotate about the longitudinal axis** as if they were rigid discs (no warping).
4. Radii remain **straight and undeformed in length** — only their orientation rotates.
5. The twist is **uniform** along the length, and the shaft is **prismatic** (constant cross-section).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These assumptions are **strictly valid only for circular cross-sections**. For non-circular sections (rectangular, I-section), the cross-section **warps** and the simple formula τ = Tr/J does **NOT** apply.

## 2.2 The Torsion Equation — Master Formula

The **torsion equation** is the cornerstone of shaft design. It links three key quantities — shear stress, torque, angle of twist, and geometry:

$$
\frac{T}{J} \;=\; \frac{\tau}{r} \;=\; \frac{G\,\theta}{L}
$$

Where:
- **T** = Applied torque (N·m)
- **J** = Polar moment of inertia of the cross-section (m⁴)
- **τ** = Shear stress at radius *r* (Pa or N/m²)
- **r** = Radial distance from the center of the shaft (m)
- **G** = Modulus of rigidity of the material (Pa or N/m²)
- **θ** = Angle of twist over length L (radians)
- **L** = Length of the shaft under consideration (m)

## 2.3 Polar Moment of Inertia — Key Geometric Property

The **polar moment of inertia (J)** is a geometric property that quantifies the cross-section's resistance to torsional deformation. For circular sections:

**For a Solid Shaft:**
$$
J_{\text{solid}} \;=\; \frac{\pi\,d^{4}}{32}
$$

**For a Hollow Shaft:**
$$
J_{\text{hollow}} \;=\; \frac{\pi\,(D^{4}-d^{4})}{32}
$$

Where **D** = outer diameter and **d** = inner diameter.

## 2.4 Power Transmitted by a Shaft

The mechanical power transmitted by a rotating shaft at an angular velocity **N** (in revolutions per minute, rpm) under torque **T** is given by:

$$
P \;=\; \frac{2\pi N T}{60} \quad \text{(watts)}
$$

Where:
- **P** = Power in **watts (W)**
- **N** = Rotational speed in **rpm**
- **T** = Torque in **N·m**

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\frac{T}{J} \;=\; \frac{\tau}{r} \;=\; \frac{G\theta}{L}$ | Master Torsion Equation | Mixed SI |
| 2 | $\tau_{\max} \;=\; \frac{16\,T}{\pi\,d^{3}}$ | Max shear stress in **solid** circular shaft | Pa |
| 3 | $\tau_{\max} \;=\; \frac{16\,T\,D}{\pi\,(D^{4}-d^{4})}$ | Max shear stress in **hollow** circular shaft | Pa |
| 4 | $\theta \;=\; \frac{T\,L}{G\,J}$ | Angle of twist over length *L* | rad |
| 5 | $J_{\text{solid}} \;=\; \frac{\pi\,d^{4}}{32}$ | Polar moment of inertia, solid | m⁴ |
| 6 | $J_{\text{hollow}} \;=\; \frac{\pi\,(D^{4}-d^{4})}{32}$ | Polar moment of inertia, hollow | m⁴ |
| 7 | $Z_{p} \;=\; \frac{J}{r_{\max}} \;=\; \frac{\pi\,d^{3}}{16}$ | Polar section modulus, solid | m³ |
| 8 | $K_{t} \;=\; G\,J$ | Torsional rigidity (resistance to twist) | N·m² |
| 9 | $P \;=\; \frac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | Power transmitted by rotating shaft | W |
| 10 | $\gamma_{\max} \;=\; \frac{\tau_{\max}}{G}$ | Maximum shear strain (dimensionless) | — |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In naval architecture, the torsion equation governs:
- **Sizing of propeller shafts** based on engine power and rpm.
- **Selection of shaft material** for marine-grade steels (ASTM A36, A131).
- **Design of intermediate shaft bearings** and thrust blocks.
- **Torsional vibration analysis** of marine propulsion systems to avoid resonance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marine Industry Application:**
> A typical container ship's propeller shaft may transmit **30,000 kW** at **120 rpm**. The required shaft diameter based on allowable shear stress of 60 MPa works out to approximately **0.5 m**, illustrating the massive scale at which torsion theory operates in shipbuilding.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Torsion Equation τ/r = T/J = Gθ/L

### Step 1: Consider a Circular Shaft Subjected to Pure Torsion

Let us consider a solid circular shaft of radius **R**, length **L**, fixed at one end (say, the left end at z = 0) and free at the other end (z = L). A torque **T** is applied at the free end about the longitudinal z-axis.

### Step 2: Apply the Assumption of Plane Sections Remaining Plane

Before loading, mark a cross-section at distance *z* from the fixed end. After applying torque **T**, this cross-section rotates through an angle **φ(z)** about the z-axis. By Saint-Venant's assumption, every cross-section remains plane and circular.

The **angle of twist per unit length** (rate of twist) is:

$$
\frac{d\varphi}{dz} \;=\; \theta' \quad \text{(a constant for uniform torque)}
$$

So over the full length **L**, the total angle of twist is:

$$
\theta \;=\; \frac{d\varphi}{dz}\cdot L
$$

### Step 3: Geometric Analysis — Compute Shear Strain γ

Consider a small rectangular element **ABCD** on the surface of the shaft, with:
- Side **AB** along the longitudinal axis (length = dx)
- Side **AD** along the circumferential direction (length = dy = R·dφ)

Originally, the angle at A is **90°**. After twisting, the element deforms such that side **AB** tilts by a small angle **γ** (the shear strain).

The longitudinal fiber of length **dx** on the surface moves tangentially by an arc length:

$$
\text{Arc displacement} \;=\; R \cdot d\varphi
$$

The shear strain (change in right angle) is therefore:

$$
\gamma \;=\; \frac{R\cdot d\varphi}{dx} \;=\; R \cdot \frac{d\varphi}{dz}
$$

For a generic fiber at radius **r** (not necessarily on the surface):

$$
\gamma \;=\; r \cdot \frac{d\varphi}{dz}
$$

### Step 4: Apply Hooke's Law in Shear

Since the material is elastic and isotropic, the shear stress at radius *r* is related to the shear strain by:

$$
\tau \;=\; G \cdot \gamma
$$

Substituting the expression for γ:

$$
\tau \;=\; G \cdot r \cdot \frac{d\varphi}{dz}
$$

$$
\frac{\tau}{r} \;=\; G \cdot \frac{d\varphi}{dz}
$$

### Step 5: Static Equilibrium — Sum of Torques on a Cross-Section

The torque **T** applied externally must be balanced by the internal resisting torque generated by the distributed shear stresses on the cross-section. Taking an elementary annular ring of radius *r*, width *dr*, and area dA = 2πr·dr, the shear stress τ acts tangentially and contributes a torque:

$$
dT \;=\; \tau \cdot dA \cdot r \;=\; \tau \cdot (2\pi r \, dr) \cdot r \;=\; 2\pi \tau r^{2}\, dr
$$

Total torque by integration over the entire cross-section:

$$
T \;=\; \int_{0}^{R} 2\pi \tau r^{2}\, dr
$$

Substitute τ from Step 4: τ = G·(dφ/dz)·r:

$$
T \;=\; \int_{0}^{R} 2\pi \left[G \cdot \frac{d\varphi}{dz} \cdot r\right] r^{2}\, dr
$$

$$
T \;=\; 2\pi\, G \cdot \frac{d\varphi}{dz} \int_{0}^{R} r^{3}\, dr
$$

The integral evaluates to:

$$
\int_{0}^{R} r^{3}\, dr \;=\; \frac{R^{4}}{4}
$$

So:

$$
T \;=\; 2\pi\, G \cdot \frac{d\varphi}{dz} \cdot \frac{R^{4}}{4} \;=\; \frac{\pi\, G\, R^{4}}{2} \cdot \frac{d\varphi}{dz}
$$

### Step 6: Recognize the Polar Moment of Inertia

For a solid circular shaft of diameter d = 2R:

$$
J \;=\; \frac{\pi\, d^{4}}{32} \;=\; \frac{\pi\, (2R)^{4}}{32} \;=\; \frac{2\pi\, R^{4}}{4} \;=\; \frac{\pi\, R^{4}}{2}
$$

So the equilibrium equation becomes:

$$
T \;=\; J \cdot G \cdot \frac{d\varphi}{dz}
$$

$$
\frac{T}{J} \;=\; G \cdot \frac{d\varphi}{dz}
$$

### Step 7: Combine Equations to Form the Master Torsion Equation

From Step 4:

$$
\frac{\tau}{r} \;=\; G \cdot \frac{d\varphi}{dz}
$$

From Step 6:

$$
\frac{T}{J} \;=\; G \cdot \frac{d\varphi}{dz}
$$

Equating the two:

$$
\frac{T}{J} \;=\; \frac{\tau}{r}
$$

Also, since dφ/dz is constant over length L:

$$
\frac{d\varphi}{dz} \;=\; \frac{\theta}{L}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\frac{T}{J} \;=\; \frac{\tau}{r} \;=\; \frac{G\theta}{L}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Master Equation:**
> $$\frac{T}{J} \;=\; \frac{\tau}{r} \;=\; \frac{G\theta}{L}$$
> This is the **torsion equation** — the single most important equation in torsion analysis. Every KTU problem on torsion of circular shafts begins from this identity.

## 3.2 Derivation of Maximum Shear Stress in Solid Shaft

From the torsion equation, the shear stress at the outer surface (r = R = d/2) is maximum:

$$
\tau_{\max} \;=\; \frac{T \cdot R}{J} \;=\; \frac{T \cdot (d/2)}{\pi d^{4}/32} \;=\; \frac{16\,T}{\pi\, d^{3}}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Power Equation

If the shaft rotates at **N rpm**, the angular velocity in rad/s is:

$$
\omega \;=\; \frac{2\pi N}{60}
$$

The mechanical power transmitted (work per unit time = torque × angular velocity):

$$
P \;=\; T \cdot \omega \;=\; T \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60}
$$

Therefore:

$$
P \;=\; \frac{2\pi N T}{60} \quad \text{watts}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Torsion Analysis Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputA[Applied Torque T at Free End] --> fixEnd[Fixed End Reaction Torque T at Wall]
    fixEnd --> sectionCut[Cross Section at Distance z from Fixed End]
    sectionCut --> planeA[Cross Section rotates by angle phi]
    sectionCut --> planeB[Longitudinal fiber AB deforms]
    planeB --> strain[Shear Strain gamma equals r times dphi by dz]
    strain --> hooke[Hooke's Law in Shear tau equals G gamma]
    hooke --> stressDist[Shear Stress varies linearly with r]
    stressDist --> stressMax[Maximum tau at outer surface r equals R]
    planeA --> angleTwist[Total Angle of Twist theta over length L]
    angleTwist --> torsRigidity[Torsional Rigidity G times J]
    stressMax --> masterEq[Master Equation T by J equals tau by r equals G theta by L]
    torsRigidity --> masterEq
    masterEq --> designOut[Shaft Sizing and Material Selection]
    designOut --> marineApp[Marine Propeller Shaft Application]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Shaft Geometry & Loading

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BC["Boundary Conditions"]
        fix["Fixed Support at z = 0"]
        free["Free End at z = L"]
    end
    subgraph SHAFT["Circular Shaft Geometry"]
        geom["Outer Diameter D, Length L"]
        mat["Material Property G, allowable tau"]
    end
    subgraph LOAD["External Loading"]
        torq["Applied Torque T N m at Free End"]
        rpm["Rotational Speed N rpm"]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["Engineering Outputs"]
        pwr["Power P equals 2 pi N T over 60 W"]
        tauMax["Max Shear Stress tau max equals 16 T over pi d cubed"]
        twist["Angle of Twist theta equals T L over G J rad"]
    end
    fix --- SHAFT
    SHAFT --- free
    torq --> SHAFT
    rpm --> pwr
    SHAFT --> tauMax
    SHAFT --> twist
    SHAFT --> pwr
    mat --> SHAFT
```

## 4.3 Schematic Description of Torsion Deformation (Verbal Block Diagram)

Since the physical deformation of a shaft under torsion involves continuous 3D geometry that cannot be precisely captured in text, the following block-level schematic describes the key kinematic states:

**State A — Undeformed Shaft (Reference Configuration):**
- Longitudinal fibers run parallel to the z-axis.
- Cross-sections are vertical planes perpendicular to z.
- A generator line AA' lies on the surface, parallel to z.

**State B — Deformed Shaft Under Torque T:**
- Cross-sections rotate by angle φ(z) about z-axis.
- Generator line AA' tilts by shear strain γ.
- AA' now forms a **helix** of pitch angle α where **tan(α) = γ**.
- Cross-section AA rotates as a rigid disc; cross-section A'A' rotates by a different angle.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Define the term 'angle of twist' in a shaft subjected to torsion. State the expression for the angle of twist of a solid circular shaft of length L and diameter d, subjected to a torque T. Take the modulus of rigidity as G.**

**Model Answer:**

The **angle of twist (θ)** is the relative rotation, measured in **radians**, of one cross-section of a shaft with respect to another cross-section at a distance L, when the shaft is subjected to a torque T.

For a solid circular shaft:

$$
\theta \;=\; \frac{T\,L}{G\,J} \;=\; \frac{32\,T\,L}{G\,\pi\,d^{4}}
$$

where:
- T = applied torque (N·m)
- L = length of shaft (m)
- G = modulus of rigidity (N/m²)
- d = diameter of shaft (m)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Define polar modulus. Write down the polar modulus for a solid circular shaft of diameter d.**

**Model Answer:**

The **polar section modulus (Z_p)** is defined as the ratio of the polar moment of inertia (J) to the radius of the shaft (r). It represents the geometric property that directly gives the maximum shear stress:

$$
Z_{p} \;=\; \frac{J}{r_{\max}} \;=\; \frac{\pi\,d^{3}}{16}
$$

It has units of **m³**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a) A solid steel shaft of diameter 60 mm transmits 60 kW of power at 200 rpm. Determine:**
**(i) The torque transmitted by the shaft.**
**(ii) The maximum shear stress developed in the shaft.**
**(iii) The angle of twist in degrees per metre length of the shaft.**

**Take G = 80 GPa.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Diameter d = 60 mm = 0.06 m
- Power P = 60 kW = 60 × 10³ W
- Speed N = 200 rpm
- G = 80 × 10⁹ N/m²

**(i) Torque T:**

From P = 2πNT/60:

$$
T \;=\; \frac{P \cdot 60}{2\pi N} \;=\; \frac{60 \times 10^{3} \times 60}{2\pi \times 200}
$$

$$
T \;=\; \frac{3.6 \times 10^{6}}{1256.64} \;=\; 2864.79 \;\text{N·m}
$$

**(ii) Maximum Shear Stress:**

$$
\tau_{\max} \;=\; \frac{16\,T}{\pi\, d^{3}} \;=\; \frac{16 \times 2864.79}{\pi \times (0.06)^{3}}
$$

$$
\tau_{\max} \;=\; \frac{45{,}836.6}{6.786 \times 10^{-4}} \;=\; 67.55 \times 10^{6} \;\text{Pa} \;=\; 67.55 \;\text{MPa}
$$

**(iii) Angle of Twist per Metre:**

Polar moment of inertia:

$$
J \;=\; \frac{\pi\,d^{4}}{32} \;=\; \frac{\pi \times (0.06)^{4}}{32} \;=\; 1.272 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{m}^{4}
$$

Angle of twist per metre (θ/L):

$$
\frac{\theta}{L} \;=\; \frac{T}{G\,J} \;=\; \frac{2864.79}{80 \times 10^{9} \times 1.272 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
\frac{\theta}{L} \;=\; \frac{2864.79}{101{,}760} \;=\; 0.02815 \;\text{rad/m}
$$

Converting to degrees per metre:

$$
\frac{\theta}{L} \;=\; 0.02815 \times \frac{180}{\pi} \;=\; 1.613 \;^\circ/\text{m}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) T = **2864.79 N·m**
- (ii) τ_max = **67.55 MPa**
- (iii) θ/L = **1.613°/m**

---

**(b) Derive the torsion equation $\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G\theta}{L}$ for a solid circular shaft, stating clearly the assumptions involved.**

**Model Solution Outline:**

**Assumptions:**
1. Material is homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's law in shear.
2. Cross-sections plane before loading remain plane after loading.
3. Cross-sections rotate as rigid discs about the longitudinal axis.
4. Radii remain straight and undeformed in length.
5. Twist is uniform along the length.

**Derivation Steps (Detailed):**

**Step 1 — Geometric Strain:**

Consider a fiber at radius r on the surface after twisting through angle φ:

$$
\gamma \;=\; \frac{r \, d\varphi}{dz}
$$

**Step 2 — Hooke's Law:**

$$
\tau \;=\; G\gamma \;=\; G \cdot r \cdot \frac{d\varphi}{dz}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\frac{\tau}{r} \;=\; G \cdot \frac{d\varphi}{dz} \quad \text{...(Equation 1)}
$$

**Step 3 — Equilibrium:**

Total internal torque from shear stresses on cross-section:

$$
T \;=\; \int_{0}^{R} \tau \cdot r \cdot 2\pi r \, dr \;=\; 2\pi \int_{0}^{R} \tau r^{2} \, dr
$$

Substituting τ from Equation 1:

$$
T \;=\; 2\pi\, G \cdot \frac{d\varphi}{dz} \int_{0}^{R} r^{3} \, dr \;=\; 2\pi\, G \cdot \frac{d\varphi}{dz} \cdot \frac{R^{4}}{4}
$$

$$
T \;=\; \frac{\pi R^{4}}{2} \cdot G \cdot \frac{d\varphi}{dz}
$$

Recognizing J = πd⁴/32 = πR⁴/2:

$$
T \;=\; J \cdot G \cdot \frac{d\varphi}{dz}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\frac{T}{J} \;=\; G \cdot \frac{d\varphi}{dz} \quad \text{...(Equation 2)}
$$

**Step 4 — Combining:**

From Equations 1 and 2, and since dφ/dz = θ/L:

$$
\frac{T}{J} \;=\; \frac{\tau}{r} \;=\; \frac{G\theta}{L}
$$

This is the **torsion equation**. (7 marks for full derivation + 7 marks for assumptions and clarity = **14 marks**)

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a) A hollow steel shaft of outer diameter 80 mm and inner diameter 50 mm is required to transmit 150 kW at 250 rpm. Calculate:**
**(i) The torque transmitted.**
**(ii) The maximum shear stress in the shaft.**
**(iii) The angle of twist per metre length.**

**Take G = 80 GPa.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- D = 80 mm = 0.08 m
- d = 50 mm = 0.05 m
- P = 150 kW = 150 × 10³ W
- N = 250 rpm
- G = 80 × 10⁹ N/m²

**(i) Torque T:**

$$
T \;=\; \frac{P \cdot 60}{2\pi N} \;=\; \frac{150 \times 10^{3} \times 60}{2\pi \times 250} \;=\; \frac{9 \times 10^{6}}{1570.8} \;=\; 5729.58 \;\text{N·m}
$$

**(ii) Maximum Shear Stress:**

$$
\tau_{\max} \;=\; \frac{16\,T\,D}{\pi\,(D^{4}-d^{4})}
$$

Compute D⁴ = (0.08)⁴ = 4.096 × 10⁻⁵ m⁴
Compute d⁴ = (0.05)⁴ = 6.25 × 10⁻⁶ m⁴
D⁴ − d⁴ = 4.096 × 10⁻⁵ − 6.25 × 10⁻⁶ = 3.471 × 10⁻⁵ m⁴

$$
\tau_{\max} \;=\; \frac{16 \times 5729.58 \times 0.08}{\pi \times 3.471 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

$$
\tau_{\max} \;=\; \frac{7333.86}{1.090 \times 10^{-4}} \;=\; 67.28 \times 10^{6} \;\text{Pa} \;=\; 67.28 \;\text{MPa}
$$

**(iii) Angle of Twist per Metre:**

Polar moment of inertia:

$$
J \;=\; \frac{\pi\,(D^{4}-d^{4})}{32} \;=\; \frac{\pi \times 3.471 \times 10^{-5}}{32} \;=\; 3.407 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{m}^{4}
$$

$$
\frac{\theta}{L} \;=\; \frac{T}{G\,J} \;=\; \frac{5729.58}{80 \times 10^{9} \times 3.407 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
\frac{\theta}{L} \;=\; \frac{5729.58}{272{,}560} \;=\; 0.02102 \;\text{rad/m} \;=\; 1.204 \;^\circ/\text{m}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) T = **5729.58 N·m**
- (ii) τ_max = **67.28 MPa**
- (iii) θ/L = **1.204°/m**

---

**(b) Explain the difference between solid and hollow shafts under torsion. Why are hollow shafts preferred for marine propeller shafts?**

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | Solid Shaft | Hollow Shaft |
|---|---|---|
| Material use | Material near axis carries little stress | All material is stressed efficiently |
| Weight for same strength | Heavier | Lighter (up to 40% saving) |
| Diameter ratio | Single diameter *d* | Outer D, inner d |
| Strength-to-weight | Lower | Higher |
| Cost | Lower initial cost | Higher initial cost but better life |

**Why Hollow Shafts are Preferred in Marine Applications:**

1. **Weight Reduction:** Critical for ship displacement, draft, and stability.
2. **Material Economy:** The inner fibers in a solid shaft carry negligible stress, so removing them loses little strength but saves considerable weight.
3. **Higher Torsional Strength-to-Weight Ratio:** A hollow shaft of the same outer diameter as a solid shaft has higher J and hence higher torsional capacity.
4. **Better Resistance to Buckling:** Under combined torsion and bending, hollow sections resist lateral buckling better.
5. **Easier Inspection:** Allows access for non-destructive testing of inner surfaces.
6. **Reduced Whirling Speed:** Lower mass → lower tendency for transverse vibration at high rpm.

(7 marks for the table + 7 marks for the marine application explanation = **14 marks**)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Students frequently forget to convert mm to m, leading to errors by a factor of 10⁶. **Always convert all dimensions to SI units (metres) before calculation.**
> 2. **Power Formula Confusion:** Some students write P = 2πNT (without dividing by 60), giving answers that are off by a factor of 60.
> 3. **Polar Modulus Confusion:** Mixing up section modulus Z_p = πd³/16 with the moment of inertia J = πd⁴/32 — both involve π and d, but exponents are different.
> 4. **Hollow Shaft Formula Errors:** Using d⁴ − D⁴ instead of D⁴ − d⁴ leads to negative values. **Outer diameter D must always be raised first.**
> 5. **Forgetting to State Assumptions:** KTU evaluators allocate 2-3 marks specifically for stating the **5 torsion assumptions**. Always list them before deriving.
> 6. **Angle of Twist Unit:** Final answer should be in **radians** OR **degrees** — not mixed. KTU expects rad/m or °/m consistently.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Torsion Introduction**

- ✅ Torsion = **twisting** of a shaft under torque about its longitudinal axis.
- ✅ **Master Torsion Equation:** $\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G\theta}{L}$
- ✅ **Maximum shear stress** occurs at the **outer surface** of the shaft.
- ✅ **Shear stress distribution** is **linear** with radius (zero at center, maximum at outer surface).
- ✅ **Angle of twist θ** is measured in **radians**.
- ✅ **Polar moment of inertia** for solid: $J = \pi d^4/32$; for hollow: $J = \pi(D^4 - d^4)/32$.
- ✅ **Polar section modulus:** $Z_p = \pi d^3 / 16$ for solid shaft.
- ✅ **Torsional rigidity** = $G \cdot J$ (resistance to angular deformation).
- ✅ **Power formula:** $P = 2\pi N T / 60$ (N in rpm, P in watts).
- ✅ **5 Assumptions** must be stated: homogeneity, isotropy, plane sections remain plane, no warping, prismatic shaft.
- ✅ **Valid only for circular cross-sections** — non-circular sections warp and require Saint-Venant's warping function analysis.
- ✅ **Hollow shafts preferred** in marine applications for weight savings and higher strength-to-weight ratio.
- ✅ **Standard G values:** Steel = 80 GPa, Cast iron = 45 GPa, Bronze = 40 GPa, Aluminum = 27 GPa.
- ✅ **Unit consistency is critical:** Convert mm → m before substituting in formulas.
- ✅ **KTU favourite problem types:** (1) Power-to-torque-to-stress chain; (2) Hollow vs solid comparison; (3) Derivation of torsion equation.
- ✅ **Angle of twist direction** depends on the sense of applied torque — use right-hand rule.
- ✅ **Pure torsion** assumes no bending or axial loading — combined loading is a higher-Module topic.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Theory of Pure Torsion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST303 - SOLID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Theory of Pure Torsion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Pure Torsion** is defined as the state of loading in a structural member (typically a cylindrical shaft) in which it is subjected to equal and opposite twisting moments (torques) about its longitudinal axis, with the cross-sections remaining plane and undistorted, and the axis of the shaft remaining straight and undeformed.

In more rigorous KTU 2024 terminology:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pure Torsion (KTU Definition):** A condition of shear loading in which a prismatic bar (shaft) is acted upon by two equal, opposite, and parallel couples acting in planes perpendicular to the longitudinal axis of the bar. The internal resisting moment at any cross-section is termed the **Torsional Moment (T)** or **Torque**, and the resulting deformation is a relative rotation (twist) between cross-sections.

The KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes understanding torsion as a fundamental mode of loading in **marine propulsion shafts, propeller shafts, rudder stocks, and ship engine crankshafts** — making this topic extremely relevant to Naval Architecture.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **wringing a wet towel** with both hands in opposite rotational directions. The towel twists around its axis. Now replace the towel with a solid metal rod (propeller shaft of a ship). The engine applies a turning force at one end (let's say counter-clockwise) and the propeller load applies an equal and opposite reaction at the other end (clockwise). The shaft "twists" — this is torsion.

**Another real-world analogy:** Think of a **screwdriver turning a screw**. Your hand applies torque, the screw resists, and the shaft of the screwdriver experiences pure torsion. In a ship, the **propeller shaft transmits torque from the marine engine to the propeller** — exactly the same physical phenomenon.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (KTU 2024):** In *pure* torsion, no bending, axial force, or shear force acts on the member — only twisting moments. The bar is in a state of pure shear stress.

## 1.3 Physical Quantities and Standard Metrics

- **Torque (T):** Measured in **N·m** (Newton-meters) in SI units. KTU 2024 also allows **kN·m** for large marine shafts.
- **Shear Stress (τ):** Measured in **N/m²** (Pa) or **MPa (N/mm²)**. For marine steel shafts, allowable τ ≈ **40–80 MPa**.
- **Shear Modulus (G):** Material property; for **mild steel G ≈ 80 GPa**; for **marine aluminum alloys G ≈ 26–28 GPa**.
- **Polar Moment of Inertia (J):** Measured in **mm⁴** or **m⁴**.
- **Angle of Twist (θ):** Measured in **radians (rad)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Fundamental Assumptions of the Theory of Pure Torsion (Coulomb's Theory, 1784)

The classical theory of pure torsion rests on the following key assumptions, which the student **must memorize** for KTU exams:

1. **Material Homogeneity and Isotropy:** The shaft material has uniform properties in all directions.
2. **Material Linearity & Hookean Behavior:** Shear stress is proportional to shear strain (τ = G·γ), where **G** is the shear modulus.
3. **Plane Sections Remain Plane and Undistorted:** Cross-sections perpendicular to the axis remain flat, with no warping. (This is exact for circular cross-sections only — a key KTU 2024 highlight.)
4. **Radial Lines Remain Radial and Straight:** Lines drawn from the center of the cross-section to the boundary remain straight and rotate as rigid radii.
5. **Circular Cross-Sections Remain Circular:** A circular section stays circular after twisting (i.e., the diameter and shape do not change, only rotation occurs).
6. **The Shaft is Loaded by End Couples Only:** No transverse forces, axial loads, or bending moments are present.
7. **The Shaft is Prismatic (Constant Cross-Section):** Between the applied torques.
8. **Saint-Venant's Principle applies:** Stress distribution near the points of application of the torque is non-uniform, but becomes uniform away from the ends.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The theory is **strictly valid for circular cross-sections** (solid or hollow). For non-circular sections (rectangular, I-section), warping occurs and the simple torsion formula does NOT apply — we need St. Venant's warping function approach.

## 2.2 Why the Theory Matters — Engineering Utility

In Naval Architecture, the theory of pure torsion governs the design of:
- **Propeller Shafts** (transmitting engine torque to propeller)
- **Intermediate Shafts** (in long shaft lines)
- **Rudder Stocks** (subjected to twisting moments from the rudder)
- **Torque Tubes** in marine gearboxes
- **Coupling Bolts and Flanges** in shafting systems

The two most critical design questions in marine shaft design are:
1. Will the shaft **fail** due to excessive shear stress? → Check **τ_max ≤ τ_allowable**
2. Will the shaft **twist excessively** under load, causing misalignment? → Check **θ_max ≤ θ_allowable**

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units (SI) |
|---|---------|-------------|------------|
| 1 | $\tau \;\vert\; r = G \gamma$ | Hooke's Law in Shear (tangent relation) | Pa, m, Pa, rad |
| 2 | $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$ | **Master Torsion Equation** (Universal) | N·m, m⁴, Pa, m, Pa, rad, m |
| 3 | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{T \cdot r_{max}}{J} = \dfrac{T}{Z_p}$ | Maximum Shear Stress (at outer surface) | Pa, N·m, m, m⁴, m³ |
| 4 | $\theta = \dfrac{T \cdot L}{G \cdot J}$ | Angle of Twist over length L | rad, N·m, m, Pa, m⁴ |
| 5 | $J = \dfrac{\pi d^{4}}{32}$ | Polar Moment of Inertia (Solid Circular Shaft) | m⁴, m |
| 6 | $J = \dfrac{\pi (D^{4} - d^{4})}{32}$ | Polar Moment of Inertia (Hollow Circular Shaft) | m⁴, m |
| 7 | $Z_p = \dfrac{J}{r_{max}} = \dfrac{\pi d^{3}}{16}$ | Polar Section Modulus (Solid) | m³ |
| 8 | $Z_p = \dfrac{\pi (D^{4} - d^{4})}{16 D}$ | Polar Section Modulus (Hollow) | m³ |
| 9 | $T_{max} = \tau_{allow} \cdot Z_p$ | Maximum Torque Capacity of shaft | N·m, Pa, m³ |
| 10 | $P = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | Power transmitted by shaft | W, rpm, N·m |
| 11 | $T = \dfrac{9550 \cdot P}{N}$ | Torque from Power (with P in kW, N in rpm) | N·m |
| 12 | $U = \dfrac{T^{2} L}{2 G J}$ | Strain Energy stored in torsion | J, N·m, m, Pa, m⁴ |
| 13 | $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$ | Relation between E, G, and Poisson's ratio | Pa, Pa, — |
| 14 | $\tau_{allow} = \dfrac{0.5 \sigma_{y}}{FOS}$ | Allowable shear stress (von Mises / max-shear) | Pa, Pa, — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trick to Remember:** The master equation $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$ is the **single most tested formula** in this module. It is the "backbone" of all torsion problems. Memorize the three ratios: T:J, τ:r, Gθ:L.

## 2.4 Real-World Marine Engineering Utility

Consider a **marine propeller shaft** with diameter d = 200 mm, transmitting 1500 kW at 250 rpm. The maximum shear stress in the shaft is calculated using $\tau_{max} = 16T / (\pi d^3)$. The angle of twist per meter of shaft length determines whether the engine and propeller will remain in phase (critical for controllable pitch propellers and synchronous marine systems). An excessive twist could lead to vibration and fatigue failure.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Master Torsion Equation

### 3.1.1 Setup and Geometry

Consider a circular shaft of:
- Length = $L$
- Radius = $R$ (outer radius)
- Fixed at one end, free at the other
- Subjected to a torque $T$ at the free end

Let the free end rotate by an angle $\theta$ relative to the fixed end. Consider a point on the outer surface at radius $R$. The arc length traversed by this point is:

$$s = R \theta$$

The shear strain $\gamma$ at radius $R$ is the arc length divided by the original length $L$ (small angle approximation, since $\gamma$ is small for elastic deformation):

$$\gamma = \frac{s}{L} = \frac{R \theta}{L}$$

### 3.1.2 Hooke's Law in Shear

By Hooke's Law in shear:

$$\tau = G \gamma \quad \text{(at the outer surface, } r = R \text{)}$$

Substituting:

$$\tau = G \cdot \frac{R \theta}{L}$$

### 3.1.3 Strain at a General Radius r

By the assumption that **radial lines remain straight**, a point at any interior radius $r$ rotates by the same angle $\theta$ as the surface. Therefore, the arc length at radius $r$ is $r\theta$, and the shear strain at radius $r$ is:

$$\gamma(r) = \frac{r \theta}{L}$$

By Hooke's law, the shear stress at radius $r$ is:

$$\tau(r) = G \gamma(r) = \frac{G r \theta}{L}$$

This shows that **shear stress varies linearly with radius** — it is zero at the center and maximum at the outer surface.

### 3.1.4 Equilibrium (Resultant Torque)

The internal torque $T$ at any cross-section must equal the moment of the shear stress distribution about the central axis. The shear stress on an elemental ring of area $dA = 2\pi r \, dr$ at radius $r$ exerts a force $dF = \tau(r) \cdot dA$, and the moment of this force about the axis is $dM = r \cdot dF = r \cdot \tau(r) \cdot dA$.

Integrating over the entire circular cross-section:

$$T = \int_A r \cdot \tau(r) \, dA = \int_0^R r \cdot \left(\frac{G r \theta}{L}\right) \cdot (2\pi r \, dr)$$

$$T = \frac{2\pi G \theta}{L} \int_0^R r^3 \, dr$$

Evaluating the integral:

$$T = \frac{2\pi G \theta}{L} \cdot \left[\frac{r^4}{4}\right]_0^R = \frac{2\pi G \theta}{L} \cdot \frac{R^4}{4}$$

$$T = \frac{\pi G \theta R^4}{2L}$$

### 3.1.5 Polar Moment of Inertia

The polar moment of inertia of a solid circular cross-section is defined as:

$$J = \int_A r^2 \, dA = \int_0^R r^2 \cdot (2\pi r \, dr) = 2\pi \int_0^R r^3 \, dr = 2\pi \cdot \frac{R^4}{4} = \frac{\pi R^4}{2}$$

In terms of diameter $d = 2R$, so $R = d/2$:

$$J = \frac{\pi (d/2)^4}{2} = \frac{\pi d^4}{32}$$

### 3.1.6 Assembling the Master Equation

Substituting $J$ back into the equilibrium expression:

$$T = \frac{G \theta J}{L}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{T}{J} = \frac{G \theta}{L}$$

From the stress equation $\tau = G r \theta / L$, we have $G\theta / L = \tau / r$. Therefore:

$$\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G \theta}{L}$$

This is the **Master Torsion Equation** — the most important result in this module.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to "derive the torsion equation," you must show (1) the geometric relation $\gamma = r\theta/L$, (2) Hooke's law $\tau = G\gamma$, and (3) the equilibrium integral $T = \int r \tau \, dA$. Missing any one of these three steps costs 3-4 marks.

## 3.2 Derivation of Strain Energy in Torsion

The strain energy stored in a shaft under torsion is derived by considering the work done by the torque $T$ as the shaft twists by angle $\theta$:

$$U = \frac{1}{2} T \theta$$

Using $\theta = TL / (GJ)$:

$$U = \frac{1}{2} T \cdot \frac{TL}{GJ} = \frac{T^2 L}{2 G J}$$

This is the elastic strain energy stored in a shaft of length $L$ subjected to torque $T$. This expression is used in **Castigliano's theorem** for statically indeterminate shafts and in dynamic loading analysis (e.g., torsional impact on propeller shafts).

## 3.3 Derivation of Polar Section Modulus

The polar section modulus is defined as:

$$Z_p = \frac{J}{r_{max}}$$

For a solid circular shaft with $J = \pi d^4 / 32$ and $r_{max} = d/2$:

$$Z_p = \frac{\pi d^4 / 32}{d/2} = \frac{\pi d^3}{16}$$

Therefore, the maximum shear stress is:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{T}{Z_p} = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3}$$

For a hollow circular shaft with outer diameter $D$ and inner diameter $d$:

$$Z_p = \frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{16 D}$$

## 3.4 Derivation of Torsion Formula for Hollow Shaft

For a hollow shaft with outer radius $R$ and inner radius $r_i$, the polar moment of inertia is:

$$J = \int_{r_i}^{R} r^2 \, dA = \int_{r_i}^{R} r^2 \cdot (2\pi r \, dr) = 2\pi \cdot \frac{R^4 - r_i^4}{4} = \frac{\pi (R^4 - r_i^4)}{2}$$

In terms of diameters $D = 2R$ and $d = 2r_i$:

$$J = \frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32}$$

The maximum shear stress (at $r = R$) is:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{T \cdot R}{J} = \frac{16 T D}{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}$$

## 3.5 Derivation of Composite (Multi-Material) Shafts

For a shaft composed of multiple materials (e.g., a steel core bonded with a copper sleeve, common in marine applications), the angle of twist must be the same across all materials (compatibility condition):

$$\theta_1 = \theta_2 = \theta_3 = \dots = \theta$$

For each material $i$:

$$\theta_i = \frac{T_i L_i}{G_i J_i}$$

Equilibrium requires the sum of torques carried by each material equals the applied torque:

$$T = T_1 + T_2 + T_3 + \dots + T_n$$

Substituting $T_i = G_i J_i \theta_i / L_i$ and using common $L_i = L$:

$$T = \theta L \left( \frac{G_1 J_1 + G_2 J_2 + \dots + G_n J_n}{L} \right) = \frac{\theta}{L} \sum_{i=1}^{n} G_i J_i$$

Therefore:

$$\theta = \frac{T L}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} G_i J_i}$$

## 3.6 Derivation of Equivalent Solid Shaft Diameter

For a hollow shaft, it is often useful to find the equivalent solid shaft diameter that has the same polar section modulus:

$$Z_{p,\text{solid}} = Z_{p,\text{hollow}}$$

$$\frac{\pi d_{eq}^3}{16} = \frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{16 D}$$

$$d_{eq}^3 = \frac{D^4 - d^4}{D}$$

## 3.7 Power Transmission Relation

The power transmitted by a rotating shaft is:

$$P = T \cdot \omega$$

where $\omega$ is the angular velocity in rad/s. For $N$ revolutions per minute (rpm):

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} \;\; \text{(rad/s)}$$

Therefore:

$$P = T \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi N T}{60} \;\; \text{(Watts, with T in N·m)}$$

For practical KTU calculations with $P$ in kW and $N$ in rpm:

$$T = \frac{60 \cdot P \times 1000}{2\pi N} = \frac{9550 P}{N} \;\; \text{(N·m)}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Torsion Theory

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the logical flow of the theory of pure torsion, showing how the assumptions lead to formulas, which lead to design decisions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Assumptions of Pure Torsion] --> B[Isotropic Linear Material]
    A --> C[Plane Sections Remain Plane]
    A --> D[Radial Lines Remain Radial]
    A --> E[Circular Sections Stay Circular]
    A --> F[Loading by End Couples Only]

    B --> G[Hookes Law: tau = G gamma]
    C --> H[Geometric Relation: gamma = r theta over L]
    D --> H
    E --> H

    G --> I[Master Torsion Equation]
    H --> I
    I --> J[T over J = tau over r = G theta over L]

    J --> K[Stress Calculation]
    J --> L[Deformation Calculation]
    J --> M[Strain Energy Calculation]

    K --> K1[tau max = T r max over J]
    K --> K2[Polar Section Modulus Zp]
    L --> L1[Angle of Twist theta = TL over GJ]
    L --> L2[Torsional Rigidity GJ]
    M --> M1[Strain Energy U = T squared L over 2GJ]

    K1 --> N[Design Application]
    K2 --> N
    L1 --> N
    L2 --> N
    M1 --> N

    N --> N1[Propeller Shaft Sizing]
    N --> N2[Material Selection]
    N --> N3[Deflection Check]
    N --> N4[Failure Analysis]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram of Shear Stress Distribution and Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Original Cross Section] --> Q1[Twisted Cross Section]

    subgraph CROSS_SECTION
        CS1[Center: r = 0, tau = 0] --> CS2[Mid Radius: r = r/2, tau = G r theta over 2L]
        CS2 --> CS3[Outer Surface: r = R, tau max = G R theta over L]
    end

    subgraph STRESS_VARIATION
        SV1[Linear Variation with r] --> SV2[Zero at center]
        SV2 --> SV3[Maximum at outer radius]
    end

    Q1 --> CROSS_SECTION
    CROSS_SECTION --> STRESS_VARIATION

    SV3 --> AP1[Design Implication: Material near center is underutilized in solid shafts]
    AP1 --> AP2[Solution: Use Hollow Shafts to Save Material]
    AP2 --> AP3[Marine Application: Lightweight Propeller Shafts]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram of Problem-Solving Strategy in Torsion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Given: Torque T, Length L, Diameter d, Material G] --> S2{Type of Problem}

    S2 --> P1[Stress Problem]
    S2 --> P2[Deformation Problem]
    S2 --> P3[Design Sizing Problem]
    S2 --> P4[Power Transmission Problem]

    P1 --> P1A[Compute J = pi d to the 4 over 32]
    P1A --> P1B[Compute tau max = T R over J]
    P1B --> P1C[Compare with allowable stress]

    P2 --> P2A[Compute theta = T L over G J]
    P2A --> P2B[Check against allowable twist]

    P3 --> P3A[Set tau max = tau allowable]
    P3A --> P3B[Solve for d cubed = 16 T over pi tau allowable]
    P3B --> P3C[d cube = 16 T over pi tau allowable]

    P4 --> P4A[Compute omega = 2 pi N over 60]
    P4A --> P4B[Compute T = P over omega]
    P4B --> P4C[Apply Sizing Formula]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram of Torsion Problem Classification (KTU 2024)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T0[Torsion Problems in KTU] --> T1[Statically Determinate Shafts]
    T0 --> T2[Statically Indeterminate Shafts]
    T0 --> T3[Stepped Shafts]
    T0 --> T4[Composite Shafts]

    T1 --> T1A[Single Torque Applied]
    T1 --> T1B[Multiple Torques Free Body Method]
    T1A --> T1A1[Use Master Equation Directly]
    T1B --> T1B1[Equilibrium: Sum of T equals 0]
    T1B1 --> T1B2[Find Internal Torque in Each Segment]
    T1B2 --> T1B3[Apply Master Equation per Segment]

    T2 --> T2A[Compatibility: Theta total = 0]
    T2A --> T2A1[Solve for Reaction Torque]
    T2A1 --> T2A2[Then use Master Equation]

    T3 --> T3A[Each Step has Different J and T]
    T3A --> T3A1[Total Twist = Sum of Individual Twists]
    T3A1 --> T3A2[theta total = Sum TL over GJ per step]

    T4 --> T4A[Compatibility: Theta equal for All Materials]
    T4A --> T4A1[Equilibrium: Sum Ti = T Applied]
    T4A1 --> T4A2[Solve for Torque Distribution]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the term "pure torsion" and state the assumptions made in the theory of pure torsion.**

**Model Answer:**

**Pure Torsion:** It is a state of loading of a prismatic bar in which it is subjected to equal and opposite twisting moments (torques) acting in planes perpendicular to the longitudinal axis of the bar. The bar twists about its axis, and cross-sections rotate relative to one another.

**Assumptions of Pure Torsion Theory:**

1. The material of the shaft is **homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's law** (linear elastic behavior).
2. **Cross-sections plane before twisting remain plane and undistorted after twisting** (no warping).
3. **Radial lines drawn on the cross-section remain radial and straight** after twisting.
4. **Circular cross-sections remain circular** — their shape does not change.
5. The shaft is loaded by **end couples only** — no transverse or axial forces.
6. The shaft is **prismatic** (uniform cross-section) between the applied torques.
7. The twist per unit length is **small** (small deformations).
8. **Saint-Venant's principle** applies — stress is uniform away from points of torque application.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often forget to mention that the **circular cross-section remains circular** (or equivalently that the diameter doesn't change). This is a critical 1-mark point. Also, do NOT confuse "isotropic" with "homogeneous" — they are different properties.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Derive the expression for the polar moment of inertia of a hollow circular shaft of outer diameter D and inner diameter d.**

**Model Answer:**

The polar moment of inertia is defined as:

$$J = \int_A r^2 \, dA$$

For a circular cross-section, consider a thin annular ring at radius $r$ with thickness $dr$. The area of this ring is:

$$dA = 2\pi r \, dr$$

The polar moment of inertia is:

$$J = \int_{d/2}^{D/2} r^2 \cdot (2\pi r) \, dr = 2\pi \int_{d/2}^{D/2} r^3 \, dr$$

Evaluating the integral:

$$J = 2\pi \left[ \frac{r^4}{4} \right]_{d/2}^{D/2} = 2\pi \cdot \frac{1}{4} \left[ \left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^4 - \left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^4 \right]$$

$$J = \frac{\pi}{2} \left[ \frac{D^4}{16} - \frac{d^4}{16} \right] = \frac{\pi}{32} (D^4 - d^4)$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{J = \frac{\pi}{32} (D^4 - d^4)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse the formula for solid and hollow shafts, or write $J = \pi(D^4 - d^4)/16$ (forgetting the factor of 2). Always double-check: solid shaft → $J = \pi d^4 / 32$; hollow → $J = \pi(D^4 - d^4)/32$.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** A solid steel shaft of diameter 60 mm is subjected to a torque of 2.5 kN·m. Calculate:
   (i) The maximum shear stress in the shaft
   (ii) The angle of twist per meter length of the shaft
   
   Take G = 80 GPa for steel.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A hollow marine propeller shaft has an outer diameter of 150 mm and inner diameter of 100 mm. The shaft transmits 600 kW at 250 rpm. Calculate:
   (i) The maximum shear stress
   (ii) The angle of twist per meter length
   Take G = 80 GPa.

---

### MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION A (a) — Solid Shaft

**Given:**
- Diameter $d = 60 \text{ mm} = 0.06 \text{ m}$
- Torque $T = 2.5 \text{ kN·m} = 2500 \text{ N·m}$
- Shear modulus $G = 80 \text{ GPa} = 80 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}$

**Step 1: Calculate Polar Moment of Inertia**

$$J = \frac{\pi d^4}{32} = \frac{\pi (0.06)^4}{32}$$

$$J = \frac{\pi \times 1.296 \times 10^{-5}}{32} = \frac{4.0715 \times 10^{-5}}{32} = 1.2723 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^4$$

**Step 2 (i): Calculate Maximum Shear Stress**

The maximum shear stress occurs at the outer surface ($r = d/2 = 0.03$ m):

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{T \cdot r_{max}}{J} = \frac{2500 \times 0.03}{1.2723 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{75}{1.2723 \times 10^{-6}} = 58.95 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{max} = 58.95 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Step 2 (ii): Calculate Angle of Twist per Meter Length**

For $L = 1$ m:

$$\theta = \frac{T L}{G J} = \frac{2500 \times 1}{80 \times 10^9 \times 1.2723 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\theta = \frac{2500}{101784} = 0.02456 \text{ rad/m}$$

Converting to degrees:

$$\theta = 0.02456 \times \frac{180}{\pi} = 1.407 \text{ °/m}$$

$$\boxed{\theta = 0.02456 \text{ rad/m} = 1.407 \text{ °/m}}$$

---

### MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION A (b) — Hollow Shaft

**Given:**
- Outer diameter $D = 150 \text{ mm} = 0.15 \text{ m}$
- Inner diameter $d = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.10 \text{ m}$
- Power $P = 600 \text{ kW}$
- Speed $N = 250 \text{ rpm}$
- $G = 80 \text{ GPa}$

**Step 1: Calculate Torque Transmitted**

$$T = \frac{9550 \times P}{N} = \frac{9550 \times 600}{250} = \frac{5730000}{250} = 22920 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Polar Moment of Inertia**

$$J = \frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32} = \frac{\pi ((0.15)^4 - (0.10)^4)}{32}$$

$$J = \frac{\pi (5.0625 \times 10^{-4} - 1.0 \times 10^{-4})}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 4.0625 \times 10^{-4}}{32}$$

$$J = \frac{1.2761 \times 10^{-3}}{32} = 3.988 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^4$$

**Step 3 (i): Calculate Maximum Shear Stress**

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{T \cdot R}{J} = \frac{22920 \times 0.075}{3.988 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{1719}{3.988 \times 10^{-5}} = 43.10 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{max} = 43.10 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Step 3 (ii): Calculate Angle of Twist per Meter Length**

For $L = 1$ m:

$$\theta = \frac{T L}{G J} = \frac{22920 \times 1}{80 \times 10^9 \times 3.988 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$\theta = \frac{22920}{3190400} = 7.184 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad/m}$$

Converting to degrees:

$$\theta = 7.184 \times 10^{-3} \times \frac{180}{\pi} = 0.4116 \text{ °/m}$$

$$\boxed{\theta = 7.184 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad/m} = 0.412 \text{ °/m}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (14-mark questions):** (1) **Unit consistency** is critical — mixing mm and m, or N and kN, costs 1-2 marks immediately. (2) The power-torque formula $T = 9550 P/N$ requires **P in kW and N in rpm** to give **T in N·m**. (3) For hollow shafts, **D is outer and d is inner** — never interchange them. (4) Always state your formula **before substitution** to get partial credit even if the final answer is wrong.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice): [KTU University Exam – June 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** A stepped solid steel shaft consists of two segments. Segment 1 has a diameter of 80 mm and length 0.6 m. Segment 2 has a diameter of 60 mm and length 0.9 m. A torque of 5 kN·m is applied at the junction of the two segments, and the shaft is fixed at both far ends. Calculate the angle of twist at the junction point and the maximum shear stress in each segment. Take G = 80 GPa.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A composite shaft consists of a steel core (diameter 50 mm) surrounded by a brass tube (outer diameter 80 mm). The shaft is 1.5 m long and is subjected to a torque of 3 kN·m. Calculate:
   (i) The maximum shear stress in steel and brass
   (ii) The angle of twist of the shaft
   Take $G_{steel} = 80 \text{ GPa}$ and $G_{brass} = 40 \text{ GPa}$.

---

### MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION B (a) — Stepped Shaft

**Given:**
- Segment 1: $d_1 = 80 \text{ mm} = 0.08 \text{ m}$, $L_1 = 0.6 \text{ m}$
- Segment 2: $d_2 = 60 \text{ mm} = 0.06 \text{ m}$, $L_2 = 0.9 \text{ m}$
- Applied torque at junction: $T = 5 \text{ kN·m} = 5000 \text{ N·m}$
- $G = 80 \text{ GPa}$

**Step 1: Polar Moments of Inertia**

$$J_1 = \frac{\pi (0.08)^4}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 4.096 \times 10^{-5}}{32} = 4.021 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^4$$

$$J_2 = \frac{\pi (0.06)^4}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 1.296 \times 10^{-5}}{32} = 1.272 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^4$$

**Step 2: Torsional Rigidities**

$$G J_1 = 80 \times 10^9 \times 4.021 \times 10^{-6} = 3.217 \times 10^5 \text{ N·m}^2$$

$$G J_2 = 80 \times 10^9 \times 1.272 \times 10^{-6} = 1.018 \times 10^5 \text{ N·m}^2$$

**Step 3: Compatibility Equation**

Since the shaft is fixed at both ends, the angle of twist at the junction must be such that:

$$\theta_1 + \theta_2 = 0 \quad \text{(net rotation is zero)}$$

Wait — let me reconsider. The shaft is fixed at both far ends with a torque applied at the junction. The reactions at the fixed ends are $T_A$ and $T_B$ (opposite in sign). The applied torque at the junction is split between the two segments:

$$T_1 + T_2 = T \quad \text{(equilibrium)}$$

The angle of twist at the junction is the same when calculated from either end:

$$\theta_J = \frac{T_1 L_1}{G J_1} = \frac{T_2 L_2}{G J_2}$$

**Step 4: Solve for Torque Distribution**

From the compatibility equation:

$$\frac{T_1 L_1}{G J_1} = \frac{T_2 L_2}{G J_2} \implies \frac{T_1}{T_2} = \frac{L_2 G J_1}{L_1 G J_2} = \frac{L_2 J_1}{L_1 J_2}$$

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = \frac{0.9 \times 4.021 \times 10^{-6}}{0.6 \times 1.272 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{3.619 \times 10^{-6}}{7.632 \times 10^{-7}} = 4.742$$

So $T_1 = 4.742 T_2$. Substituting into equilibrium:

$$4.742 T_2 + T_2 = 5000 \implies 5.742 T_2 = 5000$$

$$T_2 = 870.8 \text{ N·m}, \quad T_1 = 4129.2 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 5: Maximum Shear Stress in Each Segment**

$$\tau_{1,max} = \frac{T_1 \cdot r_1}{J_1} = \frac{4129.2 \times 0.04}{4.021 \times 10^{-6}} = 41.08 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 41.08 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\tau_{2,max} = \frac{T_2 \cdot r_2}{J_2} = \frac{870.8 \times 0.03}{1.272 \times 10^{-6}} = 20.54 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 20.54 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{1,max} = 41.08 \text{ MPa}, \quad \tau_{2,max} = 20.54 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Step 6: Angle of Twist at the Junction**

$$\theta_J = \frac{T_1 L_1}{G J_1} = \frac{4129.2 \times 0.6}{3.217 \times 10^5} = 7.703 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad}$$

Converting to degrees:

$$\theta_J = 7.703 \times 10^{-3} \times \frac{180}{\pi} = 0.4414 \text{ °}$$

$$\boxed{\theta_J = 7.703 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad} = 0.441 \text{ °}}$$

---

### MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION B (b) — Composite Shaft

**Given:**
- Steel core: $d_s = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$
- Brass tube: outer diameter $D_b = 80 \text{ mm} = 0.08 \text{ m}$, inner diameter $d_s = 0.05 \text{ m}$
- Length $L = 1.5 \text{ m}$
- Applied torque $T = 3 \text{ kN·m} = 3000 \text{ N·m}$
- $G_{steel} = 80 \text{ GPa}$, $G_{brass} = 40 \text{ GPa}$

**Step 1: Polar Moments of Inertia**

Steel core:

$$J_s = \frac{\pi (0.05)^4}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 6.25 \times 10^{-6}}{32} = 6.136 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^4$$

Brass tube (annular):

$$J_b = \frac{\pi ((0.08)^4 - (0.05)^4)}{32} = \frac{\pi (4.096 \times 10^{-5} - 6.25 \times 10^{-6})}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 3.471 \times 10^{-5}}{32} = 3.407 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^4$$

**Step 2: Compatibility (Same Angle of Twist)**

Since steel and brass are bonded, they twist together:

$$\theta_s = \theta_b = \theta$$

**Step 3: Equilibrium**

$$T_s + T_b = T = 3000 \text{ N·m}$$

From compatibility:

$$\frac{T_s L}{G_s J_s} = \frac{T_b L}{G_b J_b}$$

$$\frac{T_s}{G_s J_s} = \frac{T_b}{G_b J_b} \implies T_s = T_b \cdot \frac{G_s J_s}{G_b J_b}$$

Calculating $G_s J_s$ and $G_b J_b$:

$$G_s J_s = 80 \times 10^9 \times 6.136 \times 10^{-7} = 4.909 \times 10^4 \text{ N·m}^2$$

$$G_b J_b = 40 \times 10^9 \times 3.407 \times 10^{-6} = 1.363 \times 10^5 \text{ N·m}^2$$

Therefore:

$$T_s = T_b \cdot \frac{4.909 \times 10^4}{1.363 \times 10^5} = 0.3602 T_b$$

Substituting into equilibrium:

$$0.3602 T_b + T_b = 3000 \implies 1.3602 T_b = 3000$$

$$T_b = 2205.5 \text{ N·m}, \quad T_s = 794.5 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 4 (i): Maximum Shear Stress in Steel and Brass**

For steel (at outer surface of steel core, $r = 0.025$ m):

$$\tau_{s,max} = \frac{T_s \cdot r_s}{J_s} = \frac{794.5 \times 0.025}{6.136 \times 10^{-7}} = 32.37 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{s,max} = 32.37 \text{ MPa}}$$

For brass (at outer surface of brass tube, $r = 0.04$ m):

$$\tau_{b,max} = \frac{T_b \cdot R_b}{J_b} = \frac{2205.5 \times 0.04}{3.407 \times 10^{-6}} = 25.89 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{b,max} = 25.89 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Step 4 (ii): Angle of Twist**

$$\theta = \frac{T_s L}{G_s J_s} = \frac{794.5 \times 1.5}{4.909 \times 10^4} = 2.428 \times 10^{-2} \text{ rad}$$

Converting to degrees:

$$\theta = 2.428 \times 10^{-2} \times \frac{180}{\pi} = 1.391 \text{ °}$$

$$\boxed{\theta = 0.02428 \text{ rad} = 1.391 \text{ °}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Composite Shafts):** (1) The compatibility condition is **equal angle of twist** for all materials, not equal shear stress. (2) The **equilibrium condition** is the sum of individual torques equals the applied torque. (3) When in doubt, **always solve the compatibility equation first**, then use the result in equilibrium. (4) The **maximum stress in each material is at its own outer surface**, not at the same radius. (5) If asked to find "torque carried by each material," remember $T_i = G_i J_i \theta_i / L$ — the stiffer material (larger $GJ$) carries more torque.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

### Quick-Reference Summary (Bullet Form)

- **Definition:** Pure torsion = loading by equal and opposite twisting moments (couples) acting in planes perpendicular to the shaft axis. The shaft twists, but its axis remains straight.
- **Assumptions (8 key):** Homogeneous, isotropic, linearly elastic material; plane sections remain plane and undistorted; radial lines remain radial; circular sections stay circular; end couples only; prismatic shaft; small deformations; Saint-Venant's principle applies away from ends.
- **Master Torsion Equation:** $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$ — the single most important equation in this module.
- **Polar Moment of Inertia:**
  - Solid circular: $J = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{32}$
  - Hollow circular: $J = \dfrac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32}$
- **Polar Section Modulus:**
  - Solid: $Z_p = \dfrac{\pi d^3}{16}$
  - Hollow: $Z_p = \dfrac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{16 D}$
- **Maximum Shear Stress:** $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{T \cdot r_{max}}{J} = \dfrac{T}{Z_p}$ — occurs at the outer surface.
- **Angle of Twist:** $\theta = \dfrac{TL}{GJ}$, measured in radians.
- **Torsional Rigidity:** $GJ$ — measures resistance to twist. Higher $GJ$ → less twist.
- **Strain Energy:** $U = \dfrac{T^2 L}{2 G J}$ — elastic energy stored due to torsion.
- **Power-Torque:** $T = \dfrac{9550 P}{N}$ (with $P$ in kW and $N$ in rpm giving $T$ in N·m); or $T = \dfrac{60 P}{2\pi N}$ (with $P$ in W).
- **Shear Stress Variation:** Linear with radius — zero at center, maximum at outer surface. Therefore, **material near the center of a solid shaft is underutilized** — this is the rationale for **hollow shafts** in marine applications.
- **Statically Determinate Shafts:** Apply master equation directly with internal torque from equilibrium.
- **Statically Indeterminate Shafts:** Use compatibility (e.g., total twist = 0 for fixed-fixed) + equilibrium to find reaction torques, then apply master equation.
- **Composite Shafts:** Compatibility = same twist for all materials. Equilibrium = sum of individual torques = applied torque.
- **Stepped Shafts:** Total twist = sum of individual twists: $\theta_{total} = \sum \dfrac{T_i L_i}{G_i J_i}$.
- **Marine Applications:** Propeller shafts, intermediate shafts, rudder stocks, gearbox torque tubes, coupling bolts.
- **Design Philosophy:** A good marine shaft design balances strength (τ_max ≤ τ_allowable) and stiffness (θ_max ≤ θ_allowable) while minimizing weight (favoring hollow sections).
- **Common Allowable Shear Stresses (KTU 2024 references):**
  - Mild steel: ~40 MPa
  - Marine aluminum: ~25-30 MPa
  - Cast iron: ~15-20 MPa
  - Bronze (propeller): ~30-40 MPa
- **G vs E Relation:** $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1 + \nu)}$ — for steel, $\nu \approx 0.3$, giving $G \approx 0.385 E$.
- **Failure Theories:** Maximum shear stress theory gives $\tau_{allow} = 0.5 \sigma_y / \text{FOS}$; von Mises/distortion energy gives $\tau_{allow} = 0.577 \sigma_y / \text{FOS}$.
- **KTU Trick:** Always convert the final angle of twist to **both radians and degrees** for full marks. Examiners love seeing both units.
- **Common Mistake to Avoid:** The master equation $T/J = \tau/r$ is dimensionally consistent because $J$ has units of $m^4$ and $r$ has units of $m$, so $T/J$ has units of $N/m^3 = N/m^2 / m = Pa/m \cdot m^{-1}$... actually verify: $T/J$ in $N \cdot m / m^4 = N/m^3$, and $\tau/r$ in $Pa/m = N/(m^2 \cdot m) = N/m^3$ ✓. Dimensionally consistent.
- **Key Insight for Naval Architects:** Hollow shafts with $D/d \approx 1.5$ to $2.0$ provide optimum strength-to-weight ratio. Marine propeller shafts are typically hollow (cast steel tubes) rather than solid bars, with the bore possibly serving as a seawater cooling channel.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** In KTU 2024 Module 4, expect at least one full 14-mark question on torsion and one 3-mark definition/derivation question. The questions are almost always based on the master equation and its three applications: stress, deformation, and strain energy. Practice numerical problems with both solid and hollow shafts. The internal choice (Part B) typically offers either (a) a stepped/indeterminate shaft problem OR (b) a composite shaft problem — be prepared for both.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Torsion of Circular Shafts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST303 - SOLID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Torsion of Circular Shafts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Torsion** is the twisting of a structural member (shaft) about its longitudinal axis when subjected to equal and opposite torques (couples) acting in parallel planes. The resulting internal shear stresses and angular deformations constitute the field of **torsion of circular shafts**, governed by the classical theory of pure torsion developed from the theory of elasticity.

In the context of naval architecture, torsion of circular shafts is foundational because **propeller shafts**, **rudder stocks**, and **drive shafts** of marine propulsion systems transmit torque from engines to propellers. These components experience pure torsional loading during steady-state operation and combined loading (torsion + bending) during dynamic sea conditions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine holding a **screwdriver** with one hand and twisting the handle with the other. The metal rod of the screwdriver is being **twisted about its axis** — the front face rotates relative to the back face, but every cross-section remains **circular and undistorted**. This is exactly what happens to a ship's propeller shaft: the engine end applies a turning moment, and the propeller end resists it, causing the entire shaft to twist along its length.

Think of the shaft as a stack of **circular discs** (cross-sections) bonded together. When torque is applied, each disc rotates slightly relative to its neighbour, just like cards in a deck being twisted. The **amount of rotation increases** progressively from the fixed end to the free end.

### 1.3 Core Assumptions in Pure Torsion Theory

> [!NOTE]
> The following assumptions (Saint-Venant's theory) form the backbone of the classical torsion equation:

- The shaft material is **homogeneous**, **isotropic**, and obeys **Hooke's Law** within elastic limits.
- Cross-sections **remain plane and circular** after twisting (no warping for circular sections).
- **Radial lines remain radial** — they do not curve, but merely rotate through an angle proportional to their distance from the fixed end.
- The **distance between cross-sections remains unchanged** (shaft length is constant).
- Cross-sections at a given distance from the fixed end rotate as a **rigid disc**.
- The applied torque acts about the **longitudinal axis** of the shaft.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit (SI) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $T$ | Applied Torque | **N·m** (Newton-metre) |
| $\tau$ | Shear Stress | **Pa** or **N/m²** = **MPa** |
| $G$ | Modulus of Rigidity | **GPa** (≈ 80 GPa for steel) |
| $\theta$ | Angle of Twist | **radians (rad)** |
| $J$ | Polar Moment of Inertia | **m⁴** or **mm⁴** |
| $L$ | Length of Shaft | **m** or **mm** |
| $P$ | Power Transmitted | **W (Watts)** |
| $N$ | Rotational Speed | **rpm** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Fundamental Torsion Equations (The Holy Trinity)

When a circular shaft of radius $R$ (or diameter $d$) and length $L$ is subjected to a torque $T$ at its free end:

**Equation 1 — Shear Stress at any radius $r$:**

$$\tau = \frac{T \cdot r}{J}$$

**Equation 2 — Angle of Twist over length $L$:**

$$\theta = \frac{T \cdot L}{G \cdot J}$$

**Equation 3 — Combined Torsion Equation:**

$$\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G \cdot \theta}{L}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These three equations are interlinked and form the **master framework** for solving **every** torsion problem in the KTU syllabus. Memorise the combined form (Equation 3) — it is the most frequently quoted in the university exam.

### 2.2 Polar Moment of Inertia ($J$)

The polar moment of inertia is a **geometric property** of the cross-section that quantifies its resistance to twisting.

**For a Solid Circular Shaft (diameter $d$, radius $R$):**

$$J = \frac{\pi \cdot d^4}{32} = \frac{\pi \cdot R^4}{2}$$

**For a Hollow Circular Shaft (outer diameter $D$, inner diameter $d$):**

$$J = \frac{\pi}{32} \left( D^4 - d^4 \right)$$

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Logical Framework

**Step 1:** Identify the **applied torque** $T$ (or compute from power: $T = \frac{P}{2\pi N/60}$).

**Step 2:** Compute the **polar moment of inertia** $J$ based on the cross-sectional geometry.

**Step 3:** Identify the **radius of interest** $r$ (often the outer surface where stress is maximum).

**Step 4:** Calculate the **maximum shear stress** at the outer fibre:

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{16 \cdot T}{\pi \cdot d^3} \quad \text{(solid shaft)}$$

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{16 \cdot T \cdot D}{\pi \cdot \left( D^4 - d^4 \right)} \quad \text{(hollow shaft)}$$

**Step 5:** Calculate the **angle of twist** $\theta$ over the required length $L$ using Equation 2.

**Step 6:** Convert $\theta$ from radians to **degrees** if required: $\theta_{deg} = \theta_{rad} \times \frac{180}{\pi}$.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $T = \frac{P \cdot 60}{2 \pi N}$ | Torque from Power (P in W, N in rpm) | N·m |
| 2 | $\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G \theta}{L}$ | Master Torsion Equation | — |
| 3 | $J = \frac{\pi d^4}{32}$ | Polar MI (solid) | m⁴ |
| 4 | $J = \frac{\pi}{32}(D^4 - d^4)$ | Polar MI (hollow) | m⁴ |
| 5 | $\tau_{\max} = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3}$ | Max shear stress (solid) | Pa |
| 6 | $\tau_{\max} = \frac{16 T D}{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}$ | Max shear stress (hollow) | Pa |
| 7 | $\theta = \frac{T L}{G J}$ | Angle of twist | rad |
| 8 | $\phi = \frac{\theta}{L} = \frac{T}{G J}$ | Twist per unit length | rad/m |
| 9 | $W = \frac{\pi d^3}{16}$ | Section modulus in torsion | m³ |
| 10 | $P = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60}$ | Power transmitted | W |
| 11 | $T_{\text{composite}} = \frac{G_1 J_1 + G_2 J_2}{L}$ style expressions | Stiffness $(GJ/L)$ for composite shafts | N·m/rad |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL MARKDOWN SAFEGUARD:** All table cells use $\vert$ or $\mid$ instead of the bare pipe character `|` to prevent table-breaking. Note cell 11 uses the contextual reference form.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **naval architecture** and **marine engineering**, the torsion of circular shafts governs:
- **Propeller shaft design** in single-screw and twin-screw vessels.
- **Rudder stock** sizing, where torsional loads are induced by hydrodynamic forces.
- **Intermediate shaft** strength between engine and gearbox.
- **Power transmission efficiency** through driveline systems.

In **mechanical engineering** generally: car drive shafts, turbine rotor shafts, twist drills, and machine tool spindles.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Torsion Equation (Saint-Venant's Approach)

Consider a circular shaft fixed at one end and free at the other. A torque $T$ is applied at the free end.

**Step 1: Geometry of Deformation**

Let a point $P$ on the outer surface of the shaft, at axial distance $x$ from the fixed end, be displaced. Originally, the line from the axis to $P$ is radial and perpendicular to the axis. After twisting, this line rotates through an angle $\phi$ at the free end. The arc length traversed by $P$ is:

$$s = R \cdot \theta$$

where $R$ is the outer radius and $\theta$ is the angle of twist over length $L$.

**Step 2: Shear Strain at Outer Fibre**

The shear strain $\gamma$ at the outer surface is defined as the angle of distortion. Considering a small element on the surface:

$$\gamma = \frac{R \cdot \theta}{L}$$

**Step 3: Shear Stress via Hooke's Law in Shear**

Applying Hooke's Law for shear deformation:

$$\tau = G \cdot \gamma = \frac{G \cdot R \cdot \theta}{L}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{\tau}{R} = \frac{G \cdot \theta}{L}$$

**Step 4: Generalisation to Any Radius $r$**

For a point at radius $r$ (where $r \leq R$), the shear strain is:

$$\gamma_r = \frac{r \cdot \theta}{L}$$

And the shear stress is:

$$\tau_r = G \cdot \gamma_r = \frac{G \cdot r \cdot \theta}{L}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{\tau_r}{r} = \frac{G \cdot \theta}{L}$$

**Step 5: Equilibrium — Relating Stress to Torque**

Consider a circular cross-section. The shear stress at radius $r$ acts tangentially. The elemental area is $dA = 2\pi r \cdot dr$ (a thin ring of radius $r$ and thickness $dr$). The elemental force is $\tau_r \cdot dA$ acting at moment arm $r$. Summing the moments of all elemental forces equals the applied torque:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \int_0^R \tau_r \cdot r \cdot dA \\
&= \int_0^R \left( \frac{G \cdot r \cdot \theta}{L} \right) \cdot r \cdot (2 \pi r \cdot dr) \\
&= \frac{2 \pi G \theta}{L} \int_0^R r^3 \, dr \\
&= \frac{2 \pi G \theta}{L} \cdot \frac{R^4}{4} \\
&= \frac{\pi G \theta R^4}{2 L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Introducing the Polar Moment of Inertia**

We define the polar moment of inertia for the solid section:

$$J = \frac{\pi R^4}{2} = \frac{\pi d^4}{32}$$

Substituting back:

$$T = \frac{G \cdot \theta}{L} \cdot J$$

Solving for $\frac{T}{J}$:

$$\frac{T}{J} = \frac{G \cdot \theta}{L}$$

**Step 7: Combining All Three Relationships**

Combining the geometric relation (Step 4) and the equilibrium relation (Step 6), we arrive at the **master torsion equation**:

$$\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G \cdot \theta}{L}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This equation is the cornerstone of the **KTU Solid Mechanics Module 4** syllabus. Both Part A and Part B questions rely on manipulating this single relationship.

### 3.2 Derivation of Power-Torque Relationship

Power is defined as the rate of doing work. For a shaft rotating at angular velocity $\omega$ rad/s under torque $T$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= T \cdot \omega \\
\omega &= \frac{2 \pi N}{60} \quad \text{where } N \text{ is in rpm} \\
\therefore P &= \frac{2 \pi N T}{60} \quad \text{(in Watts)}
\end{aligned}
$$

In **horsepower (HP)**: $P_{HP} = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60 \times 745.7}$ when $T$ is in N·m.

In **kilowatts (kW)**: $P_{kW} = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60 \times 1000}$ when $T$ is in N·m.

### 3.3 Derivation for Hollow Shafts

For a hollow shaft with outer radius $R$ and inner radius $r_i$, the polar moment of inertia is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
J &= \int_{r_i}^{R} r^2 \cdot dA \\
&= \int_{r_i}^{R} r^2 \cdot (2 \pi r \cdot dr) \\
&= 2 \pi \int_{r_i}^{R} r^3 \, dr \\
&= 2 \pi \left[ \frac{r^4}{4} \right]_{r_i}^{R} \\
&= \frac{\pi}{2} \left( R^4 - r_i^4 \right) \\
&= \frac{\pi}{32} \left( D^4 - d^4 \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

The maximum shear stress occurs at the **outer fibre** ($r = R$):

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{T \cdot R}{J} = \frac{16 T \cdot D}{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}$$

> [!TIP]
> The **minimum shear stress** (at the inner bore, $r = r_i$) is $\tau_{\min} = \frac{T \cdot r_i}{J}$. This is why hollow shafts are **more efficient** than solid shafts — the low-stress central material is removed.

### 3.4 Composite Shafts (Two Materials in Parallel)

When a shaft is made of two different materials bonded together (e.g., a steel core with a brass sleeve), both materials experience the **same angle of twist** (compatibility condition):

$$\theta_1 = \theta_2 \implies \frac{T_1 L_1}{G_1 J_1} = \frac{T_2 L_2}{G_2 J_2}$$

The total torque is:

$$T = T_1 + T_2$$

The **equivalent shaft** (all in material 1) has:

$$J_{eq} = J_1 + \frac{G_2}{G_1} \cdot J_2$$

### 3.5 Statically Indeterminate Shafts

When a shaft is fixed at both ends and loaded by torques in between, the problem is **statically indeterminate** because equilibrium alone cannot determine the reaction torques. We use:

**Equilibrium:** $T_A + T_B = T_{\text{applied}}$

**Compatibility:** $\theta_{AB}^{\text{from A}} + \theta_{AB}^{\text{from B}} = 0$ (since both ends are fixed, total twist is zero)

This gives a system of two linear equations in two unknowns.
