## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Torsion Problem Solving Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Problem Input: Geometry, Material, Loading] --> B[Identify Shaft Type: Solid or Hollow?]
    B --> C[Compute Polar Moment of Inertia J]
    A --> D[Compute Applied Torque T from Power or Given]
    C --> E[Apply Torsion Equation T/J = tau/r = G theta/L]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Compute Maximum Shear Stress tau_max at r = R]
    E --> G[Compute Angle of Twist theta over length L]
    F --> H[Check Against Allowable Shear Stress]
    G --> I[Check Against Allowable Twist Angle]
    H --> J[Design Verified: Report Outputs]
    I --> J
    H --> K[Design Fails: Increase Diameter or Change Material]
    I --> K
    K --> C
    J --> L[Final Solution: tau_max, theta, Safety Factor]
```

### 4.2 Block Architecture: Stress Distribution in Circular Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[Solid Circular Shaft] --> N[Polar MI: J = pi d^4 / 32]
    M --> O[Shear Stress: Linear from Centre to Surface]
    O --> P[tau = 0 at Centre r=0]
    O --> Q[tau = tau_max at Outer Surface r=R]
    R[Hollow Circular Shaft] --> S[Polar MI: J = pi D^4 minus d^4 over 32]
    R --> T[Shear Stress: Linear in Material Region]
    T --> U[tau = 0 only if r=0 included inside bore]
    T --> V[tau = tau_max at Outer Surface r=R]
```

### 4.3 Schematic: Twisting of a Circular Shaft

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Fixed End of Shaft] --> A2[Free End with Applied Torque T]
    A2 --> A3[Cross-sections rotate through progressively increasing angle theta]
    A3 --> A4[Outer fibre experiences maximum shear strain gamma = R theta per L]
    A3 --> A5[Inner fibres experience linearly smaller shear strain proportional to r]
    A4 --> A6[Result: Shear stress varies linearly from 0 at centre to tau_max at surface]
```

### 4.4 Block Architecture: Power Transmission System (Naval Application)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E[Marine Engine] -->|Torque T1| GS[Gearbox]
    GS -->|Torque T2| IS[Intermediate Shaft]
    IS -->|Torque T3| PS[Propeller Shaft]
    PS -->|Torque T4| PROP[Propeller]
    PROP -->|Thrust Force| WATER[Water: Converts to Vessel Motion]
    IS --> TS[Torsional Stress in Shaft]
    PS --> TS
    TS --> TS1[Check: tau_max less than tau_allowable]
    TS --> TS2[Check: theta less than theta_allowable]
    TS1 --> SAFE[Safe Operation]
    TS2 --> SAFE
```

### **PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)**

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**State the torsion equation for a circular shaft. Define each term with units.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> The torsion equation for a circular shaft is:
>
> $$\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G \cdot \theta}{L}$$
>
> Where:
> - $T$ = Applied torque (N·m)
> - $J$ = Polar moment of inertia of the cross-section (m⁴)
> - $\tau$ = Shear stress at radius $r$ (Pa)
> - $r$ = Radial distance from the centre (m)
> - $G$ = Modulus of rigidity of the material (Pa)
> - $\theta$ = Angle of twist over length $L$ (radians)
> - $L$ = Length of the shaft over which twist is measured (m)

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between the polar moment of inertia of a solid shaft and a hollow shaft. Why are hollow shafts preferred for power transmission?**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Solid Shaft | Hollow Shaft |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Polar MI | $J_s = \frac{\pi d^4}{32}$ | $J_h = \frac{\pi}{32}(D^4 - d^4)$ |
| Weight | Higher | Lower (less material) |
| Material utilisation | Poor (low stress at centre) | Excellent |
| Strength-to-weight | Lower | Higher |

Hollow shafts are preferred because:
1. **Higher torque-to-weight ratio** — the central material carries negligible stress but adds significant mass.
2. **Material economy** — removes low-stress material, reducing cost.
3. **Greater torsional stiffness** per unit weight.
4. **Reduced inertia** in rotating applications.

### **PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)**

---

**Question A** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**(a) [7 Marks]** A solid circular steel shaft of diameter **60 mm** transmits **45 kW** at **300 rpm**. Determine:
(i) The torque transmitted.
(ii) The maximum shear stress developed in the shaft.
(iii) The angle of twist in degrees over a length of **2 m**. Take $G = 80 \text{ GPa}$.

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Diameter $d = 60 \text{ mm} = 0.06 \text{ m}$, so radius $R = 0.03 \text{ m}$
- Power $P = 45 \text{ kW} = 45000 \text{ W}$
- Speed $N = 300 \text{ rpm}$
- Length $L = 2 \text{ m}$
- $G = 80 \text{ GPa} = 80 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}$

**(i) Torque Transmitted:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{P \times 60}{2 \pi N} \\
&= \frac{45000 \times 60}{2 \pi \times 300} \\
&= \frac{2700000}{600 \pi} \\
&= \frac{4500}{\pi} \\
&\approx 1432.4 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Maximum Shear Stress:**

Polar moment of inertia for solid shaft:

$$J = \frac{\pi d^4}{32} = \frac{\pi (0.06)^4}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 1.296 \times 10^{-5}}{32} = 1.272 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^4$$

Maximum shear stress at outer surface:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{\max} &= \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3} \\
&= \frac{16 \times 1432.4}{\pi \times (0.06)^3} \\
&= \frac{22918.4}{\pi \times 2.16 \times 10^{-4}} \\
&= \frac{22918.4}{6.786 \times 10^{-4}} \\
&\approx 33.77 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} \\
&\approx 33.77 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Angle of Twist:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta &= \frac{T L}{G J} \\
&= \frac{1432.4 \times 2}{80 \times 10^9 \times 1.272 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&= \frac{2864.8}{101760} \\
&= 0.02815 \text{ radians} \\
\theta_{\text{deg}} &= 0.02815 \times \frac{180}{\pi} \\
&\approx 1.613^{\circ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:** $T \approx 1432.4 \text{ N·m}$, $\tau_{\max} \approx 33.77 \text{ MPa}$, $\theta \approx 1.613^{\circ}$.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A hollow shaft of outer diameter **80 mm** and inner diameter **50 mm** is required to transmit **120 kW** at **250 rpm**. The maximum allowable shear stress is **60 MPa**. Calculate the length over which the angle of twist must be limited to **1.0°**. Take $G = 84 \text{ GPa}$.

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Outer diameter $D = 80 \text{ mm} = 0.08 \text{ m}$
- Inner diameter $d_i = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$
- Power $P = 120 \text{ kW} = 120000 \text{ W}$
- Speed $N = 250 \text{ rpm}$
- Allowable shear stress $\tau_{\text{allow}} = 60 \text{ MPa} = 60 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$
- Allowable angle of twist $\theta_{\text{allow}} = 1.0^{\circ} = \frac{\pi}{180} \text{ rad} = 0.01745 \text{ rad}$
- $G = 84 \text{ GPa} = 84 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}$

**Step 1: Compute the Applied Torque:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{P \times 60}{2 \pi N} = \frac{120000 \times 60}{2 \pi \times 250} \\
&= \frac{7200000}{500 \pi} = \frac{14400}{\pi} \\
&\approx 4583.66 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the Polar Moment of Inertia:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
J &= \frac{\pi}{32} (D^4 - d_i^4) \\
&= \frac{\pi}{32} \left[ (0.08)^4 - (0.05)^4 \right] \\
&= \frac{\pi}{32} \left[ 4.096 \times 10^{-5} - 6.25 \times 10^{-6} \right] \\
&= \frac{\pi}{32} \times 3.471 \times 10^{-5} \\
&= 3.407 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Verify Maximum Shear Stress:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{\max} &= \frac{T \cdot R}{J} = \frac{4583.66 \times 0.04}{3.407 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&= \frac{183.35}{3.407 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&\approx 53.81 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 53.81 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $53.81 \text{ MPa} < 60 \text{ MPa}$ (allowable), the **strength criterion is satisfied**.

**Step 4: Calculate Length for the Twist Criterion:**

Using $\theta = \frac{T L}{G J}$ and solving for $L$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= \frac{\theta \cdot G \cdot J}{T} \\
&= \frac{0.01745 \times 84 \times 10^9 \times 3.407 \times 10^{-6}}{4583.66} \\
&= \frac{0.01745 \times 286188}{4583.66} \\
&= \frac{4993.98}{4583.66} \\
&\approx 1.089 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $L \approx 1.089 \text{ m}$ (approximately **1089 mm**).

---

**Question B (Alternative for Internal Choice)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**(a) [7 Marks]** A shaft is fixed at both ends and carries three pulleys. Pulley A at the left end, Pulley B at the middle, and Pulley C at the right end. Torques applied: $T_A = 6 \text{ kN·m}$, $T_B = 4 \text{ kN·m}$, $T_C = 2 \text{ kN·m}$ (with directions as per standard convention). The shaft has uniform diameter throughout. Determine the reactions at the fixed ends if the length between A and B is **1.5 m** and between B and C is **1.0 m**. Take $GJ = 80 \text{ kN·m}^2$.

**Model Solution:**

**Setup:**
Let $R_A$ and $R_C$ be the reaction torques at the fixed ends A and C respectively.
Let $T_B$ be the applied torque at the middle pulley (assume applied externally).

**Equilibrium of the entire shaft:**

$$R_A + R_C = T_B \quad \text{(assuming proper sign convention)}$$

$$R_A + R_C = 4 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Compatibility Condition:**
Since both ends are fixed, the total angle of twist between A and C must be zero. The torque in segment AB is $T_{AB} = R_A$ and the torque in segment BC is $T_{BC} = R_A - T_B$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_{AB} + \theta_{BC} &= 0 \\
\frac{T_{AB} \cdot L_{AB}}{GJ} + \frac{T_{BC} \cdot L_{BC}}{GJ} &= 0 \\
\frac{R_A \times 1.5}{GJ} + \frac{(R_A - 4) \times 1.0}{GJ} &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solving:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
1.5 R_A + 1.0 R_A - 4.0 &= 0 \\
2.5 R_A &= 4.0 \\
R_A &= 1.6 \text{ kN·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Therefore:**

$$R_C = 4 - 1.6 = 2.4 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Verification by twist:**
- $\theta_{AB} = \frac{1.6 \times 1.5}{80} = 0.030 \text{ rad}$
- $\theta_{BC} = \frac{(1.6 - 4) \times 1.0}{80} = \frac{-2.4}{80} = -0.030 \text{ rad}$
- Sum = 0 ✓

**Final Answer:** $R_A = 1.6 \text{ kN·m}$, $R_C = 2.4 \text{ kN·m}$.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A composite shaft consists of a **steel core** of diameter **40 mm** surrounded by a **brass tube** of outer diameter **70 mm**. The shaft is subjected to a torque of **2 kN·m**. Determine:
(i) The torque carried by each material.
(ii) The maximum shear stress in each material.

Given: $G_{\text{steel}} = 80 \text{ GPa}$, $G_{\text{brass}} = 40 \text{ GPa}$.

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Steel core: $d_s = 40 \text{ mm}$, so $r_s = 20 \text{ mm} = 0.020 \text{ m}$
- Brass tube: $D = 70 \text{ mm}$, inner diameter = 40 mm, so $R = 35 \text{ mm} = 0.035 \text{ m}$
- $G_s = 80 \text{ GPa}$, $G_b = 40 \text{ GPa}$
- Total torque $T = 2 \text{ kN·m} = 2000 \text{ N·m}$

**Step 1: Compute Polar Moments of Inertia:**

For the steel core:

$$J_s = \frac{\pi d_s^4}{32} = \frac{\pi (0.040)^4}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 2.56 \times 10^{-6}}{32} = 2.513 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^4$$

For the brass tube:

$$
\begin{aligned}
J_b &= \frac{\pi}{32}(D^4 - d_s^4) \\
&= \frac{\pi}{32}\left[(0.070)^4 - (0.040)^4\right] \\
&= \frac{\pi}{32}\left[2.401 \times 10^{-5} - 2.56 \times 10^{-6}\right] \\
&= \frac{\pi}{32} \times 2.145 \times 10^{-5} \\
&= 2.106 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply Compatibility and Equilibrium**

Compatibility: $\theta_s = \theta_b \implies \frac{T_s L}{G_s J_s} = \frac{T_b L}{G_b J_b}$

Equilibrium: $T_s + T_b = 2000 \text{ N·m}$

From compatibility:

$$
\frac{T_s}{T_b} = \frac{G_s J_s}{G_b J_b} = \frac{80 \times 10^9 \times 2.513 \times 10^{-7}}{40 \times 10^9 \times 2.106 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{20.10}{84.24} = 0.2386
$$

Therefore: $T_s = 0.2386 \cdot T_b$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.2386 T_b + T_b &= 2000 \\
1.2386 T_b &= 2000 \\
T_b &= 1614.7 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$T_s = 2000 - 1614.7 = 385.3 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 3: Maximum Shear Stresses**

For steel (at radius $r = 20$ mm):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{s,\max} &= \frac{T_s \cdot r_s}{J_s} = \frac{385.3 \times 0.020}{2.513 \times 10^{-7}} \\
&= \frac{7.706}{2.513 \times 10^{-7}} \\
&\approx 30.66 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

For brass (at outer radius $R = 35$ mm):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{b,\max} &= \frac{T_b \cdot R}{J_b} = \frac{1614.7 \times 0.035}{2.106 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&= \frac{56.51}{2.106 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&\approx 26.83 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:** $T_s \approx 385.3 \text{ N·m}$, $T_b \approx 1614.7 \text{ N·m}$, $\tau_{s,\max} \approx 30.66 \text{ MPa}$, $\tau_{b,\max} \approx 26.83 \text{ MPa}$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert $N$ (rpm) to $\omega$ (rad/s)** in power-torque calculations. KTU examiners specifically test $T = \frac{60 P}{2 \pi N}$ — a single mistake here costs **3 marks**.
> 2. **Mixing units**: $d$ in mm and $G$ in GPa without converting. Always use **consistent SI units** (metres, Pa, N·m) throughout.
> 3. **Using diameter instead of radius** in the shear stress equation $\tau = T r / J$. Many students write $d$ instead of $r$ and lose 2-3 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert radians to degrees** (or vice versa) in the final answer for angle of twist.
> 5. **In composite shafts**, the most common error is failing to check **compatibility of angle of twist** — a 1-mark deduction each time.
> 6. **In statically indeterminate shafts**, students often forget that the **compatibility equation is essential** because equilibrium alone is insufficient. KTU expects both equations to be written explicitly.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Torsion of Circular Shafts**

- ✅ The **master torsion equation** is $\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G\theta}{L}$ — know it by heart.
- ✅ **Polar moment of inertia** for solid shaft: $J = \frac{\pi d^4}{32}$. For hollow: $J = \frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32}$.
- ✅ **Maximum shear stress** always occurs at the **outer surface** ($r = R$).
- ✅ Shear stress is **zero at the centre** and varies **linearly** with radius.
- ✅ **Angle of twist** $\theta$ is in **radians**. Convert to degrees using $\frac{180}{\pi}$ only for final answers.
- ✅ **Power-Torque relationship**: $T = \frac{60 P}{2 \pi N}$ (with $P$ in Watts and $N$ in rpm).
- ✅ **Composite shafts**: Use **compatibility** ($\theta_1 = \theta_2$) and **equilibrium** ($T = T_1 + T_2$) together.
- ✅ **Statically indeterminate shafts** require **two equations**: equilibrium + compatibility.
- ✅ **Hollow shafts** are preferred for power transmission due to higher strength-to-weight ratio.
- ✅ Units must be **consistent throughout**: convert mm to m, kN·m to N·m, etc.
- ✅ For naval applications, **propeller shaft design** uses the torsion formula directly, often combined with bending.
- ✅ The **section modulus in torsion** $W = \frac{\pi d^3}{16}$ is the **polar section modulus** — useful for quick stress calculations: $\tau_{\max} = \frac{T}{W}$.
- ✅ When asked for **length for given twist**, use $L = \frac{\theta \cdot GJ}{T}$.
- ✅ When asked for **diameter for given stress**, use $d = \left( \frac{16 T}{\pi \tau_{\text{allow}}} \right)^{1/3}$.
